VISCOUNT BANGOR: I am authorised by the Prime Minister, on his behalf,

The Linen Trade.
THE SENATE
QUESTIONS TO MINISTER.
UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE
FUND.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he can state the
amount of the contribution paid by the
British Government to the Unemployment
Insurance Fund of Northern Ireland this
year for the purpose of keeping this fund
on a parity with the Unemployment Insurance Fund of Great Britain; and the
average yearly amount of the Land Purchase Annuities now paid into the Exchequer of Northern Ireland and formerly paid into the Exchequer of the
United Kingdom?
VISCOUNT BANGOR: I heard only
this morning that the noble Viscount, the
Leader of the House, is unavoidably prevented from being in his place to-day,
and in his absence I have been asked to
reply to these questions. The hon. and
learned Member will realise that he is
asking two questions which are not related to each other.
In regard to the hon. and learned Member's first question, the contribution received in respect of the nine months of the
present financial year is £280,000, or, if
he refers to the calendar year, the amount
received in 1929 was £369,000.
year
With regard to the second question, the
estimated amount to be received this
by the Northern Ireland Exchequer in
respect of Land Purchase Annuities to be
retained by the Exchequer of Northern
Ireland under Section 26 of the Government of Ireland Act, 1920, is £665,000.
These annuities were not formerly paid
into the Exchequer of the United Kingdom. but into the Land Purchase Fund.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask the
noble Viscount if the result of the first
set of figures he has given is that unemployment insurance in Northern Ireland
would be unworkable without the huge
grant-in-aid from the Imperial Treasury.
VISCOUNT BANGOR: I think 1
must have notice of that question. I am
afraid I cannot answer it off-hand.
THE LINEN TRADE.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether-.
(a) in the years 1290-21-22 over 400
Ulster firms were engaged in the
linen trade, and linen exports were
about £16,000,000 a year;
(b) in the year 1929 the Ulster firms
in the linen trade numbered about
300, and linen exports were somewhat over £9,000,000;
(c) linen products from the Continent
are sold in Northern Ireland at
prices with which home manufacturers cannot compete; and
(d) the Ulster linen trade now depends
for 80 per cent. of its flax upon
foreign crops?
Viscount BANGOR: I have also been
asked by my right hon. Friend, the
Leader of the House, to reply to this
Question.
The questions
learned Member has put do not
admit of precise answers, inasmuch as
they concern matters in respect of which
official figures are not available. Accordingly, I must ask him to accept the
information which I am able to give as
the best procurable and as furnishing at
least an indication of the actual position
in regard to the several matters touched
upon in his questions.
which the hon. and
In
There are no official statistics of the
number of firms engaged in the Northern
Irish linen industry either for the earlier
years mentioned in part (a) of the question or for 1929. On this point, however,
the Register of Joint Stock Companies
affords some information which will be
helpful in indicating the position as between the two periods mentioned.
the year 1922 there were 228 "living"
textile companies in Northern Ireland
shown on the Register. In 1924 this
number had increased to 278, while in
1926 and 1928 it stood at 276. I should
here explain that these figures include
not only manufacturers and merchants
engaged in the linen industry, but also
manufacturers and merchants engaged in
other textile industries, principally
cotton and woollens. As the hon. and
learned Member will however understand,
the great bulk of the total for each year
represents firms engaged in the linen industry. To the totals which I have
quoted must be added, of course, frmis
which do not come within the category
of Joint Stock Companies, but as to the
number of these there are no official
figures available.
505 The Linen Trade.

14 JANUARY 1930
The Linen Trade.
Turning now to the exports of linen
goods, I should first explain that the Imperial Customs Authorities do not compile
statistics of the trade in goods passing
between Great Britain and Northern
Ireland, all such shipments being regarded as internal. The official customs
figures, therefore, relate only to the
linen exported from the United Kingdom
of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
direct to places abroad. The returns of
these exports published by the Board of
Trade show that in 1921 the linen goods
re-exported
exported and
from the
United Kingdom amounted to just under
£9,000,000, while the same class of goods
exported in 1922 and 1923 were valued
The
at
approximately £10,800,000.
complete figures for 1929 are not yet
available, but those for 1928 show that
the linen goods exported from the United
Kingdom in that year were valued
at slightly more than £8,800,000.
While, as I have said, the Imperial
Authorities do not compile statistics of
the trade between Great Britain and
Northern Ireland, the Ministry of Commerce here has endeavoured to assess the
value of such trade from year to year on
the basis of the returns furnished by the
various Northern Irish harbour authorities. In quoting the figures thus compiled, however, I should explain that
these are, and can only be, estimates.
inasmuch as the harbour returns do not
include particulars as to value, but are
confined to gross weights. The hon. and
learned Member must, therefore, regard
these estimates as being the nearest approximation to the actual values which in
the circumstances it is possible to procure.
The Ministry's estimates begin with
the year 1922, in which year, exclusive
of exports to the Irish Free State, which
were not availabie and in any case would
not materially affect the total, 15
million pounds worth of linen goods were
exported from Northern Ireland, mainly
to Great Britain. A large proportion of
this amount is, of course, included in the
figure of £10,800,000 which I have
quoted as representing the value of linen
exported from the United Kingdom durthe same year. In 1924 the value of
linen goods exported from Northern Ireland, as estimated by the Ministry, was
approximately 18 millions, while in 1926
the corresponding figure was £15,360,000.
In 1928, the latest year for which complete estimates are available, the value
is estimated as being £14,760,000.
Although the figures which I have
quoted do show some appreciable fluctuations in the value of the linen exported
from Northern Ireland since 1922, they
do not indicate any marked decrease in
1928 as compared with those for the
years 1922 and 1923. I do not suggest
that these figures can be taken as implying a wholly satisfactory state of affairs.
in the linen industry-the unemployment
figures would sufficiently contradict thisbut I do think that they may be accepted as indicating the absence of any
progressively serious depression in the industry during the period in question.
As regards point (c) in the hon. and
learned Member's question, I think it is
common knowledge that the Continental
countries producing linen in competition
with the Northern Irish industry do enjoy certain advantages in the matter of
cost of production. While it is scarcely.
a matter on which the Government is in a
position to express an opinion, I think
there can be little doubt that the prices.
at which certain classes of foreign linen
goods are offered to the public not only in
Northern Ireland, but also in Great
Britain, are less than the prices at which
linen goods intended for the same purpose could be manufactured in Northern
Ireland. Such a comparison, however, to
be of any use, would require to take
account not merely of price but also of
quality, and here it is. I think, that the
explanation as to the difference between
the foreign and the home-produced article
is largely to be found.
In regard to the last part of the question; on the assumption that all the flax
fibre produced from home-grown flax in
a particular year, together with the net
imports of foreign flax into Northern Ireland during the same year, is actually
used in the manufacture of linen in
Northern Ireland during that year, it is
found that the percentage of foreign flax
used by the Northern Irish linen industry
since 1922 to 1928 inclusive has varied
between 62 per cent. and 83 per cent.
The average percentage over the whole of
the period is 71 per cent. Unfortunately
it is impossible to make an exact comparison between these percentages and
that for any pre-war year, as separate
figures for Northern Irish importations
are not obtainable prior to 1922.
ever, taking the total production of fibre
from flax grown within the United King-
How-
507 The Linen Trade.

THE SENATE
The Linen Trade.
[Viscount Bangor]
dom in 1913 and the net imports of fibre
into the United Kingdom for that year.
it is found that approximately 90 per
cent. of the flax used in the manufacture
of linen in the United Kingdom during
1913 was of foreign origin. As the hon.
and learned Member will see, therefore,
there is no reason for thinking that the
Northern Irish linen industry is becoming increasingly dependent in any degree
upon foreign sources of supply for its raw
material.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask whether
or not the recent shrinkage in the linen
exports which the noble Viscount's figures
show is not largely due to the lack of any
definite policy of the Government with
regard to this vital trade?
Mr. MCALLISTER: Arising out of
the answer of the noble Deputy Leader
of the House I would like to address a
remark or two to the last portion of the
question. I think it is a depressing and
appalling fact in a country so essentially
agricultural as this that the amount of
foreign flax used in the manufacture of
linen should be as high as 90 per cent. in
the year 1913 and that the average between the year 1922 and the present
should be 82 per cent. I think it is an
appalling fact, and one that reflects anything but credit on everyone concerned in
the industry, and on the Government of
Northern Ireland. If we take that as a
test of the way things are carried on in
Northern Ireland at the present time then
it is a very depressing outlook for the regeneration of industry and the restoration
of trade in Northern Ireland.
I submit to the Government that if we
stand idly by, watching this state of
affairs continuing without finding ways
and means of remedying it, and finding
the reason why we in Northern Ireland
are not able to supply the flax required
for the linen industry, I think it will be
an impossible outlook for things generally
in Northern Ireland. If I am told that
the fault is with the flax produced, that
it is not essentially as suitable as Continental flax, I suggest that that is no
answer, because I am satisfied that in a
country like Ireland with a soil as fruitful
and as capable of growing abundant.
erops as the soil of any country in the
world, and at all events the farmers can
produce flax.
If the deficiency is in the question of
handling the flax, then I say it is the duty
of the Government to examine what is defective in the handling of the flax and
why the flax here should not be as suitable as that of other countries. I quite
recognise that a subsidy is perhaps the
last resort, but if the Germans find it
necessary to subsidise potatoes, it is essential in the present state of farming that
the Government should subsidise the
growth of flax in order that the farmers
of Northern Ireland may be put in the.
position of farmers of other countries.
Mr. DORMAN: A hundred years ago
the Government, of that time found it
necessary to subsidise the cultivation of
flax in Northern Ireland. If my memory
serves me right I think the subsidy extended to £30,000 or £40,000 a year, all
of which came to Northern Ireland for the
purpose of improving the species of flax
being cultivated in our area. It is quite
possible that attention being drawn to
this particular fact may induce the
Government to look up how this particular industry was assisted by the Imperial Government, after it abolished the
right of the Irish Parliament to grant a
certain portion of the duty on tea and
coffee for the purpose of developing the
linen industry. My memory does not
serve me, but I think if the Government
had continued the advantage which the
linen industry had under Grattan's
Parliament it would have come to some
£200,000. The Government abolished
that and gave a grant of only some
£35,000 a year.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN: As one
very intimately connected with the linen
industry both in this country and on the
Continent might I say with reference to
the hon. Senator's question (c) that the
linen industry on the Continent which is
competing with us in Northern Ireland is
also at a very low ebb indeed. It is a
great deal worse than even ours is at the
present moment. In Germany and
Czecho-Slovakia where these cheap things
come from thev are doing very badly indeed. About half the mills are closed
and a great many of the factories, and so,
in a way, if they are competing I do not
think we have as much to fear from them
as we had before.
With regard to the flax question before
the war 75 per cent. of the flax used was
grown in Russia, so you see there is not a
great deal of difference between what is
brought in from foreign places now and
what there was before. And, of course,
The Linen Trade.
Adjournment
14 JANUARY 1930
Russia grows very little flax now, because
the people have got to grow something to
eat before they grow anything for sale.
With regard to Irish flax I was very interested to hear what was said about it.
We use a tremendous lot of flax; we are one
of the largest buyers of flax. At one time,
just when I came into the business, we
used nothing but Irish. Then we used some
Courtrai. At the present time we use very
little Irish indeed. It is not that we do
not want to use it, but we cannot get the
quality out of it. If something could be
done to improve the quality of Irish flax
it might help. We hear all sorts of things
about it. Some say it is the seed.
I remember in 1888 we could not get enough
bad flax, the flax was so good. But that
sort of flax is not to be seen. It is not
to be seen in the Courtrai flax either.
the same time you do get the quality there
to spin yarn.
If something could be
done to improve the quality of Irish flax
we would be in a much better way and
would help the Northern farmers.
At
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: In
regard to the portion of the question
speaking of the trade of the country I
would like to point out that it is not
alone the linen business but the cotton
business as far as I can see has decreased
from 1922 to last year by between 25 per
cent. and 33 per cent. In considering
this I think the demand for textiles
through the change of fashion of summer
goods has been a going down business.
I would like to ask the Government in
considering this question to take into
consideration also the question of the decrease in cotton. They might find that
the two had a common cause.
Mr. ARMSTRONG : I come from a
flax-growing district, and I think in discussing this question of the production.
of flax one has to remember that it is a
very exhausting crop, and it ought not
to be grown more than once in seven years
on the same land. The consequence of
this has been that the high prices that
were given at the time of the war tempted
the farmers to over-flax the country. I
think that accounts to a great extent for
the deterioration in the quality. Of
course there is the question of the uncertainty of the price they may get and
of the seasons. I have seen excellent flax
grown in what is now the Free State.
think it is a great pity that its cultivation
is not adopted to a greater extent. Of
I
course
the handling of it afterwards.
might be rather diflicult for them for a
time. The flax might be sent up here.
I have seen a finer crop of flax in County
Longford than anywhere else.
Viscount BANGOR: With regard to
the hon. and learned Senator's supplementary question it is based on the assumption that there has been a marked
shrinkage in linen exports over the
period in question. I think the information I have been given from the
Ministry in my reply has shown that such
is hardly the case.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I refer to recent
years.
Viscount BANGOR: I really do not
quite understand what the hon. and
learned Senator means by recent years.
Mr. CAMPBELL: For one year, 1924,
the noble Viscount gave the figure of 18
millions. Since that time there has been
on his own showing a steady decline.
Viscount BANGOR: I do not think
the figures I gave show that. However,
with regard to the suggestion of the hon.
Senator Dorman that the Government
should encourage the cultivation of flax
in Northern Ireland I may point out that
the Government is giving effective assistance towards an increase in flax culture
in Northern Ireland by grants to the
Flax Industry Development Association.
Over and above this of course the Government has given assistance to the Northern
Ireland farmer in the rating relief
scheme.
Adjournment.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,
"That this House do now adjourn.'
""
Mr. CAMPBELL: I should think the
noble Deputy-Leader of the House might
give some information as to why precisely we are asked to adjourn to-day for
an indeterminate period. This has been
an unusually protracted session. With
intervals we have sat here since June.
In the ordinary course the business should
have been wound up. I cannot see why
we should break up now for an indefinite
period.
It may be you are awaiting
some Bill from the Imperial Parliament,
Adjournment
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
but surely we are not to be constantly
waiting for headlines from Westminster.
We should have some initiative of our
own with regard to our own domestic
legislation.
be
Viscount BANGOR: I think I may
able to throw a little light upon this.
We are waiting for a Measure from the
Imperial Parliament which is the Insurance Bill. I understand that that will
be dealt with in another place on or
about the 18th of February, or it may
be a little bit earlier than that. Anyway it will be about the second week in
February. Shortly after that this House
will reassemble to deal with that Measure.
I think that is all the information I am
able to give.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to express
the hope that the Bill will be circulated
to us as soon as it is in print, even although it has not yet been brought into
this House. I am sure that in the special
circumstances we must put up with the
rather protracted Session, but I cannot
refrain from remarking, as it was necessary to meet to-day and again next
month, that it is a little difficult to understand the extreme pressure to which
before
subjected shortly
we were
Christmas.

until such date as
