SUSPENSION OF STANDING
ORDER.
Moved: That the Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting,
for the purpose of passing the Unemployment Insurance (Amendment) Bill.-
(Viscount Charlemont).
Mr. CAMPBELL: Under ordinary
circumstances I would feel compelled to
oppose the Motion which is very drastic
in type and probably unprecedented in
the history of this Parliament, to carry
through at one sitting all the stages
of a Parliamentary Bill of first-class importance. But as I believe the Bill to be a
good Bill and a necessary one, I do not
think it right or advisable to follow that
course.
J
Private Members' Bill
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
On Question, Motion agreed to.
PRIVATE MEMBERS' BILLS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the attitude
of the Government towards the Pocr
Prisoners Defence Bill this Session, that
they could not in any event accept a private Member's Bill imposing a charge
upon the funds of local authorities, forms
a precedent for the attitude of the Government in future towards any Private Members' Bills, which, like the Bill in question,
impose a charge upon local rates as
subsidiary feature resulting from their
provisions, but not a charge upon the
public revenue?
a
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It would
be impossible to lay down any hard and
fast rule in a matter of this character, as
every Bill must be dealt with purely on
its merits.
It is a well-established and necessary
principle that Bills involving expenditure
from or charges on public funds should be
initiated only by the Government.
The recent Derating Act which imposes
such a heavy charge on the State in connection with the refunds to local authorities in respect of the sums lost by them
through derating has undoubtedly created
a closer link between local expenditure
and State expenditure, as it is obvious
that any increase in local expenditure under existing conditions will involve a corresponding additional charge on
Exchequer funds.
the
Under these circumstances it is not unreasonable that it would be improper that
Private Members should introduce Bills
which will involve additional expenditure
by local authorities, primarily for the
reason already indicated, but secondly, because in the case of a Private Member's
Bill the local authorities cannot have the
opportunity of considering beforehand or
fully the details and re-actions of the Bill
as they would have in the case of a Measure
brought forward by the Government, in
which case, as the hon. and learned Member is aware, steps are usually taken to
consider carefully the effect on local expenditure in consultation with the representatives of local authorities.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Arising out of the
answer of the noble Lord I would like to
point out that a corresponding Bill——
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member
has put a star to the question, and it cannot be debated. I would not have
stopped him if it had been unstarred.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish merely to
ask the noble Lord whether or not a corresponding Bill is in progress at the
present time in the Imperial Parliament
dealing with the same subject matter and
containing precisely identical clauses
necessary to effecting the purpose of the
Act; and whether this particular objection
taken in this House by the Government
was taken at any stage in the progress of
that Measure by the Imperial Government?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have no
information to the effect mentioned in
the hon. and learned. Senator's question,
but I will try to get the information for
him subsequently.
THE DEPRESSION IN THE LINEN
INDUSTRY.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether having regard to the fall in the value of linen goods
exported from Northern Ireland, from
approximately £18,500,000 in the year
1924 to approximately £14,760,000 in the
year 1928, the Government propose to
take any, and if so what, measures to
check the depression in the linen industry.
The ex-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
port values quoted by the hon. and learned
Member are apparently the estimates
cited in the reply given to a previous
question on the subject addressed by him
on 14th January last. If, however, the
hon. and learned Member will refer to
the export values for all the years mentioned in that reply he will observe
that the value of the 1924 exports was
very considerably higher than for any
other year between 1922 and 1928.
other words, 1924 was a relatively good
year for the Northern Irish Linen Industry as compared with the years immediately preceding and following.
In
Although it would be idle to dispute
the fact that the linen industry has experienced its full share of the general
trade depression affecting British industries as a whole since 1920, I do not think
that a comparison of the export values for
the years 1924 and 1928 accurately indi-
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
21 FEBRUARY 1930
cates the trend of conditions in the linen
industry for the whole of the period in
question. The figures for 1928 do, however, show a falling off as compared with
those for any of the earlier years since
1922, but the extent of the decline as
compared with the years 1922, 1923 and
1926 varies between only £460,000 and
£600,000.
Moreover, the estimated value of the
1929 exports, excluding those consigned to
the Irish Free State, was approximately
£15,460,000. Reckoning the value of the
linen exported to the Irish Free State at
the normal average of approximately
£170,000 it will be seen that the total 1929
exports are almost exactly £1,000,000 in
excess of those of 1928. Decline, however, even in a small degree, is a matter
of serious concern, and is one which calls
for the closest attention from both the industry itself and the Government. The
importance of the subject was recognised
right from the time when the Government
of Northern Ireland commenced to function and steps were taken as early as 1922
to help the industry to recover the ground
which had been lost.
The hon. and learned Member is doubtless familiar with the initial steps to which
I have referred, namely, the grant of
financial assistance to the industry with
the object of helping it to secure larger
and cheaper supplies of raw material.
The means adopted towards this end were,
first, to evolve a strain of flax seed which
would be suitable for cultivation in those
parts of the Empire which offered prospects of successful development, and would
at the same time provide a larger proportional yield of fibre. This initial work
was carried out by the Linen Industry
Research Association, and is being pursued with progressively satisfactory results.
up to the present moment. The second
step was the bulking of the seed evolved
by the Association up to the stage at which
it could be distributed to growers for further bulking. By bulking, I should perhaps explain, is meant growing for seed
from year to year and sowing each year
the whole of the seed resulting from the
previous year's crop.
This work has been carried out in Somersetshire, where conditions are exceptionally
favourable to the
the saving of seed.
Quantities of the seed thus bulked have
been distributed throughout the Empire,
particularly in Canada, where a considerable acreage was put down from Somersetshire grown seed. A proportion of the
seed has been made available to Northern
Irish growers, although the fact that seed
saving presents serious difficulties under
Northern Irish conditions has necessarily
restricted the quantity which it has been
possible to allocate to home growers.
The assistance which I have described
was, as the hon. and learned Member will
observe, primarily directed towards the
improvement of supplies of raw material.
As a complement of the Government's
action in this respect there is also the assistance which has been given to help the
industry in an improvement of the fibre
production processes and in the development of its markets for the finished fabric.
Within the past year the Government
has undertaken to contribute proportionately with the Empire Marketing Board
to the financing of a scheme to be conducted by the Linen Industry Research
Association covering not only the evolution of later and improved strains of flax
seed, but also research and experimental
work on a commercial scale in the retting
and scutching of flax. The work under
the scheme is just being begun so that it
is as yet too early to indicate the results
which are likely to be achieved.
The Government's assistance in the development of the industry's markets for
the finished product has taken the form of
grants towards the expenditure incurred
in carrying out the Linen Publicity Campaign in the United States, the Dominions,
and the home market. In considering the
results which have followed from this publicity work it should always be borne in
mind that owing to various causes such
as change in fashions, the advent of new
textile fabrics, and increased foreign
tariffs, the industry has been combating
with very great difficulties in maintaining
its position. These difficulties, however,
have been by no means peculiar to the Northern Irish Linen Industry, but have been
common to all linen-producing countries
throughout the world. Although, therefore, the exports over the period during
which the campaign has been in operation
may not show an increase as compared with
previous figures there is little doubt that,
if the campaign had not been undertaken,
conditions during the last three or four
years would have been even more unsatisfactory than they proved.
In order that the hon. and learned
Member may gauge the extent of the Government's assistance to the industry as indicated by the amount of the grants made
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont j
towards the various purposes which I have
mentioned I would add that the total of
the Government contributions from 1922
to the present date is £75,736.
JURISDICTION OF MAGISTRATES.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is the intention of the Government to introduce
early legislation divesting ordinary magistrates of the remnant of jurisdiction left
to them in courts of petty sessions, and
vesting exclusive jurisdiction in such
courts in salaried magistrates holding office
during the good pleasure of the Government?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not
aware of any intention on the part of the
Government to introduce early legislation
on this subject.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Having regard to
the fact that the noble Leader of the
House stated early in December last that
the question of the revision of summary
jurisdiction in the petty sessions courts was
under the consideration of the Government at that time, I should like to ask if
in the meantime the Government have decided to shelve the matter indefinitely?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I can only
refer the hon. and learned Senator to the
answer I have given him. The question
was whether the Government intended to
introduce early legislation on this subject,
and I have replied in the negative.
BUILDING OF SUBSIDY HOUSES.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether
(a) the Government will consider the
introduction of legislation next Session
for so amending the Housing Acts as to
provide decent subsidy houses at rents.
within reach of the poorer paid working
classes now living under slum conditions;
(b) whether they have had under
consideration the recent report of a
weighty committee of the National
Housing and Town Planning Council to
the effect that a decent family house
cannot with the present subsidy in England be let under 10s a week, inclusive
cost, and that the 7s house needed to rehouse slum dwellers requires a further
subsidy which should be given only to
the large poor family, i.e., should be in
the form of a children's rent allowance ?
With re-
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
gard to the first part of the question, as
the hon. and learned Member is aware, the
policy of the Government with regard to
housing was debated at considerable
length, both in this House and in another
place in December last. Since the inception of the subsidy it has been the aim of
the Government to secure the building of
houses which could be let at a rent which
would be within the means of the poorer
paid working classes. It was for this
reason that, when a house was permitted
of the maximum area of 950 superficial
feet and it was found that the tendency
of builders was to build to this maximum
and to produce a house which was beyond
the means of the working class, the Government asked Parliament to reduce the
maximum superficial area and to increase
the subsidy. There can be no doubt that
this change of policy has secured a large
number of working class houses which are
available for letting purposes.
The Government are convinced that the
only sound method of reducing rents permanently is to secure an adequate supply
of houses. Houses are being completed at
the rate of about 400 per month and this
is a very substantial contribution towards
the solution of the housing problem.
Much still remains to be done, but a proof
that the policy of the Government is having effect is to be found in the fact that
there is clear evidence of a tendency for
rents to fall appreciably, and that the
pressure on the poorer type of the old
houses has been to some extent eased.
view of the satisfactory progress which is
being made at the present time the Government do not think it desirable to make
any change in their housing policy which,
however, must again come up for review
towards the end of the year.
In
With regard to the second part of the
question, the Government are aware of the
report referred to, but they are not satisfied that the remedy suggested is the
propriate one. It is understood that it
has been tried on a small scale in one or
two places without much success.
ap-
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask the noble
Lord whether even one subsidy house has
so far been built in Northern Ireland to be
let at a rent within the reach of a family
earning from £2 to £3 a week?
521 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 21 FEBRUARY 1930

Second Reading
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to know what the hon. and learned
Member would consider a reasonable rent.
Mr. CAMPBELL: A rent within the
reach of a family earning from £2 to £3
a week.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: With
reference to this question, might I point
out that the Belfast Corporation-
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member
can only ask a question.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have no
definite information on that point now,
but I will try to give it to the hon. and
learned Member at a later date.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Unemployment Insurance (Amendment) Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Unemployment Insurance (Amendment) Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I beg
to move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time." In asking that this Bill
should receive a Second Reading I need
hardly remind the House that the similar
Measure recently passed by the Imperial
Houses of Parliament has occasioned a
good deal of discussion. Admittedly it is
largely experimental, and it was finally
decided to limit its operation to a period
of three years, during which time it is
thought that the success or otherwise of
the experiment will be fully established.
As hon. Members are aware, we enjoy
the advantages of a re-insurance agreement
with Great Britain, as a result of which it
is possible to maintain the workers of
Northern Ireland in the same position for
unemployment benefit as the workers
across the Channel, without imposing on
this country a financial burden which alone
it might find hard to bear. No one in this
House, I am sure, would wish to alter that
favourable state of affairs. We all want
our people to have the same standards in
this respect as their brethren on the other
side.
The present Bill, therefore, is
framed on lines exactly parallel with the
Imperial Measure. Like it, our Bill is
largely experimental, and is limited in
operation to three years. It will be observed that the final Clause of the Bill,
Clause 20, sub-clause (2), provides specifically that it shall only be in force until
the 30th June, 1933. Similar limiting
phrases are inserted in appropriate places
in the Bill, that is, Clause 6, Clause 16 (3),
and Clause 17.
The first Clause in the Bill proposes to
lower the age for entering into insurance
to the age of 15 and when the entering
age is similarly lowered in Great Britain.
With this Clause are linked Clauses 14 and
15 of the Bill which will come into force
at the same time as Clause 1. The general
intention of these two latter Clauses is to
encourage young persons to continue their
education; and I need hardly say that in
my own official position I am fully in
sympathy with that principle. Clause 14
provides that when a person who passes the
new minimum insurance age continues at
school he shall be credited with one contribution for each two weeks at school until he
reaches the age of 16, but that these
credited contributions shall not be taken
into account for benefit purposes after he
passes the age of 18. Clause 15 provides
that if the Ministry of Labour are satisfied that a youth who is claiming benefit
should receive further instruction, and
there is a suitable place in the neighbourhood at which he may receive that instruction, they may require him to attend
there; and if, within due course, he fails
to do so, he may be deprived of benefit.
Returning to the numerical sequence of
the Clauses, I come to Clause 2, which
makes increases in the rates of benefit in
certain cases, and allows benefit to be paid
in respect of certain dependents for whom,
formerly, it was not payable. The increases are set out in the White Paper
which Members have no doubt seen, and
I need not refer to them further. As regards the new dependents who are admitted to benefit by Sub-section (2), the
White Paper sets out very fully the persons who will be admitted. In general it
may be said that these are all persons who
in fact were more or less dependent on the
claimant for their living, but for whom,
under the existing law, an allowance could
not be claimed. I may take, for example,
the case of the young brother of a claimant
whose parents are dead. The previous
Acts, as interpreted by the legal authorities, ruled such a child out for pur-
523 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
poses of benefit, although he might in fact
be as dependent on his brother as he would
have been on his father, if the father were
still alive. Then, again, we have the case
of the wife for whom the husband could
not draw dependent's allowance simply because she was making a few in causal
pence
employment, or giving board and lodging,
without any real profit at all, to her
brother or brother-in-law purely as a
matter of family convenience.
This section also proposes to increase the
allowance for an adult dependent from
7s to 9s. It will be understood that a
claimant can draw benefit for only one
adult dependent. This increase, which
goes to the class of persons who are in the
most difficult financial position, will alleviate a great number of cases of hardship.
I now come to a very important part of
the Bill, namely, that contained in Clauses
4, 5, 6, 7 and 8. I may, perhaps, summarise the effect of these Clauses by saying that they take away from the claimant
the onus of proving that he is genuinely
seeking work, and they throw upon the
Ministry of Labour the onus of proving
that he has not satisfied the statutory conditions that entitle him to benefit.
Whether a man is genuinely looking for
work is a matter that can be known with
certainty only by the man himself. When
it comes to be tested by the Department,
they can judge only from his actions, and
of course a mistake can be made.
period of depression in a particular trade
when most of the employers are discharging their hands for lack of work, and when
the few employees who are able to avoid
this are only just able to do so, it seems
hardly reasonable to require workers in
that trade to keep asking for the kind of
employment that everybody knows is not.
really available.
In a
This Bill, therefore, proposes to remove
that condition altogether, and to substitute for it a provision that a claimant
shall be disqualified if the Department can
show that he has refused or neglected to
look for a definite situation that was suitable to him and was vacant or about to
become vacant; or that he failed to carry
out written directions given him by an
officer of the Employment Exchange with
a view to assisting him to find work; and
there are provisions for deciding whether
an employment is suitable in a particular
case or whether the directions given the
claimant were reasonable. In general, the
claimant will get his benefit unless the
Court of Referees decides that he shall not
get it, and their judgment, if disputed, is
upheld by the umpire on appeal.
Passing over some minor Clauses, I come
to the last vital part of the Bill-Clause
14. The Blanesburgh Act, as it is called,
was based on the theory that in normal
circumstances the claimant should not get
benefit unless he had 30 contributions in
the two years preceding the date of his
claim.
Mr. LESLIE: May I interrupt the
noble Lord for a moment? He referred
us to Clause 14.
I beg
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
your pardon. It is Clause 16. Owing,
however, to the heavy unemployment tha
current, it was provided that for a year
from the date of that Act a person might
get benefit, notwithstanding that he had
not the 30 contributions, if he could satisfy
certain alternative conditions which
brief were (1) that he had eight contributions in the last two years or 30 at any
time, (2) that he was normally employed
in insurable employment, and (3) that he
had a reasonable period of employment in
the last two years.
This last condition has
given rise to such difficulty in interpretation and application, that it is now proposed to repeal it, leaving the other two
standing.
As the unemployment situation had
shown no very marked signs of improvement at the end of the original period of
12 months, that period was extended for
a further 12 months, and the present Bill
proposes to extend it for yet a third 12
months. These two extensions have
thrown upon the Unemployment Fund a
burden which it was not calculated to bear,
and in the present Bill it is proposed,
therefore, to make them a charge upon the
Exchequer.
I have now, I believe, outlined the main
features of this Bill. The remaining
points are not such as to call for any special
comment. This Measure of ours is essential to keep the workers in Northern Ireland on the same level as regards unemployment benefit as the people of Great
Britain. I should like to say that we have
the advantage of the presence of the
Minister of Labour, who will reply to the
debate and take the Bill through the Committee stage. I am sure that that arrangement will be agreeable to Members of
the House. I beg to move.
525 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 21 FEBRUARY 1930

Second Reading
Mr. CAMPBELL: When one sees this
Bill introduced by the Northern Government one is inclined to say:
Do I sleep, do I dream,
Do I wonder and doubt;
Are things what they seem,
Or is visions about?
It
in
I feel in my heart that the Northern Government do not like this Bill, but they
must take it or take the consequences.
is certainly an amazing thing to find the
old Conservative party of Ulster being led
into these strange Socialistic bypaths by
the extreme Labour party in England. I
hope the journey will not end in Moscow.
The more Ministers seem to be with Mr.
George Lansbury the happier they all seem
to be. I noticed quite recently that Mr.
Churchill said that Bills of this class were
vast soup
converting England into a
kitchen. Unemployment insurance
Northern Ireland cost the British Treasury
something over £300,000 last year. That
was a huge grant in aid, but I believe
that it will be a larger amount this year
under this Bill. Nobody, of course, in
our part of the world worries very much
about a raid on the British Treasury. Dr.
Johnson on one occasion, when the Union
with England was mooted-and this ought
to appeal to the Leader of the House,
because one of his forebears was intimate
with Dr. Johnson-said to an Irishman:
"Do not make a union with us, sir; we
should unite with you only to rob you."
Northern Ireland has in this matter given
that saying a twist the other way about.
In the matter of payments under the
Equalisation Scheme and under the Unemployment Insurance Agreements between
Northern Ireland and Great Britain,
Northern Ireland has decidedly got the
best part of the bargain.
Looking at the Bill itself I do not see
why it should be limited to three years.
It is put on the Statute Book as a permanent Measure for all practical purposes. The Labour Government, whose
footsteps we are only following, intended
it to be a permanent Measure.
As it left
the British House of Commons it was a
permanent Measure, but the House of
Lords, I believe, altered it by inserting
a period of one year. That was done to
show their tender affection for it and how
dearly they longed for it. The Labour
Government, under strong protest, compromised with a three years' limit. I
submit that that limit is quite illogical,
and ought not to stand. The Bill
is now on the Imperial Statute Book and
no Government will ever dare to interfere
with the duration of it. If it is a wise
Bill the Government should treat it as a
wise and necessary Bill. Why should we
put in a three years' probation? I do not
see any particular reason for it.
It may
be that the real reason is that the Government have no affection for the principle
of the Bill itself and want to make the life
of the Measure as precarious as the life
of a Bill can be.
One reason that appeals to me for supporting this Bill is that it holds out a
promise of relief for the out-of-work
classes. It likely is the only relief they
Unemwill get from the Government.
ployment is increasing. Hardships are
increasing among the poorer classes. The
very party who are bringing in this Bill
claim Protection as a panacea for curing
unemployment. I noticed that the distinguished leader of the Unionist party
when in Belfast the other day was told
that the day after he again becomes Prime
Minister of Great Britain the Belfast linen
merchant will be sitting on his doorstep
begging like Lazarus for Protection. I
would say begging in vain. I wonder will
the Belfast shipbuilder go to the Prime
Minister, seeing that Great Britain is
building more ships under Free Trade than
all the rest of the world. It would be a
wonderful picture for Sir John Lavery to
paint. I am glad to support this Bill
because it brings some ray of light and
comfort into the homes of many of the
poorer classes.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems desirable that
there should be some examination of this
Bill. Some examination more than perfunctory seems to be called for. The
examination may have to be perfunctory
partly because the time is short, and I may
digress for a minute to say that I should
like to be informed, a little later, on why
this time is so short. What is the mystic
attraction of the 13th March? We were
not told when we passed the Motion for
expediting the Bill through its stages in
this House. We admit the time is short,
and we must admit, too, that the Bill is
not one very easy of comprehension, particularly by those people who have no personal experience of the working of similar
Measures. I imagine that if an example
of legislation by reference carried to a fine
art was desired the Bill before us would
furnish that example, but I make no complaint of that in this instance because
it is manifestly impossible to codify a
Measure which is of temporary duration;
527 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
in fact the whole unemployment code,
which is partly our production and partly
and originally the production of Great
Britain, has been altered so frequently that
it would be almost impossible to keep in
step with it by any other method than
that which has been adopted.
Although the Bill may be difficult, I do
not admit it is impossible to follow. I do
say it requires a certain amount of study,
and I am much afraid that that study will
not be given to it outside the walls of this
House. But there is another reason why
the Bill ought to be examined, and that
is this it is a far more important reason
than the other-if this Bill is going to
become an Act it is going to be an Act of
Parliament, it is not going to be an Act of
the Government, whether the Government
of Northern Ireland or anywhere else. The
responsibility for the Bill will rest with the
Parliament of Northern Ireland. and the
Parliament of Northern Ireland and the
Government of Northern Ireland are not
the same thing. That point cannot be too
often stressed.
it seems to be assumed that the Government can do this or that. Very often they
can, but only because they have Parliament behind them. I repeat that this
Bill will be a Measure of Parliament for
which neither Houses of Parliament nor
any Member of either House can escape
full responsibility.
Even in Parliament itself
There has been very heavy criticism outside Parliament of the proposals in this
Bill, and of the announced determination
of the Government to introduce it into
Parliament. It has even been suggested
that the Chamber of first instance where
it was originally introduced was at the
moment composed of Members who were
elected for an entirely different purpose
than the production or
or adoption of
Socialistic measures. I have tried to read
this Bill myself, and it is not a Socialistic
measure. Quite definitely I say that.
Socialism is one thing, but it seems to me
that a recognition of the plain fact that
unemployment insurance is fundamentally
a scheme of contributory insurance puts
the matter beyond the chance of being reproached as Socialism. The fact that it
has been necessary to extend the periods
of benefits strictly covered by insurance
seems to me to have nothing to do with the
main point any more than the fact that
what I would call personal arrears accumulating during periods of State assistance
are to be regarded necessarily as free gifts.
It is true that the State has acted on more
than one occasion as fairy godmother and
made good deficiencies, and perhaps it may
have to do so again, but even so that is not
a ground for charging the measure with
Socialism.
As I indicated, I do not think the criticism of this Bill is always put forward
by those who take the trouble to inform
themselves of its details, and as a particular instance I would mention the criticism
levelled against the first Clause of the Bill,
that is the proposal that the minimum age
for entry into insurance under the principal Act shall, instead of being
the age of 16, be the age of
15. That Clause has been represented as a free gift of benefit to every
boy and girl in Northern Ireland. I
really think I am correct in saying that
that is what has been stated. It seems
scarcely necessary to point out that the
Clause does not do that at all. A great
Ideal of what will be done under that
Clause and Clauses 14 and 15 is, I understand, of a purely bookkeeping character.
I think it has to be recognised that the
age for entry into insurance is one thing,
and the actual entry into insurance is
quite another; that is to say, a boy or girl
does not enter into insurance merely because he or she attains the age of 15 years.
The right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Andrews)
will correct me if I am wrong. That is
how I understand the Bill.
The particular provision may require a
little discussion at a later stage in conjunction with Clauses 14 and 15, but from the
point of view of the principle that is as far
as it goes. I think it is a most mischievous
thing to misrepresent that important
Clause in that way, and I think it is
perhaps almost as mischievous to cast insinuations wildly about the country that
the Parliament in this country will not
accept a Measure merely because it has
been introduced by another Parliament
with which the majority in Northern
Ireland do not happen to be in sympathy.
It seems to me to make an assertion of
that kind argues a complete misunderstanding of the constitutional position.
But there is another kind of criticism,
and that is that too much is made of the
binding character of the agreement between Great Britain and Northern Ireland. That is the agreement which the
noble Viscount, I think, rather happily
referred to as the Re-insurance Agree-
I am going to refresh the memory
of the House by reading the fifth Clause
ment.
529 Unemployment Insurance Bill- 21 FEBRUARY 1930

Second Reading
of that agreement, which was issued as
an appendix to a memorandum in 1926
and will also be found as an appendix to
the Insurance Acts of the proper year.
That Clause runs as follows:-
If at any time the classes of persons
insured, the rates of contribution or
the rates or conditions of benefit differ
in the two countries, there shall be
made such adjustments in the amount
of the equalisation payment and the
contribution from one Exchequer to the
other as the Joint Exchequer Board,
after consulting the Government
actuary, consider just.
I say that this leaves it perfectly open for
this Parliament to introduce changes if it
likes, and renders it possible for an adjustment to be made. An adjustment is one
thing and a complete tearing up or cancelling of the Agreement is another.
Added to the same White Paper was a
letter which passed between our Minister
of Finance and Mr. Winston Churchill,
who was at that time Chancellor of the
Exchequer, and a similar letter was sent
in 1929 when the agreement was extended
from its limitation and made for all practical purposes indefinite. Two things are
stated in the letter to which I am about
to refer. There is the acceptance on our
part of a limitation of £1,000,000 in any
one year as the extent of the Imperial
contribution to our fund, and there is also
the assurance that our Minister of Labour
will continue to follow any Regulations
adopted by the Imperial Minister of
Labour. This was accepted by our Minister of Finance in relation to the agreement which had just been entered into.
It seems to me to follow from that that
if we are under an obligation to follow
the Regulations we must first make
similar Regulations to those made in
Great Britain and obtain power to do so
through our own Parliament.
I revert to the criticism that the electors of Northern Ireland recently elected
Parliament to do one thing, or, rather,
not to do another thing. I do not know
with what mental reservations the electors of Northern Ireland recently voted
for the party which is in support of the
Government now in office, but I do know
that they did record a determination
through their votes to support what is
commonly known as the "step by step"
policy. Whether they meant to limit
themselves to step by step on the level
Dr upward direction and meant to protect
themselves against the possibility of the
step by step becoming roll by roll in descending finances I do not know. It seems
to me that is rather for those who sit in
another place than for us.
I do not deny that there are some things
in the Bill that I do not myself like, but
there are things in it that I do like, and
I am not going to make what I must admit
are comparatively petty dislikes an occasion for attacking the whole policy of the
Imperial Government, which is also our
own policy as regards dealing with unemployment questions and unemployment
insurance. I do entertain some misgivings
in the case of young persons' insurance.
I do not altogether like imposing a further
burden upon the employer. Allowing
boys to be qualified to enter into insurance at fifteen means, I presume, that the
employer of those boys will have to pay
I can
the unemployment contribution.
see no escape from that.
I rather dislike adding any further
burdens on employers. We have to remember that there is not only unemployment insurance but there is health insurance, although, of course, it is just the
sort of charge I would expect to be made
outside that health insurance and unemployment insurance have to be added together in the case of a boy of fifteen.
Unless my memory is quite wrong they
have not, because boys of fifteen are not
insured under the Health Acts, but there
are the health burdens in the case of the
other workers, and in 1936 there is a chance
of these burdens being increased. I hope
they will not be increased. Beyond that,
is it quite fair to this young person that
the insurance should be credited to him
when it is the fund that makes the profit
out of it? But I do not know if that is
of great importance. I am rather inclined to think it is so much a bookkeeping transaction that it loses any importance that at first sight it appears to
have.
I pass from that to the extra cost which
is entailed. Undoubtedly there will be
extra cost entailed. The White Paper
gives the main item as "Changes in conditions for receipt of benefit and in
methods of administration (Cl. 4, 8, 9,
and 16 (3)), £360,000." That is a large
sum of money, but I will come to that
again.
There is the question of the alteration
in the "genuinely seeking work" condition. I understand from the report of a
speech made by the right hon. Gentleman
the Minister of Labour in another place
531 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Lesne]
that he himself looks forward to the working of the new provisions, I will not say
with fear or trepidation, but at all events
with some slight hesitation, and that there
is some anticipation that the road may be
rather more rocky than he would wish.
I rather share the anticipations, but I do
not think it will serve any useful purpose
to indicate possible causes of difficulty,
particularly, perhaps, in making the proposed arrangements between the Exchanges and the employers. I think it is
better to leave those to be decided by experience.
I would like to issue one little warning.
There are two sorts of employments.
There is the single employment where the
employer employs one man, and there is
the other and more important class of
employment where people are employed in
batches. The difficulties of the one are
not the same as the difficulties of the
other, but the difficulties of the smaller
seem to be very much more likely to be
heard of than the other, and quite possibly a false sense of proportion might
easily be introduced. Before I leave the
genuinely seeking work Clause I want to
ask the Minister to explain whether I am
correct in assuming that he was not
wholly correctly, or perhaps not completely, reported.
As I read the report of his speech in
the newspaper it did seem to me that his
remarks about the pendulum being allowed to swing too far against the worker
was probably not what he meant to convey, because it did seem to convey something of a reproach on those who had been
working these courts of arbitration of one
kind or another which are concerned in
these matters. With such information as
I possess on the subject, which is not very
extensive, I can say that I have not heard
reports in my district suggesting for a
moment that the gentlemen occupying the
Judicial position of chairmen of the tribunal ever in any circumstances allow the
pendulum to be unfairly weighted as
against the worker. Rather on the contrary the attempt was made to see if the
applicant could be included within the
scope of the benefit. I should like to be
assured that there is no intention to
complain of the manner in which that
work was usually performed.
One's misgivings boil down almost entirely to the question of expense, because
we all hope that unemployment will decrease and that benefit, not strictly covered
by insurance, will not extend for ever.
As regards the expenses we have, I am
afraid, to reconcile ourselves to the fact
that we have got to pay somehow or other
by sharing in extra taxation which may
be imposed. The arrangement with regard to the maintenance of parity between the two funds is all very well, but
the money put up to ensure the maintenance of our fund must be provided by the
Chancellor of the Exchequer.
But against that we must remember
that thanks to the House of Lords-I look
at it rather differently from the learned
counsel (Mr. Campbell)—this Bill is only
to endure for three years. I know, and
indeed the hon. and learned Senator indicated as much, it is remarkably difficult
to put the clock back in a matter of this
kind. There is an old saying that it is
very easy to roll down a slope, and that
the forces of destruction at the bottom
are waiting for you night and day, but
that the real task is to get back again to
the upper air and the sounder position.
I think I am right in saying that since
insurance was introduced benefits and so
forth have not invariably been moved
upwards. I think there have been readjustments downards. There is no reason
why that should not, if circumstances
admit it, be done again.
I do not think that the whole Bill can
be described as a leap in the dark. To
some extent it is perhaps a leap in the
twilight, but twilight is usually after a very
short period of darkness followed by the
dawn, and I hope that will happen in this
Practically the House will have to
weigh the advantages and disadvantages,
the one against the other, and assume the
responsibility of rejecting the Bill or of
accepting it with a good will, or, if it prefers, without it. I think we shall, most
of us, be prepared to accept.
case.
We shall have the courage of our convictions, and those of us who are of Conservative tendencies will undoubtedly be
attacked for passing this Bill. We shall
have to face those attacks. We shall have
to have the courage of our convictions, and
we shall have to say that we took the decision to accept the Bill neither recklessly
nor wantonly, but because, having regard
to all the circumstances of the case, we
thought the best interests of the Province
would be served by our acceptance of the
Measure. In that spirit I myself shall
certainly not offer any opposition to a Bill,
some of which, I admit, I would prefer
had remained unenacted.
533 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

21 FEBRUARY 1930
Second Reading
a
Mr. DORMAN: The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) was quite right in
stating that this is not Socialistic
measure. As I understand Socialism, it
makes a demand that the abstract right to
live which we are guaranteed under the
Constitution shall be transformed into the
concrete right of access to the means of
life. To enjoy an abstraction may be all
very well if it is not accompanied with
the right of access to the means of life,
and it is simply because under our Constitution that the right of life has not been
of
accompanied with the right
to the
means
access
of life, that legislation, such as the Unemployment Insurance Acts, has been placed upon the
Statute Book. If those who live by the
exercise of their labour power from day
to day are excluded under the Constitution from access to the means of life, they
have no alternative but to suffer the
pangs
of hunger and to die. The State must
necessarily see to it that that does not
take place, and it is because of the widespread unemployment that exists in the
post-war period that extensions of these
various Acts have had to be placed upon
the Statute Book.
I welcome this Measure as a step
toward Socialism, because it is a recognition of the responsibility of the Government towards the individual citizen, that
he shall be afforded an opportunity of
working; and if he has not the privilege
of working extended to him by those who
own and control the only means whereby
he can work, then the State must provide
some subsistence for him. I do not think
that the Government recognise the circumstances that are prevailing in the area
of Northern Ireland amongst those who
are on the unemployed list.
There are
numerous individuals unemployed to-day
who, after they pay the rent of their
dwellings and provide three meals a day.
for those who are depending upon them,
have just one penny left for each meal.
The allowance in Northern Ireland is completely insufficient to meet the lowest
standard of comfort on which one could
expect an unemployed man to live. It
will amaze hon. Members when I tell
them that there are unemployed men in
Belfast who are paying 30 and 40 per
cent. of their unemployment allowance
for shelter for themselves and their offspring.
Unemployed men who rallied to the call
of their country when their country was
in need, who were told that a grateful
country would never forget them, who
were assured that they would come back
to a country fit for herces to live in, are
now situated in such circumstances that
they are paying one-third and practically
one-half of their unemployment allowance
for mere shelter for themselves and their
families. I have just interviewed one
man who is paying 8s. a week rent for a
room 9ft. high, by 8ft. 10in., by 6ft. 4in.
-a hero who went and did his bit. The
family of five individuals depending upon
that hero are sleeping in that apartment
in Belfast. If that man committed a
crime and was incarcerated in the jail he
would be provided with a minimum space
of 13ft. by 9ft. by 7ft. In other words,
that hero is compelled, because of poverty,
to live in a room that has 200 cubic feet
space less than would be afforded to
him by law if he became a criminal. I
mention this so that the Government may
arouse themselves, and try to ascertain
what are the conditions. The Leader of
the House referred to the fact that rents
have been reduced. I think that when
the unemployment allowance is considered
there should be some right and power of
differentiation, so that the unemployed
individuals should have at least a minimum standard for food allowance.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon.
Member will soon get on to the Bill itself.
He has taken a long time to deal with matters that have very little to do with the
Bill under discussion.
Mr. DORMAN: I am sorry. There
are certain allowances made under this
Bill, and my desire is to illustrate the insufficiency of the unemployment benefit.
that is afforded to the unemployed in this
area. But perhaps I have said enough on
that point. I welcome the Bill for the one
great principal reason that it removes the
stigma from many honest, upright men of
not genuinely seeking work. I also welcome
the Bill because it has brought in adult
dependents who were shut out under the
previous Acis from receiving any unemployment benefit. I think, on the whole,
the Measure goes as far as the Government
across the water could go, and as far as
the Government here could go, until such
time as the people have their attention
concentrated upon things that matter.
The thing that matters, after all, is the
standard of comfort that prevails in the
homes of the masses of the people. With
these few remarks I welcome this Bill, and
shall give it my support.
535 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: 1 am
glad that the Government have adopted
the same attitude towards the legislation
of the Socialist Government as they did
to the legislation of the Unionist Government. I suppose one reason for my own
attitude is that I had the impudence to
take the authority upon myself to assure
an Under Secretary that this Bill would
be introduced in Northern Ireland and
carried through. I am very glad, therefore, that this Bill has been brought forward. Apart from that, I think it is
absolutely necessary that this insurance
scheme should be conducted on exactly
the same lines as the Imperial Government's scheme. We have a free passage
of workers from Northern Ireland to Scotland and England. Men are continuously
moving about seeking work, and it would
be an unfortunate thing if there were any
differences in the benefits payable under
a schemę like this.
It is really very necessary that we should
move on parallel lines with Great Britain
in this matter. Another thing is that, no
doubt, many anomalies are abolished, and
several very unfair provisions of the old
Acts have been removed, for instance, in regard to dependents' benefits. The old provisions operated very hardly in some cases,
such as the women who, as was stated by
the noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
sought to increase the income of the household by keeping a relative as a boarder.
In those cases the poor women, on many
occasions, did not know it was necessary
to report the fact, and when it became
known there was considerable difficulty in
calling upon women to refund the benefit
which their husbands had drawn. It is
well to see that hardship removed.
The great and the important change in
the Bill affects the provision regarding
those genuinely seeking work. No matter
what one may say or think, it was impossible for a court of referees to decide
whether a man or a woman was or was not
genuinely seeking work. No matter how
wise the chairman of the court may have
been, he could not possibly decide definitely and honestly whether a person was
genuinely seeking work or not. I found
that persons attending those courts very
often told of the firms they had visited in
search of work, and it was questionable
if they had been out of their homes on
the day named. I noticed some time ago
that the Ministry of Labour had prosecuted one or two persons who had asserted
that they had made application for work
to firms, which in fact they had never attended. There were many cases like that.
Then we found other persons who were
strictly honest regarding the firms they
had called upon, and because their report
was not attractive enough for the court
of referees they were rejected. I am very
pleased, therefore, to see the abolition of
that test. At the same time, I must admit that something should be done to see
that a person who is not genuinely seeking work, and who has no desire for work,
should not receive benefit. At first the
insurance scheme was drawn up on trade
union rules. Even trade unions, before
the introduction of the insurance scheme,
did not pay a workman benefit if they
found he was not making an effort to obtain employment.
I remember very well that if a man was
too long on the donation benefit he was
presented with a travelling card and he
had to leave the district in which he had
been drawing the benefit, say for eight,
ten or twelve weeks. He had to go away
-it did not matter where he went-to
seek employment. Even the trade unions
never thought of paying benefit to a man
unless he was making an effort to obtain employment, and it is rather a singular thing
that an insured person should be allowed
to draw benefit without giving some proof
that he was anxious for work. Up to the
present, so far as the insurance scheme is
concerned, I do not know any way of being
able to decide whether or not a person was
genuinely seeking work. It is well that
the benefit should be paid, and that the
onus should be left on the Ministry of
Labour either to provide work, to send
him to a job, or to find out if he has refused work. By that means they will
have an opportunity of refusing him benefit.
I
As it is, I do say that it was impossible
to carry out the test, and in many
cases innocent persons suffered. It is just
as well to see that provision removed.
do not know that there is anything further in the Bill that is likely to create
controversy. Undoubtedly, the portion
to which I have referred has aroused a certain amount of criticism on the part of
employers and other classes in Northern
Ireland, but I think when they consider
it thoroughly they will admit that it is just
as well to remove the provision to which
I am referring. I, for one, believe it will
be much easier for the Ministry of Labour
to administer the Act than it was under
21 FEBRUARY 1930
537 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

Second Reading
the old conditions, and I certainly intend
to support the Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I should also like to
support the Second Reading of this Bill.
I agree with Senator Joseph Cunningham
that it was necessary to make some amendments in the previous Act. I came here
to-day with my mind more or less prejudiced against Clause 1, but from a conversation I have had with one who knows,
previous to the assembling of this House,
and from what I have learned from the
explanation of the Bill given by the noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, I am quite
satisfied now that Clause 1 is a very suitable Clause. I had the impression that
young people on leaving school were entitled to benefit, but I find now, from the
explanation given, that they have got to
attend school for a certain period, and I
also think they have to find employment
for some weeks previous to drawing benefit. I do not know whether that is right
or not, but I have that impression, and,
therefore, I am satisfied that this Clause
will be of great benefit to our boys and
girls. My reason for having the opinion
of this Clause which I previously held was
that so many employers employed boys of
fourteen years of age. They had their
services for two years and at the end of
two years, when they became insurable,
these boys were dispensed with, put upon
the streets, and their parents had to support them. I am very pleased that Clause
1 of this Bill will now get the parents over
that trouble, and that a boy will have
something when he reaches 16 years of age.
""
With regard to Clause 4, I had an idea
that if the provision about genuinely seeking work had been modified to some extent
it might have done just as well and have
been just as useful as the words "suitable
employment.' I remember in this House
some time ago the late Senator McDowell
asking for a definition of genuinely seeking work. Of course he did not get one,
and, to-day, I would like a definition of
suitable employment. Who is to decide.
what is suitable employment? There was
nobody to decide what genuinely seeking
work meant, and the result was that the
regulations which had been drawn up
caused great hardship to a great many
working men and women. It was like a
doctor providing one class of medicine for
all classes of diseases. No matter what
excuse you made-if you made an excuse
-and no matter what truth you told it
was all the same. I remember meeting a
you
young man a few weeks ago and asking
him if he had ever been to the Labour Exchange, and he said he had." Well, now,"
I said, "How do you go on when have
an interview?" "To tell you the truth,"
he said, "I never told a lie till I went to
the Labour Exchange, but since I have
gone to the Labour Exchange I have told
bagfuls of lies, because there is no use in
telling the truth.” This new Clause will
remove that evil; and I hope it will be
for the good of our working people.
If any person thinks that this Amendment to the Act is going to improve
matters I am afraid they are very far mistaken. It is not likely that Government
officials are going to run about the country
and through the towns and villages finding
work for the unemployed. You never
find Government officials running. They
are never in a hurry. And another thing
is that, supposing a Government official
did run about the country and find work
for all those who are unemployed to-day,
it would mean that he himself would be
put upon his notice and his services dispensed with, and, therefore, he would be
very foolish to try to find employment for
everybody. From what I have heard today from the noble Leader of the House I
think the Government officials will be able
to do a little, but there should also be
some responsibility placed upon the working man himself, and he should be asked
to do a little in the way of seeking employment. If the regulations under the
old Act had been altered or amended in
some way it might have worked just as
well as this "suitable employment" provision. I do not know whether it will be
a success or not. I hope it will.
ever, I have pleasure in supporting the
Second Reading of the Bill and I will vote
for it.
How-
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would just like
to say in a few words that I am very glad
to see the defects of Clause 4 remedied
under this Bill. During four years' experience in another place I had opportunities of seeing the very unsatisfactory,
and at times the unfair and unjust
operation of the Clause in the prior Bill.
It was subjected, and rightly so, in many
cases to the most cynical criticism.
have no doubt that if the present Bill is
fairly and sympathetically administered
it will remedy the injustice that existed
under the former Clause.
I
Notwithstanding what Senator Leslie
has stated about this not being Socialist
539 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. McAllister]
legislation, I have not the slightest hesitation in describing it as advanced Socialist
legislation. It is legislation introduced
by a Socialist Government, and, whatever
this scheme may have been in its earlier
stages, it has now undoubtedly arrived at
the stage when it is Socialist legislation.
It is not in the ordinary sense a contributory insurance scheme. It could only
exist for a few weeks on the Unemployment Insurance Fund. It can only be described to-day by what is an unhappy and
disagreeable term to apply. It has now
simply become a dole, and, therefore, I
say that at this stage we have the Government of Northern Ireland, with all their
avowed Conservative principles, adopting,
swallowing, and passing an advanced
Socialist Measure.
He
The hon. Senator (Mr. Barclay), who
sits behind me, seems more or less to foresee a difficulty about how the Government
officials will carry out this scheme. I have
no doubt, unless the officials get a private
intimation, if they are put in an independent position they will justly, fairly,
and equitably carry out this scheme.
has stated that you do not often find
Government officials running. All I can
say, from my experience of the officials
whom I have met in Northern Ireland, is
that they have shown anxiety to meet all
reasonable requests I have put before
them as expeditiously, as satisfactorily,
and with as much despatch as possible.
What the hon. Senator was probably
thinking of was the Government themselves. If the officials do not run we have
certainly most indisputable and uncontradictable evidence here of members of
the Government running from their
avowed convictions. I do not intend to
take up the time of the House any longer,
but will say in conclusion that I am delighted to see the advance and improvement in this particular Bill compared
with its predecessors.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise to give my
sincere support to the Bill and to say how
pleased I am that the Government have
provided that the people of Northern
Ireland may receive the same benefits as
those in Great Britain. It would have
been more satisfactory if the Socialist
Government had provided that work for
all about which we heard so much some
time ago, especially during last May. The
unemployment barometer has gone higher
and higher still. Instead of unemployment insurance we would have gladly welcomed work. I am glad to see that that
much discussed and well-known Clause
about not genuinely seeking work has been
removed. I would not say that it is a
really satisfactory solution of the difficulty
to remove it. As Senator Barclay has
said, I think there should be some test of
whether a man or a woman, or a boy or
a girl, is really seeking work. For some
time I have had the experience of attending Courts of Referees almost daily, and
I must say that the chairman and those
who sat with him, and also the officials
always gave consideration and fair treatment to the applicants. In some cases I
was disappointed because I really thought
that those claims should have been allowed, but I must say that every fair consideration is given in those Courts if a
decent case is put forward. Employment, as I have said, would have been
more acceptable than the removal of this
Clause, for the insertion of which in an
Unemployment Bill the Socialist Government were entirely responsible. Now
they have swallowed that Clause.
Mr. DORMAN: No.
Mr. QUIGLEY: They have swallowed
that Clause because they have not been
able to provide the necessary employment
which they promised. I have great
pleasure indeed in supporting the Bill.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): It is always a great pleasure
to me to come to this honourable House
because I am invariably treated with so
much kindness and consideration, and I
would like to say at the very beginning
of the few remarks which I feel it necessary that I should make to-day that the
criticism of this Bill, whether advanced
by my political friends or my political opponents, has been very reasonable and very
fair. The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell), who began the debate, spoke
about a number of matters of very considerable interest. Hon. Senators, no
doubt, are aware that the reason why the
G.S.W. condition-the fourth statutory
condition-has been removed is because it
was really a psychological test, and a
psychological test is very difficult to apply.
But the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) began by applying a psychological test to the Government. He
said that in his opinion the Government
were really opposed to this Measure and
were bringing it forward simply because
Unemployment Insurance Bill— 21 FEBRUARY 1930
Second Reading
they could not do otherwise. Obviously
it was impossible for the hon. and learned
Senator to know what was in the minds
of the Government just as sometimes it
was very difficult for the Courts of Referees
to know what was in the minds of the
claimants.
I would point out to my hon. Friend
that the Government feel that they are
doing the right thing in introducing this
Measure. It really has not worried the
Government to the smallest extent what
Government introduced the Bill at Westminster or who was responsible for it. It
is what is in the Bill itself that we have
got to consider, and I have not the
smallest hesitation in saying that in all
its Clauses it is an advance along the line
of progress and reasonableness on its predecessors.
But the hon. Senator went on to ask
why it had been limited to three years.
I want to point out that it is getting a
longer lease of life than any of its predecessors. Since 1911 we have had no
fewer than 22 Measures on unemployment
insurance, and I myself created a record
by being responsible for no fewer than 15
in the last eight years. So roughly speaking the length of time that this Measure's
predecessors have endured is about six
months. I do not think it is unreasonable
that the time of this Measure shall be
limited to three years. In fact, I would
be very much surprised if it remains on
the Statute Book, no matter which
Government is in power, for that length
of time.
In the next place I would like to congratulate the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Leslie), who spoke secondly in the
debate on the very interesting and able
speech which he made. He asked me a
few questions, and one I feel in particular
was of some importance especially to this
House. He asked me what was the hurry
about getting this Bill on the Statute
Book, considering the fact that its operations do not begin until the 13th of March.
There is, however, one Clause in the Bill
which gives the right to the Ministry of
Labour of doing away with this fourth
statutory condition for the receipt of
benefit and establishing new conditions
instead. Of course that is the most important provision in the whole Bill. It
is true that the Imperial Ministry of
Labour have not issued any regulations
yet dealing with the matter. But I am
most anxious to be in a position to issue
regulations here at the same time that
they are issued in Great Britain, so as to
carry out the Government's considered
policy of keeping in step with Great
Britain not only as regards legislation, but
also as regards administration.
The hon. Senator mentioned the fact
that, after all, the Governments of the
day do not decide these matters. It is
Parliament. That is quite true. Governments propose, but Parliaments decide. As
regards the White Paper which contained
the Agreement between the Northern
Government and the Imperial Government
-not the present Imperial Government,
but its predecessors which really is called
the Re-insurance Agreement and which, I
think, will probably be called the charter
of the working people here ensuring for all
time, I hope, that whilst this Government
cannot do better for the working people
than is done for their fellow-countrymen
in the rest of the United Kingdom, at any
rate the Government of the day will do
equally well as regards these social services.
An interesting point was raised by the
hon. and learned Senator when he said
that the Agreement did not make it absolutely necessary for us to follow the ups
and downs that occurred at Westminster
in this connection. Well, I take a somewhat different view because although it is
true that one of the provisions of that
agreement would give us power to vary
our conditions, in practice, in my opinion,
it is not possible, and I believe that the
general sense of opinion, not only in our
House of Commons but also in this House
is that we should not do so. Besides the
hon. Member himself read out the fact
that as regards regulations we were bound
to keep in step with Great Britain. It
would be somewhat difficult to have inequalities as regards provisions and
equality as regards regulations.
Courts of referees were referred to by
the hon. Senator, and he said he hoped
that no remarks of mine could be construed to be a reflection on those courts.
I assure him that I have over and over again
with such strength as I possess, defended
those courts in the other House and felt
I was perfectly entitled to do so. Beyond
a shadow of doubt the courts desire to do
justice to every claimant But when they
had to apply a psychological test it was
perfectly obvious that sometimes the
pendulum did swing, unintentionally no
doubt, against the claimant, and claimants
were deprived of benefit which they would
543 Unemployment Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Andrews]
have received if the courts had known
the truth, the whole truth, and nothing
but the truth as regards the claimants.
I think the hon. Senator finished his
speech by saying that, no doubt, we Conservatives would be attacked for introducing this Measure. Of course we will be
attacked. Personally speaking, for eight
years I have known what it was to be
attacked from at least two sides at the
same time. But you grow accustomed to
these attacks, and you soon arrive at a
stage when you do not mind, provided you
are doing the right thing, and therefore I
am perfectly happy in connection with this
matter.
Senator Dorman made a brief speech.
I am sorry it was not longer, because I
thought he was going to be very interesting in the beginning when he began to
give a treatise on Socialism. I am quite
satisfied that he is a great idealist. I
rather like idealists. But when he tells
us that all the evils from which we are
suffering are due to the fact that we have
not adopted Socialism I entirely part
company with him. If he could point to
Russia and say, there is a great socialist
State that has produced a happy land for
everybody to live in then we might begin
to think.
But we all know that Russia is
the most coerced country in the world at
the present time. The people of Russia
are suffering the greatest misery endured
by any people in the world. An attempt
is being made to banish religion from the
country. Therefore, I think we who are
opposed to Socialism are perfectly
justified in saying there is a country which
we will not follow, while with Great
Britain we are perfectly prepared to keep
in step. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
I now come to Senator Barclay's speech
in which he said there might be as much
difficulty in defining what suitable employment was as there was in defining
whether a man was genuinely seeking
work or not. Fortunately for me the
decision is not going to rest in my hands.
In the first place if the claimant feels
that he is being asked by the exchanges
to accept employment which is not suitable he is to have the right of appeal to
the courts, and the courts will judge
whether the employment is suitable or
not. I think the hon. Senator will
admit that it will be a much easier thing
for the courts to decide, because they
will not have to attempt to get into the
own
man's mind in order to give a decision.
They had to get into the minds of the
men or try to get there as regards
whether he was genuinely looking for
work or not. But they will be able to
make up their minds and follow their
inclinations in deciding whether
In other
employment is suitable or not.
words, if the man is a joiner, and is
offered work as a joiner, undoubtedly it
would be quite easy to decide that it was
suitable employment. If he is offered
work at another trade that he has not
served an apprenticeship to it is quite
easy to decide that it is not suitable employment. But on the other hand if he
is offered a position as a postman or as
a caretaker, or as a driver of a motor car
after a certain amount of training, the
courts might decide that that was suitable
employment. So that I really do not
anticipate trouble in this connection such
as occurred when one was endeavouring to
find out whether a man was really seeking
work or whether he was not. I have
endeavoured to answer most of the
questions raised, and I am sorry if I have
detained the House at too great length.
On Question, Motion, "That the Bill
be now read a Second time, agreed to.
""

Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.

employment Insurance Acts to employment abroad), ordered to stand part of

CLAUSE 14 (Crediting of contributions in respect of persons continuing
education).
Question proposed, that the Clause
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask
the Minister for a little further information as to the instruction
or classes
mentioned at the head of page 10. The
only point is, I assume that the Minister
himself intends to provide that these
classes or courses shall be of a suitable
character. As the text stands they seem
to be left a little bit in the air. They
are taken away apparently from the purview of the Ministry of Education. One
naturally wants some assurance that
the Ministry will deal with them. There
seem to be some regulations, and the
point arises just as well on this Clause
as on any other. I presume it is the intention that any regulations made under
this Bill will be laid before Parliament
as is the custom under all the other employment Acts but it is not mentioned.
as
It is
Mr. ANDREWS: The position
regards this Clause is, I think, not difficult. If a young person remains on at
school that will be considered to be education within the scope of this provision,
but it is usual for the department to
establish, and arrangements have been
made for the establishment of juvenile
training centres. The Ministry of
Education is not being left out of the
picture in that connection. My department is working in friendly co-operation
with the Ministry of Education.
proposed at that centre to teach these
young people quite a number of things,
to give them to some extent a secondary
education, to give them a certain amount
of training in connection with arts and
crafts, to train the girls in what is called
domestic economy, to teach them to cook
and wash, and to do household duties of
that kind. They will also, no doubt, get
a certain amount of physical training,
and under another provision in the Bill
we will have power to compel insured
persons to go to these classes, which I
think is all to the good, or else they will
be disqualified for benefit. They will
get their benefit in consideration of taking
advantage of these classes which will be
established. In Belfast we have purchased a building and fitted it up suitably for the requirements we have in
view. In other places we will use the
technical schools, and generally endeavour as far as possible to carry out
the intention of the Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I am glad to have
elicited so interesting a statement from
the Minister. The only point, of course,
was to ensure that these things should
be regularised, and that the Minister
should have the necessary power by
Regulations.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 15 (Provision of approved
courses of instruction for persons under
eighteen), 16 (Further amendment of s.
18 (2) of 18 and 19 Geo. 5. c. 3), 17
(Minor amendments), 18
18 (Transitory
provisions), 19 (Interpretation, repeal,
and short title), and 20 (Commencement
and duration), ordered to stand part of

SCHEDULES agreed to.
Title agreed to.
Ordered That the Bill be reported.
without Amendment to the House.

resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.

Moved, "That this Bill be now read the
Third Time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass.'
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Monday." On that day the House
will meet at twelve noon, when the
Prorogation will take place.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Five minutes after Five
o'clock, until Five
minutes after Twelve
o'clock on Monday next.

Prorogation
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Monday, 24th February, 1930.
The Senate met at Five minutes after
Twelve of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.

31st December, 1929.

Order, 1929, (Provisional).

Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
ROYAL ASSENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform
the Senate that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of, and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the
following Bill:—
Unemployment
ment).
Insurance
PROROGATION.
(Amend-
Resolved: That the Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod be commanded to inform the Commons that the Proclamation
of His Grace the Governor of Northern
Ireland proroguing this present Session
of Parliament is to be communicated
forthwith, and that this House invite the
Commons to attend in order to hear the
same. (Viscount Charlemont).
The Commons being come with their
Speaker, the Speaker of the Senate read
the Proclamation as follows:-
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION.
For Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by Section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by, and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said
Act, that the Governor of Northern Ireland, shall in His Majesty's name
summon, prorogue, and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland.
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that
this present Parliament of Northern
Ireland should be prorogued to the
eleventh day of March, One Thousand
Nine Hundred and Thirty.
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT EDWARD,
DUKE OF ABERCORN, KNIGHT
OF THE MOST NOBLE ORDER OF
THE GARTER, KNIGHT OF THE
MOST ILLUSTRIOUS ORDER OF
ST. PATRICK, Governor of Northern
Ireland, by this Proclamation do publish and declare His Majesty's Pleasure
that the said Parliament be, as from the
reading of this Proclamation in the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, prorogued to Tuesday, the eleventh day of
March, One Thousand Nine Hundred and
Thirty.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the
said eleventh day of March, One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Thirty,
assemble and be holden for the despatch
of divers urgent and important affairs,
and the Senators and Members of the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland
are hereby required to give their attendance accordingly.
Given at the Council Chamber, Belfast,
this twenty-fourth day of February, One
Thousand Nine Hundred and Thirty.
Signed by his own hand.
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
End of the First Session (opened 29th
May, 1929), of the Third Parliament of
Northern Ireland, in the Twentieth year
of the Reign of His Majesty King George
the Fifth.
