UNIVERSITY of CALIFORNIA
AT
LOS ANGELES
LIBRARY
UC SOUTHERN REGIONAL LIBRARY FACILITY
A 000 175 242 7

Tuesday, 11th March, 1930.
The Second Session of the Third l'arliament of Northern Ireland appointed to
meet on 11th March, 1930, in the
twentieth year of the Reign of King
George V, was opened by the Governor
(His Grace the Duke of Abercorn, K.G.,
K.P.) in person.
The House met at Twelve of the Clock,
Mr. SPEAKER (Mcst Hon. the Marquess of Dufferin and Ava, D.S.O., D.L.)
in the Chair.
KING'S SPEECH.
His Grace the Governor being seated
on the Throne, commanded the Gentleman Usher of the Black Rod to let the
Commons know "It is His Grace's
pleasure that they attend him immediately
in this House."
The Commons being at the Bar with
their Speaker, His Grace delivered the
following Speech from the Throne:-
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
I have noted with gratification the
continued progress of our great Shipbuilding Industry, and I am glad
to observe also that within our Linen
Industry action is being taken to
examine new methods for advancing its
prosperity. It is my sincere hope that
success may attend this effort and that
a practical solution may be reached of
those problems which have beset this
important Industry for so many years
past.
It will continue to be my Government's special endeavour to assist by
every means in its power Agricultural
and Industrial undertakings, and to
encourage, so far as is possible, the
development of new sources of employment in order that useful and productive work may be available for the
People.
I am glad to learn that the need
for co-ordinated effort to widen our
industrial basis has been met by the
creation of an Industries Development
Association. This body has undertaken
the dual task of encouraging the
demand for home products within our
A
King's Speech
King's Speech
THE SENATE
area, and of attracting to Northern
Ireland such new Industries as offer a
prospect of successful development. As
complementary to these activities, my
Government proposes to continue to
foster the marketing of produce from
Northern Ireland by participating in
Exhibitions in various industrial
centres organised by the Empire
Marketing Board.
A Bill will be submitted to you proposing certain amendments and additions to the Education Acts for the
purpose of making secure the position
of simple Bible teaching in public
elementary schools and making further
provision for representation on regional
and borough education committees,
the better regulating of school management committees,
other
and for
purposes calculated to increase the
usefulness of the Measures taken by
Parliament since 1922 to improve the
educational system. The success which
has attended these Measures and the
improvement in the facilities provided
in all classes of schools since the passing
of the Education Act of 1923 have
afforded me much satisfaction.
My Ministers view with concern the
serious extent to which sickness has
prevailed over a number of years
amongst persons insured under the
National Health Insurance Scheme,
and the general mortality figures
which are regrettably high. A Measure is being prepared for your consideration to make a more extensive
provision for the medical treatment of
insured persons with the object not
only of improving the health of the
working population but also of facilitating the ratification by His Majesty's
Government of the United Kingdom of
the International Convention on Sickness Insurance.
Proposals will be laid before you for
according to Local Authorities powers
in connection with Town Planning.
Proposals will also be laid before
you designed with the object of effecting
certain necessary reforms in the Lunacy
Laws and of ensuring a more systematic
provision for the care and treatment of
mentally defective children.
My Government in the development
of its policy for the better marketing of
agricultural produce will introduce two
further Measures dealing with the
preparation of meat for export and
with the marketing of fruit.
the following
Bills dealing with
matters will also be submitted for your
consideration:-
The amendment of the Law relating to Joint Stock Companies.
The amendment of the provisions
of the Railway and Canal Traffic
Act, 1888.
The amendment of the Law relating to Weights and Measures.
Electricity supply in Northern
Ireland.
Personation at Elections.
Tenancies in Urban areas.
Provision of Labourers' cottages in
Rural areas.
Members of the House of Commons.
The Estimates for the Public Services
will be laid before you in due course.
Members of the Senate and of the House
of Commons.
My Government has arranged for an
Industrial Census to be taken in
respect of Production in Northern Ireland during the year 1930. I trust
that this enumeration, when considered
in conjunction with the results of the
corresponding Census of 1924, will produce results of the highest value to the
Industries of Northern Ireland.
A comprehensive survey designed to
ascertain the possibilities of providing a
supply of electrical energy on a large
scale is now in progress.
It is my
earnest hope that this may result in
a scheme of Electrical Development
which will confer wide benefits upon
our Industries and our People.
I pray that the blessing of Almighty
God may rest upon your deliberations.
Then His Grace was pleased to retire,
and the Commons withdrew.
House adjourned during pleasure.
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
11 MARCH 1930
House resumed.
PAPERS PRESENTED.
Ministry of Finance:
Return made by the Official Assignee
of the Belfast Local Bankruptcy Court
for the year ended 31st December, 1929.
The Queen's University of Belfast Abstract of Accounts for the year ended 31st
July, 1929.
Teachers (Miscellaneous) Superannuation Scheme, 1930.
Laid before the House, pursuant to
Act, and ordered to lie on the Table.
LOCAL BILLS.
The Speaker acquainted the House that
the Clerk of the Senate had laid upon the
Table the certificates from the Examiner
that the Standing Orders applicable to
the following Bills had been complied
with:-
-:
Belfast Corporation,
Mater Infirmorum Hospital,
Larne Urban District Council.
Ordered: That the said certificates do
lie on the Table.
ADDRESS IN REPLY TO HIS
MAJESTY'S MOST GRACIOUS
SPEECH.
Mr. SPEAKER having read the King's
Speech,
Mr. BARCLAY (in morning dress):
I beg to move: That an humble address
be presented to His Grace in the following
words:
Your Grace,
We, His Majesty's most dutiful and
loyal subjects, the Senate of the Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg leave to
offer our humble thanks to your Grace
for the Speech which your Grace has
addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
I appreciate the honour that has been
conferred upon me in affording me the
great privilege of moving this expression
of thanks to His Grace on this occasion
of the opening of a New Session of this
Parliament. I do not look upon the
honour as personal, but as one conferred
upon the working-class people whom I am
pleased to represent in this House.
I rejoice to know that the health of His
Gracious Majesty the King has been restored, and that he is again able to move
among His beloved people, and I earnestly
pray that He may be long spared to reign
over us. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Our Parliament, during the last Session,
passed many Measures which have been of
great benefit to the people, for which they
deserve to be congratulated. The Housing, Drainage, Marketing of Dairy Produce, Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic
Bills, and the Widows', Orphans', and
Old Age Contributory Pension Bill are
only a few of the number which received
the approval of this House. All these
have contributed much to create a more
peaceful and contented feeling among the
people. I am sure this House will appreciate and welcome the useful and practical Measures now outlined in the
Gracious Speech, and give them their
cordial support when they are brought
forward at a later date.
The shipbuilding industry, I am glad
to see by the Gracious Speech, has made
progress during the past year, and it is
to be hoped that the improvement will
continue so that more employment will
be available for our workers. The linen
industry has not been all that could be
desired, but reference is now made in the
Gracious Speech to new methods which
are to be adopted. It is hoped that these
methods will solve the problem and that
prosperity will return to this important.
industry in Northern Ireland.
The other Measures mentioned in the
Gracious Speech need no further comment
at the moment, as they will come before
the House in due course for consideration.
All this legislation, which is to be introduced during this Session, will occupy the
attention of the Members of the Senate
for some time, but I am sure that the
Members will apply themselves diligently
to the work which comes before them, and
I hope their efforts will be productive of
much good for Northern Ireland.
May I thank you for the patient hearing given to me, and for the consideration
extended to the few words I have been
permitted to say. I beg to move.
Major DOBBS (in the uniform of a
Deputy-Lieutenant): In rising to second.
the Address of Thanks for the Gracious
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Major Dobbs]
Speech from the Throne, may I be permitted to say how deeply I esteem that
privilege; and that I consider a great compliment has been paid to me, and through
me to the people of County Antrim. The
progress in our staple industries of shipbuilding and of the manufacture of linen
that has taken place, and the further
progress that is foreshadowed in the
Speech, is a matter of the deepest concern
and gratification to all classes of the community, and to none more so than to that
class that looks to agriculture for its
livelihood. For the prosperity of the
agricultural industry is linked up directly
with the prosperity of industry as a whole,
through the spending power of the wageearners of the country.
Incidentally may I express a hope that
the reasons why the Ulster farmer is unable to provide the linen industry with the
finest grades of flax may be probed to a
successful conclusion, and that a solution
may be found to this problem, enabling
our farmers themselves to provide flax of
the finest quality, and thus oust
foreigner from our market. The activities
of the Empire Marketing Board must, if
vigorously and energetically fostered,
have a marked effect on the development
of the trade both industrial and agricultural of Northern Ireland.
the
But I much deprecate the advertising boards of this Empire Marketing
concern being filled with pictorial displays enhancing the merits of the products
of the Irish Free State, at a time when
the Press of that State is trying to boycott
within its boundaries the sale of goods from
Northern Ireland, a state of affairs which
occurred within the last twelve months.
The fact that efforts are being made to
attract new industries to our Province is
an encouraging feature of the Speech.
Years ago Belfast attracted to herself the
shipbuilding industry, and with her
favourable position for sea-borne trade,
with, we hope soon, an abundant and
cheap supply of electricity, with
transport by rail and road improved, our
postal facilities increased, and our mails
speeded up, there should be no reason why
Belfast should not become the centre of a
great manufacturing area.
A vista of
our
of our green fields, of the view of our
pleasant hills and loughs will be a heavy
price to pay, even for Ulster's prosperity.
Therefore I trust our Minister of Agriculture will, with the help of our farmers,
quickly bring back agriculture to its proper
place as the premier industry of Northern
Ireland.
The advance that has taken place in the
last few years in our public elementary
schools, under an intelligent and progressive Ministry of Education, has been surprising, and if the position of simple Bible
teaching in these schools can be adequately
secured and stabilised it will go far to dot
the i's and cross the t's of education in
Northern Ireland, and will meet with the
approval of the great majority of the
electors
Anything that can be done to improve
the health of the working population will
meet with the approval of this House.
And any reforms in the Lunacy Laws,
which ensure a more systematic provision
for the care and treatment of mentally
defective children will have the wholehearted and most sympathetic consideration of every Member here.
A Speech that breathes such a spirit of
optimism and at the same time lends a
helping hand to guide our feet into the
paths of prosperity cannot help but inspire,
and in so doing is entitled to the wholehearted support of each one of us.
I beg
to second the Address of Thanks.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I be permitted to congratulate the hon. Member
opposite (Mr. Barclay) on the admirable.
tone of his speech. I always listen to
him with interest and although I do not
always agree with the wisdom of his
speeches, I never on any occasion have
the least misgivings regarding his sincerity. I would also like to congratulate
the hon. and gallant Member (Major
Dobbs) on the nature of his speech, with
one solitary exception. What appealed
to me about his speech was that it was
the simple direct speech of a soldier.
He made a passing reference which came
to me as a very great surprise, about the
attitude of the Press of the Irish Free
State towards the exports of Northern
Ireland. His statement has come to me
I have been
as a complete surprise.
concrete roads, concrete
dwellings, concrete factories, steel pylons.
and black cables would, I am sure, delight
the eyes of hon. Senators opposite, but to
a plain person like myself the blotting out
totally unaware of any such endeavour
as he alleges on the part of the Irish
Free State or on the part of any section
of the Press or any section of the people
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
11 MARCH 1930
of the remaining twenty-six counties of
Ireland. I think it would be regrettable that in this Senate, where every word
counts in Irish affairs, a single word
should be said that could be construed as
being unfriendly to the people of that
neighbouring territory, or that would in
the least degree ruffle the relations existing between Northern Ireland and the
Irish Free State. It has always to be
remembered that the Irish Free State
covers some 27,000 square miles of Irish
territory, whereas we in our
lesser
sphere are covering some 5,300 square
miles, and I hope that we in Northern
Ireland will never forget that we are all
Irishmen. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear).
It
or
the
The reference in the Gracious Speech
to the Education Bill will spread indignation and dismay through every
Catholic parish in the Six Counties.
is clear from the nature of that reference
that there is no sign of any willingness
on the part of the Government to understand and to appreciate the viewpoint
of the minority here or to mitigate their
grievances To the majority it holds out.
every such hope. I will say little at this
stage, but I might even yet express the
hope that the Bill will not be an exclusive or partisan Measure. The air
has been cleared by the recent reasoned
joint statement of those members of the
Catholic Hierarchy whose dioceses
parts of whose dioceses lie within
confines of the Six Counties. That
statement has the loyal unanimous
adhesion of the Catholic clergy and laity
of Northern Ireland. The Catholic body
ask for the restoration of the equal
educational grants for the different denominational schools under the system in
vogue here for 100 years under the
British regime and still in vogue in the
other 26 counties of Ireland. There the
Protestant clerical managers of schools,
whose people form about 7 per cent. of
the population, have been treated by the
State with generosity and goodwill.
Will the new Bill be a gesture of peace
and goodwill to 35 per cent. of the population here? In Ulster the schools are
denominational in substance and fact--
Catholic and Protestant schools. You
cannot disguise this reality by naming
the schools provided, transferred, or committee schools.
The Education Act of 1923 forbade the
education authorities to provide religious
education in provided and transferred
schools. Will the new Bill endow in
these schools a type of religious education which will satisfy the Protestant
conscience alone? Work fresh injustice
now and you do it with your eyes open.
Lord Charlemont said on 12th July last
that "an education system which does
not give equal justice to the faiths of all
denominations is not worthy of a civilised
country." Such is the present system.
This Bill will also test the worth of
that declaration. Later at the Armagh
Protestant Diocesan Synod in October
he spoke of the amending Bill as an
effort to "safeguard Protestant interests
in the future." Is this to be the be-all
and end-all of the Bill? The best
friends of education here are hoping
almost against hope that the Bill will
not prove to be a Measure under which
72,000 Ulster school children will for
conscience sake be put at a permanent
disadvantage and the whole machinery
of educational advance clogged.
The Address held out a promise of
legislation this Session for the amendment of the law of landlord and tenant
in respect of town holdings. One must
recognise that a Torv and landlord Government cannot wax enthusiastic over
reforms which may hit some landlords.
How can it be expected to sympathise
with the grievances of building tenants?
They are a class common enough here.
Building tenants are men who contract
in their leases or otherwise, with their
landlords to erect buildings upon the
lands let to them. The Town Tenants
Act of 1906 says they are to be debarred
from compensation at the end of their
leases for any improvements made by
them in pursuance of their contract.
If a tenant takes a bit of ground and
covenants with his landlord to pay him
rent and also to build on it a house at a
cost of £500, and builds the house and
also pays the rent right through his
tenancy, that £500 house passes to the
landlord on the termination of the
tenancy and the tenant cannot claim one
penny of compensation for it.
I submit that that is an unjust and indefensible system. The same thing
applies in a contract to build a house of
£1,000 or £5,000. It is a system peculiar
to Northern Ireland. It hardly exists in
Scotland and it does not exist over great
areas in England. I believe it existed
in the old days in Turkey, but Turkey is
hardly a model.
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
And the point is that before the tenant
came along and took the piece of land the
land may not have been worth more than
a few shillings in the hands of the landlord
He comes along and through his energy
and enterprise the value of the land
jumped up ten or twenty fold. The
tenant clears the land and sinks his savings
in building a house and perhaps mortgages
his property to help to pay for it. He
suffers many things and denies himself
many things to make ends meet. Then
what happens? In due time his lease runs
out and the landlord, who has done nothing and spent nothing and risked nothing,
steps in and seizes and appropriates that
house. Well, I have heard of Job's three
comforters and one of them spoke of the
man who took the house which another
built and gathered
gathered the vintage of
another's vineyard, and I think he must
have had a landlord of that type in his
mind when condoling with Job in his
miseries.
Another point of crucial importance in
regard to landlord and tenant in Northern
Ireland is that the tenant of business or
professional premises ought to have the
right on the expiration of his lease to a
renewal of the tenancy for some definite
time, at least, on reasonable terms. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). He is entitled
under the Town Tenants' Act to compensation for unreasonable eviction. But
compensation for unreasonable eviction
may be to him and often is a quite inadequate and illusory remedy. It may
mean ruin to him to surrender the shop
in which he has built up a prosperous
business with toil, care and energy.
In
England under the Landlord and Tenant
Act, 1927, the tenant has a claim subject
to certain conditions to the right of renewal in circumstances like those, and
unless the coming Bill settles these two
questions the Government in promoting it.
will be simply ploughing the sands.
Sir WILLIAM COATES: In the first
place I should like to join with the last
speaker in congratulating the mover and
seconder of the Address for what they
have said and the way they have said it.
May I be permitted to draw the attention of the Senate to two Clauses in the
King's Speech. The first is one on page
4, with reference to town planning. This
I welcome very much, and I think it will
be welcomed not only here, but by all
local authorities in Northern Ireland, and
very especially by the Belfast Corporation.
We have been anxious to have something
of this sort for quite a long time and legislation in regard to it is undoubtedly overdue. Therefore it will be welcomed.
The
The second paragraph in the King's
Speech to which I should like to draw
attention is the one having reference to
reforms in the lunacy laws. This is a
very necessary piece of legislation.
present arrangements are very unfair to
the local authorities, both in regard to the
capitation grant paid by the Government,
which has not varied now for a long period
of years, and, secondly, in regard to the
arrangements which at the present time
are made for an influx of these mentally
defectives from other towns to any really
well run mental hospital.
I do hope that both these Measures when
they come before us will receive most careful consideration, and that the particular
parts of the subject I have mentioned will
be productive of much good locally and
throughout Northern Ireland.
Mr. DORMAN: I desire to join in congratulating the proposer and seconder of
the Address to His Grace. I welcome the
suggested legislation, and I wish to refer
to a matter mentioned by the hon. and
gallant Gentleman who seconded the Address the importance of attention being
paid to the production of more raw
material for the linen industry. It is a
well known economic fact that that nation
is most prosperous and gives a brighter
outlook both to the employing and employed class that utilises to the greatest
possible extent its own raw material and
carries it from the raw state into the very
highest stage of the finished product.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear). It appears
to me that Northern Ireland-the people
and the Government-has not paid
sufficient attention especially to the revival of the linen industry. Unfortunately, during the period of the War
those engaged in that primary industry
of Northern Ireland were shut out from
many of their foreign markets, and attention has not been paid to the development
of the raw material, which I have been
informed ca
can be produced of as fine a
texture and as suitable as any flax coming
from any part of the world. I therefore
welcome that particular part of the
Speech. There is another paragraph I
wish to refer to, and that is the paragraph
with regard to the Industrial Development Association. Ireland, on the whole,
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
11 MARCH 1930
has been the only civilised nation in the
world whose population has consistently
been depressed decade after decade for a
hundred years. We occupy an area that is
capable of producing as large an average
of bushels of cereals-and certainly the
largest possible tonnage of good crops-as
any area in Europe; and notwithstanding
that we have these advantages in the most
thinly populated area we have from decade
to decade sent our children off to seek a
market for their labour-power at the end
of the world. I speak with considerable
feeling on this particular question as my
own family are seeking that right of selling their labour power at the ends of the
earth, whereas I would desire rather to see
them working up the products of our own
particular area here.
on
a
atten-
I think in this particular,
tion ought to bę drawn to small
things of everyday use, and that the
Government should take steps to
carry
propaganda of small
things. For instance may I mention a
few of these things-boot polish, matches,
sweets and such things. If any hon.
Member of this House would perambulate
the streets in which most of the retail
shops are and take the trouble to ask for
things required of Ulster or Irish Origin,
they would be surprised at the number of
houses in which they would be unable to
obtain any products of their native
country. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
While we are training our children to go
out to work on the land in our colonies
and other countries we are neglecting to
support all these various industries that
are of considerable importance in view of
the unemployment.
There is another paragraph I would
like to refer to, and that is the paragraph
with regard to the treatment of mentally
defective children. I had the privilege
about 17 years ago of visiting Bradford.
and of examining the institutions of that
city, especially those for the purpose of.
trying to restore to their homes and industry the mentally defective children.
and I was surprised to find from the
superintendent in charge of that institution the very large proportion of the
children who came in under his care who
were returned to their homes and who
became most efficient industrial workers
in after life. As a matter of fact the
institution itself I was informed was
designed by the inmates. The younger
children were kept until they were 14
years of
age. Then they were taken
charge of by another institution, and
care was taken not merely with a view to
the restoration of their mental faculties
but investigations were made to ascertain
if the mental deficiency had been due to
any improper conduct on the part of the
children themselves. I often regret that
no such attempt had been made in Belfast with regard to the mentally defective
children in our midst.
There is another question I would like
to say a word on. It is the question of
personation. I do hope the Government
will take steps at least to abolish what I
call the underground railway through
which this nefarious system of personation is carried on, and that the Bill
promises at least to clear out the polling
booths and thereby destroy the underground railway enabling personation to
be carried on. Very few people will risk
voting for another person if they do not
know whether that person has voted. I
hope the Government in the Bill will
move in that direction. I also wish to
say I welcome whole-heartedly the suggested Bill to regulate tenancies. I
will not say anything further upon that
as I have a Resolution down to speak on
it and I hope to be able to give the Government some startling statistics with
regard to what is carried on day after
day.
Mr. LESLIE: Like the others I
should like to add my humble tribute of
respect to the mover and seconder of the
Address for the manner in which they
have performed their task this afternoon.
I do not recollect hearing the mover
address the House to greater advantage,
and he has been for some years a Member of this House. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). The hon. and gallant
Member who seconded the Address has
not been with us so long, but his speech
this afternoon gives assurance that the
promise made when he first addressed this
House will be amply fulfilled. He spoke
of his selection as a compliment to County
Antrim. I should like to assure him
that County Antrim is very proud of its
Senator.
Passing to the Speech, the Speech
seems to have been arranged in paragraphs almost in order of importance.
The first paragraph is that which deals
with the amendment of the Education
Acts. The principal Act is, of course,
the Act of 1923. The other two subsequent Measures are of comparatively
minor importance. It cannot be denied
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I think, that to-day in 1930 the atmosphere is more favourable for the formation of a clear idea of what it is the
people of Northern Ireland really want
in the way of education in public
elementary schools than it was in 1923.
Those of us who were in the House at
that time will remember that both in
this House and in another place great
difficulty was experienced in arriving at
a solution of what was called the difficulty of religious education.
I think that the greater number accepted what was put into the Act of
1923 as being at the most of a tem-
I am satisfied-and
porary nature.
indeed I fear I have said this beforethat we none of us expected the actual
terms of the Act of 1923 to last for
ever. But since 1923 it has been impressed upon us more and more, collectively and individually, and I imagine
it has been impressed on no one more
than upon the Noble Lord, the Minister
of Education, that the people of Northern
Ireland as a whole do not desire that
their children should be educated without having their minds and their
energies directed towards religious questions. I think that has been impressed
upon us year by year with great and
increasing force, and has necessarily
given rise to the suggestion that the Act
should if possible be amended so
make the position sounder than some of
the people imagine it to be at the
moment.
as to
Any attempt to recast the Education
Act in that matter would be fraught
with the gravest difficulties, and I must
say I myself shall watch with the greatest
interest to see how it is proposed to
handle the difficulty, for I think it
would be impossible to support any proposal--and I must say I do not expect
such a proposal will be made which
took away the very foundation of the
Act of 1923. There certainly can be no
attempt at compulsory religious education. I do not for a moment imagine
there will be. That cannot be done, and
we must recognise it is impossible for us,
either in 1930 or any other year, to prepare a scheme that will last for ever. We
may bring in a Bill to make sure of
simple Bible teaching, but we cannot
ensure the continuance of that scheme.
That must remain at the mercy of future
Parliaments elected by the people of
Northern Ireland.
I was rather sorry to hear the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) say
that the reference in the Speech to
simple Bible teaching would be received
with dismay by all those whose articles
of faith and forms of worship are the
same as his own, and I was also sorry to
hear him advance the abstract statement
that those to whom I have referred suffer
as regards equality of grants in comparison with those of another form of faith.
I do not think that an assertion of that
sort ought to be made without advancing something in the way of concrete
instances of the unfairness that is complained of. So far as I have been able
to observe, there is no distinction made
as regards grants or anything else between one form of faith and another.
but there is the distinction made in the
amount of grant or allowance according
to the amount of public control which is
admitted over the school in question.
One of the main foundations of the
Act of 1923 was, in effect, that those
who paid the piper should call the tune,
or rather you may put it the other way
and say that those who do not pay the
piper shall not call the tune. It is open
to those of every form of religious belief in
Northern Ireland to maintain their own
schools and to teach in them any form of
religion they like at their own expense.
That privilege has not been taken away
from them, and I cannot myself see that
justice to Northern Ireland as a whole, as
well as compliance with the terms of the
Imperial Act of 1920 would admit of any
more departure in the future than what
it has been possible to do in the past.
Indeed, it will be remembered that the
Noble Lord, the Minister of Education.
has not wholly escaped something in the
nature of censure because of arrangements which he made for the training of
teachers of a certain kind in Hammersmith, and comparisons have been made
as to the relative cost of the training of
those teachers as compared with the cost
of the training of teachers at, I think,
Stranmillis. I do not think there was
any foundation for the charges as regards any infringement of the Act of
1923, but the fact is that censure has
been offered, rather than inflicted, upon
that action of the Minister.
Passing from that, I should like once
more to say that if this whole question of
religious education is a problem, as it is
a problem, it must be remembered that
the Parliament of 1923 had to face tha+
Debate on Address
11 MARCH 1930
problem without the assistance of those representing the same ideals as the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Campbell). Had
they chosen to avail themselves of the
right conferred upon them they might
have attended in another place and given
the Government and Parliament of that
day their assistance in the formation and
framing of the scheme.
Now I come to the suggestion as regards
the National Health Insurance scheme.
I think it is essentially a matter on
which we should wait until we see the
terms of the Bill, and in the nature of
things the Bill cannot originate in this
House. Speaking more for the rural
districts of the Province, I hope the report on Local Government administration has not been forgotten. It is to be
remembered that the majority of those
who signed that Report, the large
majority, were opposed to any interference with the dispensary system, and
that even amongst the signatories to the
minority report there was one who suggested that the adoption of medical
benefits should be limited to the urban
areas.
I now come to the proposed reform of
the Lunacy Laws, which has already been
received with approval by the hon. Senator opposite (Sir William Coates) and by
Senator Dorman. If I may say so, I
think perhaps I take rather a wider view
of the proposals, and I hope I am right,
although I must say that Senator Dorman seemed to take the same view as
myself. I think that, although the
question of capitation fee is of importance-and the Lord Mayor of Belfast
and myself both addressed the House on
that subject seven or eight years ago—
what is of more importance is the reforms the new legislation is likely to
effect in the administration of the Lunacy
Laws themselves, and particularly the
arrangements made for the prevention of
lunacy, or rather for the checking of the
development of mental weakness. Those
seem to me to be matters that do require
attention. The amendment of the law
relating to Joint Stock Companies is
legislation that is due, because it is inconvenient that our law in this respect
should differ from that on the other side.
The question of the electricity supply
in Northern Ireland is one that has been
receiving increasing attention of late.
years, and it is a matter on which the
time is certainly ripe for action. It is
most important that the supply of elec-
Debate on Address
tricity should be co-ordinated,
DA

SO to
speak,
efficient companies and there rising
as to prevent small and inup and acting not in the interests of
immediately surrounding area but for
their own benefit by acquiring perhaps
some vested interests which may be opposed to the interests of the general
public. I do not think there is anything
else in the Speech on which I need detain the House, but I should like to remind the Government once
more that
we are still waiting for a small Measure
of Criminal Law Amendment which was
promised more than a year ago and which
has not yet found its place in the King's
Speech.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: What did
it relate to?
Mr. LESLIE: Extending the time during which prosecutions for certain offences
might be brought. I do not know
whether it is proposed that we should
have a Bill on that subject this Session.
If not I am quite prepared to bring in
a private Bill dealing with the matter.
Mr. MCALLISTER: It is an unpleasant duty for a public representative to
have to indulge in these more or less
controversial matters. I think Senator
Leslie, who referred to the Education
Act from his point of view, rather dealt
with it from a purely theoretical standpoint. After all, when we get down to
rockbottom, I do not see how you can
suggest that in the circumstances as they
exist at the present time those whom my
colleagues and I represent do not suffer
materially as regards grants compared
with their position before the Act of
1923 was passed. Those whom the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) represents have had grievances under this Act.
If I read aright the forecast of the
King's Speech, that grievance is to be
redressed, because there was difficulty
about the transfer of their schools owing
to their religious views not being protected. We have the same difficulty, but
the hon. and learned Member suggests
that those who have difficulty in subscribing to the terms of the 1923 Act
have the right to erect and maintain
their own schools.
It is the argument of the hon. and
learned Member that those who differ
from us should have the Bill amended
so as to meet their particular religious
point of view and assuage their con-
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
science,
fairy
no roadle
te to meet the
and
equitable,
reasonand because
effort was ever
just,
able demands of one-third of the people
of Northern Ireland they should not only
build, equip, extend, and maintain their
own schools, but should also be compelled
by law to contribute their quota to the
upkeep of the schools of the hon. and
learned Member's religious persuasion?
If the matter is pursued I think you will
find that up to the present, owing to the
conscientious views of those whom he represents-and I respect and admire them
for adhering to their conscientious
opinions-the Act up to the present has
been unacceptable to them.
I am sure
that when he comes to reply the Leader
of the House will bear out my statement
that a comparatively small number of
schools have been transferred up to the
present.
Reference has been made to the fact
that we did not participate in the preparation of the Bill of 1923. Surely our
demands must be just and reasonable and
fair when our most substantial grievance
is that you will not introduce a Bill on
the lines recommended by the Lynn Committee, a committee which, with one exception, was composed of members of your
own persuasion. We ask you to legislate
on the finding of the Lynn Committee,
and if you do you will meet the most substantial objection we have to the present
Act. Prior to the Act of 1923, all
schools of all denominations were entitled
to a grant of two-thirds of the cost of
building and extending schools. The
Lynn Committee upon which we had no
representation at all recommended that
this system should be continued, and it is
up to the Government now to justify the
fact that they refused to accept the finding of their own Committee.
And I say
that when we are willing to accept the
finding of a purely Unionist Committee
set up officially by the Government, the
demand which we now make can surely
not be held to be unreasonable. That is
the answer to the hair-splitting speech of
the hon. Senator. I think that surely
brings this question to bed-rock, when we
are able to put our case, not as we would
like to make it, but able to put a case
made by a Committee upon which we
had only one representative, and that
was a public servant. So far as we personally are concerned we have no objec-
We have
tion to this Bill as forecast.
no objection to simple Bible teaching in
this area. So far as I am personally concerned I think it would be a tremendously
bad thing for Northern Ireland if those
belonging to any section are to be brought
up without any respect for the religion of
which they are nominally adherents.
With regard to the Speech itself, the
Speech from the Throne is, I must say, a
very disappointing speech. I think it
is the most depressing and disappointing
document of its nature I have ever heard
introduced into any House. And speaking from the viewpoint of its so drab
colours, I think the two hon. Senators who
proposed and seconded this resolution are
deserving of congratulation for the manner in which they justified the speech out
of the stuff which it contained. I would
only take exception to one reference by
the hon. and gallant Senator who
seconded the Address. I think the reference was probably made without thought,
but I should say it was unworthy of him.
It was a rather petty reference to the Free
State. That might have very serious consequences. I think it is very unfortunate
that petty references of that description
are made by public representatives in an
assembly such as this, because I happened
to look at the "Telegraph the other
night and I saw that an institution which
we are all proud of in Northern Ireland
-I understand it is the biggest manufacturing concern of its kind in the world-
Gallaher's tobacco factory-I saw displayed in the pages of the "Telegraph "
a new factory being established in the
Free State by this firm which has its home
in Belfast, a factory manufactured of
steel with the intention that it is going
to endure there. Therefore I think it is
an unfortunate thing for the people of
both States that references of this description should be made.
The hon. and gallant Senator has referred to the fact that he is a County
Antrim man and proud of it. I am also
proud of County Antrim and proud of its
scenery
which he has referred to. I
think there is no county in Ireland which
surpasses Antrim in scenery. If we
sacrifice Antrim scenery by introducing
electric power which will bring sunshine
and pleasure to the unemployed and depressed people at the present time I, for
one, will be prepared to make that
sacrifice. I think especially from the
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
11 MARCH 1930
agricultural point of view it is a deplorable Speech. It is a notorious fact that
potatoes are being sold at the present
time by the farmers at 10s a ton, and the
only remedy suggested after what we
must consider to be thoughtful and profound consideration by the Government
in that Speech is that we are going to
continue sending our products to various
exhibitions. I also note that the Prime
Minister stated that he was making a
deep study and contemplating how the
bogs of Ireland could be rendered fertile
and fruitful.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN: A difficult job.
Mr. MCALLISTER: If we are going
to render the bogs of Ireland fertile and
fruitful while the farmer is selling potatoes at 10s a ton it is a terrible waste of
time. (Laughter.) With regard to the
Town Tenants Bill I notice that the hon.
Senator who sits behind me welcomes the
Bill. I will hesitate to welcome it. I
I will see it before I welcome it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
worthy of County Antrim !
That is
Mr. MCALLISTER: There is one particular grievance under which I think
town tenants of Northern Ireland
suffer. It is the hardship inflicted upon
them after spending so much money on
what are commonly called building
leases. I submit that any reasonable
landlord or any fair or just landlord has
nothing to fear from submitting his proposals to a judicial tribunal. I have
often noticed that when you go through
Northern Ireland and go into any village
you can see the character of the landlord written
on every house in that
village. I know villages in Northern
Ireland which are practically dilapidated.
I know three-story houses, substantially
built houses, and recently these tenants
have been denied new grants of leases,
and you cannot sell these houses for one
single penny above the ground rent that
has been recently imposed upon them.
How is any town or district to develop
and progress under that system? It is
a fact when the Town Tenants Bill was
introduced in 1906 by a Member of the
Irish Party and adopted by a Liberal
Government of the day it was introduced
for the purpose of dealing with this particular class of grievance. But when it
went to the House of Lords, the House
of Lords by the introduction of a rather
ingenious amendment provided that
leases to the tenant for valuable consideration should be exempt. The Landlord representatives of that assembly
boasted of the fact that by the introduction of this ingenious amendment
they had made the Act practically valueless.
Now unless the Government intend to
deal with that outstanding and vital
grievance it is only loss of the time of
the Government and the House to deal
with minor grievances which tenants may
suffer under. I may conclude by saying
that this is to me, and I think to those
with whom I am associated, and to all
thoughtful people of Northern Ireland
who look for some forecast or promise
on which they can build the hope that
there may be some development and progress in Northern Ireland and who
look for the amelioration
of the
present law, the most depressing Speech
that could be dealt out to a long-suffering
and depressed people.
Colonel SINCLAIR: May I join in
the chorus of praise and congratulation
to the two Members who so eloquently
moved and seconded the Address to the
Throne. I rise merely for a moment in
regard to the proposals outlined in the
fourth page of the Gracious Speech, that
is to say, the reform adumbrated in the
lunacy laws and the systematic care and
treatment of mentally defective children.
It would be altogether improper and inconvenient to discuss these Measures at
the present stage. I rise to ask whether
the noble Viscount, the Leader of the
House, can tell us whether these Measures, either or both of them, will be
introduced in this Chamber or whether
the first we shall hear of them in the
Senate will be when they come to us from
another place? The two Measures are of
very great value and are long overdue,
and they are fraught with immense social
advantage to the community, and it has
occurred to me that, not being primarily
financial questions, one or other of them
might be introduced in this House. I
should like to have an early intimation
as to which House is, first of all, to take
these Measures.
Mr. LONG: I should like to join the
other speakers in expressing the pleasure
with which I have heard the mover and
seconder of the Address, a pleasure which
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
has never been greater during the nine
years of our life here. I particularly
welcome the very sympathetic reference
to agriculture as the key industry of our
area, and although sneering references
have been made to the part of the proposals of the Government in regard to
agriculture, I understand from those who
grow fruit and export meat that the
Measures that are promised are calculated
to achieve a considerable amount of good,
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear), and some
good results are expected therefrom.
I am sorry I have to admit that the
statement of Senator McAllister is perfectly right that potatoes are being
disposed of at 10s per ton.
In many
cases farmers are unable to dispose of
their crops even at that price. I know
of instances where farmers are unable to
dispose of them-400 or 500 tons-and
these are being emptied up behind banks
and against ditches. I have about that
quantity myself and we are quite unable
to dispose of them. I should like to be
assured that the Government are exploring every possible avenue in that regard
to provide a market if possible for the
produce of Northern Ireland especially
the potatoes. I suggest that further representations should be made to Canada
with a view to the admission of our produce.
The last part outlined here I notice
is the provision of labourers' cottages
in rural areas. I am extremely glad to
know that further supplies of money will
be available for
for the building of
labourers' cottages, for there are not
sufficient labourers' cottages available in
the rural areas. I understand that the
loans for this purpose will expire shortly.
I do hope they will be renewed. In the
earlier stages of the Labourers Acts a
sum of money was granted to each
county. County Londonderry received
£1,170 per annum. The board of guardians that built the most cottages got
the most money.
This £1,170
divided equally for the different cottages.
If 1,170 cottages were built each year
the board of guardians got just £1 each.
That provision was withdrawn and a better arrangement was come to. I am glad
to see that there is a prospect of loans for
labourers' cottages being continued.
was
I look with some misgivings at the proposal of the Government to introduce
medical benefits into rural areas in
Northern Ireland. As a member of the
Poorlaw Commission I was one of a
majority who were opposed to the introduction of medical benefits and in my
view it will be an unmerited reflection on
the dispensary doctors of our area in
Northern Ireland.
There may be cases in which the dispensary doctor is not properly dicharging
his duties, but I have never heard of one.
As a member of a board of guardians for
thirty-five or forty years, and as chairman
of that board, it is my duty to examine
the books of the various dispensary doctors monthly. I am thus in close touch
with the dispensary medical officers and I
can vouch for this fact, that the poor are
attended with the utmost promptitude,
and with all the skill the doctor can
bring to bear upon the case. I deprecate this scheme, and I look upon it
with misgiving. I think there will be
overlapping. It seems to me ridiculous
that an insured man will be treated by
a panel doctor and his wife and children
will be treated by the dispensary doctor.
I shall probably have an opportunity of
dealing further with the matter when the
Bill is introduced, but I wish now to express my misgivings with regard to the
proposal, although further knowledge of
the provisions of the Bill may in some
measure mitigate the disfavour with
which I regard it at the moment. For the
present, however, I dislike the proposal
intensely.
areas.
It is quite true that the dispensary
doctors in existence at present will not
suffer, but it is not on their account
that I oppose this project. I am surprised, as Senator Leslie was, that the
Government should introduce medical
benefits into the rural areas. It may be
properly applicable to the urban areas,
but I distinctly say it will be a gross
mistake to introduce it into the rural
This is not the time for me to
deal at length with the services that the
dispensary medical officers have rendered
in the past, further than to say that they
have given complete satisfaction as
whole to the boards of guardians who
have supervised their work. I trust the
Government will agree to apply this Bill
to the urban districts only and exempt
the rural districts. I welcome the Speech
from the Throne as a whole, and I think
the Measures that have been forecast
will result in much good for the Province.
a
11 MARCH 1930
Debate on Address

Debate on Address
is done in other countries, but they
should begin by knowing fully the commercial and economic details of their own
Province. I should like in this connection to second what Senator Dorman said
about the support of Ulster goods. I
only regret that the services of my own
Ministry to show the people the advantages of the manufactures of their own
Province ceases at the age of fourteen
and cannot go beyond that. However,
I hope the publicity given to Senator
Dorman's suggestion will induce people
to adopt it, because the practice of going
into the shops and asking for Ulster
made goods is the best possible way to
get the shopkeepers to stock them.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): At the beginning of another
new Session it is again my pleasing duty
to offer my congratulations to the Mover
of the Address, who has spoken in such
felicitous terms. He said that he does
not look upon the honour as a personal
one, but rather as a tribute to our working classes. I think, however, that I
can speak in the name of all the
Members of this honourable House when
I say that we are also delighted for his
own sake that Senator Barclay should
have been Mover on this occasion. His
contributions to our debates since his
election to the Senate over four years
ago have always shown first-hand knowledge of the subject under discussion.
I would like also to congratulate the
Seconder of the Address, who has been
more recently admitted to our counsels.
His name is, however, a very old one and
the practical good sense of his speech
gives high promise of future material
assistance to this House in its deliberations. I think we can fairly claim that
this House is composed of a reasonably
balanced selection of people who have
widely different interests and experiences
and that it is, therefore, in the best
sense fully representative of the community.
Turning to the matters mentioned in
the Gracious Speech, I think we will
all agree that a proper emphasis has
been given to the need for the encouragement of industry. The Mover and
Seconder of the Address made special
mention of both the commercial and
agricultural aspects, and I do not wish.
to say more than that it will always be
the chief interest of the Government to
do everything in its power to promote
their revival and expansion. We, of
course, realise that this Parliament has
not entirely a free hand in such matters,
nor indeed can any Government by
legislation alone hope to bring about a
revival. It can only hope to stimulate the
efforts of those responsible for the industries themselves, and towards this
end an important step has been taken
by the creation of the Ulster Industries
Development Association.
I have hopes that my own Ministry
may also be able to assist in this desirable object by bringing specially to the
notice of pupils of schools the facts in
regard to Ulster Industries. It is well
that children at school should learn what
In regard to the legislation for the
coming session which has been foreshadowed, hon. Members will have heard
the mention of an Education Bill. I
do not think it would be wise to anticipate the debates which will arise when
the Measure is actually before. the
House, but I would only say at this stage
that one of the main objects of the Bill
is to secure in an even greater degree than
in the past the co-operation of the
various elements of the community without disturbing the democratic character
of the Education Act of 1923. There
are however, one or two points mentioned by the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell) to which I must refer,
although I do not want to make specific
allusion to them. He compared a speech
I made on 12th July with one I made
at the Armagh Diocesan Synod. In the
one I said I would not be a party to an
unjust Measure of education being
forced on the people of this Province.
What did I say in the Synod? I have
forgotten. It is easy to forget when you
make so many speeches.
Mr. CAMPBELL: It impresses me.
(Laughter.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I said the
Bill would be an effort to safeguard Protestant interests. That is very true, but
in a Christian community safeguarding
Protestant interests does not necessarily
mean doing an injustice to other interests.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Why do you not
safeguard other interests?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
they are safeguarded already. I do not
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
think the Education Act forced an injustice on any section of the community,
and I do not believe the proposed amending Bill will do so. I certainly would
not be a party to any Measure that
would do that. In the view of all impartial people I do not think it will be
found that any injustice has been inflicted on anyone. There is one question
I would like to put to Senator McAllister, who spoke about the Report of the
Lynn Committee. It is, of course, never
an advantageous or civil act to say that
anybody who refers to a particular
recommendation of a Committee is not
quite certain what the recommendation
really is, but I am a little doubtful as
to what Senator McAllister thinks
this particular recommendation really
does. I think his interpretation of
it is that building grants should be
given to all voluntary schools. Is that
so?
Mr. McALLISTER: It is so long
since I read the Report that I cannot
recall it.
This
small
Ulster.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is
not the case. The Lynn Committee
recommended the granting of money for
building purposes for enlarging vested
schools; that is to say, schools which had
been originally built with the assistance of Government money.
would affect only a very
proportion of the schools in
I cannot say definitely the number, but I should say about 300 of such
schools in all, of which about two-thirds
would probably be Catholic, and possibly
not so many. However, that is a matter
I would rather speak about in the
future, probably when the Bill is before
this House.
The proposal to introduce a system of
medical benefit in connection with the
National Health Insurance Scheme will
bring this branch of our social services
into complete conformity with that of
Great Britain. The provision of treatment and medicine during illness will
undoubtedly prove a valuable contribution to the health and happiness of our
insured persons.
The
Town planning is a matter which has
been arousing a very considerable amount
of public interest in recent years.
evils of defective town planning are evident in the layout of our cities and towns,
and in those parts of the country districts
contiguous, where building development
has taken place. Towns and cities have
grown up more or less haphazard without
due regard to the necessities for open
spaces or the public convenience, and the
necessity for some measure of control in
the matter of development is evident.
In addition to amending the existing
law in regard to Lunacy in certain respects where the present provisions are out
of date or inadequate, the proposed Bill
will contain provisions for dealing with
certain matters which were included in
legislation passed in Great Britain as far
back as 1913, but which was not made
applicable to Ireland.
The proposals to be placed before you
are also designed to make provision for
the treatment of mental defectives by
modifying the enactments in force in
Great Britain in such a manner as to
adapt them to local conditions, and to
secure corresponding advantages for persons coming within this category without
placing any unnecessary burden on public
funds.
The hon. and gallant Senator (Colonel
Sinclair) asked whether these Bills
would originate in this House. I think
the answer is in the negative, although
they are Bills which might well be introduced in this House. The business in
another place is in the first place verv
largely concerned with Estimates, and it
is rather an advantage for them to pass
Bills which can be discussed in this House
while the Estimates are under discussion
in the other House.
In regard to the further Measures dealing with agricultural produce, namely,
the legislation which has been foreshadowed in regard to the preparation of
meat for export and the marketing of fruit,
the House is, of course, aware that similar
provisions have already been made for
other farm products, such as eggs, potatoes, and dairy produce. The success
which has attended the Measures already
in force has encouraged the Government
to extend this type of legislation. The
Marketing of Eggs Act, which was the
first Measure of the Marketing Code, has
now been in operation for over five years,
and has enabled us to capture for
Northern Ireland eggs a place on the
English market second only to English
National Mark Eggs and above all
our competitors, whether from Denmark.
Debate on Address
Debate on Address
11 MARCH 1930
the Irish Free State, or any other country.
The Marketing of Dairy Produce Act was
passed only last session, and the Ministry
of Agriculture is at present engaged in
drawing up rules and regulations under
that Measure, so that it is, of course, too
early to give any particulars as to results.
In regard to the Marketing of Potatoes.
the Act which controls this branch of produce has been in force for over a year, and
although this year has been one of extreme
difficulty, there is no question that the
improvement in the quality of our potatoes will ultimately redound to the
advantage of the producer. I am sure
that all the Members of this House will
sympathise very much with the farmers
in the low prices which they have been receiving for this crop last season owing to
circumstances outside their control and
outside the control of the Government.
A number of amending Measures have
been mentioned in the Gracious Speech,
namely, those relating to the Law of
Joint Stock Companies, the Railway and
Canal Traffic Act of 1888, and the Law
of Weights and Measures.
The Companies Bill will be an amending Measure incorporating most of the
recommendations of the Company Law
Amendment Committee, which was appointed by the Board of Trade in 1925 to
consider and report what amendments
are desirable in the Companies Acts,
1908 to 1917. As the result of this report a Measure was passed in the
Imperial House in 1928 and our Bill
will follow that Act closely. Parallel
legislation is specially desirable in this
case so that the decisions of the Courts,
the text-books, and in fact the whole
fabric and practice of Company Law
in the United Kingdom may be uniform
so far as circumstances admit. It is
hoped also to prepare and introduce a
Consolidation Bill and until this latter
Measure has received legislative sanction
it is the intention of the Government
that only two or three clauses of the
amending Bill should come into operation in the meantime.
The Bill amending the Railway and
Canal Traffic Act, 1888, is designed to
remove certain difficulties which have
arisen in connection with the procedure
which is prescribed in the present Acts
for the abandonment of an unnecessary
canal. The main obiect of the Weights
and Measures Amending Bill is to bring
within the ambit of the existing Acts
certain classes of measuring instruments,
for example, petrol pumps, which at present are not subject to inspection and
verification by the inspectors of Weights
and Measures. It will also have the
effect of bringing our legislation
generally into line with recent legislation
in Great Britain.
Mention was also made in the Gracious
Speech of a proposed Bill dealing with
electricity supply. In Great Britain the
Imperial Government has been actively
engaged in carrying out the recommendations of a Committee presided over by
Lord Weir and is proceeding expeditiously with the reorganisation of the
electricity supply throughout the
country. The problem there centres
round the organisation of supply rather
than the creation of a demand. Here in
Northern Ireland the circumstances are
widely different. Agriculture is still our
principal industry. Futhermore, many
of our manufacturing industries, although widely distributed over the Province, still find it necessary to use steam
for process work. For these and other
reasons it will be appreciated that the
problems in connection with electricity
must be approached with caution and accordingly the Government has thought it
well to secure the services of an electricity consultant of outstanding reputation to advise it as to the sanest lines
upon which development should proceed.
This gentleman's report is expected at
an early date and it is proposed to give
legislative effect to such policy as may
be determined after this report has been
received and fully considered.
It is of course impossible to indicate at
this stage the nature of the contemplated
legislation. I may assure this House that
when the Government is considering it
full attention will be given to what was
mentioned by the hon. and gallant Member who seconded the Address. It would
be a lamentable pity if all the country
areas-even in Antrim-(laughter)-
were defaced by the hideous pylons or
other things for the carrying of current
which deface many other countries, chiefly
abroad. But there is no reason why if
pylons or other erections are made they
should not be at least inoffensive.
many cases I have seen in other countries
electricity erections of that nature made
almost beautiful. Not quite, but almost.
At any rate one gets quite used to them,
and one does not see that they are there.
That is the best that can be hoped for.
In
Debate on Address
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
With regard to personation, a committee was appointed by the Minister of
Home Affairs last year to inquire into the
whole question of personation, and to
ascertain how the law might be improved.
The Bill, which has been prepared and
will be introduced at an early date,
follows generally the lines of the Committee's report. This matter has been
causing considerable concern to all parties,
and it is hoped that the amendments to be
made in the law will have the effect of
checking the abuse.
The law relating to tenancies in urban
areas was also the subject of a Committee
of Inquiry, and in the framing of the Bill
the Government are giving the most careful consideration to the recommendations
put forward by that Committee. As the
Bill will come on I think it would not be
advantageous for me to answer the specific questions raised by the hon. and
learned Senator who spoke. I understand
a resolution will be moved to-morrow on
which I may be able to give a little more
information.
Owing to the fact that the provisions
of the Labourers Cottages Loans Act
(Northern Ireland), 1927, applied only
to schemes for the erection of labourers'
cottages lodged not later than the 31st
March next, it will be necessary to make
further provision in this respect, and the
Bill mentioned in the Gracious Speech
is for this purpose.
Mention has also been made of an industrial census of production which is
to be undertaken in Great Britain
during this year. The information which
will be accumulated should be of the
greatest interest, especially when compared with the corresponding results of
the 1924 census.
There are one or two minor Measures
which have not been mentioned in the
King's Speech, more especially that one
in which the hon. and learned Senator
Leslie is interested which will be placed
before you in due course.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved: That a humble address be
presented to His Grace in the following
terms:-We, His Majesty's most dutiful and loyal subjects, the Senate of the
Parliament of Northern Ireland, beg
leave to offer our humble thanks to your
Grace for the Speech which your Grace
has addressed to both Houses of Parliament.
Ordered: That the said Address be
presented to His Grace by Privy Councillors of Northern Ireland.
Adjournment.
Moved: That this House do now adjourn. (Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
Minutes before Five o'clock.
Bill Presented
Oral Answers

THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 12th March, 1930.
The Senate met at a quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.

Moved, "That the Senators following
be named Deputy-Speakers of the Senate
during the present Session of Parliament:
Mr. J. C. Glendinning,
Mr. T. S. McAllister.'
(Viscount Bangor.)
""

be named of the Joint Standing Committee on Divorce Bills:-

JOINT STANDING ORDERS

be named of the Joint Standing Orders
Committee:-
