BILL PRESENTED.
Abolition of Corporal Punishment
Bill (S.),
to provide for the abolition of corporal punishment," was presented by
Mr. T. J. Campbell, supported by Mr.
Dorman and Mr. McHugh; read a First
time, and ordered to be read a Second
time on the next sitting day, and to be
printed. (Bill No. 1.)
Questions to Minister.
HOUSING OF SLUM TENANTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the Government intends to bring in a Bill this Session for the
clearance of slum areas and the proper
housing of slum tenants?
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): As the hon. and learned
Member will have seen from the King's
Speech it is the intention of the Government to introduce during the present
Session a Bill conferring on local authorities in Northern Ireland powers in connection with town planning.
These powers will apply not only to undeveloped areas but to built-up areas, and
taken in connection with the powers which
are already conferred on local authorities
by the Housing of the Working Classes
Act, 1890, local authorities in Northern
Ireland will have no difficulty in formulating and putting into operation schemes
for the clearance of slum areas and the
proper housing of slum tenants, where
such conditions exist.
CASES UNDER MOTOR ACTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the attention of the
Ministry of Home Affairs has been drawn
to the public inconvenience caused by the
impasse at several recent petty sessions in
respect of prosecutions brought under the
Motor Vehicles Acts owing to the ordinary
magistrates in attendance being without
jurisdiction and no resident magistrate
being available; also to the increasing frequency with which ordinary justices at
petty sessions have to retire from the
bench when cases are called in which their
jurisdiction has been taken away and to
resume their places when cases are called
on which they can adjudicate; and
whether arrangements of petty sessions
work can be made that will minimise
these inconveniences?
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I am
aware of the fact that from time to time
courts have had to be adjourned owing
to it not being possible for a resident
magistrate to be in attendance to hear
cases in which resident magistrates alone
many adjudicate.
Oral Answers
Profiteering in Rents
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
The main cause for this difficulty is
that in many cases two separate courts in
a resident magistrate's district are held
on the same day.
I may say that the Ministry of Home
Affairs has under consideration at the
present time the question of altering the
districts of certain resident magistrates
in order to avoid the difficulty to which
the hon. and learned Member has drawn
attention, and resulting from this rearrangement it may be necesary to endeavour to have the day on which certain
courts are held, altered in order that they
will not coincide with the date of some
neighbouring court.
As regards the last part of the question, I do not really think that the difficulty would arise if some local arrangement were made whereby the cases for
adjudication by justices of the peace were
taken first and the resident magistrates'
cases later. This would save the local
justices a considerable amount of time,
and I am aware that in certain districts
arrangements of the sort indicated have
already been made and are working.
satisfactorily.
VALUE OF LINEN IMPORTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House-
(1) The respective values of the following imports from foreign countries into
Northern Ireland for the year 1929: Flax,
tow, yarn, flax line and tow yarns, linen
piece goods, and all other manufactures
of linen;
(2) whether the value of the exports to
foreign countries of linens from Great
Britain and Northern Ireland fell from
£14,786,899 in the year 1924 to
£9,290,849 in the year 1928, and again
to £9,240,340 in the year 1929, and
whether the value of the exports of yarns
fell from £1,425,862 in the year 1924 to
£1,151,201 in the year 1928, and again
to £1.102,620 in the year 1929, and
whether these goods were predominantly
of Northern Irish manufacture?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In regard
to the first part of the question I should
perhaps remind the hon. and learned
Member that the only statistics of
Northern Ireland's imports, excepting
those from the Irish Free State, compiled
by the Imperial Customs Authorities, relate to Direct Imports, that is to say, to
goods brought into Northern Ireland from
places abroad without having undergone
transhipment in Great Britain. There is,
moreover, no means of distinguishing in
the statistics of imports compiled by the
Northern Irish Harbour Authorities
goods of foreign as distinct from crosschannel origin. The following figures for
1929, whch have been furnished by the
Customs Authorities, include all Direct
Imports-Dominion as well as foreign,
with the exception of those from the Irish
Free State:
Flax
Tow and Codilla
Flax and Tow Yarns
Manufactured Linen Goods
£
1,867,442
276,293
840,714
6,537
was
The figures quoted in the second part
of the question are correct, except in respect of the exports for the year 1924.
The total value of the exports of linen
goods, excluding yarns, for that year, as
given in the Trade and Navigation Returns of the United Kingdom,
£13,320,491, and not £14,786,899, as
stated in the question. The figures
quoted in the latter portion of this part of
the question relate to the exports of flax
and hemp yarns, to which commodities it
is assumed that the hon. and learned
Member refers in quoting them. There
is no means of distinguishing between
goods manufactured in Northern Ireland
and those manufactured in Great Britain
in dealing with the export figures for the
United Kingdom, but the hon. and
learned Member's assumption that the exports of linen goods in the years in question
were predominantly of Northern Irish
manufacture is scarcely open to question.
Profiteering in Rents.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to move,
That in the opinion of this House,
with a view to mitigating the sufferings
of the working classes caused by
profiteering in rents, the Government
should take steps to set up a Fair Rent
Court to fix a fair rent for the town
dwellers in Northern Ireland.
As it is with a deep sense of personal
responsibility that I have placed this resolution on the Order Paper and solicit the
support of hon. Members of this House.
In a civilised community I conceive it to
37 Profiteering in Rents
12 MARCH 1930

Profiteering in Rents
be the duty of every individual to draw
the attention of the Government to any
injustice or wrong prevailing in the community with a view to inducing them to
carry out the reforms necessary to enable
such injustices and wrongs to be removed.
In the chate in which we are living the
primary requisite of human life, health,
and happiness consists of food, clothing,
and shelter, and if that part of the community that owns and controls any one of
these requisites of human life and wellbeing is permitted by the Government to
profiteer and to exploit and oppress the
other part of the community which must
necessarily have access to these requisites
in order that they may live, a grievous
social injustice is thereby inflcted upon
the people.
a
appears
Profiteering in any one of these three
requisites is a serious matter indeed, but
profiteering in dwellings is a far more
serious matter than profiteering in either
food or clothing. If a person profiteers
on a working man's shelter to the extent of 10 per cent. of his income he is
thereby deprived of spending that 10 per
cent. upon food and clothing, the use of
both of which commodities creates
demand for labour, and it
to me that in the present sad state
of unemployment a very grievous injustice indeed is being inflicted upon
the community through the freedom
which houseowners at present have to
profiteer at the expense of unfortunate
tenants. House property is not like wine.
It does not improve either in quality or
price with age. It begins to deteriorate.
the very moment it is completed, and it
is well known to every Member of this
House that if any merchant approached
a bank for the purpose of obtaining an
overdraft for carrying on his business and
deposited for security papers relating to
house property, if the manager of that
bank did not periodically reduce the value
of the security he would get very short
shrift indeed at the hands of his directors.
And yet it is in this particular commodity, if I may use the expression, that
profiteering is now going on to an
enormous extent in our midst.
houses, because the tenants I have interviewed have requested me not to mention
their addresses or their names. Indeed,
I was struck by the fact that a prosperous
business man who has more than one busi
ness house in the city pleaded with me not
to mention his name or address because
he is still at the mercy of his landlord
who might again proceed to give him
notice to quit and increase his rent. With
these few prefatory remarks I would ask
for the indulgence of Members while I
refer to a number of these particular
cases.
Here is one house of six rooms and a
kitchen situated in the centre of the city,
in what is considered a slum area. In
the front room there is a husband and
wife and one child paying 12s a week.
In another front room there is a husband
and wife and two children, four persons,
paying 12s a week. In another room is a
husband and wife and three children paying 12s a week, and in rooms over that
again there is a husband and wife and
four children paying 10s a week. In au
attic there is a husband and wife, two persons, paying 10s a week, and in another
back attic there is a husband and wife and
three children paying 10s a week. That
is a total rent of £3 6s Od a week for six
working-class families for single apartments. Each one of these families would
have been able to command a six-roomed
parlour house before the war broke out
for £74 6s Od per year less than they are
now paying for these single apartments in which they have to perform all
the functions of a civilised home.
In the very centre of the city there is
a small street and the controlled rent of
one house with a shop attached was £25
a year. Immediately the house became
decontrolled the rent was jumped to £60,
an increase of £35. There are two or
three similar cases in that particular
street. Here is another instance of a
house, the controlled rent of which was
£31 a year, and immediately the premises
became decontrolled a rent of £90 was
demanded and obtained, an increase of
£59 a year, or practically 200 per cent.
Here is the case of a professional man
who in the centre of the city built up a
professional connection. He paid a controlled rent of £45 a year, and he was
asked to pay £114, and he left the premises, thereby endangering his professional practice to a large extent rather
than pay that rent of £114. At the far
421779
I have picked out a number of cases
from various wards in the city, and I can
assure the House that they cover from
the Lisburn Road to York Road, and from
Holywood Arches almost up to Ligoniel.
I do not intend to mention the specific
Profiteering in Rents
Profiteering in Rents
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
end of the city there is another case of a
controlled rent of £28 being raised to
£60, an increase of £32. Kitchen
houses and there are hundreds of these
in the city that were formerly 6s and
5s 6d a week were raised immediately
they became decontrolled to 10s and 12s
a week.
Now I come to a sad case, indeed. It
is a house in which there are nine families,
and the circumstances are these. In the
kitchen there is a husband and wife and
two children paying a rent of 6s a week.
In a small back room eight feet square
one person is paying 4s 6d a week; in
a front room there are six persons paying
6s a week; in another front room eight
persons paying 7s a week; and in back
rooms are five persons, two persons, three
persons, six persons, making a total rent
of £2 9s Od per week, which is being extracted from the users of single rooms in
that house, the controlled rent of which
is 15s a week, an increase of £1 14s Od a
week in a single residence. This is in
another ward. A pre-war rent of 4s 6d
a week was raised to 12s a week, and it was
recently raised to 20s, an increase of 15s 6d
a week compared with the controlled rent.
In
I have here some particulars of houses
owned by a gentleman who is a notorious
profiteer, but whose name I will not
mention. The houses are in the neighbourhood of the County Courthouse.
the front parlour of a house is a husband
and wife and one child paying 9/6 a week;
the kitchen and scullery is occupied by a
husband and wife and two children paying 7/6 a week; in a front room are two
persons paying 9/- a week; in a back room
a husband and wife and one child paying
7/- a week; in a top front room people are
paying 9/- a week; five persons in a top
back room are paying 6/6 a week; and for
a front attic with a small return room
8/6 is charged, making a total of £2 7s 6d
a week, or £148 a year for a house, the
pre-war rent of which was £50 a year.
This is a striking instance of profiteering.
It is the case of a hairdresser who occupied
premises, the controlled rent of which was
£16 18s 0d a year, and he is now paying
£70. I would ask hon. Members to consider what this means.
At the present price that this man is receiving for his labour, regulated by the
trade union with which he is connected,
he would have to shingle the hair of 1,062
young women, or shave 4,248 men, or cut
the hair of 2,124 individuals to make up
the difference in the rent which is imposed
upon him, because owing to the decontrol
of his house his landlord has the right to
take whatever rent he desires for the use
of his premises. In the very same neighbourhood there is a gentleman who was
paying £35 a year, and immediately the
house became decontrolled the rent
jumped to £150, an increase of £115 per
annum. Here is a case in a provincial
town. A business man occupied a house
and shop for £65 a year. Immediately
the house became decontrolled he was
noticed and requested to pay £200 a year
for the shop. The first landing is let at
£65, the second landing at £50, the third
landing at £40, in addition to rates in
each case, a jump from £65 to £355 a
year. Through this increase of rent that
gentleman is in the position of having
£5,800 in War Loan. That is what
profiteering in those particular premises
has meant to this person.
was
I think I have given sufficient figures to
convince all Members of the House of the
necessity of something being done to check
this evil. I can assure the House that
this unfortunate state of affairs is prevailing all over the City to-day, and the working classes are suffering intensely as a result of the position in which they are
placed. We
We hear much about simple.
Bible teaching, but when thinking about
this particular subject my personal feeling
to paraphrase some simple Bible
teaching, and personally approach these
men and say,
Go to, now, ye house
profiteers. Weep and how for the
miseries that are coming upon you. The
cries of your rack-rented tenants have
entered into the ears of the Lord of
Sabaoth." I would rather the cry of
these people would enter into the ears of
the Government, because the Government, and the Government alone, have
the power by legislation to set up some
scheme of rent courts or to re-enact the
Rent Restriction Act to prevent a continuance of profiteering in our midst. I
beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think the industry the hon. Member (Mr. Dorman)
has displayed in this matter is to be
commended. I was very interested in
the figures he brought before the House,
and I feel sure, having regard to the
high integrity of his character, that they
are reliable statistics. When the Imperial Government imposed rent restric-
Profiteering in Rents
Profiteering in Rents
12 MARCH 1930
tion after the outbreak of the war many
thought they should not have curbed the
right of the strong and unscrupulous to
fleece the weak. The same folk will say
the same about any move by the legislature to establish courts to fix fair rents.
There ought to be and there is not an
abundance of cheaper and better houses
at fair rents where people can have decent homes. Laissez faire critics will say
tenants are free to make their own contracts and the State ought not to interfere with these contracts. Nor should
it if there is real freedom of contract,
if the tenant has freedom of choice as
well as the landlord. Many landlords
are fair and reasonable, but anyone who
knows even a little of the conditions prevailing in urban areas knows that in
numbers of cases, where it can be done
within the law, the landlord exacts the
uttermost farthing of rent and the
tenant agrees to pay it because he must
if he is to get a roof over his head.
Why should the State not set up
machinery to fix fair rents in these cases?
Why should it not protect against extortion a tenant whose necessities disable
him from protecting himself? The
moneylender cannot exact from his borrowers
an unconscionable rate of
interest. Why should a harsh landlord
be free to exact an unconscionable interest on his investment? Under the
Rent Restrictions Act, 1925, a landlord
of a dwelling house of which the rateable valuation exceeds £8 who got possession of the whole house at any time
after the passing of that Act was freed
from all restrictions on rent raising. He
was left free to parcel out the house to
different tenants. That decontrol should
never have been enacted.
In one house in a leading road in Belfast, about ten minutes' walk from Castle
Junction, which Senator Dorman and I
visited, there were, I was informed, 7
tenancies in 7 apartments. The top garret had 2 occupants, paying 7s 6d a week.
A return room held a father, mother and
4 children, and the rent was 8s 6d. A
front room sheltered a father, mother
and 3 children, and the rent was 10s.
26 people inhabited that house.. The
aggregate rent from this collection of
tenancies under one roof was £3 3s Od a
week, or £163 16s Od a year. The pre-war
rent of the house was £45 a year.
What
have tenants who pay such rents left for
the poorest food and clothing of themselves and their children? Tenants in
turn are profiteering. I was informed of
a small parlour house let at 4/11 a week.
The tenant had let the front room at 5/-,
the parlour at 4/-, and the back room at
4/-, total, 13/- The tenant of this house,
in a working-class district, was rent free
and making a profit of 8/1 a week. Many
small shopkeepers are being fleeced, and
they are a singularly uncomplaining class.
In conclusion, I submit that the figures
submitted by Senator Dorman are conclusive evidence of the necessity of the
step he suggests.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
While I think the state of affairs disclosed
by the figures given by Senator Dorman
is deplorable, there is one point we should
consider before we make up our minds on
this question. Before the war a farm
labourer was satisfied with a wage of 18s.
a week, and now he is getting 35s a week,
and, as a rule, the rent of his house has
been raised. The figures given by
Senator Dorman would incline one to
sympathise with the tenant, but, on the
other hand, is it not a fact that he has
a larger income with which to pay his
rent. When considering these figures I
would ask hon. Members not to overlook
this phase of the question. I know of one
landlord, an honest landlord, who, when
he had paid insurance and head rent, and
allowed a reasonable sum for repairs, was
not getting one per cent. for his houses.
That is another side of the subject.
I fear that the real blame is not so
much on the original landlord as on the
person who takes a house and then tries
to make a profit on the rent by subletting portions of that house. If something could be done in those cases it would
be a good thing, but before we form a
definite opinion on the Motion before the
House it would be well that we should
take into consideration all the pros and
cons. It is these hard cases such as
Senator Dorman has quoted that we
mostly hear about, and we hear very little
of the great many cases in which the landlord is not profiteering, but is only charging a rent in proportion to the increase
in the wages the tenant has received since
pre-war days. In the bulk of these cases
I do not think there is any necessity for
legislation on the lines suggested in the
Motion before the House.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I must say
in commencing that the notice of the
Resolution given to Members of this
43 Profiteering in Rents

THE SENATE
Profiteering in Rents
[Mr. Leslie]
House is somewhat short and, consequently, perhaps we have not had an
opportunity of refreshing our memory as
regards any recent statistics available
dealing with these matters, and particularly refreshing our memories with the
recent report of the Commission, on which
sat more than one Member of this House,
dealing with the general question of town
tenancies.
I was rather inclined at first to complain that the hon. Senator (Senator
Dorman), who has moved this Resolution,
was rather begging the question in suggesting that the working classes were
being victimised and that there was profiteering in rents; but I do not think it
would be altogether fair to pursue that
line of argument in view of the figures
he has given, although he did not give
any names or localities for the reason he
gave, a reason which no doubt justifies
his action, although it renders an examination of his statements a little difficult,
and renders it even more difficult than it
would otherwise be to consider what can
be said on the other side of the question.
I am not myself in sympathy with profiteering whether it be in land or any
other commodity. I do not myself hold,
or have I ever held, any town residential
property.
a
Passing from that, the hon. Member
has taken rather strong step, and I
think it is a step unprecedented in the
history of our Parliament. He has invited this House to subscribe to a Resolution calling upon the Government to do
something or other. I think I am right
in saying that this is the first occasion on
which this House has been asked to pass
a Resolution of that character. Senator
Dorman did not tell us and Senator
Samuel Cunningham laid stress on thisto what extent the wages of the classes
concerned have risen since the war. He
did not give us any indication of the rateable value of the premises where the profiteering is alleged to exist; he did not
tell us anything about the selling price
of property of that description; and, if
I remember rightly, the question whether
the property has changed hands or not by
sale is a matter which has a distinct bearing on the application or otherwise of
decontrol. Nor did he tell us and this
is a point on which I should have liked
to hear him-whether the overcrowding
which he mentioned-a deplorable amount
of overcrowding undoubtedly-was
or
was not worse than the overcrowding before the war.
The rents he stated as being paid by
families for one room were such as would
have enabled those families, I think, to
obtain one of the new houses erected more
or less under the influence of the Belfast
Corporation. I think he was referring
mostly to Belfast cases, and I think I ani
right in saying that the subsidy houses
erected by the municipal authorities in
Belfast can be obtained at a rent of about
12s a week.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Then there are
travelling expenses backwards and forwards to work.
Mr. LESLIE: Senator Dorman did not
tell us about the travelling expenses of
the occupants of these rooms. That is a
matter which must be left, I think, to be
governed by individual circumstances, but
it is obvious that a man who elects to live
here to-day because it suits his work and
elects to live there to-morrow because it
may suit him there, a person who is itinerant in that way can scarcely expect to
enjoy the cheapest of commodities. Passing from that, and reverting to the
original question, apparently Belfast Corporation houses can be obtained at
approximately the rent I mentioned, and
I think I am correct in saying there are
still a number of such houses which are
unoccupied. I think that is so.
I want to come back to what I said before as regards the step taken by Senator
Dorman in asking this House to pass this
Resolution. He asks us to pass a Resolution calling upon the Government to
set up a fair rent court. Of course, the
Government could not set up a fair rent
court on its own motion. If it decided
to do so it would have to come to Parliament for authority. The point I desire
to make is that the setting up of a fair
rent court in dealing with house property
would be an infringement of the terms of
the Government of Ireland Act.
it is beyond the power of this Parliament
to take property of any description without compensation. I do not think that
it would be competent for this Parliament
to set up a fair rent court to deal with
matters of this kind, and if I may draw
so far on my memory I do not think the
recent report dealing with the relations
of landlord and tenant as regards town
property suggested that a fair rent court
should be set up.
I think
Profiteering in Rents
Profiteering in Rents
12 MARCH 1930
I am not quite sure about that, but I
have certainly not yet seen a Bill which did
contain a suggestion for a fair rent court,
using the expression "fair rent court" in
perhaps rather the limited sense of a court
dealing with a fair rent and not a court
dealing with other incidence of tenure of
property, which is not quite the same
thing. While deeply sympathising with
the cases brought before us, and while
saying nothing that could be construed
into approval of any action that could be
described as grinding the faces of the
poor, which would be repellent to all of
us, I think we should not accept the Resolution which has been moved.
Mr. DORMAN: I should like to be
permitted to say that I can guarantee the
genuineness of all the cases, except two,
which I have mentioned. I have visited
every one of the rooms, and I have ascertained direct from the tenant what the rent
was. I have also taken steps to bring the
matter before the notice of the public
health authorities by approaching the
medical officer of health for Belfast.
Every statement I have made has been
obtained directly from the tenants.
So
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
far as I am concerned I have no doubt as
to the genuineness of the statements made
by Senator Dorman. I can say that much
of him. I believe he would be most
particular in making statements of this
kind, but, after all, they only serve to convince us that there are some unscrupulous
persons living in Northern Ierland just as
there are in other countries. We all know
there are people who are greedy, and all
the Acts of Parliament you could pass
would not be able to control these people
who are out to make money quickly. It
is not only the ordinary landlords who are
to blame, because, unfortunately, a great
many people who sub-let portions of their
houses are really worse in regard to
profiteering than the landlords.
see how
I cannot for the life of me
these people can be prevented doing what
they are doing. I remember one case of
a poor man who came to me one Sunday
morning asking for help. He was an
engineer, belonging to the same trade as
myself, and he told me he had no food
in the house, and that he was paying to
a riveter 10s a week for the upper portion of the house. One would naturally
have expected that a fellow tradesman
would have been more sympathetic towards
a worker who had no food in the house,
and that he would not have charged him
such an exorbitant rent for a small apartment, but such was the case. It is unfortunate that we in this country, like
other countries, should have in our midst
such greedy and unscrupulous persons.
I noticed that Senator Dorman gave
figures applying to a provincial town.
which were even worse than the Belfast
cases he quoted. I am pleased that he
has not confined his charges entirely to
Belfast. We have got to recognise that
there are a number of people who are
continuously imposing on their fellows
in this way, and I am sorry that such a
thing does occur.
But, after all, has the Government not
done all it possibly can do to relieve the
housing congestion. It is a case of supply
and demand, and the Government has
done a great deal to encourage the building of houses. The Corporation has endeavoured to build as many houses as possible, and I do not know what more can
be done. The Corporation have erected
houses on the Seaview site which are let
at 8s 9d a week, and the demand for
those houses is not from the people who
have no dwelling, but from people who
are in better class and larger houses who
are anxious to live in houses in such a
nice healthy locality and at a cheap rent.
As far as the Government is concerned,
I think they have done all they possibly
can to satisfy the demand for houses.
They have confined their efforts to encouraging builders to erect the smaller
type of houses suitable for the working
classes.
I
So far as business premises are concerned, there will always be competition
for them, and a person who wants business premises in a certain locality is ready
and willing to pay a good rent for them.
That, I suppose, will always be the case,
and it applied long before the war.
agree with Senator Dorman that it is terrible to think that some people, especially
our own fellow-workmen, are so inhuman
as to impose on their fellows in the way
referred to this afternoon, but I am satisfied that both the Government and the
Corporation have done all they can do to
ease the situation.
Mr. BARCLAY: While I sympathise
with the case put forward by Senator
Dorman, and while I agree that there is
a certain amount of profiteering, I cannot
Profiteering in Rents
Profiteering in Rents
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
see what benefit it would be to pass this
Resolution, or what benefit would result
from the setting up of a fair rent court.
And I would say again that it is not
altogether the landlord that is to blame
for those increases of rent and those cases
of profiteering. I will give you one instance in North Belfast of a house that
became vacant some time ago. Previous to
its becoming vacant the rent of it was 12s
per week. A prospective tenant came along
and offered 15s, and the next evening
another tenant came along and offered
18s. Then the following day another
came along and offered £1.
Of course
the landlord let the house at £1 a week.
Could you fault the landlord for not refusing to take £1 a week that was offered
to him?
There is a great deal of what is called
profiteering due to the house hunters and
people wanting houses. They will give
practically anything for the house so long.
as they get into it. There was a case
before the court the other day of a man
who took a house and gave £40 for the
key, and he was willing also to pay an
increase of rent for the house. And then
when he had got into the house he began
to think to himself that he had played the
fool and so he took law proceedings
against the landlord. I think six months
is given to make application for keymoney that has been demanded. The result was that this tenant was over the six
months and he lost his £40, but he recovered the extra rent. The reason I
mention that is to show that this man
was willing to give £40 for the key and
was willing to pay an increase of rent
until he got into the house. Then when
he got in he refused to keep the contract
which he had made.
I cannot really support this Resolution.
I am sorry I cannot because it only takes
in a very small circle. If it had taken
in the rural districts I might have been
encouraged to support it more wholeheartedly.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I have listened with
very great care to Senator Dorman's appeal to this House. I am rather struck
with the figures, but it would not appear,
I think, to this House that these figures
would show that the landlords are responsible. A man takes over a house and
pays £30 a year for it to the landlord or
agent. He sublets that house to six or
seven tenants, and in some cases receives
10s, 12s or, perhaps, almost £1 a week
for an apartment. Why do these people
take this room in which six people have
to reside and pay 10s or 12s for it? Our
Corporation have built some beautiful
houses at Seaview, as has been referred to
by Senator Cunningham, at 8s 9d a week.
Why do they not make an effort in some
part of the city to get a house instead of
paying enormous rents of 9s, 10s or 12s
as we have heard? Senator Dorman has
quoted, I think, all the cases in Belfast.
Mr. DORMAN: No, no.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I think he really has
quoted all the cases in Belfast of this
kind. We have got no figures regarding
similar cases in Dublin, Manchester, or
Liverpool. I think, on the whole, the
Government have done a great deal in regard to the building of houses in the city,
and made it easy for the people. I have
not myself participated. I was too late
in regard to the kind of house I required.
But yet at the present time our people,
if they wished, could build and get assistance to build, because the Corporation
have not ceased to build. I do not know
whether or not Senator Dorman has seen
these houses referred to. If he has I ask
why do these people pay 10s a week for a
small room. Of course it is in the centre
of the city.
But it cannot be stated that
the landlord or his agent is concerned in
those cases. It is the poor taking advantage of the poor, and I think it is a very
eat pity; but I do not see how we can
support this Resolution.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: While
every fair-minded person on either side of
the House must deplore deeply the conditions mentioned by the mover of the Resolution, it is hard to think that better
conditions would be obtained by the
means which he has adopted, that is to
say, the setting up of fair rent courts.
That is, quite apart from the point raised
by Senator Leslie as to whether or not
the Government could institute such a
thing under the Government of Ireland
Act. The effect of a fair rent court would
be that no man would be likely to build
a house, and if once the principle was
introduced it would be difficult to know
where to stop. The price of building
material, bricks and mortar, would have
to be investigated and regulated as well.
12 MARCH 1950
Profiteering in Rents

Adjournment
tion against sub-letting imposed on purchasers under the Land Purchase Acts.
They point out that in the absence of such
restrictions the relationship of landlord
and tenant would again arise, bearing in
its train exactly the same problems as
trouble us at the moment.
It must be remembered that there is no
analogy between fixing rents for houses
and fixing fair rents for agricultural land.
The quantity of land available is fixed by
nature, whereas the number of houses
built depends on the willingness of people
to invest capital in the business, and unless they get an adequate return they will
not build. The amelioration of the conditions referred to I think entirely depends upon the remedy for the housing
shortage which the Government has taken
a great step towards effecting.
In connection with this matter, I would
also refer the hon. Senator to the Report
of the Committee on the Law of Landlord
and Tenant-page 11-where it is stated
that the witnesses who appeared before
them in support of this proposal had not
apparently considered fully the
consequences, especially where there was subletting. The Committee state that it became evident that those who advocated
fair rents on the analogy of the Land Purchase Acts in agricultural holdings would
not have accepted willingly the restric-

to encourage building till so many houses

the demand.
With regard to business premises, one
of which Senator Dorman mentioned, the
matter of amending the law relating to
landlord and tenant has been the subject
of a report by a recent Departmental
Committee, and it is now under consideration by the Government, and a Bill on the
subject was referred to in the King's
Speech.

Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,

Questions to Minister.
UNEMPLOYMENT BENEFITS-
REPORTS FROM EMPLOYERS.
The following Question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr.
Quigley :-
To ask the Leader of the House if he
can state what action is taken with regard
to an employer when it is found on appeal
to the Court of Referees that an untruthful, misleading, or unfounded report received from him has caused the applicant
for unemployment benefit to be notified
by the Insurance Officer that he or she is
disqualified for payment for a period of
six weeks owing to misconduct.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I am moved to ask
this question in the interests of the working people. I have had experience of a
Court of Referees as the representative of
working people who have been making
claims for unemployment benefit. Frequently reports are submitted at that
Court from employers regarding employees who have been dismissed for
various causes such as unsatisfactory work,
bad time-keeping, leaving their employment, and, in some cases, for refusing
employment. It happens that some of
those reports are questioned by the applicants. I admit that if those reports from
employers are correct unemployment benefit could not be paid.
I would like to bring before the House
one recent specific case that came under
my notice where the report from the employer was not in accordance with the
actual facts. A workman was employed
in a city firm for six months. Owing to
bad trade his employer had to dispense
with his services. He gave that workman a most excellent discharge, a discharge, indeed, that any workman might
be proud of. I have the original
discharge here and I will read it, omitting
the employer's name and that of the workman.
The bearer, Mr. X, has been employed by me as a general man for the
past six months. During this period he
has been most attentive to his duties
and I am now compelled to dispense
with his services owing to slackness of
work. I have much pleasure in recommending him to anyone or answering
any question regarding him at any
time in the future.
nor
Nothwithstanding that excellent discharge, the workman referred to received
a notification from the insurance officer
that under Section 8, paragraph (2) of the
Unemployment Insurance Act he was disqualified for unemployment benefit for six
weeks. The workman came to me and
said he could not understand why his employment benefit had been withheld,
could I. After reading that letter of discharge and inspecting the notification.
from the insurance officer I advised the
workman to appeal to the Court of Referees, and I told him that if he did so
I would attend the Court as his representative. I will now give the House the
reason why the insurance officer had to
notify this man that he was disqualified
from receiving benefit for six weeks.
26 MARCH 1930
Oral Answers

Oral Answers
He
We have the benefit of the presence here
this afternoon of the Minister of Labour,
whom we are very glad to welcome.
very kindly consented to attend and no
doubt he will be able to amplify my reply
and clear up any points I have omitted to
deal with.
The day after he was discharged he
called with his employer for his unemployment and health insurance cards.
His employer told him he would get his
cards to-morrow. He called the next day
and the employer put him off, but on the
third day he received his cards with not a
single stamp to his credit for the six
months that he was in the employment
of this employer. In consequence the
man had no alternative but to report the
matter to the Ministry of Labour, with the
result that the employer had to pay up.
It was not until after this that the untruthful report regarding the employee
was sent in by the employer to the insurance officer. I attended the Court of
Referees with the applicant and I put
forward his case to the chairman, explaining the entire facts. The chairman of the
Court at once decided in favour of the
man, and allowed his unemployment benefit from the date of his discharge. The
important point is that if that workman
had not appealed to the Court of Referees
he would probably to-day be without his
unemployment benefit. I might add that
application was made to the chairman of
the Court of Referees for that report of
the employer so that a prosecution might
be taken against him, but, of course, the
chairman could not agree to hand over the
report.
Viscount BANGOR: I have been asked
by my right hon. Friend, the Leader of
the House, to reply to this question. I
am glad to say that the records of the
Ministry of Labour do not disclose any
cases in which an employer has maliciously
communicated wrong information to the
Court of Referees in regard to the circumstances relating to the termination of a
workman's employment. There have been
cases in which the Courts of Referees or
the umpire have considered that the conduct which led to the termination of employment did not amount to "misconduct'
to the extent that disqualifies a claimant
for benefit, but in none of these cases did
either the Court or the umpire express
any suspicion that the information was
given by the employer in bad faith. The
Ministry has no power to proceed against.
an employer who may have made an untruthful, misleading or unfounded statement in this connection, but, if the applicant for benefit is of opinion that such a
statement has been made maliciously, it
is open to him to bring an action for libel
against the employer.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): I have been asked by the
noble Lord who is acting as the Leader
of the House to reply to the speech of the
hon. Senator (Mr. Quigley). I would like
in the first place to congratulate him, if
I may, on the very able and impressive
way in which he has presented the case.
I must say first of all that I have really
no powers to deal with an employer who
has acted in the way it has been pointed
out to me this particular employer has
done. I hold no brief in his defence. If
an employer has acted in this way I think
he has been guilty of a very shameful act
and I deprecate it very much. While
that is so, I would point out that no injustice has been done to the claimant except to the extent that for a week or two
he was deprived of benefit which, when his
case was afterwards heard by the Court of
Referees, he received. I think that proves
how carefully these matters are investigated by these Courts, because if I admit
on the facts as presented by Senator
Quigley that an injustice was done in the
first instance, that wrong was righted a
very short time afterwards and the claimant received the benefit to which he was
entitled.
We have very few complaints of this
kind. I think this is the first that has
ever been brought before my notice, and
I hope it will be the last. Under the recent Act which was passed by this House
only a few weeks ago even the small injustice which has been done to this
claimant could not have taken place, because now the insurance officer has no
power to disallow any claim. All that he
can do is to refer the claim to the Court
to investigate the matter fully as soon as
it is possible for the Court to do so. I
do not at all object to the hon. Member
having brought this matter forward, because it is the wish of the Government
that justice should be done to all claimants, and that every claimant should get
the benefit to which he or she is entitled.
At the same time it is the duty of the
Department to see that those whom the
law does not allow to get benefit do not
receive it. It is a difficult matter to ad-
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Andrews]
minister, as the hon. Member knows, but
I can assure the House that the Department is doing everything possible to see
that the law is complied with, and the
Courts, I think the hon. Member will
admit, discharge their very difficult and
responsible duties with great fairness and
impartiality.
COMPOSITION OF REGIONAL
COMMITTEES.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House if he can state (1) the total
membership of each Education Committee and Regional Education Committee in
Northern Ireland, viz., in Belfast County
Borough, Londonderry County Borough,
Antrim Region, Ballymena, Larne, Lisburn and Belfast, North Antrim,
Armagh, Down, Fermanagh, Coleraine,
Londonderry and Limavady, Magherafelt, Clogher, Cookstown, Dungannon,
Omagh, Strabane and Castlederg; (2)
the number of Catholic members of each
such Committee; (3) what steps have been
taken or directions issued by the Ministry
of Education since the enactment of the
Education Act, 1925, to facilitate the
ving of the religious instruction known
to some as "simple Bible teaching" in
provided and transferred schools; (4)
the number of elementary schools under
Catholic management which have been
transferred to Education Authorities
under the Education Act, 1923, and the
number of such schools under non-
Catholic management which have been so
transferred.
Viscount BANGOR: I have been also
asked by my right hon. Friend, the
Leader of the House, to reply to this
question. The full details of the answer
to the first part of the question are in the
form of a table, which will be printed in
the official report; I am also sending a
copy of this table to the hon. and learned
Member. The number of members on
each Education Committee varies considerably; there are as many as 35 members on the Down County Regional
Education Committee, and as few as 13
on the Clogher Committee.
In reply to the second part, no information is collected as to the religious persuasion of members of these public Committees.
In answer to the third part of the
question, a circular letter was issued by
the Ministry on 4th July, 1925, to
County Borough and Regional Education Committees in which it was stated
that the Minister was advised that an
Education Authority may, if they so
desire, adopt a programme of simple
Bible instruction to be given by the
teachers in any or all of the provided and
transferred schools under their management in the period set apart in the Time
Table of the school for the purpose of religious instruction, provided that such
programme shall not include instruction
according to the tenets distinctive of any
religious denomination.
Attention was also drawn to the
following considerations:-
(1) Any arrangement for undenominational religious instruction of the kind
referred to does not restrict in any way
the freedom of the teacher to give
denominational instruction as provided
in section 26 of the Act.
(2) Such an arrangement imposes no
obligation on the teacher to give denominational instruction.
(3) Provided a teacher can show
that he has given instruction, in a
bona-fide manner, in accordance with
the programme of Bible instruction
adopted by the Education Authority,
he shall not be liable to dismissal on
grounds connected with the efficiency
of his work in giving such instruction.
(4) No pupil shall be under any
obligation to attend school while the
instruction so arranged is proceeding.
I am also sending a copy of this
circular letter to the hon. and learned
Member.
A number of references have since been
made by the Ministry to simple Bible instruction in various official publications
and documents; these are all based on, or
are in terms similar to the views expressed
in the circular letter to which I have referred.
In reply to the last part of the question, 430 schools have already been
transferred to Education Authorities.
None of these schools at the time of transfer had a Roman Catholic clergyman as
Manager. In the case of four schools the
transferring trustees included Roman
Catholic clergymen.
Oral Answers
Messages
26 MARCH 1930
at E
Mr. CAMPBELL: Arising out of the
answer to the second part of my question,
I wish to ask the noble Lord if is not a
fact perfectly well known to the Ministry
of Education and the Government that
not two per cent. of the members of these
committees represent the Catholic population of this Province, and if that is
the reason why the information asked for
is not forthcoming. In regard to the
answer to the third part of my question,
which again is no answer--
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member
must not make a speech.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I was going to ask
if the Government still persist in the view
that simple Bible teaching is not denominational teaching.
Viscount BANGOR: The reply I gave
to the second part of the hon. and learned
Member's question was that no information is forthcoming. I cannot go further
than that.
Mr. CAMPBELL: It is most unsatisfactory.
Viscount BANGOR: We really cannot
go into each Committee and find out what
religious denomination each member belongs to. It is not a religious meeting,
it is an education committee.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think the information is perfectly well known to the Department.
Viscount BANGOR: As to the other
part of the question about simple Bible
teaching, I think the reply I gave, together with the circular which will be sent
to the hon. and learned Member, will
answer his question. As I pointed out in
my reply, no pupil is under any obligation
to attend school while the instruction so
arranged is proceeding. Therefore I cannot see where the grievance is.

MESSAGE FROM THE GOVERNOR.
Viscount BANGOR: I have to report
to the House that a Privy Councillor of
Northern Ireland has waited on his Grace
the Governor with the Address from the
Senate, to which his Grace has been
pleased to return the following answer :-
I have received with great satisfaction the expression of your thanks for
the Speech with which I have opened
the present Session of Parliament.
MESSAGE FROM THE COMMONS.
The Commons have read a Second time
the Belfast Corporation Bill, the Larne
Urban District Council Bill, and the
Mater Infirmorum Hospital Incorporated (Extension) Bill.
They have passed the Consolidated.
Fund Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
59 Consolidated Fund Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
Consolidated Fund Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount BANGOR: In moving the
Second Reading of the Consolidated Fund
Bill I would like to point out that it is
a Money Bill and is therefore not subject
to Amendment by this House. The objects of the Bill are explicitly set forth
in the Clauses and I do not think they
call for any further explanation on my
part.
Mr. CAMPBELL: There is one aspect
of this Bill that interests some Members
of this House. That is on the fact that
the British Treasury last year made a
grant of £369,000 to keep the Northern
Ireland Unemployment Insurance Fund
on its feet. The Imperial Government in
the same year acquiesced, as they were
bound to do under the Government of
Ireland Act, 1920, in the Northern Ireland Exchequer keeping land purchase
annuities amounting to £665,000, while
exacting these annuities from the other
These
twenty-six counties of Ireland.
two items make over a million pounds.
The Imperial Government is at the same
time spending large sums of money on
buildings and works in Northern Ireland, and the net contribution from Northern Ireland to the Imperial Services is a
dwindling sum. Some of us on these
benches are concerned to know whether
in the net result Great Britain is any
richer by its connection with Northern
Ireland.
There is another aspect of this Bill
which also interests some of us. Are we
in Northern Ireland any the richer, to
put the converse question, as a result of
the same connection? Year by year our
expenditure is increasing, and the claims
on our Consolidated Fund are mounting
up steadily year by year. We are maintaining all the pomp and paraphernalia
of a semi-sovereign State in a territory
smaller than one English county, the
County of Yorkshire, and smaller than
the two Irish counties of Cork and
Galway combined. What industry in
all the long list of industries in Northern Ireland is faring better to-day
than it did before the Government of
Northern Ireland was established? I venture to say not one. Perhaps I should have
excepted one industry, that of the emigration ship, which is brisk. Men, women,
and children are streaming from Northern Ireland to Canada and Australia.
Quite recently in Belfast there was a very
important State affair, and on the same
identical day four liners were anchored
in Belfast Lough waiting to take out to
Canada their quotas of Ulster emigrants.
These services are mounting up, this expenditure is being piled high, and we in
Northern Ireland are not holding our
own.
If we are holding our own we are doing
so very much in the sense in which once an
American regiment was holding its own.
It was marching over prairie lands on a
hot day, and the men were tired and
thirsty and anxious to get to the next
town as soon as possible. A rancher
rode up and the commander asked him
how far it was to the next town. He said
it was only a couple of miles, so the men
marched on for an hour until another
rancher appeared. The same question was
put to him and he replied that the next
town was only two miles off. They continued to march for another half-hour
when they met a third rancher to whom
the same question was put. He assured
them that the next town was two miles
distant and no more. "Well," said an
optimistic sergeant, "thank Heaven, at
all events we are holding our own."
in Northern Ireland are not holding our
own even to that extent.
Colonel
We
PAKENHAM: This Bill
affords one an opportunity for a bit of a
grumble. I notice that the Member who
preceded me had a grumble, and I want
to say a few words on the question of the
treatment of blind children over school
age. Last Session I asked a question
about this, and I was assured the Education Department looked after children
until they were sixteen years of age, and
after that they were left to either private
benevolence or to some of the county
councils. I noticed that in another place
the Minister of Home Affairs in answer to
a question last week assured the questioner
that he had no intention of setting up ar
Advisory Committee for dealing with the
blind, and he said that if such a Committee was considered necessary it could
be set up by the county councils themselves. Last Session there was a project
for the amalgamation of county council
schemes for the treatment of blind
children, but I do not think it has come
to anything. If it had the Minister of
Home Affairs would not have given the
reply he did in another place.
Consolidated Fund Bill
Adjournment
26 MARCH 1930
we
0:
I
C
As the Government has treated large
numbers of unemployed with generosity,
as far as their means allow, I do think it
is up to them to take some steps
to provide for the future welfare
of these unfortunate children who
start life with no hope, and for whom
nothing is done. I do not think it is a
big matter, because there are not a great
number of these children in Northern Ireland, but at the same time it would be but
a matter of justice to them if some definite action was taken by the Government
on their behalf, and I appeal to the Government very strongly to take such action.

Criminal Law Bill-
Second Reading
1 APRIL 1930

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 1st April, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Criminal Law and Prevention of Crime
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): This Bill deals with two
matters. Firstly, the amendment of the
law in regard to offences against persons
under the age of 16; and, secondly, a
matter in connection with disciplinary
powers in Borstal Institutions.
The first point is dealt with in Clause 1
of the Bill. In the Criminal Law (Amendment) Act of 1885 it was laid down that
prosecutions for sexual offences against
girls between 13 and 16 years of age must
be instituted within three months of the
date of the commission of the offence. This
time limit was found to be too short, and
in 1904 it was extended by the Imperial
Parliament to six months, and again in
1922 to nine months. Our Parliament
here adopted the latter extension by an
Act passed in 1923.
Two years ago the Imperial Parliament
again considered the matter, and further
extended the period to twelve months for
reasons which I think must be obvious to
hon. Members. It is most desirable that
the law here should be similar to that in
Great Britain, and, apart from this, I am
sure the House will agree that on its own
merits this is a very desirable Amendment.
The second part of the Bill deals with
the question of corporal punishment in
Borstal Institutions. The position at the
moment in Northern Ireland is that there
is no power to resort to the cane as a means
of enforcing discipline. This power does
exist in Borstal Institutions in England,
and it will be within the painful recollection of hon. Members that it is occasionally
practised in our ordinary schools.
Clause does not mean that flogging or
birching will be made possible, but merely
that the usual form of punishment with
the cane will be allowable, and that only
in accordance with the regulations which
The
will be drawn up by the Ministry of Home
Affairs and with the sanction of two members of the Visiting Committee of the
Institution.
The regulations will ensure

that any
caning which has to be administered will
be applied in the proper place-my brief
says in," but I mean "on" the proper
place and will only be given after a
medical officer has certified that the boy is
fit to receive such punishment. Some
comment has been made in another place
that two members of the visiting committee is too small a minimum number
for ordering punishment, but I might explain that the ordinary practice of the
committee is that they meet monthly and
then appoint a visiting rota of two members to visit the school during the month
and to deal with any complaints, etc. It
is arranged that all the members do this
duty in turn, fresh names being taken
each month from the full list of the Visiting Committee.
I might mention a small Amendment
which the Government proposes to
introduce in the Committee stage. It is
the addition of the word "reasonable" in
line 10 of page 2 before the words "corporal punishment." The object of this
Amendment is, of course, to ensure that
any punishment which may be given may
be subject to an interpretation of what is
reasonable under the circumstances. I
beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this Bill be read a Second time this
day six months.' The first Clause of this
Bill is quite acceptable, but the Bill itself
is very exceptional. The subject matter
of the first and second Clauses is entirely
disconnected, and I have a very strong objection to the second Clause. I submit to
the House that it is a most re-actionary
Clause and that it is a step backwards.
Whipping has been almost abolished in
these Islands as a punishment for crime,
and here it is being introduced as a
punishment of these lads who are really
waifs and strays in the custody of the
State. Public opinion has long set in
against whipping as a punishment of the
child delinquent, and I notice that a proposed Bill promoted by a non-party Committee, known as the Penal Reform
Group is now under the consideration of the British Home Office.
It proposes amendments to the Children Act of 1908, one of which
is that whipping shall be abolished.
B
Criminal Law Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
That
for children and young persons.
is the proposal of a non-party Committee, and if it becomes law whipping
will cease in England in the case of
offences by children and young persons.
No justification has been given for a retrograde step of the kind proposed in this
Bill, which will have no reformative influence. I beg to move.
Mr. McHUGH: I second the Motion
proposed by the hon. and learned
Senator.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to welcome this
Bill, and particularly the first Clause,
which deals with a matter which I have
myself directed attention to more than
once. It has long been felt, as the noble
Viscount said in moving the Second Reading, that dissimilarity in the law dealing
with the same unpleasant subject between
Great Britain and Northern Ireland was
very unfortunate and a very undesirable
thing, and I am glad that steps are now
being taken to rectify it. An hon. Mema
ber who sat for some years in another place
and who has now passed to his rest, who
sat as representing the University, and who
had previously practiced for some years
as Coroner and Dispensary officer in the
County of Derry, more than once, I think,
drew attention to the difficulties which
were experienced in administering the law
owing to the limit of the time for prosecution being too short. I am glad we are
now doing away with that.
The extension of the date brings with
it certain little difficulties of its own, and
I think I shall have in the Committee stage
once more to direct the attention of
Parliament to the desirability of confining
jurisdiction in these matters to Courts of
Assize, as is done in England, rather than
leaving them to be dealt with either by the
Assize Court or the Court of Quarter Session, as circumstances dictate. It will be
obvious, I think, that any risk there may
be of blackmail-and I do not think it is
a serious risk-is necessarily increased by
the extension of three months in the time,
and it is also, perhaps, increased by the
fact that during these twelve months it
may be that physical results have taken
place which render it more desirable perhaps on the part of the evildoer to put
the responsibility on someone else.
England, as I have said, all jurisdiction
under the Criminal Law Amendment Act,
In
1885, is taken away from Quarter Sessions,
and I think, perhaps, it would be well if
we followed that example. Circumstances
have changed considerably since 1923,
when I last directed attention to this
matter, and I think from a passage in the
Gracious Speech circumstances may change
still further in the future.
It will perhaps be necessary, if the Committee decide
to follow the English example, to make a
trivial alteration in the Title of the Bill.
The
Now I pass to the Second Clause of the
Bill, and I may say at once that I listened
with interest to the hon. and learned
Member (Mr. Campbell) when he pointed
out that this Bill dealt with two completely
disconnected subjects. I think there is
ample precedent for that course in the
Bills dealing with criminal matters.
precedent that occurred to me first of all
was the Act of 1912 which extended and
amended the Criminal Law Amendment
Act of 1885, and the second and perhaps
the better precedent is the Criminal Justice Administration Act to which most of
us will have referred in connection with
the second Clause of this Bill. There is
no objection that I can see from that point
of view to bringing in a Bill which deals
with two or half a dozen subjects.
On the general objection to this Clause,
I am quite unable to agree with the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell). I
think that anybody who will think over
what Borstal Institutions are for and the
class of persons who go to them, and who
will further consider the offences they commit, must feel that every means ought to
be taken to avoid having recourse to what
is the last expedient of maintaining discipline in those institutions.
It will be remembered that there
is a power now resting in the Secretary
of State, as named in the original
Act, but the Ministry of Home
Affairs now, of removing an offender from
these institutions and sending him back to
prison. It is surely desirable to do everything possible to avoid having to take that
step, and the steps proposed are really
nothing more than has been alluded to as
the steps taken to control a very different
class of offender in other institutions.
think you will remember that what is
known as sors tertia is still recognised in
every first-class educational establishment
across the water, and is likely, I believe, to
continue.
I
On the other hand it is obvious that any
corporal punishment in Borstal institutions
Criminal Law Bill-
Second Reading
1 APRIL 1930
must be administered with care. The ages
of the subjects range normally from 16 to
21, but they may extend up to 24, because
a young fellow of nearly 21 may be sent
there for three years. If the special
powers conferred in the original Act have
ever been put into force it is possible that
the age might go as far as 26, because
there is the power of extending Borstal
treatment to a young man, or for that
matter to a young person, who is apparently under the age of 23, and it is obvious
that corporal punishment by means of the
cane to be administered to an adult can
only be done after careful consideration.
But most of us will agree that two visiting
justices are about as good a court as could
be selected to decide as to the propriety of
that punishment, and they are a more acceptable court than a boy at one of the
English public schools would get.
The Clause has, I know, been attacked,
and I think perhaps not altogether unjustly attacked, on the ground that the
Clause itself does not give any definition
of corporal punishment. The noble Viscount has said that it is proposed to use
the cane, but the Clause itself does not say
so. It simply uses the expression "corporal
punishment," and that is not defined anywhere. It is used in numerous Acts dealing with prisons, and when so used it does
sometimes include the use of the cat-ofnine-tails. That, of course, is not the intention here, and I am not satisfied
at the moment that the sub-section of the
Act of 1908 referred to in the Clause goes
quite far enough and gives the necessary
powers to the Minister to make regulations.
I therefore propose at a later stage to suggest the addition of some few words which
may clear up the matter, or at all events
serve the purpose of enabling the noble
Viscount to tell us that the powers of
regulation do in fact exist.
The point is that our whole prison code
has in the course of years become very
complicated, and it is rather difficult to
trace out the origin of this or that authority. In fact, so much is that impressed
upon me that I am inclined to suggest that
we should take the opportunity afforded
by this Bill to introduce a short Borstal
code of our own, following the practice
which is adopted with other codes of legislation when the number of Acts dealing
with the subject becomes extensive. That
could be done, of course, quite easily by
means of a short Schedule. On the ground
that the first Clause meets a want that
should have been met before, and that the
second Clause devises a means of preventing recourse to relegating boys from a
Borstal institution to prison and so losing
the merits of Borstal institutions, I shall
support the Bill. Borstal institutions
have commended themselves for years past
to our judges, for they again and again
send boys there, and the records prove
that in spite of the unpromising nature of
the material the percentage of success is
unexpectedly high. It would be the
greatest possible mistake to do anything
which would prevent boys staying at a
Borstal institution for the full time, and
to prevent the possibility of that I shall
vote for the Bill as it stands.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
welcome this Bill, and I think that when
we are dealing with schools of this nature
we must allow a certain amount of discretion. In most homes corporal punishment is not looked upon as a hardship.
There is one thing I should like the
Government to do under this Bill, and that
is to see that a register should be kept in
which should be recorded all punishment
that is inflicted. Then if it was found
that one class of boy was always being
punished some other remedy might be
tried and the boy might be treated differently. I also hope that the Government will take the same step in connection
with ordinary schools, and that the master
will be compelled to record in a register
each case of caning a pupil. My experience has been that sometimes the
master was as much to blame as the boy,
and if it was found that a master was always punishing one particular set of boys
we might begin to ask whether it was the
fault of the master or the boys. I would
ask the noble Leader of the House to see
if it would be possible to have such a
reigster kept in the schools so that we
might be able to see whether it was always
the poor boy who was punished, while the
boy of influential parents was passed by.
I cordially support the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: Corporal punishment
of all kinds is in my opinion a relic of
barbarism. Civilised man has been passing away rapidly from the infliction of
physical suffering upon his fellow creatures
as a means of reparation for wrong and
also as a deterrent from wrong-doing in
the future. There is not a man, including
even Members of Parliament who does not
possess a vestige of his savage ancestors
that will at times assert itself in the character of the best of men. For fifty years
Criminal Law Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
I have concentrated my thoughts upon a
study of this particular subject. I have
had unique opportunities of moving in and
out of the homes of the various classes in
the community, and I have lived long
enough to observe the results of physical
suffering inflicted as a corrective even on
the offspring by the parent. Before that
I had experience of serving in His
Majesty's Navy for a period of ten years
and I have witnessed corporal punishment
of every description inflicted upon offenders
in the Service at that particular time.
an
I have never known a case of corporal
punishment being inflicted upon
offender as a corrective that ultimately
resulted in the permanent reformation
of that particular individual, or in
giving any restitution to the offended
During the past week I was
person.
speaking to a well known and most respectable professional man whose father
pious individual
a very pious
was
was
and
old
whose relationship with his offspring
mainly inspired by that
proverb, Spare the rod and spoil
the child." I
assure this House
that it would bring sadness to the
heart of anyone to hear that man, now
about sixty years of age, expressing his
feelings produced by the severe treatment on the part of his father who sent
him upstairs to strip in order that he
might be caned for some trivial juvenile
delinquency he had been guilty of during
the week. After chastising him that
father would bring him down from the
room for family worship. It is something lamentable to behold when anyone
to-day mentions the memory of his
father to that man.
It has been stated that this particular
institution is well conducted, and that
there has been no outbreak so far justifying corporal punishment, with the exception of a boy breaking a window. My
memory goes back to the early seventies
of the last century. I was serving in the
Navy, in a ship at Plymouth, and a
draft of boys was sent up to the training
ship Impregnable in order to be
firmed. When they came back there was
one short, and we all wondered what had
occurred. What had happened was this.
When the reverend gentleman administering the Holy Communion, after the
confirmation of the boys, was passing
round the Holy Communion cup he
happened to fill it and hand it to a parconticular boy. The boy drank it all up
and said, "Fill it again please." That
boy was marked down and corporal
punishment was inflicted upon him.
That is well over fifty years ago. There
were from 400 to 800 boys in training in
the ships Impregnable, Circe, and
Foudroyant, and that man did more harm
to the cause of true religion in the minds
of those youths than he would undo if he
lived to the age of Methuselah. I will
not attempt to repeat in this House the
language used by that boy with regard
to that reverend gentleman. Suffice it
to say that he was called a consummate
hypocrite for professing to be a teacher
of the Prince of Peace, and at the same
time inflicting this corporal punishment
on this boy.
It has also been stated that the Visiting Committee of this institution have
been unanimous in recommending to the
Government the infliction of this corporal punishment. I would make bold
to say that if any Member of this House
would take the trouble to proceed to that
institution and interview any of the boys
who have a scintilla of human sympathy,
if they honestly expressed their opinion
they would declare that this Visiting
Committee had declined in their estimation to an enormous extent by reason of
their having recommended this corporal
What does it mean?
punishment.
It
means that when a boy is caned on the
buttocks only one garment is left between the weapon and his body. It
means that probably six, or eight, or
twelve weals as large as that knife will
be left upon the buttocks of that boy
and will remain there for days. Probably the members of this particular
Committee are subscribers to and
speakers for the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals, and if
they had brought before them in a
Petty Sessions Court in Belfast any lad
for inflicting contusions on the body of
a beast of burden they would probably
send him to gaol for a month.
If
We live in a paradoxical world.
we want to know what the human race
is capable of so far as corporal punishment is concerned the best thing we can
do is to read the history of this question.
I have very great pleasure in supporting
the Motion for the rejection of this Bill,
and I may have something more to say
on the subject on the Bill introduced by
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell).
Criminal Law Bill-
Second Reading
1 APRIL 1930
Mr. McALLISTER: I do not intend to intervene in this debate except
to make a personal explanation which
may seem more or less inconsistent. I
am a member of the Committee of the
Borstal Institution that is referred to.
I was not present at the meeting of the
Committee at which these recommendations were made, and I am not going to
pass any censure or express any opinion
on the action of the Committee, because
the proper place to do that was at the
meeting. No matter what my own personal views may be, I feel I could not
consistently be a member of that Committee and oppose by my vote in this
House the recommendations of that
Committee. A suggestion has been
made that these boys should be visited
and consulted as to their views on the
action of the Committee in recommending corporal punishment. In my visits
to that institution I must say that I was
struck, and greatly struck, by the spirit
of good feeling that seemed to prevail
between the boys and those responsible
for the discipline and administration of
that institution.
I have been through every part of
that school, and I did not see a single
sign of complaint on the part of any of
the boys. I think it is only fair to say
that. Whilst I feel that I cannot vote
against the recommendations of the Visiting Committee of which I am a member,
at the same time I must take into consideration the fact that up to a short
time ago I represented similar interests
in another place, and that my colleagues.
there, voicing the feelings of those they
represent, felt constrained to oppose this
Bill. Therefore, while I do not like to
sit silent and not express my views on
any question before the House, I feel
that under the circumstances I have
mentioned I must on this occasion play
a neutral part and not record any vote.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In all
these questions of caning or not caning
in large institutions one must have a
certain amount of common
sense and
remember that this Borstal institution
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Bangor, The Viscount
I
CONTENT
Dorman, Mr. R.
deals with a class of boy who is very
often not amenable to discipline of any
kind. Without having this power in the
background it would very often be more
difficult to maintain discipline than it
is at present. I think the Visiting
Committee were quite unanimous in
their recommendations, and although it
is quite possible that the boys of the institution may regard the members of
that Committee with less friendly feelings than they have hitherto done in
consequence of their decision, that does
not mean that the decision of the Committee is unjust.
With regard to the suggestion made
by Senator Samuel Cunningham, one
of the rules to be drawn
up is to
the effect that all inflictions of
corporal punishment, together with
the number of strokes ordered to be
given by the Visiting Committee,
shall be recorded by the Governor
in
the Punishment Book, and also
in the personal record of the inmate
concerned. With regard to the hon.
Senator's suggestion that all corporal
punishment given in the ordinary
schools should be marked in a book kept
for the purpose, I must say that I will
make representations to the Minister of
Education (Laughter)-because I do
not know whether such a book is kept
at present or not. I think it can be
taken for granted that no unnecessary
pain or indignity will be inflicted.
As regards the suggestion of Senator
Dorman that the infliction of corporal
punishment would produce contusions or
weals, it says in the Bill that the punishment is to be given in the place
ordained by nature for such punishment. The delinquent would also have
his clothes on, and so he would scarcely
be in the same position as an animal,
because it is unusual for an animal to go
about the streets dressed.
Question put, "That the word 'now'
be left out."
The House divided; Contents, 3; Not
Contents, 14.

The Bill was accordingly read a Second Time, and committed to
