Committee of the whole House for to-morrow.
Public Roads Bill
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Public Roads Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: This is a very
brief Amending Bill. Its object is to repeal the provision in the Grand Jury Act
of 1836, which limited the width of public
roads to 50 feet. The State of affairs in
1836 was, of course, very different from
what it is to-day and it is not anticipated
that the powers which are now sought will
be used to any great extent by local
authorities. Hon. Members will, however, understand that it may be occasionally desirable to have the power to make
or widen a road more than 50 feet in the
clear, which of course will include the
carriage way, footpaths and boundary
fences. The permanent character of our reinforced concrete surfaces makes it very desirable that they should not be broken up
again for the purpose of laying pipes or
other underground conveniences, and the
intention of allowing a greater width
than 50 feet is not so much that the road
itself should be widened beyond that limit,
but that an additional strip should be provided to take these pipes for water, gas,
electric light, etc., without disturbing the
surface.
The Bill is in no way mandatory; it
only permits local authorities to do this
if they think fit. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for to-morrow.
Abolition of Corporal Punishment Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read a Second
time." No complaint can be made as to
the length of this Bill. It consists of only
two Clauses. The first Clause states that
No male person shall upon conviction
on indictment for any offence be sentenced to corporal punshment.
The second Clause reads:-
Every enactment passed before the
passing of this Act which is inconsistent
with this Act is hereby repealed so far
as inconsistent with this Act.
Our law has abolished the rack and the
thumbscrew. It has discarded the stocks
and the treadmill. Let us excise this last
vestige of physical torture from our penal
code. The British House of Commons
has approved the principle of this Bill in
its current session. Northern Ireland
ought to keep step with Great Britain in
this matter. Flogging is neither a deterrent against crime, nor conducive to reform, nor consonant with humanity.
Most European countries have eliminated
the lash or rod from their penal codes.
In our civil courts it is never now inflicted
except under the authority of Statute,
which usually limits the number of strokes.
Flogging for breaches of discipline has
been abolished in the Army since 1881.
In prisons this punshment is now rarethis Bill does not extend to punishment
for prison offences-and arises only in
cases of mutiny or incitement to mutiny
or gross personal violence to an officer or
servant of the prison, and only when
authorised by careful authorities. In this
respect we lag behind the United States,
for if I err not only one State in the
American Union-Texas-allows prisoners
to be flogged as a means of prison discipline. But in Texas a little while ago a
small boy was sent to a reformatory for
spending his pocket money on a copy of
Shakespeare. (Laughter).
My recollection is that no offender in
Scotland above 16 years of age can be
whipped for crime against person or property, yet robbery with violence has not
been more prevalent there than in England, which has had the Garroters Act
since 1863 punishing this crime with
flogging. The punishment of whipping is
sanctioned here on conviction of certain
offences under the Malicious Damage Act,
1861, the Offences against the Person Act,
1861, the Larceny Act, 1916, the Civil
Authorities (Emergency Powers) Act,
1922, and a few other Acts. This special
punishment can thus be inflicted only in
a limited range of cases. The lash is a
survival of a code which was the severest
in Europe a little over a century ago, when
200 offences were punishable with death.
It kills self-respect in the offender,
brutalises him, and tends to make him
an irreclaimable criminal. I beg to move.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I intend to oppose this Bill. A man told me
he was in Liverpool at a time when robbery was common in the streets of the
city, and he said flogging was the first
thing to put down that crime. I am sorry
to think there are some crimes to which
flogging is not applicable as a punishment.
I think a criminal offence against a child
by a man, and the case of an old person
Second Reading

77 Corporal Punishment Bill-
1 APRIL 1930
who is robbed in his or her house and left
half-killed should be punished in part by
flogging. Hon. Members speak about
flogging degrading a person, but if it has
been found to be a deterrent to crime it
would be far better to flog a man than that
he should commit the offence a second
It has been found that the threat
time.
of flogging has kept crime down, and I
think it would be a great mistake if this
House removed that deterrent from the
Statute Book. There is another matter
I am not quite clear about. The Bill says
that no male person shall be subject to
It appears to me,
corporal punishment.
therefore, that a murderess would be excluded from the provisions of the Bill,
because if it passes I understand-although I may be wrong-there will be no
more hanging of men, but a female may
be hanged.
Mr. LESLIE: The afternoon seems to
be devoted to the discussion of punishment
which none of us desire to see inflicted.
I should myself welcome the abolition of
corporal punishment, and I hope that day
will some time come, but I hope it
will come because the offences which made
corporal punishment necessary have ceased.
Until that happens I do not think it
would be wise for society to abandon this
There are
method of discouragement.
one or two things that are perhaps worth
Occasionally murders
inquiring about.
occur. How many cases, if any, are there
on record where a convicted murderer is
a man who had previously been flogged.
How many cases
I think very, very few.
are there on record where a man who has
been flogged for robbery with violence.
again commits robbery with violence and
is again sentenced to flogging. I think
there are very few.
one case
within the
I do know of
last few years, and the hon. Senator (Mr.
Samuel Cunningham) must forgive me for
mentioning it, because it occurred in
a rule it does not
Liverpool-but as
happen. A man who is once flogged does
not offend that way again. I do not think
modern sentiment is altogether in favour
of abandoning this punishment. It may
be remembered that as recently as 1912
the scope of flogging was extended, but it
was extended to catch that kind of offender who lived on the immoral earnings
of a prostitute. My recollection isalthough I am not sure that special permission was included in that Statute to
enable the same punishment to be inflicted
in Scotland. The law of Scotland, I believe, has always differed from that in
England in this respect. I seem to recollect having read that in the days of one of
the Stuart Monarchs-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: James II.
Mr. LESLIE-
-people were removed
from England to Scotland because it was
illegal to torture them in England. The
rack and the thumbscrew did not exist in
England then, but they were legal in
I do believe that
Scotland at that time.
stocks are still a possible method of punishment, although they have been out of use
I do not quite follow
a great many years.
the hon. Senator (Mr. S. Cunningham)
in his allusion to the corporal punishment
of women. I do not think he meant to
suggest that if this Bill passed it would be
no longer possible to hang a person.
Mr. CAMPBELL: This is a Bill for
the abolition of corporal punishment, not
capital punishment.
Mr. LESLIE: Exactly. That brings
us back to the difficulty of defining corporal punishment. I think that on the
whole the House would be well advised
not to accept this Bill, and if they did
accept it I do not think they would find
that public opinion would be in support of
it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The
merits of this Bill ought to be considered
from a practical rather than from an
academic point of view. I will be able
to give some figures from which I think
the House will come to the conclusion that
in Northern Ireland at any rate corporal
punishment has proved its usefulness as a
deterrent, more especially in cases of what
might be termed "cowardly crime." No.
decent person wishes to inflict pain either
physically or by incarceration, but what
one has to consider is the good of the
greatest number. The infliction of corporal punishment may not be altogether
uplifting to the person concerned, but that
is of small consideration if the result is
I would like hon.
as I claim it has been.
Members to take particular notice of these
figures.
In January, 1922, there were 30 outrages
and there were no sentences to corporal
punishment, as it had not then been introduced. In February there were 374
outrages, in March, 523, in April. 506,
79 Corporal Punishment Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Keading
[Viscount Charlemont]
and in May, 606. In June, when corporal punishment came into operation,
there were 604 outrages and 4 floggings.
In July the number of outrages had
dropped to 487, and there were no cases
of flogging. In August and September
there were no floggings ordered, and in
October the number of offences punishable
by flogging had declined to 201, and in
that month 8 floggings were ordered. In
November there were 193 outrages, and
in December 192, but in neither month
were there any cases of flogging. Taking
the year as a whole, in 1922 there were
4,502 outrages and 12 cases of flogging;
in 1923 the number of outrages had fallen
to 1330, a diminution of nearly 3,000, and
corporal punishment was ordered in 30
cases. In 1924 there were 1,359 outrages
and no floggings were ordered. In 1925
there were only 2 cases of flogging, in
1926, 1, in 1927, 4, in 1928, 1, and in
1929, 3 out of 1,437 outrages.
One must consider the position of defenceless persons, more especially those
living in remote country districts, and it
is for offences against such persons that
in most cases corporal punishment has
been ordered. I think the figures I have
given show that while at one time there
was a very great necessity for the infliction
of this kind of punishment it has had to be
very rarely resorted to in latter years.
think the reason for this is that the potential criminal is afraid of receiving pain,
however lightly he may inflict it on others.
For this reason I think it would be a
wrong step to consider its abandonment.
I
Ordinary imprisonment has very little.
terror for the hardened criminal. It is
almost the unanimous opinion of those
engaged in the administration of the law
that the power to administer flogging is
one of the most valuable weapons for the
stamping out of such crimes as armed
robbery. It may be pointed out that a
medical certificate as to the capacity of
the prisoner to receive corporal punishment is in all cases obtained before a
prisoner is sentenced to this punishment.
While the whipping is being inflicted the
medical officer is in attendance, and if he
is of opinion that injury to health may
result he has power to remit the remainder
of the sentence. In my estimation the
sentiments underlying this Bill are false
ones, and I think the abolition of the
power to order flogging would be detrimental to the administration of justice in
this Province.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In reply to the
noble Lord, I venture to submit to the
House with confidence that the recent vote
in the British House of Commons on this
matter represents more truly enlightened
public opinion and enlightened public
sentiment to-day than the attitude taken
up by the noble Viscount, the Leader of
the House.
Mr. DORMAN: In looking at the
figures quoted by the noble Leader of the
House, I notice that in the year 1923 there
were 30 cases of flogging where there were
1,300 offences. One would anticipate if
the infliction of flogging was a deterrent
against the committal of these 1,300 crimes
that in the following year, 1924, when the
memory of the large number that had
been flogged in 1923 was known to the
community, many of the criminals who
were disposed to commit similar crimes
would be restrained from committing
them. But instead of being restrained
there was actually an increase in the
crimes of violence in the year after the
maximum amount of corporal punishment
was inflicted.
At one
This is a very old problem.
time it was thought that discipline could
not be maintained in Her Majesty's Navy
without flogging, and the Navy was remarkable for the amount of flogging. One
of the most celebrated officers, the personal friend of the celebrated and historical Lord Nelson, Lord Collingwood, in
his memoirs and correspondence has used
the expression that he could never comprehend the religion of a man who prayed
all one day and flogged his men all the
next. He was remarkable at a time when
flogging was of almost daily occurrence for
having the most disciplined and healthy
ships' crew in the British Navy in the
most unfavourable stations that ships were
accustomed to frequent at that particular
time, and as I have already suggested his
conduct has been continuously referred to
by writers on the subject of physiology
and of health.
A remarkable incident occurred while he
was serving in the Mediterranean as commander of the then "Excellent."
A man
was sent to the "Romulus" who had been
flogged on another ship, and the first
morning he was there he trained a gun on
the forecastle, filled to the mouth with
shot, towards the quarter deck and stood
by it with a live match in his hand, declaring that unless he received a promise
81 Corporal Punishn ent Bill-

1 APRIL 1930
Second Reading
that he would not be flogged in that ship
he would blow all the officers from the
ship. He was sent to Captain Collingwood on the "Excellent." The Captain's
method was this: He met the man at the
gangway with 15 or 20 of his crew, and
confronting him said: "My man, I know
you. I have perfect confidence in every
member of my crew, and if you try any
of these tricks, or attempt to incite to insubordination or mutiny, I will have you
headed up in a cask and throw you into
the sea.'
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That would
be worse than flogging.
Mr. DORMAN: Yes; that would be
worse than flogging. "But," he continued, "I shall not remember anything
you have done before. You shall be
treated as all my crew are treated." And
he sent him to his duty. What is the
moral to be drawn from this? It was this.
He made an appeal to the good that was
in the crew. He made a promise to the
man that he would treat him as he treated
these men.
That man, it is left on record
in his Lordship's memoirs, never committed a crime after that particular incident. Now, at that particular stage, as
no doubt hon. Members are aware, men
were flogged for the crime of insubordination. Boys then occupied the position of
midshipmen at the age of twelve years,
and frequently Lord Collingwood had to
inflict punishment upon his men because
it was the service punishment. If a midshipman brought a man before him and
proved that he had been guilty of insubordination he had no alternative but
to inflict the service punishment under the
Articles of War. But what was his Lordship's method? I have extracted this
from his memoirs and it will show a moral:
When a midshipman brought a complaint Captain Collingwood would order
the man for punishment the next day,
and, in the interval, calling the boy
down to him, would say, "In all probability the fault was yours, but whether
it was or not, I am sure it would go to
your heart to see a man old enough to
be your father disgraced on your account, and it will therefore give me a
good opinion of your disposition, if,
when he is brought out, you will ask
for his pardon." When this recommendation, acting as it did like an order,
was complied with, and the lad intervened for the prisoner, Captain Collingwood would make a great apparent difficulty in yielding, but at length would
say, "This young gentleman has pleaded
so humanely for you, that, in hope that
you will feel a due gratitude to him for
his benevolence, I will for this time overlook your offence."
Now Captain Collingwood had the reputation of not having an average of one
flogging in all his ships per year, when it
was a frequent and almost daily occurrence for the crime of insubordination in
the other ships of the fleet.
My memory takes me back to the first
ship in which I went to sea. I ask the
patience of hon. Members while I tell this
story. We had as captain a man of the
most splendid type, a Captain Chadwick.
This took place in the early seventies of
last century. By some unexplainable
means the rumour got around that the
I have
captain was a flogging officer.
never ascertained how that report was first
started. But before we left port for our
station in the south-east coast of South
America nearly seven per cent. of the crew
jumped the ship. At the first port we
entered several men jumped the ship. A
whole cutter's crew of twelve men and the
coxwain jumped the boat and left the
midshipman only in charge. Just at that
period a young Irish lad was down with
yellow fever and was isolated away in the
forecastle. Instead of the ship remaining
there and sending him to hospital the
captain decided to go away south to a more
equable climate and go into quarantine.
He attended that boy, and no father could
be more tender to a child. His conduct
towards that young Irish boy completely
transformed the outlook of the whole of
the crew, and they immediately refused to
believe the false report that had been disseminated about this kindly tender-hearted
man. For four years after that no men
left the ship with the exception of two
who were assisted by the crew to get off.
Mr. SPEAKER: I am rather puzzled
to see what is the connection between this
and the Bill.
ex-
Mr. DORMAN: My object is to show
that when this particular description of
punishment was inflicted upon men
tensively it had not the deterrent effect
that hon. Members expect that the continuance of flogging in our area will have
upon those disposed to commit crime. I
was speaking to a gentleman this morning.
who occupied the position of instructor for
Corporal Punishment Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
29 years in the Navy, and he told me that
he knew not a single instance during the
whole period of men being flogged and reforming. In every instance but one they
were afterwards flogged again and dismissed from the Service in disgrace. I
tell this to show that an appeal to the good
that is in men has more effect.
I do not think our learned judges ought
to be permitted to inflict corporal punishment in the shape of flogging upon a delinquent in the courts of justice without
first bringing the offended individual into
the public court and seeking his or her
not
They should
inflict
permission.
grievous bodily punishment without the
consent of the individual who has been
wronged by the offence.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise to support
this Measure. I am sure I can say with
my hon. and learned Friend (Mr. Campbell), who sits beside me, that there is no
one in the House who has more anxiety
for the preservation and observation of the
law than he or I have. But I look upon
flogging, from all I have heard about it,
as a form of torture to which no human
being should be subjected. I quite agree
with Senator Dorman when he states that
it is entirely demoralising and degrading,
and destroys the outlook of any individual
subjected to it. Any man who has the
slightest chance of reforming or with the
least ambition to improve himself and reestablish himself in civil society, after
being flogged would have that ambition
entirely destroyed.
It is bound, in my opinion, to destroy
all that is best in human nature, and when
listening to this debate to-day my mind
went back to a very recent occurrence in
Northern Ireland. We heard discussed in
another place the sentence to a term of
imprisonment on a man and the sentence
to several strokes of the lash. Fortunately
the strokes of the lash were not carried
out, but it transpired that this man was
innocent although he was found guilty
and he was granted a free pardon. Now
what a horror it would have been had
that man been subjected to the various
strokes of the lash that he was sentenced
to!
I do not see why at the present time
we in Northern Ireland should be singled
out as a people requiring special, brutalising, criminal laws established in connection with this, especially when we consider
that in practically every civilised country
in Europe this is a form of punishment.
that is not resorted to. After all, in the
Army, where the highest discipline must
be maintained and looked for, when they
found in 1881 that flogging was not necessary and was disadvantageous to the Army,
surely now in 1930 the people of Northern
Ireland as a civilised people should abolish
this form of brutalising and shocking
punishment.
or
Mr. BARCLAY: I just rise to express my humble opinion regarding this
Bill. I think it would be a serious
matter if the Government of Northern
Ireland removed from the Statute Book
corporal punishment. We have been
told a great deal this afternoon about
the pain and suffering caused to those
guilty of certain crimes. But we have
not heard very much of the pain and
suffering inflicted upon the person
persons by those who have received corporal punishment. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). We take for instance an
old man or an old woman, as somebody
has mentioned here to-day, who has a
little business in a back street or along
the country road, and some man with a
desire in his mind to commit a felony
enters that little shop and abuses that
old lady or old man and leaves them half
dead. What about the suffering of
these people? Not a word is mentioned
about that to-day. I say again it would
be a serious mistake if corporal punishment was removed from the law to-day,
because if it was, Northern Ireland
would become the happy hunting_ground
all the criminals of Europe.
for
(Laughter).
I am not the oldest man in the world
and I can look back ten years to the
time referred to by the noble Viscount
the Leader of the House-1922 and
1923, when the working men in the
shipyard and in the linen factories were
getting nervous as regards going home.
on Friday evening with their wages in
their pockets. I remember coming from
the shipyard week after week with all
these men standing with a box in their
hand and with confederates a considerable distance off. A little bit of Union
Jack was placed over this box and it was
shaken before a man's nose as he passed
up, and if he did not give something well
he was in danger perhaps of receiving
something from the men's confederates.
If this law had not been brought into
existence at that time I say here to-day
it would have been impossible for work-
85 Corporal Punishment Bill-

1 APRIL 1930
Adjournment
ing men to come home alone with their
wages in their pocket, never to speak of
business people.
I would be very sorry to know that
this corporal punishment would be removed because I believe the best way to
have peace is to be prepared for war,
and while we have this in force it will
be a preventative for those who, perhaps, are, or would be, inclined to offend
if they got off scot-free. Therefore, I
oppose this Bill.

Both the hon. Senator and Senator

stage of this Bill to-day, but I hope to

8 APRIL 1930
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 8th April, 1930.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs.
Report for the year 1929 on the Retreat Established in Northern Ireland
under the Inebriates Acts, 1879-1900.
Joint Nursing and Midwives' Council
for Northern Ireland:
(1) Conditions under which Hospitals are approved as Training
Schools:
(2) Schemes under which Nurses
may be trained for admission to the
State Register.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move, "That
Standing Order No. XXV be suspended
for this day's sitting, for the purpose of
taking the Second Reading of the Parliamentary and Local Government Elections
Bill, the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill, and the Housing Bill."
These Bills were circulated last week
and, therefore, I do not think hon.
Senators will have any objection to
taking the Second Reading to-day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mater Infirmorum Hospital (Incorporated (Extension) Bill.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move
"That the Senators following be named
of the Joint Unopposed Committee on
the Mater Infirmorum Hospital Incorporated (Extension) Bill:
Mr. J. C. Glendinning,
Mr. A. Quigley.
Old People in Industry.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that the unemployed in Northern Ireland
number over 51,000, and whether the
Government, with a view to some reduction in these figures, have considered the
practicability of removing old people.
from industry by means of increased
pensions.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
answer to the first part of the question is
in the affirmative, if the hon. and learned
Member includes in the number of unemployed those persons who are only temporarily or casually unemployed and
those who have recently registered at the
Employment Exchange in order to test
their chances of obtaining benefit
following the Amendment of the Unemployment Insurance Acts.
With regard to the second part of the
question, financial considerations render
the suggestion impracticable.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Is it a fact that
the great bulk of these unemployed belong to industries whica are sick to
death?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like a more detailed question on that subject. I have not the necessary information at my disposal to state definitely
what industries are sick to death.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think nearly all
in Ulster.
A ques-
A Question of Privilege.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
tion of privilege has been raised in
another place. The Committee whose
actions were referred to was a Joint Committee including three Members of this
House. The matter has been referred
to the Committee of Privileges which is
set up by, and which will report to, the
other House. I think the most advisable
course for the Senate appears to be to
leave the matter in the hands of that
Committee of Privilege, and finally in the
hands of the other House. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Criminal Law Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
Criminal Law and Prevention of Crime
(Amendment) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved, "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair]
CLAUSE 1 (Period of limitation
under 48 and 49 Vict., c. 69 and 13 and
14 Geo. 5, c. 8 (N.I.) s. 2, extended)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Infliction of corporal
punishment in Borstal Institutions, 8
Edw., 7, c. 59).
If the governor (or in his absence,
the chief officer) of a Borstal Institution for male young offenders within
the meaning of Part I, of the Prevention of Crime Act, 1908, reports to the
visiting committee of such Institution
that an offender detained therein has
been guilty of grave misconduct for
which no appropriate punishment is
provided by the enactments, regulations and rules which apply to the Institution, the visiting committee, or
any two members thereof, shall inquire
into the facts of the case, and, if they
are of opinion that the case cannot be
met except by the corporal punishment
of the offender, may order the infliction
upon the offender of such corporal
punishment as they consider necessary
to meet the case, subject, however, to
such general regulations as may be
made by the Ministry of Home Affairs
under sub-section (2) of section four
and section eighteen of the Prevention
of Crime Act, 1908.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in page 2, line 10, after the word
"such" (such corporal punishment) to insert the word "reasonable.” I hardly
think it is necessary for me to deal at any
length with this Amendment, which it
is proposed to insert, in view of representations which were made in another
place. It is self-explanatory, and will,
perhaps, help to remove any idea that
the punishment to be inflicted will err
on the side of severity, although, having
regard to the very limited nature of the
punishment which it is proposed may be
inflicted, it seems to me there is little or
no ground for any apprehension in this
respect. I beg to move.
Amendment agreed to.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, in Clause 2, page 2, line 12, to
leave out from "regulations" (general
regulations) to the end of the Clause, and
to insert:
"with respect to corporal punishment as may be made under this
section.
The Ministry of Home Affairs may
make regulations for the purposes of
this section and the provisions of paragraph (b) of section 18 of the Prevention of Crime Act, 1908, shall apply to
any such regulations."
on
In moving this Amendment I would
like to say that from the Amendments
which have been put down by the hon.
and learned Senators (Mr. Leslie and Mr.
Campbell) there appears to be a certain
doubt as to whether or not the Section as
drafted is sufficiently explicit, and
consideration of the matter it has been
thought well to alter the drafting of the
section so as to place it beyond doubt that
the same procedure will be followed in
the case of regulations dealing with corporal punishment as in the case of other
regulations, and Members of the House
will thus have ample opportunity for
criticising any regulations made.
I may say that in the regulations which
have been provisionally drafted, amongst
other things it is laid down that the only
instrument to be used is to be a cane of a
pattern to be approved by the Ministry,
that the number of strokes to be inflicted
shall in no case exceed twelve, that all
infliction of punishment, as ordered by
the Visiting Committee, must be recorded in a Punishment Book, and that
no corporal punishment is to be inflicted
unless the medical officer certifies the delinquent is in a fit condition to receive
such punishment. The House may,
therefore, rest assured that the objects
which the hon. and learned Senators have
in view in their Amendments will be
fully met, and I hope that under these
circumstances the Amendments will be
withdrawn.

down rather as a basis for discussion

Elections Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Parliamentary and Local Government Elections
Bill, the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill, and the Housing Bill, to
which they desire the concurrence of this
House.
Parliamentary and Local Government
Elections Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read a
Second time."
This Bill is concerned with the evil of
personation at Parliamentary and Local
Government Elections. Hon. Members
are, I am sure, aware that a Departmental
Committee recently
recently investigated
investigated this
question and issued a report in which
certain important recommendations were
made. These recommendations form the
basis of the Bill. I do not propose to say
much in regard to the evil of personation.
The existing law, which is undoubtedly
somewhat difficult to follow, is contained
in no less than five Acts of Parliament
and makes personation a statutory felony
rendering a convicted person liable to a
substantial term of imprisonment. There
is no difference of opinion, I think, in regard to the gravity of the offence; the Bill
is rather designed to make the law clearer
and more effective in operation.
portunity has been taken to consolidate
the various existing enactments and of
bringing them together into one statute:
and, at the same time, of embodying in
addition the substance of the recommendations made by the Departmental Committee.
The op-
I need not detain the House with the
First and Second Clauses of the Bill, beyond pointing out that the definition of
personation for Local Government Elections is now assimilated to that for
Parliamentary Elections. This was one of
the recommendations of the Committee.
The main difference in the existing law is
that in a Parliamentary Election if a person applied for a ballot paper in the
name of another person, he thereby committed the offence of personation; whereas
in a Local Government Election he must
have actually voted in order to commit
the offence.
In regard to Clauses 3 and 4, which
deal with the machinery to be used when
a charge of personation has been made, an
important matter to notice is that the responsibility for the prosecution will devolve on the Crown. This again is one
of the more important recommendations
of the Departmental Committee.
Another recommendation has been embodied in Sub-section (3) of Clause 4.
namely, that the existing provisions
whereby an offender connot be returned to
trial unless on the evidence of not less than
two witnesses should be repealed. The
procedure will now be similar to that required for any other indictable offence.
Clause 5 deals with the question of compensation and does not alter substantially
the existing law in this respect. It is, of
course, reasonable that innocent people
should be indemnified against wrongful
arrest and such a person may consent to
accept any sum not exceeding £10, or
less than £5, as damages. If he does not
consent to this he can of course bring a
common law action.
Clause 6 deals with another recommendation of the Committee and empowers
polling clerks to order arrest in the
absence of the presiding officer.
In Clause 7 the qualifications for the
barrister appointed to try Election Petitions in the case of Local Government
Elections has been reduced from fifteen
years' standing to ten years' standing.
This is to remedy the inconvenience that
has arisen and will increase the panel of
qualified persons to an adequate number
for the purpose of these Petitions. The
remaining provisions in the Bill do not
require any special comment. I beg to
move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move.
"That this Bill be read a Second time this
day six months."
a
This is a sham Bill. It declares personation a felony. Personation was
felony for 60 years past. It makes it
punishable with 2 years' imprisonment.
Elections Bill-
Second Reading
8 APRIL 1930
Such was the punishment for 60 years
past. It purports to permit the appointment of agents to detect personation, but
there is no novelty in this. The Bill
makes one or two microscopic changes and
stops short. It put on the public purse
the trifling expenses of the prosecution
at petty sessions and it makes a local
government election petition triable by a
barrister of 10 years standing, instead of
15 years.
The public cares nothing for
these trifles. Can we expect any real attempt by the Government party to cope
with this sinster evil? I recall the words
of Senator Dorman in this House on 4th
April, 1929, describing the arrangements
for personation accidentally discovered by
him in the Central Orange Hall in Belfast:
"As an Orangeman and a member of
the Institution . . I took the obligation
of that Institution fifty-two years ago.
I have continuously followed it out during my life and I find it easier to fulfil
the conditions of the solemn obligation
outside the Institution than I would
to fulfil it inside. Six years ago last
January it was my privilege to contest
the Court Ward as a representative of
the Labour party of Northern Ireland.
During the day I met with an accident,
at least, I broke my glasses. . . Having
defective sight I accidentally entered
the great centre of the Orange Institution in Northern Ireland instead
of the Methodist School, and I there
discovered in a robing room
a miscellaneous collection of all kinds of
women's clothing. Before I left the
precincts of that hall I was assaulted.
It was thought that I had gone in as
a spy, but I went in by accident because
I had not my glasses to see where I was
entering. The party that can descend
to the defiling of the headquarters of
an institution that makes any man take
the solemn obligation of an Orangeman
by transforming it into a quick change
artists' depôt for the purpose of helping
people to steal other people's votes
would descend to anything."
That hall was on that occasion a hive
of personation, where people were robed
and equipped to go forth and commit the
crime of personation. That was done in
a fortress of landlordism and Torvism. Is
anyone with experience of elections in
Ulster surprised to learn of Unionists
working a busy factory for personation?
Then the Government will not trust
ordinary magistrates to investigate the
simple issue whether Bill Brown should
stand his trial for personating Jack Jones.
This Bill casts cne more slur upon the unpaid justices. The Government lose no
chance of snubbing that body. Is it because J.P.'s are untrustworthy, or uninformed, or unintelligent, or on some undefined grounds undesirable? I beg to
move.
Mr. LESLIE: The fact that personation has taken place over, I am afraid,
a long period of years at elections in
Northern Ireland is well known. I do
not think it is any use for anybody to
stand up here and to suggest that the
blame for that practice, and that practice
itself, has been in the past confined to any
one party. Speaking, if I may, as one
who has had some little concern with
elections in Northern Ireland, I can only
say that I have again and again observed
instances of personation on both sides,
and I should like to add that I have, so
far as I could, done what lay in me to
discourage the practice. But the scandal
has been great, and it seems to have been
entirely forgotten that personation is an
offence against the public. I would like
to commend that particular thought to
the hon. and learned Senator in front of
me (Mr. Campbell), for if we admit, as we
must admit, that personation is an offence
against the public, it does not seem unjust that the public should bear some of
the cost of detecting and punishing the
offender.
It is not, I think, to be wondered at
that the greatness of this scandal should
have moved the Government, actually the
Minister of Home Affairs, to appoint a
Committee to report on the subject generally. I think that all of us who have
read the Report must admit that it is a
very good Report, the production of at
strong Committee. The details of the
Report have been followed in the Bill and
I venture to think that, speaking generally, they will commend themselves to us.
Certainly one of the most outstanding
features is the recommendation that the
procedure at Local Government elections
be assimilated to that at Parliamentary
elections, and to that none of us can possibly object. There is another point that
rather appeals to me, and that is that in
spite of a Minority Report the Committee
recommended, and the Bill adopts, a proposal that personation should continue to
Elections Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
be an indictable offence, and that it should
not be competent for a Court of Summary
Jurisdiction, however constituted, to deal
with the matter.
The Report says-and again one is glad
to have one's opinion supported by the
Report that the law is adequate. The
Report goes on to say that if the evil is to
be checked it can only be by an improvement in public opinion. Public opinion
must learn that personation is a dirty trick
reflecting no credit on those who use it
and depriving them of the glory of a successful Parliamentary election. The Bill
itself is a very technical Measure and one
that is very hard to follow, but I do not
think its details will give us
trouble. I regret that I have to say that
in my humble opinion, unless one important Amendment can be introduced
into the Bill, it is a Bill that will be useless and even mischievous. That Amendment does not arise until towards the end
of the Bill, and I will not dwell on it
further until I get there.
much
Going through the Bill itself, one observes in Clause 1 that very extensive use
has been made of those powers of repeal
for consolidation purposes which were
given to us by the Imperial Act of 1928.
One observes too, and I rather regret this,
that nothing has been done to make it
even more difficult than it is at present to
poll what is called the child vote. It
does rather occur to me that the attempt
to poll a child vote might almost be
brought in as personation on the ground
that a child, although its name does appear on the Register, is personating a
person lawfully on the Register.
As we know, although I am rather
afraid the public do not know, it is an
offence to endeavour to record a vote, even
if your name is on the Register, if your
name is not lawfully on the Register. It
is an offence for a person of sixteen to try
to vote, and it is an offence for a person
to abet a child of seven, as has been done,
in trying to vote. It seems to me rather
questionable whether that might not have
been brought in in the same way that
people are brought in who apply in the
name of a fictitious person, because the
person is a fictitious person whom the
child seeks to personate. However, I
shall not suggest that to the House, because I imagine it has been considered
and thought that nothing further can be
done to check the evil, which is rather
more widespread than one could wish,
although it does not seriously affect
elections.
The Bill makes the offence of personation a felony, or, as the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) said, continues
it as a felony, because it has been a
felony, I think the hon. and learned
Senator said, for sixty years. I can only
myself trace it as far as the year 1883,
because before that,
in the earlier
Statutes applying to Ireland, the thing appears to have been merely a misdemeanour. I cannot help wondering why, in
1883, the Parliament of the United Kingdom turned a misdemeanour into a
felony, because it has not been the
practice since 1870 to create new
felonies. There are two examples: one
in the same year in the Explosives Act,
but it is rather unusual, and one wonders
whether there was any particular reason
for doing it. At all events the law is
there and we are not proposing to alter it.
Now we come to Clause 4, about which
the hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) has spoken. That is the Clause
confining the committal for trial of
an alleged personator to the resident
magistrate. I am bound to say that
this is a further abatement of the
powers of ordinary magistrates, but
I do not think it can properly be
described as depriving the ordinary
magistrate of jurisdiction because he
never had jurisdiction in this matter.
He could only deal with it as a
court of first instance. He could not
determine it. I cannot help thinking
that the proposal is rather an unnecessary
one because a magistrate can only go
wrong in two directions. He can commit
for trial when he ought not to commit,
and he may not commit when he should
commit. If he commits for trial when he
ought not to commit there remains the
ordinary panopoly of justice, the
judge and jury and the Attorney-
General and SO on,
and no great
harm is likely to be done. If he
does not commit when he should commit
the matter is not necessarily serious because the Attorney-General has full
power to send up a Bill. Therefore. I
cannot quite understand why this proposal was inserted in the Bill. I am almost driven to wonder whether it was
not brought in partly as a compliment
to the Chairman of the Committee, who
Second Reading
Elections Bill-

8 APRIL 1930
is one of the junior, but by no means least
able, of our resident magistrates, and
partly as a kind of sop to the author of
the Minority Report, who suggested that
personation should be made a matter of
summary jurisdiction and then dealt with
by a resident magistrate sitting alone.
I
If it had been proposed to make the
matter one of summary jurisdiction I am
afraid that, contrary to my usual prac
tice, I should have supported the jurisdiction being confined to the resident
magistrate, not because he is necessarily
any better than ordinary magistrates, but
simply to get away from any form of local
influence in a matter of politics.
would have consented to that proposal in
the same manner as the House of Commons in England consented to the trial
of election petitions being taken from the
House itself and given to the judges. I
assume that the powers it is proposed to
confer on resident magistrates will not in
any way interfere with their ordinary
powers under the Children Act. I think
that is so, but I should like an assurance
that no change in that respect is in contemplation or likely to result.
Then there is this question of repealing
the rather extraordinary provision that
a man should not be committed for trial
on a charge of personation except on the
evidence of two credible witnesses. So
far as I know, in Northern Ireland at all
events, there is only one crime which requires the evidence of two witnesses, and
that is the crime of treason. In England
perjury has been brought into the same
category, but that Act does not apply
here. The question of two witnesses is
entirely distinct from the numerous cases
where evidence is required to corroborate.
I cannot see any particular object in requiring two witnesses in a case of personation, unless it be to protect a man
against an obviously spiteful accusation.
However, I shall not myself oppose the
proposal to confine the inquiry to the
resident magistrate, but it seems to me
that if the resident magistrate is given
these exclusive powers it follows that the
Bill should definitely provide that the indictment under the Act should not be
heard by a Court of Quarter Session. I
do not think it is at present the practice
to bring any of these indictments before
Courts of Quarter Sessions, but I have
been unable to find that there is any definite statutory prohibition operative in
Northern Ireland for preventing a Court
of Quarter Session hearing cases of personation. There are such prohibitions
operative in the case of bribery and undue influence, but, so far as I know, the
necessary connection has not been made.
I therefore propose to put down a definite
Amendment on the matter, and I shall
not be altogether surprised if I am told.
it is already the law. If so I must admit
that I have not been able to find it.
As regards the alteration proposed in
Clause 7, I see no reason to make any
objection, but I should like the noble
Viscount to explain at a later stage how
that particular Section 92 of the Act
quoted comes to be part of the law of
Northern Ireland, for the Act from
which it is taken does not apply to Ireland, and the Fourth Schedule to our local
Government Act, which enabled numerous enactments to be applied and to which
one naturally referred, does not include
this particular Section 92. I think we
ought to be told by what inclusion in
some other enactment this particular Section has been brought within our law,
and I think that on general grounds it
would be as well to insert the reference
in the Bill before us.
Now one arrives at the Eighth Clause,
and there it is, I think, that the Amendment I have referred to should come in.
The Bill as it stands deals, as it tells us
in the Eighth Clause, only with elections
for the Parliament of Northern Ireland,
or for various local governing bodies. It
seems to me that unless we can bring in
elections for the Parliament of the
United Kingdom our Bill is not only useless as hopelessly impotent to abolish the
evil complained of, but it is positively
mischievous. It would leave us with two
separate codes. One of the objects of the
Bill is to assimilate the Local Government
code to the Northern Ireland Parliamentary code, but we do not want while introducing similarity in one way to introduce dissimilarity in another. It is a
curious thing that in the Warrant of Appointment to the Committee the Minister
of Home Affairs said nothing about the
question of there being any distinction
between elections, and the Report is
equally silent, but the Bill speaks with
no uncertain voice. Now, I think it must
be admitted that it would be desirable there should be complete assimilation, and one has therefore to inquire
what it is that prevents this Parliament
from making the change I have suggested.
One turns at once to the Government
Elections Bill-
Second Reading
'HE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
of Ireland Act, and in that Act, Section
19, paragraph (b), we find that the Parliament of Northern Ireland is prohibited
from touching the election laws. That is,
I am
the expression, "election laws."
going to try to argue that if we include
the Parliament of the United Kingdom
in this Bill we are not touching the election laws at all. I say it is a mere
matter of police and nothing else. Let
us see what we are doing. We are not
touching either the election laws or even
the law of personation. The very title
of the Bill does not say we are touching
the law of personation. It says, "matters
relating to personation," which, in this
instance, is not the same thing. Let us
recapitulate what we are doing. We are
repealing, consolidating, and re-enacting
the existing law, for which we have definite Statutory authority. We are giving
the preliminary investigation to the resident magistrates, which is surely a matter
for the police. We are interfering with
the two witnesses, and as regards this
question it seems to me very doubtful
whether the requirement of two witnesses
in a case of personation in England is
binding on a judge or court when the
case comes to be tried, or whether it is
not limited to the investigating magistrate. That is a point I have not been
able to elucidate; neither have I been
able to make up my mind with certainty
that it would apply here even to the investigating magistrate in a case in which
he intended to admit the respondent to
bail instead of committing him for trial.
As regards the matters of prosecution
and putting the prosecution on the
Crown, reference to the Report will remind us that the Attorney-General has
power to prosecute. As regards the
question of ejecting people from the polling station, that is nothing but a matter
of maintaining order in
in the polling
station, and as regards allowing poll
clerks to act on the instructions of their
chief, that seems to present no difficulty,
for there seems no limit to the number
of officers who might be appointed, by
whatever name they were called. It seems
to me that there is nothing in this Bill
touching the law relating to personation
as such or to the election laws.
Now let us see what we have done in
the past as a guide to what we may do
in the future. In 1921, when dealing
with the licensing laws, we deliberately
took power to close public houses on the
day of an election. That is to say we took
power to prevent a man who had come a
long distance to record his vote from obtaining the alcoholic sustenance which he
required to go on and vote. You
can stretch that particular Section
to that extent, but I think nobody
would stand up here and
an
say that
that was an interference with the
election laws. Again, we took power
in the following year to interfere with
meetings and we made no saving for
election meeting, although in
troublous times an election meeting is
perhaps just the meeting where it
might be necessary to exercise greater
supervision and greater care than usual.
But we did not exclude meetings in relation to elections. In the same year,
1922, under circumstances which are, perhaps, best forgotten, we passed an Act
which did not in practice become operative whereby we altered the court by
which cases of treason might be tried.
Treason is one of the matters which are
expressly removed from the purview of this
Parliament by the Government of Ireland
Act, but the provisions of the Act to
which I have referred allowed cases of
treason to be heard without a jury before
two judges, a most definite alteration of
the law, not the law of treason, but the
law relating to conviction and punishment
for treason. That is an extremely strong
case. We did that in the past.
particular Bill did not arouse much discussion in this House because of the circumstances under which it was introduced,
but it did arouse a very great deal of discussion and consultation outside and that
particular point was, I know, very carefully discussed, thought over and decided.
The opinion was that this Parliament had
power to pass an Act providing for the
constitution of the court to try cases of
treason.
the
The
In 1926 we dealt generally with the
Jury laws, and incidentally we altered.
the method of selecting a grand jury for
City Commission and abolished
grand juries at Quarter Sessions. We
did not make any saving for cases of personation, and we left personation and
other offences against one or other of the
election laws to take the ordinary
course. I cannot see why we should go
out of our way to avoid doing that again.
If we can correct that matter we shall be
given a good Bill, but if we cannot do it
I think the Bill should be dropped, for we
Elections Bill-
Second Reading
8 APRIL 1930
can deal with local Government matters
under existing powers by means of an
administrative Order.
If there is any one definite thing in the
Bill which prevents the Bill's application
to the Parliament of the United Kingdom
it would be better to drop that provision
than to pass the Bill with it. I hope it
will be found possible after consultation
to make the Bill apply to all elections
for the Parliament of the United Kingdom as well as the Parliament of
Northern Ireland. I shall vote in favour
of the Bill in the hope that it tend
may
towards the abolition of personation, if
that be indeed possible.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN: I would
like to support this Bill. Senator Campbell referred to some remarks made by
Senator Dorman on a previous occasion,
and I think I am like Senator Dorman,
because I am very blind, like he is on
occasions.
have fought two very
strongly contested elections, and I have
been unfortunate in them. The only
difference between Senator Dorman and
myself was that my glasses were not
smashed and I avoided the turnips or
whatever it was. Senator Dorman referred to Orange Lodges in connection
with personation. I fought an election.
in a part of the world where party feeling
is very strong, and the personation on the
other side was very bad. You can
imagine the opportunities for personation
in townlands in mountainous districts
where everybody has the same name and
all are related. It was probably worse
than in the Orange Lodges of Belfast. The
late Mr. Russell and I very nearly tossed
up for a vote, and in the end the man
was a personator.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
support the Second Reading of this Bill,
and I am satisfied that no one will give
it a more hearty welcome than the
I
unionist electors in Northern Ireland.
believe they will be only too pleased if
the Bill proves strong enough to do away
with the corrupt and dishonest practice
which has been so prevalent during the
past few years. We must recognise that
the Unionist party has suffered considerably from this practice and unfortunately
the Unionist electors, who happen to be
the more respectable section of the community, could not bring themselves to
retaliate. The result was that the other
side had it more or less to themselves.
We always have a certain number of
wild men in politics who think the world
is not going fast enough for them, and
they will adopt any method to defeat the
sane and common-sense electors if they
think they can thereby attain their
object.
There is one matter in the Bill that I
wish the noble Leader of the House would
try to explain for my benefit. Sub-section
(2) of Clause 5, referring to the payment
of compensation to a person falsely
charged with personation, says that the
sum shall be levied by warrant upon the
agents in the first case. If the sum is
not satisfied it will then be levied on the
candidate. I cannot see why the agent
should in the first case be held responsible
for the payment of the compensation. It
must be borne in mind that in these elections there are agents and sub-agents, and
this refers to the sub-agent or personation
agent. If the personation agent is to be
made responsible for the payment of compensation in the case of an error honestly
committed then the very object of the
Bill will be defeated, because no personation agent will risk carrying out his
duties if he thinks he may be called upon
to pay compensation.
I
This is one very serious defect in the
Bill, and, unless something is done to
rectify it, it will defeat the purpose for
which the Bill has been introduced.
hope the Government will be able to
satisfy us on that point. I trust the Bill
will be sufficiently strong to put an end
to a practice that has been carried on to
a considerable extent. I am sure every
candidate will be quite content either to
win or lose so long as he wins or is defeated by honest votes. I give the Bill
my cordial support.
Mr. McHUGH: I am in the position
of the last speaker-I believe the Bill is
not strong enough, nor is it an honest attempt on the part of the Government to
deal with this evil. One of the particular
drawbacks in the Measure is that the
policeman who is in every polling booth,
and who knows the voters, is prevented
from interfering with a personator no
matter how often he may enter the booth
and personate other people. That is one
of the most serious flaws in the Bill. Personation used to be carried on on the other
side of the Border and the Government
of the Free State introduced a Bill which
imposed on the police the duty of detecting personation, and attempts to personate. The result was that personation
there vanished into thin air. In another
Elections Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McHugh]
place the reply of the Government to a
similar suggestion was that a constable
might be biassed.
My experience of the police, extending
over half a century, is that if you leave
a constable to carry out his duty and do
not interfere with him he will do it impartially. It is only when officers or
other influential people interfere with him
that he fails to discharge his duty properly. Senator Joseph Cunningham told
us that the Unionists are the more respectable class of the community and they almost felt ashamed when they had to fall
back on personation. Surely the speech
of Senator Dorman some time ago, describing a dressing room like a draper's
shop in a Belfast Orange Hall, is the
I wonder where
best reply to that.
Senator Cunningham was that he did not
detect that.
Mr. J. CUNNINGHAM: It was an old
clothes shop Senator Dorman walked
into.
Mr. McHUGH: Had he gone into a
clothes shop the man behind the counter
would have been willing to sell him
clothes, but, apparently, the Senator was
assaulted for being a spy, and the clothes
were not for sale. I do not think it is
necessary to labour the point, because
both sides have been guilty of personation.
I do not say the Nationalists did not personate, and no honest Unionist would
deny that the Unionists did so.
A very
remarkable incident took place in Fermanagh and Tyrone on an occasion when
we decided not to contest the election.
The Unionists, however, were opposed,
and when the election was over, it was
found that the Unionists had polled 135
per cent., the most remarkable poll ever
known in the world.
Therefore I hold that the Bill is not an
honest one, and I have great pleasure in
seconding the Motion that this Bill be
read a Second time this day six months.
Mr. DORMAN: I think all honest
men of all parties will welcome any effort
to put down the practice of personation.
I would like to say that I do not think
this Bill will accomplish that object, or
any other Bill unless the machinery that
has been utilised for the purpose of aiding and abetting personation is brought
within the ambit of whatever Measure
the Government promotes for the purpose of abolishing the practice. Unless
the Government includes in the Committee stage a Clause making it an offence to
have tally rooms immediately in the
vicinity of the polling booths it will be
utterly impossible to stop. personation.
It is attempted with practical immunity
because of the fact that there are tally
rooms where personators obtain the numbers of the individuals who have not
voted
That is where the evil mainly existsthe passing out from the polling station
of the names and numbers of individuals
their
who have not registered
votes.
These names and numbers are passed out
from the nefarious tally rooms for the
purpose of getting persons to go in and
personate them, and they do it with
practical immunity knowing they are not
going to vote for a person who has
already voted, in which case the poll
clerk or presiding officer could arrest
them.
I believe the Bill will not accomplish.
anything unless the Government prohibits these tally rooms or specifies a
particular minimum area around the polling booth where the tally room may be
set up for the purpose of facilitating
voters in obtaining particulars as to their
position on the register.
Seeing the statement I made in this
House has been referred to I wish to reaffirm that statement. I wish there to
be no doubt about the matter. I did
enter that hall. I did see in that hall a
miscellaneous collection of women's
clothing of various descriptions.
I was
assaulted in the vicinity of the hall.
wish to state here and now and to give the
committee in charge of the hall credit for
the fact that in no elections since then
has that particular institution been used
as a quick change artists' depôt.
I
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to
support this Bill generally speaking, but
I would like to stress what my hon.
Friend on my right remarked about assimilating the Parliamentary elections
for the Imperial Parliament and for the
Northern Ireland Parliament, because if
you have one law for one election and a
different law for another I do not think
you are going to do very much work with
this Bill. I do consider that by some
means or other we ought to make the
two Parliamentary elections agree in the
matter of personation. With that exception I think the Bill will certainly
have some effect
What will effect this question of personation most is the education of the
Elections Bill-
Second Reading
8 APRIL 1930
In this House I was glad to
public.
hear Senators on both sides say that the
fault lies equally on both sides. That is
undoubtedly true, that no side is innocent
in this offence of personation. I regret
to say that in moving about the country
I find even now that people are ready to
boast about having tried to do it three
times and only been able to do it twice.
As long as we have that psychology in the
country we are going to have personation carried on, and no human power is
going to stop it. I do not know what
the
measures you
take outside
can
in this Bill. It would be
a much stronger Bill if it made the
elections in accordance with the elections
in Great Britain. I do give general support to the Bill. I hope the noble Viscount will tell us something about Parliamentary elections in Great Britain.
measures
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support
the Second reading of this Bill. I might
say with other hon. Members that it was
necessary that a Bill of this nature should
be brought in. I am very glad to hear
both sides of the House to-day admitting
that personation has taken place in both
parties. That takes in the Socialist as
well as the Nationalist and Unionist
parties. There are some of us here to
day with memories that go back to the
time when the Unionist party did not
practice personating to the same extent
as has been done within the last few
years. But another party practised this
personation to such a great extent that
the Unionist party found it was their loss
and of course one evil creates another,
and so I am very glad that to-day all
parties have admitted that they have
been guilty of this offence.
Senator Dorman made mention of the
tally rooms and said that they should be
done away with. Tally rooms are not an
evil, and any person who has anything to
do with tally rooms knows quite well that
tally rooms are necessary. I have been
outside a tally room on more than one
occasion, and I found that a great number of people when they come to record
their vote have lost the papers which have
been sent round to their homes by the
candidate, and they come to the polling
booth without having their number.
When they go to the tally room they give
their name and address and their number is found for them and given to them
on a little slip of paper. When they go
into the polling station there is no time
say
lost. I the tally rooms are a necessity.
They are useful, and they are not an
evil so far as personation is concerned.
I am very pleased that this Bill has
been brought in. I am sorry it does not
go far enough. I believe if we are going
to do away with personation we will have
to make voting compulsory for all. If
you had compulsory voting you would find
there would not be such an opportunity
for personation. I am sorry to say that
even business people and the professional
class in Belfast do not think it worth
while to go to register their votes on election day, either municipal or parliamentary. That opens the door for personation,
for others are brought to record the vote
on whatever side they belong to. I think
if there had been a Clause in this Bill
making it compulsory for every man and
every woman who has a vote to register
that vote it would go a long way to stop
personation. I have great pleasure in
supporting the Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise with very sincere pleasure to support this Bill, which
has for its object the doing away with personation. I think the Government have
done well in introducing such a Bill. I
do not look upon it as a sham Bill as it
has been referred to by the hon. and
learned Senator Campbell. This Bill is
an honest effort to do away with personation. I was delighted, and I think it is
the first time I have heard both sides of
the House admit this evil.
Reference has been made to an Orange
Hall. Perhaps Mr. Dorman might not
have been aware that there was probably
some entertainment there on the previous
night where such clothes were required.
The Bill, in my opinion, is one that the
Government deserves very great credit for.
Personation is the one thing that I abhor,
and in connection with elections we can
do a great deal ourselves to prevent personation, and we have been doing it. The
Unionists have been doing it for some
time past to prevent personation at the
elections in Belfast. In the admission
that has been made it has not been stated
that it had its birth in West Belfast, and
the Unionists suffered very severely for a
number of years in connection with personation in that district. I have the
greatest possible pleasure in supporting
the Bill which has for its object the doing
away with personation.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
like to be able to give the assurance on
111 Financial Provisions Bill-

THE SENATE
First Reading
[Viscount Charlemont]
the point raised by the hon. and learned
Senator Leslie about Parliamentary elections for the Imperial Parliament. I am
afraid I am not in a position to do so at
the present moment. I can only say that
in the time that elapses between now and
the next time the Bill is taken the Government will consider the matter very carefully.
As regards the general principle of the
Bill I myself remember a time when personation was looked upon in an entirely
different way from what it is to-day. I can
look back some thirty years, when it was
supposed to be a rather estimable thing
to do.
From that time it has gone
through various changes until it is recognised to-day as an evil by which either
party would be ashamed to profit. I think
it is the opinion of the Government that
by the pronouncements in this Bill they
at least lend their voices in denouncing the
evil which it is intended to abate. I hope
subsequent amendments will strengthen it
and that the House will be content to give
it a Second Reading to-day.
Mr. SPEAKER: Does the hon. Member (Mr. Campbell) realise he can make
another speech.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not desire to.

Bill accordingly read a Second time and

Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time.-(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount BANGOR: This is a Money
Bill, and, therefore, not subject to amendment by this House. It may, however,
be of some interest to hon. Members if I
summarise briefly its various points.
The First Clause deals with a matter
which has arisen in connection with houses
provided for ex-Servicemen by the Irish.
Sailors' and Soldiers' Land Trust. The
proposed increase in this Clause from
£30,000 to £35,000 in the maximum
amount to be charged on the Consolidated
Fund arises from the fact that the Trust
has found itself in a position to provide.
more houses than it had originally contemplated. In the Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Act of 1927 it
was arranged that the Trust should
receive from the Government a sum of £80
per house in lieu of the ordinary housing
subsidy (for which they were not eligible).
A maximum limit of £30,000 was then
fixed, but owing to the increase in the
number of houses to be built by the Trust
it is now necessary to raise this maximum
to £35,000.
It
The Second Clause of the Bill extends
the powers given to the Ministry of
Finance under the recent Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Act of 1929 in regard to loans made by banks to farmers.
The extension of these powers is for the
purpose of giving loans for the purchase
of live stock, including poultry; the existing powers do not cover these items.
is hoped that some of our agricultural
community who have been hard hit by
losses on crops, such as potatoes, may be
induced to launch out in other directions,
and it has been represented that loans for
live stock and poultry would be a desirable
direction in which to extend the existing
powers.
The Third Clause deals with the limitation which was imposed by an Act of 1872
of 22 years' purchase in respect of persons
extinguishing tithe rent charges. It is
now desirable that the Government should
have a freer hand in the redemption of
these charges.
Financial Provisions Bill
Housing Bill-
8 APRIL 1930
Clause 4 is designed to give the Comptroller and Auditor-General the benefit of
the revision of the salary scale which has
recently been made for permanent heads
of Departments. The arrangement is that
this post is graded on the same basis as
that of the departmental heads.
The Fifth Clause of the Bill is concerned with the matter of accommodation
of our Incorporated Law Society. It has
been arranged that accommodation should
be provided for this Society in the new
Courts of Justice, and it has been thought
equitable that the Government and the
Society should make contributions on a
basis of 50 per cent. each for this purpose.
Under the former régime in Dublin the
Society had very considerable facilities of
their own, and the new organisation in
Northern Ireland has not had time to build
up the funds necessary to provide entirely
out of their own resources the accommodation here. I beg to move the Second
Reading.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid that not for
the first time I must take exception to the
statement of the noble Viscount that this
is a Bill which this House has not power
to amend. I think that is what I understood him to say. I do not think we can
allow or ought to allow the restriction imposed upon us in the 1920 Act as regards
dealing with the appropriation of public
money or Bills initiating charges on the
public-we ought not to allow those restrictions to be increased to any extent.
Having said that I do not want to be
taken as objecting to anything in the Bill
itself, but I should like to be assured that
the money which may be required under
Clause 2 will come out of the original
quarter of a million which was allocated
by the original Bill, that is to say, the
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill
of last year.
It will be remembered that
Ι
the Ministry of Finance had power to
guarantee payments in respect of the purchase of seeds and artificial fertilisers. The
total amount there was £250,000.
should like to be assured that loans for
livestock and poultry will come out of
that sum of a quarter of a million, that is,
that the original liability is not going to
be increased. I understood the noble
Viscount to say definitely that livestock
and poultry were not exclusive terms,
that is to say, it is not necessary that a
man who bought livestock should also
have bought poultry. It is a matter
very likely to have attention called to
it in a Bill which will shortly come
before us I should like to be assured that
we may take it in the ordinary sense.
Otherwise I have no objection to the Bill.
Mr. LONG: Some time ago representations were made to me that County
Londonderry had not received an adequate
proportion of houses for ex-servicemen.
Other districts had received more than
enough. In fact houses were vacant which
had been built for ex-servicemen in those
districts, and they could not be procured in
County Londonderry. I am not sure that
that is the real state of the case, but I
should like inquiries to be made and if
that is so that it should be made right.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like to
have some more information as regards ex-
Servicemen's houses in the district in
which I live. Years ago an officer who
was quartered in Dublin came and
made inquiries. There were applications for twelve houses in that
district. Year after year we have
made application for ex-Servicemen's
houses, and year after year we have had
no reply. We have now one built, but
the applications number something like 13
or 14. I should be glad if I can get some
assurance that a few at least will be built
in the district I come from, the district of
Crumlin in County Antrim. I have endeavoured to get something done, but I
have never been able to get any building
started. I should like to make a protest
and to hope that we shall get something
done in this matter.
Viscount BANGOR: With reference to
the matter which hon. Senator Leslie has
raised about the source from which the
loans are derived I will endeavour to
obtain the information for him; also as
regards ex-Servicemen's houses in Londonderry which hon. Senator Long raised,
and the houses in Crumlin which Colonel
Pakenham raised.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Housing Bill.
Moved: That this Bill be now read a
First time.-(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
introduces no new principle, and is not
intended in any way to alter the law as
Housing Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
laid down in the Act of 1929 and previous
Acts. It is merely intended to clear up
some doubts which have arisen from the
wording of Section 2, so that the intention
of the Act, which was to prevent abuse of
the housing subsidy, may be fully
attained.
The first Amendment is the substitution
of the words "type or dimensions" for the
words "type and dimensions" wherever
they occur in the Section. This is to
make it clear that the subsidy is recoverable if the house is altered in one or
other of these respects.
The second Amendment is the dropping
of the proviso to Sub-section (7). This
proviso was introduced when the Act of
1929 was going through Parliament, with
the object of making it plain that a person who had built a subsidy house under
the previous Acts, limiting the area to 650
square feet, would be permitted to enlarge it to the new limit of 700 square feet
without being called upon to refund the
subsidy. There is, however, some doubt
about the exact effect of this proviso, and
it has therefore been thought better to
repeal it. The proviso is really not required, as under the Section itself no
penalty is incurred by any person who increases the size of a subsidy house provided
he does not increase it beyond the limit
prescribed for the time being.
A third alteration has been made in
Sub-section (4) of Section 2, which has
been revised so as to remove any doubt
about local authorities having power to
recover, by means of a deduction from the
building grants payable to a person in
respect of a house, the grant paid in
respect of another house which has been
enlarged or altered. I beg to move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment.

move, "That this House do now adjourn.

stages of some of the Bills we have had today.

Parliamentary Bill, I imagine?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No,
other ones.

AND AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Issues from Consolidated
Fund to Land Trust in aid of Housing).
Viscount BANGOR: In regard to
Clause 1, a question was raised yesterday
by Senators Long and Colonel Pakenham
about houses for ex-Service men in
Londonderry and in the Crumlin district
respectively. I may say that almost all
the houses recommended as the quota for
Northern Ireland have been built, with
the exception of the extra hundred houses
for which a Government grant is provided
in this Bill. It is of course appreciated
that many more applications for houses
were received than could be met, and in
any case this is a reserved service, and the
Northern Government have nothing to do
with the administration of the Trust.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Extension of power of
S. 2 of
Ministry of Finance under
19 Geo. 5, c. 3).
Viscount BANGOR: Senator Leslie
asked yesterday whether the limit of
£250,000 mentioned in Section 2 of the
1929 Act still remains the same. The
answer is in the affirmative. It is not
necessary to extend the limit for the purpose of loans for these further items of
livestock and poultry. The loans already guaranteed for seeds and fertilizers
have been availed of only to a small extent.
Mr. LESLIE: I am much obliged to
the noble Viscount for getting that information. I think that, perhaps, it is
much to be regretted that the public
generally do not know how little advantage has been taken of the offer made in
that way.
There was a great demand for
facilities for purchasing seeds and fertilizers. A grant of £250,000 has been
made and it has not been taken advantage
of.
Viscount BANGOR: There was another
point raised by Senator Leslie about the
words "livestock" and "poultry." He
asked whether "livestock and poultry"
precluded the guaranteeing of loans to the
one without the other; whether it means
livestock, or poultry, or both. I have obtained a ruling on that subject, and the
Government view is that it would include
the one without the other or with the
other.
Mr. MCALLISTER: Might I ask the
noble Lord if the reason why advantage
has not been taken of these loans is on
account of the cumbersome method by
which they are administered. Is it not a
fact that when a farmer goes to negotiate
a small loan for this purpose, no matter
how solvent he may be as regards the value
of his property, he has to get two other
farmers to go to the bank to secure him.
I suggest to the noble Lord that it is the
cumbersome regulations made for administering these loans that has made the
scheme practically inoperative.
Viscount BANGOR: I will make inquiries into that.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
