Oral Answers
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 15th April, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Agriculture:
The Marketing of Potatoes (Northern
Ireland) Rules, 1930.

Message from the Commons.
The Commons have read a Second time
Priestly's Divorce Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I am making a rather unusual proposal to the House, without
notice, namely, that the House should
suspend Standing Orders to-day in order
to take the Second Reading of Priestly's
Divorce Bill.
I am putting this proposal forward
purely in the interests of the parties concerned in the Bill. It is necessary to give
considerable notice of a Committee on a
Divorce Bill, as witnesses have to be
collected, and that is often a matter of
some difficulty. The agents for the Bill
were given notice some little time ago that
the Committee would be held on April
30th, and I think I am expressing the
views of hon. Members when I say that
we do not wish unnecessarily to add to the
expense and inconvenience of the parties
promoting the Bill. Owing to the fact.
that we are not taking the Committee
Stage of the Parliamentary and Local
Government Elections Bill at present,
there will be no business for the Senate
before April 30th, the day fixed for the
Divorce Bill Committee. Therefore, unless the Senate meets to-morrow, for a
purely formal sitting to take the Second
Reading of Priestly's Divorce Bill, which
course I do not think will appeal to hon.
Members, the only other course is to
suspend Standing Orders. I, therefore,
beg to move, "That Standing Order
No. XXV be suspended for this day's
sitting for the purpose of taking the
Second Reading of Priestly's Divorce
Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: The only remark I
should like to make on that proposal is to
inquire whether 30th April is necessarily
the date on which the Committee must sit.
Would it be equally convenient if the date
were 1st May or 2nd May?
I think
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
notice has already been given for the Committee to meet on 30th April.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
a Second Time.

On Question, Motion agreed to
GOVERNMENT EXPENDITURE.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House (1) whether the
estimated expenditure of the Government this year is about £9,000,000, or
equivalent to £7 per head of the population of Northern Ireland (2) whether
the expenditure of the Imperial Government during the past year in Northern
Ireland was over £5,500,000, or equivalent to about £4 10s per head of the
population of Northern Ireland; (3) the
total amount paid in Northern Ireland
to the Imperial Government in taxation,
direct or indirect, during the past year;
(4) whether the Imperial Government
makes any contribution to the £40,000
a week, on the average, expended in
Northern Ireland on education and educational services?
Oral Answers
Slum Clearance
THE SENATE
of
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and learned Member will appreciate that
owing to the somewhat intricate interfinancial relations between the Imperial
Government and the Government
Northern Ireland, it is somewhat difficult to give, by means of Question and
Answer, a lucid exposition to this House
on a complex subject, or even an explanation which will enable hon.
Members, including the questioner, to
carry away any tangible picture with
them. I must, therefore, apologise to
the hon. and learned Member if my reply
to the four heads of his question does
not appear to be sufficiently direct, but
he will, I am sure, appreciate that I
wish to be as helpful as possible in the
manner in which my answer is framed.
a
Government, but the amount of the share
of the Reserved Taxes collected in Northern Ireland and paid over to us by the
British Government after deducting the
Imperial contribution and the expenditure
on Reserved Services. The amount
paid over last year was about 5 million
pounds.

With regard to the question at (3), I
am not at all clear as to what figure the
hon. and learned Member is thinking of,
but possibly he is referring to the actual
amount paid over in Imperial contribution during the past year. The sum as
disclosed in the statement published on
the 31st March last in respect of this expenditure was provisionally fixed
£600,000.
at
With regard to the inquiry relating to
the Imperial Government's contribution
towards Education and Educational
in Northern Ireland, I can
say quite definitely that no such contribution is received by the Northern Irish
Exchequer.
Problem of Slum Clearance,
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That in the opinion of this House, the
Government should deal immediately
and effectively with the grave problem
of slum clearance and the re-housing of
slum dwellers."
The estimated expenditure budgeted
for in the Estimates presented this year
in another place amounts, as stated, to
Services
sum of about £9,000,000; to speak
more accurately, £8,800,000, but of this
sum more than £800,000 is repaid by the
British Treasury in respect of new
buildings at Stormont and assistance towards the solvency of the Unemployment
Insurance Fund, leaving the true total
of the Estimates at about £8,000,000.
The figure per head of the population is,
therefore, over-stated in the question. It
must, however, be borne in mind that
Northern Ireland Estimates are not the
full measure of Northern Ireland's
expenditure, because in addition an
Imperial contribution is paid, together
with the cost of the Reserved Services in
Northern Ireland, such as the Post
Office, Land Purchase Commission, and
the Supreme Court of Judicature.
What the expenditure under these two
heads in the current year will be, it is
not possible for me to state, but it will
no doubt be disclosed by my right hon.
Friend, the Minister of Finance in
another place in due course.
Turning now to the second part of the
question, as I have already stated,
certain expenditure met out of Northern
Ireland revenues collected by the Imperial
Government is incurred by that Government in Northern Ireland, although, in
fact, ultimately paid for by this Government. I assume that the figure of
£5,500,000 quoted by the hon. and learned
Member is intended to represent this expenditure last year. It is possible that
the figure is quoted under a misunderstanding and may be intended to represent not expenditure by the Imperial
I submit to the House that slum clearance is a problem of the first importance.
We have our slum area in Belfast and
other urban centres. We have had no
Slum Clearance Act. We have had
Housing Acts in a long rotation. These
Acts have been of no utility to the slum
dwellers. It was hoped that when the
better paid artisans and clerks and middleclass folk removed into the subsidy houses
there would be a "filtering up" from the
slum houses into the vacant houses.
Nothing of the kind has happened. Great
Britain has built 1,500,000 houses since
the war. If the present rate of subsidy
housing continues for two or three years
more Great Britain will have expended on
it the vast capital sum of £1,000,000,000.
On the ratio of population Northern Ireland should have built 40,000 houses.
The poorer paid workers have been unable
to afford the rents of these houses and have
lived on in slum houses which are more
overcrowded and much more ruinous than
at the end of the war. They cannot pay
more than 6 or 7/- a week inclusive
125 Slum Clearance

15 APRIL 1930
Slum Clearance

rent. The unskilled low-paid labourers
with families are out in the cold. The
lot of the working classes best able to pay
and with the smallest families has been
cast on more pleasant lines, for the subsidy houses have gone to them. The
children of the slums are still pining and
sobbing in silence. 2,000,000 children in
Great Britain live or rather vegetate in
slums. How many thousands in Belfast
and other towns are in that plight?
Overcrowding is a terribly tragic aspect
of this problem. Great numbers of these
slum houses have not the three-rooms
accommodation absolutely necessary where
there is a growing family. Frankly, this
should be no party question. Good men
and good women of every party are
longing to see this evil swept away. They
long to see the families of the poorest
living in houses where children will have
a glimpse of some "sweetness and light"
in their lives. Can nothing be done to
get them out of their slum surroundings?
Yes, everything can be done if the
problem is tackled manfully and courageously, but the Government must do its
part. The recent unanimous report of a
committee of the National Housing and
Town Planning Association composed of
persons of wide experience in housing and
ef different political views recommends
that the only way to clear the slums is for
the local authorities to build good family
houses at rents which the slum-dwellers
can pay, and in localities not unreasona bly far from their places of employment.
so that the cost of travelling would not be
too high. The minimum standard house
for the slum-dweller must be one which
can be let at an inclusive rent of 7/- a
week. This report says there ought to be
in such houses a graduated system of
children's rent allowances based on the
number of children in the family if the
largest families are ever to be got out of
the slums. When I ventured to recommend the scheme a little while ago in this
House the Government belittled it.
It is some satisfaction to find that the
British Minister of Health, Mr. Greenwood, in his comprehensive Bill for slum
clearance now before Parliament, sanctions
the scheme which this Government poohpoohed airily. He gives local authorities a
discretion to apportion the new State subsidy in such a way that the rents of the new
houses may be varied to suit family circumstances, taking account, if the local
authorities so decide, of the number of
children in the family. Mr. Greenwood
offers a special subsidy on condition that
the new houses are tenanted by former
slum-dwellers. He hopes half the new
houses may be let at 5/- a week plus rates.
This Bill offers 45/- for forty years per
person rehoused. Assuming an average
of 5 persons a house, the new subsidy will
amount to £11 5s Od against the Wheatley
subsidy of £7 10s Od. In respect of slum
clearances on expensive sites he offers 70/-
for each person rehoused. In rural
areas he offers 50/- a person. The local
authorities are to fix the rents so as to
cover their expenses and no more.
This is a long stride forward. This is
statesmanship. This is no petty peddling
with a question fraught with great issues.
In the long run it will pay the State. The
money spent in protecting society from the
disease and crime of the slums is colossal.
Doubtless, Christian homes flourish in the
slums. Heroic souls are there, models in
the conduct of life to many who are rich
in the goods of this world. We marvel
and bow our heads, for we know how apt
are the slums to sap and disintegrate the
family life on which our civilisation is
built, and on which our civilisation depends. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: There are
several aspects to be considered in connection with the matters referred to by
the hon. and learned Member. In the
first place consideration must be given
to the necessity for slum clearance.
and rehousing in Northern Ireland.
Secondly, regard must be had to the
steps which have been and are being taken
in connection with the matter, and in
the third place reference must be made
to the arrangements which are in contemplation on the same lines. Taking
each of these points in turn, it should
be stated at the outset that up to the
present at any rate the chief concern of
the Government and of Parliament was to
provide houses to meet the housing shortage which is at the root of the conditions
of which the hon. Member so rightly
complains.
It is estimated that at the date of
transfer of services to the Government
of Northern Ireland there was a housing
shortage of at least 10.000 houses in Belfast alone, and of probably another
15,000 houses in the remaining Counties
and the County Borough of Londonderry.
In other words the Government were
faced with the problem of trying to
overcome a shortage of at least 25,000
127 Slum Clearance
Criminal Law Bill
THE SENATE

[Viscount Charlemont]
houses. The success which has been
achieved in that direction by the generous housing subsidies which are paid by
the Government and which are supplemented in many cases by grants or other
financial advantages on the part of the
local authorities may be gauged from the
fact that up to the end of last month no
fewer than 17,528 houses of the working
class type had been erected and completed
in Northern Ireland with Government
assistance, while on that date there
were no fewer than 4,177 additional
houses of the same type in course of
erection.
The extent to which the Government
has encouraged the building of houses to
meet the shortage can perhaps be gauged
by the amount which has been disbursed
since the present housing policy was initiated in 1923-24. During that period
£127,120 has been paid over to local
authorities by way of subsidies to assist
them in erecting houses. Within the
same period £1,205,720 has been disbursed in subsidies to private builders.
The total cost, therefore, to the State
during the past seven years has been
£1,332,840, and the House may be interested to know that the greatest activity
has been during the past year when
£30,200 was paid to local authorities and
£458,740 to private builders.
It will be agreed, therefore, that the
Government has to a very large extent
met the first and urgent need of the
country in regard to housing requirements,
and it will also be admitted that until the
housing shortage problem itself is nearer
solution it will be more or less impossible
to consider the question of demolishing
existing houses, as if such a principle.
were put into operation to-day it would
be impossible to find accommodation for
the people who would thus be necessarily
dispossessed. One other special feature
in connection with Northern Ireland
conditions that is worthy of notice is the
fact that the slum evil does not exist
here to the same degree as it does in some
of the older cities and towns in Great
Britain and elsewhere.
Undoubtedly there are some areas in
our older towns and possibly in parts of
Belfast where there is congestion and
where it would be desirable that clearance work of the character referred to by
the hon. and learned Member should be
effected, but the main consideration at
the moment is the provision of additional
houses, and to that great object the
efforts of the Government and of Parliament must be directed for some little
time to come. At the same time it is
not suggested that the Government is
not alive to the necessity in the near
future for schemes such as the hon. and
learned Member suggests, and in this
connection attention might be directed
to the fact that, as already intimated in
the King's Speech, it is the intention of
the Government to introduce during the
present Session of Parliament a Bill
according to local authorities in Northern Ireland town-planning powers
which, incidentally, will enable them to
deal with built-up areas as well as undeveloped land. By this means it will
be possible for these authorities at one
stroke to get rid of slum areas whereever
they exist, and at the same time to enhance the amenities of their respective
districts.
On Question, Motion negatived.

Question to Minister.
CIVIL SERVICE SELECTION
BOARD.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House what is the constitution of the Selection Board of Northern
Ireland in connection with the Civil Service and other Government appointments,
under what statute or regulation the
Board functions, and what are its duties
and powers, and whether any such Selection Board functions in connection with
Imperial Civil Service appointments?
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Before replying specifically
to the question asked by the hon. and
learned Member I wish to point out that
the Boards in question act as Selection
Boards only in connection with Departmental appointments, that is those of a
professional, technical or otherwise
specialised nature; their function in connection with general service appointments,
that is, those of a non-specialised character, is that of an Interviewing Board. For
example, in the case of an open competitive examination the Board allots marks
for the interview and these marks are
added to the marks awarded for the
written papers to determine the final
order of merit. I wish once again to
stress that in such cases the Board interviews in the sense of conducting a viva
voce examination, but that it does not
make a final selection. The Boards are
specially constituted by the Civil Service
Commissioners on each occasion on which
their services are required.
An Interviewing Board comprises normally the Financial Secretary to the
Ministry of Finance as Chairman, two
private Members of Parliament, a Civil
Service Commissioner and one other member. Its duties and powers are to award
to each of the candidates interviewed such
marks as appear to it just and proper,
within the maximum laid down in the
Regulations governing the competition,
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
30 APRIL 1930
in respect of the qualities of alertness, intelligence and general outlook and other
qualities of value for situations in the
Civil Service. The final placing of candidates in order of merit is, as has been
indicated, the function of the Civil Service Commissioners and is based strictly
on the total marks obtained in the
written examination (including oral or
practical tests in languages or science subjects) and interview combined.
A Selection Board on the other hand
usually consists of the Financial Secretary as chairman, a Civil Service Commissioner, two representatives of the Department in which the vacancy to be filled
exists, and one other member. Its duties
and powers are to recommend to the Civil
Service Commissioners for appointment
to existing vacancies such of the candidates appearing before it as in its judg
ment possess the highest qualifications.
In deciding upon its recommendations the
Board is instructed to take into consideration the personal qualities of the candidates as shown in the interview and such
other factors as may be laid down in the
special regulations governing admission to
the situation or in any special instructions
given by the Commissioners, and on its
estimate of those matters to frame its
conclusions.
The functions of the Selection and Interviewing Boards are not statutory but
are derived from their appointment by the
Civil Service Commissioners, whose
powers, in turn, are derived from Orders
made by the Governor, the most important of which is dated the 11th July, 1923,
and was published in the Belfast Gazette
of the 27th July, 1923.
Appointments to situations in the Imperial Civil Service of a specialised nature
are usually made as the result of interviews by Selection Boards appointed by
the Civil Service Commission of the
United Kingdom. In connection with
certain other situations candidates are required to attend before a Selection Board
as a condition of admission to examination
or are interviewed by a Board as part of
the competition as in the case of the Northern Ireland general service appointments.
Mr. McALLISTER : Is it not a fact
that the Selection Committee are empowered to add as many marks as 20 per
cent. of the total for this interview, thereby rendering the real examination, from
a competitive point of view, a perfect
farce?
I am
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
afraid the hon. Member must put down
a separate question on that subject, but
I must contradict the suggestion that the
appointments are a farce.
ALTERNATIVE VOTE AT
ELECTIONS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that at the last General Election for the
House of Commons of Northern Ireland,
of the votes polled in the 26 constituencies
which were contested (not counting the
Queen's University), 147,101 were cast
for supporters and 143,408 for opponents
of the Government; and whether from
this almost even poll the Government
secured 18 Members and the Opposition
only 8; and whether in the event of the
present British Parliament adopting the
method of the Alternative Vote the Government of Northern Ireland will thereupon submit legislation to apply the
Alternative Vote in elections for this
Parliament?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not
prepared to dispute the figures quoted by
the hon. and learned Member in his
Question, but I would like to point out
that these figures do not take into account
those 22 constituencies in which there was
no contest. As these constituencies
roughly represent 50 per cent. of the total
constituencies in Northern Ireland, it is
obvious that the figures quoted by the
hon. and learned Member have not the
significance in regard to the election which
he attached to them.
In any event, the hon. and learned
Member will remember that the whole
question of the system of voting for Parliamentary elections in Northern Ireland
was fully considered by the Government
and the Parliament of Northern Ireland
immediately prior to the last election and
whatever steps may be taken in this regard elsewhere it is not the intention of
the Government to make any alteration
in the present system.
Elections Bill-
Committee

CLAUSE 1 (Personation at parliamentary and local government elections).
(3) References to the offence of personation, and to the offence of aiding,
abetting, counselling or procuring the
commission of the offence of personation, where they occur in any enactments relating to parliamentary elections and local government elections,
shall be construed as references to those
offences as defined by this section.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 1, page 2, line 17, to leave out
the word "and" ("elections and local")
and to insert the word "or."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not suggest that
the trivial alteration I am moving in line
17 makes any essential alteration in the
operation of the Bill, but as the word
"and" is a very possible trap for the unwary I think it would perhaps be well
that we should make the alteration in accordance with the Motion on the Paper.
I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept that Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
page 2, after line 19, at the end of Clause
1, to insert the following new sub-section:
"(4) A court of quarter sessions shall
not have jurisdiction to try any offence.
arising under the provisions of this
section."
As regards this Amendment, there are,
I think, three things to be considered.
First of all, is it desirable to insert that
the offence of personation should not be
tried at courts of quarter sessions;
secondly, whether that prohibition, so to
speak, is not already the existing law, and
thirdly, if it is the existing law is it desirable for us, so to speak, to re-enact it in
a Measure in which we are repealing certain sections of former Acts. As regards
whether it is desirable that a court of
quarter sessions should not have jurisdiction in these matters, the present practice is to send all such cases to the Assizes.
Further, there is now, as the Committee
is aware, no Grand Jury at quarter sessions. Still further, we are proposing in
the Bill before us to remove from the
ordinary magistrates the right of inquiry
into any charge of personation, and it
would not seem to me to be logical to do
that on the one hand, and on the other
to allow a charge to be preferred at
quarter sessions where the majority of the
magistrates-if it were ordinary
quarter sessions court and not a Recorder's
Court-would be those very magistrates
who were not permitted to inquire into
the case at an earlier stage. I should not
be surprised, as I said on the Second Reading, to be told that it is considered that
the law is already sufficiently strongly
supported by Statute to ensure that these
cases shall be sent to the Assizes and not
to quarter sessions. But failing a definite
assurance from the Government itself, on
the recommendation of the law officers of
the Crown, I think it is only right to call
the attention of the Committee to the
matter and to move the Amendment I
have on the Paper.
an
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have
caused the matter to be looked into, and
I am advised that the position is, that
offences of personation will not in any case
after the present Bill has been passed be
triable at quarter sessions. Section 3 of
the Corrupt and Illegal Practices Prevention Act, 1883, provides that personation, as defined by Part III of the Third
Schedule to that Act, is a corrupt practice
within the meaning of the Act, and the
hon. Senator will find that the definition
or "personation" contained in the Schedule is exactly the same as that contained
in the Bill. Section 53 of the same Act
extends the provisions of Section 10 of the
Corrupt and Illegal Practices Prevention
Act, 1854, to personation, etc., "in like
manner as if such offences were bribery
within the meaning of the Act of 1854,
and the proviso to Section 10 of the Act
of 1854 prevents offences for personation
from being triable before
quarter sessions.

court of

It is true, that were it not for the provisions of this Bill, which assimilates the
law with regard to municipal elections to
parliamentary elections, there would be
reg nothing apparently to prevent a case of
personation at a municipal election being
tried at quarter sessions, because the Act
of 1850, which relates to the offences of
personation at such elections did not make
personation a corrupt practice, nor did the
Act of 1883 extend to an offence under
that Act. For these reasons I cannot
accept the Amendment.

(2) Nothing in this section shall be
construed as authorising a presiding
officer to prevent from voting, or reject
the vote of, any person who duly
answers the questions and takes the oath
permitted by statute or election rules
to be asked of and to be administered
to voters at the time of voting at a
parliamentary or local government election, but in that case the presiding
officer shall cause the words "protested
against for personation" to be placed
against the vote of any such person who
is charged with personation, when that
vote is entered in the poll book.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 3, page 3, line 13, to leave out
from "the" (against the) to the end of
line 15 (the poll book), and to insert instead,
"name of any such person who is
charged with personation in the marked
copy of the Register of Electors used at
the Polling Station."
This Amendment is simply designed to
bring a somewhat old Statute, the Act of
1850, into line with the conditions
they exist in more modern times. The
poll book has long been obsolete and it
as
does not seem desirable to preserve it in a
Statute where it may become inaccurate
and perhaps misleading.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government are prepared to accept this
Amendment as it removes from the Bill
an anachronism which was unfortunately
overlooked in the Clause.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, at
the end of Clause 3, to insert the following
new sub-sections:
"(3) It shall be the duty of a constable in a polling station to arrest any
person whom he believes to be guilty of
personation in his presence, and to
prosecute such person in due course of
law, and such constable shall not be
answerable in damages or otherwise for
any bona fide mistake made by him in
the execution of such duty."
"(4) The Returning Officer in any
Parliamentary or Local Government
election shall so arrange the distribution
of the ballot papers among the Presiding Officers that the maximum number
of ballot papers to be issued at any polling station by a Presiding Officer shall
not exceed two hundred and fifty."
I move these two sub-sections in no
political or controversial spirit. We are
all agreed that this offence of personation
is one that ought to be stamped out. We
are all agreed, in the words used by the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) on
the Second Reading of this Bill, that personation is what he called "a dirty trick."
I would describe it as even worse than that
and call it a low down offence. As the
law stands at present a constable is not
only justified but he is bound to arrest a
person whom he in his own presence sees or
believes to be committing a felony of any
kind. Therefore the first sub-section as to
the duty of a constable in the case of this
felony does not alter the law as it exists,
and it does not purport to alter the law as
it exists. The peculiarity in regard to
personation matters is that under the
Statute it is recognised to be the duty of
the presiding officer to cause the arrest of
a person who is believed to be guilty of
personation, but since the year 1850 he
only orders the arrest of a person guilty
or supposed to be guilty of an offence when
he is set in motion by one of the polling
agents.
Elections Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
The settled practice for many years has
been for the police not to interfere in a
matter of that kind. They stand aloof,
they stand indifferent, and they never
take the initiative in a matter of personation at all. I do not see why they
should not. I do not see why personation,
being a felony, should be treated in any
way different from any ordinary felony
so far as the custodians of the peace are
concerned. I submit to hon. Members that
it would be a most salutary check on this
evil of personation if the personators knew
as they would soon come to know
that if they intended to engage in that
practice they would be liable to arrest
there and then by a constable stationed in
the polling booth who would deal with
them just in the same way as he would
with a man about to commit a burglary
or theft.
Personation is a mean and cowardly
crime and to a person guilty of that crime
we ought to extend no consideration and
no mercy.
This new sub-section which I
move would at least be a head-line to the
police. It would impose very little extra
duty on them because once the impression got abroad that the police were empowered and entitled to detect cases of
personation and to make arrests it would
be a very salutary check on evildoers.
The second part of sub-section (3) entitles a constable to perform his duty in
this respect without fear of any evil consequences in case he has committed a
bona fide error of judgment. Therefore
he would be quite fearless in regard to
any risk he was taking, and he would be
quite free from any apprehension of being
made answerable for any bona fide mistakes he might make in carrying out the
duty cast upon him by this Statute, if
this Amendment is accepted. It might
be said that a provision of this kind might
involve the police being exposed to the
suggestion that they were taking sides in
an election, but I submit there is no real
weight in that objection. I think the
Members of the police force would do their
duty with perfect impartiality, and I am
sure there would be no desire on the part
of any section of the citizens to criticise
them unduly. Therefore, in a non-controversial spirit, and in the earnest desire
to stamp out this evil of personation, I
ask the House with some little confidence
to accept this Amendment.
I believe the rejection of the Amendment will convey the impression to many
outside this House that the Bill is not
a real Bill but a sham Bill, dealing not
with essential matters but only with minor
and incidental matters connected with
personation, and in no way calculated to
check the evil which has given rise to the
presentation of this Bill to this House.
Sub-section (4) has the same object in
view. I have been told on very good
authority that having regard to the very
large increase of electors, and especially
having regard to the extension of the
franchise to women, it is exceedingly difficult for personation agents to keep in
any kind of touch with the voters in their
respective districts, and although they are
able to check 200 or 300 voters who live
near where they reside, when it becomes a
matter of 2,000 or 3,000 voters it is an
utter impossibility for any personation
agent to have any effective control or
check on them, so as to know whom to
challenge. I believe it to be a fact that if
each particular polling booth dealt with
only 250 voters in a particular area and
had allotted to it one personation agent
each of those 250 people would be known
to that agent and there could be no attempt
made on the part of personators to vote
for other persons, because such attempt
would at once be detected.
That is the object with which I put
down this Amendment. I quite recognise
that, if incorporated in the Bill, it will
involve some extra expense, but in matters of this kind, where the public interest
is so vitally concerned, and where a real
evil has to be stamped out, strict considerations of economy should not be allowed to prevail against the greater public interest that is involved. I beg to
move.
This
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Amendment deals with two distinct
matters. First, it is proposed that the
police should take the initiative in
arresting a person whom the constable believes to have committed personation in
his presence, and, secondly, it is proposed
to reduce the number of electors to be
dealt with by any Presiding Officer.
With regard to the first point, the
House will agree to the general principle
that it is most undesirable that the police
force should have imposed on them any
duties which might lay them open to the
charge of partisanship in elections. It
would be very rare indeed that a con-
Elections Bill-
Committee
30 APRIL 1930
know
stable would be in a position to
B definitely whether a voter was committing
deal the offence of personation or not.
It is
the duty of the personation agents appointed under the powers given by
cal Statute to watch for personation, and it
would be entirely against the public
his interest that the police should become perthe
sonation agents as it would
me c object of the Amendment to make them.

I would, however, point out to the hon.
and learned Senator who has moved this
Amendment that Clause 1 of the Bill
makes personation a felony, and, therefore, it is the duty of the police to exercise the same duties with regard to this
offence as to any other felony and that is
perfectly proper, but the Government
would strongly object to anything in the
nature of imposing on the police with regard to this offence different functions
per than those which they exercise in regard
to any other felony. If a policeman
knows that personation is being committed
fact
in any instance within his sight, it is
de clearly his duty to take steps even on his
own initiative under the ordinary law,
ati and nothing further is required or is de-

IM
It is the considered view of the Government that the police should exercise
their ordinary functions in this matter,
but that they should not have imposed on
them any special functions or any special
obligations in regard to this offence.
With regard to the second sub-Clause
proposed, the Government cannot accept
the suggestion. The average number of
voters assigned to a Presiding Officer is
between four and five hundred, which is
by no means an excessive number, and,
of course, it is extremely rare that 100 per
cent. of this number go to the poll.
To adopt the suggestion of the hon. and
learned Senator would enormously increase
the cost of elections to everyone
concerned. It would mean that the
number of officials would have to be
greatly increased, the accommodation
would have to be increased, and the expenses to candidates would also be greatly
swollen. More personation agents would
have to be employed.
The Government cannot see that any
and for the
advantage would accrue,
reasons I have stated cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. McALLISTER: The hon. and
learned Senator, in moving his Amendment, stated that if it was not accepted
many outside this House would not believe
that this was a real and genuine effort
to deal with this shocking question of
personation. There is at least one Member
inside this House who believes that if the
Government do not accept this Amendment they are not making a genuine or
an honest attempt to abolish this shameful crime of personation. Could there be
anything more detrimental to respect for
the law than to see a constable standing
in a polling booth and watching a person
voting in another person's name, when
the constable knew he was guilty of personation and was committing a crime. It
is all very well to say that at the present
time a person who personates another is
guilty of a felony, but the fact remains
that under the Statute law the initiative
for prosecution for personation rests
with the presiding officer and not with the
police.
I have had some experience of elections
and one instance occurs to my mind which
happened at the election preceding the
last election for the Northern Parliament.
There are, as we all know, certain areas
in all constituencies in Northern Ireland
where it is impossible for one side to know
of
voters
the names or appearance many
belonging to the other side. I was a candidate for a constituency in County
Antrim, and it was impossible for me in
the first place to get a personatíon agent,
and in the second place it was impossible
for me to get a personation agent in sympathy with my views who knew to any
extent the voters in the district, because
most of them held political views different
to my own. Policemen were there all day,
but it is a notorious fact, and it has been
admitted to me by an agent on the other
side, that in that particular district, because there was
no personation agent
representing the views I stood for, the
entire electorate was polled, and those
who did not attend to vote were personated.
I submit that nothing could bring the
law into greater contempt or disgrace
than for law-abiding citizens to stand by
and see this crime of personation committed in the presence of policemen. It
is making a laughing stock of the election
laws, and if you bring one section of the
law into ridicule you destroy all respect
for the various codes of law. If they are
earnest in this matter the Government
Elections Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
will accept this Amendment. It is strange
that such an Amendment should be moved
by the hon. and learned Senator representing the views he and I represent in
this House. I think that is a very
significant fact and one from which the
public will naturally draw the conclusion
that at all events we have done our best
to abolish this hideous crime of personation which has brought Northern Ireland into ridicule so far as the conduct of
elections is concerned.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I venture to submit
to the House that the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, has not given any
convincing reason why this Amendment
should be rejected. It is brought forward with an honest desire to purify our
election system in Northern Ireland. The
noble Lord said the police could not be
expected to know the people who came.
forward to commit this crime of personation. I appeal to the experience of every
Member of this House if there is anyone
better acquainted with the people in their
particular area than the sergeant and
constables in a particular barracks. They
know the whole countryside from A to Z.
It is not my intention to make the police
personation agents, but simply to ensure
that it is their duty to intervene when
the necessity to prevent a crime such as
personation arises.
That duty should not be cast upon the
presiding officer alone. That has been
the practice for almost eighty years, and
the police have had to stand by helpless
while this crime has been committed
almost under their very eyes.
I believe
that in the Free State the police are empowered to intervene, and as a result personation in the Free State has been almost
completely stamped out. Is it the desire
of the dominant party in Northern Ireland, notwithstanding the camouflage of
this Bill, that personation should continue
unchecked by the ordinary custodians of
the law?
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to support the
Amendment.
I believe that if this power
is given to the police it will do more to
check personation, especially in Belfast,
than anything in the Bill. I speak with
wide experience of elections, having contested various constituencies for the
Belfast Corporation with tally-rooms and
without tally-rooms, and with and without personation agents, and I believe that
if the Government accept this Amendmore
ment they will do
to
prevent
personation than they will if the Bill is
passed in its present form.
Mr. LESLIE: If I thought the Amendment put on the Paper by the hon. and
learned Member would really in truth
and in fact tend to militate against the
practice which we all condemn, I should
support it. I must say that in my judgment it would not help us by inserting
these words in the Bill. I think the noble
Viscount, the Leader of the House, has
already touched on the point that if these
words were inserted it would tend to shift
the responsibility from the personation
agent to the constabulary. Such experience as I have of elections in the country
districts of Northern Ireland makes me
share that view. I think it would be said
that it was the policeman's duty and nobody else's, and the personating agent
would not take the usual steps, and the
presiding officer would also be less prone
to take action than he is already. I must
point out, again drawing on my experience of country elections, that the police
on duty at a polling station are by no
means the police ordinarily resident at
the barracks next to the polling station.
The police are sent all round the district.
They very often have no more knowledge
of the identity of the individual than you
or I.
Again, as regards the second of the two
Sub-sections, it seems to me that if we
accept Sub-section (4) we should be increasing the difficulty of preventing personation, because we should be requiring
the unfortunate candidate to produce
double the number of personation agents
he requires at present, and Senator
McAllister has already drawn attention to
the difficulty in some districts of candidates of this or that political profession
finding people who are sufficiently
competent to act in that capacity.
Therefore, I do not think the Committee
would be wise in accepting the proposals,
but at the same time the whole Committee will welcome the assurance of the
hon. and learned Senator, that he and
those who think with him are entirely at
one with those who do not think with him
in desiring that the evil of personation
should be stamped out.
Mr. McHUGH: The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) has told us some very
remarkable things. First, he says it is
the duty of the presiding officer to detect
the crime of personation.
Elections Bill
Committee
30 APRIL 1930
C
Mr. LESLIE indicated dissent.
Mr. McHUGH: I beg to inform this
Committee that presiding officers have no
such power. I have probably had greater
experience of elections than anybody in
this House. I have had over forty years'
experience, first when a schoolboy as a
check clerk, afterwards as a poll clerk, as
a personation agent, and finally as a presiding officer. I have seen people come
into the polling booth and vote who were
not on the register at all, and there was
no power vested in me as presiding officer
to interfere with them. It is important
to remember that this Amendment has
been moved by the Opposition, a very
small body in this House, and we wish
to give this power to the police, who are
of your political persuasion, or at least
seven-eights of them are. (Hon. Members: No.) Three-quarters of the police.
of Northern Ireland are of your political
and religious persuasion, and we have
sufficient faith in them to believe that they
will discharge their duty in this respect
in a straightforward and impartial
manner, and no reflection will ever be cast
upon them by those whom we represent,
because we know they will discharge their
duty to the best of their ability, and they
will try to stamp out what is one of the
black spots in Ulster.
Ulster is the one portion of Ireland
where personation has been rife for a
long number of years. On the occasion
of the Second Reading of this Bill an hon.
Senator behind me said personation was
first introduced in West Belfast, and that
it was some years in existence before they
really discovered the evil. That is a
terrible state of affairs. We ought to do
all we can to so amend this Bill that it
will stamp out this terrible crime once
and for all. It has been said that the
policeman does not know many people
except those living in the vicinity of his
barracks, but I would inform Senator
Leslie that it is the first duty of a policeman when he goes into a district to get to
know the people, otherwise he is valueless.
Therefore you could select no better person
to carry out this duty effectively and impartially. I have great pleasure in supporting the Amendment.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like it to be known that so far as
the Unionist Party is concerned they are
as anxious as anyone that this Bill should
strengthen the election law and do everything possible to stamp out the evil of
personation. When the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) states that the
police know all the voters he probably has
in mind the small villages, but this Bill
will also apply to the elections in big cities
like Belfast and Derry, and in places like
those the police would have no knowledge
whatever of the people who passed in and
out of the polling booths. If you give the
police this power, and they exercise
it, they are liable in certain districts
where one party predominates to
become very unpopular with the
members of that party if they carry out
their duty, or vice versa on the other side.
It would bring the police into a very unpopular position. Furthermore, you want
to bear in mind it is quite possible for the
police to make mistakes. They would be
very apt to arrest a person who in their
opinion was personating and make a mistake. And who is going to pay the
compensation in cases such as that? There
is a great deal to be said about giving the
police power in a small village where they
know every person probably in that
village, but in cities like Belfast it would
be very wrong. I for one would be very
dilatory about voting for this Amendment
for fear of making the position worse than
it is.
With reference to the second Amendment I quite agree it would considerably
increase the cost for the candidate. I do
not think the school houses in Belfast
would be sufficient to accommodate properly all the polling stations that would
be required for an election, if the electors
are distributed over the different stations
to that extent. I know the school houses
in my own particular division have considerable difficulty in finding accommodation for the polling stations required, and
to extend them would make it more
difficult. They would have to erect special
buildings in a good many cases to accommodate the polling clerks, the presiding
officers and the personating agents.
Furthermore you would have to have
considerably more officials and you would
be casting the responsibility on a greater
number. I do not think it would have
the least effect in preventing personation. I think the law as it is is much
better than if the two Amendments were
inserted.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to say something about the remark
of the hon. and learned Senator opposite
Elections Bill
Committee
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
who said that under this Bill the police
would be disarmed and helpless. There
is nothing of the kind suggested in the
Bill. On the contrary I want to make it
perfectly clear that personation is now an
ordinary felony, and the police can certainly arrest a personator on their own
initiative quite apart from the personation officer. After all, a policeman can
arrest a person whom he sees fumbling at
a lock with a skeleton key, and a policeman under this Bill can arrest a person
whom he sees fumbling with a pen and
attempting to personate.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Might I ask if a
policeman has ever done so in the past
100 years?
Campbell, Mr. T. S.
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Amendment negatived.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I cannot answer questions of that
kind. If a policeman can arrest a person
intending to commit a felony of the
ordinary kind I think he can arrest under
this Bill. In future the personator will
have to run the gauntlet between two
people, the personating agent and the
policeman, and I hope he will not get
away with it.
Mr. SPEAKER: I propose to put the
two parts of the Amendment separately.
Page 3, after line 15, to insert the new
subsection (3) as set out on the Order
Paper. The Question is, that the subsection be there inserted.

Mr. SPEAKER: The next

Amendment negatived.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Arrest of person charged
with personation and subsequent proceedings)-
(1) An order given under this Act
by a presiding officer to a constable to
arrest a person charged with personation
shall be sufficient warrant and authority
to that constable for so doing; and
subject to the provisions of this Act a
charge of personation shall be prosecuted in the same manner as any other
charge of felony.
(2) Where an order for the arrest of
any person charged with personation
has been given as aforesaid, the following provisions shall have effect:
(c) a resident magistrate shall inquire into the charge of personation
within the time specified in the recognizance, or on some subsequent day;
McHugh, Mr. J.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Porter, Mr. John Porter
Quigley, Mr. Andrew
Sinclair, Colonel Thomas
(d) If a person charged with personation fails to appear before a
resident magistrate at the time and
place fixed for inquiry into the
charge, the resident magistrate may
issue a warrant for the apprehension
of the person so charged, and shall
inquire into the charge when the
person is brought before him.
(3) Where a resident magistrate is
required by this section to inquire into
a charge of personation, he shall proceed
in the same way and have the same
powers as in a case where a person is
brought before him on a charge of
having committed an indictable offence.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move on
page 4, line 1, to leave out "resident."
("(c) a resident magistrate shall inquire'). All these Amendments, page 4,
line 1, line 6, line 8, and line 12, deal with
the same subject-matter; also the Amendments in lines 17, 24, and 25, and
likewise the Amendment to Clause 5, on
page 5, line 5, and on page 6, lines 10 to
13 inclusive. This matter raises the old
question again of the jurisdiction of
resident magistrates and ordinary magistrates. I thought that question had been
quieted and given a period of rest, but
we are face to face with it again to-day.
There has been, as the House is well aware,
Elections Bill-
Committee
30 APRIL 1930
a gradual process going on in recent Acts
of Parliament of clipping the wings of the
ordinary magistrates in regard to jurisdiction; and I cannot put my case any
better or more strongly, applying to this
particular Bill, than it was put in a recent debate in this House on the Motor
Vehicles and Traffic Bill by the hon. and
learned Senator, Mr. Leslie. He gave
very convincing reasons on that Bill why
the magistrates should not be deprived of
jurisdiction to deal with motor-car
offences. I hope he is still of the same
mind, because the reasons he gave on that
occasion apply to this Bill, and apply with
still greater force.
The arguments he advanced then as to
why magistrates should not be superseded
on that class of offence, I think have
almost irresistible force in regard to the
question of personation. And, therefore,
although I have some apprehension,
having regard to the first few lines on the
second page of the Amendment sheet, I
hope he is still of the same mind as he was
a few weeks ago. I hope he is not going
to leave those who in their own humble
way stood stoutly and loyally by him on
that occasion. I do not wish to anticipate the Amendment he is to move later
on, but I appeal to him on one consideration that he ought not to be afraid of
being in a minority. I am very often in
a minority in this House. I do not know
what the feeling is like of being in a
majority, and I do not know what my
state of exaltation would be if that state
of things came about. I can assure the
hon. and learned Member if he votes with
us in this Bill he will be voting consistently
with his past speeches and record. He
will have in after days the consolation of
knowing that a minority is sometimes.
right and a majority not.
I am perfectly certain that no argument he can advance here to-day when we
are stripping magistrates of their jurisdiction in personation cases would convince
me that he was wrong when he spoke last
with regard to this matter. The unpaid
magistrates in a matter of this kind,
which is an indictable offence, have simply
investigating powers. They have
power finally to try whether a man is
guilty of personation or not. All they do
is to sift a charge, and if they think there
is a case for investigation before a high
court of justice they send the man accused
forward before that court. On the conno
trary if they think there is no case they
do not send him forward.
They can do no harm in the matter,
whichever way they exercise their jurisdiction. If they send a man forward who
should not be sent forward the Attorney-
General can step in, the judges can step
in, and there is every machinery provided by law to prevent an injustice
being done. On the contrary if they refuse informations and do not send the
man forward the Attorney-General can
step in and say, notwithstanding that
decision, the case must go on for trial.
They cannot do any harm. What is the
difficulty about personation? It is a
matter of deciding whether or not "A"
has personated "B" or if there is a prima
facie case against him. It postulates no
deep legal requirements. It does not
need a man to be learned in any sense
of the word. It involves common sense
and common intelligence, and I think the
ordinary magistrates can exercise these
fairly well in this matter.
As the hon. and learned Senator reminded us in his speech on the Motor Car
and Vehicles Traffic Bill it is an extraordinary position that the ordinary
justices of the peace can try whether or
not a man should be sent forward for
murder or manslaughter. They can refuse informations in manslaughter and not
send him forward, and yet under this Bill
we divest them of power to send a man
forward for personation. What is the
logical reason for that? What is the real
point of step by step depriving magistrates
of jurisdiction in matters of this kind? I
venture to say their jurisdiction has been
well exercised in the past, and no reason
has been given why we should now step
in and set it aside. I beg to move.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I hope the hon.
and learned Senator will be pleased to
know that I rise to support his Amendment, and I am not afraid to find myself
in a minority. I am in favour of the
ordinary magistrates being allowed certain jurisdiction. I do think that in these
circumstances in this particular Bill it
should be left to them, because as the hon.
and learned Senator remarked, it is only
to inquire into the matter. I think they
are perfectly capable of doing that. Also
I think it would be very good for the
ordinary magistrate to have an inquiry
into this matter before him, because he
Elections Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Colonel Pakenham]
knows all the people in his own district,
and it will make him feel, and other people
feel as well, that it is a very serious crime.
I am very much in favour of the ordinary
magistrate being allowed to make these
inquiries. I think the Government should
accept this Amendment, and I have pleasure in supporting it.
Mr. LESLIE: I may at the outset
assure the hon. and learned Senator in
front of me that I stand by every word
I said in support of the ordinary magistrate as against the resident magistrate,
not only in the Motor Car Acts-I say
Acts, for I think I have spoken in this
strain more than once-but also I stand
by what I said on the Second Reading of
this Bill before us. I remarked on the
Second Reading that I could see no object
in the provision which had been inserted,
and I still see no particular object in it.
I am not myself, I think, particularly
afraid of being in the right with two or
three, but when I am in that position I do
like to be sure that I am in the right.
On this particular occasion I am going
to appeal to the hon. and learned Senator
in front not to press this Amendment to
a division (Laughter)—and the reason
why I ask that is one which I think,
perhaps, will appeal more to the hon.
Senator on his left (Mr. M'Allister), the
hon. and learned Senator who moved the
Amendment, and the hon. and gallant
Member for the University, than to other
Members of this House. The reason is
this: However inane the proposal itself
may be I do not think it is a good proposal that the ordinary magistrate should
be deprived of power to inquire into these
matters. It is only power of inquiry; it
is not real jurisdiction at all-however
foolish the proposal may be, we must look
at it in this way. The Bill comes to us
from another place, and the matters with
which the Bill deals are matters peculiar
to another place. They relate to the
election of Members to serve in that other
place, and incidentally members to serve
on local Government bodies. As it has
pleased those who sit elsewhere to insert
this provision, which does not really affect
the general public, it seems to me it would
be unbecoming for this House to refuse
to accept their decision. That is the point
of view I take, and, therefore, if it is
decided that this proposal should go to
a division, although I do not think I can
bring myself to vote against it, I shall
be obliged to abstain from supporting it
with my vote. I do put that matter of
constitutional etiquette before the Members of the Committee, and I feel pretty
It is
confident that I am right about it.
a matter for them, and not for us, and,
therefore, I cannot support the Amendment.
Mr. McALLISTER: In reply to the
hon. and learned Senator's apology for
all his previous votes on this transaction
I think his explanation is a rather extraordinary one. He rather suggests that
as this Bill peculiarly interests another
place it would be rather discourteous for
us to accept this particular Amendment.
But it is an extraordinary fact that on a
Bill, the interests of which are peculiar
to another place, he should have more
Amendments on the Paper apart from
that question than-
Mr. SPEAKER: I think Senator
Leslie must be left out. Really what Mr.
Leslie does has nothing to do with the
question before the Committee.
Mr. McALLISTER: I do not intend
to pursue the question any further, more
than to say that so far I really thought
I was keeping closely to the terms of the
Amendment. However, I do not intend
to pursue that question any further. With
regard to the general question involved
in the Amendment, I have always consistently supported the view that the
ordinary magistrates fulfilled the duties
and functions that devolved upon them
to the entire satisfaction of the community. They had many advantages over
a tribunal constituted of a single magistrate. They had their collective wisdom.
In this particular case I put it on another ground-in justice to the man who
is going to be arrested for personation.
There is, I think I am right in saying,
about one resident magistrate for each
county, and all the Parliamentary elections for Northern Ireland are held upon
one day. If there are arrests for personation all over the country, are these,
perhaps, innocent men to lie in prison for
two or three days until the resident magistrate is available to make inquiry, not as
to whether they are guilty or innocent,
but whether a prima facie case is made
out at all? I think it would be a tremendous injustice to an innocent man.
Innocent men have been arrested for
alleged personation. I think that is a
state of affairs for which the House has
Elections Bill—
Committee
30 APRIL 1930
responsibility towards the general public,
when it is perfectly evident that under
this Bill as it stands, under this particular
Clause, innocent men will undoubtedly
be arrested and kept in prison for one or
two days certainly for a longer period
than would be necessary if the ordinary
magistrates were allowed to discharge
their functions.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
Justices of the peace may have been unfairly treated in the past by being robbed
of authority and power with reference to
matters such as motor-car cases and other
cases, but in this I do believe it is necessary
for the resident magistrate to officiate.
This is a case of politics. Where a case
of politics arises it is quite possible to get
justices of the peace really committing
offences by favouring their own side and
attending on the bench for that particular
purpose. Gambling is another thing in
which it has been known that favour was
shown, and drink. We know perfectly
well where licences were concerned there
was a possibility for them to act unfairly.
It
In this case I do think the majority are
speaking in the interests of the minority.
While the majority may be the Unionist
Party they might fare very well in having
the justice of the peace enabled to try
these cases, yet I think they will agree
it would be most advisable it should be
confined to the resident magistrate.
is no use saying they have no power.
They have power sufficient to liberate a
man, and it is not likely that the
Attorney-General can go so closely into
every case. They have power also to return a man to the assizes. I do think
it would be best in this case to leave the
resident magistrate to deal with it. I
am opposed to the Amendment.
Campbell, Mr. T. S.
Dorman, Mr. R.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Amendment negatived.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
Committee may be aware, it was a specific
recommendation of the Committee that
charges brought under this Act should be
investigated by a resident magistrate, and
indeed if ever there was a case where
charges should be investigated by a R.M.
this is the type of case where such a rule
should obtain. At the time of elections
party feeling runs high, and it is undesirable that cases of this kind should be investigated by magistrates who may have.
been actively engaged in the election. The
resident magistrate is apart from politics,
and from every point of view it is obvious
that it is desirable that such cases should
be investigated by a person holding an
independent position and who takes no
part in politics. I am, therefore, obliged
to resist the Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In reply to the
remarks of the hon. and learned Senator
Leslie as to the point of constitutional
etiquette, I may say they do not appeal
to me. It may be my density. I cannot
appreciate that a point of constitutional
etiquette is involved in this Amendment.
I think the same objection would apply
to any Amendment in this House with
regard to this particular Bill. Therefore,
that objection does not appeal to me. I
must press the matter to a division.
regret the Government have not seen their
way to accept the Amendment, because
now we are making a change after threequarters of a century, the necessity for
which I think has not been proved to the
satisfaction of the Committee.

I
That the word resident'
