Amendments (to delete "resident'
where it occurs elsewhere) not moved.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page
4, after line 16 (end of Sub-section 3) to
insert the following new Sub-section :-
(4) Nothing in this Act shall prevent
a prosecution from being instituted
Elections Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
apart from this section against a person
believed to have been guilty of the
offence of personation.
Provided that a person charged with
the said offence shall not be brought
before any Justice of the Peace other
than a resident magistrate for inquiry
into the charge.
The object of this proposal is to catch.
the case where the prosecution does not
start until the offender or alleged offender
has left the polling station. The Bill
provides for the arrest of the man, but it does
not go on to say what is to be done if the
prosecution is not initiated immediately.
The proviso to the proposed Sub-section is,
of course, only moved in accordance with
the decision at which the Committee has
just arrived. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government are prepared to accept this
Amendment, which is decidedly necessary
so far as the proviso is concerned.
There does not appear to be any doubt
that under the Bill, as it stands, a prosecution could be instituted in respect of
the offence of personation if sufficient proof
were available even though the procedure
set out in Clause 4 of the Bill were not
adopted. The ordinary law and procedure with regard to felonies would
apply, but, clearly, it is desirable that the
same rule with regard to bringing such
charges for investigation before a resident
magistrate should apply to the charge
whether it is brought under the provisions
of Clause 4, or under the ordinary law.
This point was, I am afraid, overlooked
in the drafting of the Bill, and I am
obliged to the hon. Senator for drawing
attention to it.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause as amended ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5
(Compensation
where
charge is unjustly made or not prosecuted.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
beg to move, in Clause 5, page 4, ne
36, to leave out "agent by whom the
charge was made, or, if the same are
insufficient, shal! be levied in like manner
on the goods and chattels of the candidate by whom the said agent," and
insert "candidate by whom the agent
(who made the charge)." I think
everyone is anxious that this Bill should
be put into operation and that it should
work satisfactorily, and that any small
snags such as the one I am moving to
amend, which might interfere with the
smooth working of the Act, should, if
possible, be removed. It seems rather
strange that the personation agent who
is appointed by the candidate and who
is, for the time being, the servant of the
candidate, should be held responsible for
the payment of compensation in case of a
mistake. Yet the Bill states that if the
compensation cannot be recovered from
the personation agent it can be recovered.
from the candidate, so that ultimately
the candidate is responsible. If the
Clause is allowed to remain as it is it will
have a very serious effect on personation
agents because no personation agent will
be inclined to run the risk of objecting
to a person who might be committing
an offence if he thought that in case of
a mistake he would be held responsible
for the payment of compensation. I am
sure the Committee will realise the necessity for this Amendment, which I beg
to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The hon. Member
proposes by his Amendment to make a
very drastic change in the law. For upwards of eighty years the law has always
been as now set out in Clause 5 of this
Bill. I believe I am right in saying that
since 1850 the law has stood in practically
the same state as it is in these two subsections. The idea is that if an agent
does make a mistake he has to be answerable for it himself. In the background.
of course, is the candidate who, I suppose, in normal circumstances would never
see an agent left in stress on account of
an error committed on the candidate's
behalf. Even under the ordinary law
if a man is out driving a car on behalf
of his employer and he happens to run
into a person he is answerable himself.
That is the universal principle of our
common law. An agent guilty of negli
gence is directly answerable to the person
concerned although by the fact of being
an agent he implicates his employer. In
the normal course the person
proceeds
against the employer as being a man of
substance. I would be glad to support
the hon. Member if I could see my way
to do so, but his Amendment might lead
to abuses and might encourage recklessness on the part of agents in dealing
with innocent people coming forward to
record their vote.
I
am
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
afraid the Government cannot accept this
Amendment. I appreciate what the hon.
Elections Bill—
Committee
30 APRIL 1930
7% Te ir A
T
M
Senator has in mind, namely, that the
personation agent who makes a mistake in
making a charge of personation against an
individual should not be liable for his
acts, but that the candidate who appointed
him should bear the responsibility. I
fear, however, that his Amendment is
founded on a misapprehension of the
position, and would not carry out what he
has in mind. Perhaps the hon. Senator
does not realise that the provisions of
Clause 5 do not alter the ordinary law
with regard to false imprisonment or
malicious prosecution, and that quite
apart from the provisions of this Clause,
the person who is arrested as a result of an
error of the agent would be able to take
an action for damages, an action which, if
successful, would entail a much greater
financial liability than anything mentioned in the Clause. What the Clause
does is simply to provide an
easy and
cheap method whereby compensation can
be fixed if the person so injured consents.
If the person injured does not consent, but
elects to retain his remedy under the
ordinary law, the agent would not be in
any way released from his liability, even
if the Amendment proposed by the hon.
Senator were inserted.
It does not appear that really there is
much foundation for the fears of the hon.
Senator, because the agent can always
protect himself by obtaining from the
candidate an indemnity against any civil
liabilities that he may incur in the course
of acting as agent, and in practice the
agent is, I believe, always indemnified.
In any event the proposal of the hon.
Senator would be contrary to the general
law with regard to the liability of agents.
in matters other than those relating to
contract. In such cases the person
wronged has always the right to go against
either the agent or the principal. For
instance, "A" is driving "B's" car as the
servant or agent of " B," and injures
"C," "C" can sue either "A" or "B."
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I am
not anxious to press the Amendment
under the circumstances.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
par of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Amendment of law as to
powers of poll clerks) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Mr. DORMAN: I beg to move, to insert the following new Clause after Clause
6:-
:-
"No tally room or meeting place for
electors shall be established within a
radius of three hundred yards of any
polling station during the hours of
polling for any parliamentary or local.
government election."
It may appear to be a far-fetched suggestion to restrain the setting up of tally
rooms in the vicinity of a polling station
during the period of an election, but the
Bill does not increase the penalty hitherto
attached to the committal of that particular crime, and has not so far acted as
a deterrent to prevent people from
attempting to personate at elections.
It
is not likely to do so in the future while
facilities for carrying on this infamous
practice are within the reach of the individual personator. Although tally rooms
may originally have been set up for the
convenience of the electors by helping
them to ascertain their number on the
register, they have been transformed from
carrying out that useful function into
machinery for the purpose of carrying on
personation. Many members of this
House are aware of that fact. The system
that is carried out by reason of the tally
rooms being so close to the polling booth
is that the names or the numbers of the
individuals who have not voted and whose
votes the personation agent inside the
booth wishes his friends outside to secure
by personation is passed out into the tally
room. Persons are placed to watch
whether the voter it is intended to personate enters the polling station after his
number has been passed out, and the
personators, therefore, have certain knowledge that the voter they are going in to
personate is not in the booth. They are
safe unless the personation agent happens
to know the parties. Meeting places are
arranged as near as possible to the polling
station for the purpose of changing
women's clothing. I believe that if this
Clause is included in the Bill, and the
police perform their duty by keeping the
vicinity of polling booths free from
loiterers the Government will do more to
stamp out personation in Belfast than
they otherwise will do. I beg to move.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM :
Judging by his speech, the hon. Member
(Mr. Dorman) seems to be thoroughly
acquainted with the system of personation.
I do not see what possible benefit will be
derived by the acceptance of his Amendment. There are polling stations where it
would be impossible to get a tally room
Elections Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
within 300 yards, and there are cases
where it would be necessary for the tally
rooms to be nearer the polling station.
The distance stipulated in the Amendment would place the candidates and their
agents in an awkward position. Further,
I think it should be recognised that these
tally rooms are intended for the benefit
of the electors who wish to ascertain their
number, and they are not for the purpose
with which Senator Dorman is so well
acquainted. I do not see how the Amendment would benefit the Bill in any way.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This matter
was very carefully considered by the Committee which investigated this subject.
Their observations on the subject are
set out on page 13 of the Report. The
Committee came to the conclusion that
tally-rooms were a great convenience to
voters, facilitated the work of the presiding officer, and were essential to the
proper conduct of the election. They
pointed out that, even assuming
that they had been abused on
occasions, they do not believe that their
abolition would prevent the evil which is
aimed at by the hon. Senator, for they
are of opinion that were tally-rooms
to be prohibited, unofficial rooms would
certainly take their place. These unofficial rooms would not be open to the
public, and the result would be not a
diminution but an increase in the alleged
abuse. The Committee point out in their
Report that they asked the witnesses who
were in favour of abolition whether they
could suggest any way of meeting this
difficulty, but they were unable to obtain
any assistance in this direction. For
these reasons I regret the Government
cannot accept this Amendment.
Amendment negatived.
CLAUSE 7 (Amendment of law as to
court for trial of local government
election petitions), ordered to stand part
of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Interpretation and repeal).
(1) In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires, the following expressions
have the meanings hereby respectively assigned to them, that is to say:-
"Parliamentary election" means a
contested election of a member or
members to serve in the Parliament of
Northern Ireland for any constituency:
"Local government election" means
an election of aldermen, councillors,
guardians or town commissioners for
an electoral division or ward which is
regulated by rules made under article.
five of the schedule to the Local Government (Application of Enactments)
Order, 1898.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page
5, line 29, to leave out from "serve'
("members to serve in the") to the end
of line 31 ("constituency") and insert
"for any constituency in Northern Ireland, in the Parliament of the United
Kingdom, or in the Parliament of Northern Ireland." I approach this Amendment with more confidence than usual
that it will have the sympathy of every
Member of this Committee. I am quite
sure it cannot be the desire of any Member of this Committee that we, in a Bill
which is designed amongst other things
to remove anomalies should introduce
and perpetuate another anomaly. As the
Bill stands at present we have, or shall
have, one code for elections to our own
Parliament and Local Government elections, and we shall have a different code
for elections to serve in the Parliament of
the United Kingdom. That would be a
ridiculous position.
I do not think it can really be said
that the effort to suppress personation is
honest and consistent if we do not take
every step which is in our power to prevent such an anomaly as I have indicated
from arising. I am quite sure that everybody would wish to make the alteration.
I have suggested. The only thing is it
is a somewhat technical and legally difficult question whether it is or is not within
the power conferred on this Parliament
by the Government of Ireland Act. Now
this Parliament is prohibited by the
Government of Ireland Act, Section 19,
I think, from interfering with the election laws, and the expression "election
laws" is defined towards the end of that
Act in this way. The expression "election
laws" means laws relating to the election
of Members to serve in the Parliament of
the United Kingdom other than those
relating to the qualification of electors
and includes all the laws respecting
registration, elections, the issue and execution of the writ, the creation of polling
district, the taking of the poll, the
method of voting, the counting of votes,
the questioning of elections, corrupt or
16:
Elections Bill-
Committee
30 APRIL 1930
illegal practices, the oath, the qualification and disqualification of Members, the
vacating of seats.
I think it must be admitted that the
only prohibition to be found there that
can possibly come into discussion is the
prohibition relating to corrupt practices.
Corrupt practices as we know are defined
by the Act of 1883, which has been
quoted to us more than once this afternoon. And there are earlier enactments
which are not repealed by that Act.
Corrupt practice as it stands at this
moment is limited to bribery, undue influence, and personation. Personation is
in the Act of 1883. I say that the Bill
does not deal either with the law of election or with the corrupt practice of
personation. What it does deal with or
tries to do is to make the offence of personation more difficult and detection more
certain. I cannot myself see that if the
Committee accept this Amendment we
shall in any way be going beyond our
power or infringing the election laws.
I think I stated on Second Reading
that it seemed to me that what we are
doing is a mere matter of detail entirely
outside the principle of the election laws.
If we were to endeavour to pass a Measure here, let us say, to bestow the
franchise on boys of 15 or to prevent a
man from voting at Parliamentary elections unless he had a certificate that his
children had been vaccinated we should
undoubtedly be going beyond our powers.
But I can only say that I do not see that
there is anything in this Bili which can
properly be said to be infringing the
election laws. I beg to move, and I hope
the Government will be able to assure us
that they are advised that this Amendment can be accepted. If it is not it
seems to me that it is very doubtful indeed whether the House should pass the
Bill as a whole. But that is a question
which can be dealt with on Third Reading.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I venture to submit
to the House that in following the course
the hon. and learned Senator has indicated we would be trespassing on very
debatable ground. This Amendment I
submit with all due deference raises a
large constitutional issue. Has this House
or Parliament any right whatever to
legislate in regard to elections pertaining
solely to the Parliament of Great Britain
and Northern Ireland? The position may
be ridiculous, but that does not meet the
point. It may be a constitutional anomaly
that we will have one set of personation
laws dealing with Northern Ireland elections and a separate set dealing with
elections in the same constituencies for the
Imperial Parliament. That all may be,
but to my mind at least, it does not meet
the point as to whether we have really
any right whatever to interfere in this
matter.
This evening in reply to a question I
ventured to put, the noble Lord the
Leader of the House intimated that in
case the alternative vote was accepted by
the Imperial Parliament it was not the
intention of the Parliament of Northern
Ireland to follow suit. That would be an
anomaly. That would mean that in this
very area of Northern Ireland in Imperial
elections we would have the system of
the alternative vote in vogue, and it would
not apply to elections to the local Parliament. That would be a constitutional
anomaly also.
At
Speaking of constitutional anomalies, is
not our position in the British Commonwealth of Nations anomalous? Is there
any parallel in the British Commonwealth of Nations for the position
Northern Ireland fills? We have not the
position of a colony; we have not the
status of the Dominion of Canada. I
believe the Irish Free State enjoys that
status. We are supposed to enjoy something higher. We here, in our Parliament of our own Members control all
matters of legislation except a few reserved matters of Imperial concern.
the same time we send Members to London
as Members of the Imperial Parliament
with the right to intervene in matters of
everyday importance to the English and
Scottish people, and those people have no
right to intervene in similar Ulster
affairs. They have no voice in such concerns at all, whereas our representatives
in London have the right to intervene in
British affairs without restriction.
is a constitutional anomaly. I do not
know what attitude the Government is
taking up on this particular matter, but
I submit to the Committee that in following the lead of the hon. and learned
Senator we would be trespassing on very
debatable ground.
That
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is with
considerable regret that the Government
find themselves unable to accept this
Amendment.
Elections Bill
Adjournment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont |
The hon. Senator on the Second Reading made a very interesting and able
argument contending that it would be
within the competence of this Parliament
to insert a provision of this kind in the
Bill, and it was to ensure that that argument should receive the fullest consideration that we did not take the Committee
Stage of this Bill before the Recess.
The matter has been fully considered
both by the Parliamentary Draftsman
and by my right hon. Friend the Attorney-
General, and I regret to say that they
have come to the conclusion that such an
Amendment would be ultra vires.
The Government realise that it would be
very desirable that the law with regard to
personation should be the same for elections to the Parliament of the United
Kingdom and the Parliament of Northern Ireland, so far as it relates to
Northern Ireland constitutencies, and
were it possible they would accept this
Amendment. They do not feel, however,
that the subject-matter of the Bill can be
treated as being anything else but a series
of Amendments to the election laws. That
expression as defined in Section 74 of the
Government of Ireland Act includes the
laws respecting the method of voting, the
question of elections and corrupt and
illegal practices, and the Bill touches upon
all these. In these circumstances I am
sorry that the Amendment cannot be
accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
noble Viscount for having caused so exhaustive an inquiry to be made into this
matter. I recognise, of course, to use a
common expression, there is no use in
endeavouring to flog a willing horse to
enable him to take a load of stones through
a brick wall. The point is of some importance. We have had this Parliament
now nine years, and there have been a
number of cases when we did require
definite judicial decisions on points such
as this as to the exact extent of our
powers. It must remain just a question
of opinion whether the law would allow
this or that. Anamolies, no doubt, there
would be, but there would be no greater
anomaly than the original one whereby
the lower House here was returned under
Proportional Representation, a principle
which the Imperial Parliament took remarkably good care not to apply to elections in England and Scotland, and so on.
I must ask the Committee to give me
leave to withdraw the Amendment. I
venture to hope that the noble Viscount
would be so good as to cause it to be borne
in mind, and if necessary impress on those
across the water that the opinion does
prevail that it is very undesirable to have
a difference in the incidence of elections
to this Parliament and to serve on the
other side.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie:
Page 6, after line 21, insert the following new Sub-section :.
(4) Nothing in this Act shall be
construed as affecting any matter with
respect to which the Parliament of
Northern Ireland has not power to make
laws."
Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Short title) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
Schedule agreed to.
Preamble and title agreed to.
Ordered, That the Bill, with amendments, be reported to the House.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported, with amendments, to the
House.

Royal Assent.

authorised by the Prime Minister,

I am
on
his
behalf, to acquaint the House that His
Grace the Governor of Northern Ireland,
in the name of and
on behalf of His
Majesty the King, has been pleased to
give his Assent to the following Bills:-
Housing.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions.
Criminal Law and Prevention of
Crime (Amendment).
Public Roads.
Adjournment.
Moved: "That this House do now
adjourn until such time as the Speaker
shall appoint."-(Viscount Charlemont).

BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I beg to move:
"That Standing Order No. XXV be
suspended for this day's sitting, for the
purpose of taking the Second Reading of
the Companies Bill and the Unemployment Fund Bill."
I should like to say that while there is
no great urgency for these Bills, at the
same time we are getting on in the Session,
and I think the House will agree that it
is advantageous to expedite our business
as much as possible.
Mr. LESLIE: May we take it that the
Committee stage of the Companies Bill
will not be taken this week?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will not.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Mater Infirmorum Hospital (Incorporated) Extension Bill.
The Amendments made by the Joint
Committee were considered and agreed to.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
LOCAL GOVERNMENT
ADMINISTRATION.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether he can
indicate the probable date on which the
Government Bill based on the Report of
the Departmental Commission on Local
Government Administration will be introduced?
re-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It has
already been intimated to Parliament that owing to the extensive
and complicated nature of the
commendations contained in the Report
of the Local Government Commission, involving in effect reforms in four
separate codes of enactments, it has been
decided to deal with the question of
lunacy reform first, and it is hoped that
a Bill with this object in view will be
introduced into Parliament during the
course of the year. It is also hoped that
early next Session a Bill or Bills dealing
with poor-law reform, and with public
health administration will be introduced.
It was the intention of the Government
to have dealt with local government reform some time ago, but the introduction
of the principle of derating, which has
made such a drastic alteration in the
whole basis and system of local government administration, has rendered it
necessary for the recommendations of the
Local Government Commission to be reconsidered in the light
of the
conditions.
new
CONTROL OF TALKING FILMS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Government are aware of the uneasiness prevailing among the public at the improvisations
of vice and crime characteristic of some recent releases of "talking" films, and of the
feeling that strong steps should be taken
to meet the menace to public morality;
and whether the Government will consider if the law requires to be strengthened
so as to ensure effective public control over
films of an objectionable type.
In asking this question I wish to make
one or two remarks. The picture houses
of these countries, including those in
Northern Ireland, are attracting enormous
numbers of all classes from week to week.
Youthful cinema-goers form a high proportion of these numbers. The picture
plays presented to them are in too many
cases of a debasing and demoralising
character. The moral tone of such productions is lamentably low. Highway
robbery is idealised; armed ruffians are
depicted as heroes, and crooks are presented as clever fellows. There is too
much glorification of crime and criminals.
Vice is hinted at with grossness and
offensiveness. The marriage bond is
ridiculed, and other steady notions of life
are made the butt of sarcasm. These
things have a sad influence on the minds
of the young. Doubtless, the producers
of sordid and lowering realism make
money by catering for debased minds, but
surely the State ought to exercise real
effective control over these people and
their works. No one objects to clean.
healthy films, whether silent or "talkie,"
but films of the baser sort are a grave
menace.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This matter
has been constantly before the Government of Northern Ireland for some years
past, and while it is possible that some
films may have been released in Northern
Ireland which may have been the subject
of objection, on the whole the arrangements for control and supervision in such
matters appear to be sufficiently appro
priate to prevent any real or substantial
ground of complaint.
As the hon. and learned Member is
aware, unless in exceptional circumstances
every film shown in Northern Ireland has
already been passed by the British Board
of Censors, and this supervision is regarded by the Government as being
adequate.
If it transpired later that there was
any reason to believe that films were
being shown in Northern Ireland which
have not been passed by the British
Board of Censors, the matter can be
adjusted by the local authorities who,
under the powers vested in them in pursuance of the Cinematograph Acts, are
in the position to refuse to license any
cinema, theatre or other place at which
cinematograph films are shown unless on
the condition that no films are shown
other than those passed by the British
Board of Censors.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Belfast
Corporation Bill, Priestly's Divorce Bill,
the Companies Bill, and the Unemployment Fund Bill, to which they desire
the concurrence of this House.
Companies Bill-
First Reading
21 MAY 1930
어
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Viscount Bangor.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Companies Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Bangor.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
to which I ask the House to give a Second
Reading is a somewhat formidable document, and is one which concerns our industrial community in a great variety of
inatters. It will not, however,
be
necessary, I hope, for me to enter into
any detailed examination of the proposals
in Second Reading stage.
I propose
only to give a brief outline of the circumstances which render such a Measure
necessary, and also to touch on a few of
the major points which are raised in the
Bill.
This is an amending Bill and can,
therefore, only be read in close connection
with the parent legislation which it proposes to alter. The existing law in regard to joint stock companies is embodied
in the Consolidation Act of 1908, a
Measure which applied to Ireland as well
as to Great Britain. That Act aimed at
covering all the matters concerning the
administration of companies which at that
period it was considered might reasonably be governed by statutory provisions,
and that Act was, I think, regarded as an
efficient and adequate Measure at the
time. There has been no change in the
law in the meantime, apart from a few
minor alterations; but in view of the considerable developments and changes which
have occurred, especially during the
post-war period, it has become necessary
to make some amendments in the law.
The need for comprehensive amending
legislation was recognised in Great
Britain some considerable time ago and
a very able Committee presided over by
Sir Wilfrid Greene, K.C., reviewed the
whole question in 1925 and 1926, and
presented a Report. In 1928 the
Imperial Parliament embodied the chief
recommendations of this Report in a Bill,
which became law in that year, and is
known as the Companies Act, 1928. This
Report and this Act are closely followed
in substance in the present Bill.
I have said that this Bill is an amending Bill, and in an amending Bill hon.
Members will realise that it is inevitable
that there should be a certain amount of
legislation by reference. We have tried,
however, to avoid this as far as possible,
and in many cases have re-enacted Sections or Sub-sections of the 1908 Act as
amended rather than follow the example
of the 1928 Act and make limited amendments which would scarcely have been
intelligible otherwise than by reference to
the 1908 Act. Following the procedure
which has been adopted in Great Britain,
we propose that this Bill should not come
into operation until a new Bill consolidating company law generally in Northern
Ireland is introduced and passed. There
are, however, two important exceptions to
this in which we are following the practice
which was adopted by the Imperial
authority, namely, in regard to the reconstruction of companies and share hawking.
The Clauses concerning these matters
will take effect immediately on the passing
of the Bill, and they are Clauses 54
and 96.
In addition to these two matters which
I have just mentioned, there are a few
other Clauses to which I now propose to
draw the special attention of the House.
Clause 14 deals with a matter of considerable importance. It will prevent the
use of the resources of a company for the
acquisition of its own shares. This is a
very important Amendment.
Clauses 30 and 31 and the First
Schedule of the Bill deal with prospectuses.
The first of these two clauses provides that
any document by which an offer is made
to the public of shares shall for all purposes be deemed to be a prospectus issued
by the company, and that all the liabilities
pertaining to prospectuses shall apply in
such case. This is principally intended to
deal with the practice which has grown up
in recent years where shares or debentures
are sold in bulk to an issuing house, so that
the latter may offer them to the public. In
some instances this was done with the
object of avoiding the onerous conditions
attaching to prospectuses within the meaning of the Act. The public regarded the
documents which they received as prospectuses, but they were not prospectuses
in the legal sense. Under Clause 30 the
directors of the company will be liable to
be called to account in regard to documents which offer shares to the public in
cases where the company sold such shares
in bulk with the object of their being
Companies Bill-
First Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
offered to the public. In Clause 31 and
in the First Schedule the particulars to be
stated in the prospectus are extended with
the object of protecting the general
public.
Among the most valuable provisions of
the Act is the publicity which is called
for as to the accounts of companies. When
the Act comes into operation every company other than a private company will
be required to file a copy of its balance
sheet, and all persons who desire to see
such balance sheets can do so at the office
of the Registrar of Companies.
Moreover, all companies limited by guarantee,
including licence companies, will have to
bring in annual returns showing particulars of their directors, a statement of the
total amount of the indebtedness of the
company in respect of all mortgages and
charges, and a true copy of the company's last balance sheet audited by its
auditors.
With regard to the remuneration of
directors, further publicity is also called.
for by the Bill in the matter of the disclosure of any loans made to directors and
of the amount paid to the directors as
remuneration for their services by way of
directors' fees. Members and debenture
holders of companies, including private
companies, will also be entitled to obtain
copies of balance sheets. Also in regard
to the matter of publicity, directors will
be placed by Clause 86 under obligation
to disclose to their fellow-directors particulars of any contracts or proposed contracts made with any company in which
they have a personal interest.
I do not propose to go into detail in
regard to any of the other matters in the
Bill. No doubt hon. Members who are
interested may desire to raise various
points on individual Clauses, and these
can, of course, be dealt with on the
Committee stage. I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move:
That this House, while welcoming.
a Measure of company reform, cannot
assent to the Second Reading of a Bill
which, by its many Clauses embodying
legislation by reference, makes the law
unintelligible, and which fails to secure
sufficient publicity from limited liability
companies.
This Amendment which I submit to the
House follows closely on the lines of an
Amendment moved to the parent Bill
when it was before the Parliament at
Westminster. An acute critic of the
Bill at that time said that an apt title for
the Bill would be "A Bill for the better
Protection of Pigeons," pigeons, of course,
being pigeons of the unfeathered species.
I understand that in the stock and share
market pigeons are gentlemen who are
rather more childish than they ought to
be, and certainly more childish than the
gentlemen to whom they give their money
for the purpose of investing. Most men
must recognise that if a fool in his folly
wants to gamble in stocks and shares no
Act of Parliament will stop him, and I do
not hope that this Bill will have any
great result in that direction either.
There are several provisions in this Bill
of a welcome character, and some, as I
will submit to the House, of a rather objectionable character. We can all
recognise that in these days the money
that is invested in public corporate companies is of colossal proportions. I believe that in Great Britain there are
85,000 joint stock companies, and that
10,000 of these are public companies and
75,000 private companies.
There is one Clause in this Bill which,
I think, will be most acceptable to this
House, and that is Clause 96, which absolutely prohibits the hawking or pushing
of shares from house to house.
That evil
is acute across the channel and has
brought heart-breaking distress to many
poor homes. It is not prevalent, if it
exists at all, in Northern Ireland, but I
think it is just as well that the Bill prevents the possibility of that evil cropping
up in our midst. Some will regret the
absence from the Bill of a Clause prohibiting the holding of a multiplicity of
directorships by one man. Business
people will be glad to see that under
Clause 23 a special resolution
can be
passed finally at one meeting instead of
having to be passed at two successive
meetings as at present. One grave drawback to the Bill, however, is that it is a
very flagrant example of legislation by
reference. We look at Clause after Clause.
and we often find not a Clause complete
in itself, but a Clause which is only intelligible by harking back to the Companies Consolidation Act of 1908.
venture to say that to the ordinary layman many of these Clauses will be absolute hieroglyphics. We ought to make
the Bill as clear as possible so that the
business man will be able to understand
what exactly he is bound to do and what
he should avoid.
I
Companies Bill-
First Reading
21 MAY 1930
The
I was very glad to hear from the noble
Lord that this Bill will be followed by
a Consolidation Bill. We have had none
since 1908, a matter of twenty-two years.
Another grave draw-back to the Bill is
that it does not ensure sufficient publicity in the case of the balance sheets of
limited liability companies, and, what is
hardly less important, sufficient intelligibility.
public balance sheets
issued by these companies are very often
far from giving a true account of their
financial position. The real profits of a
company
can be disguised, and the
dividends can be shown in such a way
as to bear no relation to the profits. It
is said that figures can prove anything,
but very often in these balance sheets
the figures prove nothing. Even shareholders find in the balance sheets of
public companies very little information.
on matters about which they are anxious.
To my mind, this is a matter of importance in view of the fact that to-day
enormous numbers of wage-earners are
employed by corporate bodies, and when
wages and conditions of labour crop up
it is very difficult to know whether the
statements issued on behalf of these companies accurately represent the real position of the company. The result is that
there is suspicion and misgiving and
doubt, and very often strikes and industrial troubles are accentuated by want of
confidence on the part of the wageto the real profit-making
power of the company employing them.
earners
as
I am very glad the noble Lord emphasised the importance of the Clause prohibiting a company from providing
financial assistance for the purchase of
its own shares. It would be well if the
public knew that under this Bill directors,
managers and other officers of a company
would be liable to a long term of imprisonment where proper books of account were
not kept by the company throughout two
years preceding a winding-up. Another
provision makes the directors of a fraudulent company personally liable without
any limitation of liability for the debts of
the company. One Clause open to strong
objection is Clause 94, which requires
every company incorporated outside Northern Ireland which, after the commencement of the Act, establishes a place of
business here, and every company so incorporated which, before the Act, established a place of business here and continues such place, to exhibit at the place of
business and in all billheads, notices and
where it
letter paper, the name of the country
is incorporated except it is a
company incorporated in Great Britain.
Every Free State company must do this,
but no English or Scotch company need.
A company in Monaghan or Louth or
Donegal is put on the same plane as a
company in Czecho-Slovakia. This differentiation would be resented in the Free
State. These elfish pin-pricks are very
bad business and very bad policy. I do
not see why the Free State should be
treated as a foreign country in this
Clause. Why adopt this irritating attitude towards our next door neighbours,
who ought to be, and I trust will be as
the years go on, our very best friends.
Mr. LESLIE: It is not, I think, very
convenient in speaking this afternoon to
endeavour to separate the Bill from the
Motion, which is in effect a Motion for
rejecting the Bill on the ground given by
the hon. and learned Senator on the
Paper. Therefore I propose, as far as I
can, to endeavour to deal with both matters together. I should say at the outset
that I intend to vote for the Bill, and that
I do not intend to vote for the Amendment. The object of the Bill is, as I
think the noble Viscount told us on this
occasion, and as we were certainly told
in the Gracious Speech at the beginning
of the Session, to assimilate our law relating to companies to that prevailing in
Great Britain, with the object of consolidating our law as speedily as possible in
the same way as has been done in Great
Britain. That is a course which this
House has already approved.
Briefly, the object of the Bill is to make
the way of the transgressor somewhat
harder and to give further protection to a
would-be investor, the pigeon to whom
the hon. and learned Senator referred.
That is the protection which the pigeon
is to enjoy while he is yet unplucked and
still has his feathers. There are, as the
noble Viscount reminded us, three main
Statutes which we must bear in mind,
namely, the Act which is called the paren
Act, the Act of 1908; the Amending Act
of 1928; and finally the Consolidating
Act of 1929. That Consolidating Act
came into force generally on the 10th
May of last year, but it did not apply to
Northern Ireland except in a few isolated
instances. I should like to say in passing
that I hope that when we do come to our
Consolidating Act we shall put a Clause
into it just reminding students and the
Companies Bill-
First Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
people who use our new Act for reference
of the existence of that Act of 1929. It
can quite easily be done, but it is a matter
perhaps which is not strictly pertinent to
à Consolidation Bill except for the purpose of general information. Apart from
those Acts I have mentioned there are one
or two minor Acts, and to one of those
minor Acts I propose to direct the attention of the House for a few minutes.
I
Our time has not been very long for the
consideration of this Measure. I myself
received a copy of the Bill on Saturday
last and this is Wednesday afternoon.
have found it possible to practically
identify every Clause in the Bill with the
corresponding Clause of the Amending
Measure of 1928. But there have been
exceptions, and those exceptions have
been, I think I may say, invariably
Clauses where, with a view to making
things simpler for the future, the
draughtsman has departed from legislation by reference and has set out on the
path of re-enactment. I am going to say
at once that much as I object to legislation by reference where it can properly
be avoided, I do think, as I have endeavoured to show by this example, that in
a Bill such as this, it is not only the best
course but it is a course which facilitates
reference and makes the path of the
student far easier than it otherwise
would be.
Going through the Bill, it will be
found that the numbers of the Clauses
follow very closely the numbers of the
Sections in the Act of 1928. There are
occasional
but the position is regaps,
adjusted. There are gaps, for instance,
in page 7, where we notice that Sections.
10 and 11 of the Act of 1928 do not appear. That is, of course, because those
two Sections relate to matters with which
His Majesty has power to legislate by
Order in Council. Taking it on the
whole, it seems to me that the more you
read this Bill the more you become convinced of the really admirable and careful manner in which the work has been
done, and how very much has been done.
to make a very difficult and technical
subject easy to be followed by the amateur
and the layman. If anybody will take
this Bill and read it with the other three
Acts I do not think they will have any
serious difficulty in following it. There
are 125 pages in this Bill. I may claim
to have read it through once at least, and
part of it more than once, and in the
whole of those pages I have discovered, I
believe, one misprint to which I will
direct attention later on. Bearing in
mind the number of lines in every page
I do not think that can be described as
a very heavy bag.
There are two other small matters I
will mention now. I am going to put
something on the Paper about them, although I believe both will be found
capable of explanation. One of them relates to Clause 9 itself, the Clause to
which the Noble Viscount drew our
special attention as being the Clause
which was to come into immediate operation. Clause 9 itself is to be found on
page 97 of the Bill and on other pages,
but the particular Sub-clause I refer to
is Sub-clause (5). It will be observed
that Sub-clause (5), which deals with
certain penalties, does not give any indication as to whether those penalties are
to be recoverable summarily or on indictment. That is apparent from the
wording of the Bill in every other case,
except, of course, that, following the
enactment in the principal Act, all cases
in which offences are punishable by fines
can be dealt with summarily. Another
one is the somewhat curious position in
which the Parliament of the United
Kingdom has left the Foreign Interests
Act of 1917.
It seems to me that the Parliament of
the United Kingdom has left that Act
in exactly the same position as
Mohammed's coffin. It was a short Act
concerning alterations in the Articles of
Association dealing with holdings by
aliens. The English Amending Act provided that the Statute should cease to
have effect. If it had stopped there
there would have been nothing to be said,
but the very next Section of the English
Act goes on to say that nothing therein
shall apply to Northern Ireland, except
small portion of the Clause
responding to the first Clause in the
Bill before us. We are left in complete
doubt as to whether the Act of 1917 is
or is not now in force in Northern Ireland. I am almost certain that superior
knowledge will furnish us with an explanation on those two points, but in order
that we may get that explanation I will
put something on the Paper.
a
cor-
I must support Clause 94 to which the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell)
objected. I think he rather took the
view that there was something in that
Clause distinctly hostile to our neighbours
Companies Bill--
First Reading
21 MAY 1930
in the Free State, and that that is the impression he intended to convey. I cannot
take that view at all. It seems to me
that it is a perfectly proper thing to
require companies operating in Northern
Ireland to indicate that they are branches
or so on of companies which are not subject to the law of Northern Ireland. I
think that is the whole point. There is
no difference between the Free State,
France, Belguim, the United States, or
anywhere else, but there is that difference
and it seems to me that it is a proper
difference to be impressed. I do not
know that on the whole it is fair to suggest that there is any motive of hostility
to the Free State in the insertion of that
Clause. As a matter of fact the Clause
is practically the same as Section 89 of the
Imperial Amending Act, so that we did
not invent it ourselves, and it is not our
imagination at all.
Passing from that I want to come to
some rather more serious business in
which I am not disposed to agree with
the Bill. I observe it is proposed to confine summary jurisdiction under this
Bill to resident magistrates. I can see
no reason for that at all on this occasion.
Not very long ago I am afraid I sacrificed such character for consistency as I
may have possessed in the minds of some
hon. Senators because I was able to refrain from opposing a similar suggestion
on the ground that it came from the
House of Commons and related to a
matter peculiar to themselves. This is
not that case at all. This is purely a
general matter, and for reasons which I
will give later on I see no reason for
taking jurisdiction in these matters from
the ordinary magistrates. Anybody looking through the Bill and observing what
matters these are will see that some of
them are extremely simple. Following
on that we are presented with the question of jurisdiction of Quarter Sessions.
That is not a question which will arise
at all if summary jurisdiction is not
taken away from the ordinary magistrates, but if summary jurisdiction is
taken away from the ordinary magistrates, then, I think, the question of
Quarter Sessions arises.
But there is another point which seems
to me of far greater importance, certainly of far more importance than
Clause 94, and it is a point from which
we
are departing from the law of the
United Kingdom. We provide throughout this Bill, very properly no doubt, for
very considerably enhanced penalties on
certain defaulters, but we are not saving
for those defaulters their ancient right
of being tried before a jury.
We apparently provide for sentences up to twelve
months imprisonment, and sentences of a
court of summary jurisdiction. I cannot
agree with that at all, and I think that
can only have arisen because we have
not in Northern Ireland any general
provision giving to
giving to anybody whose
alleged offence subjects him to a certain
measure of imprisonment a right to go to
a jury irrespective of what a court of
summary jurisdiction may wish to do.
In England, as the House is no doubt
aware, there is such a provision,
there is no general provision I know of in
Northern Ireland saying that a man
whose alleged offence subjects him to more
than three months imprisonment shall as
of right have the right to claim to be tried
before a jury.
but
There are special cases, and what is
called the Criminal Justice Act is the most
familiar, but that Act, sometimes spoken
of as "the under five shillings Act," is
strictly limited to larceny and kindred
offences. I shall, therefore, ask the
House in Committee to add a Clause to
the Bill based on the Clause in the
English Summary Jurisdiction Act of
1879, although, I think, that perhaps the
Committee will decide that a somewhat
shorter form than is made use of in those
ancient days may now be provided. No
doubt other matters will arise which can
be dealt with in the Committee stage,
which I am glad to learn is not to be
taken too soon.
The
The hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) made two complaints against
the Bill. The first was legislation by
reference, and as regards that I can only
repeat that the Bill can be followed more
quickly that way than any other.
other point he made was that sufficient
publicity was not given to the affairs of
limited companies. I confess that I
should have liked to have had a little
more notice. I did not see the Paper until
I got into the House this afternoon, but
so far as I remember from what I picked
up in reading the Bill I did not observe
that there was any inducement to conceal. But I have to admit on the other
hand that unless a man can understand
a balance sheet-and most people including myself, are hopelessly incompetent to
understand a balance sheet prepared by a
competent accountant or auditor-unless
Companies Bill
Unemployment Fund Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
you can manage to make a man understand a balance sheet I am not sure that
you will do very much good by an Act of
Parliament. If there is anything more
the hon. and learned Member thinks
ought to be done as regards limited companies I suggest to him that it would be
possible to put down Amendments for the
Committee stage, and provided that
sufficient notice is given it might be that
some of the things he desires may
receive our support.
On the whole, I am convinced that the
Measure is a necessary one, and that it
is a Measure that will, as time goes on,
earn the approval of the Province.
Although the penalties imposed are in
some cases very much more severe than
hitherto, I do not think the Bill is one
that will work any hardship on anybody.
I am satisfied that when we get the Consolidating Measure we shall have taken a
further step to put the law of Northern
Ireland on a more useful basis, and on a
less inaccessible shelf in our local libraries
than it is at present.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Nearly all
the points raised by the hon. and learned
Senator Campbell and the last speaker can
be dealt with more properly and more
easily in the Committee stage. I therefore do not intend to say very much about
it except to say in general that I am glad
the Bill has had a favourable reception,
although as a matter of fact the Motion
put down by the hon. and learned Senator
opposite is really akin to a Motion for the
rejection of the Bill. Still I hope the
House will not give expression to that.
Of course it is quite impossible, as the
hon. and learned Senator said, to bring
in any legislation that would protect absolutely and certainly a given type of investors. We have alluded to them this
afternoon as pigeons and Senator Leslie
referred to them as pigeons not yet
plucked. This House must remember that
a certain portion of them, whether they
have feathers on their bodies or not, are
always feather-brained and cannot be protected by any Act of Parliament. At
the same time it is necessary to protect
them as far as possible and I think this
Bill does that. For the reason I have
stated I do not think I will go on to explain the various points which we can take
to better advantage at a future moment.
But I should like to deal with one question. On a certain Clause the hon. and
learned Senator opposite said it was meant
to be a pin-prick to the Free State,
Nothing of the kind whatever
was intended. The matter was put in purely
and simply from the fact that Northern
Ireland is part of the United Kingdom,
the Irish Free State is not, and therefore
the registration of companies in the Free
State might follow a different procedure
from the rest of the United Kingdom.
We must follow the law in Great Britain
in the matter. No animus was intended
or inferred against a friendly country.
Question ("That this House, while welcoming a measure of Company Reform,
cannot assent to the Second Reading of a
Bill which, by its many clauses embodying
legislation by reference makes the law unintelligible, and which fails to secure sufficient publicity from limited liability companies') put and negatived.
On Question ("That the Bill be now
read a Second time") Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday.
Unemployment Fund Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: This short Bill
has for its object the extension of the borrowing powers of our Unemployment
Fund. At present under Section 5 of the
Unemployment Insurance (Agreement)
Act (Northern Ireland), 1926, the total
amount of the advances which
made and which may be outstanding at
any one time from the Ministry of Finance
is limited to a maximum of one million
pounds.
may
be
The present actual amount so advanced
is nine hundred and fifty-six thousand
pounds and an increase in the maximum is
accordingly desirable. It is proposed in
the Bill to increase it to one million seven
hundred and fifty thousand pounds.
This proposed increase follows naturally
on a similar increase in borrowing powers
in Great Britain from forty millions to
fifty millions which has recently been
made, as under the Re-insurance Agreement the unemployment funds of the two
countries are kept in parity.
I beg to
move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am quite sure
the House realises the significance of this
Bill. The Unemployment Insurance Act
of 1926 gave statutory agreement between our Minister of Finance and the
British Treasury with a view to assimi-
181 Unemployment Fund Bill

21 MAY 1930
Adjournment
lating the burdens of the respective Exchequers per head of the insured
population of Northern Ireland and of
Great Britain in respect of unemployment
insurance. That Act limited the advances which the Ministry of Finance can
make for the Unemployment Fund to the
sum of one million pounds. This Bill
now before the House augments that
limit to a sum of one and three-quarter
millions. That is an index to the extent
of the ravages of unemployment in our
midst at the present day.
The unemployed figures are going up
and up.
The position is growing worse
and worse. The Government as far as
one can see appears absolutely powerless
to afford the least remedy for the appalling position in which we are placed in
Northern Ireland. I notice that this week
the Member for West Belfast at Westminster stated that there were 20,000 unemployed in his constituency. I wonder
if any constituency through the length
and breadth of the United Kingdom has
such an appalling swollen total as that.
When the question of unemployment is
raised in this House the noble Lord who
leads the House of course is profuse in
his expressions of good will and benevolence for the workless people and
tells us we should be happy because
we have the best of all possible Governments here, and then like Mr. Micawber
he tells us to wait in the hope of something turning up. The unfortunate
thing is nothing is turning up and nothing
affords the least hope of turning up. It is
a most regrettable situation. I suppose
this year again the British Treasury
under this re-insurance equalisation agreement will have come to your help.
Last year they advanced a sum of nearly
£400,000 in order to keep unemployment
benefit here on the same level as in Great
Britain. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear).
Some hon. Members say "Hear, hear.'
Yes, you, of course, are all agreed in this
part of the world that the more you
get from the British Treasury the better.
The position is not improving and this
Bill is an index of that fact.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
do not wish to make a speech, but only
to answer a remark of the hon. and
learned Senator about the Government
doing nothing at all. I would ask the
hon. Senator if we should accept Mr.
Henderson's advice and then we would
probably know what ought to be done.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday.

Mr. LONG: I must suggest that Tuesday would not be a suitable day for

27 MAY 1930
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 27th May, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
PAPERS PRESENTED.
Ministry of Education:
The Secondary School Grants Regulations (Northern Ireland), 1930.
Regulations for the Payment of Attendance Grants to Technical Schools and
Classes, 1930.
Ministry of Labour:
Contributory Pensions (Oversea Voluntary Contributors) Regulations (Northern
Ireland), 1930.
Ministry of Commerce:
Report of the Registrar of Friendly
Societies for Northern Ireland-Part A.
for the year ended 31st December, 1929.
Presented pursuant to Act and ordered
to lie on the Table.
Mater Infirmorum Hospital (Incorporated) Extension Bill.

the Third time.
Priestly's Divorce Bill.
Amendments made by the Joint Committee were considered and agreed to.
ABATEMENTS OF RATES ON EDU-
CATIONAL BUILDINGS.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the educational buildings of the Assembly's
College, and of five secondary schools in
Belfast, are under Acts of Parliament entitled to abatements of 82.91 per cent. of
rates; whether a like privilege is possessed
by any secondary schools outside Belfast;
whether some 60 secondary schools in Northern Ireland recognised by the Ministry
of Education pay rates without abatement;
whether the controlling bodies of these 60
schools are bound to apply the revenue derived from fees to educational purposes;
whether promising children of necessitous
parents are encouraged by abatements of
fees and by scholarships in these schools;
whether the rates on these schools total
between £4,000 and £5,000 a year; and
whether, in view of the heavy burdens on
their resources, it is in the view of the
Government expedient that they should
be put on a parity with the Belfast institutions referred to.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): Under the Belfast Corporation Act (Northern Ireland), 1925, the
educational buildings of the Assembly's
College and of five secondary schools in
Belfast are entitled to abatements of 82.91
per cent. of the local rates.
I am not aware whether a like privilege
is possessed by any other secondary school.
I believe, however, that the other secondary schools that are in receipt of grants
from the Ministry pay rates without abatement.
All grant-earning secondary schools are
required to devote the entire income of
the school from all sources to educational
purposes. Some of these schools offer
scholarships to promising children. I am
not aware of the exact amount paid in
rates by these schools, but have no reason
to believe that the estimate given in the
Question is inaccurate.
The question of the promotion of legislation with a view to placing individual
schools in the same favourable position as
those referred to in the question is one
for the decision of the local authorities
concerned. I am not prepared to express
any opinion on the general question of
the expediency of such action.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I would make a
very earnest appeal to the Ministry of
Education and to the Government to give
the very best consideration they can to
this subject.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government will certainly consider the matter.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
CUTTING OF ANCIENT TREES
AT BALLYNURE.
Major DOBBS: Before asking the question which stands in my name may I make
an explanation of the question and point
out that Ballynure graveyard has many
claims to historical interest. In the midst
of the graveyard are the ruins of the old
church where Dean Swift in former years
officiated. On one occasion, the sexton
being the sole congregation, he commenced
the service with the words, "Dearly beloved Roger, the Scripture moveth us in
sundry places.' These ruins are bound
up with legends of the Dean.
ear
One corner of the graveyard has a small
watch-house that was used at the time
when the body-snatchers plied their loathsome trade. Until a few days ago the
graveyard was the pride of Ballynure
village. Here the eye was charmed with
the beauty of the foliage and the
soothed with the cawing of the rooks.
But now there is only one dead fir tree
and one sycamore to remind us that that
was once a sylvan retreat. Woman's hair
is her glory even in these days of bobbed
hair. Cut off her hair and her bald and
shorn pate becomes a travesty of beauty;
and so that graveyard now lies awful in
its nakedness.
And why? A few rooks misbehaved
themselves and someone complained to the
rural district council, and they in the
majesty of their power decreed the assassination of these rooks and ordered the
trees to be felled to the ground. Surely
if they wanted to do away with the rooks
they could have done it by some other
means than by cutting down the trees.
The Chinaman will tell you that you need
not burn the pigsties in order to get roast
pork. I view with the gravest concern
the cutting down of trees in such places as
this. In these times when the building of
county roads is the order of the day and
buildings are rising along the lengths of
the roads and lanes it is to such places as
this we must look in future for our trees.
I appeal to the noble Lord, the Leader of
the House, to do all in his power to preserve our graveyards, and if possible to
put them under the protection of the
Ministry of Finance's Department of
Ancient Monuments.
I beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether he is aware that the ancient trees
in the historic graveyard of Ballynure
have been cut down, and whether the
Government have power to prevent such
an act of vandalism being perpetrated in
the future.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before the noble
Lord replies. The hon. and gallant Member has referred to ancient monuments.
May I ask if the Government takes any
interest in the preservation of ancient
any
mouments except a few on the Treasury
Bench?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will confine myself to answering the original
question. It will be safer than going into
the definition of ancient monuments mentioned by the hon. and learned Senator.
(Laughter). While I personally would
wish if practicable that no one should cut
a tree without the permission of the
Ministry of Finance at the same time that
is not practicable. The graveyard in
question is vested in the Larne Rural
District Council under the Public Health
Acts, and the Ministry of Home Affairs
has no jurisdiction to interfere with the
discretion of the local authority in regard
to the matter referred to in the question.
Inquiries, have, however, been made on
the subject and the Ministry is informed
that the Burial Board have been reluctantly compelled to cut down a number of
trees which were causing a nuisance and
which were interfering with the proper use
of the ground. The roots of the trees were
spreading through the ground and were
actually overturning gravestones.
siderable difficulty was also experienced in
connection with the opening of graves
owing to the presence of these roots.
Apart from this fact, they were the cause
of dampness not only to the graveyard itself but to the roadway over which they
hung.
Con-
Generally speaking, it is the experience
of the Ministry of Home Affairs that the
local authorities in Northern Ireland are
fully alive to the desirability of preserving
the amenities of their respective areas in
connection with such matters as trees and
other natural objects of beauty and interest, and that it is only in cases of
necessity, such as in the present instance,
that they find themselves compelled to
resort to the expediency of cutting down
such trees.
BELFAST
CORPORATION TRAM-
WAYS AND FREE STATE COINS.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether it is a fact
that the Belfast Corporation, or the Tram-
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
27 MAY 1930
ways Committee thereof, have instructed
their employees to refuse to accept payment of tramway fares in currency of the
Irish Free State, and, if so, whether the
Government will make representations to
the Corporation to have this direction cancelled.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
In reply
to the hon. Member, I have been informed
by the Town Clerk that after full consideration the Tramways Committee have
endorsed the instruction that tramway employees are not to accept payment of tramway fares in currency of the Irish Free
State. I have also been informed, by the
courtesy of the manager of the undertaking, that quite apart from the fact that
Free State currency is not legal tender in
Northern Ireland, there are obvious difficulties if any considerable amount of Free
State coinage finds its way into the takings
of the day. Before the money is deposited
with the Banks the amounts have to be
checked, and as is done by large businesses
and banks, this check is often carried out
by merely weighing the silver or copper.
Free State coinage is of a different weight
to British coinage and obviously more
work would be bound to arise at headquarters. Moreover, although banks as
an act of courtesy take occasional lodgments in Free State currency from their
customers, they have to remit such lodgments to the Free State and pay for the
cost of transit and insurance. Obviously
no bank could be expected to take in such
Free State money in considerable quantities. I do not, in fact, believe that inconvenience is caused to passengers because, as the hon. Member is aware, there
are still, and will, for many years, be a
considerable amount of British silver and
copper coinage still current in the Free
State, and any person coming from there
to Northern Ireland is almost certain to
have British currency amongst his Free
State coins. The Government, therefore,
is not prepared to make any representations to the Belfast Corporation to cancel
a direction which appears quite reasonable.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I should like some
further information upon this question. I
would like to know from the noble
Lord, the Leader of the House, if
the suggestion is from the Belfast
Corporation or from information from
the banks that the banks would not
facilitate the Belfast Corporation in receiving this money or that they hesitate
to receive the coinage from the Free State
when lodged with them. It would seem
to me an extraordinary thing if that is
so, because I rather think a great number
of these banks are carrying on a very extensive business in the South of Ireland.
I would be slow to believe it; and I do not
accept the statement which I think must
be from the Belfast Corporation that it
is on account of the banks throwing difficulties in the way that the Corporation
refuses to receive these coins.
In view of the far-reaching consequences
of petty, childish, and effeminate conduct
such as this on the part of the Belfast
Corporation, which must more or less affect the material interests of the North of
Ireland and bring North of Ireland public
bodies into more or less contempt and
ridicule in the face of reasonable public
opinion, I would urge the Government to
make further representations to Belfast
Corporation to have this ridiculous regulation removed.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: May
I ask the Leader of the House whether the
Free State Government did not introduce this coinage for their own exclusive
use, and if there is any reason why they
should be deprived of their childish
pleasure of having a coin of their own.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of what has
been said, may I ask the noble Viscount
whether any consideration has been given
to the action of the London omnibus companies some forty years ago, I think round
about the time when the importation of
French pennies was prohibited. Speaking entirely from memory, I think the
refusal by the omnibus companies to
accept French pennies was before the restrictions on the importation of foreign
copper coin were brought in by Parliament. The objection to the French
penny, or dix centimes piece, as it was
called, was because some enterprising firm
of soapmakers ceased to rely on the reproduction of well-known works of art,
and took to printing their name across
the pennies. And I believe further that
the surplus of these coins in England ultimately was disposed of by means of
advertisements at the railway stations
stating that the automatic machines took
French pennies.
Education Bill--
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
personally remember what the general
omnibus companies did with French
pennies forty years ago, but I think the
main difficulty in the way of the Tramways Committee accepting Free State
money is that it is not legal tender here.
In the event of a person on a tram in
Belfast having Free State money he is
allowed to remain on the tram and is
asked to call at the Junction and make
arrangements to pay there.
I do not
think there is any large measure of public
opinion in Northern Ireland which would
be likely to force the Belfast Corporation
to accept money that is not legal tender,
and I do not think the Government is
likely to make representations to the
Corporation on this subject.
Message from the Commons.
passed
the
The Commons have
Labourers Bill, and the Appropriation
Bill, to which they desire the
currence of this House.
Appropriation Bill.
con-
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."-(Viscount Bangor).

Labourers Bill.

Education Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
The MINISTER OF EDUCATION
(Viscount Charlemont): Before entering
on the details of this Bill, of which I now
beg to move the Second Reading, I would
like to make it clear that no radical
alteration in the principles of the 1923
Act is contemplated. In the many
criticisms which have been directed
against this present Measure, both in
another place and outside, there has been
the suggestion that it alters in fundamental particulars the democratic basis.
of our existing legislation. I would like
to say that this is not the view of the
Government. The 1923 Act placed for
the first time a large measure of educational control in the hands of the people
themselves through the medium of the
education authorities and the education
committees, and the present Bill is designed only to give better effect to these
principles of administration. I quite
realise that it may be a matter of opinion
whether some of the provisions are in
advance in this direction, but I venture
to say that, taking a broad view, the Bill
when passed will have the effect of widening the popular interest in educational
matters which is so necessary and so
valuable if a real advance in education
is to be achieved.
The opening Clauses of the Bill-
Clauses 1, 2 and 3-seek to improve in
certain respects the administrative
machinery which was set up by the
Act of 1923. As the House is aware,
that Act established county and county
borough councils as education authorities
who function through education committees which are in some cases co-terminous in the extent of their area with
the authority, and in other cases administer sub-divisions of the whole
county. The county boroughs of Belfast and Londonderry and the counties of
Down, Armagh and Fermanagh have
each a single education committee, while
Counties Antrim, Tyrone and Londonderry are each sub-divided into a number
of regional areas.
The proposal in Clause 1 of the Bill for
a nominated element leaves the composition and powers of the education
authority itself untouched, and seeks to
add to each of the education committees,
whether it is a committee for the whole
area of the authority or for a subdivision only, a nominated element which
is to be representative of the transferors
of schools transferred in that area to the
education authority. It has been asked
why such a nominated element is considered desirable, and if so, why other
interests in addition to the transferors of
schools should not also be included. The
answer to the first question is that it is
often advisable to recognise existing interests, particularly in a matter such as
education. Schools were conducted on a
voluntary basis in Ireland for nearly one
hundred years by managers, who were
for the most part the clergymen of the
various churches.
They provided buildings, in some cases
with the assistance of the State, and un
dertook all the multifarious duties of
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
school management, including, of course,
the appointment of teachers. It is only,
I think, fair to say that the longstanding exercise of these functions gave
these voluntary bodies something in the
nature of a special interest in education.
It is true that the large proportion of
the necessary expenditure, including in
more recent years the whole of the remuneration of the teachers, was paid by
the State and it is also a fact that the
school buildings have not been all that
they ought to have been. Yet the Government considers it reasonable that when
these schools are transferred to the public
authorities, the transferors should receive
representation on the regional education
committees which assume the management.
An interesting parallel to this may be
adduced from the Local Government Act
of 1898 which superseded the old Grand
Juries and set up the present county
councils. An arrangement was made in
that Act for the co-option for some years
of former Grand Jurors on the new
bodies an arrangement which was admittedly of great advantage in inaugurating the new system.
The other criticism which may be
offered, namely, that other interests in
addition to those of the transferors of
schools have a claim to be included, may
be answered in a practical manner. It
would be quite impossible to give representation to all the other parties who
might for one reason or another be interested in education without unduly
increasing the size of the education committees, and in any case their interests
are hardly on a par with the special interests of those who formerly had control
of the schools. I hope, therefore, that
this Clause will meet with general approval and that it will have the effect
of facilitating the transfer of more schools
to the education authorities by those who
for one reason or another have hitherto
held the view that they should not relinquish their interest in education. I
believe that this nominated element
should and will prove very useful on the
education committees, as they will
naturally have an intimate contact with
the parents and the children of their
area.
In the Second Clause of the Bill an
effort is also made to associate more closely
with the transferred or provided school
the interests of the transferring parties
and to ensure that the composition of the
local school management committee really
represents the interests of the children in
attendance at the schools. As everyone
is aware, the question of the selection of
a teacher is of paramount importance in
this matter, and special provisions are
accordingly made so that no teacher can
be appointed to any school who is likely.
to be out of harmony with the opinions of
those primarily interested in that school.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Appointed by the Ministry?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Ministry have the last word. You must have
a final authority somewhere. The Committee is to be composed in such a way
that of its members not less than onehalf are representative of the transferors
and former managers, while the remaining members are to be composed of nominees of the parents and of the education
authority, together with the teachers'
representatives.
The safeguards in relation to the appointment of a teacher are quite simple.
It is laid down that the education
authority, acting of course through the
regional or county borough committee,
receives all applications for the appointment and transmits those to the local
school committee, which selects a short
list of three desirable applicants. Clause
3 of the Bill prescribes the manner in
which this list is to be dealt with by the
education authority, the various possibilities being given in sub-clause (b) on
page 6.
The general purport of the
arrangement is that the teacher shall be
acceptable to the local committee, subject
always to the right of appeal on the part
of the education authority to the
Ministry.
In Clause 2, sub-clause (5), further
duties are specifically laid down for these
local school management committees but
no appreciable change is proposed from the
arrangements which already exist in
practice.
I now come to Clause 4 of the Bill,
which deals with the provision of Bible
instruction in provided and transferred
schools, and here I wish to make a few
preliminary remarks in regard to the
course of events which has led up to this
provision. It has been said that the former system of education in Ireland prior
to 1923 was a denominational system;
that the Act of 1923 definitely set up
a secularist system and that in the present
D
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
Bill we are again reverting to practical
denominationalism so far as the schools
under the control of the local education
authorities are concerned. I think I can
say that this is not a true historical
picture of the question.
Generally speaking, it is correct to say
that the system of education prior to 1923
was in practice denominational, although
in theory the schools were open for united
secular and for separate denominational
instruction. I do not, therefore, dispute
the statement that the original system was
denominational, but I submit that it is
quite a mistake to say that the Act of
1923 was definitely secularist. Owing to
the fundamental differences between
voluntary control and control by a public
authority it was obviously impossible in
the Act of 1923-as indeed it would be
impossible in the present Bill to
-
countenance in public schools under an
authority a definite and compulsory
association with any particular Church
and with a particular form of religious
belief. For this reason it was necessary
in the 1923 Act to exclude from among
the duties of an education authority that
of compelling it to provide catechetical
or denominational instruction for the
children at the schools. There is, however, a great difference between excluding
such a provision from among the duties
of the authority and preventing the
