authority from allowing such instruction
to be given.
Section 26 of the 1923 Act, on the
other hand, definitely made it the duty
of the education authority to afford opportunities for such instruction; that is
to say, every facility was to be given for
this instruction on a voluntary basis,
whether given by the teachers or by outside persons such as clergymen. This
should dispose of any allegation that it
was a secularist Act; a secularist Act
would have definitely prohibited the possibility of any such instruction in the
school-as indeed is the position in the
council schools in England. Furthermore, our Act gave a definite and
specific right of entry to people such
as clergymen that is, the right to
visit a school and give to or examine the children in the form of
denominational instruction acceptable to
their parents, which again is very far from
secularism. I hope that this disposes of
the suggestion which has been made that
our Act of 1923 was on a secularist basis.
no
I now turn to the Clause in the present
Bill which has been labelled as a reversion
to denominationalism. We here propose
to ensure that Bible instruction which is
definitely non-denominational shall be
given in the provided and transferred
schools. In this we only make statutory
what is the general state of affairs at the
present time. In the 1923 Act there was
mention of undenominational instruction, and it has been found to be
perfectly possible and legal, under that
Act, to make it a condition of the terms
of the employment of teachers in schools
under the authorities that they should
give Bible instruction; and a clause to
the effect that it shall be provided in
schools transferred to the education
authorities has been embodied in many of
the transfer deeds. The clause in the
present Bill only seeks to make statutory
what is already the general practice. It
had been represented that this course was
advisable in view of the fact that the
guarantees embodied in deeds of transfer
applied only to the schools which were
transferred and not to the new provided
schools, and also that the arrangement
made in regard to the obligation to give
the instruction as one of the conditions of
employment of the teachers might be terminated at any time in the future. Every
effort has been made to secure that
reasonable protection should be given to
the teachers in regard to the giving of the
instruction and that the instruction
should be entirely undenominational and
such as could be accepted by any body of
religious opinion.
Clause 5 emphasises still further the
protection for teachers in that it cannot
be made a condition of their appointment that they should undertake denominational instruction.
In regard to Clause 6, which has been
criticised in some quarters as an unwaranted extension of the powers of the Ministry——
Colonel
Hear hear.
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Viscount CHARLEMONT-in relation to the functions of Local Education
Authorities. I would like to say that no
real departure from the principles of the
1923 Act is intended. It is rather that
we seek to rectify what we regard
omission in Section 98 of that Act. This
latter section makes mention of the Minas an
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
istry's powers to make an order, firstly,
where an Education Authority makes default in performance of its duties;
secondly, where it fails to comply with an
Order of the Ministry in relation to any
of those duties; and thirdly, where it acts
in any manner which in the opinion of
the Ministry is prejudicial to the promotion of elementary or higher education.
This would appear to be already very
comprehensive, but the Ministry has been
advised that its intervention is only possible in the case of default by an
authority in relation to its duties which
it must perform and not in relation to its
powers, which it may exercise at its discretion.
This certainly reveals a defect in the
existing provisions, and the present Clause
of the Bill merely supplements Section 98
by including among the matters in which
the Ministry may make an Order those
functions which fall within the powers of
the authority, though not within its
duties. In any case, all such Orders,
whether under the existing law or under
that which is proposed, can only be made.
after a full public inquiry, and it is inconceivable that the Ministry could take
action unless it were fully supported by
public opinion.
The next Clause to which I would
direct particular attention is Clause 10,
which will empower the Ministry to make
grants of one-half of the expenditure involved in regard to the building of new
schools or the enlargement of existing
schools even though the conditions which
have hitherto been in force are not
fulfilled, that is to say, under this Clause
grants of 50 per cent. will in future be
available to managers of voluntary
schools, who do not for some reason wish
to accept any form of outside control.
Under the Act of 1923 education
authorities were empowered to assist in
the provision of voluntary school buildings with grants which have usually
been two-thirds of the cost, but as
condition it was necessary that the
school should be placed under school
committee on which the local education
authority had representation, known as
a "four-and-two" committee.
a
a
This form of school management,
which, it should be remembered, does
not alter the voluntary character of the
itself to
school, has not commended
some of the elements in our community,
and as a concession the Government now
seeks to make it possible for these persons to receive the 50 per cent. grant
while continuing the old system of individual school management.
I may
add that this is the limit of concession
which can be given.
The only other provisions of the Bill
to which I would direct special attention at this stage are those in relation to
school attendance. These will be
found in Clauses 16 and 17. The
present limits of age for compulsory
school attendance are from six to fourteen years, provided, however, that an
education authority may make a by-law
prescribing that the lower limit shall be
reduced to five years. It is not legally
possible to fix any intermediate limit by
by-law between five and six years, and
it has been suggested that this is desirable. The age limits prescribed in the
Act do not refer to the actual sixth or
fourteenth birthday of the child, but to
the term dates which are fixed for that
area by the authority, so that a lower
limit of six years for commencing
attendance may in practice mean anything up to six years and some months,
and it has been represented that this is
too advanced an age for the child to begin to go to school. The normal lower
limit is five years in both England and
Scotland, but it is hardly yet possible
to make this the general rule in
area. As a compromise therefore in Clause
16 an authority will have the further
discretion of prescribing five-and-a-half
years as the commencing age for compulsory attendance throughout its area,
or in part of its area.
our
The other matter in connection with
school attendance is to be found in subclause (1) of Clause 17. Representations have been made that the existing
procedure for the enforcement of school
attendance is too protracted, and that
as long a time as six months may elapse
from the date on which the first action
is taken until a conviction is secured at
the court. The existing procedure is in
four stages a warning to the parent; a
school attendance order made by the
education authority, through an attendance sub-committee; the ordering of a
prosecution on the non-fulfilment of
such an order, and the hearing of the
case at Petty Sessions. It is proposed to
omit the first of these stages, that is to
say, the warning to the parent. In any
case, before
an attendance order
made, the parent has an opportunity of
offering
before
an explanation
the
is
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
School Attendance Sub-Committee, and
it seems reasonable that this stage in the
proceedings should be entered on without
any preliminary warning notice.
The other Clauses in the Bill to which
I have not made specific reference are
either designed to clear up some omissions and ambiguities in the 1923 Act, or
are to supplement that Act in certain.
directions which have appeared desirable
as a result of experience. I do not propose to enter into them in detail at this
stage. I hope that the House will
agree that the Bill fulfils the purpose
for which it was intended, namely, to
make our educational system even more
acceptable to all classes in the community and to further the purpose to
which all educational legislation is
directed, namely, the better advancement of the young people of the country.
I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The keystone of
this Bill is the Clause which endows
Protestant religious teaching. (Hon.
Members: Oh). This teaching is to be
part of the compulsory curriculum in the
provided and transferred schools-the only
schools supported wholly at the public
charge. In the letter which the then
Chief Secretary for Ireland, Lord Stanley,
penned in 1831 to the Duke of Leinster,
the first president of the Board of Commissioners of Education, setting up the
national schools, he said scrupulous care
must be taken not to discriminate between
any description of Christian people. This
Bill discriminates.
You will pardon me for saying you are
not taking a peculiarly opportune time in
which to discriminate. Just now in the
east of Europe Christendom sees what it
has never seen before-the Government of
a vast population engaged in an intensive
campaign of atheism-a Government
whose avowed object is the abolition of
Christianity and one of whose members,
one Lenin, declared that "war on
Christians of all kinds is for us an absolute duty."
On the second day on which this Bill
was being debated in Committee in another place the General Synod of the
Protestant Episcopal Church in Ireland
was eulogising the fairness-the generosity
-of the Government of the Free State in
respect of the education of Protestant
children in that territory. The Right
Rev. Dr. White, Bishop of Limerick,
said: "The Government of the Free State
has dealt with us most liberally regarding
educational facilities in rural schools.
The Rev. Canon
"The
Hughes said:
Minister for Education has been extraordinarily courteous, and has gone a great
way to meet us. We are in an extraordinarily favourable position as regards
education. As long as we can get ten
children of our faith together the Government will pay the teachers, and we are entitled to give our children the exact type
of religious teaching we wish

In that territory the non-Catholics form
about seven per cent. of the population.
The Government of the Free State does
not draw a dividing line between the
schools of the denominations. The minority does not suffer there. The Prime
Minister quoted on the Second Reading of
this Bill in another place a saying that
minorities must suffer. I think the late
Lord Balfour coined that phrase. (Hon.
Senators: No. Mr. Birrell). I speak
under correction. I am told it was Mr.
Birrell. I distinctly recollect Lord
Balfour making use of that expression in
the time of the Boer War. In any event
be the coiner of the phrase either Lord
Balfour or Mr. Birrell-and they were not
kindred spirits, they did not see eye to
eye on many subjects-whoever the author
was there is at least one minority that
can say the saying is perfectly true.
Let me remind the House what Lord
Acton said about minorities: "The most
certain test by which we can judge
whether a nation is really free is the
amount of security enjoyed by minorities." Ask the minority in your midst what
they feel about your régime-not in
educational matters only-and you will
have their answer in the Jewish words:
"O daughter of Babylon, blessed shall he
be who shall repay thee as thou hast paid
to us.
I
This Bill gave the Government an opportunity of working equality in public
grants between the two sets of denominational schools in operation in Ulster.
am sorry the Government have missed that
opportunity. A question which demanded
breadth of outlook on the part of the
Government has been left unsettled. The
spectre of educational discontent still
walks the province. The Catholic
minority concede to the Government the
fullest public control in all secular subjects of education. They will not-they
cannot submit to the teaching in their
schools of "county council religion."
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
Their schools will give the State as full
value as the provided and transferred
schools in the broad domain of secular
learning. This Bill decrees they must
pay a penalty for following, in the words.
of Sydney Smith, the highest of all
considerations, the noblest of all guides--
their conscience. I do not recollect reading in any Act of Parliament this term
"Bible Instruction." Professor Henry,
of the Queen's University, said lately that
"this idea of simple Bible instruction is
one which distinguishes Protestantism
from Catholicism.' Bible instruction
must fall under the category of either
religious instruction or secular instruction. Under which do you say?
The Bill says a teacher in the provided and transferred schools must give
this "Bible instruction" in a bona-fide
What does "bona-fide" mean
manner.
here? His instruction must also be instruction "based upon the Holy Scriptures," not, mark, the mere reading of
the sacred text, but instruction, expository and interpretative, of the text. He
must exclude "instruction as to any
tenet of any denomination." Surely
you give him an impossible task. He
must only refer to common tenets, I
suppose. What tenet is common to
Christian denominations? What tenet is
common even to non-Catholic denominations?
Beliefs the common heritage of
all Christendom for 1900 years, are in
these latter days challenged, assailed and
sapped in high places of Anglicanism and
Nonconformity.
The noble Viscount, the Leader of the
House, in an amusing little homily in
the Senate last session mildly chided
Catholics for a fancied unwillingness to
allow their children to be taught the
fundamental beliefs of all Christians.
There is not now, there never at any
time has been, such unwillingness-nay,
it is the very opposite. Who teaches the
fundamental beliefs of Christianity but
Catholics? Can the noble Viscount name
even one fundamental doctrine accepted
by all Protestant bodies nowadays?
Then the parents of 10 children in any
provided or transferred school can compel
the education committee to provide this
Bible instruction. This provision is a
proof that this Bill cannot and will not
do justice to Catholics. It is a proof also
that the Leader of the House did not err
on the side of incaution when he predicted on 12th July last year that "so
far as the Government can do such a
thing the Protestant faith in the Protestant schools will be saved from any
attacks." When asked here later on why
he did not extend like protection to the
Catholic faith in Catholic schools his
answer was it was protected sufficiently
already. What childlike naiveté!
con-
One would not be rash in surmising
that the four-and-two Committee system
has got its quietus under this Bill as far
at any rate as the Catholics are
cerned. Your pretext that it made for
popular control by the inclusion of two
representatives of the Education Committee was hollow and insincere. These two
nominees were not elected by the people.
The manager did represent his own people.
It was once argued that the two would
be the watch-dogs of the ratepayers.
Since the Derating Act the ratepayers in
rural areas have practically no rates to
pay and the local authorities none to
collect. In one county 99 per cent. of
the payments of the Education Committee are now made by the Government.
Did it ever strike you that this system
put on the manager the burden and heat
of the day-the drudgery without the
authority? Very few of the educated
classes have condescended to the humdrum
routine of helping to run primary schools.
Doubtless when teachers are to be appointed and well-paid posts to be filled the
committees will be as busy as bees in June
after a long hibernation. This so-called
"popular control"-to adopt a phrase of
Burke-shuts one door to unpresuming
merit and opens another and a wider
to impudent importunity. One hears of
complaints of wirepulling, complaints that
the best men are not appointed, complaints that merit counts less than friends
on committees. Let us hope that the
children do not count least of all.
Whilst on this topic let me say what
the friends of the Government shut their
ears against, that the managers have
never got anything like their due for
their services to primary education. The
Act of 1923 aimed at scotching them.
They survive. Where there has not been
a campaign of misrepresentation about
their work there has been a conspiracy of
silence. But there are some who do not
forget what a dynamic contribution to the
progress of the elementary schools the
managers made, almost unaided, for one
hundred years. The Tory Party had no
need for "popular control" of the schools
through that long period.
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
The Bill gives to the Manager one-half
of the cost of providing and equipping a
new voluntary school, including the acqui-
"Prosition and preparation of the site.
vision" includes, I assume, such a necessary incident as the purchase of a rent
or rent charge on a site. The Bill also
gives one-half of the cost of the enlargement, alteration, reconstruction or
equipment of an existing voluntary
school. This proposa! embodies a
valuable admission, and is a contradiction to many things said by rash
supporters of the Government in the past.
The admission is that the voluntary schools
under individual clerical management are
a necessary part of the educational equipment of Ulster. The contradiction is to
the sayings of gentlemen who boasted that
the day of the managerial system was
over. It was forgotten that the Catholic
laity would have a word to say about that.
Their resistance was the rock on which
your well-laid plans were shattered.
This "Half" of the initial capital outlay
on a new school, or the enlarging of an
old school, is a grudging and inadequate
concession. It does not extend to current
outlay on repairs of an existing school.
Let us see how it works out in a concrete
case. Take the case of a voluntary school
to be provided at a cost of £10,000. The
Catholics concerned will have to find
£5,000 of this sum. The interest on the
£5,000 will be £250 or £300 a year. That
is to say, about £5 or £6 a week will be
paid by the managers of the school for the
privilege of giving five or six hours definite
religious teaching a week. Did the
managers not insist on these five or six
hours being so allotted the school would
be provided without any cost whatever to
themselves.
No Catholic free voluntary school has,
I think, been built since the 1923 Act, but
nearly £1,000,000 has been budgeted for
in the same period by the State for the
erection of provided schools and the reconstruction of transferred schools--every one
of which is for all purposes a Protestant
school in substance and in fact. I wish
to stress even at this stage of the Bill
the reasonableness of our claim regarding
repairs. The Bill concedes one half of
the capital expenditure on building or rebuilding. Why not concede the current
repairs? In all fairness why leave upon
the shoulders of a people, most of whom
are poor, the whole burden of the necessary repairs of their schools? Why not
give the current cost of lighting, heating
and cleansing instead of the fraction of
one-half? The sum would not be large.
For
Numbers of our schools have been long
in use. The provisions of the new schools
for the Catholic people will not put an
undue demand upon the public purse.
Repairs of existing schools are, however,
a recurring source of concern and worry
to many managers and their people.
the committee schools you provide 50 per
cent. of the cost of repairs. For the provided and transferred schools 100 per cent.
For the free voluntary schools no per cent.
Can you justify this differentiation to an
unprejudiced reasonable mind?
To a sparse and scattered congregation
the yearly outlay on the repairs of their
parochial school is a burden fraught with
anxiety. By refusing concessions in this
direction you perpetuate a sense of inequality and grievance. It is the children
who must suffer from schoolhouses in want
of repair.
One fresh point I wish to mention. I submit to the House that in equity your concessions ought to be extended to the case
of voluntary schools, the site of which has
been acquired since the passing of the Act
of 1923, or schools which since that date
have been reconstructed, renovated or
enlarged under plans approved of by the
Ministry of Education.
I know of one school in County Tyrone
which has been enlarged and renovated
recently and made practically a new
school at a cost, including equipment, of
slightly less than £950. The Ministry of
Education were pleased with the work
done. The inspector was highly gratified
at the way in which the manager had put
his hand to the plough. Yet that sum of
£950 is still to be raised and is now a
parish debt. Surely that is a case which
is within the spirit, if not within the
letter, of the concession under Clause 10
of the Bill.
This Bill recognises that the voluntary
schools are a necessity to Ulster, yet not a
penny is to be given from the public funds
towards the current year-by-year repairs
of these schools, and only one halfpenny
out of each penny expended on their
lighting, heating and cleansing.
They
are to be kept on starvation diet and are
not to have nourishing food. It all seems
so narrow, so stingy. You tolerate them
on sufferance.
Almost all the Catholic schools in the
rural areas are small parochial schools.
That type will endure; the best fruit of
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
scholarship will be garnered in these
schools and not in your mammoth palatial
- schools.
Those little schools will need
repairs as the days go by. The birds may
fly through their worn roof-trees for aught
the State cares. The children may wince
under the rain and wind, but the Government will smile and smile and say: "Our
withers are unwrung."
Very well; but
see if your law-favoured schools fare any
the better or prosper any the more for
that.
The spokesmen of the Catholic voluntary schools have toiled hard to obtain
full justice for those schools. To a limited
extent only, judged by material standards, have they succeeded. But a
principle has been vindicated; a trail has
been blazed which others may follow
after. This question cannot rest where
this Bill leaves it. The Catholics will
struggle on to the goal of just laws and
equal treatment; they will keep a tight
grip of the schools which are so priceless
to them and so necessary to the public
weal. Those schools will in secular knowledge equal or excel your State schools.
The Catholic voluntary schools, though
crippled, will stand out in isolation and
dignity, symbols of a generous sacrifice by
conscientious parents who have few friends
in this Parliament but who are strong in
numbers and firm in purpose, and who
will at the end of the day, be the day long
or short, be invincible in the justice of
their cause.
Mr. LONG: During the delivery of the
very able and interesting speech of the
hon. and learned Senator who has just
sat down, I was wondering whether if that
eminent counsel had been briefed for the
other side he could not have delivered an
even more earnest and convincing address.
I believe he could.
Now, as chairman of an education
authority for three years, and as chairman
of a regional committee, I have noted the
way in which the Education Act was administered in County Londonderry. From
my own personal experience of the working of the Act I would say there has been
no differential treatment as between Protestants and Roman Catholics. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear.) Of course the
House knows that by far the major
portion of the amount expended on elementary education is the payment of the
teachers' salaries. That forms the bulk
of the expenditure, and this is paid direct
by the Government to the teachers. I have
never heard of a case of differential treatment under this head. Different conditions are embodied in the Act for
transferred and voluntary schools, but
this is not confined to Northern Ireland,
where, indeed, the conditions in respect of
certain grants are more favourable to
voluntary schools than in other countries.
The grants for heating, lighting and
cleaning are the only items of which the
local authorities pay a part. In the
United States of America as Senators no
doubt know, State schools are erected and
the Church is informed that if they wish
to have their own they could pay for them.
In England, where I made inquiries last
week, the conditions for voluntary schools
are not as generous as in Northern
Ireland.
In the working of technical instruction
schemes large numbers of Roman Catholic
pupils avail themselves of these schemes,
and the grants earned by junior technical
schools under Roman Catholic control
would probably be three or four times the
amount earned under the old régime in
Dublin.
In the matter of health and well-being
schemes, books for necessitous children
and meals and clothing for children, I
should say that more than half the expenditure is on Roman Catholic children.
In the scholarship schemes which we
award to pupils of promise whose parents
are not able to afford a secondary education there has never been any differentiation in the County of Londonderry. The
great majority of these
Roman
Catholic.
are
In the matter of the care and education of afflicted children, in the grants
for rural science and other subjects of
practical instruction the same thing applies. In secondary education also there
is no differential treatment and it would
be interesting to obtain from the Ministry the amount of the very large increase
in capitation and other grants now given
to Roman Catholic secondary schools compared with the grants obtained before
the services were transferred to the
Ministry.
I regret that I had to mention this
matter, but owing to the tone of the hon.
and learned Senator's speech I thought
it right to give the House that information. In a transferred school at Ballykelly, in County Londonderry, in a recent
year, first place in the Diocese of Derry
was gained by Roman Catholic pupils in
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
the Roman Catholic religious instruction
examination. In this transferred school
a Roman Catholic assistant teacher gave
the religious instruction.
In Slaughmannus school about half
the pupils are now Roman Catholics.
The Regional Committee last year appointed a Roman Catholic junior assistant mistress who looks after the
religious instruction. of the Roman
Catholic children. The work of the
school is going on most harmoniously.
The number on the rolls has been more
than doubled since transfer.
In regard to the Act, I do not see that
it makes very much difference in County
Londonderry. The procedure there was
that the education authority nominated
outsiders to act on the committee, and these
outsiders included two or three priests
and two or three Protestant clergy. I
daresay I am not quite sure
of
the numbers-the nominated element
amounted to a fourth of the total, or
thereabouts. Then, with regard to Clause
2 the only difference is that we had no
school teachers on our committees in the
past in the matter of school attendance
committees. The power of appointment
of the teacher has been as laid down in
this Act. That has been the procedure
in County Londonderry.
With regard to Clause 4 the procedure
has been that the Clause providing for
Bible instruction was inserted in every
conveyance. The Clause is as follows:
Bible instruction shall be given by
the teaching staff during the half hour
set apart for religious instruction, the
programme to be adopted by the education authority in accordance with the
memorandum of the Ministry of Education dated 4th July, 1925.
With regard to Clause 6 the matter
does not interest us in County Londonderry as the Ministry, I am sure, will
never be called upon to supersede them
for any dereliction of duty.
applies to Clause 7.
The same
With regard to Clause 8, that is, the
estimates of expenditure submitted to the
education authority by the managers of
schools we found that in the past these
were very often inflated-I would not say
fraudulently, but I might even be justified in using that word. However, I will
not.
Our secretary was surprised to get
a 'phone message from a merchant in
town quite recently asking if there was
any rule saying that prices should be
doubled in regard to these matters.
(Laughter.) Our secretary replied that
there was no such rule and that he should
put in net prices.
With regard to Clause 10 I am not
yet convinced that there was any necessity for the Clause at all. And when it
is so ungraciously received I would be in- |
clined to withdraw or delete it altogether.
In certain quarters it has come to be recognised as the highest political wisdom
to pretend to be dissatisfied with all concessions no matter how generous and with
all grants no matter how adequate. The
policy of the horse-leech is still being
pursued with considerable success. In
my view the provision is generous.
With regard to Clause 13 I should have
liked the provision providing hostels for
pupils attending certain schools to be applied also to technical schools. In the
Committee stage I will put down an
Amendment to that effect. With regard
to Clause 25, I do not think that that
Clause was necessary at all. An education authority should reciprocate with an
adjoining education authority and no contribution should be payable by either.
On the whole I support the Bill and I
believe it will be a contribution towards
the settlement of this controversy.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
will not detain the House as long as the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell)
did. I think his speech would have been
more appropriate to Amendments on the
Committee stage, and he will, no doubt,
make just as long speeches when he moves
Amendments to the Bill in Committee. In
my humble opinion we are suffering at
the present time from what I would describe as too much under-education and
over-education. What I mean is that
sufficient attention is not paid to undereducation in our primary schools, and
hon. Members will understand what I
mean when I tell them that in a depôt
not far from this City there were three
recruits in one batch who could neither
read or write. I heard of another case
of a boy who went to a technical school,
and they had to begin to teach him primary education before they could teach
him technical education. I am a great
believer in the old three R's. That type
of education better fitted a boy for going
out into the world than does some of this
fancy education that is imparted nowadays. What I mean by over-education is
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
overthat there ought to be more restriction on
those who go in for University or other
higher education, and more attention
should be paid to the particular profession
they intend to pursue in after life. I am
a poor miserable specimen of an
educated man. I was four years at a
distinguished University, of which I am
a B.A. and M.A. I made a great many
friends there and had a good time, but
I do not believe that at any time afterwards have I benefited from the education I received at that University,
although I may have a broader mind than
I otherwise would have had. I think that
such higher education ought to be more
suited than it is to the profession one
intends to adopt after leaving the
University.
There is another point I want to refer
to.
It is all very well to talk about voluntary schools, technical schools, and the
building of new schools, but we have got
to consider in the future where the money
is to come from to pay for these schools.
In the case of my own County of Down,
there will be an enormous demand for
educational expenditure during the coming year. I would like those who represent the ratepayers to realize that in my
county we have received the limit of the
Government grant for ordinary educational requirements, and that in future,
except in special circumstances, the ratepayers will have to pay twopence for what
they have got for a penny in the past. I
believe that if this scheme is carried out
in County Down the rate for education
instead of being fivepence, as it is now,
will be nearly tenpence. The rates for
education in England and Scotland have
risen in some cases to 1/6 and 2/-, and
they are going up every year. Therefore,
I hope the Regional Committees, not only
in County Down but in the other five
counties, will be careful to send forward
moderate schemes, because if they do not
the education authority will have to step
in and cut them down.
With regard to the Bill itself, I am not
going into the religious question, but I
think that one of the greatest injuries done
to Irish education was inflicted many years
ago. I have been told that in former
days Roman Catholic and Protestant
children went to the same school, played
the same games, entered into each others
lives, and were happy. Now, in these
separate schools, the children are brought
up from their very infancy to hate each
other. My last point is this. The
Government have been given a great deal
of credit for trying to meet everybody's
wishes and desires. Some they have met
and others they have not, but there is one
very important body they have never tried
to meet, and that is the county councils.
Instead of giving the county councils any
advantages under this Bill they are going
to be shorn of a great deal of their power.
On the Committee stage of the Bill I intend to move Amendments dealing with
the powers and duties of county councils,
although I may have no chance of carrying them. I know the Bill will be passed,
and I will give it my support, but if I
do not make my protest on this particular
point now and then refer to it afterwards,
the Government might ask why I did not
make my protest when the Bill was before
the House. With these few remarks I
wish the Bill good luck, although I feel
that the county councils have not been
treated fairly in this matter.
I
Mr. LESLIE: I must admit that I
approach the consideration of this question without any form of enthusiasm.
can scarcely conceive, if I may say so, a
Bill less likely to provoke enthusiasm
than the Bill before us. It may be perhaps that I am singularly hard to move
by any form of propaganda for altering
the educational system of the Province.
The fact is-and I do not seek to disguise
it; I never have sought to disguise it, and
I have admitted it on more than one occasion both in this House and elsewhere-I
am, as regards the Act of 1923, the
Londonderry Act, about as complete a
whole-hogger as it would be possible to
find. I believe that that Act was broadly
based on principles that, had they only
been appreciated, would have secured for
that Act the meed of praise and the recognition which it has hitherto lacked.
I think one must say that the fact is that
the scheme of the Londonderry Act was
too broad for the extremely parochial
sense of the majority of the people in
Northern Ireland.
Had that Act been given a little more
time, and had some of those who rather
suggest those small quadrupeds who yap
at the heels of larger creatures remained
silent, I think perhaps that public opinion
would have settled down and come to its
own conclusions, and they would have
been of a distinctly laudatory character.
We have just heard from the right hon.
and gallant Gentleman (Colonel Sharman-
Crawford), how very much better in his
view-and we most of us agree with it-
Education Bill-
Second Reading
TH SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
were the older days when children of this
or that form of faith joined together, as
little worldlings, if you like, but as products of the one Creator, without spending their entire time in dwelling on what
they understood to be their religious
differences, which were in fact and in
truth, simply the creation of those who
should have taught them something
better about religion.
Passing from that to the principles, as
it seems to me, on which the Londonderry Act was based, there is not time
now to go into them all, nor is this occasion, some seven years after the introduction of the Bill, a peculiarly suitable
occasion for making a Second Reading
speech on that Bill. We are concerned
more with the Bill before us. Amongst
the principles of the Act of 1923-and
these are the principles which I had
hoped would have endured for everthey are quite apart from the minor
matters of development and so on which
would necessarily happen as time went on
-these principles were distinctly public
control in exchange for public money, the
recognition of parental authority, and
complete freedom as regards religious
matters whether for the teacher or for the
pupil. I may sum that up by saying
that the whole of this was constructed in
order to obtain the most complete compliance with both the spirit and the letter
of the 1920 Act. I am afraid that the
Bill before us does not entirely conform
to these principles. It seems to me that
at the outset, in, I think, the first Clause,
the Bill violates one of the principles to
which I have alluded. It is endeavouring to reproduce altogether too much of
the nominated element, and in so far as
it does that it is working against the
system of popular control in exchange for
public money to which I have alluded.
It may be remembered that when the
original Act was before us suggestions
were made for introducing a nominated
element.
The noble Marquis, who was then in
the House, and who was Minister of
Education, pointed out that if he provided for nominated members of the committees he would be simply taking those
who, for some reason or other, the education authority did not want. It was, I
think, pretty universally admitted that
any nominated members added to those
committees would not be received with
peculiar grace on the part of their colleagues. It has been suggested sinceindeed, I think I made the suggestion
myself that one member might be added
to any regional committee in an advisory
capacity, after the manner in which the
old Department of Agriculture and
Technical Instruction was represented on
the technical instruction committees.
I am inclined to think that, although
I may attack the Bill here and there, the
attacks will not be of a very serious
character, and such faults as I shall find
can, I think, be very easily remedied.
One passes now to the question of Clause
4, which deals with Bible instruction. I
do not like the introduction of this Clause.
It seems to me, for one reason, to be an
attempt to divorce Bible instruction from
religious instruction.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: They are
two different things.
Mr. LESLIE: I quite see that they are
two different things.
The child is to
have a half-hour of religious instruction,
plus half-an-hour of Bible instruction.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No.
Mr. LESLIE: Can that possibly occur!
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No.
Mr. LESLIE: You may have either
simple Bible teaching, what has been
called county-council religion, or religious
instruction.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The local
authority cannot give religious instruction.
Mr. LESLIE: They have half-an-hour
during which the school is to be open for
religious instruction, which may be given
by the teacher, but that half-hour is not
to be on the same footing as Bible
instruction.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No, Bible
instruction is to be given in the half-hour
which is non-compulsory.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member should sit down if the Minister
wishes to speak.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry. Perhaps
we had better leave the question.
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
܂
V
GWEN.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In the opinion of
the hon. and learned Senator, is Bible instruction secular instruction or religious
instruction?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Simple Bible
teaching is given in the non-compulsory
half-hour. The education authorities are
not allowed to provide denominational
instruction of any kind, but the teacher
can give it by arrangement if he is wellknown and none of the parents of the
children object.
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Lord has not
told us which of these two half-hours is
the extra half-hour. The school will be
open for the necessary public curriculum
for whatever the number of hours is, plus
half-an-hour for religious instruction,
plus half-an-hour for Bible instruction.
The hon. and learned Senator in front of
me asks whether this Bible instruction is
secular instruction or religious instruction.
I think that question had better be
addressed to the authors of the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
there is still a difference of opinion about
this. The half-hour is compulsory for
Bible instruction; the denominational instruction can be given at any other time.
They cannot be given concurrently.
They cannot have simple Bible teaching
and extended religious instruction with
denominational ideas given at one and the
same time. There is only one half-hour
for simple Bible teaching.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Unless they are
both the same.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: We will
not go into that at present.
Mr. LESLIE: If a parent desires
that his child should obtain religious
instruction the child will have to
attend school for another half-hour. That
is using the opportunity which the education authority is bound to afford for the
religious instruction that it does not provide, in addition to the half-hour without
it.
That seems to be the answer. What
I am thinking of is that the burden may
be too heavy for the child to bear.
Passing from the question of Bible instruction, one comes to Clause 10. I have
always supported the Londonderry Act
of 1923, and I find it a little bit difficult
now to turn round and support a proposal
in Clause 10 to give a grant for the building of a voluntary school without requiring the four-and-two committee which
was previously insisted on. I really cannot say one thing one day and the exact
opposite the day after. Had this offer of
the 50 per cent. been likely to secure peace,
it would, perhaps, have been worth while
to have sacrificed something that I regard
as a principle in order to ensure it; but it
does not seem that peace is likely to ensue.
It is held everywhere that this 50 per
cent. is a concession to Roman Catholics.
If that were so, I should be obliged to say
that to my mind it would be contrary to
the principles of the Government of Ireland Act. But it is certainly not so
expressed in the Bill. The concession of
50 per cent. is to be given to any voluntary
school if the Ministry is satisfied that the
school is a necessity, and that the necessary
provision cannot otherwise be made.
Apparently the judgment is to rest with
the Ministry. That seems to me to point
to an objection on the usual grounds. It
is giving the Ministry power to dispose of
public money at its own sweet will.
We have to bear in mind that the
amount of public money available for the
purposes of Clause 10 will have to be
voted by Parliament year after year. We
cannot expect that it will be a very great
amount, but, I think, we must expect
that the scramble for a share of it will
attract a very large number of participants, and those participants will certainly.
not be confined to those who are Roman
Catholics. I must confess that I was impressed by the hon. and learned Senator's
argument, that if you give the money to
build a school and yet refuse to give any
money towards its upkeep you are placing
yourselves in a somewhat curious position.
If it is necessary to build a school, presumably it is intended that it should be
used. The voluntary school gets nothing.
but its half share of heating and lighting,
which it gets if the local authority is
satisfied with its estimates. I do not
think any great case has been made out
for a departure from the terms of the
original Act in extending these grants.
I am not quite satisfied that there is
sufficient protection in the Bill for
teachers. It seems not impossible that
some of them may be shut out. Other
details, such as will be found in Clauses
11 and 12 about teachers' residences and
caretakers, are no doubt admirable, and
are the sort of natural development of the
Londonderry Act that one would expect.
I:
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
As regards Clause 6, which extends the
powers given under Section 98 of the
Act, I rather expected that I should feel
it incumbent on me to attack that Clause,
but the noble Viscount has told us distinctly that there is nothing in this Clause
which will enable the Ministry to exercise
their power under the original Clause
without the public inquiry provided for
by Section 98.
There remains then nothing more to say
except that the Bill should be given a
Second Reading, but that when it comes
to Committee there are details which cer-
I must
tainly will require attention.
hold out the warning that there are details which it is not competent to deal
with in this House. We cannot make any
suggestions involving the additional expenditure of public money, but we can
deal with the appointment of nominated
members, and when we are doing that we
shall, I have no doubt, remember what
the noble Viscount told us about the
appointment of aldermen in the early
days of the county councils, but we shall
not forget that those aldermen held office
for only three years, and that they came
in at the commencement of a new régime,
not after it had been some seven or eight
years at work.
the
Mr. ARMSTRONG : I think
latter part of this Bill represents a great
improvement, but there are Clauses in
the early part of it to which exception
might be taken. As to the complaints of
the hon. and learned Member opposite
(Mr. Campbell) regarding the position of
Roman Catholics, I have to say that in
the County of Armagh we have a transferred Roman Catholic School, and
it is working with perfect satisfaction
to everybody. The clergy visit it, the
teacher is a Roman Catholic; when a
vacancy occurred some time ago a Roman
Catholic was appointed. The fact is that
the thing is working in a perfectly satisfactory manner. We have Roman Catholic
members on the Education Committee,
and we all work together in the most
friendly way. They take part in all the
proceedings, and they assist us when the
reconstruction of Protestants schools is
taking place. They are perfectly honest
in all the appointments they make, and
give consideration to everybody. I think
that if people were left alone they would
suit themselves to things as they are.
The Bill, of course, is important in two
particulars. It gives assistance to denominational schools-I would rather call
them voluntary schools-and though in
principle objection may be taken to that
I think the course adopted by the Govern-
I do not
ment was, perhaps, inevitable.
think the grievance that exists at the
present time is a very serious one, but
taking a practical view of it I think fewer
schools will be transferred. That will
ease the burden on the education authorities who would be overwhelmed if all
the schools were transferred.
If I was
interested in education and was possessed
of means I do not think I should transfer
my school at all so long as I could keep
it up to the requirements of the Ministry.
In our educational area there are some
fifteen schools which will never be transferred in the lifetime of the present owners
for the reasons I have given.
The next thing is the nomination of
persons on these committees, that is
persons who represent a class not specially
identified with educational interests.
Some may be so identified and some may
not, but they are to be appointed, and,
of course, we shall have to do the best
we can. I think they will be somewhat
disappointed when they become members
of the education committees, because ninetenths of our work is dry business. We
have nothing to do with education, strictly
speaking. That rests with the Ministry.
What we have to do is to keep the machine
working, and we do that pretty successfully. I think it is rather disagreeable
that the persons who go on in a different
capacity do not go on as representing the
general taxpayers. They do not pretend
to have any special qualifications for dealing with this business matter, and I am
afraid they will be very much disappointed.
Then with regard to simple Bible
teaching, I do not see how anybody can
object to it. The facts recorded in the
Bible are common to all Christian bodies.
and with regard to the teaching of it an
ex-manager told me it is much better
taught and more often taught now than
under the National Board, because now
there is half an hour given every day,
whereas under the National Board it was
taught only once a week. I think the
grievance with regard to that is not a very
serious one.
Then there is a grievance that the exmanagers nominated on the committees
were not nominated by the education
authority. I myself am not a mem-
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
I
ber of an education authority.
am only a humble co-opted member
of the committee on which I serve.
I do not think any disrespect was meant,
but I heard that in one county they were
nominated at the first selection, but subsequently they ceased to be co-opted as
the work was heavy and the time short,
and many of them came from a distance
and had a long way to go to get home,
while the facility for self-expression which
these gentlemen enjoyed rendered the discussions somewhat protracted. (Laughter.)
That is the only objection I have to them.
The appointment of the teachers is a
thing that really matters, and I fancy
that is the reason why so many of these
transferors are anxious to get on the committees. Our system
of appointing
teachers is very much that outlined
under the Bill. A select list is sent up,
and we select the best, generally the firstmentioned. On those management committees at present there are from four to
five ex-managers or transferors, and they
Therefore,
are generally in the chair.
I do not think that this will really help
them very much. They are too numerous
-one half of the committee-and when
coupled with one-quarter representing the
parents, who in country districts know
nothing about the qualifications of
teachers, they will really be given too
much power. The transferors will
dominate the committees. It is true the
Ministry and the education authority have
power to control them, but that means
friction, and we wish to avoid that.
Then it is said the transferors are parting with property worth a million of
money. After my seven years' experience
on an education committee, I think the
estimate of a million is rather too high,
but if it is not it will involve an expenditure of a similar amount in reconditioning the buildings. After all, these
buildings were not built by the transferors or their predecessors, they were
built by the laity, and, I think, it shows
a lamentable want of confidence in the
representative laity who have been placed
by the education authorities on these committees. It shows they do not consider
them sufficiently representative of the
churches to which they belong.
I have some sympathy with the Government. I think they have done their best
in a very difficult situation, and although
there is a good deal in this Bill that I do
not very much like I do not feel in a
position to oppose it. At the same time,
I think the Government are running a
very serious risk not of killing but of
maiming their own promising infant of
which until recently they and the community were so proud. It has been a
great surprise to me during the many
years I have served to find the work going
on with such smoothness, without complaint from teachers, parents, exmanagers, or anybody else. Of course it
may have been inevitable that some change
should be made at the present time, but,
I think, it was perhaps unfortunate to
disturb the harmony which was going on
so well.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am very
glad that this difficult Measure has been
received as well as it has been, and I would
echo Senator Leslie's remark that nobody
would enter into an Amending Education
Bill with any degree of enthusiasm,
whether he spoke as a private Member or
as a Member of the Government. At the
same time, I think that this Bill will not
in effect unduly curtail the powers of
those who have administered the Act so
well up to the present time or, indeed,
make very much difference in the
ordinary procedure carried out at present.
I should like as our time is getting short,
and we have some other business to do,
to confine myself to the definite attack
that has been made on the Bill by the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell)
speaking for the Roman Catholic part of
the population.
His attack was really based on the
assumption that simple Bible teaching is
an entirely Protestant system. I do not
think that that can be proved. Everybody
nowadays must have regard to the advantage of religious instruction in schools. I
am now taking religion in the broad sense,
not in the sense in the Education Bill, as
it is the most important thing the young
person can be taught. We see the result
of the lack of such teaching in Russia.
Senator Campbell alluded to the conditions there. They are very dreadful. It
is inconceivable that those conditions could
prevail in a civilised country, but something analagous in nature might happen.
Therefore, it is essential to my mind that
the youth of this country should be well
grounded in matters of religion, and
matters of religion can best be taught,
like secular matters, when one is young.
Education Bill-
Second Reading.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
The State does not wish to secularise
education in any way, but it has to adopt
some principle which will be acceptable
to all, and I think it has done that in so
far as it is possible to do so. Simple Bible
teaching is a fundamental of Christianity,
but no Church, either Protestant or
Roman Catholic, would be content with
that alone. In the North of Ireland the
only thing it is obligatory on the education authority to provide is simple Bible.
teaching from some authorised version of
the Scriptures, and that can be either the
Douay version of the Bible or an authoritative scheme drawn up by the Roman
Catholic Church. It is only obligatory on
education authorities to provide the very
fundamental part of Christianity. The
rest can be provided by the clergy and
others who have the right of entry and
who have the ability to teach it.
I think that system is a very generous
one to Roman Catholics, and I do not
think that in the opinion of this House
they have any right to say that transferred
or provided schools are provided for Protestants only. The State can only make
it applicable to all. If Roman Catholics
do not wish to take advantage of it we
cannot force them to do so. If people
have a religious prejudice against doing a
certain thing which the State considers to
be perfectly fair, I think the concession
which has been given is a generous concession, and it is our limit. I do not
think it will cut across the four-and-two
committees, as has been suggested, because
they will get the two-thirds maintenance
grant which will be applicable to the
others.
In regard to the other points raised, I
should like to give an assurance that the
Clause which deals with the amending of
Section 98 of the principal Act in regard
to compulsory powers will not be put into
operation by the Ministry without a public
inquiry being held. In my experience of
the Education Act during the comparatively short time I have had experience
of it, I have never known any education
authority which came within measurable
distance of having such powers invoked,
and it is inconceivable that it will be enforced against education authorities as
they are at the present day. I shall take
the other points raised when the Bill is
in Committee.
Mr. McALLISTER: I should like the
House to recognise that we on these
benches voice the united opinion of over
36 per cent. of the children on the rolls
of the public elementary schools, and of
33 per cent. of the population of Northern
Ireland. The noble Lord, the Leader of
the House, in introducing this Measure,
stated that he hoped it would widen the
interests of the people of Northern Ireland
in this question of education. Surely, if
you want to widen the interests in this
Bill, it is desirable that the Government
should make such fair, just, and reasonable proposals as would meet with the
whole-hearted support and co-operation.
of all the people, including the 33 per cent.
minority whom we represent.
As representing that minority we are entitled to
examine the motives and the history leading up to this Amending Bill, and to see
what really the purpose of this Bill has
been from the Government point of view.
The 1923 Act was a secularist Measure.
For seven years the Government on every
platform pointed it out as one of the
greatest gems on the Statute Book, but
just before the last General Election the
united and organised voice of the Protestant Churches, for which I admire them,
and to their credit be it said, pointed out
to the Government that it was absolutely
necessary from a conscientious point of
view that simple Bible teaching, embodying their rule of faith, should be taught in
the schools. The Government had professed up to that point to believe that
there was no substance in the criticism of
the 1923 Act, but why did they turn to
the representative bodies of the Protestant
Churches and say they could understand
their conscientious viewpoint, but when
we put our case forward their answer was
that our conscientious viewpoint could not
be understood.
was
It is significant that it
just
immediately before the election that that
transformation in their outlook took
place. The 1923 Act was passed contrary
to public opinion-well-informed public
opinion, Protestant as well as Catholic.
During the seven years that the Act was
so unpopular an infinitesimally small
number of schools was transferred, and
the church bodies having secured the cooperation of that powerful political
organisation, the Orange society, prevailed
upon the Government to meet their full
claim, and give them 100 per cent. of
finance and 100 per cent. so far as their
religious views were concerned. I think
it is common ground that prior to the
1923 Act we, with all other denominations
owning voluntary schools, were entitled to
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
TE
a two-third grant for building purposes,
and it is also common ground that for
asserting our conscientious views as the
Protestant churches did up to that we are
now to be penalised to the extent of 50
per cent.
I suggest to the Government that in
the interests of harmony, concord and
goodwill, and from my point of view in
the interests of justice and fair play,
that they should extend that grant so as
to promote a feeling of tranquillity and
peace and remove what undoubtedly has
been a distinct and abiding grievance
with the people whom we represent.
We
do not seek to control the schools in the
sense the Government say. All we have
ever had was the power of the appointment of teachers. The whole financial
arrangements were subject to the control
of the Government. They arrived at the
curricula; we adopted them and worked.
them, and it is admitted that our schools
worked them as successfully as any other
schools.
I think the noble Lord minimised the
concession that the Government have
made, and rightly made, in giving the
right to have simple Bible teaching in the
transferred schools, because in giving
them that he satisfied their conscience
completely. It enables the children to
grow up in the full and complete religion
of their parents, and to which they were
entitled, but the Catholic position is
different. The Catholic belief is not
simply Bible teaching alone, but Bible
teaching plus the interpretation of the
living voice of the church and tradition.
Therefore, it is an absolute impossibility
unless Catholics are prepared to sacrifice
their conscientious opinions to adopt the
Bill now before Parliament. In order
that my view on this point may not be
the only one quoted, I go so far as to say
that this Bill is endowing the Protestant
religion. I am not alone in that
opinion, because a Member of another
House, one of his Majesty's Counsel, Mr.
Davison, stated in Armagh:
There is no more direct method of
endowing religion than by paying for
the teaching of that religion.
I would like to associate myself with
the appeal that has been made by the
hon. Senator, who sits beside me (Mr.
Campbell), with regard to the question of
repairs to schools. I think my hon.
Friend took the case of a school costing
sum.
£10,000. The managers of the voluntary
schools will have to raise £5,000 of that
sum. It may be a comparatively simple
thing to raise the balance of the principal
When the school is being originally built and provided for that can be
done by a bazaar or some special function
or effort; but how is the £300 a year,
which the hon. Senator spoke about, to
be provided for? I think the noble Lord
in introducing the Measure to-day, referred to the unsatisfactory state of the
voluntary schools. I have heard the
Parliamentary Secretary on several occasions state that the greatest need to-day
in Northern Ireland is the question of
schools. I would appeal to the House
when this Bill comes into Committee to
take a wider view and a more practical
view and to extend the grant of 50 per
cent. to a grant for repairs and upkeep.
I have only one exception to take to the
line which the debate followed to-day,
but knowing the hon. Senator as I do I
do not think he meant his remarks in the
sense which we perhaps in the circumstances would be inclined to take them.
In criticising his speech I would point out
that we do not look upon this as an
ordinary political question or the ordinary
class of question debated in this House.
We look on this as a profoundly deep ind
important question to us entirely outside
politics. Therefore, if I thought that
Senator Long, who is not here at the
moment, had that viewpoint before him
when he accused us of not being in
earnest, I would have had a distinct
and decided grievance. I do the hon.
Senator justice knowing him as I do in
giving him the benefit of believing that
he did not intend to be offensive in his
remarks.
The hon. Senator who sits behind me
(Mr. Leslie) pictures himself as one of
the men of broad and wide outlook
amongst a very narrow-minded community.
I only wish that some months ago his
view and vision had been broader when
he and another gentleman gerrymandered
Nationalist representation out of the
county to which I belong. At this hour
of the evening I do not intend to detain
the House any longer, but I hope when
the Bill comes into Committee there will
be calm reason without passion, and that
it may be managed in such a way as to
go further at all events to improve what
we believe, and honestly believe, to be
an unfair and unjust Measure.
Education Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
regret at a departure from the fundamental principles laid down in the Act
of 1923, and at the radical change proposed especially in the functions of the
Department of Education in Northern
Ireland. Under the 1923 Act every
section of the Christian church, or any
other church for that matter, might have
a separate and distinct religious education
outside the hours set apart for moral
and secular teaching. It was not the
function of the Department to provide
religious education for any section of the
community, but in this Amending Bill it
is proposed to impose that function of
religious teaching upon the Department.
of Education in Northern Ireland.
Seventeen hundred years have passed
over human history since the State first
began to interfere with religion. The
State's first interference with religion and
education meant the dividing up of
Christians into warring sects, each one
accusing the other of heresy and using
the arm of the State for the specific purpose of suppressing freedom of opinion
amongst those who differed from them.
It is not likely that this Amending Bill
will bring peace in the educational camp
in Northern Ireland seeing how diverse
and how irreconcilable are the differences
between the various sections of the
Christian Church in our midst. There is
no hope of reconciling the common Protestant interpretation of the Scriptures
and the right of individual judgment with
that of another body of Christians who
deny the individual the right of private
judgment except through the authoratative interpretation of the Church.
Roman Catholics necessarily must depend
upon the interpretation of the Scriptures
as given by the authority of their Church
accompanied by the traditions handed
down from the various councils beginning
1,700 years ago. So the best thing for the
State as a State to recognise here is that
there is no possible hope of ever reconciling these two warring elements in our
midst. They never have been reconciled;
they never will be reconciled, and never
can be reconciled, but they can be brought
up in such circumstances so far as the
education of the youth is concerned that
they will respect the opinions of those.
who differ from them.
If there is one thing that is necessary
in the interests of the common peace, prosperity and happiness of our country, it is
that our citizens shall be taken in hand
by the State and educated as good citizens
for this world-educated in our primary
schools through the teachers on their
rights, privileges and duties as citizens of
this present world. The children are
leaving our schools without that necessary
education being impressed upon their
minds, and they have no conception of
what their rights, privileges and duties.
are as citizens of this world. What is the
meaning, I would ask the noble Lord, the
Leader of the House, of simple Bible
teaching? The Bible is composed of 66
books, covering a period of 1,500 years of
human history-giving the history of a
specific people who claim to be specially
the children of God, forming their social
value from the nomadic or patriarchial
state through the national to the international position set up by the founder
of our common religion, the Lord Jesus.
Are we to teach the children in our
primary schools the ethics of the nomadic
and patriarchial community when the
father had the absolute control over the
life of the family, or are we to teach them
the ethics of the tribal phase of the social
civilisation of the people of Israel, under
which when one individual belonging to a
tribe or collection of tribes committed
some grievous offence against an individual
of another tribe, they were so superstitious
that they thought their God commanded
them to go forth and slay all the individuals of the other particular tribe?
the
I'mention these matters simply because
they must necessarily confront
Education Department in Northern Ireland. I believe that if the Government
had left the Act of 1923, where perfect
equality was given to all sections of the
Christian Church, where they could have
had a certain time set apart for the
specific purpose of instructing their supporters or followers in their particular
tenets regarding Christianity, it would.
have been far better than imposing on the
Department of Education the necessity of
attempting to do that which is practically
impossible owing to the diverse opinions
of the various sections-fundamentals,
moderns, and all the other sections-of
irreconcilable individuals in our community in Northern Ireland.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I did
not intend to take any part in the discussion this afternoon, but reference was
made a short time ago to the action of
the Orange Order. The fact that I am a
member of the county body I think
Education Bill-
Second Reading
27 MAY 1930
justifies me in saying that in my opinion
the action of the Orange Order has been
vindicated. All that they, along with
the heads of the churches, have asked for
from the Government has been simple
Bible teaching. They have not even
demanded religious instruction in Protestant schools, and for the life of me I
cannot understand why any person, let
him be a Roman Catholic or any other
person, could have any objection to
the instruction of the Bible in the
schools providing it does not go too
minutely into details or the tenets of
the different bodies.
afternoon
Much has been said this
about the splendid 1923 Act. I am sure
everyone admits that it was drawn up in
such a way that it should have been satisfactory to all. But who was it that
really prevented the 1923 Act from becoming really successful? It was the
Roman Catholic party, who refused to cooperate in any way in the working of it.
It was only they. And when the Protestant section found that one party refused to associate in the working of it,
but that, of course, the funds of the
State were being used for the payment
of their teachers and such like, they came
to the conclusion that they were justified
in asking for something in the nature of
plain Bible teaching in
in the schools.
Preferential treatment has been referred
to continuously to-day. In my opinion
if there is any preference being shown at
all it is being shown to the Roman
Catholic party.
Mr. McHUGH: How?
In
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
the first instance special provision has
been made for the training of their
teachers, which is certainly more than is
done for any of the other bodies. And
now a further concession has been made.
The noble Leader of the House has said
that the Government have gone to the
utmost limit. I think they really have
gone to the utmost limit when they have
offered fifty per cent. payment for the
building of schools. No matter how you
may hide the thing, it is quite clear that
this is only intended for Roman Catholic
schools, because the other schools are
likely to be transferred.
Mr. CAMPBELL: They will get 100
per cent.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: Yes,
but the other schools are under popular
control. If the Roman Catholic schools
were under the control of the State they
would have the same financial benefits as
the Protestant schools. But even after
all that has been offered and all that has
been done for them, not one word of appreciation or of gratitude has been
exhibited. The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) in his statement
to-day told us that when the Synod met
in Dublin they were congratulating the
members from Southern Ireland on the
splendid treatment meted out to them by
the Southern Parliament. But have the
minority in Northern Ireland the decency,
or the courage even, to thank or to offer
the slightest gratitude for, or appreciation
of, what has been done for them here,
which is even more than has been offered
either in the Free State or in England or
Scotland? It is very annoying to find
continuous concessions being made and
not the slightest appreciation of them
being shown. And now, even after a
grant of fifty per cent. for the building
of new schools has been offered, we are
asked to pay for the upkeep of the
schools. I do believe that if the Government were willing to pay the whole 100
per cent. for building new schools they
would demand ornaments and music afterwards. There are some people who cannot be satisfied, and I do believe that the
minority in Northern Ireland are a party
who, under any circumstances, no matter
what treatment were meted out to them,
would accept it without one word of
appreciation or gratitude.
I certainly support the Bill, and am
very glad the Government have introduced
it. I believe it will do away with any
little dissatisfaction that exists amongst
the Protestant section. It will provide
for Bible teaching in the schools, and I am
sure every one of us is anxious that our
children should have some little knowledge
of the Bible given to them in their youth,
so that they may carry it through the rest
of their lives. Furthermore, I believe that
the Bill secures for Protestant children
Protestant teachers, and that is another
very important thing.
I support the
Second Reading of this Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I wish to support the
Second Reading of this Bill. I think
the Government have put forth an honest
effort to settle this religious question in
the schools. It has caused a great deal
Education Bill.
Companies Bill
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
of annoyance and trouble in the past, and
I hope that we have now come to the end
of all the controversy that has been going
on.
The Press has been full of speeches
that have been made by different parties,
some for the Act of 1923 and others
against it. Sometimes when I read those
speeches, if I did not know otherwise, I
would come to the conclusion that the
Roman Catholics under the Government
of Northern Ireland were treated in a
most tyrannical way. In fact, one would
think that the treatment of the Roman
Catholics by the Government of Northern
Ireland was no better than that which
was meted out to them in Mexico sometime ago, or in Soviet Russia at the present time. That is not the case, and
statements such as these made in the
public Press cause a very wrong impression
to outsiders and those who know no better.
They cause them to look upon us here in
Northern Ireland as no better than barbarians. Well, We know we are a
Christian people.
Mr. SPEAKER: I hope the hon.
Member will get down to the discussion
of the Bill. All that he has been saying
has had nothing at all to do with the
actual Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: Thank you, Mr.
Speaker. As far as the Bill is concerned,
I cannot see where the grievance comes
in. The Government have tried to satisfy
the Roman Catholics in every way.
As
has been pointed out to-day, the Act of
1923 made provision for convent schools.
The teachers there are acknowledged and
pensions are provided for them when they
come to an age when they can teach no
more. In that matter the voice of Southern Ireland is quite different from the
voice of Northern Ireland. At a conference some time ago the chairman stated
that the teachers from Northern Ireland
should congratulate themselves on being
under the Government of Northern Ireland and enjoying the privileges of the
Northern Ireland Act.
"We in Southern Irelandhe said
have not the same privileges or the
same opportunities as
you have in
Northern Ireland. We, as a Roman
Catholic nation with a Roman Catholic
Government, have not our convent
schools acknowledged, and no pensions
are provided for our teachers in those
schools. You in Northern Ireland,
under the Act of 1923, have provision
made for those schools and pensions for
those teachers.
I cannot see where the grievance comes
in. The Government have done everything possible to meet their claim. Surely
they do not want to take public money
and hand it over to private individuals
to use it as they think fit. I am not
going to say anything more as time is
advancing. I support the Second Reading of the Bill.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of the Bill and to congratulate the Government on a Measure
which holds the scales equally between all
sections, be they Roman Catholics or
Protestants. It should give entire satisfaction to all classes and creeds throughout the Six Counties. I believe that
education will be greatly benefited by the
Bill, which is an honest attempt by the
Government to provide for all classes
alike. It places Roman Catholic schools
that are not transferred to four-and-two
committees in a much better position than
they were under the Act of 1923. I believe it will be most helpful to all parties
and I feel sure that the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) and the hon.
Member (Mr. McAllister) in their hearts
and souls must see and know well that
the Government, by the 1923 Act and the
Bill now before the House, have gone a
long way in providing for our Roman
Catholic schools. It is a pity that the
sectarian spirit seems always to be kept
to the front by our Roman Catholic fellowcountrymen, and that, no matter how exceptionally well treated they are by the
Government, they will not admit it. By
the Bill before the House they have received every consideration, and I believe
they realise it. I have pleasure in supporting the Bill.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.

Major DOBBS: I beg to move, after
Clause 15, to insert the following new
Clause:-
In the case of a company whose share
capital is divided into different classes
of shares, these shares shall be of the
same face value.
new
I am sure that if it was possible to accept this
Clause it would mean
cleaner finance. I will give an illustration which I think will make my point
clear. A company is formed with a
capital of £120,000, made up of £100,000
in one pound preference shares, and
400,000 shilling ordinary shares. All the
shares have the same value as regards
votes. The issue is made and 300,000 of
the 400,000 ordinary shares are taken up
by the company promoter and his friends.
probably not all for cash. There is issued
to the public the remaining 100,000 one
pound preference shares, and the remaining 100,000 shilling ordinary shares. They
cannot take up an ordinary share unless
they take up a preference share.
prospectus is so worded that it makes the
ordinary shares seemingly, and probably
correctly, of great value, but my point is
that the great bulk of the money obtained
for this company, that is £105,000, is got
The
Companies Bill-
Committee
28 MAY 1930
from the public. Yet they have only
200,000 votes. The remaining 300,000
votes, representing 300,000 ordinary
shares, belong to the company promoter
and his satellites. They have complete
control of the company, and they can
do what they like with it. There
used to be an old maxim that he
who pays the piper should call the
tune, and I wish we had this maxim
more before us in everyday life.
company promoter, if he is fraudently inclined, can do exactly what he likes, and
he has done what he likes during the last
This
few years. Such a company lends itself
to financial manipulation by outsiders,
whereas if you make all the shares of the
same face value you will have a perfectly
sound company.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think the hon. and gallant Senator has
advanced sufficient reasons why a company
should be obliged to make all its shares of
the same face value. I do not think there
would be any justification for imposing
such restrictions upon companies.
I presume the hon. and gallant Member
appreciates the difficulty that his Amendment would create in the case of companies
already registered. For these reasons I
regret that I am unable to accept the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 16 (Power to issue redeemable
preference shares).
(5) Where new shares have been
issued in pursuance of the last foregoing
sub-section, the capital redemption
reserve fund may, notwithstanding anything in this section, be applied by the
company, up to an amount equal to the
nominal amount of the shares so issued,
in paying up unissued shares of the
company to be issued to members of
the company as fully paid bonus shares.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 16, page 12, line 36, subsection (5), to leave out the word "the"
(sub-section, the capital) and insert "and
a capital redemption reserve fund has
been created, such." This is purely a
drafting Amendment for the purpose of
clarifying the meaning of sub-section (5).
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
