CLAUSES 17 (Reduction of capital),
18 (Amendment of s. 62 of principal
Act), 19 (Amendment of s. 65 of principal Act), 20 (Convening of extraordinary
general meeting on requisition), 21 (Provisions as to meetings and votes), 22
(Representation of companies at meetings
of other companies of which they are
members), 23 (Provisions as to extraordinary and special resolutions), 24
(Registration and copies of certain resolutions and agreements), 25 (Inspection
of minute books), 26 (Appointments of
directors), 27 (Restrictions on appointment or advertisement of director), and
28 (Resolutions passed at adjourned

CLAUSE 29 (Register of directors).
Major DOBBS: I beg to move at the
end of Cause 29, to add the following
as a new sub-section :-
That on the resignation of a director
notice of such resignation shall be sent
in writing to all shareholders in the
company, within fourteen days of such
resignation taking place.
Any investor in a company looks first
to having his money safe, and especially
to the financial status of the company.
One way to do this is by knowing the
board of directors and possibly some
special director in whom he has complete
confidence. As long as that director re
mains on the board the investor will keep
his money in the company with every
confidence, but it is rather strange that
sometimes it is nine or ten months after
the resignation of that director has taken
place that the fact is made public. I
think that for the sake of those investors
who look to the personality of the directors
rather than to the company itself, this
safeguard should be inserted in the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and gallant Member asks for the insertion
of an amendment requiring companies to
notify all their shareholders on every
resignation of a director. I am afraid
this would entail an enormous amount of
labour and a good deal of expense in the
case of companies that have a very large
body of shareholders, and I am not aware
of any compensating advantage. Accordingly, I regret that I cannot accept the
amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Companies Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Question put, "That the Clause stand
part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask the
noble Viscount whether the Government is
satisfied that the expression
"British
title" on line 4 of page 25 includes Irish.
I imagine it does so on the analogy of
"British Empire" and "British subject,'
but as we know, there was a rather lengthy
period when the titles attributable to this
or that part of the British Isles were in
an anomalous position. The word
"British" occurs, I may say, in the
English Amending Act and also in the
Consolidating Act. I think we ought to
be sure.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think the
description in the Bill is correct, but I
will make inquiries and give the necessary
assurance on the Report stage.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 30 (Provisions with respect
to offers of shares or debentures for sale).
Mr. LESLIE: It has been brought to
my notice that there is some difficulty
experienced in connection with debentures,
and this is the first Clause in the Bill
which deals with debentures as such.
Therefore, I suggest it is appropriate to
mention the matter now. The difficulty
that has been suggested to me is this. A
company may have creditors or a creditor,
and one creditor will go to the company
and ask that as security for his debt or in
satisfaction of his debt the company should
issue to him a debenture. The effect of
that would normally be, assuming that
there was no flaw in the matter and that
the matter could not be upset, that that
creditor would obtain an advantage over
the other creditors. That, perhaps, is not
as it should be. I thought it better to
mention the matter this afternoon, and
put down an Amendment to be moved on
Report stage to enable us to consider the
question more effectively than we could do
this afternoon. Moreover, the question is.
a somewhat wide one, and it might very
well be we should not think it right in
the first instance to go beyond the discussion stage.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
am very glad this question has been raised,
because I am a trustee of a company's
debenture holders, and that question has
been heard of naturally in regard to debentures. I am very anxious that steps
should be taken
debenture holders.
protect present
It
is
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
obviously a question on which I cannot
give a satisfactory answer, but I will consider the matter if the hon. and gallant
Senator and the hon. and learned
Senator will put down Amendments for
the Report stage.

CLAUSE 80 (Information as to pending liquidations).
Mr. LESLIE: On Clause 80 I should
like to ask the noble Viscount if the
Government is entirely satisfied with the
word "untruthfully" which occurs in
line 36. I think the point is to make
sure that the word "untruthfully" there
means wilfully untruthfully. It does not
necessarily follow that because a statement is untrue it is of a fraudulent
character. As the right hon, and gallant
Senator (Colonel Sharman - Crawford)
who spoke just now as a trustee of debenture holders, knows there is a decision
dealing with the matter to the effect that
that which is "untrue" is not necessarily
fraudulent. I imagine the Committee will
be satisfied with very little explanation, because it may rest with the court
to deal with the matter rather perhaps
as contempt of court than in any other
way. I should like to ask the noble
Viscount if he is satisfied. It can be
considered at a later stage. I think, perhaps, the same word will be found in the
English Act.
Colonel
SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
It is no protection to debenture holders
to put in the word "wilfully." It would
take the trustees of debenture holders a
very long legal process to find out how
you are going to prove that a man made
a statement wilfully untrue.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: I will
give the assurance later on. I would not
like to go into a definition at the present
moment of the legal difference between
untruthfully and wilfully untruthfully.
If the hon. Senator will defer the matter
to a later stage I will receive legal advice later on.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSES 81 (Notice by liquidator of
his appointment in a winding-up by the
court), 82 (Unclaimed dividends, etc., to
be lodged in court), and 83 (Rules for
winding-up in Northern Ireland) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 84 (Accounts to contain particulars as to loans to directors, etc.).
(1) The accounts which in pursuance
of this are annually to be laid before
every company in general meeting
shall contain particulars showing-
(a) the amount of any loans which during the period to which the accounts
relate have been made either by the
company or by any other person
under a guarantee from or on a
security provided by the company to
any director or officer of the company, including any such loans
which were repaid during the said
period; and
(b) the amount of any loans made in
manner aforesaid to any director or
officer at any time before the period
aforesaid and outstanding at the expiration thereof:
Provided that the foregoing provisions shall not apply—
(i) in the case of a company the
ordinary business of which includes
the lending of money, to a loan made.
by the company in the ordinary
course of its business; or
(ii) to a loan made by the company to any employee of the company if the loan does not exceed
two thousand pounds and is certified
by the directors of the company to
have been made in accordance with
Companies Bill-
Committee.
28 MAY 1930
any practice adopted or about to be
adopted by the company with respect
to loans to its employees.
(c) the total of the amount paid to the
directors as remuneration for their
services, inclusive of all fees, percentages, or other emoluments, paid
to or receivable by them by or
from the company or by or from any
subsidiary company:
Provided that in the case of any
director who holds any salaried employment or office in the company there
shall not be required to be included in
the said total amount any sums paid to
him except sums paid by way of directors' fees.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move on page 83, line 34, to leave out "in
the case of any" ("Provided that in the
case of any director') and insert "this
provision shall rot apply in relation to a
managing director of the company, and in
the case of any other." This amendment
will bring the text of the proviso into entire conformity with a similar proviso in
the English Act, and there will be advantage in this, having regard to the requirement in Clause 6 as to the filing of balance
sheets and auditors' reports which, in
many cases, will have to be filed both in
Great Britain and Northern Ireland because places of business have been established in both countries.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 94 (Requirements as to companies established outside Northern Ireland).
(5) Every company to which this
section applies shall-
(a) in every prospectus inviting subscriptions for shares or debentures in
Northern Ireland state the country
in which the company is incorporated;
and
(b) conspicuously exhibit
on
every
place where it carries on business in
Northern Ireland the name of the
company and the country in which
the company is incorporated; and
(c) cause the name of the company and
the country in which the company is
incorporated to be stated in legible
characters in all bill heads and letter
paper, and in all notices, advertisements and other official publications
of the company; and
(d) if the liability of the company is
limited, cause notice of that fact to
be stated in legible characters in
every such prospectus as aforesaid,
and in all bill heads, letter paper,
notices, advertisements and other
official publications of the company in
Northern Ireland and to be affixed on
every place where it carries on its
business:
Provided that the provisions of paragraphs (b) and (c) of this sub-section
shall not apply to a company incorporated in Great Britain.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, to
insert "or the Irish Free State" after
"Great Britain." This Amendment
deals with a matter which I hope only
requires to be cleared up in order to be
set right. Clause 94, Section (5) requires
every company to which this Section applies to conspicuously exhibit on every
place where it carries on business in Northern Ireland the name of the company
and the country in which the company is
incorporated; and cause the name of the
company and the country in which the
company is incorporated to be stated in
legible characters in all bill heads and
letter paper, and in all notices, advertisements and other official publications of
the company.
This Section is of general application,
but it is cut down by the proviso to Subsection (5), which says provided that the
Companies Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
provisions of paragraphs (b) and (c), the
two paragraphs which I have just read,
shall not apply to a company incorporated
in Great Britain. The general effect of
Clause 94 is to require all companies registered outside Northern Ireland which
establishes a place of business here after
the begining of the Act, or has before the
beginning of the Act established a place
of business and continues it, must comply
with these requirements.
Under the Consolidation Act of the
Imperial Parliament, Section 348, every
company in Northern Ireland which
establishes a place of business in Great
Britain after the beginning of the Consolidation Act or established it before the
beginning of that Act and continues it
in Great Britain-every Northern Ireland company under these circumstances
must do exactly what this Bill requires
to be done by foreign companies, which
are not British in the sense of being
English or Scotch. That is to say, every
company in Belfast or Derry or any of
our smaller towns which established a
place of business in England or Scotland
since 1929, or had a business there before
1929, and continues it, must conspicuously
exhibit in the place of business in Great
Britain the words "Northern Ireland"
and put the words "Northern Ireland"
in all its billheads and letter paper and
notices. That is the position with regard to all Northern Ireland companies
to-day so far as Great Britain is concerned.
The position is identical with regard
to all companies in the Free State. Every
company in the Free State doing business
in Great Britain under these circumstances must do likewise. The net result
is under the Consolidation Act of 1929,
the Imperial Act, the Ulster companies
and the Free State companies are in the
same category as far as England and
Scotland are concerned. They are treated
alike as foreign companies. For these
purposes, which I have just read, the
British Act treats Northern Ireland and
treats the Irish Free State as foreign entities in this matter. That, I believe, is
the correct result of the interpretation of
the Act of 1929.
What do you propose to do under the
circumstances here? They exclude you in
England and Scotland, and they exclude
equally the Free State. They say "if you
are trading in Great Britain you will
have to put the words 'Northern Ireland'
on all your papers.' We get not one
scintilla of reciprocity in this matter. We
are doing without reciprocity from our
friends across the water, and we are now
putting the English and Scottish companies having offices in Northern Ireland
in the same position as native registered
Ulster companies. They will rank just
the same exactly and be free from any
condition to put anything on their notepaper. It frees the companies in Great
Britain from this obligation, but it fixes
the obligation on companies in the
Irish Free State. I do not see any reason
for it. After all, the Irish Free State is
part of Ireland. I do not see any logical
reason for differentiating between Irish
Free State companies and companies in
Great Britain, especially having regard
to the fact that the British Act treats us
here in Northern Ireland as a foreign
entity, exactly the same as it treats the
Irish Free State. The Leader of the
House when this matter about the Free
State was mentioned on Second Reading,
when I ventured to say it was an elfish
pinprick, said, "Not at all. It is in no
sense a pinprick at all. It is simply a
necessity because Northern Ireland is
part of the United Kingdom, and the
Irish Free State is not." I think that
when the noble Lord made that remark
he had not before him the provisions of
the Consolidating Act of 1929 dealing
with this identical matter. The reason
may be that in England they think the
law in Northern Ireland may diverge
from and be different to the law in England.
our
I think that Senator Leslie, when he
spoke on the Second Reading and made
a very interesting contribution, as he always does, to our debates, took practically the same line as the noble Viscount.
He said this was not a new invention at
all, and had not originated on
part; it was to be found in Section 89 of
the Companies Act of 1928. I have no
doubt it is, but I think the hon. and
learned Senator must have forgotten that
the Act of 1928 is repealed in toto by the
Consolidation Act of 1929. There is not
a vestige of the 1928 Act left now, and
Section 89 has gone with the rest of that
Act. My Amendment does no more than
put companies in the Irish Free State on
the same plane as companies in England
and Scotland. The law in the Irish Free
State in respect of this matter is practically the same as the law in England. I
would ask the Committee, having regard
Companies Bill—
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
to the contents of the English Consolidating Statute, to adopt this gesture of
friendliness towards the Irish Free State.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not propose to
attempt anything in the nature of an answer to the hon. and learned Senator,
because that is not my business at the
moment, but I rise to join issue with him
distinctly on the suggestion that, because
the Imperial Amending Act of 1928 has
been repealed in toto by the Consolidating
Act of 1929, that, therefore, no vestige of
that Act of 1928 remains. That, I must
submit, is entirely contrary to the fact.
The effect of the Amending Act endures
in spite of its repeal and re-enactment in
a Consolidating Measure. The whole
object of these intermediate Acts, such as
the English Act of 1928, and the Bill before us, is to bring the law up-to-date in
accordance with the opinions prevalent at
the time, in order that it may be swept
away, or rather swept into a new Consolidating Measure.
As a matter of fact, speaking from
memory, the greater part of the Act of
1928, to which reference has been made,
never came into operation at all.
It was
provided in, I think, probably the last Section of the Act, or at all events towards
the end, in that kind of stokehold in
an Act of Parliament where the machinery
is kept it was provided that different
Sections should come into operation on
different dates, usually appointed dates.
The result was that a great deal of the
Act only came into operation on the date
of the repeal of the Act. I should not
like it to go out that that Act is no longer
effective, because I am sure the hon. and
learned Member will agree with me that
the effect of an Amending Measure is not
lost because it is repealed and reenacted in a Consolidating Measure. He
will remember an important Act of Parliament which deals with the effect of
repeals, and the Courts are accustomed to
refer back to earlier Statutes in order to
explain the meaning of modern ones. I
am not, of course, touching on the general
question before the Committee.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The proviso to which Senator Campbell takes exception does not change the existing law
for, at the present time, Irish Free State
companies are under obligation to comply
with the requirements in question. The
assumption that this is a new concession
in favour of Great Britain is, therefore,
erroneous, though excusable, in that it no
doubt arises from the fact that instead of
attempting some amendment of Section
274 of the Companies (Consolidation) Act,
1908, we are repealing Section 274 and reenacting it, with amendments, in Clause
94 of the Bill. By this proviso we are
continuing in favour of companies incorporated in Great Britain an exemption
which the great majority of them already
enjoy under the provisions of the Government of Ireland (Companies, Societies,
etc.) Order, 1922. It is true that Great
Britain has not given us the same concession, but the question was not raised when
the British Measure was going through
Parliament. In spite of that, however, I
think it would be difficult to justify the
extension of the concession to companies
incorporated outside the United Kingdom.
Amendment put.

(5) If any person acts, or incites,
causes or procures any person to act, in
contravention of this section, he shall
be liable to imprisonment for a term
not exceeding six months or to a fine
not exceeding two hundred pounds or
to both such imprisonment and fine,

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 96, page 97, line 6, after the
word "liable" (shall be liable) to insert,
"on summary conviction." There is a
curious apparent omission in the line indicated. We are not told whether this
particular penalty is to be imposed by a
court of summary jurisdiction or on indictment. In every other case in the Bill
we are told which it is. I think it must
be intended that it is to be a matter for
a court of summary jurisdiction, and I
therefore move the insertion of the words.
The fact is, of course, that unless we do
give a court of summary jurisdiction power
to hear cases of this sort it has no inherent power, but if we do give it this
power and the court decided that the case
is more properly one for indictment it
can, of course, take the necessary steps.
In the English Act, for some curious
reason which I am unable to fathom, the
expression I move is left out.
If it should be thought on further consideration that, having regard perhaps
particularly to sub-clause (8) of this
Clause the matter is more appropriate to
indictment that is an alteration which
can be made on Report. For the moment
I move the insertion of the words on the
Paper. I think I have already said that
in every other passage of the Bill, including Clauses 37, 38 and 76, the court
to which this reference applies is specified.
There is also a possible suggestion that
might be made, and that is that the old
Clause in the principal Act, section 276,
which extends summary jurisdiction to all
matters in which fines were being imposed,
might be held to cover this matter, but,
as I have said before, in every other
Clause in this Bill we are distinctly told,
and I think we ought to be distinctly told
here also.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am prepared to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.

CLAUSE 100 (Constitution of court in

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to leave
out Clause 100. Not for the first, but I
hope for the last time I rise to move the
omission of a Clause abating the Commission given to the justices of the peace.
The proposal before us is to make offences
which a court of summary jurisdiction
has power to try, whether they arise under
the Bill before us or under the principal
Act, triable only before a court consisting
of one or more resident magistrates. In
considering that matter the Committee
will, I think, first of all inquire what cases
these are that the ordinary magistrates
are unable or unfitted to adjudicate upon.
Taking the first in the Bill before us we
begin at Clause 4, sub-Clause (3). If the
Committee will refer back to it they will
see there the offence is that, an alteration
having been made in the memorandum of
the company, every copy of that memorandum issued after the date of the
alteration shall be in accordance with that
alteration, and if the company issues any
copy of the memorandum which is not in
accordance with the alteration they shall
be liable to a fine not exceeding one pound.
numerous
I suggest that that is a very simple
question of fact which any magistrate
might deal with. Passing on to Clause 5
which deals with a mere matter of indexing, and Clause 9, refusing an inspection
other
of the register, and
Clauses-I do not propose to take the

should remark in fairness that there are
some Clauses such as 31 and 34 where the
maximum penalty is very much in excess
of £1-in one case £5 and in another
£100. Under that Clause I think there
is a possibility of imprisonment.
Let us
So much for the Bill before us.
go back to the principal Act of 1908.
After all that Act has been in operation
for twenty years. One wonders in what
respect the magistrates who administered
it have fallen short in their duty.
Again
I do not think it is necessary that I should
take the Committee through every Clause
in that Act. I may say that in general
the matters are usually of a trivial character, sometimes more serious than others.
Now as regards the special jurisdiction of
resident magistrates we must remember in
all cases the defendant has an appeal. So
far as that goes it does not matter whether
a man is tried before a resident magistrate
or an ordinary justice, he can appeal. I
hope to get for him the option of a jury,
but that is for the future.

When these matters have been brought
before the Committee or the House before
reference has been made to the status in
England of stipendiaries having jurisdiction there. There is the City of
London. I suppose more cases of alleged
offences against the company laws arise in
the City of London than anywhere else.
But stipendiaries do not in fact sit either
at the Mansion House or in the Guildhall.
The Lord Mayor of London presides
there or ordinary aldermen and
resident magistrate. I cannot
reason for depreciating
Ulster product in the shape of magistrates. As a matter of fact the suggestion
is also made that the modern resident
magistrate is not appointed to that position unless he is a barrister or solicitor of
a certain number of years actual practice.
Arrangements for that method of apmade some seven or
pointment were
eight years ago. I should like to
ask how many of the present resident magistrates have that qualification of seven years actual practice.
It is, of course, perfectly open to Parliament to abolish the ordinary magistrates
if that is really intended, but I do not
think there is any case for this perpetual
pecking at them. We have made an
experiment in that direction in the Motor
Act.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
And a great improvement.
Mr. LESLIE: The hon. and gallant
Senator says it is a great improvement. I
have not observed a great diminution in
the number of motor accidents since their
jurisdiction was taken from them.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: A
more just penalty.
Mr. LESLIE: I have not observed
In
either a great increase in the fines.
fact if there is one tribunal more ready
than another to take refuge in the minimum fine I suggest it is the resident
magistrate. I observe the path of the
resident magistrate in motor cases has
been made somewhat easier, and his
decision rendered somewhat less unpopular by the fact that the last Act,
which gave him exclusive jurisdiction,
made things a little easier in that a defaulting bus driver's previous convictions
Companies Bill----
Committee
28 MAY 1930
L
outside twelve months were wiped out;
and even later I have observed that the
rate at which an omnibus can travel has
been increased. Things therefore have
been made easier for the resident magistrate.
The fact remains that a fine of £100 or
£200, to take the more serious cases, even
if there is an appeal is not a matter which
should be left to one man. The defendant
is entitled to the collective judgment of
several men. They are likely to be practical men with a knowledge of the circumstances. I hope the Government in this
instance does not propose to insist on, as
I say, still further abating the Commission
of His Majesty's magistrates. I beg to
move.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
We have listened with great interest, as
we always do, to the hon. Senator behind
me. Sometimes he tries with his greater
wisdom than we have in legal affairs to
muddle us.
My views on the magistrates
are well known. It is not against the
magistrates of the old type appointed for
the special purpose of carrying out the
law. Now it is a sort of reward for
political views. If the magistrates were
to act, the whole system of resident magistrate judiciary would have to be altered.
The City of London I happen to know.
Of course in the court of the aldermen
the Lord Mayor presides. Very often the
Lord Mayor sits by himself. He actually
holds the same position in my opinion as
a resident magistrate holds in County
Down. I hope the Government will not
give way even in the slightest degree. I
am probably one of the oldest magistrates
in this room, and I support the Government and hope they will not give way.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Irise to support
my hon. and learned Friend's Amendment. I have always been a supporter of
the unpaid magistracy. I think it really
has come to the point now if the Government mean to take away more powers from
them they should really abolish them
altogether.
(Hon. Members: Hear,
hear). That would be a perfectly understandable action at the present time. I
am a magistrate of nearly as long standing as my right hon. Friend.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Not quite.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Very nearly.
It is hardly worth while sitting on the
bench at all. In nearly everything that
comes on the clerk says "This is only for
a resident magistrate." It is really a
farce. I think the Government should
say quite openly that the J.P. is merely
honorary. The ordinary magistrates
should be allowed to sign a warrant and
nothing else. Then we should understand
the situation. I have the honour to support my hon. and learned Friend's
Amendment..
Mr. CAMPBELL:
I rise to support
to the fold.
the Amendment moved by the hon. and
learned Senator, and I am very glad
to welcome him back
(Laughter). I may say our welcome is as
grateful as any welcome ever given to a
prodigal son. I thought he left us for a
time on
the Parliamentary and Local
Government Elections Bill, but I felt confident it was only for a time and that he
would come back again.
Reference
has been made to the
stipendiary magistrates in England. Undoubtedly they act, but through the
greater part of England and in all the
smaller towns, as I understand it, the
jurisdiction of the ordinary unpaid magistrate is still undiminished. He deals with
this class of offence and many other classes
of offences. I have never heard a demand
made in any responsible quarter in England for the abatement of the jurisdiction
of the unpaid magistrates.
I do not know what the driving power
in Northern Ireland is. There is some
driving power in some of the Departments
of State. In almost every Bill it inserts
a Clause, as in this Bill, that all offences
under the Bill will be dealt with by paid
magistrates. I think it would be better
for the Government if this system of
creeping paralysis (laughter) is to go
on to step in and say "We will put an
end to all this business completely. We
will abolish the unpaid magistrates."
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Tell us what has happened in the Free
State.
Mr. CAMPBELL: You have just
shut, barred, banged and bolted the door
against the Free State, and I do not think
you need take advice from the Free State.
But I am glad to see you think the Free
State have a virtue in some respects.
Take the City of London, I think it is
Companies Bill—
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
It
there nearly all these cases are heard.
is the hub of the mercantile world, the
very centre of the commerce of the United
Kingdom. This class of case crops up
from time to time and the man who tries
them is very often not a practising
barrister and not a stipendiary magistrate, but the Lord Mayor or one of the
Aldermen, and not a man of special legal
training for trying these classes of
offences.
Surely if any class of offence is a matter
for unpaid business men it is offences of
this kind. This is a business Act, and
these are business offences. It would take
a great deal of argument to convince me
that a barrister or solicitor of seven years
standing fresh from the University would
be better fitted to deal with a matter of
this kind than a man who has been thirty
or forty years in business. I do not see
why this perpetual whittling down of the
jurisdiction of the ordinary magistrate is
going to continue. I thought it was
going to stop. I thought that with the
recent Bill this matter was going to stop.
But now the hon. and gallant Senator
says that magistrates are not as they were
in the old days. I do not know whether
he means it as a compliment or otherwise.
Does he mean they are less intelligent or
more intelligent? He says nowadays they
are appointed for their political views.
Of course they were never appointed for
that in the old days.
Colonel
Never.
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Mr. CAMPBELL: There never was
an appointment made until recent years
on the ground of politics alone.
Colonel
Quite right.
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not undervalue
your knowledge of history, but I think
you will get very few men to agree with
you. Of course we know none of these
appointments or any other appointments.
are made on the ground of politics.
That never happens at
all. It is
only unpaid justices who are appointed
on political grounds, and all other
apointments are made on merit. Do you
really believe that? I have great satisfaction in supporting this Amendment.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: There
is one question I would like to ask the
Government. A hint has been thrown
out that we should ask the Lord Mayor
to sit in Court and try one of these cases
himself. The Lord Mayor is a busy man
already, and I would like to know what
the intention of the Government is with
regard to the suggestion that has been
made.
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like to
support this Amendment. I have always
consistently supported Amendments of
this nature, and my particular reason for
doing so is on account of the fact that you
get the collective judgment of a number
of men on a pure question of fact, and
that is always preferable to the individual
opinion of one person. Furthermore, I
see a difficulty if this procedure of confining cases to resident magistrates is continued, because great inconveniences will
be caused to the public and the legal profession. How are we going to get the
work of the ordinary magistrates court
carried through when resident magistrates are not available. There is bound
to be adjournment after adjournment, and
the greatest possible inconvenience. The
right hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Sharman-Crawford) made reference to
the old type of magistrates--I do not
know what he means by that—and he also
said that magistrates were appointed on
political grounds. Surely the greatest
tribute that I could pay to the impartiality of the ordinary magistrates is the
fact that I am one of the strongest advocates of maintaining their jurisdiction,
although as hon. Members who belong to
my county know very frequently I have
to appear before magistrates not one of
whom shares my political views.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD :
They let you off, did they?
At all events, 1

hope the magistrates of the present

Mr. MCALLISTER: I imagine he
would be prejudiced, although I am sure
he would not do anything that he did not
think was strictly honest. I have the
greatest possible pleasure in supporting
the Amendment, as I believe it will be for
the benefit of the administration of justice
and the convenience of the general public.
I believe that in the past the ordinary
magistrates have enjoyed the confidence of
the people, and no tribunal that I know
of carries more confidence than does the
magistrates' court.
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
may be accused of dropping into bad
company if I vote for the Amendment
that has been proposed, but I feel compelled on this occasion to take that course.
When the same question was before us on
previous occasions I voted with the Government in favour of resident magistrates
acting alone in connection with offences
under the Motor Acts and the Intoxicating Liquor Act. In those cases there was
a possibility of influence being brought to
bear on ordinary magistrates, but I think
the cases that will arise under this Bill
are especially cases for the ordinary magistrates to deal with. They are essentially
cases for business men to determine, and
I believe business men will be able to
carry out this duty much more satisfactorily than even a trained lawyer. Therefore, I feel justified in voting for the
Amendment.
We have a considerable
number of very honourable men who hold
the Commission of the Peace, and those
men are called upon to do very menial
work, such as signing summonses, declarations, affidavits, and so on, for the
workers in working-class districts, and
yet they are being robbed of their more
important duties and are not allowed to
act in their proper capacity.
The time is coming when the Government must make up its mind whether it
is going to wipe magistrates out altogether
or allow them to discharge the duties for
which they were appointed, and that is
to adjudicate on the bench. In workingclass districts such as the one I live in the
workers are even now put to considerable
inconvenience on account of the reduction
in the number of magistrates. It is often
very difficult to get a magistrate to sign
affidavits and passports. I would advise
the Government to take this matter into
consideration as well as the general question of the jurisdiction of ordinary magistrates. For these reasons I feel justified
on this occasion in voting for the Amendwent.
Mr. DORMAN: I also support the
Amendment. Judging by the number of
Bills brought before this House in which
this practice of depriving ordinary justices
of their jurisdiction is embodied it will
only be a year or two before the Government has absolutely deprived magistrates
of all the duties for which they were
appointed. Senator Campbell has described it as creeping paralysis, and he
asked where this move originated.
understand that creeping paralysis has a
very vicious origin, and probably there
was a vicious origin behind this proposal.
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Senator
Leslie has quoted several Clauses containing offences which are quite easily understood by anybody, but there are unfortunately other Clauses, such as Clauses
73, 74, 76 and 96, reference to which will
enable Members to realise the complexity
of the matters which may be brought before a court of summary jurisdiction. If
the hon. and learned Senator can suggest
any form of words which would ensure
that such cases would be dealt with only
by magistrates who are experienced
business men it might be possible for the
Government to re-consider the matter on
the Report stage. As it is, if this provision was not embodied in this Clause,
it would be competent for ordinary
country magistrates to try offences of such
extraordinary complexity that I have
some difficulty in understanding them.
The Government cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid I must
confess that I am entirely unconvinced by
what the noble Lord has said. It does
not seem to me that any of the Clauses
he has cited present any insuperable
difficulty to ordinary magistrates, and
none of the matters are more involved or
more difficult than those submitted to a
jury in criminal or civil proceedings. I
do not think I should be justified in withdrawing the Amendment. I should like
to assure the right hon. Gentleman (Mr.
Samuel Cunningham) that I did not.
suggest that the Lord Mayor of Belfast
should sit in Belfast after the manner of
his compeer in London. I was not dealing particularly with Belfast magistrates.
Question put, "That Clause 100 be left
out."
The Committee divided: Content, 8;
Not Content, 9.
Companies Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
The Lord Mayor of Belfast
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph

McHugh, Mr. J.

Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.

Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount

R. G. Sharman
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, after
Clause 100 to insert the following as a
new Clause:-
:-
(1) A person, when charged before
a court of summary jurisdiction with
any offence under the principal Act or
under this Act in respect of the commission of which an offender is liable
on summary conviction to be imprisoned
for a term exceeding three months,
may, on appearing before the court and
before the charge is gone into, but not
afterwards, claim to be tried by a jury,
and thereupon the court of summary
jurisdiction shall deal with the case in
all respects as if the accused were
charged with an indictable offence and
not with an offence punishable on summary conviction, and the offence shall,
as respects the person so charged, be
deemed to be an indictable offence, and
if the person so charged is committed.
for trial, or bailed to appear for trial,
shall be prosecuted accordingly.
(2) A court of summary jurisdiction
before the charge is gone into in respect
of an offence to which this section
applies, shall, for the purpose of informing the defendant of his right to
be tried by a jury in pursuance of this
section, address him to the following
effect:-"You are charged with an
offence in respect of the commission of
which you are entitled, if you desire it,
instead of being dealt with summarily,
to be tried by a jury; do you desire to
be tried by a jury?"
This is really a more difficult question.
I think, than that which has gone before.
As I am sure, the Committee is aware it
is one of the root principles of the
British Constitution that a man should
not be liable to a sentence of imprisonment of any length unless he has had the
opportunity of being tried by a jury. That
right is supposed to be secured to him by
Magna Charta, which, after all, has still
a very considerable influence on our legal
system. As the Committee is aware, the
Charta was not originally an instrument
of enactment, and it did not immediately
Dobbs, Major Arthur Frederick
Glendinning, Mr. J. C.
Quigley, Mr. Andrew
find a place on the Statute Book. But
some seventy years or so after it had been
passed it was brought in as an Act of such
Parliament as there was in those days, and
it has never yet been repealed. It is provided there definitely that a man shall
not be sent to prison except on the lawful
judgment of his peers, and it has been said
by an eminent judge that all courts of
summary jurisdiction are an excrescence on
the common law. In later times summary jurisdiction was introduced as a
matter of convenience, but it has never
been the custom in Northern Ireland to
allow any sentence exceeding six months'
imprisonment to be passed by a court of
The only excepsummary jurisdiction.
tions I am aware of are for special cases.
There is the Firearms Act of 1920, an
Imperial Act, the Civil Authorities
(Special Powers) Act, and the Dangerous
Drugs Act, all of which are distinctly
special cases.
In England the condition is
is met
by a general provision in the English
Summary Jurisdiction Act of 1879
providing that where any man is
liable to more than three months' imprisonment he shall have the right to be
tried by a jury and be informed of that
right. We have no such provision in Northern Ireland, although we have it to a
limited extent in what I think is called
the Criminal Procedure Act of 1855, an
Act well known to ordinary magistrates,
and I presume acted on even by resident
magistrates. In case of larceny and kindred offences the Act provides in certain
circumstances that with the consent of the
accused he may be dealt with by a court
of summary jurisdiction, but the sentence
is limited to six months. The actual wording of the Amendment is borrowed from
the English Act of 1879. I was rather
inclined to apologise for the verbosity of
it until I came to look into the matter and referred to the later Act of 1925
which extends very materially the powers
of the courts of summary jurisdiction.
About a page and a half of offences are
specified and it repeats almost word for
Companies Bill-
Committee
.28 MAY 1930
[Mr. Leslie]
word what I have on the Paper; and it
definitely requires the man to be informed
of his right to be tried by a jury. If
he consents to be tried summarily he may
be sentenced to not more than six months.
In the Bill before us we have for the
moment to get back to the principal Act of
1908, and the maximum sentence I can
find imposed by a court of summary jurisdiction is one of four months. That was
for false representation which is a misdemeanour and may be tried by indictment. That Act has been in force in
Northern Ireland, so that a man has been
liable for the past twenty years to four
months for offences against the Companies
Act. In England the penalties were
raised to twelve months and there was no
necessity to put in a special clause because
of their Summary Jurisdiction Act. In
dealing with the matter here it seems imperative to deal with it in this way, unless
we are to accept conditions here contrary
to the position in every civilised community I can think of. It is necessary
we should deal with it somewhere.
I have, therefore, put down in the first
instance an Amendment based on the
English Act in which I use the expression
three months having regard to that section, 281 I think it is, in the English Act
to which I have referred, though having
regard to our own later legislation such as
some of the Motor Acts where we allow a
penalty of six months' imprisonment on a
man drunk in charge of a motor car for a
second offence, and having regard to the
Offences Against the Person Act so long
ago as 1861, when a sentence of six months
is possible, had I been starting it entirely afresh I think I would have made
the Amendment six months instead of
three months. That brings by an almost
natural evolution what seems possibly a
solution of a double-barrelled difficulty.
You must preserve to a man the constitutional right to be tried by jury.
I feel sure the Government will not
accept what they will describe as an ad hoc
Amendment when there is no general provision applicable to Northern Ireland
such as governs matters in England.
Therefore, if the Government feel unable
to accept the Amendment they might perhaps be in a position to inform me that
they would consent to accept on Report
stage an Amendment reducing the twelve
months' imprisonment to six. That
would bring it very fairly into line with
England, but it would reduce the maximum penalties. It would perhaps be the
better way out of the difficulty. It must
be understood that even for six months
a man has the right of trial by jury.
It is a little difficult for us to do anything
that would deprive him of that right.
After all, we are a second chamber.
is surely our primary duty to protect the
Constitution of the area to which we belong. I beg to move the insertion of the
two separate Clauses standing in my name.
It
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Perhaps I
might say if the hon. and learned Senator
who moved the Amendment agrees to
withdraw it I will give him the assurance
that the matter of the advisability of reducing the sentence to six months as he
has suggested, will be considered on the
Report stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that would be
the most satisfactory solution of the difficulty. The position I am in is that it
would be almost impossible for me to
withdraw on a question of this nature.
I must ask leave to withdraw, and I will
put down an Amendment for the Report
stage reducing the periods of maximum
imprisonment from twelve to six months,
except in one or two cases of twelve
months for a second offence.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move as a new
Clause: "Except when hearing an appeal
from a conviction by a court of summary
jurisdiction, a court of quarter sessions.
shall not have jurisdiction to try any
offence under the principal Act or under
this Act." This is a very small matter.
It is the difficulty of a court of quarter
sessions having jurisdiction. The difficulty would not have arisen at all if I
had been successful in carrying the
Amendment maintaining the jurisdiction
of the ordinary magistrates. If we leave
the thing alone as it is now the point is
this. The Committee has just agreed by
a narrow majority that only resident
magistrates are to have jurisdiction in
this matter. If it should happen that a
man is committed for trial at quarter sessions outside Belfast and Derry where
there is a recorder that man would be
tried before a bench consisting of the
ordinary magistrates and the county court
judge. The ordinary magistrates would
or might outvote the county court judge.
The position is illogical and I thought I
ought to direct attention to it. It is only
E
Companies Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
fair to say that I think the probability
in these cases of a man being sent to
quarter sessions is exceedingly remote,
except in Belfast where we remember that
the Belfast Recorder sits alone. The only
objection is that if you exclude quarter
sessions you might in some few instances
cause delay. But there are three assizes
-spring, summer, winter-so that the delay is not likely to be serious. It is not
a matter of very great importance.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government are of opinion that any advantages caused by taking this Amendment
would be more than counter-balanced by
the disadvantages. Although it might
not always happen in some cases there
would be some delay, considerable delay.
For that reason I am afraid the Government cannot see their way to accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg leave to withdraw
it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

For removing doubts it is hereby
declared that the Companies (Foreign
Interests) Act, 1917, shall be deemed to
have ceased to have effect in Northern
Ireland on the date whereon section one
hundred and sixteen of the Companies
Act, 1928, came into operation in Great
Britain.
Mr. LESLIE: I spoke at some little
length on this rather doubtful matter on
the Second Reading. I think the Committee will observe that I have put down
two Amendments, the one you have just
called and another on the Fourth
Schedule. I do not think the two can
possibly stand together and I must now
choose the horse on which I declare to win.
I propose with the leave of the Committee
to ask you to scratch the one before us
and leave the matter for consideration
until we go on to the other.
Amendment not moved.
CLAUSES 107 (General interpretation)
and 108 (Short title, repeal and commencement) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
First, Second, and Third Schedules
agreed to.
FOURTH SCHEDULE.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move to insert
after line 30, "7 and 8 Geo. 5, c. 18, the
Companies (Foreign Interests) Act, 1917.
the Whole Act."
If the Committee accepts my suggestion
we shall be following the precedent of the
English Amending Act of 1928 exactly,
and I think this is probably the best way
of dealing with a somewhat troublesome
subject. The particular Act deals with
matters relating to alterations in the
memorandum of a company, with special
reference to aliens. It was a War-time
Act, as the date, 1917, shows. It came
into operation I think in May of that
year. I do not think it has ever had very
much run so to speak in Northern Ireland.
At all events, it has disappeared in Great
Britain and it is proper it should disappear from our Statute Book. If the
Committee accepts this they will hear no
more about it because the repeal will not
appear in the Consolidating Act.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
the hon. and learned Senator decided to
scratch his proposed amendment to the
Clause and put forward this, because I
have pleasure in accepting it.
Amendment agreed to.
Schedule agreed to.
Preamble and Title agreed to.

Amendment to the House.
Ordered, That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported, with Amendments, to the

Labourers Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: The objects of
this Bill are, firstly, to make arrangements for the continuance of the Govern-
Companies Bill-
Committee
28 MAY 1930
ment subsidy to schemes for labourers'
cottages which was introduced in the
Labourers Cottages Loans Act of 1927,
and, secondly, to arrange that our Government can assume the burden of financing
those schemes which were in operation
prior to 1st April, 1927, for which the
loans have hitherto been found by the
Land Purchase Commission.
It is proposed to extend the period of the
Government subsidy which has expired.
this year until 1935, and there is ample
evidence that this extension is desirable
and will be made use of by the local
authorities. The other point to which I
have referred, namely, the taking over of
the schemes which have been hitherto
financed by the Land Purchase Commission is merely in order to convenience the
Imperial Home Office who do not desire
to have the responsibility of continuing in.
their legislation this purely Irish Measure.
It will be appreciated that this is largely
a Money Bill and I hope the House will
agree to give it a Second Reading. I beg
to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I intend to speak
very shortly on this subject, and I do so
with some trepidation. On one occasion
a very distinguished member of the British
House of Commons, a member of the great
House of Spencer, spoke on a similar
Measure, and he began by saying he was
not an agricultural labourer. That was
very apparent to the members of the
House. I might venture to commence my
remarks in the same way, but what I do
want is information regarding one or two
points about which some apprehension
exists amongst agricultural labourers.
The noble Viscount has just told the House
that one of the objects of this Bill is to
take over and finance previous schemes
financed by the Land Purchase Commission. I understand that under previous
Labourers Acts very considerable facilities were given to labourers for the building of cottages through the agency of
rural councils, and these cottages can be
let as low as 2s 6d a week. I believe that
in the Ballymoney district labourers' cottages can be let at a rent as low as 2s 6d
a week, and that is owing to the very low
rate of interest at which money could be
obtained and advanced by the Irish Land
Commission.
I understand that under this Bill all
such advances are to cease and that the
Land Purchase Commission are not to
make any advances whatever after the
passing of this Measure. It is a matter
that is causing some concern to the
labourers who, I think, are very deserving.
They are a very poor class of the community, and under present conditions an
agricultural labourer who has a family dependent upon him cannot afford to pay
a higher rent than that I have quoted.
Some of them think that under this Bill
higher rates of interest will be charged
than were charged by the Irish Land
Commission under the old Acts, and they
say-perhaps they are wrong-that the
higher rates of interest charged will mean
higher rents to them. They apprehend
that result very seriously. If there is any
substance in their fear I am sure the noble
Viscount will do his best to remedy it.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I am inclined to think
that the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Campbell) has, like myself, received
communication from the Route Social
Reform Society. I have a letter which,
I suppose, it is expected I should bring
before the noble Viscount and the House,
in which it is suggested that the money
needed ought to be borrowed at 2 per
cent. as was done under the Act of 1923.
I think one ought just to draw attention
to that proposal in order that one may
avoid any unreasonable anticipations
being based on the Bill before the House.
It is obvious that the Government cannot
get money at 23 per cent., and, therefore,
we cannot do as much as we could wish.
In any case, my correspondent ought to be
aware, I think, that nothing can be done
by this House, and nothing can be suggested by this House, which would entail
a further drain on public funds.
Having just drawn attention to the
matter, and tried to lay stress on the impossibility of getting money on the old
terms, it only remains to welcome the Bill
as far as it goes, and it goes as far as it
can under the circumstances. In some
districts it will prove of considerable benefit. There will be some districts where it
will not be put into operation as much as
in others, but in cases where there is an
urgent demand the rural district councils
will give effect to that demand and this
Bill will enable them to do it.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

beg to move.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD: If
we have no powers why is it necessary to
bring the Bill before this House at all?
Viscount BANGOR: Of course, the
Bill can be thrown out.

Parliamentary and Local Government
Elections Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: When we dealt with
this Bill on Second Reading I expressed
considerable doubt as to whether it would
be of any great advantage to us unless
certain alterations could be made in it.
During the Committee stage I suggested
we should amend the Bill so as to make
its provisions applicable to elections for
the Imperial Parliament as well as to the
Parliament of Northern Ireland. That
Amendment was withdrawn as the Government were advised that such an alteration
would be held to be an infringement of
the elections law and therefore outside the
province of this Parliament. I then expressed grave doubt as to whether on the
Third Reading the House ought to consent
to the passing of the Bill.
a
Since then I have reflected a little on
that subject and other subjects, and it
seems to me that, for the reasons I gave
in the Committee stage on that Measure
when I was unable to support the resolution as regards resident magistratesthat the Bill related primarily to
domestic matter of the House of Commons,
and therefore it would not be in accordance with my notions of Parliamentary
etiquette for this House to object-it
seems to me that that argument was sound
in detail and is equally sound in principal.
Therefore, although the Bill may prove
to be effete for the reason I have given,
I think that all the same we should be illadvised to refuse to give it a Third
Reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill with
some Amendments, to which they desire
the concurrence of the Commons.

Belfast Corporation Bill.
Resolved, "That this Bill be now read
the Third time."
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the Amendments
made by the Joint Committee, with
Amendment.
ROYAL NORTH DOWN RIFLES
MILITIA FUNDS.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
beg to ask the Leader of the House
whether the Government can take steps
to have distributed a considerable sum of
Regimental Funds now lying in the hands
of the United Service Trustee, and the
Regimental plate now lying in charge of

the Ulster Banking Company, Belfast,
which originally belonged to the Royal
North Down Rifles Militia, was transferred to the 3rd Reserve Battalion Royal
Irish Rifles, and finally to the 4th Reserve
Battalion, these battalions having for
many years been disbanded.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This is the first intimation
which the Government has received regarding the existence of the funds and
plate referred to by the right hon. and
gallant Member, and obviously it has not
been possible to make any investigations
since the question appeared on the Order
Paper. I would suggest to the right hon.
• and gallant Member that he should communicate either with the Cabinet Secretariat or with the Ministry of Finance,
giving as full details as possible of the
origin and amount of the funds, and
what directions were issued by the officers
concerned at the time of the disbandment
of the various Battalions mentioned. If
on receipt of the communication it appears
that suitable representations could be
made or action taken, I have no doubt
that the Government would take such
steps as appear possible.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
This matter, of course, interests you, Mr.
Speaker, and the Prime Minister, because if these funds are distributed both
the Prime Minister and yourself will receive a share.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Will no
other Members of the Senate get anything?
-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Not if they did not belong to the North
Down Militia.
Supervision of Films.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That in the opinion of this House, the
Government should take steps to secure
effective public supervision of films in
picture houses in Northern Ireland."
The Cinematographs Acts of the Imperial Parliament which apply to Northern Ireland do not mention "censorship" and are concerned practically with
the safety of the public. Those Acts empower the licensing authorities to grant
licences to picture houses on terms, and
Supervision of Films
Supervision of Films
30 MAY 1930
these terms may and do relate to subjects
other than safety. The licensing authorities are the corporation in county
boroughs, the urban council and town commissioners in urban areas, and the county
councils elsewhere. I venture to think
that few licensing authorities have exercised steady and serious supervision over
the films of picturę houses in their areas.
Unless restricted by the terms of their
licences picture house proprietors may
show films which have never been submitted to and never passed by the British
Board of Film Censors or by any censors.
In Belfast a film not sanctioned by the
British Board cannot be shown unless
notice be given to the Corporation, and
such a film must not be exhibited if the
Police Committee prohibit it on the ground
of its objectionable character. I cannot speculate how often the Police Committee have exercised this power.
The British Board, I understand,
pass one class of films for universal exhibition and a second class as quite unsuitable for exhibition to persons other
than adults-a strange classification.
It
is left, apparently, to the management of
a cinema to see that young persons are
not admitted to exhibitions of the second
class. Do thev see to this with strictness?
If they fail I am not aware of any real
risk of penalty they incur. In small
urban areas and in rural areas, I
assume the police are the only persons
who enforce by-laws where such exist
and who exercise a supervision over
films which have never been presented
to the Board of Censors. Is that satisfactory? I have never heard of a prosecution by the police on this head. Is it
a real supervision at all? Care is taken to
prevent cinemas becoming death-traps in
times of panic, but the effect of their films
on the child mind has been treated as of
minor importance. There exists no real
public check on the presentation of films
calculated to weaken in persons of immature judgment respect for morality
and good conduct. 99 per cent. of film
plays are compounded of sensation and
sentiment. Competition is keen among
many makers of these plays as to who will
produce the best "thriller." They are
out but to make money-thev and the
financiers behind the scenes. They seek to
draw a crowd.
Not a few films are, in plain words,
sinks of corruption by which a young mind
can be submerged and swamped and suffocated in filth. I have read that recently in
From
The
the course of 60 days more than 150 films
from Hollywood, a film centre in the
United States, were totally rejected by
the Censorship Boards of Australia and
Canada on the grounds of immorality,
offensive vulgarity, flouting of marriage
laws, crime and suggestive scenes.
this fact one can see the danger.
cinema, which should be a source of uplifting and instruction, of innocent merriment and becoming mirth, can become in
evil hands a grim source of demoralisation.
Of course, there are unexceptionable films,
but unhappily other products of the film.
industry are products from the sight and
sound of which Ulster youth should be
saved by an effective and real censorship
such as does not prevail at present. Since
this matter was last before the Senate
the Government have issued a regulation
under the Civil Authorities Act,
authorising the seizure and confiscation of
gramophone records and cinematographs.
Does this regulation extend to objectionable films in picture houses?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am afraid
I cannot give the information asked for
by the hon. and learned Senator about
the Order recently promulgated by the
Government. I can ascertain exactly
what it did and did not prohibit if the
hon. and learned Member will put down
a supplementary question on the subject.
Regarding the general question, I cannot
give any more information than that contained in the answer I gave to a question
put by the hon. and learned Member the
other day, namely, that the Government
have had this whole matter under consideration for some considerable time.
Of course, the important question arises
as to what films are objectionable and
what are not. Some people are far more
sensitive than others, and might regard
as indecent a film which other persons
might look at with comparative equanimity. Apart from that, I think it would
be a doubtful financial advantage to any
cinema or film exhibitor to exhibit films
that were highly objectionable or films
that could be objected to. Such films do
not pay in the long run. They might run
for a day or so, but they would not continue because certain patrons would be
perfectly certain to object, and the whole
aim of any cinema or theatre is to run
the place without any objection.
The local authorities have absolute
power to deal with them, and in the
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
rural districts the parents or guardians of
children, and the people generally, could
always make representations to the police
if they thought the young people were
being contaminated by the exhibition of
films. From my experience of some years
residence in this country I can say that
I think local opinion is very prone to
complain of anything immoral or indecent.
in the behaviour of other people. However, the Government will continue to keep
their eye on this matter, and I hope that
no films of the kind complained of will
be exhibited in Northern Ireland.
On Question, Motion negatived.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the National
Health Insurance Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
They have agreed to the Amendments
made by the Senate to the Parliamentary
and Local Government Elections Bill without Amendment.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Moved:
"That this Bill be now read
a First time."-(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Education Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).
Mr. LESLIE: I cannot refrain from
pointing out that we are really taking
the Committee stage very quickly. It has
not been possible for the amendment sheet
to reach us in the country until the first
post this morning. Further, we have to
approach the consideration of the matter
without having the Debates on the Second
Reading. I do not propose to press my
opposition. I know it was more or less
by agreement that this matter was taken
to-day. I think it is only fair to ourselves to point out that the time that
ought to be useful for consideration of
Amendments was lamentably deficient.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am
sorry that Senator Leslie has raised this
point. It was by agreement it was taken
to-day. That is really the only thing I
have got to say beyond the fact that this
Education Bill was before the general
public with varying degrees of excitement, so that any Amendment might
have been brought up.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly,
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Additional members of
education committees, 13 & 14 Geo. 5,
c. 21).
(1) The Minister of Education shall
appoint, as members of an education
committee for the purposes of the
Education Act (Northern Ireland),
1923 (in this Act referred to as "the
principal Act"), persons who, in his
opinion, are representative of the transferors by whom any schools in the education area of such committee have been
transferred to the education authority,
and such appointments shall be made
after consultation with the transferors
(to such extent as may seem to the
said Minister practicable and expedient)
and with the education authority concerned. In determining the number of
representative persons to be so appointed as members of an education
committee, the said Minister shall have
regard to the number of schools transferred as aforesaid, and such members
shall be additional to the members appointed under sub-section (2) or subsection (4) of section two of the principal Act, but shall not in any case
exceed in number one-fourth of the
total number of members of the committee (including the members appointed by the said Minister), and the
said sub-sections shall have effect as
amended by this section.
(2) In this Act and in the principal
Act as amended by this Act, the expression "education committee" means the
education committee of a county
borough council or a regional education.
committee, and the expression "transferors' means any trustees, persons or
body of persons by whom a school has
been transferred to the education
authority, and includes trustees appointed in place of such trustees, the
representatives of such persons, and the
successors of such body, as aforesaid.
Education Bill—
Committee.
30 MAY 1930
Colonel PAKENHAM: I beg to move
on page 2, line 7, to leave out "onefourth" ("Number one-fourth of the
total") and insert "one-eighth." I move
the Amendment because I believe that we
are departing a long way from the original.
Act of '23 in the matter of democratic and
popular control of education. Where
they are nominated simply by power
given to the Ministry you are not on a
democratic basis. I therefore move this
Amendment to reduce to a minimum the
numbers of the nominated members on
these committees.
I am
These education regional committees are
appointed by the county councils in the
counties and the borough councils in
boroughs out of their own membership or
from such selected persons who have knowledge of or take an interest in education.
I therefore conclude that these committees
are very fully representative both of the
popular control of the county councils and
those people who take an interest and
know something about education.
further moved to bring this Amendment
forward because by Clause 2 of the Bill,
which deals with the appointment of school
management committees, there is ample.
representation of these school management committees, transferors or trustees
and old managers of schools. In those
school management committees the transferors of schools and old managers have
ample scope for their energies in subjects
which they are well acquainted with and
conditions which they know.
They have great powers handed over
to them. They have the direct nomination and almost the appointment of the
teachers. I conclude, therefore, that the
transferors and trustees and others connected with education on the old committees will have ample scope for their energies. I do not think their utility will be
half so great on the regional committees.
Regional committees have rather different
business. They deal with the matter of
education, finance and that sort of thing.
I, myself, am a transferor on a school
management committee, and I find my
energies are sufficiently occupied in looking after the school transferred, and nine
out of ten transferors think the same.
I think the idea of nomination in the
matter of education is not to be commended and, therefore, without further
remarks I beg to move the substitution
of one-eighth for one-fourth in this case.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I should like to
support the Amendment which has been
moved by my hon. and gallant Friend.
My reason for doing so is that I think
one-eighth very adequately represents the
persons who desire to be on these committees or whom the Government wish to be
on these committees. I do not know
exactly why they wish to be on these particular committees, because they are business committees and there are only two
things that they deal with that I think
might interest them. One, of course, is
the appointment of teachers.
That is
very carefully regulated by the Act and
the final decision rests with the Ministry
of Education. I should have thought
that if they wished to have an important
voice in this matter of the selection of the
teachers it would have been better for
them to have pressed the Government to
appoint an advisory committee.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Hear, hear.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: That might assist
the Minister in his final decision instead
of bothering themselves about courts of
the first instance. I daresay the noble
Lord would welcome their assistance.
With regard to simple Bible teaching I
daresay they might exercise some watchfulness as to that teaching. They can do
that now. They have a right of entry
under the 26th Clause. I might just take
this opportunity to correct a mistake
which I think the hon. and learned Member opposite made on the Second Reading
when he stated that simple Bible teaching
was county council religion. For once in
a way I think-
Mr. LESLIE: I was using the expression as a quotation.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I was not alluding to him. There is another hon.
and learned Senator.
Mr. SPEAKER: I must point outthis matter has been going on for some
time that if an hon. Member does not
give way then another hon. Member must
sit down. There must not be two hon.
Members standing at the same time. It
is quite out of order.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: Simple Bible
teaching is very far from being county
council religion. It does not emanate
from the county council or the education
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Armstrong]
committee. It is compiled by reverend
gentlemen and it is entirely left to them
to propose it. I believe if the hon. and
learned Senator saw it he would find
nothing in it to which any person could
object. He might wish to add something
to it, but I do not think he would find
fault with it. It has been stated, I think,
that the assistance of the eight would
work usefully. I think that the eight
are not men who apply themselves to business.
I
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
oppose this Amendment for the reason
that this is an Amending Bill which is
before us. As we all know too well,
there has been a great deal of friction
on the education question. Representatives of the Protestant churches and other
institutions were called together and on
an agreement it was arranged what the
terms would be. If we reduce the number of these representatives as proposed
in the Amendment from one-fourth to
one-eighth I think we would be breaking
the agreement entirely. Therefore, on
behalf of a body whose views I think I
know, and I know my own views, I would
oppose this Amendment because it is
breaking an honourable agreement which
has been arranged.
Mr. LESLIE: I rise to support the
Amendment. It seems to me that if the
Government can accept this Amendment
it will render the Clause less unpalatable
to most of us than it is already. I must
point out that if my calculation is correct
it would seem possible to nominate seven
members. The maximum number at the
moment of any committee is 21 and the
fourth of 21 plus the nominated members
would give 28 and really one-third of the
existing number would be equal to onefourth of the future number.
Then it is to be remembered that these
new representatives are to be selected as
representatives of transferred schools and
the transferors of these schools, but their
function if appointed will not be limited.
to transferred schools at all. They will
deal with provided schools just as much
as the others. If that be so, I cannot
see what answer there is to the observations of the Lieutenant of Armagh.
It is a subversion of one of the main principles of the Londonderry Act.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: To reduce
the representatives of the transferors to
one-eighth of the whole committee would
reduce the maximum number to be appointed on regional education committees
to three persons, which would be quite
insufficient to permit of any proper representation of the diverse interests.
One of the Senators who has spoken has
talked about this being undemocratic, that
is to say, the hon. and gallant Senator
(Colonel Pakenham) who moved the
Amendment. It would be just as undemocratic to appoint one-eighth as to
appoint a quarter. Apart from that you
cannot run education on any system
of democracy or oligarchy or any of these
things. Education has to be run on a
system that will work. There has been
an agitation to include these nominated
members on the regional committees.
may say that is not really departing very
much from the 1923 Act as originally introduced. In the Second Reading speech
it was put down that the nominated element of the regional committee should be
50 per cent. less one. This idea was
withdrawn in Committee in another place
by Lord Londonderry, and under the
circumstances, as it was the first Bill of
its kind, and, may be, for other reasons,
the Amendment proposed by Lord
Londonderry was accepted, but it was not
accepted without considerable opposition,
even if the opposition was more silent than
vocal. That is to say, hon. Members,
especially those who were Members of the
Lynn Committee, did not like it at all,
and there has since been an agitation to
have a half less one put in.
I want to emphasise what Senator
Samuel Cunningham said, to the effect
that this Amending Bill is the result of a
compromise between people who wished
to amend the Bill root and branch and
those who wished to keep it in existence
absolutely as it was before the Amending
Bill came in. It is impossible to make a
compromise that does not take away something of the aspirations of one side and
a certain amount from the powers of the
other side, and that has been the case in
connection with this Bill. While those
who have agitated so strongly for the
radical alteration of the Act have not got
all they put forward demands for-and I
do not like to say very much about this
-on the other hand the regional committees and the county councils have undoubtedly come off with less than they had
before. What I want particularly to
point out is that this is a compromise,
and you cannot make a compromise in
Education Bill-
Committee.

education or anything else that will enable

the nominated element might be a quarter,
if he will read the Bill he will see that
it may not be as much as that, because
it is in proportion to the number of schools
