Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 2, line 10, at the end of
sub-section (1) to add,
(2) In the case of a county borough
the number of members of the education committee (excluding the members
appointed by the Minister of Education)
shall be not less than fifteen or more
than twenty-one.
This Amendment is of a non-contentious
character and will, I hope, be generally
accepted. Under the Education Act of
1923 a regional committee must consist
of not less than fifteen or
more than
twenty-one members, unless the Minister
otherwise decides. In the case of a county
borough the education committee must
consist of not less than twenty-one members. The Government is now proposing
in this Amending Bill to add 25 per cent.
in addition to this membership. The
object of my Amendment is to make the
provision for the number of members of
the education committee of a county
borough the same as the provision for a
regional committee, and leave it to the
discretion of a county borough education
authority to reduce the present membership of the education committee to fifteen
if it so desires. This is purely a permissive power given to a county borough and
leaves it free to have fifteen or twentyone members at its discretion. I beg to
move.
Ι
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
support this Amendment because I think
it would be very acceptable to the mem-
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
McHugh, Mr. J.
Quigley, Mr. A.
bers of the Belfast Corporation if they
were permitted to have only fifteen members on the education committee, or any
number under twenty-one. This Amendment would not prevent them having
twenty-one members if they so desire, but
it permits them to have only fifteen if
they think it is desirable. I think the
tendency to have fewer and smaller committees is a good one from the point of
view of getting the work done efficiently
and expeditiously, and for these reasons
I support the Amendment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
feel that this is a rather dangerous position for us to take up. There are only
two county boroughs that can be affected
by this proposed alteration, Belfast and
Londonderry. The Belfast City Council
and its Education Committee have already
had this matter under consideration, and
they seem to be quite satisfied with the
arrangement in the Bill; that is, with
fifteen representatives and six co-opted
members, which is the present composition
of the Belfast Education Committee. To
reduce that number or to state definitely
in the Bill that it shall not be less than
fifteen and not more than twenty-one
would be restricting the Corporation considerably.
I regard the co-opted members of the
Belfast Education Committee as valuable
to the education authority, which has
proved itself to be a very competent body.
They have interested themselves in this
education scheme and they have done an
immense amount of valuable work. The
co-opted members of that committee are
two clergymen, a very respectable and
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
sane trade union representative, and a
lady whose services will, I think, be of
considerable value to the Committee. If
you pass this Amendment it means that
you will shut out from the education committee these co-opted members, and I
think that would be very unfair. There
might arise occasions when it would be
desirable to co-opt on that committee
some distinguished educationist, but if
this Amendment is carried the committee
might be prevented from doing that. It
must be borne in mind that, taking everything into consideration, the powers enjoyed by the Ministry in this connection
are very limited. The Bill states that
the number of transferors' representatives
shall not in any case exceed one-quarter
of the total membership, and I do not
think it is likely that the Ministry will
use those powers even to the extent of
appointing one-quarter of the members.
There is another point. The Orange
Order has taken a lively interest in this
Bill. I have a right to speak on their
behalf and to say they are perfectly satisfied with this Clause as it stands. I have
been in touch with representative heads
of that Order this morning, and I further
understand that the representatives of the
Education Committee of the Belfast Corporation are also satisfied with the Bill
as it stands without any further Amendment. Therefore, when you take all that
into consideration, I do not think it would
be advisable for this House to make any
change in this Clause. I think it should
be left as it is, and I am sure the Ministry
will not abuse their powers as regards the
appointment of transferors. If the Clause
is left as it is it may be an inducement
to those school managers in the other
county borough where no schools have yet
been transferred to transfer their schools.
I regret that Senator Quigley was
able to give better reasons for proposing
this Amendment, which I feel I am compelled to vote against.
not
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am very glad to
see that two members of the small party
represented by the hon. Members behind
me have parted company for the first time,
one going one way and the other the other
way. I believe Senator Joseph Cunningham is going the right way. As I understand the Act of 1923, a regional committee in each district where there is a
regional committee must consist of fifteen
members. In Belfast the number is
twenty-one. Belfast has a population of
something like 415,000 people, and we
are asked to reduce its Education Committee of 21 to the level of some in
Armagh and Tyrone, which represent,
perhaps, about 100,000 people in each
case. I think it is absurd. I object to
this Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: In November, 1923,
when we were discussing the Local Government Bill I suggested on that occasion,
with particular reference to the Education
Act which was then about to come into
operation, that power should be given to
the county councils to increase the number
of co-opted members which under the
Local Government Act of 1898 they were
permitted to co-opt. I had the honour of
being supported by the hon. Senator (Mr.
Armstrong) on that occasion, but we were
unable to carry our point. The Leader
of the House at that time refused to
accept the Amendment, on the ground
that it was a matter relating to the county
councils, that no county council and no
body of ratepayers had asked for the
alteration, and, therefore, that he was
bound to oppose it. Perhaps if that
Amendment had been accepted some of
the difficulties of the county councils
might have been solved by further members being co-opted for the particular
purpose of being put on the education
committees. However, that is by the
way. The point remains that we
have just been assured by my hon. Friend
on my left that the education authority
in the City of Belfast has not asked for
this alteration, and, therefore, as he said,
it would be rather unbecoming for us to
insist on it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
matter entirely for the members of the
two county boroughs concerned-Londonderry and Belfast-and as neither the
Lord Mayor of Belfast nor the Mayor of
Derry is in his place to give me any
assistance in the matter, I think the best
thing I can do is to leave it to the opinion
of the House without giving a lead on
the matter one way or another. I know
what I should like myself, but in order
that there should be no misapprehension
in the matter I will vote against the
Amendment and Lord Bangor will vote
for it.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Education Bill-
Committee.
30 MAY 1930
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
beg to move, in Clause 1, page 2, line 10,
at the end to add,
Provided, however, that if an education authority has already appointed.
as members of an education committee
persons who are representative of the
transferors above mentioned, such person shall, for the purposes of this section, be deemed to have been appointed
by said Minister.
As a matter of fact, the county councils are getting nothing in this matter.
The power of the county councils in this
Bill has been diminished in nearly every
part affecting them. We happen to have
on the County Down Regional Committee
six representatives of transferors of
schools, and, therefore, I think it is only
fair that they should be the six selected
by the Government, otherwise disturbance will be created in the county
council. The County Council of Down
dies to-night. There will be a new
county council to-morrow, and they will
have the chance of reforming the whole
plan of education in the county. In my
opinion these six transferors should be
wiped off as our representatives It
would be only equitable that the education authority should be credited with
this number of representatives of transferors when the quota to be nominated
by the Ministry is being fixed. This is
a very serious matter. The Government
have made this rift in the lute, as it were,
and we do not know where it will end.
Mr. CAMPBELL: One objection I
have to the Amendment is that I think
it is not in acordance with strict
grammar, and it ought to be in connection with an Education Bill. I think
the word "person" in the third line
clearly must be "persons." I am surprised that Senator Leslie has not found
out that mistake.
Mr. LESLIE: He has not spoken yet.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Another objection
I have is that I should like to understand the Amendment, but I cannot.
How are we to know whether the persons
already appointed by the education
authority are transferors or not? The
Amendment reads:
Provided however, that if an
Education Authority has already appointed as members of an Education
Committee persons who are representative of the transferors above mentioned, such persons shall, for the
purposes of this section, be deemed to
have been appointed by said Minister.
That is to say, I know in fact, that the
gentlemen appointed are appointed by
the education authority. I know thatthat is an actual fact-and although I
know that I am asked to assume by this
Amendment that for the purposes of this
section they shall be deemed to be
appointed by the Minister. I an asked
to assume that they are appointed by the
totally different authority, though I
know they have been appointed by the
education authority. It seems to me to
be asking me to assume that two and two
make five, whereas I know two and two
make four. As Euclid says, "Inat is
absurd."
Mr. LESLIE: I should first of all
like to ask a question, and that is
whether I am correct in thinking that
when this Bill originally made its appearance in another place the exercise of this
power by the Minister was permissive,
and not compulsory. As it stands now
the Minister is bound to make these
appointments.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It has been
altered in this respect.
Mr. LESLIE: That makes the position
rather more difficult. It seems to me
that the intention of the Amendment
moved by the right hon. and gallant
Gentleman must be this, where intelligent county authorities or regional committees have appointed as representatives
people who happen amongst their other
qualifications to have the qualification of
being representatives of the transferors of
the schools, the Minister when making
his appointments should take over some
of them and thereby create vacancies in
the committee which would be filled by
the county council in the ordinary way.
That is, I believe, the plain meaning of
the Amendment. For the same reason as
in the other cases I think it would render
this Clause more acceptable to the
authorities who are principally concerned
-that is, the county councils, who, after
all, have got to find the money—and I
think their views ought to be met in
Education Bill-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
every possible way. A good deal has been
said about compromise. I hate compromises which are effected outside Parliament. Parliament is the proper place to
deal with these matters, and I venture
to suggest that Parliament is perfectly
competent to deal with them. I shall be
obliged to support the Amendment.
really do not think the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) on consideration
will have great difficulty in interpreting
the intention of the Amendment.
I
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Senator Leslie has explained the Amendment much better than I have done myself, and I think the hon. and learned
Senator on my left (Mr. Campbell) with
his great intelligence should not have
raised his silly objection.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I never
meant that this House was not competent
to deal with compromises.
When a representative of the county councils gets up
and says that by this Bill county councils
are reduced to the position of absolute
nonentities, I can only say their position
might have been still further reduced if
their interests had not been protected
by the Government on the questions which
have arisen. It is a compromise that is
before Parliament at the present moment,
and I should not assert for one minute
that Parliament is not competent to deal
with the matter.
With regard to the Amendment, the
method in the Bill is that the Ministry
shall consult with the education authority
before making any nomination-and in
the case suggested by the proviso proposed by the right hon. and gallant Member if the education authority can show
to the Ministry that the education
authority has already appointed representative transferors to the education committee that would be a factor in deciding
the Ministry as to its nominated members;
but to accept the Amendment would, in
the Government's opinion, only cause
friction by leaving a recurrent cause of
difference of opinion as to whether or not
the education authority had or had not
made such a choice as would exclude the
Ministry's nomination altogether. And
upon the whole the Government think
their plan is the better, and so they cannot accept the Amendment.
Amendment negatived.
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: On this question I think
it is necessary to say that judging from
the debate on the preceding Amendments
the Clause as it now stands is not giving
universal satisfaction. I think we can
only come to the conclusion that the effect
of the Clause will be to foist on the existing authorities members whom those
authorities do not want, and whose assistance will not, I am afraid, benefit them.
It will take years for these new members
to live down the feeling of antipathy-I
will not say hostility-a certain feeling of
antipathy. I am very much afraid it
would be better for the Committee to refuse to insert the entire Clause. Let us
throw it out. That would leave it open
for consideration and possibly a
acceptable Clause might be introduced.
later on.

more
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
On a point of order. Not knowing the
procedure very well I want to know if I
had not a right to have my Amendment
put to the vote.
Mr. SPEAKER: I put the Question
and there was no reply.
Colonel
SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
There were two "Contents."
Mr. SPEAKER: I am sorry, but I
did not hear them. Of course the whole
thing is due to Members who will not
speak up. I would like also that they
would stand up when they want to speak.
Very often I do not know whether they
are uncomfortable in their seats or
whether they want to speak.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Amendment of the principal Acts as to committees for management of schools and their powers).
(1) The following section shall be
substituted for section three of the principal Act as amended by the Education
Act (Northern Ireland), 1925:-
"3.-(1) The education authority
shall, as respects every public elementary school within their education
area which is a provided school or a
transferred school (as hereinafter in
this Act defined), make provision for
the local management of the school
by means of a school management
committee to be appointed by the
education authority; but SO that
Education Bill-
Committee.
30 MAY 1930
several such schools may be grouped
under one school management committee, if the education authority so
desire and the Ministry approves.
(2) A school management committee appointed under the last preceding sub-section shall be so constituted
that, so far as is practicable, not less
than one-half of the members to be
appointed thereto shall be representative of the following persons:
(a) the transferors of the transferred schools under the local
management of the committee; and
(b) the persons who were for
merly managers of any public
elementary schools which have been
superseded by provided schools
under the local management of the
committee, or, if those persons
were recognised by the Ministry
as such managers by virtue of any
office, then their successors in such
office;
and the remaining members shall to
the extent of not more than onefourth of the total number, be persons nominated by the parents of
children attending the school or
schools under the local management
of the committee at a meeting held
in accordance with regulations of the
Ministry, and except as last aforesaid, shall be persons nominated by
the education authority.
The education authority shall
nominate in addition, as respects
each school management committee,
one or more representatives of the
school teachers employed at the
school or schools under the local
management of the school
management committee, and such representative or representatives shall have
the right to take part in meetings of
the school management committee
but shall not have the right to vote
on any question regarding the selection of a teacher.
If any question arises as to the
right of any person or body of persons under this sub-section to be represented on or nominate a member
of a school management committee
that question shall stand referred to
the Ministry, whose decision shall be
final.
(3) Where in the case of a trans
ferred school, the transfer was made
before the first day of April, aineteen hundred and thirty, and on
condition that a separate manage.
ment committee should be appointed
for the school, or that the management committee for the school should
be constituted in some specified manner, then, notwithstanding anything
contained in either of the foregoing
sub-sections, any such condition may
be dispensed with if the transferors
consent, but not otherwise.
(4) Whenever, except in a case to
which this sub-section does not apply,
it is necessary that a teacher shall be
appointed for a provided or transferred public elementary school under
the local management of a school
management committee, the education
authority of the education area within
which the school is situate shall, in
the manner prescribed by the Ministry, issue advertisements inviting
applications for the appointment and
transmit the applications, with particulars of the candidates, to the
school management committee. The
school management committee shall
consider the applications for the appointment, and shall select therefrom and submit to the education
authority, to be dealt with under the
provisions of sub-section (3) of section sixty-six of this Act, the names
of such of the candidates as are qualified for the time being under the
regulations of the Ministry (in this
Act referred to as "qualified candidates'), or, if there are more than
three qualified candidates, the names
of three of them, and shall take such
further steps in relation to the appointment as may be required under
the said provisions.
This sub-section does not apply in
a case where the education authority
propose to appoint to a provided
school a teacher who is serving in a
school, or department of a school,
which is superseded in whole or in
part by the said provided school, nor
does it apply to the appointment of
a temporary substitute for a teacher
or to the temporary appointment of a
teacher.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I beg to move, on
page 2, line 36, to leave out "one-half"
"less than one-half of the") and insert
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Armstrong]
"one-third." I am encouraged to move
this Amendment because I heard a very
important minister of religion say he was
prepared to accept one-third instead of
half. I thought that was a very reasonable acceptance of his. With regard to
compromises being effected outside Parliament they have to be submitted to the
House. I feel there is not much hope of
carrying this one, but I think it would
have been much more satisfactory if the
division had been one-third each-onethird nominated members, one-third representatives of parents, and one-third representatives of the education authority.
I think the arguments that have been
adduced by the hon. and gallant Senator
on an Amendment akin to this one were
sufficient, and I need say no more.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think if
you will allow me I will take the particular Amendment of Senator Armstrong
with his later Amendment in page 3, line
11.
These Amendments have the effect of
reducing the proportion of ex-managers
on school management committees from
one-half to one-third and of raising the
proportion of parents' representatives
from one-fourth to one-third. The Government prefers the proportions set forth.
in the Bill. The chief object aimed at
in prescribing the constitution of these
Committees is to preserve the influence
of those who managed and controlled the
school before transfer so far as the initiative in the appointment of teachers is concerned. Any Amendment, therefore,
which would reduce the number of Committee members representatives of former
managers to below one-half would fatally
weaken the position and render it possible
for an education authority hostile to the
school interests to appoint a teacher out
of harmony with local feeling. If it be
suggested that the representative of the
parents would make up a majority of the
committee if added to the representatives
of the ex-managers in the proportions set
forth in the two Amendments of Senator
Armstrong, the answer is that this
would not be a perfectly secure arrangement, as a change might be manipulated
in the character of the pupils' attendance,
such as, for example, through the enrolment of pupils from other schools, so as to
defeat the Government's intention. I
feel, therefore, that I must press the
House to stand by the terms of the Clause
and resist any Amendment designed to
alter the constitution of school management committees as therein determined.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I beg to move
on page 3, line 7, after "their" ("then
their successors in such office") to insert
"immediate." I wish to obtain from the
Government a statement of what exactly is
their intention and the meaning of "their
successors in office." Is the thing never
to become extinct? Are their successors
to be appointed in perpetuity? Is there
no limit?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Senator
Armstrong's supposition is more or less
correct. If it were limited to the immediate successors it would gradually divorce
religious interests from the schools after
their immediate successors disappeared.
It is one of the main objects of the Bill
to obviate that. I do not like to say "in
perpetuity." I do not like that at all.
But one might say until the next Amending Bill. (Laughter). I hope that will
be the same thing.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
beg to move on page 3, line 9, to leave
out lines 9 to 32 inclusive, that is to say.
from the words "such office" to the end
of sub-clause (2), and to insert:
"(c) Persons nominated by
parents of the children attending the
school or schools under the management
of the committee at a meeting held in
accordance with the regulations of the
Ministry.
the
"(d) One or more representatives of
the school teachers employed at the
school or schools under the local
management of the school management
committee."
The effect of the Amendment will be
to secure that the remaining half of the
members of school management committees shall be nominees of the education
authority, which is only reasonable. It
might really happen that there would not
be a single member of the regional committee on the school management committee. I think that would be very
objectionable.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to take an example of what might
happen under this Amendment. A school
committee, to take the round number
Education Bill-
Committee
30 MAY 1930

12, might be as follows:-Transferors,
representatives of superseded schools,
ex-managers and SO on, the representatives of parents and teachers would
be entitled to at least half or it might be
seven, and the education authority would
have the remainder, that is to say, five,
or they might have six. The chief object of the Bill is to protect the schools
from interference by a possibly hostile
authority. I admit that such a case has
not yet arisen, but you cannot in this
country, at any rate, prophesy that it will
not arise. I say, if you had this proportion the numbers would not be
sufficient to reject the possibility of interference. You might have half and
half, and then one might be ill or away.
Many people do go away at a critical
division-not at school committees only.
You might have the very thing brought
to a head which this Bill is designed to
obviate. For that reason I regret that
I cannot accept the Amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
beg leave to move, on page 3, line 42
(last line of Sub-clause (3)) to strike out
the word "transferors" and substitute
the words "Education Authority."
The reason I propose this Amendment
is that this Sub-clause deals with schools
which have been already transferred
under different conditions to those set out
in the earlier part of the Clause, as is
the case in County Down. Under the
Clause, as drafted, it would appear that
those conditions may only be dispensed
with by consent of the transferors. There
does not appear to be any good reason
why their consent should be necessary
rather than that of the transferees, the
Education Authority. The fairest way
would be to make the consent of both the
transferors and the education authority
necessary.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The drawback to this Amendment is that the
transferors who have transferred their
schools already-meritoriously from the
Government's point of view-under the
first Education Act would be penalised.
They would not be able to accept the
more favourable conditions offered now.
I do not think the question is very likely
to arise. At the same time I do not
wish that the people who transferred before and accepted the conditions laid
down, possibly under financial pressure,
should be prevented from sharing the
conditions given under the present Bill.
Therefore, I must resist the Amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Of course, everything and everybody is
always considered more than the education authority. There is no use in going.
on, and I beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
((
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move on page
4, line 19, to leave out from the beginning of the line ("or, if there are more")
to “and” (“of them, and") in line 20,
and insert and, if there are more than
three qualified candidates, shall intimate
to the education authority the three candates who, in the opinion of the said
committee, are best qualified for the particular appointment."
This is a matter of rather different
character. Hitherto we have been dealing with the interests of education authorities and transferors. Now we are
approaching, as it seems to me, a question in which the teachers are involved.
As the Clause stands, if there are more
than three qualified candidates the school
management committee is only bound to
send forward the names of three of them.
I do not suppose it is very likely that there
will be only three applicants, or that
there will be 300. There are much
more likely to be four or five, and it does
not seem to me to be fair to the odd two,
in the case of five, that the school
management committee should omit
their names when sending forward the
list. I think all the names should be
forwarded to the higher authority. It is
perfectly right and reasonable that the
school committee should have the power
to intimate which three of them, in their
opinion, are best qualified for the particular appointment. It seems to me that is
a reasonable limit to their authority and,
therefore, I move the alteration on the
Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think it
is desirable to leave the choice of qualified candidates entirely to the feeling
of the school management committee, and
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
not to fetter it by any instructions of the
kind proposed. It is, of course, expected
that in sending forward names of teachers
committees will choose those best qualified for the position, and no doubt their
own natural sense of what is fitting and
best for the school will lead them to act
in this way.
But it must not be forgotten that the Government's desire in
this legislation is to allow the school
committee a wide degree of discretion in
choosing teachers in harmony with local
feeling that is what I have already said
-and I think that it is on the whole
best to leave the matter to them to choose
the best candidates, all of whom must,
of course, be qualified for the positionotherwise they are not eligible. For that
reason I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: Can the noble Viscount
assure us that no organised body of
teachers has taken any objection to the
proposal as it stands in the Bill? If it
has been received with approbation by
the teachers themselves, or if they have
not taken the trouble, through their
organisation, to make any complaint, I
should certainly be prepared to ask the
Committee to permit me to withdraw the
Amendment. Owing to the shortness of
the time I have not been able to look
into the matter at all. If the noble Viscount can give me that assurance, I will
ask leave to withdraw.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
I cannot
say the teachers have accepted it with
approbation, because that would be going
too far. At the same time I have received
deputations from teachers on the matter,
and I cannot say they expressed any
furious objection to it. I cannot exactly
give the assurance asked for, but I have
known proposals of another nature that
met with more objection than this one did.
Perhaps I had better say I can give a
qualified assurance.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the Committee
will, perhaps, be prepared to accept that
and allow me to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

school being a provided or transferred

education authority in accordance
with the following provisions:—

are not satisfied to appoint some
one or other of the said qualified

of such

under the last preceding paragraph

The following Amendment stood in the
name of Mr. Leslie: Page 6, lines 10 to
17, leave out paragraph (i).
Mr. LESLIE: I do not move, as this
is consequential on the last Amendment.
Amendment not moved.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD :
I beg to move in page 6, line 18, to leave
out lines 18 to 22 (paragraph ii).
The reason I suggest this Amendment
should be adopted is that under this
Clause the Ministry takes the power to
direct the appointment of a particular
teacher in the event of the education
authority refusing to appoint a teacher
recommended by a school management
committee. This is a mild invasion of
the powers of education authorities, for
which there does not appear to be any
justification. Under the scheme in force.
in County Down, one of the functions of
a school management committee is to advise in the appointment of teachers when
vacancies arise. Applications for
vacancy in the teaching staff of a school
are forwarded by the education authority
to the committee,
a
who shall have the right to advise the
authority with reference to the candidates, always provided that the education authority retains full responsibility in the actual appointment of the
teacher, and is under no obligation to
accept the recommendation of the
school committee.
This arrangement has, so far, worked
well in County Down, and there does not
seem any reason why the Ministry should
take the opportunity under this Bill of
assuming to itself further powers at the
expense of the education authority.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I beg to support this Amendment. I do not think
it reasonable that the education authority
should have any further powers filched
from them and handed over to the Ministry. The Ministry might as well take
charge of the entire Act, and if they remove the appointment of teachers, and
that is virtually what it means, in the
event of a disagreement the Ministry will
come down and cast everything aside, and
make their own appointment. Undoubtedly, there will be occasional disagreements between the education authorities,
regional committee and the local committee. I think this should be left in the
hands of the regional committee. I shall
certainly support the Amendment.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I can
quite readily understand that in the
working of this Act there may be disagreements from time to time between
these different committees. Someone is
required who will be an umpire, and I
think no one could act in that capacity
better than the Ministry.
Colonel

SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: We
have heard a great deal about County
Down, but there are other places to which

Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I

Mr. JOSEPII CUNNINGHAM: There

between these committees.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The right
hon. and gallant Senator who moved the
Amendment said it was a mild invasion.
I think it is a very mild one indeed, and
is scarcely filching, as Mr. Hill Dickson
described it. It is a fact that there must
be some umpire in the matter. There
must be some final person to direct the
appointment. At the present moment,
if the Amendment was carried, there
would be nothing to prevent an education authority refusing to appoint a
teacher by a continual rejection of all the
candidates when their names are submitted. There must be some finality
somewhere. However, I think the circumstances are extremely unlikely to
happen in County Down, even after what
Senator Cunningham said, though he
may have been sarcastic. I do not think
such a case has arisen in County Down.
It is not likely to arise there or in any of
the other counties, but if such a case did
arise in the future there should be some
final authority to deal with it. I cannot accept the Amendment.

CLAUSE 4 (Provision of Bible instruction in provided and transferred
schools).
(1) If Bible instruction is not provided by the education authority in
any public elementary school, being a
provided school or transferred school,
it shall be the duty of the education
authority to provide Bible instruction,
should the parents of not less than ten
children who are in regular attendance
at such school make application to the
education authority for that purpose
in the prescribed manner: Provided
that the obligation under this subsection shall not arise where there is
another public elementary school in
which Bible instruction is given and
which such children can conveniently
attend.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
7, line 22, after "which" ("given and
which") to insert, "in the opinion of the
Ministry."
This is rather a small point. I have
two suggested Amendments to this Clause,
and I am inclined to think that the main
principle of both is the same. The phrase
as it stands is "and which such children
can conveniently attend." The whole
question in this Sub-clause cannot, it
seems to me, arise unless there is something in the nature of a dispute between
the children and the school authority.
Therefore, the question comes up for discussion as to who is to state whether or
not the attendance at this other school
is or is not convenient for the children.
So far as I can make out, all we have got
to help us is Sub-clause 4, giving the
Ministry power to make regulations, and
I am not quite satisfied that it goes far
enough. I think it would be better to
put in an authority to settle any question
that may arise, bearing in mind, as I say,
that it will not have to be considered except in the case of a dispute. I, therefore, beg to move that we put in the words
"in the opinion of the Ministry." This
will, I think, become effectual if there is
really any serious trouble.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to accept an Amendment which will admit
that the Ministry is the final authority in
anything. (Laughter.)
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
7, line 23, at end ("conveniently attend")
to add, "and provided also that the said
obligation shall continue so long as any
of the ten children in respect of whom the
obligation arose are in attendance as aforesaid."
This is a sort of continuation of the
same thing. I am still working on the
assumption that something in the nature
of a dispute has arisen. We have got the
parents of not fewer than ten children who
have applied for Bible instruction, but in
the course of nature some three or four
of those children will attain the age of
fourteen years, and in the course of practice they will cease to attend the school.
What is going to happen in regard to
Bible instruction for those that are left
of the ten? They may, of course, be reinforced by other children, but we cannot
have this requisition going on perpetually.
It, therefore, seems to me that if we rather
strengthened up the Clause by providing
that the obligation to give Bible instruction should continue as long as any of the
ten children are there it might, perhaps,
be something more of a safeguard than
exists at present.
I do not
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like this very much. I accepted the last
Amendment, but I do not like the idea
of carrying on Bible instruction if
there is only in attendance one child
of the original ten on whose behalf
it was given. To continue the arrangement if there was only one child I think
Education Bill-
Committee.
30 MAY 1930
It would be
would hardly be reasonable.
all very well if there were ten if the authority is to be under legal obligation to provide the instruction, but if the number
diminished it might be advisable in certain
cases to make other more suitable arrangements. Moreover, it might lead to an
abuse of the arrangement if the presence
of ten children in the school at any particular time were to be considered as keeping the obligation in existence when
several of the ten had left. I think there
should be a minimum of ten. I would
prefer not to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not like to press the
Amendment very much. The point is
clear that there were those ten children
all receiving Bible instruction. Five of
them grow up and go away, and is the
Bible instruction to the remaining five to
come to an untimely end because there are
only five just men left to receive it?
think I had better ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment, and, perhaps, the noble
Viscount will look into it between now
and the Report stage.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

CLAUSE 5 (Protection for teachers
in schools provided or transferred).
No teacher in any school provided
by or transferred to an education
authority may be required, as a condition of appointment or holding office,
to teach any religious catechism or religious formulary which is distinctive.
of any particular religious denomination.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
8, line 36, after "which" ("which is distinctive") insert "in the opinion of the
Ministry."
The point may easily arise, as it seems
to me, whether a particular religious formulary is distinctive of any particular
arise, somebody has got to settle it. It
seems to me that the only authority
available is the Ministry, who after all,
apart from all their other virtues, we should
recognise, although the noble Lord casts
some doubt on that.
The Ministry,
after all, is the responsible Department.
If it is necessary to provide safeguarding
at all, I think the Ministry is the proper repository of that safeguarding.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Amendment cannot be agreed to. The Ministry.
could not undertake to determine whether
any particular religious formulary was
or was not "distinctive of any particular
religious denomination." This is eminently a matter that could only be determined in case of doubt or dispute by the
judges. The determination of this matter under English educational legislation,
where it has appeared in the well-known
Cowper-Temple Clause of the Education
Acts for the last half century, has always
been left to the Law Courts. I should
prefer, under certain circumstances, that
the court take on the pleasant duties of
the Ministry of Education.
Mr. LESLIE: I am more than content. I think it is an excellent matter
for the court.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
beg to move, in page 9, line 7, leave out

I am glad to say that this is my last

This Clause is designed to extend the
Committee
Education Bill-

THE SENATE
[Colonel Sharman-Crawford]
of 1923 on the Ministry to make, what
might be termed, compulsory orders on
education authorities. Under Section 98,
the Ministry is very properly given power,
where an education authority make default
in performing any of their duties under
the Act, or fails to comply with any Order
of the Ministry in relation to any of those
duties, or act in any manner which, in

to the promotion of elementary or higher
education, to make an Order after holding
a public inquiry, compelling the authority
to fulfil their duty, and such Order may be
enforced by mandamus.
In
Under Clause 6 (b) of the Bill, however,
this power of the Ministry of making compulsory Orders is extended to making an
Order compelling an education authority
to exercise such of their powers (as distinguished from duties) under the principal Act as in the opinion of the Ministry
require to be exercised for the promotion
of elementary or higher education.
other words, the discretion of education
authorities as to the exercise or
exercise of any of their powers is entirely
taken away and handed over to the
Ministry, and education authorities become mere cyphers, whose function will be
to carry out such Orders as the Ministry
may from time to time think fit to make.
non-
A case in point is the power given
education authorities under the principal
Act of providing food for school children
in cases where an education authority are
of opinion that any of the children in
their area are unable, by reason of lack of
food, to take full advantage of the education provided for them. (Section 51.)
This is a very proper power, and may be
very useful if properly exercised, but very
harmful and pauperising if improperly
exercised. It would seem that the education authority, being in direct touch with
the children attending their schools and
having the best means of becoming
acquainted with their circumstances, are
the proper body to decide whether this
power should be exercised in any particular case, and it is so provided in the
section.
But under the proposed Clause 6 (b) of
the Bill, this discretion will be entirely
taken out of the hands of the education
authority, and power will be given to
officials of the Ministry sitting in an office
in Belfast to decide whether the education
authority ought to exercise this power of
feeding school children or not, and to make
an Order compelling them to do so. No
doubt such Order could only be made after
a public inquiry, but it must be remembered the inquiry would be held by officials
of the Ministry, who would thus be judges
in their own cause, and have power to
make an Order no matter what representations were put forward by an education
authority. This is one illustration of how
the Clause would work out in practice,
and is cited because the question of feeding
school children in a particular area has
arisen in County Down, and the education
authority have, in the exercise of their
discretion, refused to exercise the power.
If the Clause passes, it will destroy the
last vestige of popular control of education. If the Government cannot trust
local authorities to exercise their powers
properly under the Act, would it not have
been better to withdraw education from
so-called popular control altogether and
administer it as a Government department? In conclusion, I may say that I
have been a supporter of the Government.
I am one of the oldest county councillors,
having been a member of the first county
council appointed. I would just mention
that if this Bill is to pass immediately
there will be considerable friction between
county councils and the Government.
Mr. LESLIE: I think, if I may put it
briefly, there is a great deal in the point
made. As I read the original Clause
(Section 98) in the Act of 1923, that
Clause is devoted entirely to the duties of
the authority. I do not want to go into a
long disquisition as to the difference
between powers and duties. The fact
is that the original Clause was limited
to duties. I think when this House
passed the original Clause (Section 98)
we were distinctly of opinion that it
would be limited to duties; it was only
supposed to be in the last resource, when
the authority was hopelessly in default,
that this Clause would be relied on. I do
not know myself of anything at the
moment which could possibly call for the
exercise of such a power as the hon. and
gallant Member refers to. I rather hope
that the Ministry will find it possible to
meet his wishes and excise this paragraph.
Incidentally that course will save me
moving the next Amendment standing in
my name. I support the proposal.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: The
hon. and gallant Member (Colonel Sharman-Crawford) said if his Amendment
Education Bill-
Committee
30 MAY 1930
was not passed it would cause friction. I
was discussing this Bill with some representative people last night, and I am afraid
that if the Amendment was passed it
would cause friction too. For that reason
I will vote against the Amendment, and
support the Clause in order to prevent
anything like friction in the future.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: We have had
the same powers since the 1923 Act was
passed, and the Regional Committee of
Down have never had any friction during
that time. This is a thing that will cause
friction between the Regional Committee
and the Government.
Viscount
should it?
CHARLEMONT : Why
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Because you
are going to take powers away from us.
Viscount
thetical.
CHARLEMONT:
Нуро-
Mr. HILL DICKSON: We have used
our discretion in providing anything that
was necessary for the children, and the
Government are going to take that power
out of the hands of the Regional Committee, and say we are nothing but nonentities.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
would remind the House that we are not
legislating for Down alone. We have to
consider all the counties of Northern
Ireland.
Mr. ARMSTRONG : In addition to
that we have to consider that we shall not
always have with us the present Ministry
of Education, and its drastic powers may
be a curse.
!
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to accept the small laurel thrown at the
I am
present Ministry of Education.
glad to accept anything of that kind. The
public inquiry would certainly be conducted by officials of the Ministry of
Education. It is suggested that the
Ministry by taking the discretion out of
the hands of the Regional Committee and
having an inquiry on a subject of this kind.
reduces the committee to the position of
mere nonentities. I disagree with this
idea. I think the original Section 98 was
in the main to provide for possibilities of
this kind. I will give an example of what
I mean. If an authority refused to provide any scholarships for the children and
young persons in its area, it would be depriving the people of an obvious and
highly-valued educational facility, although the cost of such facility might fall
on the rates only to a very limited extent.
I hold that it is not right that Parliament
should be without a remedy in such an
extreme case as this, and it is clear that
no Minister would use his powers under
this Clause unless the case were a flagrant
We have never had, it is perfectly
true, anything of this kind in County
Down. If the County Down people are
the only people to be legislated for no
doubt the results would be different.
one.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Hear, hear.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: We have
to legislate for other counties as well. I
suggest that a proper saying for County
Down in future to apply to that particular
section is "Who fears to speak of '98?"
The Amendment cannot be accepted.
Question put, "That those words be left
out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 4:
Not Contents, 12.

shall be so construed as to enable the

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
The question of the necessity for this
proviso is one on which, perhaps, the Noble
Viscount will advise us. I should not
have put the suggestion on the Paper but
for paragraph (b), which has just been
retained by the Committee. The point is
whether we have to safeguard further the
position as regards the holding of a public
inquiry as originally required.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think this
Amendment is unnecessary,
as I am
advised that no order can be made without
a public inquiry.

The following Amendment stood in the

Amendment not moved.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Powers for the education
of children in exceptional circumstances).
Where an education authority are
satisfied, in the case of any children,
that, owing to the remoteness of the
children's homes or the conditions under
which they are living, or to any exceptional circumstances affecting them,
those children are not in a position to
receive the full benefit of elementary
education by means of the ordinary
provision available for the purpose, the
authority may, with the approval of
the Ministry, make such arrangements,
including the provision of board and
lodging, as they think best suited for
the purpose of enabling those children
to receive the benefit of efficient elementary education, and may, for that purpose, enter into such agreement with
the parent of any such child as they
think proper:
Provided that when a child is boarded
out in pursuance of this section, the
education authority shall, if possible,
and if the parent or guardian so requests, arrange for the boarding out
being with a person belonging to the
religious persuasion of the child's
parents.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
7, page 9, to leave out line 32 ("if possible, and if the parent or guardian so
requests') and to insert "not without the
approval of the Ministry."
First of all, I have to apologise to the
Committee for the fact that my Amendment to this Clause is obviously
incomplete. In order to make my proposa!
intelligible I must ask the Committee to
imagine that the word "not" is to be
inserted after the word "person" in line
33, so as to make the Clause read:
"The education authority shall not.
without the approval of the Ministry.
arrange for the boarding out being with
a person not belonging to the religious
persuasion of the child's parents.'
I shall move in a minute as a necessary
addition to insert the word "not" between
"person" and "belonging" in line 34.
The CHAIRMAN: I have the word
"not" in line 33.
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment is in
line 32. The proposal is to leave out the
words in line 32 and to insert the words
"not without the approval of the
Ministry," and then-and this is the part
which is not in my Amendment on the
Paper-to put in the following line, which
is line 33, the word "not" between "person" and "belonging," so that the
Clause will read-
The CHAIRMAN: Will you read
exactly how the Amendment will then
read?
Mr. LESLIE:
then read-
The whole thing will
The CHAIRMAN: No. Will the hon.
Member tell me exactly how the Amendment would read if it had been printed
on the Paper? Will there be separate
Amendments?
Mr. LESLIE: There will have to be
two separate Amendments. I think it is
now understood that the word not
will have to go in to make sense. Mv
point is that I do not think the Clause
as it stands is strong enough.
Education Bill-
Committee.
30 MAY 1930
Mr. CAMPBELL: Might I respectfully
ask what is the Amendment we are discussing at the moment?
The CHAIRMAN: I think the Amendment is quite clear.
Mr. CAMPBELL: What are the particular words the hon. Member (Mr.
Leslie) is proposing to insert at the
moment?
The CHAIRMAN: Page 9, leave out
line 32 and insert "not without the
approval of the Ministry." That, I
understand, is correct, and then there is
a new consequential Amendment, which
is on page 9, line 33, after the word
"person" to insert the word "not."
Mr. LESLIE: That is right. Thank
you very much. I am sorry my mistake
has given so much trouble. The Clause
will now read:
"Provided that when
a
child is
boarded out in pursuance of this
section, the education authority shall
not without the approval of the Ministry arrange for the boarding out being
with a person not belonging to the
religious persuasion of the child's
parents."
It does not seem to me that the Clause as
it stands is quite strong enough to give
the child the protection in this matter to
which it is entitled. The Committee will
remember a somewhat similar provision in
the Children Act of 1908, and also a provision in the Poor Law Act of 1882, or
1888 rather-or somewhere about that
time-which gave the guardians power to
declare themselves guardians of the child
and then to board it out. It has always
been required that the utmost care should
be taken to see that the boarding out
should be with a person of the same religious persuasion as the parents of the
child. As the Clause stands the arrangement cannot be completed unless the
parents or guardians of the child so request. I do not think that is strong
enough, and, therefore, I move the
suggestions which are partly on the Paper
and partly not.
The
CHAIRMAN: I cannot make
out this grammar. It does not seem to
me to be right. However, probably that
is more a matter for the noble Viscount
(Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
rather like this Amendment. I believe
it makes the position much clearer, though
the next line would require to be altered
in some way to make it intelligible. But
this certainly takes the matter out of the
hands of any body which might be careless, draws the Ministry's attention to it,
and obtains their approval, which I think
is really necessary in this case. There are
some people who are very careless with
regard to the placing of children and the
religious persuasion of the foster parents.
I believe this Amendment would make
the matter even clearer and more satisfactory if the noble Leader of the House
would accept it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
like it very much I must admit. The
proviso was inserted in another place and
the words are taken from the English
Education Act, from which Clause 7 of
the Bill was copied. I think an education authority can really be trusted to
see that a child is boarded out with
parents of the proper religious persuasion,
and I do not think it would be right to
remove from the parents or guardian of
the child the powers which are given in
the Bill. The arrangement would seem
to be a really proper one as it is, and I
would prefer that the Ministry was not
brought into a thing of this kind. I
really think the education authorities can
be trusted to look after a matter of this
kind.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Might I respectfully
suggest to the noble Leader of the House
that he would take the matter into consideration between now and the Report
stage?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Very well.
I am agreeable.
Mr. MCALLISTER: My difficulty is
that I cannot at the moment make up my
mind as to whether the original proposal
in the Bill does not carry out what we all
want-what I think the whole Committee
wants more definitely and more effective than the Amendment. It would
appear to me, on looking casually at the
Amendment and at the original Clause
that the original Clause renders it compulsory for an education authority to
board a child out with people of the same
religious persuasion as the parents of the
child. That is the intention, but in the
suggested Amendment it would seem to
Education Bill- .
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
me that it leaves it optional entirely with
the education authority as to whether they
do or do not as a fact board the child out
with people of the same religious denomination as the parents of the child. I would
like to consider the matter further from
that point of view.
Mr. LESLIE: I might point out that
my chief objection to the Clause as it
stands is that it puts the duty of requesting that the child shall be boarded out in
the proper religious atmosphere on the
parent or guardian. It might be better.
to withdraw this Amendment and put
down another for the Report stage. I am
sure there is not the least difference of
opinion as to what ought to be done. It
is only a question of the best way of doing
it. As we all know, in the case of some of
these children, it is remarkably hard to
get at the parent or the guardian at all.
I do not think we ought to leave it quite
like that. We should have an anthority
somewhere, and I am afraid we shall have
to ask the Ministry to accept the responsibility. Under my Amendment the procedure would be that, first of all, the
education authority would propose to do
it, and then would have to get the approval
of the Ministry, so that it must be a very
strong case in which the Ministry would
have to interfere. For the moment, however, I will withdraw the Amendment and
put it down again for Report.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (Amendment of s. 15 of
the principal Act as to voluntary schools).
"(
Sub-section (2) of section fifteen of
the principal Act shall have effect
subject to the following Amendments:-
(a) In paragraph (a) of the said
sub-section two years shall be substituted for eighteen months;
(b) It shall not be lawful for an
education authority to refuse to approve of estimates of expenditure duly
submitted to them by the managers
or body controlling a voluntary
school unless such estimates are excessive or such expenditure is not
necessary for the efficient maintenance
of the school, and any question which
may arise under this provision shall
be referred to the Ministry, whose
decision shall be final";
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
8, page 9, line 43, at the end (" body controlling a voluntary school, unless'') to
add "in the opinion of such authority."
It seems
This is a very small matter.
to me that without some such words as I
suggest we are rather begging the question. The question must arise somewhere.
There is no definite scale, I imagine, which
can be referred to in order that an education authority can say right off whether
this or that estimate is excessive. I think
we want something just to cover that, and
then, in the event of a dispute, it goes
to the Ministry and the thing is settled.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The object
of the Sub-section is to secure that an
authority shall not refuse the grant for
heating, cleaning and repairs unless the
estimates submitted are in fact extravagant and excessive, and the last sentence
of the Sub-section makes the Ministry the
final judge of the matter in case of
dispute. But to include the words
now proposed would make the education
authority the judges in their own dispute,
and would deprive the management committee of the school of any appeal. I
think you would make them both prosecutors and judges. For that reason I
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: That is exactly what the
text does. There is nothing to guide
them. I think we ought to give them
some line to go upon.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If the hon.
Senator would like to withdraw the
Amendment, I shall consider the matter
later on.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Amendment of s. 16 of
the principal Act as to voluntary
schools).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
9, page 12, line 41, at the end to insert
as a new Sub-section :-
"(2) Where any vacancy arises
among trustees appointed for the
purposes of this section, the Ministry
may, by order, provide for the filling
of such vacancy and such order shall be
final and binding in respect of all
persons, matters and things concerned."
Education Bill-
30 MAY 1930
This Amendment was put down in
order that this matter might be considered. Under ordinary circumstances
when trustees are appointed some day or
other there is a vacancy, and then there
is a certain amount of formality and expense in appointing a new trustee.
Sometimes, perhaps more often, the
vacancy is not filled at all. It seems to
me very undesirable that in matters of
this kind the vacancy should be allowed
to continue. One trustee dies, the
second dies, the third dies, and there are
no trustees, with the result that there is
a great deal of trouble such as has already
been experienced in connection with the
transfer of schools. I think this is a case
in which something ought to be done, and
I suggest this as a possible way out.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think the
proposed new Sub-section is unnecessary,
as the deed under which the school premises were vested would ordinarily and
as a matter of course contain provisions
for the filling of vacancies amongst the
trustees. I am informed that if the deed
does not so provide there is statutory
provision in the Trustee Act for the filling.
of vacancies, but in that case the court
would appoint the trustees.
Mr. LESLIE: Obviously.
For that
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
reason I do not think it would be desirable
for the Ministry to exercise the same
powers as are exercised by the courts at
the present moment. I cannot, therefore, accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: The whole thing apparently resolves itself into a question of
administration. If the Ministry sees that
the proper Clause is put in the deed there
will be no further trouble.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Government grants and
loans for voluntary schools.)
(1) If the Ministry is satisfied that
sufficient and suitable provision for the
elementary education of any children of
school age resident in an education area
is required, and cannot otherwise be
made, then the Ministry may pay to
any person, for the purpose of the provision or equipment of a new voluntary
Committee.

school, or the alteration, enlargement,
reconstruction or equipment of an existing voluntary school, one-half of the
expenditure incurred for such purpose,
and such payment shall be subject to
the following conditions, that is to say:
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I beg to move, in
Clause 10, page 13, line 5, to leave out
the words
pay to any person, for the
purpose of," and to insert instead thereof
the words "contribute towards the cost
of."
The drafting of this Clause is somewhat
peculiar, and I think this Amendment
would improve it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government consider the words in the Clause
preferable to the Amendment, and cannot
agree to it.
These words follow the wording of the Loans for Schools and Training
Colleges (Ireland) Act, 1884, with which
the procedure in Sub-section (4) of the
Clause is related, and it is not desirable
to depart from them. Again, the form of
the Amendment is vague, as there is no
indication to whom or what parties the
"contribution" is to be made. If it be
thought that "any person" is too indefinite
a description, it is to be observed that a
grant is so limited by the statutory conditions that it cannot be made to any
irresponsible individual that may happen
to apply for it, seeing that the Ministry
must be satisfied that suitable provision
for the new school cannot be made otherwise than by a grant to this particular
applicant.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

ments of the Ministry in relation to

The CHAIRMAN: Just before the hon.
and learned Senator proceeds, I should
point out that I shall have to rule out of
order the proposed new Sub-sections 6 and
7 on the ground that they have the effect
of imposing a charge upon the Exchequer,
unless the hon. and learned Senator can
convince me that such is not the case.
Mr. CAMPBELL: No, I bow at once
to your Lordship's ruling. I beg to move,
in Clause 10, page 14, line 9, at the end
to add the following new Sub-section :-
(5) The provision of a new voluntary
school under sub-section (1) of this
section shall include the acquisition and
preparation of the school grounds, the
erection of the school buildings, and the
redemption of a rent or like charge on
the school grounds.
I put this Amendment down for the
purpose of ascertaining the mind of the
Ministry in regard to the meaning of the
word "provision." I recognise that the
word "provision" is a comprehensive
term. Perhaps it is as wide a term as
could have been chosen by the Ministry,
and I do not wish in any way to limit the
extent of its meaning. I think necessarily it covers the erection of the buildings, and I should also venture to think
that it includes such incidental matters
as the acquisition and preparation of
sites, and the redemption, that is to say,
the out-and-out purchase of the rent or
other like charge.
matters which do
It may include other
not occur to my mind
at the moment. Perhaps the Noble Lord
will tell us the mind of the Ministry in
regard to this matter.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think this
proposed Amendment is unnecessary, as
the word "provision" includes the cost of
sites and the other things mentioned in the
proposed new sub-section. I think it
would be undesirable for a second reason,
because it would appear that if the word
"provision" only included what the hon.
and learned Senator put down in his
Amendment, it might exclude other items
of expense which might conceivably arise.
I would, therefore, ask the hon. and
learned Member to withdraw his Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Having regard to
the statement of the Leader of the House,
which represents the considered opinion of
the Ministry, I beg leave to withdraw the
Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: This Clause is a very
great departure from the principle of the
original Act, and it seems to me very likely
to create many difficulties. One of the
great difficulties is that of which the hon.
and learned Senator has just spoken.
Although this Clause provides for the
building of the school, there is no provision
to keep the school in repair. That provision cannot be moved here for the reasons
you have given. But the fact remains
that Parliament has apparently provided
money for the erection of a school under
certain circumstances, of which the
Ministry will be the judge, but is apparently content that the building may
fall down again, as it makes no provision
whatever for repairs. I cannot help
wondering whether it is really desirable
that this Clause should be inserted in the
Bill at all. I do not disguise the fact that
I do not like any departure from the rule
as respects the four-and-two committees,
which were sanctioned by Parliament
seven years ago.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: This
is certainly a very far-reaching Clause.
I think those people who endeavoured to
have the Act amended so as to give them
Education Bill-
Committee.
30 MAY 1930
TATI
power to obtain Bible-teaching in the
schools have hardly benefited to the same
extent as those who are likely to benefit
under this Clause. Certainly this is a
tremendous concession, and, as Senator
Leslie has said, is a big departure from the
principle of the principal Act. However,
I expect that the Government have carefully considered the position, and are quite
prepared to carry this out.
I would not,
therefore, like to raise any objection to it
now.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
wish to go into this whole question again,
but I would say that under this Bill when
it passes the position will be that schools.
will be divided into three classes so far as
advantage is concerned. The transferred
schools will get everything; the four-andtwo schools will get two-thirds, and the
voluntary schools under this Clause will
get a 50 per cent. grant. The whole object
of an Education Bill is to produce, not
something that is logical, but something
that will work; and I think that if we were
not giving this concession the working of
the Bill in certain quarters would be
rendered difficult in the future.
Government took this view into considera-
The
tion and gave this concession.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask the Noble
Lord is it not correct that one result of the
Bill is to provide that transferred schools
will receive 100 per cent. of repairs, the
four-and-two schools 50 per cent., and the
voluntary schools not one per cent. of
repairs from year to year.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Repairs do
not come into the proviso.

Bill.

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in

any

to an
In moving this Amendment, I wish to
draw attention to the form of the Clause
in the Bill. It is a Clause giving power
education authority to provide
hostels for pupils attending certain
schools. I should like to be perfectly
clear as to the exact meaning of the
Clause. There may be I am sure the
noble Lord will correct me if I err-other
schools besides those under the management of an education authority in a particular area, and as I understand the
Clause it limits the power of providing
hostels for pupils to establishments which
are under the control or management of
an education authority. There may be
in certain areas schools that are not under
the control of education authorities. I
think there are such schools in the city of
Belfast, and I do not see why, if the education authority has taken the power to
provide these benefits for the schools of
one class, schools of other classes should
not be covered by the same benefits as are
given to schools under the management
of an education authority.
This
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Amendment goes beyond the intention of
the Government. The object of the
Clause is to enable an authority which
maintains a secondary or technical school
to provide hostel accommodation for the
pupils attending these schools, for the
reason that without such accommodation
the benefits of the schools to the pupils
may be seriously curtailed. But where
secondary or other schools are maintained
by governing bodies it would be proper
to expect that these bodies would themselves maintain their
Own hostels or
boarding establishments. I am, therefore, unable to accept the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Education Bill-

THE SENATE
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, in Clause
13, page 15, line 3, to leave out the word
"or" (intermediate or secondary). I
understand that considerable difficulty has
been experienced by girls attending technical schools in small towns for the purpose of receiving instruction in domestic
economy in obtaining safe and suitable
lodgings. Some authorities think they
should have the power to provide board
for these girls. Accordingly, I beg to
move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Both this
Amendment and the next one in the name

the Bill has already been amended in
another place to this effect. I think the
hon. Member has proposed the Amendment under a misapprehension.

CLAUSES 14 (Amendment of s. 20 of

and 15 (Further powers as to higher

CLAUSE 16 (Amendment of s. 30 of
the principal Act as to attendance at
school).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
16, page 16, line 28, at the end of paragraph (a) to insert:
"Provided that no child under the age
of six years shall be required to attend a
school under the provisions of this section
if the parent of the child objects to the
attendance of the child."
This suggested proviso deals with one of
the matters I had in my mind when I
suggested on the Second Reading that it
would be necessary for us at a later stage
to make sure that due consideration had
been paid to the difference between the
country and the town. As we know,
there are limits of distance according to
age. Whether in the town or in the
country a mile is 1,760 vards, but a mile
in the country in wet weather is a very
different proposition to a mile in the town.
It seems to me that having regard to the

original purpose of the 1923 Act, which
was to have due regard to parental responsibility, we ought not to interfere here to
such an extent as to allow an education
authority by by-law to make a child of
five years and six months residing in a
rural area attend school if the parent
thinks that the child is not fitted to do
SO. In the case of a child of tender age,
I think the opinion of the parent ought
to be the governing factor. I think this
is a very reasonable concession to ask, and
I do so particularly on behalf of the rural
districts, where the children have to put
up with a good deal in the way of weather
if they are to get to school. A child of
five and a half and a snowstorm is not at
all a suitable combination.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I must
admit that I have a certain amount of
sympathy with the Amendment, but I
have difficulty in accepting it in its present
form, because obviously if a regional committee has power to make an order for
compulsory attendance and yet give an
individual parent the power to object
to send his child to school it would
not be a compulsory law, but a sort of
voluntary law. Perhaps if the hon. and
learned Member will withdraw his Amendment for the time being we might look into
the matter again on the Report stage. In
its present form I do not think it can be
accepted owing to the difficulty I have
outlined.
Mr. LESLIE: I
see the noble
Viscount's difficulty, but my point is that
the education authority is not the parent
of the child.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In that
you would not have compulsory
attendance at all.
case
Mr. LESLIE: We are dealing with a
child under the age of six in a country
district. However, I certainly think it
would be desirable to leave the matter over
for further consideration. Am I to understand the noble Viscount will bring up an
Amendment on Report, or does he want
me to do so.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: You do it.
Mr. LESLIE: You want me to re-cast
this Amendment? I will face that, but
hope I shall be given a day or two to
do it.

(d) In sub-section (2) after the word

""
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, to leave
out paragraph (d). This is another
little difficulty. I do not know why hon.
Members find some of these difficulties
the subject of mirth. If they lived in
the country districts and had occasionally
to administer justice in school attendance
cases they would take rather a different
view. The proposal here is to take out
the proposed alteration in the Londonderry Act. The proposal is to alter subsection (2) of Section 30, and I am
afraid I shall have to read it in order to
refresh my memory.
The parent of every child between
the ages of six and fourteen, or, if a
by-law made by the education authority and confirmed in accordance with
this Act so prescribes, between the ages
of five and fourteen, shall cause the
child to attend school during such
number of days in the year, and for
such time on each day of attendance,
as are prescribed by regulations made
by the Ministry, except in so far as-
(a) the child being not less than twelve
years of age has been exempted from
school attendance by the education
authority under the provisions of the
part of this Act relating to the employment of children and
young
persons.
That paragraph has gone.
The Committee has just consented
tacitly to the repeal of paragraph (a) of
Sub-section (1). Paragraph (b) says
"Provided there is reasonable excuse for
the non-attendance of the child." The
second Sub-section says: Any of the following reasons shall be a reasonable excuse for the purposes of this Section;
that the child has been prevented from
attending school by sickness or any unavoidable cause; that the child is receiving efficient elementary education in some
other manner; that there is not in the
case of the child a school within the prescribed distance.
It is now proposed to add to that after
the words "any of the following reasons"
the words "and no other reason."
The
effect of that would be to deprive the
parent alleged to be in default of any
excuse for non-attendance other than
those three set out in Sub-section (2) of
Section 30 of the Act. I think that is
going too far. I think it is forgetting
particularly as regards country districts
that education and Education Acts exist
for the child, and the child does not exist
in order to be educated up to the age of
fourteen. There are cases, and there
always will be cases, where it is reasonable
and proper that the child should not
attend school. I do not think that ought
to be modified. I do not say that we
should be unwise to make an alteration in
this respect here.
The
The noble Viscount, speaking earlier,
quoted the English Act of 1870. I confess
when I heard that I hoped the noble
Viscount had read exactly what that Act
stated about school attendance. If he will
refer to that Act again he will find that
the Londonderry Act was based in that
respect on the wording used there. "And
no other reason' was not inserted in the
English modern Act of 1921, to which a
good deal of reference was made when the
other Bill was in Parliament.
Londonderry Act proceeded on rather
different lines from the last English Act.
The English Act introduced the expression
"after due warning to the parent," which
we adopted. We are now proposing
to discard that. The English Act requires
if the parent of any child habitually without reasonable excuse neglects to provide
education and so on. It does not quite
go so far as the proposed alteration would
take us.
The Act goes on again—“Where
a school attendance order is not complied
with without any reasonable excuse the
court of summary iurisdiction, and SO
on, and it does not limit the discretion of
the court as regards reasonable excuse.
But we have limited the discretion very
much, and I think quite rightly. I am
not complaining, but I think we ought not
to go quite so far as to say "and no other
reason."
""
We ought not to assume omniscience on
education matters. If we do, one might
reasonably ask had we omniscience in 1923
when
we adopted four-and-two committees, and if we had how does it square
with the present Bill. It is always a
mistake to legislate too far. Let me suggest that there may be nothing wrong with
the child. It has got half-a-dozen brothers
and sisters. Scarlatina is prevalent, and
the school is not closed. Can you expect
Education Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
any thoughtful parent to send his or her
child to school in such circumstances? I
beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think I can accept this Amendment. The
reasons for excusing children from attendance at school are fully contained in Subsection (2) of Section 30 of the principal
Act, and it was never intended that it
should be in the power of the education
authority or the magistrates to accept
excuses for non-attendance which do not
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Bangor, The Viscount
Charlemont, The Viscount
Crawford, Colonel The Right Honourable
R. G. Sharmanfall under one or other of the heads mentioned in the Section. To leave any
further discretion to the courts would
destroy the efficiency of the system of compulsory attendance, which is difficult
enough to administer as it is, and seriously
injure regularity of attendance at school.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry, but this is
a case in which I am unable to withdraw
the Amendment.
