CLAUSE 22 (Power of Ministry to
provide schools for feeble-minded children).
(1) The Ministry shall have power, if
it thinks fit, to establish, maintain and
manage a school or schools for educating,
boarding and lodging feeble-minded
children for whom suitable provision is
not otherwise made, and to appoint
officers and do all such things as may
be
necessary for the said purposes.
辎
Colonel PAKENHAM: I beg to move
in page 18, line 18, after "feeble-minded"
("lodging feeble-minded") to insert "and
blind."
The subject matter of my Amendment is
the children who are blind, and I feel that
they are worthy of preferential treatment.
Section 54 of the 1923 Act lays down that
blind children are "children who are too
blind to be able to read the ordinary books
}
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Dorman, Mr. R.
McAllister, Mr. T. S.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Quigley, Mr. Andrew
used by the children." Section 57 of the
original Act also gives power to the
Government to treat children who come
under the Section dealing with afflicted
children, that is Section 54, in homes or
schools. It is, of course, quite evident
that blind children, who are in a great
many ways perfectly normal in all their
functions except that of sight, cannot be
taught in the ordinary elementary schools
because there have to be special instruments for teaching them. Children who
might otherwise become very useful
citizens if they were specially taught have
thus no power of attaining to that dignity.
Clause 22 in the Amending Bill is really
a permissive Clause. The Ministry may,
if they think fit, establish schools for the
teaching of feeble-minded children. Blind
children should also be included, as they
are entitled to preferential treatment, and
also because in this Province of ours there
are not a very great number of them. They
can easily be accommodated in a school
established for the treatment of feebleminded children. I think the young blind
of this country are long due some special
legislation, and therefore I move this
Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret I
cannot accept this Amendment, because in
this particular Clause we are taking powers
for one particular school. The duty of
making provision for the education of
blind children rests with the Education
Authorities, and there are voluntary institutions for the purpose in Northern Ireland which are at present doing the work
Education Bill-
Committee
30 MAY 1930
with great efficiency. We are really taking
this power because no schools for mentallydeficient children exist in the Province,
and it is impracticable in present conditions for the education authorities to provide them. But this does not apply to
schools for the blind, and I do not wish for
that reason to include them in the Clause.
This does not, of course, imply any want
of sympathy with the blind on the Government's part. Perhaps if the hon. and
gallant Senator who moved this Amendment would ask me a question about what
has been done in this connection, how
many are being treated, in what way, and
so forth, on some future occasion, I might
be able to give him a more satisfactory
reply than I can now.
Mr. LESLIE: Is it not really a question of whether or not sufficient educational provision is made for the afflicted
children as a class, the class to which Part
IX of the 1923 Act applied, which included blind, deaf, dumb, feeble-minded,
and few
a
others? I have always
the

difficult

Colonel PAKENHAM: I withdraw the

CLAUSE 23 (Notification of mentally
defective children).
It shall be the duty of an education
authority, as respects their education
area, to notify to the Ministry of Home
Affairs the names of afflicted children
(as defined for the purposes of Part IX
of the principal Act) who, on attaining
the age of sixteen years, are about to
be withdrawn from a special class or
school for mentally defective children,
and in whose case the education authority are of opinion that it would be to
their benefit that they should be sent
to an institution suitable for such cases
or placed under guardianship.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
19, line 18, to leave out "or placed under
guardianship."
I put this Amendment on the Paper
principally because I did not understand
what the intention was in putting those
words in. Perhaps the noble Viscount
will be able to tell us. As far as I know
we have no power to put those children
under any kind of guardianship. We
have here got afflicted children about to
be withdrawn from the special class of
mentally defective children, if the education authority are of opinion that it would
be to their benefit that they should be sent
to an institution suitable for such cases
What
or placed under guardianship.
authority is there to give effect to the
opinion of the education authority? If
there is no authority the words in the
text are simply waste, and ought to be
taken out, and if there is authority and
something effective could be done I am
sure the proposal is one that would commend itself to all.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Clause
is designed to secure that mentally defective children who reach the age of 16, at
which age they are no longer the care of
the education authority, should not be
lost sight of by those who administer the
social services, and it simply enacts that
the authority is to send forward the names
of such children when they reach 16 to
This
the Ministry of Home Affairs.
course is to be taken by the authority in
the case of any young person who they
consider ought to be kept in an institution
or placed under guardianship. To omit
the last words would remove children who
require the supervision of a guardian
from the scope of the authority's action.
I think that children who are so mentally
deficient as to require the care of a guardian should not be discharged from a
special school and left to look after themselves. It would be much better that
such children should be notified to the
Ministry of Home Affairs, so that their
circumstances may be inquired into, even
if no further action were taken in regard
to them. I think it advisable not to omit
the words referred to, and I, therefore,
refuse to accept the Amendment.

Railway and Canal Traffic (Amendment) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: The brief Measure,
to the Second Reading of which I now seek
the approval of the House, has for its
object the amendment of Section 45 of the
Railway and Canal Traffic Act, 1888.
That Section lays down the procedure to
be adopted on the abandonment of a
canal. So far as the provisions of Section
45 relate to derelict canals, as distinct
from unnecessary canals, as defined
in the Act, the Government do not
propose any amendment of the existing
law. Since the passing of the Act, forty
years ago, there is not on record, in connection with the abandonment of an
unnecessary canal, any case where the
settlement of compensation as between the
Traffic (Amendment) Bul
30 MAY 1930
Adjournment
E
ད་ཟ
canal company and persons entitled to
compensation by reason of the proposed
abandonment gave rise to difficulties of
interpretation of the Statute. Such a case
has, however, now arisen in Northern
Ireland.
I should perhaps mention that under the
1888 Act two stages were necessary in
acceding to an application for the
abandonment of a canal, viz.-(a) A Warrant of Abandonment to be granted after
the compensation has been paid; and (b)
an Order of Release from all statutory and
other obligations in respect of the canal.
The second step comes, obviously, at a date
much later than the first.
Under Clause 1 (a) the procedure as to
the issue of the Warrant and Order of
Release will still apply, but the Bill now
before the House makes provision enabling
the payment of compensation to be deferred until after the Warrant of Abandonment has been granted. Under the
provisions of the Bill it will be sufficient
for security for compensation to be given.
Hon. Members will also observe that
under Clause 1 (b) (ii) provision is made
for the disposal of lands which may be in
the hands of the canal company at the
time the Warrant of Abandonment is
issued. It is not desirable that any such
lands should be abandoned and become in
effect "no man's land."
As to Clause 3, the Railway and Canal
Traffic Act, 1888, did not make any definite provisions as to how compensation
was to be determined, and the Bill
remedies this defect by laying down procedure involving the appointment of an
independent arbitrator to undertake the
task.
There is one other provision to which I
might draw attention, and that is that
where adjoining landowners or
or others
desire to acquire portions of the canal
lands, it is eminently desirable that such
lands should be capable of transfer with
the minimum expense and trouble to all
involved. In this behalf the Bill provides
for the Award and Order of Release to
operate as Vesting Orders, a short and
expeditious method of arranging for the
transfer of ownership in such cases, and,
under the provisions of the Bill, such
Vesting Orders will not be liable to Stamp
Duty.
Mr. LESLIE: There seems to be only
one question to ask in regard to this Bill,
and that is, can the noble Viscount inform
us when the principal Act came into operation? It is one of those things remarkably hard to find. Otherwise I think the
Bill is a good one.
Viscount BANGOR: I am afraid I am
not in a position to give the information
the hon. Member desires, but I will take
an early opportunity of obtaining the information, and I will give it to him.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Adjournment.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,
"That this House do now adjourn until
Monday next."
Mr. LESLIE: I do not understand
why we have to meet again on Monday.
We have been sitting here to-day, Friday,
rather at considerable inconvenience, and
now we are to have a sitting on Monday.
One ought to have some little time left
for one's domestic and private affairs. It
is very dislocating, indeed, to depart from
our established practice of sitting on Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday. We had
no sitting yesterday. If we sit on Monday
there will be the Report stage of the
Companies Bill, the Amendments to which
are not yet printed. There will be the
Report stage on the Education Bill, the
Amendments to which are not yet even
drafted. It is going too fast. I appeal
to the noble Viscount to allow us until
Tuesday.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret to
say that we shall have to sit on Monday
to get on with the business.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Ten
minutes after Six o'clock until
Monday next.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Monday, 2nd June, 1930.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.
LOANS TO FARMERS.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House (a) how many applications have been received for loans under
Section 2 of the Exchequer and Financial
Provisions Act, Northern Ireland, 1929;
(b) how many applications have been
granted; (c) what is the total amount of
capital applied for; (d) what is the total
amount which has been advanced.
Viscount BANGOR: In the absence of
the Leader of the House I will reply to
this Question. 534 applications have been
received for loans under Section 2 of the
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Act
(Northern Ireland), 1929, as amended by
Section 2 of the Exchequer and Financial
Provisions Act, 1930. Out of this number
272 applications have been granted. The
total amount of capital applied for is
£21.295, and the amount advanced to date
totals £7,441. To enlarge a little on this
reply, there are certainly very few of those
applications that have been withdrawn.
I cannot give the numbers, but 97 applications, amounting to £6,500, are still under
consideration. I think that is all the information I can give.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like to
ask if the Government are satisfied that
there would not be more applications if
there was a little less red tape about the
matter. I understand from my farmer
friends that they have to find so many
guarantors and so much security that they
cannot possibly get the loans. The procedure also is very complicated, and I
would suggest to the noble Viscount that
it would be worth while to find out if there
was anything in these allegations. We all
know the farmers of Northern Ireland are
very badly in need of money. They are
not rich and they want this money, and
they would like to get it under this scheme.
Mr. McALLISTER: I should like to
ask the noble Viscount if it is a fact that
owing to the very humiliating regulations
made under this Act the farmers do not
apply for these loans. Is it a fact that
before a farmer can secure a loan he has to
pay the costs of the application, and that
those costs are kept from him whether the
loan is granted or not. I am informed
that the costs vary from £5 to £20, and in
view of the answer of the noble Lord that
practically fifty per cent. of the applications have been turned down, a great deal
of money must have been sacrificed by the
farmers. The most humiliating and degrading regulation of all is that before a
farmer can secure a loan he has to have his
application published in the "Belfast
Gazette" and the local newspapers, and
any local resident who may have a griev
ance against him is given an opportunity
of coming in and objecting to his application being granted. Is it not a fact that
it is a very humiliating and degrading
thing that a farmer should have to publish
his application for a loan in the local
newspapers, having regard to the fact that
his application is investigated by the
Ministry.
Mr. SPEAKER: I think the hon.
Member should give notice of that question. The Deputy-Leader of the House
cannot be expected to reply to such questions if he does not receive notice of them.
Perhaps the hon. Member would put down
a question for another day.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I ask if it is
not a fact that the Act referred to limits
the grants that may be made by the
Ministry of Finance to £250,000. He has
informed us that up to the present the
total amount of loans granted amounts to
£21,000. That is not ten per cent. of the
amount sanctioned.
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. Member may
only ask a question.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The question I wish
to ask is, has there been any other case of
the complete breakdown of an Act such
as this case?
Viscount BANGOR: I seem to have
been asked a number of questions. As far
as the machinery for obtaining these loans
is concerned, I am afraid the Ministry of
Finance are not likely to relax the conditions. However, I will make enquiries
from the Ministry with a view to ascertaining whether the regulations can be
relaxed.
Companies Bill-
Report of Amendments-
2 JUNE 1930
Companies Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Viscount BANGOR: When the Companies Bill was in Committee the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) raised two
questions which it may be convenient to
dispose of before we deal with the formal
Amendments on the Order Paper.
The first was with regard to the use of
the word "British" in Clause 29, page 25,
line 4, "In the case of a peer or a person
usually known by a British title different
from his surname." I am advised that
the word "British" is adequate to cover
the requirements of the case and covers
titles in Northern Ireland.
The second question arose on Clause 80,
page 81, line 36, when the hon. and
learned Member invited opinion as to the
interpretation to be put upon the word
"untruthfully." On examination of the
context, hon. Members will see that a
liquidator who cannot conclude a liquidation within one year after its commencement is placed under obligation to send to
the Registrar of Companies a statement
containing the prescribed particulars with
respect to the proceedings in and the position of the liquidation. This statement is
to be open to inspection by a creditor or
contributory of the company by himself
or by his agent. No other persons are entitled to inspect these documents or to
obtain copies or extracts therefrom, and,
in order to safeguard the documents from
inspection by anyone who is neither a
creditor nor a contributory of the company, the section provides in Sub-section
(3) that any person untruthfully stating
himself to be a creditor or contributory
and thus getting access to these documents
shall be guilty of contempt of court, and
shall on the application of the liquidator
be punishable accordingly.
The words "untruthfully stating" mean
stating what is in fact untrue irrespective
of intention or wilfulness. If a person
states himself to be a creditor of a company or to be liable to contribute to the
assets of a company in the event of its
being wound up, and it turns out that
in fact he is not such a creditor or under
that liability, he ought at any rate to be
liable to explain the circumstances, and be
liable to punishment if he does not
succeed. I beg to move, "That this
Report be now received."
Mr. LESLIE: I thank the noble
Viscount for looking into the two matters
to which he has alluded, and for his replies,
which are as I expected. The more important point was the second, and as the
question of the untruthfulness is entirely
for the Court, I do not think there will
be any difficulty in carrying the intention
of Parliament into effect.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 30 (Provisions with respect to
offers of shares or debentures for sale).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
30, page 26, line 27, at the end of the
Clause to insert as a new sub-section :-
(5) A company shall not issue a debenture in respect of any liability previously incurred by the company, unless
an offer of debentures of equal rank and
priority is at the same time made by the
company to all persons to whom the
company has any outstanding liability.
This Amendment relates to a matter to
which I was requested to draw attention
during the Committee stage. If hon.
Members will do me the honour of reading
what is on the Paper they will see that
the proposal is somewhat incomplete, because it is couched in absolutely general
terms. It is obvious that something will
have to be added in the way of limitation,
such, perhaps, as the words, "exceeding
£100," or some other saving in respect of
liabilities which it is not intended imme-.
diately to discharge. I deliberately left
those matters over, thinking that if it was
decided to insert any words for this purpose at all it would be better to add the
limitation afterwards.
Passing to the main purpose of the proposed Amendment, it has been pointed
out that to some extent the practice
prevails of obtaining a debenture in satisfaction of a debt under circumstances
which, although not fraudulent in the sense
of the Elizabethan Statutes, which were
applied to Ireland in the time of Charles I,
and not caught by the bankruptcy
laws as last amended, were yet rather unfair to the other creditors. The suggestion is made that one creditor to whom,
let us say. £200 is owing, goes to the company and asks for a debenture.
company may issue such a debenture and
thereby the active creditor has an advantage over the other creditors. That is
not quite fair, but there are, as the House
will readily see, numerous limitations. A
The
Companies Bill-
Report of Amendments
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
company cannot issue debentures unless it
has power to do so, and so on, and it may
perhaps be thought that the whole matter
belongs rather to the bankruptcy code
than to the company code. I have put
down this Amendment with the object of
bringing up the matter now, as we are
dealing with the Amendment of the company law. We have already dealt with
the Amendment of the bankruptcy law,
and we have rather lost our chance of doing
anything more in the way of bankruptcy
before the Consolidating Bill comes along,
but we have not lost our chance of dealing
with the company law before we consolidate that law.
It does seem that there is a gap left in
the Amendments we made, chiefly, I
think, to Section 53 of the Bankruptcy
Act of 1872, which seems to lead to the
practice to which I have alluded. I understand that the practice is rather on the
increase, and is one that has given some
cause for concern. Therefore, in order
that the matter may have public attention
directed towards it, I beg to move the
Amendment standing in my name, reserving the right to ask the House to add
a word or two if the House agree to the
principle.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I rise
to support the Amendment, and the reason
I do so is that under the companies law
there is a practice of creating debentures.
That has to be done at a meeting of shareholders. The debentures are held over,
and then later on the directors by resolution issue these debentures to some
creditor. The practice as we know has
been that in many cases where there are
say ten creditors one of them gets rushed
in some way or another and gets a debenture issued to him. The meaning of
that is that he has to be paid in full before
the other creditors get anything. One
creditor really gets a preference over the
other creditors who were in equally with
him.
I would hope that we could make the
law and equity agree more than they do
at the present time. I think if people
lend money to a company they should be
all treated alike, or they should have an
opportunity to object. They have no such
opportunity when a debenture is issued.
The companies law is being looked after
now, and what I would hope to see would
be that with regard to the old debts all the
creditors of the company will be treated
alike and no preference given. If a company gets into a tight place, and if it
wants to get money say, £10,000, that
company would have security to give.
That security for value received would be
a debenture. It would be no harm to any
of the former creditors, because the money
-20/- to the £-would go into the company. They would all benefit, and therefore it would be of advantage to the
company. I think the law should be
changed, so that instead of by a mere resolution of the directors a debenture would
be given to one person or one creditor,
they should all be on the same rank and
treated alike. It would enable them for
value received-downright money received
--to give a new security for the advanage
of the company. I would support the
Amendment.
Viscount BANGOR: I gather that this
Amendment is intended to meet the case
of a company which, being strongly
pressed by a creditor, may yield to the
creditor's importunity and give a debenture charged upon the company's
undertaking and property, and thereby
place that creditor in a secured and
favourable position by contrast with the
other creditors who were not so pressing.
On becoming a debenture holder the
creditor may proceed to take steps to
realise his security by appointing a
receiver or forcing the company into
liquidation without any consideration for
the rights of the other creditors or the
interests of the shareholders and the employees of the company.
It would be a serious matter if there
were no provision in the law to meet cases
of preferential treatment of creditors by
insolvent companies, but there is already
such provision in Section 212 of the
Companies (Consolidation) Act, 1908.
which reads as follows:-
---
Where a company is being wound up,
a floating charge on the undertaking or
property of the company created within
three months of the commencement of
the winding up shall, unless it is proved
that the company immediately after the
creation of the charge was solvent, be invalid, except to the amount of any cash
paid to the company at the time of or
subsequently to the creation of, and in
consideration for, the charge, together
with interest on that amount at the rate
of five per cent. per annum.
The period of three months named in
this section is being extended to six months
by Clause 45 of the Bill.
333 Companies Bill---

2 JUNE 1930
Report of Amendments-
Thus any creditor who may feel
aggrieved by the issuing of debentures.
to a particular creditor-and he would
have knowledge of the debentures being
issued through the ordinary trade publications may take action to force the
company into liquidation, and, on this
being done, the debentures would be invalid except so far as they represented
new cash paid to the company.
So much for insolvent companies, but
the hon. and learned Member's Amendment is not confined to insolvent companies, and I must assume that it is his
wish to apply it also to solvent companies.
I cannot agree, however, that it is necessary or expedient for the State to require
a solvent company to offer debentures to
all its creditors if it should desire or be
constrained to offer debentures to one of
them-such creditor being as a rule the
A solvent company
company's banker.
must be at liberty to finance its business
in its own way.
I entertain no doubt that the position
of the average company, faced with temporary financial difficulties, would be made
much worse were the Amendment incorporated in the Bill. The average creditor
does not want debentures from his debtor,
he wants cash. A general offer of debentures would create suspicion and alarm in
the minds of the creditors, and I am afraid
that a company making such an offer
would find both its credit and reputation
gone. Accordingly, while I appreciate the
motives which have influenced the hon.
and learned Member in putting forward
this Amendment, I am unable to accept
it.
Mr. LESLIE: I think that the House
will agree that the matter has been ventilated. The noble Viscount put it very
clearly, and dealt first with an insolvent
company, and second with a solvent company. But the difficulty, as I understand
it, arises in a company which does not
really know whether it is solvent or insolvent, and the creditors are in an equally
doubtful position. As I say, the matter
has been ventilated, and it certainly would
not be desirable to press the matter
further. I beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page 72,
line 13, to leave out "twelve" (last word
but one of Clause 73), and insert "six."
I have two further Amendments on page
74, line 38. to leave out "twelve" and
insert "six," and on page 74, line 42, to
leave
""
out
twelve
and insert
"six." I think these Amendments
might be taken together. They all relate
to exactly the same thing. The difficulty
was that we were allowing in the Bill a
sentence up to twelve months to be passed
by a court of summary jurisdiction. That
is rather beyond anything we do. It is
usual for matters of that kind to go before
a jury. In the corresponding English Act
there is a definite right of appeal
jury. There is nothing in
Northern Ireland law to secure
to a
the
that.
In England the right accrues
as to a possible maximum sentence of three
months. But there is plenty of precedent
in later times for passing a sentence of
six months. I think, therefore, if the
House agrees to change the twelve months
into six months we shall have brought the
practice reasonably into line with the
English practice. I beg to move.
Mr. SPEAKER: We will take the
three Amendments together.
Viscount BANGOR: The Government
will accept these Amendments.
Amendments agreed to.
Mr. LESLIE: My remaining Amendment deals with a slightly different case.
It is page 97, line 9, after "offence"
("second or subsequent offence") to insert
"shall be liable on conviction or indictment." We have provided for a sentence
of six months on summary conviction for
a first offence. As the Bill stands there is
a liability to twelve months, also on summary conviction, for a second offence.
When speaking in Committee I said I was
not at all sure, having regard to recent
practice in England under the Act of 1914
that we need trouble very much about the
twelve months on the second offence. But
when I began to think over it it was borne
to my mind that twelve months is
twelve months whether it is a first or
second offence, and, therefore, I put the
Amendment on the Paper. Further consideration brings further difficulties. Once
a man is tried for a first offence summarily
and is tried for a second offence by indictment it is obvious the jury in the second
case will know the man was convicted
before, which they ought not to know.
think, if the House agrees, we had
better leave the matter as it is. I do not
think any harm would be done. Therefore I do not move, and if the noble Lord
agrees no more need be said.
Amendment not moved.

Traffic (Amendment) Bill 336
Companies Bill-
THE SENATE
Mr. SPEAKER: There is another
Amendment by Mr. Campbell.
Mr. CAMPBELL: If I am in order I
beg to move an Amendment which, I
regret to say, is not in the Orders of the
Day. It was only sent in very recently.
It is on page 102, line 24, at the end of
Clause 99 to insert as a new Clause:
Section 84 of the principal Act (which
relates to the liability of directors, promotors and others to pay compensation
to subscribers for shares or debentures
for loss or damage sustained by untrue
statements in prospectuses or reports)
shall be amended by adding to the
section the words: "Compensation shall
be determined by the difference between
the price paid by the person who has
subscribed for any shares or debentures,
with reasonable interest thereon, and
their fair market price at the time he
claims compensation, unless such directors, promoters, or other persons prove
that the difference was attributable to
causes arising after the issue of the prospectus for which they were not personally responsible."
I put down the Amendment in the hope
of establishing a definite criterion by which
the measure of compensation for loss
caused by untrue statements in the prospectuses of companies can be settled. As
the law stands at the present time the
measure of compensation is vague, and is
only ascertained with difficulty. I respectfully submit to the House that this
Amendment gives a simple standard, and
in this way will clarify matters and work
justice for the subscribing public.
Viscount BANGOR: With reference to
the new Clause proposed by the hon. and
learned Senator, I am advised that the
wording of Section 84 of the principal Act
is adequate to cover the point raised by
the hon. and learned Member. That
section provides that in the cases contemplated by this Amendment-
"every promoter of the company and
every person who has authorised the
issue of the prospectus shall be liable to
pay compensation to all persons who
subscribe for any shares or debentures
on the faith of the prospectus for the
loss or damage they may have sustained
by reason of any untrue statement
therein,"
and so on.
I
It will be observed, therefore, that
under existing law the court has ample
powers to deal with the question of loss or
damage sustained and to measure the compensation due. In other words, it is left
to the court. In these circumstances I am
not prepared to accept the Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: The object of the
Amendment was to give some standard or
test by which the matter might be more
easily understood. I think the standard
is not clear at the present time, but in consequence of the attitude of the Government
I beg leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Viscount BANGOR: Before proceeding

raised on the Second Reading by the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie).
asked for some information as to the date

I find that while the Act was placed on the

until the 1st January, 1889.
Mr. LESLIE: I wish to thank the

presume that it is clear that there was
no difficulty as regards the date of the 1st

has considered all that.

Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: I do not propose
to proceed with the Second Reading of this
Bill to-day. Certain representations have
been made, and there has not been sufficient time to consider them. Therefore,
the Second Reading will not be taken
until to-morrow.
Finance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: As hon. Members
will realise, this is a Money Bill, and,
therefore, is not subject to alteration or
amendment. At the same time, perhaps,
I might be allowed in moving the Second
Reading to make certain matters clear.
A certain amount of misunderstanding
appears to exist outside this House, and
I may, therefore, perhaps forestall criticism in this House itself in regard to the
nature of the evidence sought by the
Inter-Departmental Committee on Road
Fund Finance, on whose recommendations
the changes now proposed to be made in
the motor taxation scales are based.
will be seen in paragraph 59 of their report, they stated that they endeavoured
to obtain the views of the Ulster Motor
Trade Association and the Ulster Motor
Coach Owners' Association regarding the
most suitable method of altering the scales
with a view to obtaining an increased
yield.
As
The reasons why these two associations
were consulted are obvious. Firstly, they
were the bodies who six months previously
had sent a deputation to the Minister of
Finance asking for equality with Great
Britain in motor taxation. Secondly, the
Committee had come to the conclusion that
the class of vehicle which would have to
bear the increased taxation was that doing
the most damage to the roads, that is to
sav. the motor bus and the commercial
vehicle. The two associations which I have
mentioned represented these two interests.
car,
Complaints have been made that the
Royal Automobile Club or the Automobile
Association were not consulted. I cannot
see why this was necessary. Had there
been any suggestion to alter the rates of
taxation for the private motor
obviously these two bodies would have been
reasonable media for obtaining the views
of the motoring public in regard to the
most convenient method, from the public's
point of view, of amending the scales. As
no change was contemplated consultation.
was obviously unnecessary.
I am informed that in the concluding
stages of their deliberations the Committee,
finding that insufficient money was available from the increased scales which they
had drawn up for hackney and commercial
vehicles, decided that an increase must
take place in the drivers' licence fees. To
Finance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Viscount Bangor]
consult any particular association on this
point would obviously have been an
unnecessary procedure, as there must have
been no mean between a suggestion to leave
the driving licence fee at its present figure
or increase it to the figure proposed by
the Committee. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I want some information
about the driver's licence. As I understand the report of the statement made by
the Minister in another place I am not
sure that I am speaking correctly or that
he was not incorrectly reported but I
understood the statement to be that the
driver of a public service vehicle would
not have to pay the additional 5/-. At
present a driver's licence costs 5/-. It is
proposed from the 1st January to charge
him 10/-, but the statement appears to
have been that the driver of a public
service vehicle would not have to pay the
extra 5/-.
As the law stands at the present moment
a man who wants to drive a public service
vehicle, commonly called an omnibus, has
first of all to take out a licence to drive a
motor car, costing 5/-, plus another licence
to drive an omnibus, costing him another
5/-, total 10/-. The question at issue in
so far as it is in issue seems to be whether
that man has to pay 10/- and 5/-, or stand
at the original 5/- and 5/-. I do not
know that the effect on the revenue would
be very great. At all events it is a question that most of us would be asked when
we go home, and we would like to have an
answer.
As regards the other parts of the Bill, I
do not think the ordinary person who
takes an interest in roads partly as a user
and partly as one who pays for keeping
them up can object to the heavier burden
being put on the heavier class of vehicle
which does the heaviest amount of damage
to the road.
I do not think there will be any serious
opposition to the proposal with regard to
the rates of estate duty. I spoke on this
matter in this House two or three years
ago, and I have seen no reason to alter
things. As regards our own relations with
Great Britain, we are, I am afraid, almost
necessarily bound to follow their example.
in this matter. The transfer from the
Rating Relief Suspense Account was, I
think, expected. The only question I have
asked is regarding the omnibus driver's
licence.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I am sorry that
it would not be in order to propose an
Amendment in connection with this Bill,
which is a money matter, but I would like
to offer a protest against the increase on
the farmers' lorries from £16 to £24. In
the first place the roads for the use of
motors were made so slippery that they
could not get their horses to go on them,
and in consequence they had to buy these
lorries. In addition to their horses being
put off, taxation is increased from £16 to
£24 on the lorry that weighs from 1 ton to
30 cwt. I think it is a most niggardly
proposal. I do not object to the licence
being increased from 5/- to 10/-. That is
a small matter, but for farmers in their
present condition to be asked to pay 50 per
cent. more on the lorries means that they
will take them off the roads. Again, when
the Government do get hold of this money
they spend it in whatever they like without
consulting anyone. Why make tracks for
motor races and things that are not for the
advantage of road users at all? I protest
most strongly against this. I suppose it
would not be in order to move an Amendment.
Viscount BANGOR: In reply to the
question put by the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie), about the licence, I
may say that the driver of the public
service vehicle will get a special driving
licence at 5/-, and then he will get a second
licence for 5/- for driving a public service
vehicle.
Mr. LESLIE: It does not say so.
Viscount BANGOR: That is what I am
advised. As regards estate duties, the
hon. and learned Senator is quite right.
The Government have considered that it is
essential that we should keep in line with
what the estate duties are on the other side.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Would you not
recommend a reduction in the duty on
motor lorries?
Viscount BANGOR: I am afraid I am
not in a position to do that.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bills Presented.

The Annual Report on
