year
Industrial
ended 31st
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
DISTRIBUTION OF
BOGUS FINANCIAL NEWSPAPERS.
Major DOBBS: I beg to ask the Leader
of the House what steps, if any, the
Government are taking to prevent the distribution of bogus financial newspapers in
Northern Ireland.
I do not
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
think that the Government can usefully
move in the matter, because any success
the bogus papers have depends upon the
unfortunate fact that there are a great
many people of deficient intelligence in
the world, which is a thing no Government
can prevent, and while there are such
people there will adways be others to prey
upon them. I understand that the reputable financial journals regularly black list
the bogus newspapers in their
columns. I think that is the best safeguard for the investing public.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Own
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The main
purpose of this Bill is to extend to
Northern Ireland, as part of the scheme
of National Health Insurance, the benefit
known as medical benefit; that is, free
medical treatment with the necessary
medicines for insured persons who are ill.
Although this benefit was part-indeed, I
may say the principal part of the original
scheme of National Health Insurance, it
will be within the recollection of hon.
Members that during the passage of the
Bill through the Imperial House, in 1911,
the question arose as to the exclusion of
Ireland from the provisions of the Bill
regarding medical benefit, and in the Act,
as finally passed, Ireland was excluded
from those provisions.
So far as Northern Ireland is concerned,
this Bill is designed to bring the scheme of
National Health Insurance in this area into
line with that obtaining in Great Britain,
both as regards the rate of contribution
payable under the scheme and the benefits
provided for insured persons. I may say
that the principal reason actuating the
Government in introducing this Measure
is the fact that the amount of sickness
among insured persons and the general
death rate in Northern Ireland are both
much higher than the corresponding rates
in Great Britain.
It may be contended that the cash benefit paid under the National Health Insurance scheme should enable insured persons
who are ill to provide their own medical
treatment. No doubt in many cases it is
a great help, but the result of an investigation into this question showed that of
a number of insured persons in receipt of
sickness benefit approximately 26 per cent.
-or more than one-quarter-were receiving no medical treatment whatever.
may be assumed that in these cases the
cash benefit was required to provide the
mere necessaries of life.
It
Apart from those insured persons who
were receiving no medical treatment, it is
highly probable that many others, through
inability to meet the cost of medical treatment, refrained in the earlier stages of
their illness from seeking medical advice,
and did so only when the illness had increased in severity. In rendering medical
treatment immediately available to insured persons so soon as they become ill,
this Bill will do much to reduce the high
rate of sickness at present obtaining in
Northern Ireland, which, in its turn,
should reduce the death rate.
No doubt it may be contended by some
that the dispensary medical system provided under the Poor Relief Acts is in
itself a sufficient provision for those who,
when illness comes, are unable to meet
the cost of medical treatment. The fact
remains, however, that so long as a medicai
service is administered as part of the poorlaw system there are many who would
345 Health Insurance Bill-

3 JUNE 1930
Second Reading
prefer to remain without medical attention
altogether than have recourse to a form
of what they would regard as poor relief.
The fact that I have mentioned, namely
-that more than one-quarter of certain
insured persons in receipt of sickness benefit were receiving no treatment whatever,
confirms this. Under the scheme of
medical benefit proposed by this Bill the
insured person himself becomes a contributor to the cost of any medical treatment
or medicine that he may receive, and thus
there can be no suggestion of the pauper
taint. There will be free choice by the
insured person, so far as this is possible,
both of doctor and chemist.
I am glad to be able to say that the
arrangements made after negotiations with
representatives of dispensary medical
officers are such that overlapping between
the two services will be avoided, and the
Bill before the House gives effect to an
agreement with the dispensary medical
officers to ensure that they will not receive
double remuneration for attending the
same persons.
Amongst other reasons for the support
of the Bill, I may say that the passage
of
this Measure into law will relieve the
Government of a very embarrassing situation. A Convention of Sickness Insurance was drawn up at Geneva in 1927,
which required the countries which ratified
it to provide medical benefit for workers
on an insurance basis. This Convention
has already been ratified, or is in process
of ratification, by 15 foreign countries,
but Great Britain has been unable to do
so owing to the absence of medical benefit
in Northern Ireland. Successive British
Governments have exerted pressure upon
the Government here to introduce medical
benefit in order that the Imperial Government may be able to ratify this convention
as, for the purposes of the convention, the
United Kingdom, including Northern Ireland, is treated as a whole. Hon. Members will readily appreciate the anxiety of
the Government to give effect to the
Imperial Government's wishes.
I should like to refer again to the heavy
sickness experience in Northern Ireland,
looking at it from the financial point of
view. In National Health Insurance
matters Northern Ireland does not stand
alone, but our insured people are linked
with those in Great Britain in one common
insurance scheme, and but for this it would
not be possible to maintain the present
rate of expenditure in Northern Ireland
without an increase in the existing contribution. The comparatively heavy expenditure in Northern Ireland has given rise
to anxiety in Great Britain, and the
Government here has been pressed to introduce medical benefit in the hope that
sickness may be reduced.
As regards the administration of the
scheme, the Ministry of Labour will be
primarily responsible, but will be assisted
by the Medical Benefit Council representative of all parties concerned in the
scheme, and, in addition, certain local
committees will be set up to deal with
matters of a purely local interest.
As a
consequence of the introduction of the
scheme, the insurance committees which
at present exist in each county and county
borough will disappear, and the Bill provides for compensation for any officials
who may be displaced. Hitherto these
committees have been responsible for the
administration of sanatorium benefit, that
is, the treatment of tuberculosis, which
forms one of the benefits of the Health
Insurance Scheme. Such treatment of
tuberculosis as was given in the patient's
own home will be continued as part of
medical benefit, and there is provision
in the Bill to enable local authorities to
undertake treatment of insured persons
in institutions without financial loss.
Provision is made in the Bill for a sick
visiting scheme which will be common to
all approved societies operating in Northern Ireland. It is hoped that societies
themselves will undertake the formation
and working of such a scheme, but should
that not eventuate, a scheme may be put
into operation by the Ministry of Labour
in order to ensure equality of administration among these societies.
With regard to the Bill itself it will
become part of the general code relating
to National Health Insurance for the
United Kingdom, and for this reason the
Bill follows as closely as possible the
British statutes, applying, with some
necessary modifications, the provisions of
those statutes to Northern Ireland.
We have the pleasure in this House
to-day of having my right hon. Friend the
Minister of Labour with us, and he will
reply to the debate on this Measure.
beg to move.
I
Mr. LONG: I am charitable enough to
believe that this Bill is prompted by expediency and not by conviction. Geneva
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
and Westminster and the approved
societies may be satisfied-they probably
will-but there is profound dissatisfaction
in the rural areas at the proposal. There
it is considered that the Measure is uncalled for. There is certainly no demand
for it. It is, I think, the most unpopular
Measure that this Government has ever
introduced.
It was my intention to move the restriction of the Measure to the industrial
centres, omitting the rural areas following
the course of unemployment insurance.
But on examination I discovered that
unemployment and medical benefits are
not quite analogous or parallel cases.
discrimination took place between the
rural workers and those in the cities and
towns, difficulties would be experienced in
operating the Measure. There is a considerable amount of migration from the
country to the town, and a little also from
the town to the country. Many workers
in the towns, for instance, milk distributors, are considered as agricultural labourers, and others as well.
I
Then again there is a considerable
amount of migration to Great Britain,
and here the position would be extremely
complicated, as it would be necessary to
provide stamps of different values in the
Post Offices in England and Scotland.
would like to think that it would be feasible and workable to separate the industrial areas from the rural districts, but I
am very much afraid that it would be
impracticable.
I should like to protest vigorously
against the Report-the considered opinion
of the majority of the Poor-Law Commission, of which I had the honour to be
a member, being flouted in this way. They
stated on page 123 of the Report-
The dispensary system in Northern
Ireland is much wider than in Great
Britain, in that it supplies free treatment to all persons unable to pay
ordinary medical fees, and accordingly
many persons belonging to a class for
whom no provision is made in Great
Britain, other than under the National
Insurance Acts, are able to receive free
treatment in Northern Ireland.
Again, we were told that save in industrial areas where there is a large
insured population, a system of medical
benefits would be of little avail, as in
rural areas the majority of persons needing treatment at reduced cost are uninsured or uninsurable. Any scheme to
replace the present dispensary system
must provide treatment for dependents,
and the cost of introducing such a
scheme under the National Health Insurance Act would be prohibitive.
Stress was therefore laid on the fact
that the introduction of medical benefits would not result in the abolition of
the dispensary system, and the financial obligations of the community would
be greatly increased in supporting two
separate schemes for medical attendance.
The advantage of allowing patients
free choice of doctor, which is secured
under the medical benefits scheme, was
put to us, but we are informed that the
introduction of medical benefits would
not fulfil this purpose in all parts of
Rural Northern Ireland, as the Dispensary Medical Officer is, in many cases,
the only doctor in the neighbourhood,
and that in these areas insured persons
and their employers would be paying
additional taxation without receiving
any additional benefit.
Later on the Report states:
At an early stage of our deliberations
a recommendation to introduce medical
benefit on the same lines as in Great
Britain was proposed and negatived by
a majority after full discussion.
At a
later date a modified proposal was put
forward to meet the objections of those
who felt that while there was a demand
for medical benefit in the industrial
areas, there was an equally strong
opposition from the rural areas. This
proposal was that medical benefit be
introduced for all classes of insured,
with the exception of the agricultural
labourer, as in the case of unemployment benefit. A recommendation to
this effect was discussed and was carried
by a majority of those present and
voting.
Medical benefits would introduce a
fresh charge both on the employer and
the employed, which, on the lowest
computation and allowing for all possible effects, would amount to a considerable sum, and we are not convinced
that the advantages to be gained would
be commensurate with the cost, especially as the dependents-the wives and
children of the insured--would not be
included, and would have to be otherwise provided for.
349 Health Insurance Bill-

3 JUNE 1930
Second Reading
The scheme is not for the dependents
of the insured, and it seems strange to
me that the insured persons, with a
singular lack of chivalry, should desire
and obtain more favoured treatment, and
are apparently content to allow the mother
of their children and their offspring to
have the pauper taint still attached. The
Minister of Labour seems to be satisfied
that women are getting their proper place
in the scheme of things, but it seems to
me more like Chinese legislation. I cannot imagine that it will make the Minister
of Labour more popular with the female
voters.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews) That does not matter.
over-
Mr. LONG: With regard to
lapping, which is dealt with on page 70
of the Report, I should like to read what
is stated in the Report:-
The machinery for providing extern
treatment outside of hospital is no
simpler. Except in County Boroughs,
three local authorities may provide and
supervise this form of treatment-
Boards of Guardians, District Councils,
and County Councils. Boards of Guardians provide Dispensary Medical
Relief, District Councils and County
Councils may provide treatment under
the Maternity and Child Welfare
Schemes, and County Councils may
provide treatment under the Tuberculosis Acts and the Venereal Diseases
Schemes. Further, County Councils
acting through their Education Committees must provide free medical inspection for all school children, and may
provide medical and dental treatment
free for those who cannot afford to pay
for it. It might therefore be the duty
of three or more doctors, appointed by
different authorities, to be in attendance
at the same house, or even on the same
patient, at the same time. It is also
possible that under existing arrangements a Medical Officer may be paid by
two different authorities for attending
the same patient. This occurs when a
Dispensary Medical Officer attends a
poor person suffering from Tuberculosis. Under the Medical Charities Act
he receives a salary for attending all
qualified poor persons in his district.
He is also paid for attending them
under the County Tuberculosis Scheme.
I can give you a local illustration of
that. I know of a family of a husband,
wife, and ten children, and they are being
attended by Dr. Bateman, the tuberculosis medical officer; Dr. Watson, the
dispensary medical officer, and the school
medical officer. One of the children is in
the infirmary, attended by the infirmary
staff; another is in the Eye and Ear
Hospital, being attended by the staff, and
when this Bill passes another doctor still
will be added to the list, for Dr. Keown
will be attending the insured person in the
house. I think that is very serious overlapping, and I should have liked to have
had it dealt with.
Another question I should like to ask
is why the Ministry of Labour are to administer this health service when the
Ministry of Home Affairs administers all
the others. It seems to me that extra cost
will thereby be entailed. Will the panel
doctors be allowed or required to stock
medicines or medical appliances? If they
are, where will they be stored. At present,
in connection with the board of guardians,
they have medicines in their custody, and
if under this Bill they are not allowed to
have medicines, but only to operate by
prescriptions, difficulties will arise.
instance, in the case of an accident, in
which, say, a man got kicked in the face
by a horse and an artery was burst, would
the panel doctor have to wait until he
sent for a prescription-for a needle, lint,
cat-gut, and a stimulant-to the chemist,
or would he be expected to have a supply
in stock. As I said, I am afraid that extra
cost will be entailed and confusion will
arise by having these two masters.
For
No doubt the dispensary doctors have
bargained very astutely. They have a
very powerful union, but I would like to
know if the successors of the present occupants of the office will be entitled to the
same terms. I protest against the slight
that this Measure implies on a body of
men whose devoted service has been of
priceless value to the community. It is
all very well to dissemble your love, but
why did you kick them downstairs, they
might very well say. Will the insured
persons be better treated under the
medical benefits scheme than they were
formerly I question very much, because,
although the Leader of the House has
quoted statistics to show the amount of
sickness in Northern Ireland, it must be
remembered that the young and robust
and enterprising persons are emigrating,
and they are leaving a residue not quite
so fit.
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
An acquaintance of mine, in whose
veracity I have the utmost confidence,
visited a panel doctor in Great Britain,
and the panel doctor allowed him to
accompany him to his consulting rooms
where he was to meet his patients. There
was a considerable crowd gathered, and it
seemed to the medical student that the
examination would occupy a considerable
time. But it only occupied a very short
time, and it was done in this way. "All
those who have coughs or colds stand up."
A number stood up. "You come this
way." He handed them prescription
No. 1. "All those who have pains in
their limbs, rheumatism, and that sort of
thing, stand up." They stood up and
were handed prescription No. 2. "All
those suffering from indigestion, constipation, a feeling of fullness after eating"
-I know it sounds like an advertisement
-“ please stand up." They were
handed a prescription marked "A.D.T."
The medical student was curious and
asked what " A.D.T." meant. The panel
doctor replied, "My reputation has been
largely built up on that. It is in great
demand. It is a comprehensive charge of
grape-shot. If I miss mass I hit cockfighting, and A.D.T.' is most effective."
There was a man who did not rise to
either of the questions. A number of
other questions were, of course, also asked.
It was found that he was suffering from
bull goring. Even "A.D.T." would not
meet the case of a man who had been
gored by a bull, so he had to be examined
individually.
I do not say that that is a fair example of the way in which panel doctors
in Great Britain treat their patients, but
it is a true instance. No doubt cases
could be quoted in which dispensary
doctors would be held up in the same light,
but I have no doubt, personally, that as
a whole panel patients are well treated,
just as I have no doubt that dispensary
patients are 'as a whole well treated.
There are exceptions, of course, in everything. My view is that instead of dealing
with this matter in a piece-meal sort of
way a comprehensive Measure dealing
with our public health should have been
undertaken at once. A lady acquaintance
of mine felt that she required a new hat.
She bought an expensive hat which was
considered to be necessary and very suitable. At a later period she purchased
another garment, and it was then found
that the hat and the other garment did
not harmonise, with the result that the
hat had to be scrapped. The moral is
plain. Surely we are not so bereft of statesmanship or constructive ability as to be
unable to devise a scheme of public health
which would obviate most of the overlapping, and remove some of the
anomalies which I have indicated.
sorry that for these reasons I cannot support the Second Reading of the Bill.
I am
Mr. LESLIE: I hope the House wil
accept my assurance that I do not at al.
enjoy the task which I am going to endeavour to discharge this afternoon.
li
I succeed in inducing the House to accept
my assurance I hope I shall be equally
successful with the right hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Labour, for the
right hon. Gentleman knows well that I
have had the pleasure in times past of
supporting some of his steps along the
path of social progress, on general grounds.
in conformity with Great Britain, even if.
in some cases, I may have been inclined to
think that the financial result of the steps
originated in Great Britain might not
lead to an altogether desirable destination.
I do not like my task. I should have
liked it less, I may say, had it not been
for a remark which I understand the right
hon. Gentleman to have made in another
place to the effect that this whole question
of health insurance was one which should
be taken out of the region of party politics.
Mr. ANDREWS: Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE: This is not in any sense
a party matter, and I am glad to think
that we should all approach it from that
standpoint, although it is a matter which
should never be lost sight of in this House
that the claims of mere party do not obtain
to the extent to which they necessarily
obtain in other and less favoured places.
I have definitely to move the omission of
the word "now" from the Motion of the
noble Viscount. If I understand correctly the hon. Senator, the Chairman of
the Londonderry County Council stopped
short and did not make a definite Motion.
I move what amounts to the rejection of
the Bill for numerous reasons, which I
must now as best I can bring before the
House.
First of all, I desire to point out that
there has not been proper time for Parliament or for this House or for the public
to appreciate the Measure which is laid
before us this afternoon. If my information is correct, the Bill was introduced
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
3 JUNE 1930
for the first time in another place on the
13th May. It was introduced in this
House last Friday. We are asked to give
it a Second Reading to-day on the 3rd
June. The Bill in itself is necessarily of
a somewhat complicated character. The
final copy of the Bill as it left another
place reached me some fifty miles from
here about ten o'clock yesterday morning.
The greater part of yesterday I spent in
travelling to and from and attending at
this House. I suggest that no Member
of this House who happens to reside in
the country, or for the matter of that who
resides in Belfast, can possibly understand
or appreciate this Bill, and take it in all
its bearings so as to discuss it intelligently
or form an intelligent opinion on it in
the time allotted for the purpose.
Passing from that, I object to the Bill,
because it imposes an extra burden alike
on the employer and the employee. I
understand trade at the moment is at a
low ebb. I know from personal experience that the prospects of agriculture are
gloomy in the extreme, and is this a time
to add even another penny to the burden
of the employer and another penny on
each human unit of his wages list. I do
not propose to say much more about the
employer, because the employer can very
easily protect himself. A charge on him
of a penny per unit can be met by the
simple process of dispensing with one or
more of his employees.
Mr. ANDREWS: No, no.
Mr. LESLIE: I think so. I think
if a man employs twenty men, and finds
the cost too great there is nothing to stop
him
Mr. GLENDINNING: He cannot do
that.
Mr. LESLIE: He can reduce his staff
by one. If he can do without the services
of the twentieth man he can
save the
wages of the twentieth man, and those
wages can go some way to pay the nineteen pennies per week of those who are
left. Another method, which is not to
entirely dispense with the twentieth man,
would be when there is a vacancy to wait
a few weeks before it is filled up. Both
these courses can be taken, and both these
courses entail something further in the
nature of unemployment, be it for a
longer or shorter period.
The
Now we pass to the employee.
hon. Senator (Mr. Long) was thinking
more particularly of the rural districts.
The man living in the rural district says
quite properly that he has enough to pay
already. He does not want to pay that
other penny per week at all. The wages
of many of these men do not exceed 24/-
a week. The penny that may have to be
found out of the family budget may be
a penny for the purchase of a weekly
newspaper, about the only luxury he has
got. Moreover, he like other people wants
to know what he is going to get in exchange for that penny, and to that point
I will refer later on.
Although I admit I have not had time
to fully understand the Bill, it is fairly
obvious, I think, that the powers which
the Ministry seeks to attract to itself are
too great. The Ministry makes itself the
court of appeal far too often for my
liking. I am strongly opposed to any
substitution of bureaucracy in any shape
or form for a complete method of settling
disputes before the courts of law. The
Bill proposes to abolish the insurance
committees. I did not gather from the
noble Viscount in exactly what respect he
thought the insurance committees had
failed in the discharge of those duties
which were cast on them some twenty
years ago, nor do I quite understand from
what he said why it was thought that the
substitution of the Ministry for the insurance committees would result in
either greater efficiency or greater
economy. Perhaps the right hon. Gentleman (Mr. Andrews) when he replies
would be able to enlighten us further in
that respect.
Again I object to this Bill on the
ground that it has not behind it the support of the Report of any competent
board or public inquiry. There has not,
so far as I know, been any inquiry-definite inquiry-in Northern Ireland as to
the desirability of applying medical
benefits to Northern Ireland. There has
been as the House is aware an Inter-departmental Committee sitting on the
finance of the suggested scheme, but not
on the scheme itself. Rather on the
contrary we have that Report of the Local
Government Committee to which the hon.
Senator who followed the noble Viscount
referred, and that Report, as the Chairman of the Londonderry County Council
told us, was definitely against the introduction of medical benefits into Northern
Ireland.
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
I am afraid I must refer a little to
what was stated in that Report. I have
just said that there was a definite statement. It was not, they thought, desirable that medical benefits should be
introduced in Northern Ireland. The
exact phrase will be found in Paragraph
40 of the Report on page 199 :-
It
That medical benefits under the
National Insurance Acts be not introduced in Northern Ireland.
That was the Report of the Committee.
It seems necessary or desirable to look
for a moment at the composition of the
Committee that made that Report.
will be remembered that on that Committee sat three gentlemen who were, or
had been, Members of this House. There
were the Chairman of the Londonderry
County Council, who I am glad to say is
still with us; Senator Barbour, who is no
longer here, and Mr. Moore, at present
Lieutenant of Derry City, who
formerly, as Mayor of Londonderry, a
Member of this House. Those, I think,
are all the Members of this House who
sat on that Committee.
was
are
But this House does not exhaust the
composition of Parliament. There
others who have sat or sit in another
place. There is or was an hon. Lady at
that time one of the Members for the
County of Londonderry; there was Mr.
Crawford, then the Member for one of the
divisions of Antrim, and still a Member
for the Mid-Antrim division. That
gentleman is, as many of my hearers must
be aware, the Chairman of the Finance
Committee of the Antrim County Council,
and he is also, and has been for many
years, a member of the governors of the
county lunatic asylum. He has a very
long record of public service behind him,
and he is generally accepted as a very
sound authority on local government.
Those are two Members of the other
place, but there are more. There was a
learned Gentleman on the medical side
who then represented and now represents
the University. There was Mr. Miller,
a Member for County Fermanagh and
County Tyrone, and Mr. McGuffin,
formerly a Member of the other House.
There was the present Chairman of Ways
and Means. There was the late Dr.
Morrison, one of the Members for the
University, and a gentleman who had had
actual experience as a dispensary doctor
and coroner in the County Londonderry.
These gentlemen signed the majority Report, with the exception of Mr. McGuffin,
He
who signed the minority Report.
objected to the definite Report of the
majority that medical benefits should not
be applied in Northern Ireland.
Now, speaking of the Report, particularly as regards medical benefits, it is
quite impossible to keep the question of
the dispensary doctors out of the discussion. We are not discussing dispensary
doctors as such, but the whole dispensary
system is inextricably bound up with this
matter in the rural districts in particular.
The Commission say, on page 120, paragraph 363:
We found that in almost every union
the dispensary system was fulfilling its
purpose, and, generally speaking, witnesses were in favour of its retention.
I do not want to read the whole of the
Report. I imagine most Members have
read it, some of it at all events, for themselves.
I want to pass on to paragraphs dealing
more particularly with medical benefits. It was stated as an objection to the
dispensary system that the use of a dispensary doctor implied something in the
The Commisnature of pauper taint.
sion disposed of that, I think, rather
successfully by pointing out that the existence of that taint did not seem to prevent
people who were not entitled to relief on
what is called the line system, that is the
ordinary method of summoning the dispensary doctor-the existence of the
pauper taint did not seem to prevent those
in a better position in life obtaining free
treatment.
A second objection was that the
system affords no opportunity of choice
of doctor. It was, however, pointed
out that in many parts of rural Northern Ireland, however desirable such a
choice might be, geographical conditions would prevent it from being
exercised, as in these areas only one
medical man would be within reasonable
distance.
That, of course, is merely a general
statement. It was pointed out that there
were a good many areas-one has to be
perfectly fair in these matters-in which
an alternative choice was possible. The
Report goes on:
When inquiring into the suitability
and efficiency of the dispensary medical
svstem we found that there was a considerable body of opinion in favour of
Health Insurance Bill-
3 JUNE 1930

Second Reading
the introduction of medical benefits
under the National Insurance Acts on
parallel lines with Great Britain.
Evidence tendered in support of
medical benefits came from Approved
Societies, Associations of Approved
Societies, Insurance Committees, Trades
Unions, and the Tuberculosis Committee of the Belfast Corporation. The
medical profession also gave evidence
in support of the introduction of medical
benefits, but when witnesses of this
profession appeared before us we found
that there was lack of unanimity, and
that a considerable section of medical
practitioners in Northern Ireland were
opposed to the system. While we have
not been able to ascertain definitely to
what extent the profession was divided,
it appeared to us that doctors practising
in industrial areas favoured the adoption
of medical benefits, while doctors practising in rural areas were opposed to
any change. There appears to be a very
similar division of opinion in the minds
of the general public. Thus, while
practically all the representatives of
rural areas who appeared before us
thought that the introduction of medical
benefits was uncalled for and unnecessary, we heard a deputation representing a large number of Trade Unions.
and other labour organisations who
strongly urged upon us the advantages
of medical benefits, and expressed
themselves perfectly willing to pay the
increased contribution which would be
necessary.
The Commission goes on to examine the
matter generally, but having regard to
the conclusions that were arrived at, I do
not know there is much object in pursuing the matter further on those lines,
but it was admitted that suggestions were
produced to show that in Great Britain
insured persons on the panel system consult their doctors more regularly than do
people in Northern Ireland.
That, I
think, is borne out by the noble Viscount.
The Report goes on to discuss the futility
of a scheme under which sickness benefit
can be obtained without medical treatment. Then it attacks the point already
made by the hon. Senator opposite that
the system which provides treatment only.
for an insured person and not for his dependents was not very satisfactory.
A good deal more of what has been said
is, perhaps, rather out of date at the
moment on account of the arrangements
made with the dispensary doctors, and I
will pass from that, but with the reiteration that the rural area particularly
object to this Bill, and resents anything
in the nature of dictation from the trade
unions in the industrial centres. That
feeling as between town and country is
always there to some extent, and I regret
very much some remarks made by an hon.
Member in another place, a somewhat prominent trade unionist, as I understand,
to the effect that the farmer desired
nothing so much as an easy and quiet life.
I regret very much that such a remark
should have been made at this juncture,
because it does not help anything in the
nature of conciliation, and it is a remark
of a class which is far more likely to be
resented than forgotten.
There are, admittedly, what I will call
popular superstitions in connection with
the insurance question. Perhaps they are
more prevalent in the remote country
areas. I am going to call them superstitions, because people always call other
people's views superstitions, and I am not
going to say whether I do or do not myself
adopt the view I am going to put before
the House on these particular subjects.
One of these popular superstitions is undoubtedly that the approved societies have
accumulated large reserves of moneys. It
was said at the time of the introduction of
the widows' and orphans' contributory
pensions scheme that the accumulated resources were sufficient to meet the charge
for pensions without any additional contribution at all. That is one of the superstitions.
Mr. ANDREWS: That statement was
not correct.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman disclaims association with that
superstition at any rate. Then as regards
young women, some of them fulfil their
natural destiny and get married. It is
said that their contributions are lost, that
they get no benefit, and that the contributions go to swell the pool of the assured
surpluses. It is also said that many
young and vigorous men go abroad with
a similar effect, that their contributions
are lost. There follows rather from that
another superstition to which I know the
right hon. Gentleman will also object, and
it is that insurance in itself, apart from
benefit of whatever kind, is not anything
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
It is not
that the
that requires to be paid for.
generally understood even yet
mere fact that you are insured against a
certain contingency is an advantage for
which payment has got to be made.
Hence, I think it happens that in
country districts a charge is brought
against people of taking a stamp off a card
which has become non-effective, and putting it on an effective card. That is a
understand,
procedure which, I
the
Minister of Labour regards with peculiar
disfavour. However, he has the advantage of the ordinary courts to support that
point of view, but the fact remains that
there is a feeling in the country that they
do not get enough in return for what they
pay. I think it is borne out by what the
noble Viscount said.
This Bill must be described as a Bill
for the relief of international societies. I
do not know that that is a peculiarly good
recommendation. I am not clear that an
international society that entered into
bargains with individual contributors in
Northern Ireland is entitled to refuse
benefit from that bargain. That has
rather been suggested. I am going to look
at the matter in another way. I am going
to suggest that there is no certainty that
I can find that a relief of the expenses
will be found in the provisions of the
Bill itself. An arrangement has been
made, we are told, with the dispensary
doctors, that those of them who accept.
appointment as panel doctors are to receive only 7s 6d per patient instead of the
normal 9s.
Let us take it the other way. A panel
doctor, who is not a dispensary doctor,
will get 9s for every patient on his list,
while the dispensary doctor will only get
7s 6d. The dispensary doctor at the
moment, as far as I can make out, is
treating, or is liable to be called upon to
treat, any insured person in his area who
is entitled to his services. He does that
already. He is now to be given 7s 6d
more for doing it. That seems to be unanswerable. An insured person entitled
to dispensary relief at the moment is entitled to the services of the dispensary
doctor. Can that be controverted? And
he gets them for nothing. The public
funds are bearing the cost of the dispensary
doctor, and it is now proposed to endow
that dispensary doctor with an additional
7s 6d in respect of each of these insured
persons whom he has been treating for
nothing. Whether the dispensary doctor
is going to get very much out of it or
not I do not know. I think, perhaps,
he is not.
Allow me for a moment to come to the
doctor who is not a dispensary doctor. It
is proposed that the contributor should pay
another 1d a week, and if the employer
and the contributor pay between them,
something like 2d a week in a normal year
of 52 Mondays, some 8s 8d will be provided by the additional sums. Out of
that 8s 8d somebody has got to pay the
doctor 9s. Nine shillings has got to be
provided for every panel doctor, who is
not a dispensary doctor, and there is supposed to be 8s 8d in stamps to pay it out
of. I am passing over the question of
administrative expenses.
The only way, as it seems to me, in
which the finances can be made to balance
is if the anticipations held out of a decreased charge for sickness benefit are
realised. Whether that will be so or not
seems to be purely a matter of conjecture, but the view taken of it in the
country districts at the moment is this.
What the approved societies want to do
by introducing new doctors and a new
system is to curtail the amount they pay
in sickness benefit, and the insured person's view is that as long as an insured
person is sick he wants to draw his sickness benefit. A good deal is made of the
fact that a certain number of insured
persons while sick do not apply for medical
attendance. But this is rather a matter
of opinion. When you thing of the hard
lives of the ordinary insured persons in
rural districts you have to admit
the mere matters of rest and vis
medicatrix naturæ. Those two things
tend towards recovery.
•
It not infrequently happens that the
wife or mother of a working man in
the country gets some knowledge of
symptoms and what is suitable for such
things as a cold in the throat or a cold
in the chest, but I do not think you
can lay too much stress on that. I do
not think that any definite evidence can
be produced that the result of this change
will be a reduction in sick benefit except
in so far as the approved societies can
harden their hearts. We are not told
as regards medical benefit how long the
medical benefit is to endure. In a case
of sickness, we are not told, so far as I can
ascertain, in the Bill that the visits to
the doctors will continue indefinitely.
We know there is a limit to sickness bene-
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
3 JUNE 1930
fit. The fault on the whole does not
seem to be that they want to separate the
insured persons into two classes, industrial and rural, but that there seems to
be no option given to the insured person
as to whether he will enter into this
scheme or stay out. I say he is entitled
to that option. The insured person in
Northern Ireland has not the option. He
is an entity entitled to express his own
opinion, and he has not had the chance
of doing it.
When the Act of 1920 was passed and
it was said that we would, as far as we
could, keep our social services in line
with those of Great Britain, there was
no medical benefit in Northern Ireland.
There seems to be no reason why it
should now be introduced on that score.
We are told it was left out when the
Bill of 1911 was before the Imperial
House. We are not told from what
bench its omission canie nor are we
told whether any Member of Parliament
on either side of the House clamoured for
the introduction of medical benefits to
Ireland. Those are matters which are
perhaps best left alone, but the Geneva
Convention of 1927 is urged upon us, and,
I think, something was said about the
Treaty of Versailles making it imperative
upon us to accept the blandishments held
out to us. I have just pointed out that
in 1920 when we took over, not at our
own request, there were no medical benefits. Great Britain cannot turn round
and say, "We gave you a Parliament in
1920 and this is a matter in regard to
which although you have power to make
your own laws we would like you to do
this or that." That is not enough. There
is nothing that I can see that requires us
to vote for anything we do not want.
I do not think, and in this I agree with
my hon. Friend opposite, you can
separate the industrialist from the
ruralist, but I cannot see any insuperable
difficulty in giving a man an option as
to whether he will come in or stay out.
It would appear automatically from the
value of the stamp on his card. That is
what I think a man ought to get if it is
decided that this Bill is to go on. I should
like to stress for a moment what was
said as regards stores of medical drugs.
There will undoubtedly be difficulties in
that matter if the dispensary doctor and
the panel doctor are not the same; if
they are not there will be a waste of
money. It seems to me that in the interests of the rural districts, which have
not been given a clear opportunity of
voicing their opinions, one must vote for
the rejection of this Measure.
Mr. McALLISTER: I should like very
shortly to address a few remarks to the
House with regard to this Bill. I agree
with the hon. Senator opposite (Mr.
Long) who stated that this Bill was introduced not from conviction but for expediency; but, whatever the motive may
be, I welcome this Bill as a Measure that
will go far to raise the standard of health
among the people of Northern Ireland,
and thereby provide a more efficient popu
lation. It is rather astonishing to find
from the figures quoted that the standard
of health in Northern Ireland is lower
than that of England and Wales. I must
say that the figures were a shock and revelation to me. This is a Bill that will
go far to assist many of those who would
otherwise not receive medical attention.
It is a striking fact that 26 per cent. of
the insured people of Northern Ireland
do not receive medical treatment. I
think that fact alone is sufficient justification for the introduction of a Measure
of this description, and I have no hesitation in saying that, if this Bill is
efficiently and properly administered, it
will confer great and enduring benefits
upon a vast number of people.
I agree with Senator Leslie in one
proposition that he put forward. He
complains-and I think rightly complains
-about the powers given to the Ministry
in this particular Bill. I have always
opposed this system of giving wholesale.
powers to any Ministry to make regulations which practically revolutionise Acts.
of Parliament as they leave this House.
I am
sure that every Member of this
House has been shocked at times when
his notice has been called to certain regulations in connection with Bills to find
that the regulations in many instances
exceed the authority which Parliament.
intended to convey to the Ministry. I
suggest that where wholesale powers are
given to the Ministry in this way, instead
of going through the formality of laying
the regulations upon the Table of the
House, they should be supplied at the
earliest possible moment to every Member
in order that he might have an opportunity of knowing the regulations made,
and of seeing how far they conform to the
opinions held by Members when the Bill
was before the House.
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
I also join with Senator Leslie in protesting against the shortness of the time
that one is given to examine a Bill of this
description. I frankly admit that I have
not had an opportunity of giving it more
than the most elementary consideration.
I should have liked time and opportunity
to consider this Bill, about which I have
had some correspondence, but owing to
the way in which Bills are rushed in this
House before the Summer Adjournment,
or at the close of the Session, one has not
an opportunity of giving a considered
opinion upon many of them.
One thought that occurred to me,
while Senator Leslie was speaking of the
constitution of the Commission that investigated this and other matters, was
that if there had been some members of
the party to which I belong on that Commission they probably would have kept
him right. However, I do welcome the
general outline of this Bill, although there
are many things in it on which I do not
see eye to eye with the Government.
Before I conclude I should like to stress
this point there are quite a number of
employees of the committees that are to
be abolished under this Bill. They have
given faithful service to the administrative work of the committees, and, I
think, their interests should be reasonably
safeguarded by this House. The remuneration of those officials is not a
matter that should be left in the hands of
the Minister. I think Parliament should
table some scheme whereby each officer
would know exactly the amount of remuneration to which he was entitled.
Mr. GLENDINNING: I am sorry to
say that, like the first two speakers on this
Bill, I have not been convinced by the
statement of the noble Lord as to the
necessity for this legislation. I am not
convinced that the necessity for medical
benefits arises in a natural manner from
the body of the people. Certainly, in my
district I have never heard of any great
demand for them. If the Bill has
originated in the Government, I should
like to be convinced that they have considered its effect upon industry, and its
effect upon the country generally, in
adding to the already high expense of our
existence.
I should certainly like to support the
second speaker in the point he made that
this Bill is adding to the burden upon industry. For unemployment and national
health insurance the employer contributes
at the present time Is 4d per head per
week, and this Bill adds an additional
penny to that amount. That may not be
regarded as a very great amount, but it is
always adding to the existing burden. In
the case of a comparatively small industry,
employing 200 people, it is going to cost
the firm £40 a year; and of course the
cost increases the more men are employed.
I should like to be convinced by the
Minister of Labour as to what benefit the
employer is going to get out of this Bill.
He is hit in a double way. As a taxpayer and ratepayer he has got to pay the
general expenses of the Bill, and, in
addition, he has got to pay practically
half of the cost of the doctor for every
panel patient in his
his employment. I
believe he is not going to get one
iota of benefit from it. In these days
when trade is dwindling, when industries
are struggling, when the youth of the
country are leaving the shores by hundreds
every week, I view with very grave concern the action of the Government in
bringing in a Measure like this if there is
no real public demand for it.
Social reform is very well in its way,
and I really hope that this Bill, if it goes
on, will reduce sickness and illness, and
also ameliorate to some extent the hard
life that the ordinary dispensary doctor
lives at the present time. But reforms
of this character entirely depend upon
whether the people can pay for them.
whether the country can support them,
and I myself consider that sufficient time
has not been taken to see whether there
was really a proper demand in Northern
Ireland for this particular Measure.
I
I am
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
desire to support the Second Reading of
this Bill for more reasons than one.
very glad that it brings us more completely
into line with the social legislation of
Great Britain. Then, I believe it will
relieve the serious anxiety of the workers
with regard to their future health. I do
not know that there is any greater nightmare for the poor man and his wife than
unemployment and bad health. No one
can realise the risks the worker runs in his
endeavour to follow his employment.
especially when trade is not too good. I
have known men who have been, you
might say. repeatedly "drowned to the
skin" in the morning going down to their
work, and they have had no alternative
but to continue their work in that condition. To have gone home to change-some.
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
3 JUNE 1930
unfortunately, have nothing into which to
change would have been to risk losing
their jobs, which is a serious matter,
especially at the present time.
Men are compelled to work under those
conditions, and it is not surprising to find
that they are very liable to suffer from
serious ailments, such as rheumatics and
pulmonary diseases, as a result of the wettings that they continually get. This
Bill is likely to assist the worker, because
when he felt himself stiff or affected with
the rain or cold he was very often too
manly to put himself to the expense of
paying a doctor for examining him. He
tried to "work it off," as it is described.
That is a usual statement amongst the
workers in the shipyard. Some rely on
a good glass of rum; in other cases the
man's wife relies on warm buttermilk and
plenty of butter. That is the usual
treatment meted out to the worker who
finds himself suffering from cold or stiffness. He endeavours to work it off. In
future, with medical benefits, it will be
quite easy for a man of that description
to consult his medical doctor. He will not
feel that he is looking for charity.
A great deal has been said about the
dispensary system, as if every worker runs
to the dispensary doctor when there is
anything wrong. One must recognise
that there are hundreds and thousands of
workers who, although their wages are not
very high, would not think for a minute
of going to the dispensary doctor for consultation. Another thing, it would not
be convenient to do so, because a dispensary doctor can be consulted only during
the day. The workman does not want to
lose his job, and he requires to get into
consultation with the doctor in the evening during his few hours' spare time. As
I say, in future he will not look upon
going to the doctor as charity. He will
go along and claim that service as his own,
something for which he has paid. Therefore, I do think it was very necessary to
introduce this Bill. There is no doubt
that for a long time the workers of Northern Ireland made no demand for medical
benefits. But in view of the working of
the insurance scheme, with the considerable increase in sickness in Northern Ireland, and the unfortunate increase in the
death rate, in my opinion it was the duty
of the Government to introduce this
Measure. If any government are in duty
bound to do anything, surely it is to safeguard the health and life of the common
people. That is what this Bill particularly
will carry out. It will certainly assist in
prolonging life and in relieving many a
poor fellow of suffering from weeks and
weeks of sickness as a result of the cold
and wet weather to which he is compelled
to submit.
The discussion that has taken place today has centred to a considerable extent
on the financial consideration of what it
is going to cost the employer.
In my
opinion the life and health of a person
should be the first consideration, whether
that person be a worker or in whatever
position in life he may be. Surely to
goodness it is the first duty of anyone to
consider what can be done to safeguard
the life and health of the breadwinner
when there is a wife and probably a number of children dependent on him, instead
of considering the cost of one penny per
week.
I am surprised to think that even the
farming classes should raise any objection
to this Bill. I know that there is as
much sickness amongst the poorer classes
in the country districts as there is in the
cities. Rheumatics and pulmonary
diseases are just as prevalent in those districts amongst the poorer classes as they
are amongst the workers in the city. If
the introduction of this Bill will only
remove those diseases to some little extent
and will prevent the increase in the death
rate, it will have done a great deal.
Might I point out, as far as the industrial centres are concerned, that I am sure
the employers in industrial firms are able
to look after themselves, and not one of
them has raised the slightest objection to
the introduction of medical benefits and
the small increase in their contributions.
When firms like Harland and Wolff,
Workman, Clark and Company and the
big linen industries do not complain I am
surprised to hear even the hon. Senator
(Mr. Leslie) referring to employers who
pay their workers 24/- a week, complaining at having to pay another 1d per week
towards health insurance. It is ridiculous,
and I am sorry to hear the statement
made in this House, that any man in
Northern Ireland has the hardihood to
pay his workers 24/- a week and then to
refuse to pay another penny in order to
maintain good health and longer life
amongst his workers. I am very glad
that the Bill has been introduced, and am
very pleased to give it my hearty support.
Health Insurance Bill-

Second Reading
society in Northern Ireland when this Act.
came into force and I want to give hon.
Members who are opposing the Bill just
three cases which were impressed upon
my mind, and which I have never forgotten, that came under my observation
in the administration of the Act.
THE SENATE
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: After
listening to what has been stated in connection with this Bill there is one point to
which I would like to draw attention. The
doctors at the Royal Victoria Hospital
often state when they have to attend to a
man who has been hurt that if medical
attention were given sooner it would not
only save a lot of expense in treating long
cases, but would also save the patient a
lot of suffering. I was wondering could
there be any provision, when you are
making up your panel, whereby a doctor
would have so many patients to look after,
as they do in China, and while he kept
them in good health that doctor would be
paid, but as soon as they took ill his pay
would stop. Is there any way in which
you could assign to a doctor so many
patients to whom he would pay periodical
visits, and, say, when the breadwinner in
a house had a cough would try to get rid
of that cough before it became pneumonia
or bronchitis? If so, I think it would be
a great benefit and would save money in
the long run.
My
Mr. DORMAN: The first and most
important duty of any State is to provide.
for the health, happiness, and prosperity
of all the citizens under its control.
mind goes back to the time when the Insurance Act was first introduced in Great
Britain, and when the party of which
was then, and am still, a member was the
only political party in the State to fight
for the extension of medical benefits to
Ireland. The things that were then
predicted as likely to take place in consequence of the exclusion of Irish workers
from the advantages of medical benefits
have been proved up to the hilt.
It now
behoves us as Members of this House and
representatives of the people of Northern
Ireland to face the facts. We have a 17
per cent. greater ratio of sickness-actual
sick claims amongst our insured population than they have in Great Britain, and
we have a regular death rate of 25 per
cent, more than Great Britain.
Mr. LESLIE indicated dissent.
Mr. DORMAN: I think if the hon.
Senator looks up the figures he will see
that I am right. Our death rate is 25 per
cent. higher. It is in the ratio of 15 to
12. If in the one case there are 15
deaths and in the other 12, when I went
to school the difference of three was 25
per cent. of 12. The argument has been
put up that this is going to be a burden
upon the employers. I happend to be
in charge of a fairly extensive approved
The first was a factory girl, or rather a
woman of about 26 years of age who lived
at the upper end of the Shankill Road.
She was earning about 15s a week on the
average. She went on the sick list, sent
in her medical certificate, and claimed
her benefit, which then was 7s 6d per
week. I happened to visit her in her
home and I found that that woman was
paying out of that 7s 6d per week 3s 6d
for medical attendance, which left her
practically 4s to live on and pay her rent,
if she had rent to pay. I would ask hon.
Senators to conceive the position of a young
woman of 26 years of age with a total
income of 7s 6d per week out of which she
had to pay 3s 6d for medical attendance
and the medicine which she received-the
bottle as she called it-in the week. That
left her 4s a week. Imagine a woman
dependent on that for three months of
illness. What chance would the most
skilful practitioner have of restoring that
worker to a normal state of health while
she was forced to try to subsist upon the
food that would be purchaseable for 4s
per week to say nothing of lodgings?
The next case that came under my observation was that of a man in the
country-in Ballynahinch.
His wages
were 14s per week, and when he went sick
his income was reduced from 14s to 10s a
week, out of which he had to pay 3s 6d
a week for medicine and medical attendance. I believe that it is because of such
circumstances surrounding the lives of the
workers of Northern Ireland that we have
had this abnormally large ratio of sickness
claims. That does not mean individual
separate claims. The large percentage
is made up by the prolongation of sick
benefit per member in Northern Ireland
in comparison with the length of the sickness term in Great Britain. I can
conceive that this Bill will be calculated to
remove this injustice from the sick in our
community. To say that a man will dispose of his labour in order to make good
the loss incurred in paying 41d per week
for a member is, to my mind, ridiculous.
I do not think any employer would conceive the idea of disposing of one man to
make good the loss involved in paying
this necessary-
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
3 JUNE 1930
Mr. SPEAKER: Would the hon. Member please speak up?
For
Mr. DORMAN: I do not believe any
employer would be so heartless as to dispose of any of his employees for the purpose of making good the loss he will sustain in paying for these stamps.
these reasons I hope the House will pass
this Bill. I could go on and give many
other instances, but the discussion has
been fairly exhaustive. I, therefore,
conclude by saying that, as one who has
had a wide experience over from five to
seven years of the administration of this
Act, who has been interested in it from
its inception, and who struggled for its
extension to Ireland, I give it my wholehearted welcome and support.
Mr. BARCLAY: I rise to support the
Second Reading of this Bill. I might say
that I am a convert. For years I have
been more or less prejudiced against
medical benefits. I was in England when
the Act came into force there in 1912,
and, perhaps, some hon. Members will
remember the great dispute there was
amongst the doctors at that time. Some
of the doctors were not willing to have
anything to do with this Act, and the result was that it only dropped into the
hands of a few. The impression that was
made amongst working-class people in
Manchester in those days was that the
medicine they were receiving from the
doctors was not all that could be desired.
However, time has passed, and I have
come here to Belfast, and mixing amongst
the working-class people in the shipyard,
on the street, and in other places, I find
that a great number of working men and
working women are suffering because they
are afraid to lose the time, or are afraid
to go to a doctor, because a great many
of them have not the means to pay a
doctor. The result is that they continue
at their work trying, as the hon. Senator
(Mr. Joseph Cunningham) has said, to
“work it off," with the result that, instead of working it off, the disease grows
worse and many of them die and leave a
double burden upon the community in the
shape of widows and orphans.
My mind was changed when I listened
to the Minister of Labour (Mr. Andrews)
some time ago explaining this Bill. I
went further. I went to a doctor and
asked him what was the impression
amongst the doctors in the City of Belfast
on this question. He told me that the
doctors were going in wholeheartedly for
this Measure, and would do their best to
make it a success when it became law. I
said to this doctor, "Is there not a probability of its being abused." “Well,”
he said, "so far as the doctors are concerned there will be no abuse. We
doctors have formed ourselves into a guild
for the purpose of administering this Act,
and we will see that no one will take advantage." He also told me when I mentioned to him the way it was administered
in England that it would not be administered in that way here. The doctors
there supply the medicine, but here in
Northern Ireland, if the Act becomes law,
the doctors will not supply the medicine.
They will simply write out a prescription
and send the patient to the chemist. I
quite see there is no probability of the
doctors abusing it, and it is probable there
is no probability of the chemists abusing it.
There are one or two questions that have
been put to me by working-class people,
and, of course, I gave what I thought was
the answer. I will just ask the Minister
of Labour these two questions. The first
question was can the person receiving
medical benefit receive sickness benefit or
disablement benefit at the same time? The
second question is with regard to medical
attendance. There is a doubt in the
minds of some of the people I come across,
who do not read the papers and have never
seen, and probably never will see, the
Act. They would like to know if medical
attendance means only attendance at the
doctor's residence or surgery, or does it
mean medical attendance in their own
homes?
Another question has been put to me regarding the voluntary contributor. We
find that the voluntary contributor whose
income is over £250 is not entitled to any
medical benefit. But is the voluntary
contributor whose income does not exceed
£250 entitled to medical benefit, and, if
so, will he have to pay an extra contribution? Those are questions put to me
in the street. I hope the Minister will
be able to enlighten some of these people
who, perhaps, are anxious that they should
be kept right in these things. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Bill.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I will not detain the House long, but I want to say
one or two words. I am going to vote for
the Second Reading of the Bill on the
grounds that, as Northern Ireland has
such
a large proportion over Great
371 Health Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Colonel Pakenham]
Britain of sick people and such a death
rate, there must be something wrong in
the state of Denmark, and I am not going
to see the dispensary doctor unable to
carry on. For that reason, among many
others, I will vote for the Second Reading
of the Bill.
Mr. QUIGLEY: I rise with very sincere.
pleasure to support the Second Reading
of this Bill. Indeed, I would go the
length of saying that the Government
have too long delayed this Bill. It is a
Bill which will be of great benefit to our
people in this great city, and I am sure
it will be also in the rural districts though
we have heard a great deal to-day against
it from representatives of the rural districts. In regard to the Bill the people
will have the advantage of their own doctor. If they are not satisfied with that
doctor they can, I understand from my
reading of the Bill, have a change.
Like other hon. Senators who have
spoken on this Bill, I have not had
sufficient time, though I have spent a good
deal of time trying to grasp it, and from
what I have read of the Bill I believe it
will be of very great benefit indeed. One
of the hon. Senators referred to the employers' loss. The employers' loss may
be a small thing, but the health of the
community is a greater thing.
Mr. ANDREWS: Hear, hear.
Mr. QUIGLEY: And my own opinion
in regard to that loss is that the employers
will get more efficient service and better
work from their employees if they are in
good health. We know very well that
many people do not like to go to the dispensary doctor. We cannot get over the
fact that no matter how kind and considerate the dispensary doctors may be a
good many people have a sort of feeling
that they would not like to go. Here in
this Bill we are getting eminent physicians.
and surgeons,
I presume, over the city
whom our people will be able to visit and
have their very valuable assistance, and
I understand from the Bill we will get
the very best medicines.
I am delighted that this Bill has been
introduced. I have heard expressions of
opinion outside this House very much in
favour of this Bill. I was surprised to
hear some honourable Members speaking
against it in the way they did. There is
no doubt that a Bill of this kind will be
a great boon in the city.
No doubt it
will meet with opposition in the rural
districts, but you could not introduce any
Bill that would be satisfactory to all the
people. I have very great pleasure in
supporting the Bill.
Mr. ANDREWS: Invariably when I
have the privilege of coming to this
honourable House I have a feeling that its
debates are conducted in a most reasonable, thoughtful, and in my opinion,
proper way.
There has been criticism of
this Measure from certain hon. "Senators
who, no doubt, have honestly expressed
the views that they hold. Senator Leslie
complained in the first instance that the
Bill was being rushed, and that he really
had not had time to study the problem.
I think the speech that he made is, in
itself, an answer to that charge of there
not being sufficient time for him to study
the Measure, because it is quite evident
to me that he has given it most careful
study for quite a number of weeks.
has paid me the compliment of reading
some of the speeches which I made in the
other House, and from which he somewhat
freely quoted.
He
But as regards his general argument
that the Bill was unpopular, I must here
cross opinions with him also, because while
it is true that some months ago before
the Measure was introduced at all, before
it was understood what the Measure would
contain, when a great many wrong impressions existed in the minds of a number
of people in town and country alike, there
was a feeling against the Measure. But
since conferences have taken place with
interested parties, and since the Measure
has been introduced, it appears to me that
the feeling against the Measure has
entirely disappeared, and if I may say so,
it gratifies me beyond measure to find that
in the other House the people's representatives, depending upon the votes of the
people as they are, were unanimously in
favour of the Measure, and it passed all
its stages without a division, and only two
trifling Amendments to the Measure were
introduced and passed. I think that disposes of the second argument of my hon.
Friend.
Now, in the third place, I would point
out that I feel that we in Northern Ireland are not going far wrong when we are
following Great Britain in introducing a
Measure which has stood the test of almost
twenty years there, and has proved to be
an undoubted success and a blessing to
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
3 JUNE 1930
the insured persons. I was told by Senator
Long that the Government was introducing this Measure more because it was being
influenced by expediency than by conviction. I can assure hon. Senators that
that is not so. Personally my heart and
my head never worked in better unison
than in introducing this Measure, because
I maintain that the Government could not
remain inactive when it was perfectly clear
to the Government that not only the
general death rate but the sickness rate
amongst our insured persons was considerably higher than in Great Britain.
Something was bound to be done and no
one has suggested anything else than the
introduction of this Measure.
Now it has been suggested that it would
be proper to give an option to our insured
persons as to whether they would come
under the Bill or whether they would not.
Well, in my opinion, that would not be
possible in practice. Would it be an
option that must be exercised and must
be binding for all time or would it be an
option given once a month or once a year?
How long, for instance, would the option.
be binding upon the insured person? Then
it is quite possible that the insured person
when healthy and strong would say, "I
will remain out," and when the insured
person became ill he would declare in
That
favour of coming into the scheme.
would not be fair to the insured persons;
it would not be fair to the medical profession who, remember, are being paid
upon an all-round rate for those who are
upon their panel, the strong and the
feeble alike.
But it has been suggested that this is
going to be a burden upon employers.
May I say that not one single employers'
association in Northern Ireland, to their
credit be it said, has made a single protest
against the introduction of the Measure.
And, further, I have been asked what
benefit will it bring to employers. I agree
with the Senator opposite (Mr. Quigley),
who said that employers benefit greatly
when their workers are healthy and
strong, and employers suffer when their
workers are feeble and ill. We know quite
well that we can give a better account of
ourselves if we are feeling well, no matter
what kind of work we are engaged upon
than if we are feeling ill. Besides I think
employers generally take the big view in
connection with these matters. They feel
they have a responsibility for their
workers; they feel they have an interest
in their workers, and I honestly believe
their workers' welfare is very dear to the
hearts of many of them.
Senator Leslie said that this was putting
a burden upon farmers, and that farmers
at the present time could not afford to
bear any additional burden. As a pretty
large farmer, probably tilling more land
in Northern Ireland than the vast
majority of farmers, I know quite well.
that there would be some truth in this
argument if it was a burden, but I
maintain it is not a burden worth considering. It amounts to a penny per
week per worker. It means that 68 per
cent. of our farmers in Northern Ireland
are not affected at all, because their
farms are thirty acres or under. Practically none of these farmers have any
labour employed beyond the farmer
himself and the members of his own
family. But if one of these small farmers
did happen to employ a labourer it would
mean a penny a week to him. A smoke
less in the week would cover the cost.
Then let us take a large farm of, say,
eighty acres. That farmer employs, say,
four labourers, so that means 4d per week
to that farmer with eighty acres of land,
and he is the owner of the land he tills.
Surely there is no farmer in Northern
Ireland who will object to that. I have
never found one who objected when the
matter was explained to him. I have interviewed many deputations of farmers
and others in connection with this matter,
and I make that statement without hesitation. When once people get to know
what this Measure really means they are
all for it with both their hearts and
heads.
That
Senator Barclay asked me two questions
which I would like to answer, because I
feel they are very important. One was,
can a person receive medical benefit and
continue to receive sickness benefit or
disablement benefit, as the case may be?
The answer, I am glad to say, is in the
affirmative. The second question was,
would a patient be attended in his own
home or in the home of the doctor?
will depend upon the nature of the illness
and the necessities of the case. It will be
the duty of the doctor to attend the
patient where the patient really requires
to be attended. May I point out in this
connection that not only have I the
greatest possible faith in the integrity of
the medical profession, and in their desire
to do everything possible for their
patients, but it will be in the interests of
Health Insurance Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Andrews]
the doctors to please their patients, because in reality these insured persons will
be their private patients just as much as
their other patients are private patients.
The only difference is this, that while their
ordinary private patients pay in a lump
sum, we will say, under this scheme the
doctor is paid by the insured person when
he is in the best position to pay the doctor,
namely, when he is working. The fact of
the matter is, as Senator Joseph Cunningham has said, and as those of us who come
across working people from day to day
know, there are two things that our working people dread more than anything else.
One is unemployment and the other is
sickness.
Why do they dread sickness so much?
It is not so much the suffering endured
that the breadwinner feels. What he
really feels most is that when he is sick
his home is very frequently cast into a
position of more or less destitution. It is
at that time the patient feels that it is
improper of him to call in a doctor and
incur additional expense, which might deprive some of his dependents of the very
necessaries of life. One of the strongest
arguments, in my opinion, in favour of
medical benefit is this: that the payment
of the doctor is made when the patient is
working, and when he can afford to pay
the trifling penny. Then he has the satisfaction of knowing that if he does become
ill he will get medical attendance, so that
he may be once again restored to health
and become a breadwinner for his family's
sake more than for his own sake, in most
instances.
It has been suggested that this Bill
is not complete because the dependents of
insured persons are not included. That
argument very much savours of the one
that we should not give any bread because
we cannot give a whole loaf. We are
here keeping in step with Great Britain,
and if at some future time Great Britain
takes a further step forward it will be
for us to consider the advisability of following. After all, we are more or less
a subordinate Parliament, and we need
not presume to think we can lead, but I
hope that in connection with these matters
we will always have the courage and the
foresight to follow. I feel it is not so
necessary that these medical services
should be applied to the dependents of
the breadwinner as to the breadwinner
himself, because when the breadwinner
is well and working he can afford, very
frequently, to pay for the services of a
doctor for his dependents. It is quite a
different matter when the breadwinner
himself is a patient.
It has been suggested to-day that we
might discriminate between town and
country, and have medical benefits for
our towns and leave the country out.
That would not be possible administratively, and, in my opinion, it would be
unjust and unthinkable. It would not
comply with the wishes, in fact the demand of the approved societies, and it
would not enable Great Britain to ratify
the Geneva Convention in connection with
this matter. The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) said that we ought
to stand on our own in connection with a
matter of this kind, and that we ought
not to be influenced by the necessities of
Great Britain. I do not think that would
be a safe course for the Government of
Northern Ireland to pursue. Our relations with Great Britain are so inseparably warped together-I hope for all time.
if I may presume to say so-
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
a
Mr. ANDREWS- that they cannot
be separated. If we do not show
willingness to meet Great Britain when it
is Great Britain's wish that we should do
So we might be placing ourselves in a
position where Great Britain might not be
very willing to meet us when we requested
her to do so. All I would like to say in
conclusion is this: Having studied this
question probably more than any other
question I have studied for a very considerable number of years, it is with the
greatest confidence I recommend it to the
sympathetic consideration of this House.
But
Mr. LESLIE: I seem to have been
left little to reply to. The right hon.
Gentleman suggested that Great Britain
nad asked for this. I should myself
rather draw a distinction between the
voice of Great Britain as expressed by the
Government of the day in Great Britain
and Great Britain in another sense.
the principal point is this question of
option. The Minister says an option
cannot be given. Having regard to the
provisions in the Bill, to which one of the
speakers on my left drew attention, the
option of changing a panel doctor here.
and there, it does not seem to me that
the question of option is insuperable.
However, I do not want to make a long
Education Bill-
