speech about that. The right hon.
Gentleman suggested that the country districts had come round, but my recollection
is that neither the Ballymena, Antrim or
the Fermanagh Councils had published
any recantation.
Mr. ANDREWS: The councils have
withdrawn their opposition.
Mr. LESLIE: I did not know that.
A great deal has been said about the
death rate. I must read the figures
given to the Commission. The death rate
per 1,000 from 1909 to 1913, in the period.
immediately before the introduction of
health insurance, in England and Wales
was 14, and in Northern Ireland 17.7.
That is 3.7 against Northern Ireland. In
1924-26-that is the time health insurance
was in operation-the death rate in England and Wales was 12 per 1,000, and
in Northern Ireland 15.6, a reduction,
curiously enough, in favour of the country
that had not got medical benefits. I do
not hase any argument on that, because
I do not think one could, but it does seem
to show that the unfavourable position of
Northern Ireland as regards the death
rate is independent of this question of
medical benefits.
The only other thing I want to draw
attention to is the suggestion that the
worker would object to taking the dispen-
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
Glendinning, Mr. J. C.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
sary doctor as being a form of poor relief.
The same objection does not seem to apply
to taking medical benefits, which is apparently secured by a contribution from
the worker. There is no reason why the
worker should object to either.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
worker is paying for that.
Mr. LESLIE: But there is no reason
why he should object to the other, because
the ordinary worker pays towards the
dispensary doctor. He pays his rates,
and my hon. Friend must not underrate
the underrated. I should have liked to
have been able to meet the wishes of the

Motion, but I was always taught when
I was young that where one was in doubt
as to the best course to take, one should

more unpleasant course it to divide the

about the matter, I cannot withdraw the

CLAUSE 4 (Provision of Bible instruction in provided and transferred
schools).
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Porter, Mr. John Porter
Quigley, Mr. A.
(1) If Bible instruction is not provided by the education authority in any
public elementary school, being a provided school or transferred school, it
shall be the duty of the education
authority to provide Bible instruction,
should the parents of not less than ten
children who are in regular attendance
at such school make application to the
education authority for that purpose in
the prescribed manner: Provided that
the obligation under this Sub-section
shall not arise where there is another
public elementary school in which Bible.
instruction is given and which in the
opinion of the Ministry such children.
can conveniently attend.
Education Bill-
Report of Amendment
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
7, line 19, after "manner" ("the prescribed manner') insert "and such instruction shall continue to be so provided
so long as the parents of any children in
such attendance so desire.'
""
It
The first Amendment standing in my
name deals with the rather troublesome
question of what is to happen if the ten
children in respect of whom Bible instruction was to be given diminish in number.
It may be that they would diminish one
by one or they may vanish at once, and
they may or may not receive recruits.
seems to me the position is very difficult,
particularly when you remember that the
whole Clause is unlikely to operate at all
unless there is some disagreement between
the parents and the school authority
What is in my mind is really this: Consider the ten original children, and take
the case of the first born becoming 14,
leaving behind nine other brothers or
sisters. Can you possibly allow the Bible
instruction to stop just because the
number has fallen below the original ten?
It seems to me you cannot. This instruc-
We
tion is to be given on a reasonable requisition which has been fixed at ten.
are bound to continue such instruction so
long as the children are there. As will be
seen I am proposing further on an
Amendment to make a kind of loophole
for use in the event of another school being
established or coming into existence which
would be convenient for the instruction
of such children.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If the
second Amendment, which the hon. and
learned Senator is going to move, is a

I cannot agree to. I think it would be

original person who involved teaching
being given to his nine brothers and
sisters. At the present moment, as the
Bill stands, the Committee continue to
give instruction if they think fit. I
think it would be better to leave the
matter in the present position, the education authority having leave to continue.
Bible instruction, but that it should not
be made statutory.
Mr. LESLIE: I think probably the
noble Viscount's view will be taken by

to withdraw that Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page 7.
line 20, after "arise" ("shall not arise")
insert "or continue."
This is the loophole, and I move it because it seems to me it is possible that
it may be desirable to put it in quite

which may serve the purpose. In fact
it strikes me that things being as they

Clause is to be approached, point to the

not press the matter if the noble Viscount
does not like it.
CHARLEMONT:
Viscount
I think
this would be an improvement on the

CLAUSE 7 (Powers for the education
of children in exceptional circumstances).
Provided that when a child is boarded
out in pursuance of this section, the
education authority shall, if possible,
and if the parent or guardian so requests, arrange for the boarding out
being with a person belonging to the
religious persuasion of the child's
parents.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
9, line 32, leave out "and if the parent
or guardian so requests."
This is a matter we discussed in Committee under rather different circumstances. I am glad to say that the delay
has, I think, been rather to advantage
than otherwise. It so happens that I had
an opportunity of discussing this question
with the hon. and learned Senator, who
usually sits in front of me and who is
not in his place to-day (Mr. Campbell),
and he and I were able to agree that the
Amendment now on the Paper meets our
joint wishes. As the House will remember, we were all agreed that there must
be no obstacle placed in the way of a
child being brought up in the religion of
Education Bill-
Report of Amendment-
3 JUNE 1930
the parents whatever that religion may
be, but a very curious thing came to our
knowledge. The hon. and learned
Senator maintained that the words in the
text followed the English Act of 1921. I
maintained that they did not. We found
on reference to the English Act of 1921
that we were both quite right, and that
a different form of words had been used
in different sections of that Act. 1
think on the whole the House will
agree that it is unnecessary that the
words should stand in the text, and that
it should be an obligation on the education
authority to send the child to a place in
conformity with the religious opinions of
the parents.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think there can
be no objection to this Amendment. In
my opinion it very definitely improves
the Clause. It seems to me to more
effectively carry out the intention of the
Bill. It renders the initiative compulsory on the education authority, and I
think in all the circumstances it is the
more desirable and effective way of securing that the intention of the Bill would
be carried out, so that children would
be boarded in the houses of people of the
same religion as the parents.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I feel it a
somewhat rare occasion to find two hon.
and learned Senators disagreeing whether
a sentence is or is not in the original Act
of another place, and it so happening that
both were right. It is such a rare occasion
for two hon. and learned Senators to agree
on a matter of this kind that I think the
Ministry of Education would be extremely
ill advised not to accept the Amendment.
I think it makes the Clause much better.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 8 (Amendment of Section 15
of the principal Act as to voluntary.
schools).
(b) It shall not be lawful for an
education authority to refuse to approve
of estimates of expenditure duly submitted to them by the managers or body
controlling a voluntary school, unless
such estimates are excessive or such
expenditure is not necessary for the
efficient maintenance of the school, and
any question which may arise under
this provision shall be referred to the
Ministry, whose decision shall be final.
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page 9,
line 43, at end ("a voluntary school
unless") insert "in the opinion of such
authority."
This is a minor Amendment. The point
is that an education authority must necessarily be, temporarily at all events, in
disagreement with the manager or body
controlling the voluntary school, or else
the paragraph would not come into operation at all. The object of the paragraph
is to prevent an education authority refusing to approve of any estimates of expenditure duly submitted unless there is
good reason. A very good reason is dealt
with, that estimates are excessive and that
the expenditure is not necessary. In the
event of disputes seriously arising such
matters must be dealt with by the Ministry. I suggest that as the text is before
us it rather begs the question. It says:
It shall not be lawful for an education authority to refuse to approve of
estimates of expenditure duly submitted.
to them by the managers or body controlling a voluntary school, unless such
estimates are excessive,
and so on. The excess or other thing can
only be in the opinion of the education.
authority. I think, perhaps, it would be
better to put the words in, because that
would leave it to the two parties to confer
together and discuss the question before
they came to the parting of the ways and
joining issue.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sorry
that I do not like this Amendment, because the real object of the Clause, as the
hon. and learned Senator says, is to protect the school management committee
against a stingy authority, if such a thing
exists. They have an appeal to the
Ministry if it was in consequence of the
education authority which refuses the
grant saying that it was excessive, and
being thus made judges in their own cases.
I think it would be hardly fair to the
committee which the Clause was designed
to protect. After all the word excessive
is capable of a perfectly clear definition.
Supposing there was a slate off the roof
and the management committee put in a
demand for £20 to the education authority
I think it would be possible to define that
as being excessive. I think if the Clause
was redrafted as suggested it would lead
to a great deal of friction between the
management committee and the education
authority, and I am, therefore, unable to
accept the Amendment.
Education Bill-
Report of Amendment-
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: I do not hesitate to ask
leave to withdraw after that statement,
but I should like to express the hope that
in the instance given the s holl authorities will not hesitate to put on the slate
first and get authority afterwards.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
CLAUSE 16 (Amendment of s. 30 of
the principal Act as to attendance at
school).
Section thirty of the principal Act
shall have effect subject to the following
amendments:-
:-
(a) An education authority may,
if they think fit, prescribe the age
of five years and six months as the
age for the commencement of school
attendance as respects their education
area, and, where a by-law prescribing that age is made and confirmed,
Part VI of the principal Act shall
have effect accordingly ;
(b) A by-law prescribing an age for
the commencement of school attendance may be made by the education
authority so as to apply to some specified part or parts of their education
area:
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
16, line 33, at end (end of paragraph
(b)) to insert :
"and so as to provide for the exemption
therefrom of any child under the age of
six years if the parent of such child
applies to the education authority for
such exemption."
I
This is another attempt to make a little
safer the position of the child of five years
and six months if such a child happens
to reside in a remote country district and
requires a little further protection.
cannot help thinking that the importance
of the question depends on whether the
individual child is or is not the youngest
child of a family that is attending school.
If a child of five years and six months
has got an elder brother or sister to take
him or her along the country road the
position is much easier. When you are
dealing with a child of such tender years
you ought to leave rather more to the
parent than in the case of an elder child.
You allow the attendance authority proper
control when the child gets older, but
when below six years I think the parents'
views ought to have more weight attached
to them than later on, and that is particularly so when a particular child is living,
as I say, in some cottage up a bog road
two miles away from the school. If the
child has got to go the two miles in the
winter or when it is raining it is a very
long distance. Moreover, it must be remembered that in the country when it
rains it may blow sideways and wet that
child's skirts or socks, and when the child
reaches school it will be in a hopelessly wet
condition. By suggesting the addition of
those words as a kind of appendage to the
paragraph, allowing a certain amount of
latitude under the by-law, it might make
it rather easier to be accepted than the
first proposal. One sees, of course, the
obvious difficulty in having given power
to make a by-law now to cut it down, but
it is the interest of the child which should
be paramount.
Mr. DORMAN: I hope the noble Lord
will accept this Amendment. I know
for a fact that a very large number of
parents in the country have a conscientious objection to their children being
forced to go to school before they are
six years of age, and I know also there
are many medical men who consider that
the structure of the brains of children
is not sufficiently developed to enable them
to concentrate at that early age. Therefore, I hope this Amendment will be
accepted by the Government so that the
desire of these parents may be met and
the children will not be forced to go.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
accept the Amendment in spite of the plea
put forward by the hon. Senator. The
authority under the Act of 1923, as the
hon. and learned Senator knows, has
power to make a by-law reducing the age
of compulsory attendance to five years.
This Amendment asks for some mitigation
of that, but that can be done at present,
and this paragraph is to make it a little
easier should of them wish to do so.
any
I think I am right in saying that since
the introduction of the original Education
Act only two education committees have
considered the question and neither of
them went so far as to propose it. One
was a rural committee and the other was
a county borough committee. The county
borough was influenced in not proceeding
further
of
by lack
accommodation,
but in the rural area it was certainly
opposition on the part of the parents, it
being a rather out of the way and
mountainous area. I would like to say
that the Act as it stands provides that
compulsion cannot be exercised where

I
there is a reasonable excuse for nonattendance, and this should provide for

should also say that as an additional protection that the by-law is made to apply

Mr. LESLIE: I think the noble

spared me that, that the text of the Bill
provides that the authority making the
by-law should make it applicable to such
parts of their area as they see fit. That
would give them the opportunity of excluding a bog track. If my hon. Friend
agrees I would ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move that this House do now adjourn
until to-morrow. To-morrow I hope to
take the Committee stage of the National
Health Insurance Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I must ask the noble
Viscount whether he considers it anything
other than insulting to the intelligence of
the House to ask it to take the Committee
stage of the Bill to-morrow.
Mr. DORMAN: The Derby is on.
There is
Mr. LESLIE: It is absurd.
no time to prepare Amendments even if
there was any chance of their being
accepted. It is really absurd to have the
Committee stage to-morrow. The Bill is
one of great importance. After all,
Parliament did not meet this year until
11th March. This House has not sat more
than fourteen or fifteen times, and it
ought to be possible to spare a little more
time for the consideration of important.
Measures, particularly of a Measure such
as this, which, as we have seen, involves
something in the nature of a disagreement.
I should like that the Committee stage of
this Bill should not be taken this week.
I do not know what other business there
may be to-morrow beyond the Third
Reading of the Education Bill.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
National Health Insurance Bill was
introduced, I think the hon. and learned
Senator mentioned the fact, on 13th May,
and since then it has been amended in two
I think
absolutely minor particulars.
that sufficient time has been given. I
would also remind the hon. and learned
Senator that I postponed, at his own
request, the Second Reading for 24 hours.
I cannot possibly postpone the Committee
stage beyond this week. If it would suit.
him better to have it on the day after tomorrow, so that this House would sit on
Friday instead of rising on Thursday, I
would consider it. I think hon. Members
would probably find it more convenient
to take the Committee stage to-morrow
than to come back on Friday. I am
willing to consider Friday if any hon.
Member would propose it, otherwise I
should prefer that this House should rise
on Thursday.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

4 JUNE 1930
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 4th June, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER
in the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Report of the Departmental Committee
on Civil Service Re-grading.
Presented (by Command) and ordered
to lie on the Table.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved, "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill," Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[The MARQUESS OF DUFFERIN
AND AVA in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Extension of medical
benefit to Northern Ireland).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: I propose on this Clause
to make a final and perhaps desperate endeavour to get an option or co-opting-out
Clause inserted in the Bill. Owing to
the shortness of the time which it was possible to allow for the consideration of this
not unimportant Bill it was impracticable
for me to put any Amendments down
yesterday afternoon, for even if I had had
one in draft it would obviously have been
impossible to get it inserted on the Order
Paper with any chance of that Order
Paper reaching any Member of this
House before that Member arrived here
this afternoon. Incidentally I may mention that I have not yet seen the posted
copy of the Order Paper for yesterday,
and I do not expect to see the posted
copy of to-day's Order Paper until some
time to-morrow afternoon. Accordingly,
I propose not to move anything at this
stage, because it is scarcely fair to the
Minister of Labour to cast an Amendment at him without two or three minutes'
notice at all events, although I am not
quite sure that any Member of the Government is entitled to much consideration
at the hands of Members of this House
on an occasion such as this when the
House is not given more than twelve
hours' notice before the following stage
of a Bill is taken.
But I will move to-morrow, if the Report stage of the Bill is taken to-morrow,
or after the Third Reading if there is
no Report stage, to the effect that an insured person may at any time by declaration contract himself out of medical benefit, and if he makes such an election that
election shall endure for a period of five
years, when he may be open to re-consider
the matter. For the moment I do not
propose to offer any objection to the
acceptance of Clause 1.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Rates of Contribution)
ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Modification of s. 12 of
the principal Act as to medical benefit).
Question proposed, "That the Clause.
stand part of the Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: The last paragraph of
Sub-clause (4) of Clause 3 seems to be a
suitable opportunity of endeavouring to
get from the Minister of Labour a statement of what is to happen in the case I
am about to adumbrate. According to
my information, in the case of a soldier
who received an injury during the late War
and in compensation for that injury received a disablement pension amounting
to over 20 per cent., if that compensation
has been stabilised, as many of them have,
that Army pensioner is at the moment
receiving without payment something
which is as nearly akin to panel treatment
as it would be possible to imagine. If he
is in employment his card is stamped in
the ordinary way, but in addition to the
dispensary treatment to which he would
be entitled if he lived in a dispensary district he gets, without making any pay-.
ment at all, the services of what is in fact
a panel doctor. That doctor is provided
for him under the auspices of the Imperial
Ministry of Pensions. After all, the
treatment of disabled soldiers is not a
matter in which we can interfere, but the
Health Insurance Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Mr. Leslie]
point arises, is such a man to be required
to pay an additional penny on his card
with an additional penny from the employer. In the case of a disabled soldier,
who already gets every advantage that
this Bill can possibly confer upon him, is
another twopence per week to be paid in
respect of that man, and if so, what for?
Finally, has this Parliament any right to
impose that condition on a man who was
disabled in the service of his country
during the War? I do not know whether
there is anything in the Imperial Act of
1924 dealing with the point, because that
Act of 1924-I mean the Imperial Act,
the Act we call the principal Act-is of
some length, and for the reasons I have
mentioned several times already I have not
had time to study that Act.
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews): In the main the hon. and
learned Senator is quite right in what he
said, but I would point out to him what
the position really is. A disabled ex-
Serviceman who remains unemployed will
continue to get benefit. In other words,
he will get medical benefit without payment, but if the disabled ex-Serviceman
becomes employed, for the weeks in which
he is employed his card will have to be
stamped. I may say that as far as I know
they are quite satisfied with the position,
and for this reason. They feel that when
they are not employed they are going to
get all the advantages which they had
before, and when they are employed, and
when it is probable that they are not only
receiving wages but also a disablity
pension, they will be in a position to pay
a penny more per week. It is reasonable
also that their employer should pay a
penny a week, and I hope the hon. and
learned Senator will be satisfied with this
explanation. I think the arrangement is
reasonable and quite fair to disabled ex-
Servicemen whom we all want to treat
with consideration and even generosity.
The arrangement which has been made
here is somewhat more generous than the
arrangement that has been made for ex-
Servicemen in Great Britain. The benefits which they have been receiving in the
past in the way of medical treatment were not given to them for their
lifetime, as we all know. They might be
terminated at any time, while the arrangement which has been incorporated in
this provision will ensure for them at
least as good a service as other insured
persons will have, and it will be given to
them upon more generous terms, because
even if they are not employed the fact that
they are disabled will entitle them to
benefit.
Mr. LESLIE: My point is that at the
moment a disabled man whose pension has
been stabilised is under Royal Warrant
entitled to this treatment whether he is
employed or unemployed. The effect of
the Bill will be to impose on the employer an additional burden of a penny
a week. The employer, I think, scarcely
comes into the matter, because whatever
necesssities he may be under in general
cases I imagine that ex-Servicemen will
get preferential treatment. The fact remains that at the moment a man,
whether employed or unemployed, is
getting without payment all he can
hope to
this
Is
get under
Bill.
that not the case? That is the point
which was put to me by an ex-Serviceman
who suffered rather severely during the
War, but who is at the moment in employment, in employment which, I have reason
to believe, is likely to prove permanent.
But whether employed or unemployed, he
is getting the treatment; and why is he
to be asked to pay something for it? As
regards the general position, much as we
desire to help ex-Servicemen, we have to
remember that the duty towards ex-
Servicemen is not so much an individual
duty as it is the duty of us all. Therefore there is no excuse for putting any
burden, however trifling, on any individual. I should still like an answer to
the main point, that employed or unemployed the man is already getting benefit.
Mr. ANDREWS: To summarise what
I have already said, it simply means that
when the ex-Serviceman is not working
he is not asked to contribute anything.
When he is working and receiving wages,
and in addition to wages receiving a disability pension, he will be asked to contribute one penny, and so far as I have heard
no disabled ex-Serviceman objects. A
great many of them have said to me personally that they are quite satisfied. When
they are fortunate enough to have both
wages and disablement pension they are
quite willing to give a little to help the
fund from which other ex-Servicemen who
may not be so fortunate are receiving
benefit, as well as the working people
generally, of whom the ex-Servicemen
form a part.
393 Health Insurance Bill-

4 JUNE 1930
Committee.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I think after
the Minister's very clear explanation, my
hon. and learned Friend will be quite
satisfied. It seems to me the ex-Servicemen are acting very honourably in this
matter of helping other people.
Mr. DORMAN: At this stage I would
like to ask the Minister if that pension
will be taken into consideration in the
disqualification of £250. There may be at
very few comparatively young men who
may be in receipt of disablement pension,
and who may prosper and attain an income
of £200. Will their disablement pension
be included in the £250 limit which is a
disqualification?
Mr. ANDREWS: Answering on the
spur
of the moment, I have no hesitation
in saying it ought not to, and I do not
think it is.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Modification of s. 24 of the
principal Act as to administration of
medical benefit).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask the
right hon. Gentleman for a little further
explanation as regards, I may say, page 5
generally. If the Committee looks at
paragraphs number (i), (ii) and (iii), they
will see that we start by saying if the
Ministry, after such inquiry, is satisfied.
In the next paragraph it is, if the Minister
is satisfied after inquiry, and in the next,
if the Minister is satisfied, apparently
without inquiry at all. I think, perhaps,
the right hon. Gentleman will explain a
little more. I may mention I just had
time to discover that the words of this
Clause did not originate in Northern
Ireland. They are to be found in Section
24, of which this is substantially a
re-enactment.
Mr. ANDREWS: It is proposed that
the Ministry shall hold an inquiry, not
through the medium of the officials of the
Ministry but that we will have two doctors
as assistants to the chairman of a committee which will be specially set up when
occasion requires. The chairman should
be a gentleman of legal experience, probably a barrister-at-law. That will be held
as a final inquiry. But I may say that in
the first instance all complaints will be
reviewed by the local committee, and in
the second instance they will be
reviewed by the medical benefit council,
and it is only in a case where agreement
is not arrived at and there is still reason
for doubt that the special inquiry will be
set up.
It will not be a Departmental
inquiry. It will be an inquiry held by
those who are considered most competent
to hold such an inquiry.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSES 5 (Medical benefit council.
and local committees), 6 (Modification of
provisions of the principal Act as to
medical and pharmaceutical committees),
7 (Remuneration of medical practitioners
holding office as dispensary medical
officers), and 8 (Amendment of s. 2 of 33
and 34 Vic., c. 26, with respect to sales of
poisons on medical prescriptions), ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Sick visiting scheme).
Mr. LESLIE: Dealing with the sick
visiting scheme it is very doubtful, I
think, what the sick visiting scheme is,
what it is going to do or who is
going to do it. I could not myself see
any indication as to who was going to
do it, and I was rather inclined to wonder whether it is merely meant to relate
to attendance by the panel doctor at the
patient's house, or whether it is intended
to relate to some philanthropic district
visitor. In fact we are not told anything about it one way or the other, except that there will be expense incurred
in connection with the scheme, and that
will be met out of money provided by
Parliament. I am sure the right hon.
Gentleman will be able to tell us something.
Mr. ANDREWS: The expense will be
borne out of the funds of approved
societies, and the reason the sick visiting
scheme is not definitely defined is that
no decision has actually been come to
as to how best a scheme of the kind could
be brought into operation. The idea
underlying the provision is this. At the
present time some societies have got sick
visitors for the purpose of going to the
homes of those who have been certified
by the doctors as being sick, and seeing
whether their illness is such as to prevent
the beneficiary from doing any work
which he could be considered reasonably
capable of doing. It is really a check
on malingering. That is the idea underlying a sick visiting scheme of any kind.
395 Health Insurance Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
[Mr. Andrews]
I
As I have said, some societies have at
the present time quite efficient sick
visiting schemes. Other societies have
what may be called moderately satisfactory visiting schemes, while there are
others which have got none at all.
am sure the Committee will appreciate
that that leads to inequalities of administration which really cannot be defended,
and the powers given to the Ministry
that
simply mean
we can co-ordinate
them. In the first place, it is hoped that
between now and July a year hencethat is about 14 months after time has
been given, the approved societies will
come together themselves and design a
scheme which will ensure equality of administration. If the societies do that
and the scheme is satisfactory to the
Ministry the Ministry will not have to
act at all. If, on the other hand, the
societies are not able to agree amongst
themselves, or for any reason they do not
produce a satisfactory scheme, power is
given here to the Ministry to bring in a
scheme which will ensure equality of administration. I may say it is not my
intention in connection with the matter
to act as dictator even at the end of nine
or twelve months, but in the first instance
to ask the societies to meet me and discuss the matter with me so that I may be
able through negotiations to reconcile
their different views and in that way get
a scheme which will be satisfactory and
meet what I really think to be a great
need at the present time.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman has not answered the question I
asked whether this visit is a medical
visit, or who the visitors are to be. That
is not answered. What the right hon.
Gentleman has admitted seems to me
rather an argument against the Bill as a
whole. He has admitted that some of the
approved societies have no arrangements
whatever for checking malingering. The
complaint they seem to have made was
that this malingering was putting an
undue burden upon them. One would
think that approved societies would have
put their house in order before coming to
Parliament for additional contributions.
That does not arise so much. The main
point is, is this visiting to be done by
doctors.
Mr. ANDREWS: I am afraid I did
not make myself quite clear to the hon.
The
The sick
and learned Senator. I hope to be able
to do so. In the first place, I did not
say the societies had no check at the
The first
present time on malingering.
check is the medical practitioner.
second check is the sick visitor.
visitor is not a medical man. The sick
visitor is some person, male or female,
who is appointed to go to the homes of
the people for the purpose of seeing
whether the person who is receiving disablement benefit or sickness benefit as the
case may be is confined to bed, or able
only to hobble about, or whether, as sometimes occurs, when the sick visitor comes
to the home he finds the general housework of the home is being done by the
sick person.
On occasions even we have
known where the person drawing sickness
benefit was planting potatoes or working
at a plough. It is not the only check on
malingering; it is the secondary check,
which very seldom as a matter of fact has
to be brought into operation, but it is
desirable that the public should appreciate it is there if required.
Mr. DORMAN: I wish to thank the
right hon. Gentleman for his lucid explanation, because I think it will place
the comparatively small trade unions and
friendly societies in Northern Ireland in
a position of equality so far as a check
on malingering is concerned with the
large international societies. Much of
our disadvantages and backwardness in
comparison with these international
societies, I believe, is due to lack of some
co-ordinating system such as the Minister
proposes in this Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I would just like to
say from personal experience there is a
good check upon any person when he comes
upon the sick benefit. Unfortunately,
some weeks ago I had to attend a doctor
myself, and the sick visitor visited the
House the following day. A fortnight
after that the sick visitor came again, and
the sick visitor has been coming practically since. The doctor when he had
been six weeks with me began to inquire
if
my knee was any better, and, of course,
I told him it was not, but the following
week I got a notification from the insurance company to appear before the referee
doctor of the Ministry of Labour. He
gave me a thorough examination. As far
as trying to get as much as you can out
of the insurance company it is impossible,
because the insured person is well watched.
397 Health Insurance Bill--

4 JUNE 1930
Committee.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: I
think it is a very wise thing on the part
of the Ministry to try to allow the
societies to arrange this visiting scheme
themselves. There is no doubt that in
some societies you will find a little
malingering. Large organisations such
as the Orange and Protestant Society and
the Presbyterian Society, in which there.
are a considerable number of women, have
good visiting schemes, but in the ordinary.
trade union there is no need for any
such visiting scheme. I am pretty well.
acquainted with the Engineers' Society,
and the only visit in their case is the day
when the sick visitor comes along with the
sick money. He generally makes a point
to go when the person is supposed to be
indoors, and if he finds the person out
there may be a complaint. That is a
very lax or careless visiting scheme, because as a rule, there is no malingering
or scarcely any amongst these people.
One has to remember in the case of a
large organisation in which there is a
large number of women the visit is not
altogether necessary to prevent malingering, it is also necessary to prevent a sick
person who is recovering starting work too
soon. A woman with a family, who has
recovered her health to some extent and
is likely in a short time to be able to resume her employment, is very liable to
start baking bread or do some house work.
The sick visitor attending in a case like
that prevents the person from starting
work too soon-starting home duties that
might be injurious to complete recovery.
I think it is well that the societies should
arrange for a visiting scheme of their own.
They know their own people; they know
the visiting scheme which will be the most
effective not only for malingering, but
also for preventing honest cases resuming
household duties too soon.
Mr. LESLIE: There has been a prolonged discussion on the Clause when
there is no substantive Motion before the
Committee, but, I think, the explanations.
we have now received rather take us on a
side track. The right hon. Gentleman
explained that it is the intention of the
Clause to make it easy for the approved
societies to check malingering, and as my
hon. Friend says that it is all right and
quite in order, I am afraid that the
Clause is liable to misunderstanding outside. I must ask the right hon. Gentleman when we can find it stated in the
case of a sick person who claims to be sick
and desires the attendance of a panel
doctor, that the panel doctor will go to
the patient or that the patient must be
required to go to the panel doctor.
thought, perhaps, we might get an answer
to that under this Clause, but now it
transpires that the Clause does not deal
with it. I am afraid I do not know what
Clause it comes under.
Mr. ANDREWS: I am not just in the
position at the moment to name the
Clause, but I will certainly inquire into
the matter, and at a further stage of this
Measure tell my hon. and learned Friend
the exact Clause. I may say that the
whole underlying basis of the scheme,
not only here but also in England, is that
the doctor shall give efficient attendance
to the patient, and it will be to the
interest of the doctor so to do, because the
insured person is entitled to choose his
own doctor. If the attendance is not
satisfactory, obviously the insured person
will change to another doctor. The whole
idea underlying the Clause is simply that
it is necessary that the doctor shall attend
in the home of the insured sick person.
On the other hand, when the illness is not
so serious and that will not be necessary,
the patient will go to the doctor..
I say in case of any possible misunderstanding that there is no intention of
depriving any person entitled to it from
benefit under any of the provisions of this
Bill. The only object of the whole
Measure is to provide benefit for those
who are entitled to it and who require it.
The Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

(Provisions as officers of insurance committees) ordered to stand part of the

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in page 16, line 18, at end add the
following new sub-section:
(4) In Part II of the First Schedule
to the Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions Act (Northern
Ireland), 1925 (which relates to rates
of contributions payable in respect of
persons employed within the meaning
of the principal Act, who have attained
the age of sixty-five), ninepence shall
be substituted for eightpence and sevenpence shall be substituted for sixpence
as from the seventh day of July, nineteen hundred and thirty.
This is purely a consequential Amendment of the Contributory Pensions Act,
1925, necessitated by the raising of the
rate of contribution for health insurance
purposes.
Contributions in respect of persons over
65 years of age in employment are not
paid for health insurance, but Section 9
(1) of the Contributory Pensions Act,
1925, and Part II of the First Schedule
require the payment in respect of such
persons of contributions into the Pension
Fund corresponding to the rates of contribution payable by the employer for
health insurance purposes and pensions
Hitherto these conpurposes combined.
tributions have been at the rate of 8d for
men and 6d for women; these rates should
now become 9d and 7d.
Mr. LESLIE: This sub-clause is perhaps thrown at us. I do not think any
of us saw it until we came here this afternoon. I suppose we may take it that
the Government is satisfied that the proposal is one not only within the competence of Pariament but within the
competence of this House. I suppose the
Government is also satisfied that the
money having got into the Health Insurance Fund there is authority to retain
it there. The Clause by itself is a little
difficult to appreciate.
Mr. ANDREWS: I may say it is quite
within the powers of this House to deal
with this matter, because it does not
carry with it a State grant. It simply is
a matter which affects the employer, who
is obliged to pay even after the insured
person becomes 65 years of age and no
longer is entitled to health insurance
benefits, as we have known in the past.
Those over 65 will continue to get medical
benefits for life. The real object of the
Clause is to ensure that the employer does
not escape paying contributions for his
worker by simply employing a worker
over 65 years of age. The pennies will go.
to the Pension Fund, and will be available
for the general services.
Mr. DORMAN: I would like to ask
the right hon. Gentleman if those who
are at present▬▬

Member speak up? The Press cannot
hear him, and I cannot hear him.
Mr. DORMAN: I am sorry,
suffering from my throat.
I am
The CHAIRMAN: I am sorry, I did
not know that.
Mr. DORMAN: I would like to ask
the right hon. Gentleman if those who are
at present in receipt of contributory pensions will be entitled to receive medical
treatment under this Bill?
Mr. ANDREWS: Yes, all persons who
were insured prior to reaching 65 will be
entitled to medical benefit though they no
longer are entitled to health insurance
benefits.
Mr. DORMAN: Thank you.
Mr. LESLIE: Going back to what
happened before, my point is a rather
constitutional one, and I do not think the
Minister of Labour dealt with it in introducing the matter of a State grant.
The requirement of the Government of
Ireland Act is that this House shall not
so amend a Measure as to increase the
burden on the people. It seems to me it
is an open question whether this proposal
is not one increasing the burden on the
people.
Education Bill-
Third Reading
4 JUNE 1930
The CHAIRMAN: I should point out
that it is a question entirely for the
Speaker of this House as to whether an
Amendment is allowable or not. The
Minister of Labour answered the question
more clearly than I could. I had already
considered it, and come to the conclusion,
that the Amendment was permissible.
Amendment agreed to.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand

:

Education Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."
Mr. CAMPBELL: I would like to say
a few words in bidding good-bye to this
Bill in the Senate. It has been said
that we on the Nationalist side of the
House have not done the Government
justice in regard to this Measure, but we
in reply can ask have they done us justice.
It reminds me of the story of a man with
a rueful countenance, who went to a
painter and sat for his portrait. He said,
"I hope you will do me justice." The
painter looked upon him and rather
startled him by saying, "Justice! It is
mercy you need." (Laughter.) Then
we have heard a great deal in the course
of the discussions on this Measure that if
we bow down to the fetish of public
control of our schools, we will get 20s.
in the £ of the debt due to us.
we will get 20/- in the £ of the debt due
to us.
"Public control" is a delusive phrase
here. Like the word "liberty" many
wrongs have been committed in its name.
"Public control of what?" The Ministry
and its officers have the fullest control of
every wheel and cog in our educational
machinery except religious teaching.
Control of that you will never getnever. No matter what comes or goes, no
matter what is in store for us, no matter
if we have to go back to the penal times
and the hedge school, we shall not compromise on that.
The history of other
other communities
teaches us that the streams of religion run
high or low according as the banks of the
schools are well or ill-built. May I say
that what we claim we claim not for ourselves, but for all voluntary schools. We
are not alone in our conviction that when
religion goes out of the school morality
goes out, order goes out and the selflessness, which is the charm of life, goes out.
Some hon. Members
Members harp on their
principles of civil and religious liberty.
May I say that if those principles had
a real meaning for you, if they were not
an empty formula, they would have been
an absolute bar to your putting Catholic
schools in an inferior position to others.
What answer has been made to the
argument with regard to repairs? Does
that argument admit of an answer? The
forces of unreason, and they are many,
know there is none.
The denial of repairs harms us and helps you not
a jot. This Bill is not the last
word on this question. The noble Lord,
the Leader of the House said the other
day that he did not care for the word
"perpetuity," in regard to some points he
preferred to say, until the next amending Bill comes along. This Bill is not
the last word. It might have been. Had
statesmen been in the place of the
Government, they would have striven
for educational peace with honour,
for a settlement which would have
made the fullest use of the intellectual powers of all, Catholic and Protestant
alike, and made equally available to all
those stores of knowledge which add so
much to the happiness of life and are such
a solace in its trials. That statesmanship
and that settlement are reserved for other
men in times to come.
Education Bill
Finance Bill
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: We are parting with this
Bill, and I should rather like in bidding
it good-bye to express my best hopes for its
success. I cannot but feel, as indeed I
have indicated before, that it is a Bill
which has introduced us to fresh woods
and pastures new. I only hope that it will
not be found that those pastures have been
sown with dragons' teeth. I am very
much afraid that Clauses 1, 4 and 10 do
contain the germs of future discord, and
I can anly hope that the commonsense of
the education authorities and school
management committees, as well as of the
other people interested in education, either
as parents of the children or as pastors
and masters of the parents of the children,
I hope that the united common sense of
all sections of the community may render
this Bill a success in the future, and perhaps postpone for a very long time, if not
in perpetuity, the necessity of coming back
to Parliament for yet another Amending
Bill. I hope it will give the peace which
it was introduced to ensure.
As no
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
voice has been raised in opposition to the
Third Reading of this Bill, I do not think
it would be proper for me to enter into
any argument raised by the hon. and
learned Senator who spoke first and the
hon. and learned Senator who followed,
but I would like to re-echo the hope of
the latter that commonsense may be
brought into contact with any future
difficulties that may arise in the working
of the Education Act. This particular
Bill, to which we are now giving the Third
Reading, is a compromise. It is not expected that by it any party should be
absolutely satisfied, and in educational
matters I am afraid that that is an impossibility. Though I cannot claim with
any amount of reason that it is a Bill to
end all Bills, I can claim that I have given
great attention to it during the past two
years. We have endeavoured to steer a
middle course between the contending
parties, and so I re-echo the hopes of the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
that it will work well, and that the new
members of the various committees will
show commonsense in working its Clauses.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass?"
(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to inform them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill with Amendments to which they desire their concurrence.
Finance Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Viscount BANGOR: Before moving
the Third Reading of this Bill, I think I
can now give the hon. and learned Member (Senator Leslie) the information he
was asking for in regard to the cost of
licences for drivers of public service
vehicles.
Clause 5 of the present Bill raises the
drivers' licences to 10/-, except in regard
to the class of vehicles mentioned in the
proviso to that Clause, that is the commercial and the public service and
hackney drivers. They will continue to
pay only 5/- for the ordinary licence. But
the public service drivers will still be
liable for the additional licence not exceeding 5/- under Section 12, Sub-section
3: Motor Vehicles' Traffic and Regulations Act (Northern Ireland), 1926,
which in this particular is not affected by
the present Bill. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I must express my
thanks to the noble Viscount for looking
into that matter. The difficulty really
arose from the celerity with which we took
the Bill, and, therefore, I had not time to
refresh my memory as to the exact scope
of those paragraphs in the Schedule to the
Finance Act, 1920, which were referred
to. The first time we did not recognise
that the hackney carriage mentioned included a public service vehicle. I am
very much obliged to the noble Viscount.
I am sure the public will be interested in
getting a definite pronouncement on the
subject.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: When the
Leader of the House was moving the
Third Reading of the Education Bill he
said it would not be necessary for him
to reply to any opposition because there
was really no great opposition, but I do
oppose this Bill on account of the
schedules of taxation on page 5. line 34,
which were only put into our hands today. I think it was a most unfair thing
that the farmers were allowed to buy their
Ford ton lorries on the understanding
4 JUNE 1930
Finance Bill-

Third Reading
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Is it not in my
power to oppose it on the one Clause
alone?
Mr. SPEAKER: It is in your power
to vote for its rejection. The House cannot amend the Bill, but it can reject it as
a whole.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I move its
rejection.
that the taxation was to be £16 on vehicles
up to 25 cwt. Those lorries were built
to weigh from 21 cwt. to 22 cwt., and
now that they have got them the taxation
is to be increased by 50 per cent., from
£16 to £24. I think it is a most unreasonable proposition. I do not object
to a man who is keeping his lorry, let
it be what weight it may, for hire having
to pay the extra tax as he can charge
an increased price for the hire, but I hold
that the lorries used solely for haulage
in connection with agriculture should not
be increased, and instead of the tax being
£24 it should be only £6. Horses were
put off the roads owing to the roads being
made too slippery for them. Then these
men got the Ford lorries, as I have stated,
under 25 cwt. and now before they have
got them a couple of years the taxation
is increased by 50 per cent. I oppose
this Bill on these grounds unless the
Leader of the House can tell that these
motors will be classified under the heading
of vehicles for haulage used solely in connection with agricuture.
If he can do
so I will not press my opposition.
Colonel PAKENHAM: There seems to
be something in Senator Hill Dickson's
contention, and it does seem rather hard
when, as he has stated, a farmer has
bought perfectly openly a Ford ton lorry
on which there is a tax of £16 to find
suddenly that the tax has been raised
by 50 per cent. It does seem rather a
heavy incubus to put on a farmer.
farmer is as honest as anybody else, but
he is not making too much money, and
it does seem a tremendous tax to put on
him, especially when, as the hon. Senator
says, horses cannot now use the roads. I
join with the hon. Senator in protesting
against this taxation.
The
Mr. LONG: I would also like to
associate myself with the protest made by
Senator Hill Dickson. However, I am
very much afraid that we have no power
to amend this Bill in this House now. We
can only reject it, and I am sure we will
hesitate to do that. I should like to be
associated with the protest.
Mr. LESLIE: On a point of Order.
Do I understand the rejection of the Bill
has been moved?
Mr. SPEAKER: It is not quite clear
that Senator Hill Dickson moved the
rejection of the Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: The position is that there
is a Motion before us, and I am not
debarred from speaking on the subject.
Mr. SPEAKER: That is so.
Mr. LESLIE: I have really very little
to say except to appeal to my hon. Friend
opposite not to try to upset the entire
financial arrangements of the Province on
account of what is a comparatively small
point which might have been taken in
another place, in the originating chamber.
As a matter of fact I think the matter
was raised in another place. I hope the
hon. Member (Mr. Hill Dickson) will not
think it necessary to proceed with his
Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I venture to make
a sporting offer to the hon. Senator who
has moved the Amendment.
Mr. SPEAKER: I did not catch what
the hon. and learned Member said.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I venture to make
a sporting offer to the hon. Senator who
has moved the Amendment, namely, that
if he is prepared to take the consequences
and to face the responsibility of moving
the rejection of the Bill, some of us will
consider the possibility of standing by
him in the matter. It is a very heavy
responsibility.
Viscount BANGOR: With reference to
the remarks of the hon .Senator (Mr. Hill
Dickson), one of the answers given in the
Commons on this matter was that there
was a lot of abuse of the old regulations
by owners of 25 cwt. lorries. Farmers
and others put sides on their lorries and
brought them over 25 cwt., although they
continued to pay only £16. That, I
understand, was the reason for this particular change, and under this Bill that
abuse will now disappear. In any case,
as has been pointed out, this Bill cannot
be amended. It can only be rejected.

Motion for Adjournment.
BUSINESS OF THE HOUSE.
I beg to
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
move, "That this House do now adjourn.'
For to-morrow's sitting, when the final
stages of the legislation mentioned to-day
will have to be passed, I would ask if
the House would be agreeable to sit at
Two o'clock, instead of a Quarter past
Three. Another place sits at Three
o'clock and if they do not receive the
legislation from this House they will be
put to a certain amount of inconvenience.
I do not think putting forward the time
of sitting by an hour and a quarter will
affect hon. Members of this House to any
undue extent.
The House indicated assent.
Mr. LESLIE: Though nobody wants
to make a fuss about this matter, it does
make the rather serious difference that
people living in the north who travel up
here by train cannot get here by two
o'clock unless they start, as in the case
of the City of Derry, for example, at
about half-past seven in the morning.
Even people living somewhat nearer, like
myself, have to start about a quarter past
nine. The objection is that you have to
leave home before you have any hope of
getting your letters.
Colonel
aeroplane.
PAKENHAM:
Travel by
Mr. LESLIE: The trouble about that
is that there is not, as far as I know, any
satisfactory landing place in the near
vicinity of this House for aeroplanes.
Otherwise one might adopt that course.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I quite see
the objection of the hon. and learned
Member (Mr. Leslie), but I think that
for to-morrow it is unlikely that we will
have in attendance many hon. Senators
who are not present to-day. I quite
agree that, in order to get here at two
o'clock, you have to leave home at an
inordinately early hour in the morning,
because Irish trains do not go very fast.
I do not know that there is a later train
that gets in between two and three
o'clock. I admit that hon. Senators have
been hurried in the matter of legislation
that has been before them to-day, but
we cannot interfere with the fact now,
and for that reason I would suggest that
we meet to-morrow at two o'clock.
Mr. GLENDINNING: As far as I am
concerned I shall remain in Belfast all
night and so it will not inconvenience me.
Indeed, as a matter of fact, it may convenience me as I may get an earlier train
home. I am aware, however, that my
hon. Friend (Mr. Long) has of necessity
to go home to-night, but whether he will
oppose the suggestion or not I do not
know.
Mr. LONG: No. It only means that I
leave at half-past seven.

Twenty minutes after

Business of the House.
Business of the House.
5 JUNE 1930
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 5th June, 1930.
The Senate met at Two of the Clock,
Mr. SPEAKER in the Chair.
Business of the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That Standing Order No. XXV
be suspended for this day's sitting, for the
purpose of taking the Third Reading of
the National Health Insurance Bill."
Mr. LESLIE: It will be within the
recollection of some Members of this House
that you, yourself, Mr. Speaker, have
remarked that Standing Orders are meant
to be observed. It seems to me that one
may refer to that on any proposal to
suspend the Standing Orders. This proposal which the noble Viscount brings.
before us this afternoon is the culmination
of the proceedings of the last ten days or
So.
During those last ten days this House
has been asked to sit on a Friday and a
Monday, neither of which days are days
on which we are accustomed to sit, and we
have been asked to do that at a certain
amount of sacrifice of personal convenience. Personal convenience cannot be
regarded very seriously in a matter of this
kind. The public interest must come first,
but I do not know that it is in accordance
with the public interest that the normal
tenor of the course of this House should
be interrupted, and it is certainly not in
the public interest that our work should
be made more difficult than it is.
It happens that during the period I
have spoken of we have had in this House
three Bills of a somewhat difficult character all going on, in one sense of the word,
at once. Therefore, these proceedings are
not entirely due to the particular Bill to
which the Motion is directed. The whole
thing must be taken together. The difficulty of the work comes in in this way.
We have to take stages of Bills before we
have seen the printed copy of the Debates
on the preceding stage, a matter to which
I have more than once directed attention,
and it is a matter of some importance.
We have to proceed before it has been
possible for most of us who live in the
country to receive the printed notice of
the business that is to come before us. If
it happens that the House is to be in
Committee, any Amendments which we
may have put down cannot be seen by
hon. Members, and cannot be considered
either by the back bench member or by
the Government itself.
As hon. Members know, there are
Amendments of different classes. There
is the Amendment which it is hoped to
insert in the Bill, and there is the other,
and sometimes more important Amendment, which is put down in order that
some particular subject may be ventilated.
The discussions on that class of Amendment are often, I think it is agreed, of
very great value, not, perhaps, always at
the time, but later on. Then if we go at
this pace and suspend Standing Orders it
follows that the time for consideration, not
only in this House but outside, is very
materially curtailed. It is scarcely possible for hon. Members of this House to
consider three Bills all at once if there is
not sufficient time between each stage. It
has been suggested, I know, that Members
of this House can follow the Bills as
introduced in another place. That is
almost impossible. It sometimes happens
--and one of the Bills I am speaking of is
rather an outstanding example that a
really important Amendment is introduced
in Committee in another place, and until
the Bill is completed at the next stage no
outsider can possibly appreciate what has
occurred.
I do not want to oppose the Motion this
afternoon, because I think it will be
agreed that the time for opposition was
yesterday, when the noble Viscount intimated to us that we must sit to-day at
two o'clock instead of the customary hour.
That was the time if one had wished to be
obstructive-I will not go so far as to say
obstructive, but unfavourable. But
although I do not oppose the Motion, I do
ask and claim that there should be some
reciprocity about this matter. If this
House is perpetually to sacrifice its own
convenience those who sit elsewhere should
take steps to meet us. And it is not only
the other House. Something, I think,
might be done by the postal authorities.
That is a matter not under our control,
although we are of necessity brought into
contact with it. I have here the wrapper
of the Order Paper for yesterday. That
Order Paper reached me at ten o'clock
this morning, dealing with the events of
yesterday. Yesterday the House sat at
Business of the House
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
3-15, and I observe from the postmark
that the Order Paper was sent to me at
3-45. That ought not to be.
an
no
Again, passing from the Post Office,
there is surely a possibility of the Departments getting on with their Bills rather
more quickly than they do. Most of the
Bills brought before us are of a Departmental character, and surely it would be
possible to accelerate the proceedings.
there. The root cause of the difficulties
in that direction is the practice which has
grown up across the water of late years,
and which we have followed, of holding
autumn session. Were there
autumn session, and were it the practice
of Parliament to meet earlier in the year
than 11th March, and were the session
protracted, perhaps a little over Whitsuntide recess— -I think perhaps if it was
possible to do that and have no autumn
session at all, it would allow six months
for the preparation of legislation, and
then, perhaps, both Houses would have
as much time as we ourselves had here
with the Bills brought before us at an
earlier period of the session. As I say,
I will not oppose actively the Motion of
the noble Viscount, but I do think that
what I have said should receive consideration, not only consideration from the
noble Viccount-I am quite sure that our
claims are always supported by him as
Leader of the House. It is rather to the
Senators I would appeal that they should
support the Leader of the House either
tacitly in one sense or actively in another.
By opposing his proposals they should
support him in order to make clear that
they should be allowed proper time to do
their work.
Mr. LONG: I also do not oppose the
Motion before the House, but I should
just like to emphasise what the hon.
Senator, who has just spoken, has said
as to the unseemly haste with which this
Bill has been rushed through this House
It appears to me that the time is inadequate for the proper consideration of the
Measure, and such haste is not, in my
opinion, becoming the dignity of this
House. I had fully intended after the
division on the Second Reading to prepare
two Amendments, and I had them partially prepared, also a Sub-clause, but I
found I was too late. I should, therefore, suggest to those who are responsible
for the business of this House, and for its
arrangements, that they should, if possible, give more time. I know there are
difficulties, but one way of avoiding the
difficulty would be to postpone the
adjournment of this House until a later
date and so give us ample time.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I only received the
Order of the Day since I came into the
Senate, and I only received to-day the
Order of the Day for yesterday. I
received the latter about 1-30 to-day, the
House having risen some 22 hours previously. I think my hon. Friend, Mr.
McAllister, is in an even worse position,
as he has not yet received yesterday's
Order Paper. It was only by a most
fortunate juncture of circumstances that
I picked him up down town at Castle Junction or his Motion to-day might have disappeared without having been heard. 1
have not the least fault to find with the
Leader of the House. He always suits
the convenience of Senators, and we are
most grateful to him on every side of the
House. (Hon. Senators: Hear, hear.)
I do not say any fault can be found
anywhere. I am certain that the officials
do their very best. It may be unavoidable, but perhaps it is no harm to mention these things.
To take
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
the least important matter first-perhaps
it is of more general importance because
it is always present-about the postal arrangements for the Orders of the Day, it
appears to me there is a good deal of
difficulty. Any letter, as far as I know,
leaving Belfast and posted any time before six o'clock is unlikely to arrive in the
country next morning until on the average somewhere between nine and ten
o'clock. My own letters reach me, as a
matter of fact, about 11. Hon. Members
coming up for the day are almost certain,
if they live more than thirty miles away
from Belfast, to have to leave before the
post comes in. I know of no expedition,
except possibly an air-mail in the next
twenty years or so, that will get over the
difficulty. I should be very pleased to
receive representations, but I do not know
how it can be done. The assumption
must be that hon. Senators attend every
day and hear the date of the next sitting
mentioned at the Adjournment, and that
they will remember it even if it entails
having to keep it in their minds for something like 20 hours. I do not know of
any other way it can be done.
Ballymena Electricity Order 414
Business of the House
5 JUNE 1930
I have an idea which, I think, can be
brought in next session. At the first
sitting I only move the Adjournment until such time as Senators meet again.
Perhaps if I was to give some idea of
the days on which the House would meet
in the following week on my first announcement it might obviate some of the
difficulty. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
With regard to the post I do not think
it would be possible to do anything.
With regard to the premier Motion before us about the haste with which this
particular Bill was being put through
this House I am unable to disagree with
anything put forward by the hon. and
learned Senator except one thing to which
I take slight exception, and that is, that
the convenience of this House or of individual Members of this House with regard to sitting on Mondays or Fridays
should interfere with the public convenience of getting legislation through. After
all, if it is logical or right to sit on Tuesday or Wednesday or Thursday, there is
no particular reason why we should not
be called to sit on Monday and Friday.
So far we have resisted the temptation of
calling this House for Saturday or Sunday, but if I found it necessary in future
I am afraid I should move that we should
sit on Sunday.
Mr. CAMPBELL: On Sunday?
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: So far
occasion has not arisen, and I think it
is highly improbable, and as the hon. and
learned Senator opposite has shown himself so much shocked, I do not think I
will go into it. At the same time, if I
thought the public interest required it,
Saturday afternoon would be a possibility.
With regard to the Motion, a revising
second chamber like this is always liable
to be overrun with legislation at the end
of the session. I have heard the same
complaints raised in the House of Lords,
and I have no doubt the same thing would
apply to the French Senate and the Italian
chamber or any other revisionary chamber
to be found on the globe.
The Government, in arranging the timetable, are very much in the position of a
cook who hopes to produce a dish made of
various ingredients. The misbehaviour of
one ingredient may upset the whole of the
rest of the dish, and the plums of legislation which the cook hoped to divert
through the whole pudding may be all
collected in one corner and have to be
swallowed in a mouthful at the end.
It
is, I think, a very unfortunate fact that
that is so. The only thing I can possibly
say in defence of my Motion to suspend
Standing Orders is what the hon. and
learned Senator alluded to that for those
who really objected the proper and logical
way was to have objected to the Committee
stage being taken yesterday. To object to
Standing Orders being suspended to-day
in order to take the Third Reading is like
a man who has submitted to the amputation of both legs objecting to a haircut.
(Laughter.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Urban District of Omagh Electricity
Order.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,
"That the Special Order made by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland, under the Electricity (Supply)
Acts, 1882 to 1919, and confirmed by the
Ministry of Commerce under the Electricity (Supply) Act, 1919, in respect of
the Urban District of Omagh, and certain
adjoining parts of the Rural District of
Omagh, in the County of Tyrone, be
approved."
This is a Special Order granted by the
Electricity Commissioners of Northern
Ireland, and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce, conferring upon the Urban
District Council of Omagh statutory authority to supply electrical energy for public.
and private purposes in the Urban District
of Omagh and certain portions of the adjoining Rural District of Omagh, in
County Tyrone. The rules of procedure
have been complied with and the Order
follows the usual form.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Urban District of Ballymena Electricity Order.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,
That the Special Order made by
the Electricity Commissioners for
Northern Ireland, under the Electricity (Supply) Acts, 1882 to 1919,
and confirmed by the Ministry of Commerce under the Electricity (Supply) Act,
1919, in respect of the Urban District of
