Health Insurance Bill—
Report of Amendment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Bangor]
This is a special order granted by the
Electricity Commissioners of Northern
Ireland, confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce, conferring upon the Urban
District Council of Ballymena statutory
authority to supply electrical energy for
public and private purposes in the urban
district of Ballymena, in County Antrim.
The rules of procedure have been complied
with and the order follows the usual form.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
IMPORTATION OF HACKNEY CAR-
RIAGES INTO THE IRISH FREE
STATE.
Major DOBBS: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House if he is aware that
hackney motor carriages are only allowed.
the privilege of temporary importation
into the Irish Free State for a stay of
48 hours, and then only between 1st June
and 2nd October of each year, and
whether he will make representations to
the Imperial Parliament with a view to
obtaining from the Irish Free State
greater concessions for these vehicles.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The hon.
and gallant Senator may rest assured that
the matter has not been lost sight of,
but I am unable to make any statement
with regard to it at the present moment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I would like to
make one comment on the answer of the
noble Lord the Leader of the House. I
know of no reason, constitutional, diplomatic, or otherwise, why the representations referred to by the hon, and gallant
Senator in the question just answered
should not be made direct by the Government of Northern Ireland to the Government of the Irish Free State. I do not
see why there should be any need to go to
London in a matter of this kind and why
representations should not be far more
effective if made in a friendly way between one governing body of Irishmen
and another governing body of Irishmen.
National Health Insurance Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendment read.
Moved, "That this Report be now received."--(Viscount Bangor).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 1 (Extension of medical
benefit to Northern Ireland).
(1) Medical benefit shall be included
amongst the benefits conferred upon
insured persons in Northern Ireland by,
and subject to the provisions of, the
National Health Insurance Act, 1924,
as amended by subsequent enactments
(which Act, as so amended, is in this
Act referred to as "the principal Act');
and the principal Act shall have effect
accordingly, subject to the modifications made therein by the provisions
hereinafter contained in this Act.
The following Amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Page 1, line 14, at end ("contained in
this Act") to add: "Provided that an
insured person may at any time, by
declaration made in the form to be prescribed by the Ministry, elect to be excluded
from medical benefit, and in respect of
such person so electing, the provisions of
this Act shall be deemed to be modified
accordingly.
"(2) An election made under the subsection last preceding shall have effect for
five years from the date thereof, and may
be renewed from time to time.'
,,
Mr. LESLIE: I move the Amendment standing in my name as it
appears on the Order Paper, obtainable by hon. Members on entering
the Senate this afternoon. It will be
observed that I have put down this
Amendment at the earliest possible stage,
and that I have couched it in the most
general terms. I have designed it to be
as complete a contracting-out Clause as it
is possible for me to make. I have not
defined the possibility of a distinction
between the ruralist as opposed to the
urbanist, that is, country as opposed to
town. I agree with what was said on the
Second Reading by my hon. Friend
opposite of the absolute impossibility of
making a distinction on those lines on
account of the in-and-out running, so to
speak, between town and country, and the
workers passing from one to the other, but
I have suggested that we should leave the
contracting-out to the individual choice of
the person most concerned, and I have
done that all round.
My Amendment is not limited to the
special case of the ex-Serviceman suffering
from disability, with whom we dealt
yesterday afternoon, but that ex-Serviceman, I mean the ex-Serviceman with
Health Insurance Bill-
Report of Amendment
5 JUNE 1930
a disability pension exceeding 20 per
cent., who already receives treatment as of right under his Majesty's Royal
Warrant equal to anything which he can
possibly obtain under this Bill, and he
receives that treatment without having to
pay a penny for it-that ex-Serviceman
comes in under this Sub-clause, so that if
the Sub-clause be accepted, and if the ex-
Serviceman wishes, as my hon. and gallant
Friend, whom I do not see in his place
to-day, suggested yesterday, to contribute
his penny for the possible advantage of
somebody else he will be at liberty to do
so, but if he does not so desire he will not
be required to pay it. I am myself still
not satisfied that we have any right to
require him to pay this penny, because we
have no right to interfere with his pension.
Passing away from the ex-Serviceman,
there is a question as to which the right
hon. Gentleman, the Minister of Labour,
will inform me later, the question of the
Royal Ulster Constabulary. As far as I
know, the Royal Ulster Constabulary come
under the provisions of this Bill. Then
each constable, I presume will have to pay
his penny and the State will have to pay
a penny in respect of him, and, as I believe
there are some 3,000 members of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary, it would appear that
the State will have to pay £12 10s a week.
If I am wrong I shall be corrected later.
At all events, something will have to be
paid by and in respect of the Ulster constable in order that he may obtain treatment which will be in no way better or
wider than he is getting already.
Again, there are those other people
to whom allusion was made, what
I call the short-time payer, perhaps the
young woman who pays during her adolescent womanhood, but on her marriage
loses her right to any accrued benefit. I always look upon health insurance as rather more like fire insurance than
life insurance, but the fact remains that
that class of people, it may be the young
woman who ultimately is going to marry,
or the young man who is going abroad,
consider that they pay too much while they
are here. It does not always happen that
they get any sickness benefit, and they
do lose when they go away. All those
persons would be able to contract out.
Then there is the ordinary ruralist. He
need not necessarily be an agricultural
labourer. I have had just the same point
of objection put up to me on behalf of
mill workers as on behalf of agricultural
labourers. They have had no opportunity
of making their case individually, and it
is, I think it must be admitted, very
expressly their own affair whether they
are to be brought compulsorily into the
new scheme of insurance or whether they
are not. That is their business, and they
are naturally very anxious for information
on the subject, because it is stated that
one approved society which operates very
extensively in Northern Ireland-it is an
international society-already pays in sick
benefit a rate higher than that required
by the original Act. They are able to pay
more per week that they are bound to pay,
and the ruralist, and I should have
thought perhaps the town dweller, wants
to know very much if it is possible for some
of these societies on the existing basis to
pay more than they are bound to pay, why
it is necessary that a further contribution
should be asked.
The ruralist relies, I think, to some extent, on the Act of 1920, which was passed
at the time when he was exempted from
special insurance for medical benefit; he
relies on the fact that he was taken over
as a person not subject to taxation in that
regard, and he relies also on the Report
of the Local Government Commission,
about which so much has already been
said.
The fact that that Report has not
been, so to speak, superseded by the
Report of any other
any other Commission of
Inquiry, and that its pronouncements
have been accepted by default would seem
to discharge any ordinary person from the
obligation of commencing a campaign to
maintain the principles laid down in that
Report. There has been, as I say, no
special inquiry ad hoc before a commission
of competent men as to the necessity for
the introduction of this medical benefit or
as to the real advantages which would
accrue from its introduction.
In the face of these circumstances it
seems to me it cannot be argued that
people who desire to stay outside a scheme
should not have the right to do so.
Now
the loophole of this contracting-out clause
will give the Minister time-five years in
each case-to convert those who are not in
agreement with this scheme. He will
have that advantage. It will also give
the Minister, if I may quote the Minister
as being the arch propagandist of the
scheme I am not sure if he is-
The MINISTER OF LABOUR (Mr.
Andrews) indicated assent.
Mr. LESLIE: The right hon. Gentleman, I see, accepts it.
.
Health Insurance Bill—–
Report of Amendment
THE SENATE
Mr. ANDREWS: Quite.
Mr. LESLIE: I would not like to say
anything unfair to my right hon.
Friend. I think he knows that well. At
all events, he would have time to convert
the country, and to allay their natural
fears that this extra contribution may be
only the precursor of additional contributions. It will be remembered that it
is only three or four years since the penny
had to be raised in order to provide funds for contributory pensions.
Hon. Members are aware that the contribution is open to actuarial consideration
in three or four years from now, and that
it is suggested in the Pensions Act that
there will be an increase of a penny on
each side on the next occasion. Further,
the position will be examined again and
again at every decadal period, and
pennies are piled on pennies.
There is also this fear in the rural
districts that opportunity may be taken
to extend the unemployment insurance
to the agricultural labourer. If that
ever happened-I trust it will not-I
think it would be regretted. I might
quote an old proverb in its modern form
-that the last straw breaks the camel's
back. It is more commonly expressed as the
last straw that gives the camel the hump.
You do both in that case. I do hope it
will not happen, but there is a fear about
it. Again, this loophole would give the
approved societies time to put their house
in order. I think it is plain to all of us
from what the right hon. Gentleman said
yesterday afternoon, when speaking on
Clause 9 of the Bill dealing with the
scheme for sick visiting, that some, at all
events, of the approved societies do not
take such steps to prevent malingering
as would seem to be reasonably practical.
Had we had that ad hoc inquiry, of which
I have spoken, I should not have been
under the necessity of putting the Amendment on the Paper. We have not had
that inquiry in Northern Ireland.
The Local Government Report stands,
and, therefore, as we are a democratic
community, which is entitled to govern
itself, and to express its own preference
in matters concerning itself, I think we
have no choice but to give the option of
contracting out. I think we should
remember that not so many years ago the
late Mr. Bonar Law, the leader of the
party to which so many of us belong, and
have belonged for so long, suggested on
the occasion of a Medical Health Insurance Bill that it ought to be considered
whether, even at this late period, the
scheme should not be placed on a voluntary basis. Ι am not going so
far
as that, but 1 do suggest that
this new development should be left
to the individual choice of the persons
concerned. Therefore, from that point
of view, and particularly on behalf of
those who live in the rural districts, I beg
to move the insertion of the Amendment
which I have placed on the Paper.
Mr. ANDREWS: The hon. and
learned Senator who has proposed this
Amendment has done so, as he always
does in my opinion, in a very reasonable
and conciliatory manner. He made one
statement towards the end of his remarks
which gratified me very much, because
he said that if an inquiry had been held
into the question of medical benefits
generally he would not have put down.
this Amendment. Now, as a matter of
fact, it is true that beyond the inquiry
which was held by the Local Government
Commission, which considered a great
many other matters besides medical benefits, no actual inquiry was held in Northern Ireland. But I would remind the
hon. and learned Senator that in 1926
a Commission was set up in Great Britain
which inquired into the question of
health insurance generally and medical
benefits in particular. That Commission.
reported in 1928, and it will only be
necessary for me to read one extract from
that Report, I think, to convince my hon.
and learned Friend not only as to the
advantage which medical benefits
would be to Northern Ireland, but as
to the real advantage which has accrued
from the introduction of this Measure in
Great Britain almost 19 years ago. The
sentence which I would like to read to the
House is this:
We can say confidently that medical
benefit has proved in practice a successful and most valuable factor in the advancement of the health of the nation.
I would point out that that Commission was inquiring into the matter after
the Act had been in existence for a considerable number of years. They were
not inquiring into an abstract proposition
at all; they were inquiring into the
working of a scheme which was very
generally known to the whole community
across the Channel. Therefore, I venture
to maintain that the Report of a Commission such as that is far more valuable
Health Insurance Bill- 422
Health Insurance Bill-
5 JUNE 1930
than any report of a commission which
we could have possibly set up in order
to inquire into the matter here. The
conditions are practically the same in both
areas.
The hon. and learned Member raised
the point as an example that the mill
workers in Northern Ireland had not had
an opportunity to express their views. I
would remind the hon. and learned
Senator that he errs, I think, in expressing that view for this reason: The mill
workers very largely are represented by the
trade unions, and all the trade unions in
Northern Ireland are in favour of the
introduction of medical benefits. Further, the employers are represented by
their organisations, and not one single.
employers' organisation has taken any
exception to the introduction of medical
benefits. I think it is very creditable to
the employers' associations that they have
taken that view.
As regards the Amendment itself, I
really venture to say that it is not a workable proposition. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.) To contract in and contract out,
even after periods of five years, would, in
my opinion, increase the administrative
costs of a scheme of this kind to a very
considerable extent. Obviously, the result
would really be this, that the young people
enjoying good health, as the majority of
young people here do, would contract out
when they were young, and then when
thev got old they would contract in. That
would really deprive the scheme of the
young lives and saddle the scheme with the
older lives. Instead of asking for a penny
from the employers and workers to enable
the scheme to be financed I would have to
ask on actuarial basis to double the contributions the very moderate contributions
--which I am asking for to-day.
Again, I do not think it would be fair
to the medical profession to leave them
with the bad lives and take away from
them the good ones. They would have
great justification for obiecting, and I am
quite sure it would be obiected to.
I am
also sure that would cut across the reasonable and just arrangements which we have
been able to complete with the medical
profession. It is quite obvious that if the
arrangements that have been made with
the medical profession were interfered
with, and if the medical profession refused
to work the scheme until the whole matter
had been reconsidered, it would in practice
he really impossible to bring the scheme
into operation on the appointed day, as
set out in the Measure itself.
Again, I am quite sure that the
approved societies would object for administrative and other reasons; and, last but
not least, I think the British Government
would with justice object on the ground
that it would interfere with their ratification of the Geneva Convention, which,
in the interests of the whole of Europe,
should be ratified by Great Britain, who
were the prime movers in connection with
the equalisation of social services throughout Europe. No doubt they were actuated
by two motives, one, and the most important, the human motive, and the other
that competition as between industries in
the different countries should be brought
on to a fairer plane than is the case at the
present time. For the reasons which I
have mentioned, and for a great many
others which I feel it is not necessary to
take up the time of the House by mentioning to-day, I have no course open to me
but to say that the Government cannot
accept the Amendment.
Mr. DORMAN: I hope the House will
not accept this Amendment. The acceptance of it would be disastrous to the whole
object of the Bill. The purpose of the
Bill is to provide medical attendance and
medicine for insured persons, and if the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie)
would ask himself who constitute these
insured persons, and what are their
economic conditions, he would see that the
acceptance of this Amendment would make
the whole purpose of the Bill void.
Insured persons are persons who have no
means of livelihood in their own right, and
who necessarily must sell their labour
power-hand or brain-by the hour, by
the day, or by the week, to another class
who are under no obligation to continue
the purchase of that labour power for
more than an hour, a week, a month, or
so on, though the purchaser may act very
generously, charitably, or even Christ-like,
if I may use the expression, to his or her
employees. Some employers do, I know,
feel under an obligation, when their workmen take sick, to incur personally the expense of a doctor and medicine during
their illness and also make them an allowance of their salaries.
But there is no sense of security for the
insured person.
If the hon. and learned
Senator could introduce, alongside of this
right on the part of the insured person to
vote out some clause that would make it
Health Insurance Bill-
Report of Amendment
THE SENATE
[Mr. Dorman]
imperative on those who are employing
him from time to time to continue the
employment of his labour for the period.
for which he was about to vote cut, then
there would be some justification for the
Amendment. But, in my opinion, the
Amendment, if accepted, would cut right
across the very purpose of the Bill, and
would be disastrous from the point of view
of the societies, from the point of view of
the Department, and, above all, from the
point of view of the insured individual.
No insured individual has any security for
the continuance of his or her position. A
man who is living by the sale of his labour
power, hand or brain, may to-day be in
receipt of £200 a year, and may, without
any moral delinquency on his part,
through the disastrous breakdown of the
branch of business in which he is engaged,
or otherwise, be deprived of his income
and cast upon the scrap heap, and by
having voted himself out this year he may
leave himself in a disastrous position for
the following year. I hope the House will
not approve of the Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I submit to the
House that it would be unwise to accept
the principle of contracting out under this
Bill as proposed by the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie). To do so would cut
clean across the face of the Bill, and would
practically nullify its beneficial working
when it comes into effect. There can be
no doubt that the people who are most
concerned in this matter earnestly welcome
this Bill. We have in Northern Ireland,
I believe

Mr. CAMPBELL: It was only in regard to some remarks made by the
Minister and the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) to which I intended

Mr. LONG: I agree with the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie), who has
moved this Amendment. I think it is
eminently fair and right that a person
should have the right to say whether or
not he or she was in favour of medical
benefits being applied to himself or herself. Notwithstanding, and in spite of
that belief, I would appeal to the
hon. and learned Senator to withdraw the
Amendment, because I recognise, as the
hon. Senator (Mr. Dorman) and the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) have
pointed out, that it would practically
wreck the Bill, and we do not want to do
that. Much as we dislike some of the
particular provisions in the Bill, I am sure
no Senator here wishes to do that, and,
therefore, I appeal to the hon. and
learned Senator not to press the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I hope I am not usually
so deaf as the proverbial adder. I recognise with pleasure the remark made.
by the right hon. Gentleman (Mr.
Andrews) in replying, to the effect that
the opposition to this Bill was of a conciliatory character, and I welcome his
agreement with me that there has been no
definite inquiry in Northern Ireland on
this point. I was aware of the inquiry in
Great Britain in 1926, but I had forgotten the exact date of the Report, and,
therefore, I did not commit myself. But
the right hon. Gentleman has reminded
the House that the Report is dated 1928,
and, therefore, in its final form at all
events could not possibly have been before
the Local Government Commission.
do not think, however, that this discharges the responsibility of holding an
inquiry for it must be true that when
the inquiry was being conducted in Great
Britain they were not dealing with a dispensary system of the character we lave
here. However, I do not want to go into
that at all.
I
I admit that there is great point in
what the right hon. Gentleman said from
the doctors' point of view, if young people
go out, as they probably would go out,
on the ground that they get nothing for
the payments they are required to make
There is something in that, undoubtedly,
but I do not quite see why the right hon.
Gentleman laid such stress on the appointed day. I do not see what peculiar
virtue or importance there is in the 7th
July.
Health Insurance Bill-

5 JUNE 1930
Mr. ANDREWS: It is the beginning
of the insurance year.
Mr. LESLIE: I presume the insurance
year would begin again next year. However, I do not wish to stress that. As to
the International Convention, are the
conditions of labour likely to be observed
throughout Europe as honestly as they are
observed in the British Isles? In Czecho-
Slovakia
Mr. SPEAKER: I must ask the hon.

Mr. LESLIE: I come back to what has
been said by my hon. Friend opposite (Mr.
Long). He appeals to me not to press

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Viscount BANGOR: I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read the Third
time."
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before this Bill
passes away from the Senate, there are one
or two remarks I wish to make, very
briefly, in regard to the Bill itself. It
strikes me as a very noteworthy fact that
the large majority of the Departmental
Commission on Local Government Administration, which presented its report in
1927, came to the conclusion that a system
of medical benefits should not be established in Northern Ireland, and counselled
a reorganisation of the existing dispensary
system, which has been peculiar to Ireland
for a period of one hundred years. With
all due deference to the opinion of the
very eminent and learned gentlemen who
composed that majority, I think they were
wrong, and that the Government are right
in taking this step. I believe the minority
Report had the better side of the case.
Perhaps I speak with sub-conscious sympathy with minorities.
The drawback of the scheme necessarily
is that it does not provide for the
dependents of insured persons, and that
it does not provide for the
very
large number of uninsured persons in our
Northern area. For the dependents of
insured persons and the uninsured persons
there is, of course, the Medical Charities
Act, which, I think, is the only available
legal machinery to which they can resort.
I am not speaking, of course, of the magnificent voluntary institutions which we have
in different parts of the Province for the
our
treatment of the poor, irrespective of
whether they are insured or uninsured.
We have in Belfast 145,000 insured
persons, and, I think, there are 185,000
in the rest of Northern Ireland, making
a total of close of one-third of a million.
I believe that the medical profession in
towns at least will welcome this
Measure. The industrial classes will welcome this Measure, and I believe it is now
the unanimous opinion of the medical
profession and of the working-class leaders
in Great Britain, where medical benefits
have been in operation for a period of
17 years since 1913 -that the Bill which
we are now introducing in Northern Ireland has been an immense boon to all
classes of the community in Great Britain
in respect of matters of health. I believe that that will be our experience in
Northern Ireland also under this scheme.
I am glad that the Minister of Labour
has brought it forward, and brought it
forward as I believe at an opportune
time. I can see he has a very clear grasp
of these Insurance Acts. I do not know
of any Member of either House who has
anything like the knowledge that he
possesses of Acts that puzzle the most of
us. He has given laborious days and
sleepless nights to the consideration of
these Insurance Acts, and I think that
he rests with them under his pillow.
(Laughter).
Mr. ANDREWS: It is only necessary
for me to say a very few words. I feel that
the House is determined to place this Bill
upon the Statute Book, and therefore it is
necessary for me to say very little in order
to induce hon. Senators to vote for the
Third Reading. It might, however, seem
discourteous on my part if I did not reply
to the speech of the hon. Senator opposite
(Mr. Campbell). He raised the question.
of the Local Government Commission's
Report. He is quite right in saying that.
the majority of those who formed that
Commission reported against medical benefits, but it is also true to say that a great
many of those who signed that Majority
Report have since seen the error of their
ways.
Three of them happen to be Members of
the House of Commons, and I am glad to
be able to state that the whole three supported the Measure a few days ago when
the House of Commons was considering it.
The Chairman of the Commission, who
signed the Majority Report, and who is
one of our most distinguished medical men
Loans to Farmers
Loans to Farmers
THE SENATE
Mr. Andrews]
in Northern Ireland, also supported the
Measure both on its Second Reading and
Third Reading. I think that will give us
all confidence that we are doing the right
thing. May I just quote one other extract
showing what medical opinion is in connection with this Measure in Northern
Ireland? This is the opinion of the
Tuberculosis Officer of Belfast, who has no
personal interest in the matter, because he
is not a general practitioner. In his report
of 1928 he wrote as follows:-
I am still of the opinion expressed in
my last annual report that the extension
of medical benefits to the insured persons
of Northern Ireland on the same terms
and conditions as at present prevail in
Great Britain would be of enormous
value to the community here.
I think I could not quote stronger evidence
in favour of the Measure than that extract
from that report. May I again thank this
Honourable House for the very careful and
sympathetic consideration which they have
given to this important Measure, which
personally I feel will prolong human life
in Northern Ireland and relieve much
human suffering.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read the Third time) agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass.".
(Viscount Bangor.)

Regulations for Loans to Farmers.
Mr. McALLISTER: I beg to move,
That this House regrets the frustration of the intentions of Parliament expressed in the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Act, Northern Ireland,
1929, and believes the same to be due
to the cumbersome, costly and humiliating regulations issued under the Act
and the unsatisfactory administration
of the Act.
This Motion appears on the Paper in
my name for the purpose of ventilating
what, I think, is a distinct grievance on
the part of the most important part of the
community in Northern Ireland, namely,
the farmers. I hope to show to the
House that by a system of unfair and
unjust administration, the intentions of
Parliament have been frustrated, and
the intentions of the Act of Parliament
in question have been avoided. There
were certain Acts of Parliament passed for
the purpose of erecting dwelling-houses,
repairing and erecting office houses, making drainage, roads, and other field
works. That was very necessary in Northern Ireland, in my opinion. We have
had a great many discussions during the
last few days about the health of the insured people of Northern Ireland, but I
think those who know the rural life of
Northern Ireland will agree with me when
I say that a great percentage of the farmers of Northern Ireland are, unfortuna-
Therefore, I
tely, inadequately housed.
say that a Measure which would have
given them reasonable facilities for building and repairing their houses was a Measure of the highest importance.
reading these Regulations, I would almost
go the length of saying that they have
been made to discourage the farmers
from taking advantage of the Act of
Parliament. The advantages of that Act
are, in my opinion, absolutely negatived
by these Regulations. They seem to have
been drawn up on the assumption that the
farmers of Northern Ireland have lost all
sense of pride and self-respect.
After
The notice sent out with the application
form is prefaced by a warning, almost a
threat, to the farmers against taking advantage of this Act of Parliament.
The preface to this notice reads:-
Intending borrowers should read the
following Regulations carefully before
submitting an application, as whether
the loan is granted or not the applicant
is liable for all expenses incurred in the
investigation of his application.
Therefore, we see that whether an application is granted or not the applicant
has to pay the costs. These costs range
from £5 for £100 loan up to £20.
Another unfair thing in these Regulations
is this. The farmer not only pays the
costs according to the amount of the loan,
but he has to pay on the total cost of the
building. Suppose the farmer has advanced to him by the Government a sum
of £250. If he were only spending that
£250 that loan would, I think, cost him
Loans to Farmers
Loans to Farmers
5 JUNE 1930
£9; but if he expends £1,000 on the
building instead of the £250 he has borrowed, the Government charge him £20
costs for that loan of £250. I suggest
to the House that it was never the intention of Parliament when the Bill was
passed that that imposition should be put
upon the farmers.
I have here a letter
from a farmer which, I think, puts the
position as explicitly as it could be put.
He says: :-
The Government makes a generous
offer, as it were, but imposes such objectionable Regulations that they know
the people won't accept it.
He further states:-
Although we are in humble circumstances the people in authority must not
forget that we have a spirit of our own.
That is a letter from a farmer that I
have known or been in communication
with before, and I think it expresses the
feeling of farmers generally with regard
to these Regulations. I go further and I
say that by reason of the method of administration these Acts of Parliament
have been rendered a failure. We find
there were only 534 applications for loans,
and 272 of these were granted. That
means that roughly 50 per cent. of the
applications have been turned down, and
in those cases the farmers presumably
have had to pay the costs of the investigation of title and other matters.
understand that under this Act the Government were authorised by Parliament
to loan a sum £250,000, but, as a matter of fact, owing to these Regulations
being so unworkable, there has only been
applied for a sum of £21,295. Of that
sum the Government have advanced only
£7,441.
I
The most degading and humiliating
condition of all is surely this: that if a
farmer living in Northern Ireland wants
to take advantage of this Act his application has to be published in a local paper
and in the "Belfast Gazette." What is
the necessity for that? £20 is charged
for investigating title by expert Government valuers to go and inspect the site.
Therefore what advantage is to be gained
by compelling the farmer to publish the
fact that he wants a loan on his farm in
the local paper and in the "Belfast
Gazette." I say deliberately that the
Government must have had in view the
fact that if those conditions were imposed
upon farmers this Bill would not be taken
advantage of, and they would not be called
upon to advance money under it. In
addition these Regulations also state that
the formalities will occupy at least two
months. If a man applies for a loan and
furnishes his title and the necessary inspection is made, what is the necessity for a
delay of two months, and warning the
farmer beforehand that it will take two
months to get the application through. I
think I have said sufficient to satisfy the
House that the farmers of Northern Ireland are suffering a distinct grievance by
what I might also say is the mal-administration of an Act that would, if properly
administered, have performed a highly
useful and beneficient service to the
farmers of Northern Ireland.
I
Mr. CAMPBELL: I second the Motion,
and I hope it will be supported by those
Members who are so keenly interested in
the rural areas of Northern Ireland.
remember that when Polonius was hurrying off his son, Laertes, to his studies, at
Wittenberg, he enjoined him, "Neither a
borrower nor a lender be." I wonder who
is the Polonius in the Ministry of
Finance who, through the Regulations
issued under this Act, enjoins the farmer
not to be a borrower and the Ministry not
to be a lender. Whoever he is, his idea of
the mentality of the Ulster farmer is very
queer. He appears to think that when
the Northern farmer is in need of ready
cash for seeds and fertilizers he will only
borrow it in the full blaze of publicity, and
that he will broadcast his necessity; he
will have the fact communicated to all and
sundry through the local newspaper, and
through that sprightly and entertaining
journal, the "Belfast Gazette." If the
Ulster farmer were an ordinary human
being, and he is, and was hard up for
money, which is not unusual in the North
of Ireland, he would conduct his operations in a very different way. He would
go to a registered moneylender alone, and
by stealth. He would have no publicity
about it, and he would not make it known
to his next-door neighbour. But the
gentleman in the Ministry of Finance who
is responsible for these Regulations apparently holds that the Ulster farmer is a
man who will not be happy until he gets
his name in the "Belfast Gazette.'
Was there ever such trifling with an Act
of Parliament? Legislation by Department seems to contain terrors that the
people did not know of. Very often we
see in these papers that regulations have
been laid on the Table of the House deal-
Loans to Farmers
Loans to Farmers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
ing with these matters by a great many
Departments, and I sometimes wonder
where the Table is which
which they lie
on. I will find it some day.
Is this Act of 1927 really a Parliamentary joke? Was it a piece of Parliamentary trifling? Was it
Was it meant to be a
reality as far as the Government and the
Ulster farmers are concerned?
My hon.
Friend reminded the House that Parliament sanctioned loans by the Ministry of
Finance up to a quarter of a million
pounds to the Ulster farmers to assist
them in buying seeds and fertilisers, and
yet only £21,000 or less than one-tenth of
the amount sanctioned by Parliament has
been actually applied for, and only about
one-third of the £21,000 has been granted
to the Ulster farmers. We can only
draw one of two inferences from that.
Either the Ulster farmers are very prosperous and doing well and are so flush
of money that they do not need loans from
the Ministry of Finance at all. They
have no reason to seek loans at all. Does
anybody who knows the Ulster farmer believe that to be a fact?
If that is not the reason for the complete breakdown the only other reason I
can
see is that the regulations issued
under the Act or the administration or
both combined have paralysed the operation of the Act and nullified the intention
of Parliament. The machinery of the
Act has broken down and ought to be
scrapped. These regulations, I submit,
ought to be scrapped and other regulations ought to be issued in their place,
which, while safeguarding the public purse,
will not deter and humiliate the Northern
Ireland farmer.
Mr. LESLIE: I should have liked to
have had a little further opportunity of
looking into the exact provisions of the
Act quoted on the Paper, but as I have
not had that opportunity I shall have to
ask the noble Viscount, who presumably
has been looking into the matter to give
us some further information as to what
exactly it says in the Act. My recollection of the Act is that it was brought
forward to some extent as a counterpoise
in the interests of the farmers to the
Loans Guarantee Acts which had been
brought in principally for the manufacturing concerns and which were in fact
found of particular advantage to the
shipbuilding industry. I understood it
was generally thought that this was
rather in the nature of a makeam
I
weight.
pretty certain there
is nothing in the Act of Parliament requiring a period of two months for the
granting of a loan. I can scarcely imagine
that even the clerks in the Ministry of
Finance would think that if a man wanted
to borrow money to buy cattle, the cattle
are going to stand in the market for two
months. Nor can I imagine that the fields
will wait two months for the application
of fertilisers. Two months must be
altogether out of reason. Then as regards
the insertion of this notice in the "Belfast
Gazette," I am afraid the hon. and learned
Senator in front of me, speaking of the
"Gazette" in connection with the farmer,
forgot one thing. I remember there is
more than one column in the "Belfast
Gazette," and there are farmers who
appeared in that part-
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Better not
mention that!
Mr. LESLIE:
And as there is more
than one column in the "Belfast Gazette"
so there is more than one table in Parliament, and I think the hon. Senator will
find the one he wants. But I do not
remember whether regulations to be made
under this Act were to be laid on the Table
of the House. I should like to support
the proposal that something more should
be done to make the Act workable. It is
quite plain from the figures given by the
noble Viscount, the Parliamentary Secretary, the other day that the Act has not
worked. The intentions of Parliament
have somehow or other been frustrated.
That is a matter of administration that
ought to be corrected at once. There is
no excuse for that. If Parliament provides that money is to be applied to a
certain purpose it should not be left to
any Department to override legislation.
That is not so much legislation by Parliament as contra-legislation.
That
It reminds
me of the story of the old-fashioned
solicitor who was asked to express an
opinion, and when furnished with the Act
of Parliament said he did not like Acts of
Parliament. They were bad law.
is very much the position.
I hope the
noble Viscount will give some assurance
that if what is done cannot be undone at
all events some improvement will be introduced and that farmers will get the benefit
of provisions made for their benefit.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I shall certainly
support the Motion on the ground that
Loans to Farmers
Loans to Farmers
5 JUNE 1930
there may be some improvement made. I
would question really after all if there will
be. Two months delay is nothing
new or different to what we have
been accustomed to in other matters.
When we have plans prepared for a school
-and the Leader of the House is the
Minister of Education—and we send them
forward, in six months time we begin to
hear that they want a new window in some
corner, and the thing is sent back. We
used to complain about the delays when
the administration was in Dublin, and we
had to go to London, but now I think it
is worse than ever it was. This delay in
matters of this kind here at home is most
unsatisfactory. I hope that something
will be done to make this more workable,
because it is a useless Act in its present
condition.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am certain that the hon. Member who spoke last
will forgive me for not taking up the point
about delay in the Ministry of Education.
I have heard enough about education for
the month to deprecate any allusions to
delays after the passing of the Education.
Bill. I ask him to defer the question
about delays in my Ministry or the question about schools until after the adjournment of this House.
With regard to the Motion before the
House, I am glad to be able to prove that
the Ministry of Finance and the Ministry
of Agriculture are not at fault at the
present time. The fact is that the hon.
Senator who moved this Motion and those
who supported him have confused, so far
as I can see, two Acts of Parliament. The
hon. Senator (Mr.
(Mr. McAllister) who
started it, read out a provision from the
Land Improvement Act which is not the
same, obviously, as the Exchequer and
Financial Provisions Act of 1929 and
1930, which is the real subject under
review.
Mr. CAMPBELL: My speech dealt
exclusively with the last-mentioned Act.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will omit
the hon. and learned Senator, and only
refer to what Mr. McAllister said. I
want to make it quite plain they are
different Acts and different procedure.
The types of loans which require advertisement in the interesting journal mentioned.
are Improvement Loans, in which a
charge is being registered against the holding of the borrower. As the Government
charge takes precedence over all other
charges it would be manifestly unjust that
registration should take place without the
fullest notice being given to any other
person who may have a charge against the
holding which may not have been registered or otherwise secured.
That procedue does not apply to this Act, the procedure in regard to which is entirely
different. For the purpose of both
answering the Motion before the House
and also for giving publicity to this Act
I should like to state what procedure the
borrower must take in order to obtain a
loan. He must obtain two sureties, and
he must get from his bank a form which
he fills up and sends to the Ministry of
Agriculture. If the loan is sanctioned
he calls at his bank with his sureties,
signs the note, and obtains the money.
I cannot see that this procedure is cumbersome.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: He has no
bank.
Viscount
ture.
CHARLEMONT: In that
case he sends in his sureties to the Ministry direct. If he has no bank he could
not possibly use it. As regards costs, the
only cost to the applicant is a three.
halfpenny stamp on the envelope conveying the form to the Ministry of Agricul-
He does not even have to pay for
I should like to
the promisory note.
point out further that the loans are
granted at 5 per cent. interest, and I
think the farmers are, to some extent,
lucky in being able to obtain loans at this
rate of interest. The usual rate for this
type of loan is higher. The average time
it has taken from the receipt of the application to the granting of the loan has
been between two and three weeks.
Under the other Acts, which have been
mentioned, the time may be greater. I
think it would be a great advantage if
the provisions of this particular Act were
more generally known. While the
figures which have been given as to the
number of applications that have been
granted are correct, applications are still
coming in, but if the provisions of the
Act were better known they might come
in at a quicker rate than they are doing
at the moment.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I can state definitely that on 12th April, 1930, a set of

terms I have referred to, and yet the

THE TOTALISATOR ON RACE
COURSES.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Government contemplate introducing legislation for the purpose of legalising the
use of the totalisator on race courses in
Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No representations have been received by the
Government as to the advisability of introducing legislation on the lines suggested in the question, and accordingly
the Government do not contemplate introducing any legislation regarding the
matter.
WHEAT GROWING IN NORTHERN
IRELAND.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether any special
steps are being taken, or are in contemplation, by the Government to ascertain
what agricultural districts in Northern
Ireland are suitable for wheat growing
and to encourage the cultivation of wheat
in any such districts.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Ministry of Agriculture does not consider it
necessary to undertake a survey of the
areas in Northern Ireland suitable for
wheat growing. The extent under
wheat in the six counties in 1929 was only
3,617 acres, and it is not considered that
any substantial increase in this area is
likely to occur in the near future. Apart
from a few years during the war, the
general tendency of the past fifty or sixty
years has been for the acreage under
wheat to undergo a steady decline. The
bulk of the wheat grown is used for home
consumption on farms and although there
are districts in Northern Ireland where
good yields of wheat can be obtained the
soft character of the grain and the high
moisture content render it hardly suitable for the present day requirements of
the milling trade.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I be permitted
to make one observation on the answer of
the Minister to the question of the hon.
and learned Senator Leslie ?
The question suggests that the Government should
give encouragement to the cultivation of
wheat in Northern Ireland. Now I submit
that anyone acquainted with the farming
industry knows that the only way the
Government can give encouragement is to
help to make wheat growing profitable. I
notice that on Whit Monday Mr. Baldwin
announced as the main item in his agricultural policy a guaranteed price for
wheat that will enable it to be grown at
a profit. This guarantee will cost the
State about £1 for every quarter of
British-grown wheat, or, at the lowest
estimate, about £7,500,000 a year, to be
got out of receipts from Safeguarding
duties. The Ulster farmers are, I think,
outside that programme and that guarantee. The reason being that the Ulster
farmer has in general been considered an
assured voter for the Tory Party.
Royal Assent.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think the
hon. and learned Senator did not take
quite the notice I hoped he would take of
the last part of the answer to the question
which really is to the effect that the wheat
that would be likely to be grown in Northern Ireland, owing to the high moisture
content and the soft character of the
grain, would not be suitable for entering
into competition with English wheat,
which is grown chiefly in the eastern parts
of England and is more suitable for the
milling trade than our wheat would be
under any system.
Mr. LONG: In view of the statement
of the Leader of the House and the answer
he has given to the question, I suggest
that the Government should make representations to Mr. Baldwin to include all
cereals in the action he proposes to take
inasmuch as wheat is only suitable from
a limited area in Northern Ireland.
Royal Assent.

Presented pursuant to Act, and ordered

THE LATE MARQUESS OF
DUFFERIN AND AVA.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): This is the first occasion on
which this House has met since the
lamentable event in July which robbed it
of Lord Dufferin's services, and I think
it would be impossible to resume the
Session without a brief reference to the
late Speaker.
All of us here have passed through
those years when a day hardly passed on
which we did not hear of the death of
someone who was known to us either
privately or by repute; and it is, I think,
impossible to pass through such years of
tribulation and mourning without becoming hardened-to some extent-to losses
which do not affect us personally. But
I doubt whether anyone in Ulster heard
of Lord Dufferin's untimely death without deep sense that the community had
suffered an irreparable less.
Among Lord Dufferin's forebears were
men and women who shed a lustre on the
land of their birth, whose achievements
stand high in the roll of honour of the
British Nation. He was a man of whom
they would not be ashamed, although the
path which he followed had not the
splendour of the one trodden by a
Governor-General of Canada and Viceroy
of India, or the perhaps more lasting
fame that attends the ways of a Sheridan.
But Lord Dufferin gave his services
freely and ungrudgingly to the small
corner of the British Empire to which
he belonged, and I think that men in his
position who take up the burden of public
life that lies close to their hands set an
example of those about them, not inferior to those whose careers in distant
lands are fraught with a greater
brilliance.
It is difficult for one who knew him for
many years to speak in these circumstances; difficult, because it would be so
easy to give undue prominence to engaging traits in his private character,
whereas it is Lord Dufferin's loss to the
public life of this Province that this
House deplores. Perhaps I had best
conclude by saying that Lord Dufferin's
private virtues were for his family and
for those who had the honour to know
him privately, and that in publicwhether as Commander of the Ulster
Division of the Royal Naval Volunteer
Reserve, as A.D.C. to His Majesty the
King, as Chairman of the Royal Victoria
Hospital, as first Speaker of the Senate
of Northern Ireland, or in the other high
positions which he held-his most fitting
epitaph will be found in the words of the
English prayer book-he did his duty in
that state of life unto which it had pleased
God to call him.
Mr. CAMPBELL: When this House
separated in June we little foresaw that
Lord Dufferin's place here would know
him no more. To some of us no successor
will adorn that Chair as he adorned it.
He lent to our deliberations the prestige
of a great name. His chairmanship
touched the imagination of the public. In
these days the moral authority of Parliaments is not what it was. It has been
weakened by attacks from very divergent
quarters. Yet Parliaments are still
potent instruments of democracy, still
indispensable to the progress of society,
and still take fresh strength from the cooperation of men of the type of our late
Speaker.
With his advantages of birth and rank
he might have buried his talent in a napkin. His strong and compelling sense of
duty as a soldier and citizen forbade this.
He was a very gallant gentleman. Exquisite urbanity and old world courtesy
were in him hereditary traits. He had
also a dash of the Sheridan wit and
brightness. He in whose veins ran the
blood of Brinsley Sheridan could never
have been altogether a stranger to any
assembly of Irishmen. Blended with his
tact and charm was a deep sense of justice.
The Late Speaker.
Election of Speaker.
9 OCTOBER 1930
He
Lord Dufferin was a just Speaker.
stood for fair play to a minority which is
in a rather lonely and unpopular position
here. He was the most tolerant, the least
technical, of Speakers. No Member of
this House ever heard words of anger or
irritation drop from his lips. If I were
asked to name the distinguishing note of
his speakership I would say that he had a
rare gift of getting things done with the
least friction. That, I think, was the true
note of his Speakership. That, I think,
was the secret of its success. Some there
are of whom there is no memorial;
but it is not so with Lord Dufferin.
died last July in Kent. Not a few Sessions will have gone, most of us here now
will have taken our leave of these scenes,
before the inspiration of his work and the
memory of his example will die in this
House.
He
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to express my
personal regret at the loss that this House
and the Province of Northern Ireland
have sustained in the untimely death of
our late Speaker, the Marquess of
Dufferin. He was a gentleman who possessed to a marked degree a patience and
consideration for those who participated
in the deliberations of this House, and I
shall never forget the kindness with which
he treated me personally as the representative of what is the most unpopular party
in this particular area when I first entered
this House, how he gently and kindly led
me, in my ignorance, into the path of
duty and precedent in the conduct of the
affairs of the House. He was preeminently possessed of that patience
which suffers long and is kind, and was
filled with a spirit of impartiality and
kindly disposed towards every Member of
the Senate over which he so ably presided. .
But if there is one thing which I think
the late Lord Dufferin will be remembered
for it will be that probably future generations of his countrymen will look back
upon that critical decade in the history of
his native country in which he presided
over the deliberations of this honourable
assembly and remember that he never
once used any language calculated to
estrange his fellow countrymen who
differed from him. He rather directed his
attention to trying to smooth the gap
which, unfortunately, has divided our
country for so many generations, and
which has resulted in depopulation and
impoverishment. It is a great loss to this
House and a great loss to Northern Ireland, and a great loss, in my opinion, to
the Empire, that the late Lord Dufferin
was cut away in the prime of life. I very
much regret that we have lost the services
of the noble Lord as President of this Assembly.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I should not have
interposed in this vote now being passed
were it not that I saw Lord Dufferin three
weeks before his death, and he then with
characteristic kindness took me over and
showed me the goblet which the Senate
gave him, and wished me to convey to the
Senators individually his high appreciation and his admiration of the taste displayed. I am very sorry, indeed, I need
not say, to convey this message in these
painful circumstances, but I thought it
right, as it were, to convey this last message from him, and I take this opportunity of doing so.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The House
will rise for a few moments.
The Senate accordingly stood for a few
moments in silence.
Election of Speaker.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The next
business is the election of a Speaker of
the Senate.
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I am aware that
I owe the privilege of proposing the
election of a Speaker to my being a
Victorian of a somewhat early date; but
I will endeavour to discharge my duty
to the full extent of my limitations with
very great pleasure, because I feel that
the Senator whom I am about to propose
is in every way eminently qualified to
maintain the high tradition of the Chair
handed down by the late Speaker.
Having been a Member of the Senate for
nine years, and having, moreover, filled
the office of Deputy-Leader with efficiency
and, indeed, distinction, he is familiar
with our procedure, an interesting blend
of the practice of the House of Lords and
the House of Commons.
He has been Chairman since 1923 of
the Education Committee of the great
County of Down, the largest of the
Education Committees in the Province,
and won such golden opinions in that.
capacity that I have heard him described
more than once as a model chairman. He
would have been elected unanimously
President of the Association of the 18
Election of Speaker.
Election of Speaker.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Armstrong]
Education Committees of the Province,
had he been able to accept the office.
Like his lamented predecessor he has been
a soldier, and has in a scientific branch
of the Service held high rank and important posts. It is somewhat remarkable that the Speaker of the British
House of Commons, the first two Leaders
of our Senate have belonged to that
profession, and that both Speakers in
another place were at one time soldiers
and served in the Great War. This
would go to show that there is no better
training for effective public service in
peace than the profession of Arms.
The duties of the Chair in the Senate
may be less arduous than those of the
Speaker in another place, but nevertheless they are exacting, and require firmness, quick decision, close sustained
attention, strict impartiality, courtesy
and consideration. Being convinced that
Viscount Bangor possesses all these
necessary qualifications, and others which
I need not mention, I submit his name
with confidence to the Senate. I have
much pleasure in moving "That Viscount
Bangor be elected Speaker of this Senate."
Mr. CAMPBELL: We have just
voiced our farewell tribute to our late
honoured Speaker. The responsibility for
the choice of his successor rests, in the
first instance, on the predominant party
here. The hon. Member who has moved
the Motion before the House, the respected Father of the House, is the
spokesman of that party. We of the Opposition realise that in existing circumstances it would be idle to put forward
a rival candidate. The day may come
when the election of a Speaker of this
House will be determined solely by the
fitness of the candidate for that high
office, other and less laudable considerations being laid aside. In saying that I
do not in the slightest degree detract from
the qualifications of Lord Bangor for the
Speakership. The election of a Speaker
should be an unanimous election if circumstances at all permit. For the
Speakership of this House is no easy task.
It is no light responsibility. The Speakerelect should bring to his work the encouragement to be derived from the knowledge that he has the goodwill of every
element and every Member of the House.
Lord Bangor has borne himself bravely
and honourably as a soldier. We have
He
known him here as a Deputy Speaker and
a Deputy Leader of the House. In both
these offices let me say frankly he has
borne himself with uniform courtesy and
fairness and with ability. He possesses
a large fund of human sympathy.
has a sense of humour. That is an invaluable asset. Let him keep that asset.
It will serve him well. Let him remember
what Lord Dufferin never forgot that a
mild answer breaketh wrath. (Laughter.)
And if his ear catches at times, as it will,
sharp accents in debate within the limits
of Parliamentary decorum, let him not
cast a too reproachful eye upon the
speakers for they may be good men struggling with adversity. (Laughter.)
Whether they are or not will depend upon
the benches towards which his eye will
travel. (Laughter.)
Though Lord Bangor is primarily the
choice of the majority here the test, the
final test, of his Speakership will be not
their approbation alone but whether he
has won the confidence of every Member
of this House. That ought to be an aim
and an attainment to him. The moment
he takes possession of the Chair a Speaker
doffs his armour as a politician. I refuse
to believe that the Speaker-elect will allow
himself to be actuated by personal or political feelings.
I have read somewhere that at the election of a Parliamentary Speaker everybody tries to look his best, like children
at the start of a party. One never knows
what may happen by the time the party
is over. (Laughter.) Well, one likes to
think of the Speaker as a friend, to think
of him at his best. We promise him our
co-operation. We trust that he will always preserve the even mind, the judicial
calmness befitting a Speaker. We trust
that he will be a worthy custodian of the
privileges of this House, and of the rights
of minorities in this House-a Speaker of
prudence, independence and impartiality.
I beg to second the motion.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to support the
Motion. Had I been asked to nominate
a Member as Speaker of this House I
should have suggested the name of Lord
Bangor. He will have a difficult task in
following in the footsteps of the late
Speaker, but as he will have the confidence
of every Member of this House I believe
he will successfully discharge his responsible duties.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Election of Speaker.

Adjournment
SPEAKER-ELECT:
It will be my earnest endeavour at all
times to uphold the dignity of this House.
and those high traditions and that lofty
standard of conduct so admirably maintained by the late Lord Dufferin, and
finally, to the very best of my ability,
God helping me, to merit and to justify
that confidence which hon. Members of
this House have been good enough to
repose in me.
The Senate having again unanimously
called Viscount Bangor to the Chair, he
was taken out of his place and conducted
to the Chair by Mr. Armstrong and Mr.
Campbell.
9 OCTOBER 1930
Mr.
I find it
far from easy to express in words my deep
sense of the honour conferred upon me
by hon. Members of this House in selecting me to fill the important and responsible post of Speaker of the Senate
of Northern Ireland. A difficult task
lies in front of me, and for this reason.
It is indeed hard to succeed one who
occupied that Chair with such dignity,
distinction, and efficiency over a period
of nine years, ever since his Gracious
Majesty the King first opened the first
Parliament of Northern Ireland, and who
has been taken from our midst under such
painful, distressing, and terribly tragic
Lord Dufferin combined
consummate tact with impartiality and
courtesy. I am very well aware of my
own shortcomings, but the knowledge
that I am not amongst strangers, but
that I am amongst friends will make
matters a great deal easier for me. I
am personally acquainted with every
Member of this House, and I think that,
as the hon. and learned Member opposite
(Mr. Campbell) has said, I may count
each of them as a friend.
circumstances.
I should like to take this opportunity
of tendering my sincere thanks to the
Members of this House for the great
honour they have done me in electing me
as Speaker, and I should also like to
thank the proposer and the seconder and
supporter of the Motion. I should like
also to express my sincere gratitude for
the unfailing kindness and consideration.
which I have always received when I
acted as Deputy-Leader of this House,
a kindness and consideration which I feel
sure, if I may say so, will be extended
to me in my future capacity. It is my
sincere hope that I may be allowed to
follow in the footsteps of my distinguished predecessor, the late Marquess
of Dufferin and Ava, whose memory will
with us ever remain green, and whose
loss to this House and to the whole of
Northern Ireland we shall never cease to
deplore.

and Measures Acts with respect to
measuring instruments and the power to
charge fees, and the provision of local
sub-standards and scales in connection
with the testing of weighing and measuring apparatus, and with respect to the
administration of the said Acts in certain
parts," presented by Viscount Charlemont, read a first time, and ordered to
be read a Second time upon Tuesday
next. (Bill S.3).
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am not
in a position to proceed with this Bill
to-day.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Tuesday next."

Adjourned accordingly at a
Quarter to Four o'clock.
