Questions to Minister.
NORTHERN IRELAND'S CONTRI-
BUTION TO IMPERIAL SERVICES
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader
of the House whether the Imperial
Government have made any representations to the Government concerning the
fall in the contribution of Northern Ireland to the Imperial Services from over
£7,000,000 for the year 1921 to
£600,000 for the year 1929, and
£150,000 for the year 1930; whether it
is a fact that in 1929 the Imperial
Government contributed £191,000 towards buildings, and £335,000 towards
Unemployment Insurance in Northern
Ireland and if he can state what are
the Imperial contributions for these
purposes in 1930?
Viscount
CHARLEMONT (Leader
of the House): No representations have
been made by the Imperial Government
to the Government of Northern Ireland relative to the fall in the
a
Imperial contribution from approximately £7,000,000, as fixed by the
Government of Ireland Act, to
£150,000, as provisionally fixed for the
present financial year. These reductions have been made annually by the
Joint Exchequer Board operating under
the powers conferred by the Government of Ireland Act and later British
legislation. That Board, as the hon.
Member is no doubt aware, is composed
of an independent Chairman, a representative of the British Treasury, and
representative appointed by the
Minister of Finance for Northern Ireland. In arriving at their annual estiinate of what the Imperial contribution
should be, they are guided by the report
of the Colwyn Committee which, by
agreement between the two Governments, was set up in the year 1923, and
finally reported two years later as to
the method by which the Imperial contribution should be assessed. The
principles of their recommendations
have been followed ever since, and they
operate to reduce the contribution
the manner indicated by the hon.
Member.
With regard to
to the contributions
made by the Imperial Government in
the year 1929 towards the cost of buildings in Northern Ireland and by way oi
relief to the Northern Ireland Unemployment Insurance Fund, the amounts
quoted by the hon. Member are correct.
The corresponding amounts for the current year are £333,000 for public buildings, and £533,000 in respect of Unemployment Insurance. With regard
to the former of these contributions, I
would point out that the Government of
Ireland Act required the Imperial
Government to provide this Parliament
and Government with permanent buildings and departmental offices, and I consider, therefore, that it is a misnomer to
refer to this payment as a contribution.
It is an amount spent in the provision of
a statutory service, for which the Imperial Government is liable.
As regards their contribution to our
Unemployment Insurance Fund, I presume that the hon. Member is not averse
to receiving this payment, which the
British Government generously has by
statute undertaken to pay in order to
maintain our two Unemployment Funds
in parity.
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
14 OCTOBER 1930
I would point out in conclusion that
the effect of these financial arrangements
apart from the special gift of Land
Purchase Annuity interest and the cost
of new buildings is to place the peopie
of Northern Ireland in the same position
as they would have been if Ireland had
remained directly under the Imperial
Parliament at Westminster.
EXECUTION OF FREE STATE
WARRANTS IN NORTHERN
IRELAND.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the police in
Northern Ireland still co-operate in the
execution of warrants issued by the
Courts of the Irish Free State against
persons who may be found in Northern
Ireland after conviction of offences in
the Irish Free State; and whether the
police in the Irish Free State still cooperate in the execution of warrants
issued by the Courts of Northern Ireland
against persons who may be found in the
Irish Free State after conviction of
offences in Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Free
State authorities now hold the view that
warrants issued in Northern Ireland are
no longer executable in the Free State.
Notwithstanding the gravity of the
situation which has thus arisen, and in
spite of the urgent representations on
the subject which have been made by the
Government of Northern Ireland, no
tangible steps have as yet been taken by
the Free State Government to legalise
the continuance of the former reciprocal
arrangement for the execution of
warrants.
Having regard to these facts, and to
the grave doubts which exist as to the
validity of executing warrants issued by
the Courts of the Free State, the Government of Northern Ireland has been
advised that any further execution of
such warrants by the Northern Ireland
authorities would not be justified.
I can only express the hope that a
satisfactory solution of the difficulty will
soon be found. (Hon. Members: Hear,
hear.)
Mr. CAMPBELL: In view of the very
great public importance of this matter
and in the interests of law and order
would it not be desirable, I ask the
Leader of the House, that negotiations
at least should be undertaken and continued with the Free State to try to
arrive at a modus vivendi.
I think
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
that, as this is a very important and very
intricate legal business; I would not like
to answer any question on the matter
without notice having been given. 1
would like to supply the hon. and learned
Senator with any information I can
possibly give on the subject, but I should
not like to make any statement myself.
STATE-AIDED HOUSING.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House if he can state the total number of dwelling-houses completed in
Northern Ireland with State help under
the Housing Acts since the Armistice, the
total expenditure of public money on
such houses, and the average weekly
rents of such houses in urban areas; and
the total number of dwelling-houses built
with State help to re-house slum tenants
under slum clearance schemes during the
same period.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The total
number of Dwelling-houses completed in
Northern Ireland with State help under
the Housing Acts since the Armistice is
19,855. The total expenditure of
public money thereon is approximately
£2,013,133.
I am unable to give the hon. Member
particulars of the rents charged in respect
of houses erected by private builders.
With regard to the rents charged by
local authorities, these vary very considerably according to the type of house
erected. Outside Belfast the lowest
rent in respect of new houses is 5/- per
week and the highest 15/- per week.
The average would be from 8/6 to 9/-
per week.
In Belfast the rents range from 13/-
to 22/- per week, except in the case of
Seaview, where the weekly payment,
under the Purchase Scheme, works out
at from 8/5 to 8/11 per week. The
houses on this site of which there are a
very considerable number-are, of course,
of a smaller type than the other houses
built by the Corporation.
The total number of dwelling-houses
built with State help to re-house slum
tenants under the Slum Clearance
Scheme, is 20 in the town of Larne.
455 Government Administration--

THE SENATE
Censure Motion
Government Administration.
A CENSURE MOTION.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move:
"That this House views with disquiet the
long and deep depression in agriculture
and manufactures, the widespread unemployment, and the wasteful and inefficient
administration which have prevailed
during the tenure of office of the Government, regrets the failure of the Government to promote a return to prosperity,
and deems its continuance in office incompatible with the public welfare." The
position in Northern Ireland from the
standpoints of town and country is one
of unrelieved blackness. Affairs are going
from bad to worse, without a check, and
the Government is bankrupt of ideas.
The latest annual agricultural returns
show a decrease of over 28,000 acres under
crops, as compared with the previous
year.
The
The ploughed land of Northern
Ireland is down to about 53,500 acres,
the lowest area in any year since the
famine of 1847. Flax growing in Northern Ireland is less by about 6,000 acres, a
record in lowness of yield only passed
thrice in 83 years. The potato-raising
area is down by 15,000 acres.
number of milch cows has decreased by
nearly 12,000. There are now only some
672,000 cattle in the province. There is
a decrease relating to the linen trade.
The exports of linen for May, 1930, were,
I have read, 24 per cent. less in value
than for May, 1929. In 1924 the value
of linen goods exported from Northern
Ireland was approximately 18 millions,
while the figure for 1928 was £14,760,000,
a decrease of almost £4,000,000. Our
linen exports in August were, I learn,
25 per cent. less in value than the average amounts per month since the beginning of 1930. Take a few figures relating
to the traffic through the port of Belfast.
For the half-year ending 30th June
last there was a fall of 56,905 tons in
the export of potatoes, and 16,211 tons
of linen were exported from Belfast in
those six months, by contrast with 17,477
tons for the corresponding period of
1929. The £100 ordinary stock of the
railway company serving the widest area
in Northern Ireland, which once soared
to £180 in price, was on sale in Belfast
this month for less than £20. Dozens of
mills and factories have been closed down
-others are working fitfully. Most of
these concerns working part time have
paid no dividends for years. Need I
speak of the distress among the shipbuilding trades? The recent closing of
the East Yard of the Queen's Island has
meant 2,000 more thrown idle to glut
the labour market. To meander through
the working-class quarters of Belfast of
late has been a depressing experienceto see crowds of young men, not of manhood years, standing idle at the corners
or shooting marbles or kicking football or
playing cards on the pavements--and this
at mid-day. Then to find the queues
besieging picture houses soon after midday, and these houses thronged through
the long summer afternoons with young
men and young women in receipt of the
dole."

Emigrant ships sail from our shores
with full quotas. I read lately in a
leading Conservative journal that these
Six Counties threaten in the passage of
years to become a territory inhabited
mostly by old-age pensioners and the very
young. On the 6th inst. the persons
seeking work on the registers of the employment exchanges numbered 70,443about one-sixth of our working population-of whom the totally unemployed
reached the appalling number of 54,278.
This figure of 70,000 was 35,000 more
than a year before. Unemployment is
100 per cent. worse. The story of unemployment ploughs a long and deep furrow
through the nine years of Lord Craigavon's administration. We have had in
times past recurring cycles of unemploy
ment. We have had ups and downs. But
under his premiership we have had a continuous and heavy overplus of labour
which has never been absorbed, and which
has been growing more and more swollen.
Let us look at a Unionist picture of
the situation. Speaking on the 30th
August at a meeting of the Black Society
at Rathfriland, Mr. W. E. D. Allen.
Member for West Belfast in the British
House of Commons, said:
It was important to remember that
during the last twelve years when nothing, or worse than nothing, has been
done, a majority of Conservative Members had sat in the House of Commons
for ten of these years. During that
time a majority pledged to Free Trade
a majority of Liberals and Socialists
--had only held power for two years.
What has been the story of those years
in Northern Ireland? All about the
Six Counties they heard of farmers
bankrupt and the lands going out of
cultivation. All about the Six Counties
457 Government Administration-

11 OCTOBER 1930
Censure Motion
In the
the mills were closing down.
Belfast shipyards they saw the slips
emptier and emptier every month. In
West Belfast we had over twenty
thousand unemployed. He was ashamed
to go among them-for what could he
say to them?
These asked for work,
and they give them the dole-grudgingly and suspiciously as if they were
paupers and beggars.
That is a Unionist M.P.'s sketch of the
tragic outlook in Northern Ireland. During nine of those twelve years Lord Craigavon and his friends have been in office.
Is the Province any the better? Is the
working man, the business man, the farmer any the richer? No doubt you give
the "dole," thanks to the generous help
of the British Treasury upon which the
Province is a pensioner, a suppliant for
annual alms. You heart never was and
is not in the "dole." When you gave the
"dole" it was "Hobson's choice" for you.
In
"The mass of mankind," Mr. Gladstone
said, "is composed of men, women and
children who can but just ward off hunger,
cold and nakedness." That is the lot of
so many Ulster folk. They eat the bread
of sorrow. You cannot disguise this. You
cannot camouflage this grim reality. What
have you done for them? How have you
helped them? You have hardly lifted a
little finger to lighten their burden.
an area smaller than Yorkshire, with a
population a little larger than that of the
City of Glasgow you cumber and crush the
Province with the
pomp and circumstance
and outlay of an independent State.
A critic has said, "You have a crushing
load of pauperism at one end of the social
scale and a crushing load of officialdom at
the other." You have a Cabinet of sevenjust men all--(laughter)--Great Britain,
on the ratio of population, should have a
Cabinet of 250. Your Government scientifically excludes from the public administration, whether salaried or voluntary,
people of the Nationalist persuasion. The
Nationalists share no responsibility for the
unhappy circumstance that Northern Ireland is agriculturally and industrially sick
to death.
Your Government has done
nothing to solve the problem of unemployment. That is a problem within its province. British Ministers have, of course,
their unemployment problem, but the
position here is worse.
Your supporters know, too, that you
have brought the North to an economic
impasse. Test your administration by a
comparison of the state of this area in
1921 when you took it over from the Imperial authorities and its state to-day.
The inquirer will find that the sun does
not set on a Government in any Dominion
with a more disastrous record. In this
crisis the Government, true to type, is
doing what a Tory Government always
does that is to say, is doing nothing in
particular, like the House of Lords in the
opera, and doing it very well.
Northern Ireland's contribution to the
Imperial Services fixed at £7,000,000 for
the year 1922 is down to £150,000 for
this year and is nearly zero. Great
Britain is none the richer for Northern
Ireland.
I note that the trade of the Irish Free
State for the year ended August, 1926,
was £110,000,000, and its adverse trade
balance £18,000,000. For the year ended
August, 1930, its trade was £107,000,000,
and its adverse trade balance was down
to £10,000,000. A Ministerial Whip
said a short time ago that what Ulster
wants is more drums. (Laughter.)
CUNNINGHAM:
Mr. JOSEPH
That will waken them up.
Mr. CAMPBELL: It will make their
flesh creep at least once a year.
More
drums! When not one solitary slum
tenant has been helped by the Government during the past nine years under a
re-housing scheme to leave his sordid
tenement. The grievances of town tenants remain unredressed. The Prime
Minister spoke on the subject of town
landlord and tenant in Ballymena in
June, 1929, these words: "That is about
as thorny a subject as Local Option. It
would require probably more wisdom to
deal with than any other of the social
problems, but I am not afraid to tackle
it." The Prime Minister tackles nothing
-touches nothing-that he does not
adjourn. He went on: "Of course in
regard to the subject of these leaseholders, I hope that you and others outside will always bear in mind this, that
whereas in the old days there was a
certain odium resting upon landlords-it
was almost as bad as being a lawyer politician-the whole social fabric has
changed." Then follows a vain excuse
for doing nothing. The Prime Minister
should not tilt at lawyer politiciansthere are some in his own ranks. Besides,
Lord Carson might have a word to say.
Perhaps Senator Leslie might have a
word to say.
459 Government Administration-

THE SENATE
Censure Motion
[Mr. Campbell]
The Government's puny schemes to relieve unemployment remind one of Mrs.
Partington with her mop trying to roll
back the Atlantic. See-saw is the
fashion in English politics. But our
Ministers lay the flattering unction to
their souls that the electors have given
them a lease of their portfolios for life.
Queen Victoria's first Prime Minister,
Lord Melbourne, on the verge of his fall
from power was likened by a celebrated
historian to a pampered old hall porter
who, dozing in his chair, begins to look
on it as an act of rudeness if any visitor
to his master presumes to knock at the
door and so disturb him from his comfortable slumbers. Pray do not disturb
the comfortable slumbers of this sluggard
Government. Let it sleep on. Reject
this motion if you view with complacency
the falling fortunes of our agriculture and
manufactures. Reject it if you see
nothing disturbing in the spectacle of the
huge army of workless men and women
at your doors. Reject it if you see the
faintest glimmer of the dawn of prosperity. Reject it if you see any signs of
statesmanlike initiative in this Government. Reject it you may, but what will
the people of the North say about your
vote?
country finds itself. They neither have
provided employment nor brought about
unemployment. I quite agree, but I do
not think that even Mr. Campbell and
his friends have any reason to wash their
hands clear of responsibility. If they
were doing what was fair and
and
proper
were sincerely interested in the welfare
of the workers of Northern Ireland they
would put forward suggestions, if they
have any, to improve this terrible position
in which we find ourselves.
Mr. Campbell has made a charge
against the Government of wasteful and
inefficient administration. I listened
very attentively to his speech, and I do
not think he has produced any evidence.
to substantiate the charge that there is
anything wasteful or inefficient in the
administration. It is quite true that the
Government has spent a considerable
amount of money in trying to create employment. A considerable amount of
money has been spent on roads and
bridges, on docks, and on workers'
houses about which Mr. Campbell is just
after obtaining some information-9,855
houses completed-and many other
schemes of public utility in Northern Ireland, all for the purpose of trying to
alleviate the distress which is prevalent
owing to this depression which Mr. Campbell has referred to. And the workers
of Northern Ireland are not getting anything more-although he referred in a
rather sarcastic way to the dole-than
they receive on the other side of the
Channel. I question if they are as well
treated in Belfast and Derry and in the
cities of Northern Ireland as they are in
Poor-law
some places across the water.
manu-
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: The
Motion, moved by the hon. and learned
Member (Mr. Campbell), in some of its
parts would, I believe, obtain the assent
of every Member of this House. Indeed
I am satisfied that all sensible men and
all sensible persons in the North of Ireland sincerely deplore the continued depression in agriculture and in
factures with the attendant widespread
unemployment. We have waited from
month to month and from year to year
hoping and watching for an improvement
to take place. And I know there is a
considerable number of very decent industrious workingmen whose patience is
almost exhausted and whose last pound,
that they have saved during normal
times, has been spent in trying to tide
them over this abnormal period. But,
unfortunately,. there has been, or is, no
improvement up to the present.
It is quite true as Mr. Campbell states
that it has been getting worse during the
past few years. But it must be a considerable consolation to Mr. Campbell
and his friends to be able to wash their
hands and say they have no responsibility for this terrible state in which the
relief is not administered in Belfast and
Northern Ireland as a whole, like what it
is in some cities across the water.
is to some extent unfortunate.
Mr. DICKSON: West Ham.
That
I
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: But
when the Government is accused of wasteful and inefficient administration surely
there is no evidence to substantiate that,
and Mr. Campbell never used one word
to show where there was any waste.
have been through the labour exchanges
and through the Ministry of Labour, and
I must admit that I see no wastefulness
there and no unnecessary employment of
Repeatedly
my business takes me in there, and I find
members of the staff very busily engaged
in doing what every other worker outside
the members of the staff.
461 Government Administration---

14 OCTOBER 1930
Censure Motion
would be doing, busily looking after the
interests of their employers, the Ministry
of Labour.
Mr. Campbell also in the Resolution
regrets the failure of the Government to
promote the return to prosperity. Surely
Mr. Campbell or the draughtsman of
that resolution is not serious when he
states that. It is not in the North of
Ireland alone that this terrible depression exists. The depression is worldwide, and in expressing surprise that the
Government has not been able to promote
a return to prosperity, they might as well
and with as much reason say they regret
the Government has not been able to
produce better weather during the past
season. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
How could it possibly be expected that
Northern Ireland would be in any better
position than she is in at the present time?
Bear in mind we are not in as bad a
position in this particular part of the
country as they are in parts of England,
Scotland and Wales.
We are hedged in in Northern Ireland
by the Free State, which has only
recently imposed import taxes to protect
her own industry and her own trade and,
at the same time, to injure Northern Ireland to some little extent. We are connected with the British Isles where the
markets are open to the competition of
the foreigner and open to the importation
of foreign work. And at the present
time in England there are a number of
statesmen of the British Empire and
other experts from the colonies of the
British Empire who are carefully considering what may be done to
remove
this terrible depression and to help in
some way to relieve unemployment. Remarkable to say too, that the recommendation of that conference of these
statesmen from the Dominions is a recommendation in favour of the policy
which the Unionist party, of which I am
sure Mr. Campbell is not very fond, has
been advocating for years-some policy of
safeguarding and Empire marketing.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Taxation of food.
No
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
taxation of food, but the protection of
the British workman's labour against the
foreigner's labour. Let me hope that if
this question comes up to be decided in
the Imperial House of Commons that Mr.
Campbell will use his influence with his
colleagues in the Imperial House so that
they will vote in favour of protecting the
labour of the British workman in preference to the labour of the foreigner,
because if the British Government has
been more or less apathetic I understand,
from some remarks made by Mr. Allen,
that the British Government has been
very anxious to introduce that policy and
carry it through-
Mr. CAMPBELL: They never did.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM
but on every occasion the Irish Nationalist
party voted with the Free Trade people
in opposition to the Unionist party and
North of Ireland people who are now
suffering from unfair foreign competition. But to say they are surprised
or regret the failure of the Government
to promote the return of prosperity is a
statement which I think Mr. Campbell is
making with his tongue in his cheek.
There are a few politicians in Northern
Ireland who claim that they could remove
all the unemployment in Northern Ireland if intoxicating liquor were prohibited. I do not know whether Mr.
Campbell would join with that party in
trying to bring about the object they have
in view.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I thought they were
your party.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: And
yet although our Government has been so
considerably handicapped by foreign competition and the import taxes reared
against us in the Free State, it must be
admitted that the Government has done
remarkably well. They have been able to
obtain work for the shipyards and to keep
them fairly busy when the shipyards on
the other side of the Channel have been
unable to obtain work. The shipbuilding employers have referred to
the great benefit the Loans Guarantee
Acts have been, and the workers
appreciate it and know perfectly well
it has provided thousands of men with
good work and good wages in the past
few years. If it was not for the fact that
there are no orders in the market to-day
even for the best British shipyards, I
believe Belfast would still be able to
obtain a fair proportion of any shipping
that might be in the market. Unfortunately it is not so. I believe that when
shipbuilding improves there is none will
benefit quicker than the Belfast shipyards
because they have a good class of workman
who turn out a good class of job.
463 Government Administration-

THE SENATE
Censure Motion
[Mr. Joseph Cunningham]
Then again, linen has been referred to.
Surely the Government has done all that
is possible to improve the linen trade and
to assist them, although they are considerably handicapped by foreign competition. They have been supplied with
money to advertise in America, and unfortunately sometimes those who are not
fond of, or do not particularly love
Northern Ireland, try to do what injury
they can even there against our linen.
Everything has been done that is possible
to help to improve the linen industry.
Indeed I remember very well that when
the Premier visited New Zealand he did
his very
best there to advocate the
purchase of Irish linen, better than even
a paid traveller. He is interested indeed
in the welfare of the workers of Northern
Ireland, both the shipbuilding and the
linen and any other industries that can be
promoted in the Six Counties.
Then we are asked to support the Resolution which deems the continuance of the
present Government incompatible with
the public welfare. I would like to know
who is likely to take their place, who
would succeed any better than the present
Government.
Mr. MCALLISTER: They could not
do worse.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: It
seems to me as if the Opposition in this
House have an envy for the seats of the
mighty on the other side of the Table. I
think probably we might have a rehearsal
some afternoon and see how well they
would look, and see whether Mr. Dorman
would associate himself with Mr. Campbell and his friends. But I fail to see how
more could be done by the present
Government. Abuse is easy. It is easy
to criticise, but I do not think there is
anything better to be done than has been
done by the present Government. We
must recognise that we are in a better
position than many districts in England,
Scotland and Wales. Shipbuilding is
certainly better and we have never
suffered the same privations or the same
amount of unemployment as they have
suffered there. I hope the Government
may long continue in office and I sincerely
hope that the Unionist Party policy will
be adopted shortly and I believe we will
see an improvement not only in Northern
Ireland but in England, Scotland and
Wales. (Hon. Members: Hear, hear.)
Mr. McALLISTER: Before making
the few remarks I intend to address to
the Motion before the House, I should
like to say, at the first opportunity I have
of speaking under your guidance, Mr.
Speaker, that with your inherent natural
courtesy, and the obvious anxiety you
have always shown to faithfully and
honestly discharge the onerous duties of
your high and responsible office, you will
have the whole-hearted co-operation and
assistance of every Member of this House.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) I want to
make only one reference to the speech of
Senator Joseph Cunningham, who has
just replied to my colleague, Senator
Campbell. I think he described in the
most fitting way possible what any thinking person would accept as the position
of the Government during the last few
years. He described the Government as
hoping, waiting, and watching for something to turn up. That is exactly what
we say about the Government. I do not
intend to address myself to the general
question of unemployment, but I do wish
to emphasise the fact that the one indispensable industry in Northern Ireland is
agriculture, and that without a successful
agricultural industry you can have
neither a successful Government nor a
successful people. I make no excuse for
again trying to labour the question in
this House or trying to bring home to
the Government the responsibility they
have to the farmers of Northern Ireland.
I am quite convinced from what I have
seen that the Government have no idea
of the depth to which the agricultural
industry in Northern Ireland has fallen.
It is people like myself who live our
daily lives amongst the farmers of Northern Ireland who know their position, their
hard thoughts, and the awful depression
that exists amongst them at the present
time. I do not address myself to this
Motion merely from the point of view
of the general good, because I realise that
the prosperity of agriculture or the failure
of agriculture means the prosperity or
failure of industry as a whole.
Therefore, I say that from a purely personal
point of view I am anxious that something should be done to regenerate_this
once great industry in our midst. I am
quite certain that the Government have
been pursuing their activities from an
entirely wrong standpoint in recent years,
and may I say why? What is the good
of teaching farmers or assisting farmers
to grow a great abundance of potatoes
465 Government Administration--

14 OCTOBER 1930
Censure Motion
when those who can sell them have to sell
at 10s a ton, and those who cannot sell
them have to throw them at the back of
the ditches. What is the good of encouraging farmers to grow flax when
the price of flax at the best markets in
Northern Ireland last week ranged from
3s to 5s a stone. That would not pay
for the seed, the manure, and the scutching of the flax. Then there is the cost of
labour, the trouble of getting the ground
ready, sowing the seed, weeding, watering,
pulling, spreading, stooking, and stacking. For all that there is not a single
penny returned to the farmer.
a
Therefore, I say the Government have
been pursuing their activities from
wrong standpoint entirely. If they want
to benefit the farmers of Northern Ireland let them address themselves to the
marketing of the produce. Let them
secure a position by which the farmer will
buy wholesale and sell retail, instead of
buying retail and selling wholesale as he
is at present. The best service the Government can render to the agricultural industry of Northern Ireland is to create
machinery for the organised and collective
selling and buying of the produce of the
farmers of Northern Ireland, to create
fit and profitable markets in London,
Glasgow, and other large cities across the
water. That would be a profitable thing
to do. Ever since I came into public
life I have never lost an opportunity of
voicing these views. I may have been
considered an idealist, but I would like to
point out that the Prime Minister of
England, during the last two months,
stated that, if necessary, compulsion
would be brought to bear on the farmers
to organise. Therefore, if I am
idealist he also is an idealist.
an
One thing I take great exception to is
the small, futile, pin-pricking legislation
we have been passing in Northern Ireland.
It is only harassing and embarrassing
and rendering more difficult the position
of farmers in the Province. There is only
one explanation of this legislation, and
that is that we have a Parliament in Northern Ireland that must sit sometimes.
If it sits, it must pass legislation, and,
therefore, this futile and useless legislation
is brought in to harass and embarrass
farmers. Let me give the House an instance. Only last year we passed a Bill
for the grading of potatoes. We were
told what beneficial effects it would have.
but so far as potatoes are concerned the
sale of potatoes was never as difficult and
never as uneconomic as it is to-day, nor
was it ever such a huge and colossal failure
as it was after the passing of that Act
I do condemn the Government in no
unmeasured terms, but from no party
point of view, on account of their neglect
of this great industry. I do not think I
exaggerate when I say that the only effort
made by any Member of the Government
in recent years which struck the ears of
the public was the remark made by the
Prime Minister when he was studying conditions in New Zealand and Australia,
that he had come home here to teach the
farmers of Mourne and Antrim how to
grow potatoes and flax on the top of the
Mountains of Mourne, with their flocks
separated by fuchsia hedges in flower
in the late autumn. I think that is a
fair description of the futile remark made
by the Prime Minister of Northern Ireland.
Another point of view in which I am
sure I shall be supported by every practical farmer in Northern Ireland is this:
that the Government of Northern Ireland
are pursuing their activities, in many
directions, upon an entirely wrong course.
Take the matter of dairying. Forty or
fifty years ago Denmark was out of the
running so far as the dairying industry
is concerned. Since then, year by year,
they have concentrated on the question of
dairying, and they have produced a stock
capable of producing a large yield of
milk. Fifty years ago we had a wellknown dairy cow, the most economic cow
in the world to keep, most fruitful in
yield of milk, and the most suitable to
the soil and climate. But by direction of
the present Government, and, I must say
in fairness of their predecessors in Dublin
Castle, those old milking cattle in Northern Ireland have been bred out of the
country and the farmers are called upon
to support an animal which is uneconomic,
with the result that the dairying industry
has almost entirely left the country. I
know it is the view of many practical
farmers that in this uncertain and difficult
climate dairying is a branch of farming
with the greatest possibilities, and I think
that is so. But it would take years and
vears to get back to the position we held
in this industry before we were misled
and misguided by various Government
Departments.
There is another aspect of the question,
and it is this. We are told that education
in Denmark has been one of the chief
causes of that country's progress in agri-
467 Government Administration-

THE SENATE
Censure Motion
[Mr. McAllister]
culture during the last forty or fifty years.
I do not know whether there will be any
objection to this or not, but I put forward
the suggestion that the children in the
rural districts of Northern Ireland should
be taught something of the principles of
agriculture upon which they may afterwards have to rely to eke out their existence and earn their living. I cannot
speak with technical knowledge, but as an
ordinary citizen interested in this matter
I feel that there is no time when you can
better catch the imagination of a person
who has a certain future before him than
in his early youth. Therefore, I think it
would be a great advantage if more attention was paid to the teaching of agriculture to children in the rural areas of
Northern Ireland, and I will go further
and say that in the course of that teaching some little practical demonstration
should be provided for them.
There is only one other remark I would
like to make, and it is this. One of the
difficulties I have heard put forward
against collective buying and selling is
that it is not practicable from the point
of view of transport. I know that the
transport companies were invited to give
evidence on this matter before the only
Committee that was set up to investigate
this question during my period of public.
life, and they refused. That should not
be a bar to what might prove to be a
great opportunity to save the agricultural
industry in Northern Ireland, and I
would say it is the duty of the Government to create machinery to provide such
ships as might be necessary to carry the
produce of farmers to the markets in
England, and to carry back artificial
manures and feeding stuffs so as to render this great and staple industry more.
economic and more profitable than it is at
the present time.
Mr. McCORKELL: I do not want to
occupy the time of the House too long,
but I am seldom here and I happen to
have come on a day when a subject of
which I know something is being discussed. I say without fear of contradiction that there are many practical farmers who, like myself, will say that the
Government has done its best for agriculture, and that the blame for the hard
times at present being experienced cannot be laid at the door of that Government. The hon. and learned Member
who moved the Motion (Mr. Campbell)
started off by saying that the Government was bankrupt in ideas.
In my
humble opinion the mover of the Motion
is full of ideas, but they are confused.
It is better to be bankrupt than to be
upside down, because you may recover
from bankruptcy but once you have lost
your equilibrium it is very difficult to
recover. I can assure the Government
and Members interested in this question
that you cannot and never will raise the
tillage statistics by any appreciable
amount until you do have something in
the nature of taxes on food. There are
those who would without examination
turn down any proposal which touched
on the taxation of food, but I believe the
view of the Government would be that a
prosperous country with money to buy
slightly dearer food is better than an unprosperous country without sufficient
money to buy the food that is grown.
To show how unjustly the Government is
condemned, the hon. and learned Member suggested that the Government encouraged the farmers to grow potatoes
which they had to sell at 10s a ton. The
Government showed them a way. I do
not think it is a perfect way.
They advised them if they could not sell their
potatoes to feed them to pigs. I know
what I am talking about, for I took that
advice and I have fed hundreds of tons
to pigs and have shipped those pigs. I
got a price, but it was not a good price.
Why? Because these people will not
allow us to put a tariff on. What is the
good of carrying goods to Glasgow or
London, or any other city, where the
sweated labour of the Continent is admitted. I ask some of these men if they
know what the French agricultural
labourer is paid, or what the Belgian
labourer is paid, or what the Dane is
paid. Ours, God knows, are paid low
enough, but they will not be paid any
better until something is done to help
industry on its feet, and it will not be
done by those who blame the Government
and stand in the way of a remedy.
good
con-
There is just one other point I would
like to mention. That is this, that it
is no good when things are going badly
to blame someone else. It is no
saying what is world-wide is merely
fined to our Province of Northern Ireland; and it is absurd to say if we are
a small community the size of Yorkshire
that any action by our Government,
whatever that Government may be,
is going to put the world on its feet. I
469 Government Administration--

11 OCTOBER 1930
Censure Motion
stand very strongly in support of this
Government because I have followed them
carefully since their inauguration; and
I must say they had had the misfortune
to go through a period when the world.
has been going through a very bad time.
I am satisfied in this; that even if we are
not as prosperous as we might be, under
any alternative Government we have for
Northern Ireland, we would be a very
great deal worse.
re-
Sir WILLIAM COATES: My hon.
Friend opposite has just spoken on the
agricultural question. The mover of the
Resolution mentioned some facts as
gards houses and so forth, and unemployment. Now, I join with what hon.
Senator Cunningham has said that this
House views with disquiet the long and
deep depression in agriculture and manufactures, and the widespread unemployment, but I would like the Resolution to
stop there, because I think the rest of it
is most unfair criticism of the present
position. I wonder what would happen
if the mover of this Resolution happened
to go over to Westminster, as perhaps he
probably will, and there he would move
the same Resolution as he has moved today condemning the then Government. I
wonder what his friends over there would
think of his action. But I would like
to speak for the local authority which I
represent here. I would like to tell the
House that the Belfast Corporation has
pledged the future for years to come to
provide employment, and all with the
help of the Government which the mover
of this Resolution designates as wasteful
and inefficient. I should like to give the
House some statistics, but I think it
would be better not to bore them with it
at the moment. I certainly cannot agree
with the suggestion put forward in the
latter part of the Resolution, and for
myself I shall be very glad to vote against
the Resolution.
Mr. DORMAN: I think it was Lord
Byron who said that a man must "serve
a
time to every trade
save censure.
Critics all are ready made." It is an extremely difficult thing to criticise a Government above all things until one first
has worked out a clear idea of what a
Government is, and what its functions
ought to be in any community. Society
is probably the most complex thing in the
Universe. It is the product probably of
hundreds of thousands of years of effort
the
on the part of the human race to overcome the difficulties that confronted them
in supplying their needs of food, clothing,
and shelter from day to day. And the
function of a Government ought to be to
endeavour to arrive at the most equitable
distribution of the product of
associated labour of the citizens in
society. And unless one has first analysed
closely, and for years how that product is
distributed in the community, one is
not competent to pass judgment upon
the functions of a Government. That
product of the associated labour of the
citizens is divided into five different
sections rent, interest, profit, salary, and
wages.
What is the position in Great Britain
to-day with regard to the distribution of
that five-fold division during the past
three years? It is this, that the citizens
The
who are the most numerous division of
these five divisions are the only division
of the citizens who have the percentage
of their income reduced, whereas the
recipients of rent, interest, profit, and
salaries, according to an analysis of the
revenue returns of Great Britain and
Northern Ireland, have considerably increased during the last three years.
spending capacity of the vast majority of
the people, the wage earners has been
reduced, and thereby a most profitable
market has been cut away by the reduc
tion of the purchasing capacity of the
people who constitute the working classes.
It is a world problem which no Government can successfully contend with unless that Government is prepared to attack
the basic foundation of the question. It
is not protection from those who are outside that is so much required as protection
from those who are within.
I do not intend to occupy the time of
this House at any great length in discussing this question. But what is the
position? Let us take a local illustration.
I understand that land in the centre of
the area over which the right hon. Gentleman the Lord Mayor of Belfast presides
with such dignity was sold in the vicinity
of the City Hall-the Linen Hall it was,
and is now the City Hall-in the year
1829 for from £10 to £20 an
acre. I
am now informed by an eminent member
of the legal profession, who has access to
documents, that in the centre of the City
of Belfast to-day merchants are paying
as much as £50 per foot frontage for a
depth of 280 feet. That tots up to
£9,500 per acre, or if the descendant of
471 Government Administration-

THE SENATE
Censure Motion
[Mr. Dorman]
the man who purchased an acre of that
land a hundred years ago now desired to
acquire an acre of any such land he would
have to pay £200,000 to £400,000 in
order to make him the proprietor in feesimple of that portion of the centre of
our city. These are the problems that
should be attacked, but our Governments
are not prepared to attack these problems,
because most of our Governments are determined to preserve what they call
ancient rights, which, in my opinion, are
too often the foundation of modern
wrongs.
I happened to take down a celebrated
book during last week that I have not
seen for some years, the "Tour of Arthur
Young in Ireland in the Year 1784," a
time when there was a large depression
in trade. He was the guest, I understand, of the then Earl of Antrim, and
he discovered that the then Earl's father
had let practically the whole of his estate
in East Antrim for a rental of £10,000
per year. The occupiers and cultivators
of that portion of County Antrim of the
estate of the then Earl of Antrim were
paying to the leaseholders, who held from
the Earl's father, a sum of £74,000 per
year. In other words, there was a parasitic class existing in the community, and
for every pound they paid to the owner
of the original fee-simple in the form of
leasehold rent they were pocketing £5 10s
or approximately 550 per cent. These
are the problems which the Governments
ought to face.
Then I see that my hon. Friend,
Senator Cunningham, proposes protection
as a scheme to remedy this state of affairs.
May I inform the hon. Senator that there
is one State in the Great British Commonwealth of Nations that has been distinguished above all the other States for
its protective policy, inasmuch as both
the Tory and the Liberal for three generations in that particular State at election
after election have been trying to capture
some particular commodity which they
could put a tariff on, in order to secure
the votes, when a general election is
approaching in the Dominion Parliament.
But inside the barrier of protection there
has sprung up in Canada big rings and
combines, and they enrich themselves to
an enormous extent owing to the advantages they can take of the consumers
inside by the inflation of the prices of
products. In that State when there is a
10 per cent. tariff put on not only do
producers and manufacturers inside raise
the price of commodities they are selling
to people inside the barrier by the 10 per
cent tariff, but for 60 years they have
put on from 7 per cent. to 10 per cent.
over and above the tariff which was put
on.
And if tariffs and Protection would be
a solution, then the Canadian unemployment question should be solved. It
should be solved in all our Australian
colonies. It should be solved in New
Zealand. It should be solved in the
United States of America and in Germany. But it is not solved in these
protected countries, because when properly examined Protection is no solution.
What we want is Free Trade and the
free distribution of the products of the
earth. I do not rise therefore to blame
the present Government in one sense. I
recognise how impossible it is for the
present Government, unless they are prepared to attack the basic foundation and
structure of society and change that
structure, to solve these great problems
confronting us from day to day. I would
like to refer to the remarks of some
eminent men at Unionist meetings held
recently in Belfast. One eminent lady
told us we could have no salvation unless
we changed the foundation of our fiscal
policy. I have already referred to that
and shown it was not a reality. Another
lady suggested that we should all save.
Fancy that, at a time when the markets
of the world are glutted, at a time when
the producers of commodities are unemployed, because the stores are all glutted
with goods an adviser of the present Government comes along with the uneconomic
nostrum that our well-being depends on
saving, and thereby reducing the present
demand for products.
I wonder how many of these advisers of
the Government have ever studied the
fallacy of saving. Saving is all right up
to a certain point, but beyond that point
it means economic destruction. The
Government in Great Britain has for
many years been pursuing a course in this
respect which has not been calculated to
reduce the poverty, misery, squalor, and
want which existed in that country.
Saving can only be effective when it is
set apart for the specific purpose of making good the wear and tear of production.
when a certain portion is set apart for
the specific purpose of adopting up-todate means of production, and for the
purpose of enabling scientific men to ad-
Government Administration-
Censure Motion
11 OCTOBER 1930
Vise as to the best and most efficient
method to meet the needs of the people
under existing circumstances. Saving
beyond that will find no satisfactory
source of investment inside the national
area. It must be sent abroad, and it is
sent abroad for the specific purpose of
developing the railways and the industries of other countries whose competition
we are complaining about and trying to
prevent.
Some time ago some hon. Members of
this House were in the smoke-room, and
Safeguarding was the subject of conversation. One Member asked, "Do you know
what you are asking for?" He added,
"Thirty per cent. of my product is going
to Germany, and twenty-five per cent. to
Czecho-Slovakia and coming back here in
the form of a finished product. If you
put up a tariff wall against this re-entry
I must dispose of fifty per cent. of those
engaged in the manufacture of the raw
material which is exported to Germany.
and Czecho-Slovakia. I do not want to
occupy the time of the House unnecessarily, but the watch I hold in my hand
illustrates the point I am trying to make.
It is a complex piece of machinery, and
if anything goes wrong with it I take it
to a watchmaker. The watchmaker has
built it up and understands its mechanism
and the relationship of one part to
another, and how necessary it is that the
parts should work in conformity one with
the other. I would not endeavour to
disorganise the watch I have in my hand.
I would take it to a man whose knowledge
of it is greater than my own, and I would
entrust it to him.
Society, economics and sociology are just
the same. It takes years of close and intensive study for one to have a clear and
comprehensive grasp of the
the complex
structure of the society in which one
lives. I do say that the conduct of the
Northern Government has been reprehensible in one respect. The Prime Minister
is the only statesman who has publicly
made a declaration, at a time when his
party was seeking the suffrages of the
electors, that whether or not Mr. Stanley
Baldwin and the Government across the
water solved this unemployment problem
he would tell the people of Ulster that he
would solve it.
Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM: And
we will do it yet.
Mr. DORMAN: Wait and see, and I
am afraid you will have to wait a long
time, unless the Government pursues a
different course in endeavouring to solve
the question. At that period there were
approximately 20,000 unemployed m
Northern Ireland, and to-day, according
to a speech delivered by a Minister at a
Unionist meeting the other day, there
are 60,000. Seven years to solve a problem which the Prime Minister of Northern Ireland said he would solve! I do
not accuse the right hon. Gentleman of
dishonestly making use of that statement.
I believe that when he innocently made
it he did not believe he would personally
solve the problem, but that he wanted to
convey to the electors that if they gave
him a majority in this Parliament he
would, in conjunction with his Ministers,
solve this great problem of unemployment. I say it is utterly impossible for
any Government to do that unless it is
prepared to attack the basic foundation of
the present social structure and endeavour to so legislate as to produce an
equitable and just distribution of the
products of all the people within the
realm.
Recently a friend made me a present of
an illustrated calendar which I always
look at with amazement. On the righthand side there is an aeroplane, and on the
other side an artillery gun. In the centre
is His Majesty the King in his Royal
Robes; on his right there is a lawyer, his
legal guide. On his left there is a Bishop
in his Canonicals, and in the centre under
the King there is a Dreadnought.
the right-hand top corner is an engineer
or smith, in the centre an agricultural
labourer with his rake; and in the lefthand lower corner a miner engaged in extracting raw material from the earth.
Under the King are the words, "I reign
for all"; under the lawyer, "I plead for
all"; under the Bishop, "I pray for all":
under the Dreadnought, "We fight for
all"; and underneath the three working
men engaged in extracting raw material
from the soil and manufacturing the products necessary for the well-being of the
community are the words, "We pay for
all."
No Government should receive and
continue to receive the confidence of the
people that has not for its object the
creation of a state of society whereby the
same rights, the same standard of comfort, and the same opportunities are
afforded to those of its people who by
475 Government Administration-

THE SENATE
Censure Motion
[Mr. Dorman]
the exercise of their labour pay for all,
as is afforded to other classes of the community. No people should be satisfied
until they bring about peacefully, legally,
and constitutionally, that state of affairs.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In replying to the Motion before the House I
would like to say at the beginning that
the Government is only too well aware of
the bad times through which agriculture.
and industry generally is passing, and the
regrettably high figures of unemployment
which are the result. I think the speeches
this afternoon have been extraordinarily
interesting, and I would like especially to
mention the speech of Senator McAllister.
Sitting where he does he has a perfect
right to criticise the Government from his
point of view, but at the same time one
does not often get criticism which contains suggestions such as did the speech.
of the hon. Senator. I cannot say
whether his suggestions are feasible or
not because I am not a skilled agriculturist, but perhaps I may suggest to him
that at some future time he should put
them down on the Paper in the form of
questions, when I might be able to give
a more detailed reply than I am able to
do in a speech dealing with such a subject
as that before the House. I think the
hon. Senator said the Government did
nothing but wait and hope.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I was merely
quoting what Senator Joseph Cunningham said.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
see why the Government should not hope,
but I think they have done something
better than wait. I think the best defence to the Motion is to give a general
idea of what the Government has done
to counteract this period of depression.
Anyone who takes the trouble to peruse
the newspapers will appreciate that it is
world-wide and is the result of factors
which are beyond the control of any one
Government. Nearly every country in
Europe is suffering from it, and I do not
believe the condition of affairs in the
United States is any too prosperous. The
most we as a Government can hope to do
is to use every effort to enable our industries to tide over this period, pending
the return of better times, and I hope to
show that the administration of the Government which I represent in this House
has been neither wasteful nor inefficient,
and that everything possible has been
done to give encouragement to our industries, and to maintain and even extend
the high standard of social services of
which this country is so proud.
I take firstly the financial facilities
afforded under the Loans Guarantee Acts.
Since 1923 continuous employment has
been directly provided as the result of
these measures for a number of workmen
varying from four thousand to nine
thousand. The value of contracts for
new ships which have been secured locally
under these facilities is not less than nineteen million pounds, towards the raising
of which the Government has pledged its
credit to the extent of, approximately.
eleven million pounds.
The Ministry of Finance, through its
Works Department, is at present engaged
on a considerable number of major
schemes, for example, the erection or reconstruction of police barracks; in this
year alone about thirty buildings have
been completed or are nearing completion,
and tenders have just been invited for
the erection of an additional number. It
is also hoped that an announcement will
shortly be made on the provision of a
Constabulary Depot. The Bann Drainage Scheme is familiar to Members of
the House, and I need not go again into
the details of what this very important
work will mean, both in the improvement of the surrounding area and also in
the matter of providing employment in
the scheme itself. The Imperial Authori
ties are also active in the erection of the
new Parliament Buildings and the new
Law Courts; while our Government has
undertaken on their behalf the provision
of a new Telephone Exchange in Belfast
and the improvement of buildings in the
Province generally for the Post Office.
Inland Revenue, and the Customs and
Excise Services. The Works Department of the Ministry of Finance have
also been directly responsible for considerable expenditure on buildings at Stranmillis Training College and for improvements to the agricultural institutions at
Strabane and Cookstown.
As a direct contribution towards the
relief of unemployment, the Ministry of
Labour has given grants in promotion of
schemes which are carried through by
local authorities and other public bodies.
Up to the present date about 1,500 such
schemes have been approved at a total
gross cost of about seven-and-a-half
million pounds. At the present moment
Government Administration-
Censure Motion
14 OCTOBER 1930
there are almost 2,000 men actually engaged on current work, apart altogether
from those who receive employment indirectly in connection with the manufacture and transport of materials and
so forth. The larger schemes which are
at the moment in hand are estimated to
cost over one-and-a-half millions; these
include street works in Belfast, the Lagan
Boulevards, the new Children's Hospital,
and the Maternity Block at the Royal
Victoria Hospital, the new dock in the
Belfast Harbour, the improvements to the
harbour entrance at Coleraine, drainage
in Portadown, and numerous other items.
Among those schemes which are approved
and which will shortly commence I may
mention further street works in Belfast
at a cost of about £350,000, the new loop
line of the L.M.S. Railway near Greenisland at two hundred thousand pounds,
Bangor Sewerage Scheme at one hundred
thousand pounds, the Altnaheglish dam
at the Londonderry Water Works
at a cost of one hundred and seventy-six
thousand pounds, the extending of the
Mater Hospital at fifty thousand pounds,
and several other major works.
In regard to the number of unemployed, the latest figures of persons
registered at our Exchanges is 70,443,
this includes nearly 13,000 who are temporarily stopped, and also some casually
unemployed. These figures are, of course,
regrettably high, but it should not be
overlooked that at one time they were
very much worse. When the Government
of Northern Ireland came into being the
number of wholly unemployed, together
with those on short time, was in the neighbourhood of 100,000.
In regard to the activities of the Ministry of Commerce, everything possible is
being done to improve the condition of
fishery harbours round the coast and the
work involved not only affords a welcome
measure of immediate employment, but is
also calculated to improve the condition
of the fishing industry generally and to
provide additional employment within
that industry itself. Works of improvement are being undertaken at the moment
at Dunseverick and at Ardglass, and
schemes are in preparation for improvements at the harbour at Bangor, and proposals for improvements at Annalong and
Ballycastle are also being considered.
The Government has also agreed to give
very substantial assistance for the improvement of the port of Newry.
The Ministry of Commerce is always
assisting the Ulster Tourist Development
Association by means of grants, and is
doing all it can to help the linen industry
in giving publicity to the trade in the
United States, Canada, and Great
Britain, and also it assists in its research
work.
The Ministry of Home Affairs in its
capacity as central authority for the
supervision of local bodies has also been
active, both in regard to works which are
considered urgently necessary, and in regard to those of public utility which are
not so urgent, but which have been
accelerated with the definite object of providing employment. Since the transfer
of services that Ministry has sanctioned
loans for public health works amounting
to almost two million pounds, and for the
re-conditioning of hospitals and poor-law
institutions amounting to over three
hundred thousand pounds. Loans of
two millions have been sanctioned for
road works undertaken without any
assistance from the Unemployment Relief
Fund in addition to the relief works on
roads, of which I have already given some
particulars. In regard to housing, as the
House knows, a great deal has been done
by the Government through the Ministry
of Home Affairs to stimulate building;
from first to last the Government has contributed in various forms subsidies
amounting to almost two million pounds
to assist in the building of small dwellings.
My own Ministry, the Ministry of
Education, has also been active in promoting employment in the building trade,
over three-quarters of a million pounds
having been spent by local education
authorities on the building of new schools,
and it is hoped that their policy of replacing unsuitable buildings will continue and develop until Our school
accommodation is everywhere brought up
to modern requirements. As hon. Members will remember, an additional grant
was made available in the last Education
Act for voluntary managers who do not
wish to relinquish the individual control
of their schools, and already many applications have been received for assistance
towards this, of the cost of which I have
not yet got figures.
In regard to agriculture, of which
mention has been made this afternoon, it
has been the aim of the Government to
counteract as far as possible the adverse
effect on the farming community of the
world-wide decline in prices by measures
479 Government Administration--

THE SENATE
Censure Motion
[Viscount Chariemont]
designed to develop and improve farming,
and to enable farmers to dispose of their
products to the best advantage. In the
year 1924 a grant of fifty-four thousand
pounds was made, to be spread over four
years and to be devoted to agricultural
education schemes, milk recording associations, and the improvement of breeding
mountain sheep. These were such a
success that further assistance was continued when this grant was exhausted.
In addition, in 1927, forty thousand
pounds was set aside to establish a fund
for the improvement of live stock by the
extension of premium schemes in respect
of bulls, boars, and rams. Last year a
Poultry Development scheme estimated
to cost in all twenty-four thousand pounds
was also commenced, and the annual
grants to county committees of agriculture have been increased by about
£7,500. Scientific research and higher
education in agriculture have also been
provided for. In regard to research, we
can now fairly claim that we have
stations equal to those to be found in any
other country; research work has been
conducted with great success in connection with fruit and poultry, and as a
proof of the efficiency of the latter, I may
point out that our poultry station has
now been recognised by the Empire.
Marketing Board to the extent of
establishing Northern Ireland as an
Empire centre for such investigations. A
Faculty of Agriculture has been established in Queen's University, provided and
maintained from public funds. The
Ministry of Agriculture has also through
the various enactments given consideration to the improvement of marketing
of agricultural products. Marketing of
eggs, marketing of potatoes, and marketing of dairy produce have already been
dealt with, while the meat and fruit export business are now under consideration
in another place. Improved credit.
facilities have been provided for individual farmers, and grants for drainage
and other works of land improvement are
also available. Loans on easy terms can
be obtained, backed by Government
guarantee, through the banks for the
purchase of live stock, including poultry,
and for the purchase of seeds and
fertilizers.
Afforestation has not been neglected.
Since the year 1922 a sum of eightythree thousand pounds has been spent on
this work and it is the intention of the
Government to continue the work by
planting about 1,000 acres each year.
But undoubtedly the most important
financial benefit which farmers have received at the hands of the Government
has been the complete derating of agricultural lands, and, as hon. Members are
well aware, the principle of derating is
extended in a somewhat less degree to
productive industries.
In this statement I have dealt mainly
with schemes promoted by the Government
which directly or indirectly afford employment. I would like to refer to the
question of social services, in which it is
the fact that in this area we have
kept step by step with Great Britain.
I need only mention the matter
of Widows', Orphans' and Old Age
Contributory Pensions, and the extension
of National Health Insurance to include
the provision of medical attendance as
examples of our legislation. I think that
the charge of inefficiency against the
administration has been refuted and it
is, I think, equally impossible to sustain
the charge that it has been wasteful. The
desire for a return of prosperity is a
matter on which we are all agreed, and I
venture to say that no Government could
possibly have done more to deserve it
than the present administration.
Mr. CAMPBELL: As mover of the
Motion I believe I have a right to reply,
but I intend to exercise that right very
sparingly. I can hardly compliment my
hon. Friend, Mr. Cunningham, on that
portion of his speech in which he sneered
at the Nationalists for not providing employment or unemployment. I can hardly
follow that. There is a confusion of
ideas and perhaps Senator McCorkell.
who is an authority on that subject, is
better able to follow it than I am.
section of the population has at least, I
think, contributed a fair share towards
employers, and certainly more than their
fair share of the toilers who built up the
prosperity of the employers.
That
Senator Cunningham says the Nationalists have not put forward suggestions.
Where were they ever asked? Where
were they ever invited? Or is there
any intention to follow their sugges
tions? Then we are told there is
no evidence of wasteful administration.
I may merely say in reply to that
that none are so blind as they who will
not see. We are told that Northern Ireland is not the only place of economic
depression. In fact, to use the words of
481 Government Administration--

14 OCTOBER 1930
Censure Motion
an English statesman, the world is dark
with an economic blizzard. Then it is
also said it is idle to censure the Government for failure to promote a return to
prosperity, that is to say, it is no business
of the Government to try to do anything
of the kind. It is not the duty of the
Government to try to promote a return
to prosperity. Is that the suggestion?
If it is it is a most extraordinary suggestion to put forward in the defence of any
Government. Or is the suggestion that it
is powerless? If it is powerless it is an
exception to the Governments of the
world.
Then it is said we are connected with
the Free State, which has put up an inch
high tariff, with what result I do not
know, for Northern Ireland or the Free
State. Then we are in connection with
the British Isles which, Mr. Cunningham
says, bring in foreign imports without
check, and the remedy is Protection.
I do not want to say one word against
Protection or to say one word against
Free Trade. It is not a matter I intend
to go into. But dealing with confusion
of ideas I would like to ask what Senator
Cunningham means by Protection. We
have so many kinds of it. We have
Empire crusaders who want Free Trade
within the Empire, which the Dominions
say is madness and would wreck the
Empire and break it to pieces. Is that
the Protection which Mr. Cunningham
wants?
Mr. JOSEPII CUNNINGHAM: Do
not commit yourself!
Mr. CAMPBELL: Then we have Lord
Rothermere's lot. Then we have the old
firm, the Conservative party, which does
not know what it wants. The great Lord
Salisbury said once that any fool with a
map could set the Tory party in a blaze
about Protection. Mr. Cunningham says
the Nationalists always imposed Free
Trade on these countries. Free Trade
under which Great Britain prospered for
90 years! Under which it carried on an
immense war, under which it is almost
the only country in Christendom paying
20 shillings in the pound. Are the
Nationalists to blame for that?
something to think of if they have been
such a power as to build up the good name
for honesty and integrity of the British
Empire. Then it is said the Government
It is
"
have done remarkably well; that everything possible has been done by the
Government to promote the linen and
shipbuilding industry, and, in fact, that
only genuises are concentrated in the
Cabinet of Northern Ireland. No fresh
minds, it is said, are needed in this Government, the members of which, for nine
years, have been getting younger and
younger every day, and their terms of
office are to be made perpetual. One
Member asked who was to take their
place, and the Mayor of Derry said there.
was no one anywhere at all to supplant
the Cabinet of Northern Ireland. We
are in for a perpetual Premiership and a
perpetual dictatorship. In most places at
change of minds brings about a change of
conditions, but we do not need any such
change.
I was very interested to hear the Mayor
of Derry say you could not make tillage
pay or make it prosperous without
taxation of food. Let it go forth clearly
from this Senate to-day that, as far as he
can speak the Ulster Tory mind it is
taxation of food and nothing else. Let
the English working man note that. I
am afraid Senator Joseph Cunningham
will not accept that.
Mr. McCORKELL: I did not use the
words, "nothing else.'
""
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do not want to
misinterpret the hon. Senator, but I
think I am correct in saying that the purport of his words was that you cannot
make tillage prosperous without the taxation of food.
Mr. McCORKELL: I said you could
not bring it back to its old prosperity
without some taxation of food.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I understood
Senator Cunningham to say there will be
no taxation of food. There is a confusion
of ideas. The Mayor of Derry also
asked if we knew what
wages the
French and Belgian labourer was paid.
I do not, but I fancy that both France
and Belgium are protected countries, and
I think it is rather a foolish idea to set
before us the hard lot of the under-paid
French and Belgian labourer as a sample
of what Protection does. Does he mean
to say that if we adopt Protection in

Northern Ireland, our labourers, badly

Mr. McCORKELL: No. I said our

Mr. CAMPBELL: Always remember
that it was said by a distinguished Minister the other day that Great Britain will
protect herself and look after her own

You will have to be very
careful to see that Northern Ireland will

of the principal plank in the Conservative
is the protection of wheat.
programme
There is little talk of the protection
Campbell, Mr. T. J.

Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.

of oats, because that does not concern
people over there. I conclude by
saying that the noble Leader of the
House has given us a long list of
the activities of the Government. I
see nothing extraordinary in those acti
vities. They are the activities of a Government in ordinary times, but at a time
of such extraordinary crisis as we have
been passing through extraordinary

Government. I say that no extraordinary
efforts adequate to the emergency have
been put forward by this Government.
Question put, "That this Motion be

Weights and Measures (Amendment)
Bill (S.).
Order for Second Reading read.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The main
purpose of this Measure, the Second
Reading of which I now move, is to confer power upon the Ministry of Commerce
to make regulations bringing measuring
instruments within the scope of compulsory verification and stamping by inspectors of weights and measures.
principal and most important type of
measuring instrument thus brought within
the code will be the petrol pump, but
hon. Members will observe that provision
is also being made with regard to counting machines and machines for the
measurement of arca.
Clause 3 of the Bill entitles the Ministry of Commerce to charge fees for the
of local
comparison and verification
standards, and this will bring the law in
that particular in Northern Ireland into
conformity with that of Great Britain.
The Clause further affirms the power of
the Ministry and of inspectors of weights.
and measures to charge fees in respect of
the testing, examination, comparison,
Glendinning, Mr. J. C.
Leslie, Mr. James G.
Long, Mr. John A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
and verification of weighing or measuring
apparatus, whether the apparatus is found
to be correct or not.
Clauses 4 and 5 of the Bill will afford
relief to local authorities from obligations
which have in practice proved unnecessary and which, if strictly enforced
under the existing law, would be found
somewhat burdensome. The change to be
effected by Clause 4 is that County
Councils will no longer be under a legal
obligation to provide local sub-standards.
and scales in every petty sessions area,
but only in such places as the Ministry
of Commerce may direct, and Clause 5
will relieve boroughs, as distinct from
county boroughs, from the existing legal
obligation to
to provide sets of local
standards and scale beams for use in the
borough.
The Bill contains, to my mind, no previsions of a controversial nature and hon.
Members will, I think, agree that the
provision in relation to the testing and
stamping of petrol pumps are already
overdue. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure the House
will join with the noble Viscount in re-
485 Weights and Measures Bill

11 OCTOBER 1930
Expiring Laws Bill
garding this as
a non-controversial
Measure. It is, as indeed I think the
noble Viscount indicated, practically
based on the English Act of 1926, with
one exception, and that is the power of
the Ministry of Commerce to charge fees,
under Sub-clause (1) of Clause 3. I do
not think that any serious objection will
be made to that sub-clause, although the
fact that there is a slight difference there
from the English Act, which is the Act
of 1923, will perhaps not escape attention. The matter, however, is too trivial
to demand our serious attention.
There is one point that will arise on
that same sub-clause, and that is, what
is to be the destination of the fees which
are taken under that sub-clause. The
Bill does not tell us that, and I think we
are left to imagine that those fees will
be applied in the same way as other fees
which are taken under Section 8 of the
Act of 1889. We shall have, I think, at
a later stage to consider whether we can
rely on the mere inclusion in the Schedule
of the repeal of certain words repugnant
to the purpose of the sub-clause to ensure
the automatic inclusion of any such fees
there may be in the same net as that
provided in the Section of the 1889 Act.
I do not think that will take us very
long when we come to the appropriate
stage.
whether
provide
