SO on,
The only other point is as to whether
it is intended that the Bill should come
into immediate operation, or
it would not be better to
a few weeks of waiting.
tions have to be made, and
and it will not escape the attention
of hon. Members that in the Imperial
Acts, the Act of 1878 and the Acts of
1904 and 1919, there was a waiting
period provided almost up to six months.
I shall suggest, when the time comes,
that we should insert the few words
necessary to bring the Bill into operation
on 1st January next, the point being, of
course, that the preparation of Regulations can very well go on in the meantime, and we shall avoid that hiatus,
which is so objectionable, between coming
into nominal operation and its actual
operation.
The Bill on the whole will not, I
think, give rise to anything in the nature
of objection on the part of the Senate.
The suggestion for regulations in respect
of petrol pumps will certainly be welcomed.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish to make one
observation, and it is this, I would remind the noble Viscount that in 1926 a
Bill entitled the Sale of Food (Weights
and Measures) Act, 1926, was passed by
the Imperial Parliament for the better
protection of the public in relation to
the retail sale of food. It also aims at
prohibiting short weight and measure. I
bring it to the notice of the Leader of
the House with a view to ascertaining
whether the Government consider that
the Measure is suitable for introduction
in Northern Ireland.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
like to give the information asked for at
a later stage.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill (S.).
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This annual Bill of which I move the Second
Reading does not need very much explanation. It continues temporarily certain
enactments which may be regarded as
being still experimental or liable to revision. It has been framed so as to fit in
with the corresponding Imperial Bill; the
latter applies to Northern Ireland in so
far as it deals with any enactment
relating to a subject with respect to which
the Parliament of Northern Ireland has
not power to make laws, and the Bill now
before the House supplements this provision by dealing with enactments relating.
to subjects with respect to which our
Parliament has legislative power. Thus,
the two Measures, that is to say, the Imperial and our own, taken together deal
with all temporary laws applicable to
Northern Ireland.
The Schedule to the Bill, that is, the
enactments which it is proposed to continue until 31st December of next year, is
identical with the Schedule of last year's
Bill, except in one particular. The
Labourers (Ireland) Act of 1883, with
the series of amending statutes, some of
them passed by our Own Parliament,
which was included in last year's Schedule, has now been dropped and the
reason for this is simple. These statutes
enabled cottages and plots to be provided
Expiring Laws Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
Viscount Charlemont |
by Rural District Councils for agricultural
labourers, and up to the present our Parliament has continued to keep them in
force and the code has also been continued
in a similar manner by the Imperial
Parliament in so far as any enactments
therein contained dealt with subjects in
respect of which the Northern Parliament
has not power to make laws. The "reserved' aspect of the code
arose in
connection with the making of advances
by the Land Purchase Commission, Northern Ireland, to be repayable by means
of Land Purchase annuities. It has now
been decided that no further Land Purchase Advances are to be issued for
labourers cottages, and the code will not
in future be included in the Imperial
Expiring Laws Continuance Bill. The
Northern Ireland Parliament, on the
other hand, has by the Labourers Act
(Northern Ireland), 1930, given further
facilities in the shape of grants out of
voted money in aid of the repayment of
loans borrowed by District Councils for
cottages, and has provided that schemes
lodged on or before the 31st March, 1935,
shall be eligible to receive these facilities.
No doubt hon. Members remember the
debate on this Bill in May last when it was
before this House. This arrangement
assures a continuance of the Labourers
Acts for a period of several years at least,
and the assumption is made good by Section 2 of the Act of 1930, which continues
the Acts until 31st December, 1935.
cordingly the code is omitted from the
present Bill. I beg to move.
Ac-
Mr. CAMPBELL: I have a very few
remarks to make on this Bill. One is
that one of the Acts proposed to be continued in the Bill is the Local Government Emergency Powers Act of 1921,
purely an emergency Act. It is an Act
which empowers the Ministry of Home
Affairs to dissolve a local authority and to
appoint in place of the local authority a
paid commissioner to exercise the functions. I cannot see why it is desirable
or expedient or necessary in the interests
of the public that the Act should be continued. It is purely an emergency Act
measure, and it was passed in a time of
emergency.
Mr. DICKSON: What about Donaghadee?
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am not familiar
with what the circumstances in Donaghadee are,
but I know that at the
time this emergency Act was passed a
considerable number of Nationalist
authorities functioned in this area. They
have been wiped off the map. They are
now reduced to five or six. I do protest
against the power this Act vests in any
Minister. I think it is most autocratic power, and on that ground I
protest against this Act being continued.
Mr. LESLIE: I would venture to remind the House of a remark I made, I
think two years ago, when a similar
Measure was before the House. And that
was, I ventured to draw attention to the
possible danger of allowing this particular
statute to expire unless we were prepared
to put something in to take its place. As
matters stand, if this Act were allowed to
expire we should be left with nothing to
take its place in regard to the greater
number of our local authorities.
The general question of allowing
temporary Acts to remain on the Statute
Book practically indefinitely is one on
which I should be obliged to agree with
the learned Senator who has just sat
down. The question is, does the Measure
do anyone any harm, and there is an
instance at the moment where it does a
certain amount of good. I think I
caught the word "Donaghadee" from the
lips of the noble Viscount. It is a fact
that owing to the singular efficiency of
the very competent gentleman in charge
of the affairs there, peace and contentment have been introduced where formerly
they were lacking. I do lay stress on the
one fact I have mentioned, and that is
the inadvisability of removing this Act
until we are ready to provide a substitute,
and the substitute we may look for when
the local Government Measure which we
are now definitely promised, I think next
year, is brought before the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think there is very much to complain of
in the schedule complained of by the hon.
and learned Senator. I would remind
the House that such power would not be
exercised by the Minister in the dark.
He is accountable for his actions to
Parliament, and any actions which are
not in accordance with the popular wish
would be open to grave censure.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjournment
Adjournment
11 OCTOBER 1930
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move that "this House do now adjourn
until this day week." I do not think
there is any necessity to take the Committee stage of these Bills on Thursday.
Tuesday will suit.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes after
Five until Tuesday next.

THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 21st October, 1930.
The Senate met at a quarter after Three
of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in the
Chair.

Ministry of Finance:
Schedule of Ministry of Home Affairs
documents, the destruction of which is

Questions to Minister.
HOUSING LEGISLATION.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the Government will
take steps to bring in legislation on the
lines of the Imperial Housing Act, 1930,
particularly with a view to assimilating
the law in Northern Ireland to the law
in England, which requires local authorities in their choice of tenants of houses
built with State help to give special preference to tenants with the larger
families.
Viscount CHARLEMONT (Leader of
the House): I would suggest that the hon.
and learned Member should await the
issue of the Town Planning Bill, which
will be circulated this session, for information on this subject.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I will be very happy
to do so, but I hope that some steps will
be taken by the Government in the coming session to bring forward a Bill on the
lines of the Imperial Housing Act of 1930,
especially with a view to seeing if we cannot better the condition of the slum
tenants of our area.
VALUE OF LINEN EXPORTS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the value
of the linen exports from Northern Ireland in the year 1929 and the first half of
the year 1930.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In replying
to the hon. and learned Member's question, I must repeat the warning which I
gave in replying to a question on the same
subject put by him in January of the
present year. As he may recollect, I then
pointed out that as the customs authorities do not collect statistics of the trade
in non-dutiable goods passing between
Great Britain and Northern Ireland, the
only measure of the volume of this trade
is that supplied by the harbour returns
furnished to the Ministry of Commerce.
Unfortunately for the present purpose.
these returns do not include values, so
that the Ministry of Commerce, in order
to arrive at an estimate of value, is
obliged, firstly, to ascertain as accurately
as possible the average price of each class
of goods over the period in question; and.
secondly, to apply that price to the
quantities shown in the harbour returns.
Obviously, such a procedure, while the
only one possible in the circumstances, admits of error, and for this reason the hon.
and learned Member must not regard the
figures which I give as being actual values
in the same sense as he would regard those
compiled by the customs authorities in the
case of goods exported from, and imported
into, the United Kingdom.
I should further explain that it is the
practice of the Ministry of Commerce to
ascertain the average prices at yearly intervals, that is to say, the Ministry ascertains the average prices over the whole
of each calendar year. In the present
instance, however, in order to deal with
the hon. and learned Member's question
in so far as it relates to the first six
months of 1930, the Ministry has found
it necessary to ascertain the average prices
for 1930 by comparing the average prices
for the first six months of 1929 with
those for the first six months of 1930 as
shown in the Board of Trade returns for
both periods. A slight fall has thus been
disclosed in the prices of all classes of linen
goods except canvas during the first six
months of 1930, as compared with the first
six months of 1929, and the percentage
fall in each case has been applied by the
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
21 OCTOBER 1930
Ministry to the average prices on which
the 1929 values of Northern Ireland exports were calculated.
The estimated value of linen goods exported from Northern Ireland in 1929 to
all destinations, excluding the Irish Free
State, was £15,460,000. The value of
such goods exported during the first six
months of 1930 has been estimated as
The corresponding figures
£6,482,000.
for linen yarn
were £1,504,000 and
£640,000 respectively.
In the case of
canvas goods the figures were £44,000 and
£14,000 respectively.
Sir EMERSON HERDMAN: As representing the linen trade in this House I
am deeply grateful for the interest the
hon. and learned Member takes in our
staple trade. The more interest taken in
that trade and the more help given by the
people of Ulster, the better it is not only
for the linen trade itself but for our Province as a whole, because the prosperity of
Ulster depends more or less upon the wellbeing or otherwise of the linen trade.
Leaving out all economic considerations,
there are two fashions at present which
hit the linen trade very hard. One is the
use, the almost universal use, in hotelseven in Ulster hotels, I am sorry to sayof cotton sheets. The other is the almost
indecently bare dinner-table. Personally,
when I go to a hotel and see there are
cotton sheets on the bed, I ring the bell
and ask for linen sheets, and if this example was followed I am sure hotel-keepers
would come to the conclusion that it would
pay them to use linen sheets. In that
way there would be more looms and
spindles working, more employment, and
less unemployment benefit being paid.
The bare dinner-table, I am glad to say.
is not quite as popular as it used to be.
but I would ask all who use it to banish
it from their house and to try to discour
age it in every way possible.
When I hear these Jeremiahs talking
about the linen trade and making dolefui
prophesies I am always cheered up by a
story my father told of an uncle of his
who in the early thirties of the last century got married and went to the Continent for his honeymoon. The linen trade
was in a bad state, and although enjoying
his honeymoon as all young men should,
he could not banish black care from behind the saddle. One day, driving along
the Rhine, he was very silent, and his
wife said, "John, what is the matter.
Why are you not enjoying this lovely
!
scenery?" He replied, "Elizabeth, the
linen trade is finished." That was a
hundred years ago, and the linen trade
is still carrying on. I know of three
firms, including my own, which were
working then in our branch of the trade,
and which are still working and supplying the linen trade with the necessary
yarn to make their goods. Of course, I
cannot deny that at the present moment
trade is looking black, but I hope there
will be a good time coming if the electors.
of Great Britain review their fiscal policy.
That would bring renewed life to the
linen trade which, at the present moment,
though badly bashed, is still in the ring.
CENSORSHIP OF FILMS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the Government will
give effect to the recent resolution of the
Protestant Synod of Armagh, calling
upon them to exercise a stricter supervision of cinema pictures, whether the
British Board of Censors is the supervising authority for Northern Ireland,
and whether that Board is constituted by
the representatives of the film trade.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I would
refer the hon. and learned Member to my
answer to a similar question put by him
on the 21st May last, which explains the
whole position with regard to film censorship.
Nothing has arisen in connection with
the matter since then to suggest to the
Government that local authorities are
dilatory in exercising their powers under
the Cinematograph Act. An example of
the working of the existing arrangement
occurred in this City during the present
month, when a film, that had been refused a certificate by the British Board
of Film Censors, was submitted to the
Police Committee of the Corporation who
upheld the Censor's decision, with the result that the film was not shown.
In reply to the last part of the question,
I have to say that the British Board of
Film Censors is a body set up by the film
trade under the presidency of a former
Home Secretary.
Mr. CAMPBELL: In plain English,
the Government propose to do nothing
in the matter in the way of legislation?
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
that is the case, at present.
I think

Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That the
Special Order made by the Electricity
Northern

This Special Order, granted by the

Ireland and confirmed by the Ministry of

Bay Electricity Supply Company, Limited,

in the townland of Ballygrot and certain

and Ballygilbert, in the Rural District of
Newtownards, and part of the townland.
of Ballyrobert, in the Rural District of
Castlereagh, all in the County of Down.
The Rules of Procedure have been complied with and the Order, which follows
the usual form, is not opposed. I beg to
move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: 1 am sure none of
us would venture to offer any opposition
to the first Motion made by the new
Deputy-Leader of the House. May I extend my congratulations to him on his
elevation to that post. I withhold my congratulations on his appointment to
another office, that of Chief Whip to the
Government here, but perhaps I may
give him a straight tip not to drive his
team too hard no matter how unruly they
may be, and not to imitate the driving
of Jehu, which was furious.

The following Amendment stood on the

Clause 3, page 2, line 17, at the end
of sub-clause (1) to add, "and the provisions of section eight of the Weights and

such fees."
Mr. LESLIE: The object of the
Amendment on the Paper is to provide
Parliamentary sanction for the destination of any fees that may arise under the
first sub-clause. Unless Parliament does
provide for the destination of those fees
I think it is perhaps rather doubtful
whether the provisions of the Weights
and Measures Act of 1889 would or would
not automatically apply.
It is not a
desirable matter to be left in doubt, and
accordingly I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am glad
to be able to accept the Amendment.
Amendment agreed to.

that can readily be obtained by a Clause
in the Regulations themselves. Other
reasons render it undesirable that the

Therefore I do not move.

MOTION FOR ADJOURNMENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before
moving the Adjournment of the House I
have to announce that there is a Divorce
Bill which has not yet been considered
in another place, but which will be due
here for First and Second Reading on two
successive days. I would like to consult
Members as to the most convenient days
to sit. Personally, Thursday and Friday would suit me best, but if any Member has any contrary opinion let him express it now or for ever hold his peace.
499 Execution of Warrants--
Adjournment

THE SENATE
Mr. GLENDINNING: As a matter of
fact, Thursday and Friday would also be
better for those coming from a distance.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am afraid
I expressed it rather roughly. It is not
a matter of my own convenience, but if
Thursday and Friday would suit the
House I will move, "That this House do
adjourn until Thursday."
Mr. LESLIE: The noble Viscount did
not give us any idea of the business for
those two days other than the Divorce
Bill.
Viscount
formal.
CHARLEMONT:
Purely
Mr. LESLIE: Would a sitting on
Thursday not be sufficient for our purpose
if we consented to the suspension of the
Standing Order and took the First and
Second Readings of the Bill on the one
day. I think it would be more convenient
to dispose of it in one day.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am surprised to hear the hon. and learned Member advise the suspension of the Standing
Order. The last time I suggested anything
of that kind one would have thought I
was suggesting a revolution. (Laughter.)
Perhaps it would be better to leave it to
the Parliamentary Secretary to the Department of the Prime Minister (Mr.
Long) to move the suspension of the
Standing Order on Thursday with the
possibility that it will be agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before the House
rises I feel sure some hon. Members would
he interested in a perusal of any recent
communications between the Government
and the Government in Dublin relative to
the reciprocal execution of warrants in
their areas if the Leader of the House can
give an opportunity for perusal by laying
such communications on the table of the
House. It may be that communications
on this subject are still in progress, but
the matter is one of very considerable
moment. If Free State warrants are nonexecutable in the Six Counties and Northern Ireland warrants non-executable in
the Twenty-Six Counties the free working
of justice may be paralysed at times. I
do not know of any exact parallel to the
present situation. Fugitives from Texas
were wont to cross the Rio Grande to
escape American warrants. Vice versa
gentlemen from Mexico leaving their
country for their country's good cleared
over the Rio Grande to Texas for safety.
Here in our little island we have no Rio
Grande separating the two territories.
Some villages here are cut in two by an
imperceptible border line. A brisk lau
in the northern end of a border village
against whom a warrant is out or is likely
to be out may find it good strategy to retire to the southern end, while a gentleman in the southern end in a like unpleasant plight may make his way to the
northern end of the village. Whether
the warrant is for a felony or a twopenny
trespass the fugitive is immune.
If I mistake not Free State warrants
are executed in Great Britain and English
and Scotch warrants in the Free State.
Doubtless the Government consulted the
British Government before withdrawing
their sanction from the execution of the
warrants of the Free State Courts, seeing
that the Supreme Court service is within
the jurisdiction of the British authorities.
The tangle is Gilbertian in a sense, but
may be seriously harmful to the public
interest. One has the impression that
the Government's diplomacy has been at
fault.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret
the situation that has arisen just as much
as the hon. and learned Member, but the
Government do not think it desirable to
lay on the Table any correspondence in
connection with this matter.
On Question, Motion (That this House
do now adjourn until Thursday next)
agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at a
Quarter before Four o'clock
until Thursday next.

Business of the House.
23 OCTOBER 1930
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 23rd October, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Education:
Technical School Examinations Regulations, 1927, Amending Regulations,
No. 2, 1930.
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act), and ordered to lie on the Table.
Business of the House.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move :
"That Standing Order No. XXV be
suspended for this day's sitting, for the
purpose of taking the Second Reading of
McNulty's Divorce Bill, and the Third
Reading of the Weights and Measures
(Amendment) Bill."
It will be seen that the Motion for the
suspension of the Standing Order is put
down for the double purpose of taking
the Second Reading of McNulty's
Divorce Bill, and the Third Reading of
the Weights and Measures (Amendment)
Bill. There appeared to be general
agreement amongst the Members when
we last met on Tuesday that there would
be no objection to this procedure in regard to the Divorce Bill. With regard
to the proposal to take both the Report
stage and the Third Reading of the
Weights and Measures (Amendment)
Bill, the reason why the Government is
anxious to do so is so that the Bill can
get through this House and be sent to
another place for First Reading to-day
in order to facilitate that House in the
transaction of their business. I think
this is a very mild form of request for
the suspension of Standing Orders, and
I therefore beg to move that the Standing Order be suspended to-day both for the
purpose of taking the Second Reading
of the Divorce Bill, and also the Third
Reading of the Weights and Measures
Bill.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I cannot see any
special reason for the Motion before the
House. Standing Orders are prepared
for the guidance of this House in its deliberations, and I do respectfully submit
that they ought only to be departed from
for grave and urgent reasons, and no such
reasons have been given here to-day.
a
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid-or rather
I do not know that I am afraid, but I
admit that perhaps I am not altogether
free from some share of responsibility for
the Motion that has just been made by
the Parliamentary Secretary. I suggested
on the adjournment, when we met on the
last occasion, that in order to avoid the
House contravening one of its conventions by sitting on Friday, it might be
less inconvenient for us to follow
precedent we set ourselves some time ago,
the precedent of dispensing with one of
our Standing Orders and take the First
and Second Reading of a particular Bill
in one day. As the House knows, the
Second Reading of a Divorce Bill-and
Divorce Bills, as I am sure the House is
aware, never originate in this House-is
the merest possible formality. It is not
of particular public importance because.
it has to go to a Committee for detailed
examination. The only effect of sitting
again for the special purpose of giving a
Second Reading to a Divorce Bill would
be to impose upon Members the duty of
attending again for that special purpose,
and attendance here, when there is no
real occasion for it, does involve both inconvenience and expense. So far as the
Divorce Bill goes, it is a very proper case
for the suspension of the Standing
Order.
As regards the other Bill, to which
allusion was not made when we first spoke
of the suspension of Standing Orders during the adjournment debate to which I
have already referred, it is only more or
less by accident that we are not in a position to take the Third Reading of that
Bill to-day, as we are in a position to take
the Third Reading of the Expiring Laws
Continuance Bill. What happened was
that two Amendments were made to the
Weights and Measures Bill. One of them
was merely supplying what was really a
printer's omission by inserting the word
"and" in the Schedule, and the other
was a small explanatory addition which
it was thought desirable to make. Again,
I think there is no question that could
possibly arise on the Report stage of that
Business of the House.
Business of the House.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
Bill, because on the Report stage, I
would remind hon. Members, you cannot
introduce any new matter that has not
been referred to before. There is nothing
I could imagine that could be raised on
that particular Bill for which opportunity
has not already been given.
I support the proposal of the Parliamentary Secretary and I hope it will be
unanimously agreed to, as I do not regard
it as in any way opposing the line that I
and others have always taken; that is the
line that proper time should always be
given for the discussion and consideration
of important matters. That is entirely
distinct from consenting to a Motion like
this, which will have the effect of facilitating public business.
As a
Mr. McALLISTER: I rise to oppose
the suspension of this Standing Order as
a matter of principle and consistency. I
oppose the Motion because I am firmly
convinced that legislation in all its stages
is passed too hastily, with the result that
the public has not sufficient time to study
Measures and to realize how they will be
affected by them in the conduct of their
private affairs. I make that statement in
reference to the very first introduction of
a Bill either here or elsewhere.
Member of another place for four years
previous to coming into this House, I say
in all sincerity that I think the work here
is, on the whole, done with more efficiency
and with more thoroughness than it is
done in any other place. The Party
Whip here is not so strict, and Members
exercise an independence of view that
they could not possibly exercise, and
would not be permitted to exercise, elsewhere. Therefore, a greater and more
sincere effort is made in this House to
The Lord Mayor of Belfast.
Armstrong, Mr. Henry B.
Barclay, Mr. William.
Clark, Sir George S.
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dickson, Mr. J. Hill.
deal with legislation with care and efficiency. Bills are more thoroughly investigated, and there is more independent expression of opinion than there is in some
other places. The results of the Divisions
in this House show that the Members
exercise more independent thought and
are more consistent in voting according to
the views they express than is the case
elsewhere.
At the same time, I believe that
throughout all these years legislation has
been passed too quickly in both Houses,
with the result that the public are not
given the opportunity they are entitled
to of considering and grasping the effect
of Bills, which become Acts of Parliament before the general public are aware
how they are going to be affected by such
legislation. For this reason I oppose the
suspension of the Standing Order.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise also to oppose
this Motion on principle. On numerous
occasions the Government has appealed to
Members of this House to meet the convenience of another place. It appears to
me that it would be just as reasonable for
the Members of this House to make application to another place for the suspension
of their Standing Orders as it is for the
Government to continuously ask this
House to suspend our Standing Orders to
meet the convenience of the other House.
From that point of view I am opposed to
such a Motion as this except in cases of
extreme urgency when there is some
reasonable cause for the suspension of the
Standing Order.
Question put.

Questions to
EDUCATION ON
Minister.
AGRICULTURE.
Mr. McALLISTER asked the Leader
of the House if the Government will arrange to provide education on agriculture
for children attending Public Elementary
Schools in rural areas.
Mr. LONG: I am asked by my right
hon. Friend, the Leader of the House,
who is slightly indisposed, to reply to this
question.
This subject-that is, the formal teaching of agriculture--was one of those
which could earn grants in national
schools in Ireland under the old results
system and was dropped when that system
was abolished and the curriculum revised
in 1900. It was dropped, I think, partly
because the Department of Agriculture
and Technical Instruction was established
about that time, and the responsibility
for agricultural education was transferred
to the new Department; but also because
it was felt that agriculture was a scientific
subject unsuited to children of tender
age and that, as it had consisted mainly
in attempting to master the contents of a
text-book, it was of little or no practical
value.
The whole matter was again considered.
so far as Northern Ireland was concerned,
by the Departmental Committee presided
over by Sir Robert Lynn in 1922, when
it was recommended in regard to the
elementary schools that horticulture and
school gardening should be encouraged as
much as possible in rural areas. In 1928
my right hon. Friend, the Minister of
Education, referred the question of a
rural bias in country schools to the Advisory Council of Education and received
an interesting report, of which I am sending the hon. Member a copy. This report
was subsequently circulated to all those
concerned with the schools and is, I think,
leading to useful and practical results in
fostering an interest in rural life and
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
29 OCTOBER 1930
pursuits; though, as the Council rightly
pointed out, the wide-spread "towndrift" which has been prevalent in recent
years is probably mainly due to economic
causes over which the schools cannot be
expected to exercise any control. In this
report the following passage occurs:—
In regard to the curriculum, the
Council are of opinion that it is not
desirable to introduce any specialised
text-books dealing with agriculture, or
to attempt any revival of the discarded
practice of giving formal instruction in
this subject. Agriculture is a science
and a business; it involves, in fact,
several branches of science, and is not
suitable for inclusion in the curriculum
of elementary schools. Indeed, formal
teaching is in general the wrong method
to adopt in attempting to create a rural
bias. Moreover, many of the pupils
will certainly not be entering upon
country occupations, and for such pupils
this instruction would be worse than
superfluous. No variation from the
regular programme should be permitted.
which would in any way interfere with
the general basis of a sound and liberal
education; but within the compass of
the range of subjects which are accepted
as essential, the fullest liberty should
be given to the teacher to draw up
original programmes suitable to the
locality, particularly in such subjects as
nature study and horticulture.
The
Council recommend that the Ministry's
inspectors should give every encouragement and assistance to teachers who
wish to do so. Local education authorities and other school managers should
also be invited to co-operate with the
teaching staff in their efforts at creating
a local interest through the instruction.
The report goes on to make detailed
recommendations in regard to the teaching
of horticulture and nature study and indicates methods whereby the teaching of
other subjects such as English, arithmetic
and geography can be brought more
closely into line with country life and
occupations.
Shortly after the issue of this report the
Minister had conferences with representatives of the Ulster Farmers' Union and
with teachers specially interested in rural
education, and found that they were in
agreement that it would be unwise to give
anything in the nature of vocational
training to young children at the elementary stage, but that, as the Advisory
Council had recommended, the proper
course should rather be to attempt to
create an atmosphere which would encourage country pupils to take an interest
in their surroundings and in country
work. I may say that in some of our
rural areas, particularly in the Londonderry and Limavady rural districts, very
good work is being done along these lines.
I can assure the hon. Member that the
Ministry of Education are very much alive
to the importance of this whole question,
but they are convinced that any attempt
to re-introduce the formal teaching of
agriculture into our public elementary
schools would be inadvisable.
We
Mr. McALLISTER: Notwithstanding
the volume of opinion which the Deputy-
Leader of the House has brought to his
support, I still think it would be a desirable thing to educate the youth in our
agricultural districts in the pursuit which
many of them will undoubtedly have to
follow. One of the reasons given by the
Deputy-Leader of the House why this
should not be done is that many of the
students do not follow an agricultural
avocation when they leave school.
all know that there are more people in
Northern Ireland engaged in agriculture
than in any other branch of industry or
business, and I think the hon. Member
was rather unhappy in giving that as a
reason why children should not receive
this agricultural education. If they do
not receive some knowledge of agriculture
while they are at school they certainly
will not receive any theoretical knowledge at any future time.
Another reason given why this education
should not be imparted in schools was that
it was abolished in 1900 on account of
the Department of Agriculture being
established. We all know that the voluntary efforts put forth by that body attracted but an infinitesimal number of
boys who intended to pursue an agricultural career. Therefore, I say that the
only means of getting at the great mass of
the future farmers of Northern Ireland
is by educating them in the schools. Besides, I would not confine it to theoretical
education, as the Deputy-Leader of the
House has suggested. I think that in
order to do this thing efficiently and to
properly teach young boys how to become
successful farmers it would be necessary to
take sufficient land and to give on that land
demonstrations of the results of various
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
tests and experiments that have been
carried out in the past in Ireland, England, the Irish Free State, Denmark, and
anywhere else. We have heard a lot
about research in agriculture and this
is the way to demonstrate the results of
research to the future farmers of the Six
Counties, if it is to be of any value at
all.
There is one aspect of this question that
is very hard to get over, and it is this.
Everyone that I have heard or read on the
subject admits that one of the reasons for
the great progress made by Denmark during the last forty or fifty years was the
agricultural education they gave to the
youth of that country. Everyone who has
spoken or written on this subject has also
pointed out that education was the great
factor in the progress of the agricultural
industry of Germany, and if the results
of education have been such as we see in
the competitive markets of England and
elsewhere surely it is necessary that something should be done to put the youth of
Northern Ireland on the same plane to
enable them to compete with these other
countries.
"B" SPECIAL CONSTABULARY.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the
annual salary and allowances of the Chief
Officer of the "B" Constabulary in each
county and county borough; the annual
salary and allowances of a constable of that
Force; the number of its members having
in their possession firearms supplied by
the Government; and the public expenditure on that Force in the year 1930;
and whether the Imperial Government
have any control over that Force.
Mr. LONG: I am also asked by my
right hon. Friend to reply to this question.
The are six County Commandants in
charge of Class "B" Special Constabulary
throughout Northern Ireland, each of
whom is in receipt of an annual salary of
£450 and of an allowance to cover travelling expenses at the rate of £140 per annum.
The County Commandant for Antrim is
also in control of the Special Constabulary
in the County Borough of Belfast, and is
in receipt of an additional salary of £50
a year.
Apart from seven constables who are
employed full time as clerks in the various
County Commands and are in receipt of
an inclusive salary at the rate of 7s per
day, the members of the "B" Force generally are only in receipt of a bonus at the
rate of £2 per annum, payment being
conditional on the completion of the prescribed number of training drills, while
members of the Reserve Class in which no
drills are required are entitled to a bonus
at the rate of 5s per annum.
The number of members on the 1st
October in possession of firearms supplied
by the Government was 10,670.
The estimated cost of Class "B" during
the current financial year is approximately £60,000.
The answer to the last part of the Question is in the negative.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Arising out of the
answer of the Deputy Leader of the
House, there is a matter of some gravity
that I wish to allude to. Eleven days
have passed since the destruction in the
Loughgall district of County Armagh of
the Hibernian Hall. Not one person has
been brought to book in connection with
that crime.
Mr. SPEAKER:
With reference to
the remarks of the hon. and learned
Senator, this case is now sub judice, and
therefore any discussion of the subject I
must rule out of order.
Mr. CAMPBELL: On a point of
Order. I do not understand that proceedings of any kind have been taken
in any court in reference to this case.
Mr. SPEAKER: A claim for compensation has been lodged in connection.
with the destruction of the A.O.H. Hall,
and it is a question whether any reference
can be made to a matter that is subjudice. Therefore, I must rule it out of
order.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Of course, I bow
to your ruling, but I understood the
rule in such a case was that as long as
no proceedings were pending against any
individual or individuals the matter,
being a matter of great public importance, could be referred to in this
House. I think there is precedent for
that. Would your ruling mean that no
reference whatever could be made to this
particular matter of such great public
importance?
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
29 OCTOBER 1930
Mr. SPEAKER: Of course, discussion
can take place after the proceedings have
terminated. It is only while the case is
pending that the matter
discussed.
cannot
be
Mr. McALLISTER: With very great
respect, is it not in order for a Member
to comment on the fact that the destruction of a hall has taken place and
that no one has been brought to justice
or attempted to be brought to justice for
the crime?
Mr. GLENDINNING: On a point of
Order. Does this matter arise at all out
of the question?
Mr. CAMPBELL: Shall we be entitled to raise the matter on the Motion
for the Adjournment?
Mr. SPEAKER: No, I think not.
A NATIONAL MEDICAL SERVICE.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether the attention of the
Government has been drawn to a scheme
before the British Medical Association at
its last representative meeting for making available to every section of the
people of Great Britain a general national
medical service, and for extending the
medical benefits of the Health Insurance
Acts to the dependents of all persons
insured thereunder, the service to be
based on
contributory insurance
system.
a
Mr. LONG: The attention of the
Government has been drawn to the
scheme of the British Medical Association for a fuller medical service in Great
Britain and the extension of the medical
benefit system. They will watch with
interest any developments of the matter.
Colonel SINCLAIR: Might I ask the
indulgence of the House to slightly amplify the answer to the question that has
just been given by the Deputy Leader of
the House. As recently as August of this
year the British Medical Association
published a scheme of medical service for
the community. It has not, so far as I
know, been submitted to either the Imperial legislature or this legislature, but
possibly in the future, as the Deputy
Leader of the House has indicated, it may
come before us. I intervene to point out
the object of this scheme, and to say that
it is quite clear that the rapid advance
of the social services, which has been very
greatly accentuated in medical matters
by the passing of the Local Government
Act of 1929, is leading to what must be a
gradual adaptation of the medical profession to the needs of the community.
Whether it is that we are drifting in the
direction of a State medical service, or
whether some other scheme might be
adopted I do not know, but the British
Medical Association have lodged this
scheme, after due consideration, as a
scheme that might be considered alongside, but not in opposition to, a State
medical service.
Many people are of the opinion that a
State medical service might lead to a tame
and mechanical uniformity in the treatment of the sick and injured, whereas
a scheme like that of the British Medical
Association, which is one based upon the
organisation and management of the
scheme by the medical profession as
distinct from the State, might lead to a
higher type of medical service being given
to the community. In the latter case the
medical profession would be on its mettle
and would be vitally concerned with the
development and working of a scheme
which they themselves had originated.
Probably they would take a great deal
more interest in such a scheme and in
their patients, and the probability is that
such things as research and initiative
would be more likely to be fostered under
a scheme of that kind than under a State
medical service.
May 1 add one remark, and that is that
as regards medical and hospital treatment.
there is one class of the community that
is liable to be overlooked. The middle
class, not inaptly described as the new
poor, are finding themselves in this position; that they are heavily taxed for the
maintenance of many social services, although they themselves are almost deprived of the advantages which the
working class or the ordinary poor are
now furnished with. In the future it will
be desirable that this new poor class should
receive some consideration, and therefore
if a scheme is put forward on a strictly
contributory basis for the inclusion of all
people, not only insured persons but their
dependents, then I think we would like
to see a very great improvement in the
status of the class to which I have alluded.
The scheme of the British Medical Association is not before Parliament yet, but
J
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Colonel Sinclair]
it is before the community, and I think
it would be as well if the community considered whether a State medical service
or a service such as that outlined by the
British Medical Association would answer
the purpose best. Some change is inevitable when the hospitals and poor law institutions of the country are being
converted into hospitals for the relief of
the poor, and it is just possible that that
class in the community, a very numerous
and a very silent class, the new poor,
may have their interests overlooked.
Mr. LESLIE: Arising out of that may
I ask whether it is intended to lay on
the Table of this House some paper giving
an indication of the character and nature
of the scheme to which allusion has been
made? One feels rather at a loss in trying
to follow a debate relating to a scheme
which one has not seen in print.
Colonel SINCLAIR: The British
Medical Association published in August,
1930, their complete scheme, and it is
available for anyone who is interested in
the subject.
PROTECTION OF WILD BIRDS.
Mr. S. CUNNINGHAM asked the
Leader of the House whether it is the
intention of the Government, to introduce
legislation as to wild bird protection, on
the lines that were suggested some time
ago by a representative committee.
Mr. LONG: An informal Committee,
representing the various interests concerned, was arranged by the Minister of
Home Affairs to consider whether it was
desirable to amend the existing legislation in regard to the protection of wild
birds in Northern Ireland. That Committee reported some time ago, and its
recommendations have been receiving very
careful consideration, and I may say,
generally speaking, that the Government
are in full sympathy with the objects
which the Committee have in view.
Hon. Members are, however, aware
that at the present stage in this Session
it would not be possible to introduce such
a Measure, and it may be anticipated
that in the coming Session there will probably be a considerable programme of
what I may term Measures of first-class
importance to be considered by this
House. In these circumstances, unless
this Measure dealing with wild birds could
be regarded as an agreed one which could
be dealt with without taking up the time
of the House, I fear it would not be possible to give any assurance as to the date
of its possible introduction.
It is, of course, open to any Member
of the House to bring in this matter as a
Private Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: Again the same question arises. Reference has just been
made to a Report by an Advisory
Committee or an informal Committee.
Is it intended to lay that Report on the
Table.
Mr. LONG: I regret to say that I am
unable to furnish a reply to the hon.
Senator.
EDUCATION GRANTS AND LOANS.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House the total amounts of Government Grants and Loans since the passing
of the Education Act, 1923, for:-
(1) the provision of equipment of
provided elementary schools;
(2) the enlargement, reconstruction
and equipment of transferred schools: !
(3) the provision and equipment of
four-and-two Committee schools;
(4) the enlargement, reconstruction
and equipment of four-and-two Committee schools;
(5) the provision and equipment of
new Catholic voluntary schools;
(6) the enlargement, reconstruction
and equipment of existing Catholic
voluntary schools;
(7) the repairs of Catholic voluntary
schools, and the repairs of other
schools;
(8) the present numbers of provided.
transferred and four-and-two Committee schools and Catholic and Protestant voluntary schools not under
four-and-two Committees.
recon-
Mr. LONG: In reply to the hon. and
that the cost of the erection and equipment of new provided public elementary
schools and of the enlargement,
struction and equipment of existing
public elementary schools transferred to
local Education Authorities is a matter
learned Member, I should first explain
Oral Answers
29 OCTOBER 1930
for these authorities, and the Government does not make direct grants for
this purpose.
As the cost of these works
is regarded as capital expenditure, it is
met by means of loans raised by these
authorities under the powers given in
Section 74 of the Education Act of 1923,
and the loans may be raised either from
the Ministry of Finance, from the Treasurer
to the authority concerned, or in the open
market. The annual sums necessary for
the repayment of principal and interest
in respect of these loans form portion of
the approved expenditure of the authorities towards which the Ministry of
Education makes grants under the provisions of Sections 75 and 76 of the Act.
With this in mind the first and second
parts of the question may be answered
by saying that the Ministry of Finance.
has sanctioned loans to local Education
Authorities amounting to £233,140 for
the provision and equipment of new
"provided" public elementary schools,
and £8,920 for the enlargement and
reconstruction and equipment of transferred schools. In addition, loans
amounting to £499,155 for both provided
and transferred schools have been raised
from sources other than the Ministry of
Finance.
In reply to the third and fourth parts
of the question, I should similarly explain that the Government does not
make grants towards the provision and
equipment of new voluntary public
elementary schools under the management
of statutory school committees or for the
enlargement, reconstruction and equipment of existing voluntary public
elementary schools under such Committees; but local Education Authorities
are empowered under the provisions of
Section 16 of the Act to make grants
for this purpose. Up to the present the
amounts so granted were £5,216 for provision and equipment of new schools, and
£4,442 for the enlargement, reconstruction and equipment of existing schools.
As the hon. and learned Member is
aware, certain of these grants were paid
to schools which were under Roman
Catholic clerical management prior to the
setting up of these Committees.
In reply to the fifth and sixth parts of
the question, grants have been made by
the Ministry of Education amounting to
£21,130 for the erection and equipment
of new voluntary schools under Roman
Catholic management, and £5,036 for en-
Message from Commons

largement, reconstruction and equipment
of existing voluntary schools under such
management. These were direct grants
made to the managers of these schools
out of funds provided by Parliament for
the purpose, and were in fulfilment of
obligations taken over from the former
Commissioners of National Education on
the transfer of the educational services
to the Ministry.
In reply to the seventh part of the
question, I have again to explain that
the Government does not make grants
towards repairs of any class of schools,
but that local Education Authorities
contribute, under Section 15 (2) of the
1923 Act, a sum equal to one-half of the
approved expenditure on such annual
repairs as are necessary in the cases of
schools under statutory Committees. The
particulars of the amounts spent on these
repairs are not available in the Ministry.
The following are the particulars in
reply to the eighth part of the question :

Message from the Commons.
The Commons have passed the Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill,
the Electricity (Interim Supply) Bill, and
the Agricultural Produce (Meat Regulation) Bill, to which they desire the concurrence of this House.
Exchequer and Financial Provisions
(No. 2) Bill.

519 Constabulary Bill--

THE SENATE
Second Reading
Electricity (Interim Supply) Bill.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read a
First time."(Mr. Long).

Bill.

Constabulary Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This is a short Measure
which I think hon. Members will find
self-explanatory. Under the Act constituting the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
namely, the Constabulary Act, 1922, a
fund was established on the lines of the
system which had been in operation in the
Royal Irish Constabulary since the establishment of that Force nearly a century
ago for the reward or benefit of members
of the Force, and Section 4 (5) of that Act
sets out the sources of income of the Fund.
At that time the Fund received-
(1) Disciplinary fines imposed on
members;
(2) Fines awarded in the Petty Sessions Courts to Police as prosecutors;
(3) Part of the fees paid on the verification and stamping of weights,
measures and measuring instruments.
Hon. Members will recollect that power
was taken in the Constabulary Act, 1928,
to divert the Weights and Measures Fees
from the Reward Fund to the Royal
Ulster Constabulary and Ministry of Commerce Votes, and when introducing that
Bill my right hon. Friend, the Leader of
the House, explained the reasons for this
diversion. The income of the Fund is,
therefore, now limited to Petty Sessions
Fines and Disciplinary Fines.
The annual income from the sources
which I have mentioned has been on the
average over the past few years over
£2,000, and the Fund has now been
brought up to a healthy financial condition. It has a capital value fully able.
to meet the statutory claims which may
be made upon it under the Regulations,
and the Government has come to the conclusion that the diversion of the Petty
Sessions Fines to the Reward Fund can
no longer be justified. It will, of course,
be understood that the members of the
Force who act as prosecutors have no
financial interest whatever in the proportion of the fines which thus come into the
Reward Fund as a result of the prosecutions instituted by them, and the only
payments from the Fund to individual
members of the Force are the awards made
to those who have displayed exceptional
gallantry involving risk of life, or who
have shown exceptional zeal, intelligence
and ability in the discharge of duty.
Nevertheless, I think it is desirable that
there should not any longer be the
slightest suggestion that the Force is financially interested in instituting prosecutions.
even so remotely and indirectly as through
the medium of the Reward Fund.
It is, no doubt, most desirable that there
should be some fund from which actions
of exceptional merit can be rewarded, but
as I have already pointed out the present
condition of the Fund is sufficiently strong
to insure an income for the purpose as
well as to enable recreational and other
benefits for the common advantage of the
Force to be provided. In all circumstances, therefore, the Government has
come to the conclusion that it is expedient
to abolish a system for which there is no
longer any necessity and which is in fact |
no longer desirable. Possibly such an
arrangement may have been of advantage
in the early days of the Royal Irish Constabulary when the pay was very small.
but it does not appear suitable to present
conditions.
Accordingly it is proposed, and Clause
1 of the Bill now before the House gives
effect to the proposal, that these Petty Sessions Fines should, as from the 1st April
last, be appropriated in aid of the Royal
Ulster Constabulary Vote. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: It
I think,
appears,
that the Bill before us is a small administrative Bill, almost, one might say, a
book-keeping Measure with which we need
not greatly concern ourselves. But there
are two points, and one of them is certainly of some importance. It will be
seen that following the old wording of
Section 49 of that very old Act to which
Senator Long referred, the old Constabu
lary Act, we refer to damages. I think
we ought to be assured that there is nothing in this Bill which, when it becomes
law, will prevent a police constable who
521 Third Parties Bill-

29 OCTOBER 1930
Second Reading
has received an injury in the course of
his duty from receiving any compensation
which may be awarded to him. It sometimes happens in cases of assault that part
of the penalty is to be applied by way of
compensation, and I think we ought to be
told and I think it will be more convenient if we are told at a later stageexactly what the position will be as regards any compensation of the sort to
which I have alluded, and, I think, at the
same time, a little more explanation of
the expression "damages" might suitably
be given to us. I do not think it is a
very suitable case for putting an Amendment on the Paper, because the only
Amendment one could put down would be
one which almost certainly would not
materialise and would only waste time.
At the same time, I think more information might be given about the distinction.
between damages and compensation, if
any.
It will be seen that it is proposed to
repeal the whole of paragraph (b) of the
Section of the Constabulary Act of 1922.
That Section provided, as the House will
remember, among other things, for the
allocation to the fund with which we are
dealing of any sums directed by a Court
to be paid to that fund, and it seems,
perhaps, a little doubtful as to what is
to happen in the future to those payments, if any, and I do not think there
are likely to be very many. They will
presumably continue to be paid
directed, for this House could not stop
that, even if it wanted to, but when they
have been paid into the fund how will it
be administered. That, again, is a matter
we might be told later on, and meantime
there does not seem any reason for opposing the passage of the Bill.
as
Mr. LONG: I shall be in a position to
reply authoritatively on the Committee
stage to the points the hon. and learned
Member has raised.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time") agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Tuesday next.
Third Parties (Rights Against Insurers)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This is a short Measure,
and will, I think, commend itself to hon.
Members. I might explain that it is
quite distinct from the larger Bill which
is at present in another place in regard
to motor traffic, including the question
of compulsory insurance.
The present Bill now before us is to
deal with the case of a person who is already insured against third-party risks,
but who becomes bankrupt, or dies insolvent, before meeting a claim by a
third person for which he has rendered
himself liable. In such a case while the
action at law would be against the insured
person the real defendants are, of course,
the Insurance Company; and although
the Insurance Company may be perfectly
prepared and able to pay the damages
awarded, yet these damages, owing to the
bankruptcy of the principal defendant,
become part of the general assets of the
bankrupt and along with the other assets
are distributed among the creditors on a
pro rata basis, so that the unfortunate
plaintiff only secures whatever dividend is
payable, and not the total amount of the
judgment awarded to him by the Court.
If the owner of the motor car at fault
were already a bankrupt before the accident, the injured person, although
securing his judgment and although the
Insurance Company might be perfectly
willing to pay, would secure no dividend
at all, and whatever was awarded would
go in its entirety to other creditors in
existence at the time of the bankruptcy.
This state of affairs is, as I think the
House will agree, very unsatisfactory, and
we are proposing to remedy it by the
present Bill. Hon. Members may ask
whether there is any precedent for giving
a privilege in this way to a person who
is entitled to damages arising from a
motor accident. A somewhat similar
arrangement applies in the case of Workmen's Compensation under the Workmen's Compensation Act of 1927. In
Section 7 of that Act there is a similar
protection for the workman in the case
of a bankruptcy arising. I may add that
legislation similar to what we are now
proposing in this Bill was recently passed
in the Imperial Parliament. I beg to
move.
Mr. LESLIE: The only thing that it
seems necessary to add to make the matter
perfectly clear is that this Bill is not
limited to motor accidents. It is a
general Bill dealing with the rights of
third parties, and it follows exactly-
Third Parties Bill
Adjournment-
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
with the necessary alterations as regards
the two Bankruptcy codes-the Imperial
Statute to which the Deputy Leader of
the House referred, the Statute which
became law on the 10th July last. There
is one other point that might perhaps
occasion difficulty, and that is the
position of the workman. The workman
is by this Bill in no worse position than
anybody else. I only mention that because I have heard it suggested that
paragraph (b) of Sub-Clause (5) had some
rather unfair effect as regards workmen.
That, of course, is not the case. The
Bill is a Measure which is really long
overdue, not only in Northern Ireland,
but also across the water.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I would like to
ask the Deputy Leader of the House
whether it would not have been possible.
and very much more simple to have
embodied this Bill in the Motor Bill that
is at present in another place, instead of
going through the cumbersome and, to
my mind, unnecessary procedure of introducing two separate Measures both
dealing with third-party insurance?
think there should be some explanation
I
as to why two Bills are brought in instead of embodying the whole legislation
in one Bill.
Mr. LONG: I think the speech of the
hon. and learned Member (Mr. Leslie)
amply answers the question Senator
McAllister has put. This Bill does not
relate entirely to motor accidents.
On Question, Motion (That the Bill be
now read a Second time) agreed to.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That
this House do now adjourn until Tuesday
next." On that day we propose to take
the Committee stages of the Constabulary
Bill and the Third Parties (Rights
Against Insurers) Bill, and the Second
Readings of the three Bills we read a
First time to-day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Four o'clock until
Tuesday next.

Questions to Minister.
Mr. SPEAKER: Before calling upon
the hon. and learned Member to put the
question standing in his name I should
like to remind hon. Members that, generally, in putting a question they are entitled to make any explanatory remarks
they desire, but when the reply is given.
it is not in order to make a further speech
except with the general concurrence of
the House under Standing Order 17. The
mere fact of the hon. Member putting the
question is equivalent to making a speech.
Of course supplementary questions on the
reply can be put, and any other hon. Member can speak to the question.
EXECUTION OF FREE STATE WAR-
RANTS IN NORTHERN IRELAND.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether, in regard
to the question of the execution of warrants on either side of the Border, the
Government have had under consideration-
(1) the Order promulgated in 1923 by
the King in Council, under which Acts
of Parliament applicable to the execution in England or Scotland of warrants
issued by the Courts in Ireland are to
apply to warrants issued by the Courts
of the Irish Free State, and such warrants are in Northern Ireland to be
dealt with as if they had originated in
England; and
(2) the Order issued in 1924 by the
Executive Council of the Irish Free
State under which every provision of
any British statute relating to the execution in Ireland of warrants issued by
Courts in England or Scotland is to apply to the execution in the Irish Free
State of warrants issued by Courts in
Northern Ireland, and every such warrant is in the Irish Free State to be
dealt with as if it had originated in the
Irish Free State; and
whether the Government will give further
consideration to this matter.
Mr. LONG: In reply to this Question,
I can only say that the fullest consideration has necessarily been given to the
provisions of the Orders referred to by the
hon. and learned Member in connection
with the question of the execution of warrants as between Northern Ireland and
the Free State, and the whole matter continues to engage the earnest attention of
the Government's legal advisers.
WORKING OF THE RENT RESTRIC-
TION ACTS.
Mr. BARCLAY: I beg to ask the
Leader of the IIouse whether it is a fact
that in Belfast some owners of houses of
Oral Answers
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
[Mr. Barclay]
£8 and under £8 valuation refuse to let
such houses when they become vacant, and
offer them for sale at prices often beyond
their real value; whether the purchaser
has to deposit part of the purchase money,
and also pay from week to week interest
on the balance, being liable to eviction on
default of payment of principal and interest; and, if so, whether steps will be
taken to check this evasion of the Rent
Restriction Act.
Mr. LONG: I am informed by my
right hon. Friend, the Minister of Home
Affairs, that he has not received any complaints that landlords are refusing to relet houses of £8 valuation and under,
which become vacant.
It is not illegal nor contrary to the provisions of the Rent Restriction Acts for
an owner to sell a house which he has
obtained possession, and to accept payment
of the price by instalments.
Whether in any particular case the
transaction is bona fide or, on the contrary, a mere cover for exacting a rent
higher than is permitted, and is an
evasion of the Rent Restriction Acts,
would be a matter for the courts to determine.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I should like to express satisfaction that this matter has
again been mooted in this House. I think
it is a matter of very considerable public
importance in the city of Belfast, because
in the city of Belfast there are 55,000
houses rated at £8 or under in valuation,
and just about a year ago only 150
of these houses were vacant. The
House will therefore realise the intense competition there is amongst
poor tenants, and the people who devised.
the practice referred to by Senator Barclay are really driving a motor car through
the Rent Restriction Acts. Of course, as
the Deputy-Leader of the House has said
they are actually within the law, and if
the tenant understands what he is doing
there is no redress for him; but some
people looking for a house will sign any
document in order to get that house. I
do hope the matter will receive attention
at the hands of the Government.
PROTECTION OF WILD BIRDS.
Mr. LESLIE: In asking the question.
which stands in my name I should like
to explain that there is an unfortunate
mistake on the Paper, which I regret. It
is probably my own fault. The reference,
as the House will remember, was to an informal Committee and not to a Departmental Committee. I beg to ask the
Leader of the House whether the Report
of the informal Committee the
Protection of Wild Birds, to which reference was made in this House on October
29th, will be laid upon the Table.
on
Mr. LONG: I have been asked by
my right hon. Friend, the Leader of the
House, to reply to this question.
There is no objection to laying the report and recommendations of the informal
Committee referred to by the hon. and
learned Member on the Table of the

LARNE RURAL DISTRICT ELEC-
TRICITY SUPPLY.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move, "That the
Special Order made by the Electricity
Commissioners for Northern Ireland
under the Electricity (Supply) Acts, 1882
to 1919, and confirmed by the Ministry of
Commerce under the Electricity (Supply)
Act, 1919, in respect of so much of the
Larne Rural District as lies to the North
and West of the Boundary line described
in the First Schedule to this Order is
hereby approved.
This Special Order, granted by the
Electricity Commissioners for Northern
Ireland and confirmed by the Ministry
of Commerce, will confer upon the right
hon. Randal Mark Kerr, Earl of
Antrim, statutory authority to supply
electric energy for public and private purposes in portion of Larne Rural District
in the County of Antrim. The Rules of
Procedure have been complied with and
the Order which follows the usual form is
not opposed. The Order will not be fully
operative until the undertaking has been
transferred to a company registered
under the Companies Acts. I beg to
move.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
