Exchequer and Financial Provisions
(No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: We welcome the Minister
of Commerce to this House to-day. The
right hon. Gentleman will reply to any
questions that may arise during the debate.
This is a somewhat involved and technical Bill of a financial nature and for
details I would refer hon. Members to
the White Paper which is being circulated, and which gives particulars of the
various stages of this difficult question of
the finances of Unemployment Insurance.
The Bill represents the last act in connection with one of the most difficult
financial problems with which the Exchequer has had to deal since our Government came into existence. It will be remembered that five years ago, the deficit
of the Unemployment Insurance Fund was
accumulating so rapidly that it seemed as
though the conditions of contributions
and benefits could not be sustained.
However, by means of the Reinsurance
Agreement, which was ultimately arrived
at with the British Government, and
which is now embodied in Acts of both
Parliaments, we have been able to protect our workers and at the same time
maintain the finances of the Insurance
Fund in such a position that it is not beyond the resources of this country to find

the necessary capital to make the required
advances to the Fund, so long as it is not
self-supporting.
The effect of that arrangement was to
throw a burden of £3,424,434 upon the
Exchequer of Northern Ireland, representing lendings of which there was no
expectation of repayment. The question.
was how to replace or provide against this
amount. The British Treasury had
agreed to provide for a portion of the
interest on this sum, but decreasing by
equal amounts of £100,000 over a period
of thirty years; after which time the
Treasury liability would cease; and this
Government took power to repay the
public debt to the extent of £100,000 each
year. Complications arose with the
Joint Exchequer Board, however, and the
most recent arrangement, which is now
embodied in this Bill, is that we shall use
virtually all the free capital resources of
the Government by forming a new Capital
Fund, which is created by the amalgamation of the old Capital Fund and the
Reserve Fund-both of them created
under our Exchequer and Financial Provisions Act of 1923. We will then allow
this new Capital Fund to accumulate
within itself until the deficit in the Insurance Fund, amounting to £3,000,000,
is replaced. This replacement, it is expected, will be effected about the year
1952.
I may explain that the Old Capital
Fund amounted to £575,000 and the Reserve Fund to £700,000. It is proposed
to pool these sums and allow them to
accumulate at compound interest until the
£3,000,000 is reached.
The result of these arrangements as proposed in the Bill will be that the capital
position of the Exchequer in relation to
the Unemployment Insurance Fund will
at last be consolidated, and, so far as can
be seen, adequate provision has been made
to maintain the Exchequer in a solvent
position so far as capital commitments in
this matter are concerned. I beg to move.
Mr. LESLIE: I imagine that none of
us would attempt to follow this Bill otherwise than through the medium of the very
clear and explicit White Paper circulated
some days ago and again made available
to hon. Members this afternoon. I did
some few days ago endeavour to study
the Bill, and with the assistance of all the
other Acts of our Parliament to which it
referred, and of the White Paper, I was
able to appreciate the figures. Figures of
Electricity Supply Bill
Exchequer Bill-

THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that kind are not such as to remain very
readily in the head, and I can only say
that further study of the White Paper
will convince everybody that this Government has carried out a financial transaction of some difficulty with great success,
and has thereby relieved the Province of
a somewhat dangerous liability, a liability
which was none the less dangerous because
I think it was not very widely appreciated.
The Government ought to be congratulated on having brought a long and difficult business to a successful issue.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I rise merely to
protest as an ordinary private Member
against the fact that this White Paper
is available to us for the first time five
or six minutes before the Bill is presented
to us for Second Reading. I think the
House generally will agree that those
Members who wish to grasp the object of
this Measure and to know something
about it are placed in an absolutely impossible position, because until this White
Paper is issued the information which it
contains is confined to Members of the
Government. We who do not sit on the
Government bench have no opportunity
of learning the reason for bringing in this
Bill. I have not even had time to read
this White Paper yet, but in listening
to the speech of the Deputy-Leader of
the House I was unable to follow his explanation. At one period he was talking
about Great Britain coming to our aid
to the extent of three or four million
pounds, and in the next sentence he
talked about drawing on a reserve fund.
I think it is very unfair to the ordinary
Members of this House that this White
Paper should have been withheld until
almost the last minute, and it is unfair to
the general public, because it means that
the Bill must go through this House without criticism. From that point of view,
and also as a matter of self-respect, I do
strongly protest against the manner in
which this Bill has been introduced.
Mr. LONG: With regard to Senator
McAllister's complaint about the circulation of this White Paper, I should like
to explain that it has not been usual in
the past to circulate White Papers to
Members of the Senate in regard to financial Measures. On this occasion, however, it appeared to me that this was a
complex Bill which I at least had difficulty in understanding, and therefore I
thought the White Paper should be circulated to Members of this House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed.(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Bill ordered to be read the Third time
on Thursday next.
Electricity (Interim Supply) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: It will be seen that the
Measure which hon. Members are now
being asked to consider proposes to empower the Ministry of Commerce to construct certain main transmission lines and
to make available bulk supplies of electrical energy in readiness for a more
detailed scheme which, I understand, will
be submitted for Parliamentary sanction.
in the Spring Session of next year.
Hon. Members will be aware that the
problem of electricity development is one
which has been the subject of most careful
study in Great Britain for a considerable
number of years past. Earlier legislation
there was conceived at a time when electricity was used principally for lighting
and its application for power purposes was
almost unknown. The problem was the
subject of inquiry by expert committees in 1918 and in 1925, and
we can witness to-day in Great Britain
the working out of the policy which
has finally emerged, namely, the policy
of concentration of larger generating units in larger and fewer power
stations, coupled with an extensive scheme
of co-ordination and inter-connection by
means of high-tension transmission lines.
In considering the lines of development
best suited to our area, the Government
was fortunate not only in being able to
draw valuable lessons from the research
and development in Great Britain during
recent years, but also in being able to
secure, in a consultative capacity, the
services of Mr. J. M. Kennedy, a wellknown London Electricity Consultant,
who has been prominently identified with
the planning and carrying out of the
schemes now in course of completion in
Great Britain following legislation there
in 1926. I am sure that hon. Members
are already familiar with the two Reports
submitted by Mr. Kennedy, and they
£33 Electricity Supply Bill-

4 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading
will, consequently, appreciate that the
Bill now before the House covers the preliminary steps necessary to give effect to
the principal recommendations contained
in the first of these Reports relating to the
South-Eastern
area of the Province.
This is the area which naturally offers
the most fruitful field for preliminary
development. Within it lies the most
highly-industrialised area in our Province,
comprising the northern portions of the
Counties of Down and Armagh,
The system of distribution by high
tension lines has, of course, already been
given a trial in Northern Ireland. A
main transmission line now connects Belfast with Holywood and Bangor, while
another connects Belfast with Lisburn.
The latter line was provided of such
capacity as to enable it to form the first
link of a main line joining up the various
industrial centres in that portion of the
country which, as I have said, has been
marked out as the first field of development. Partly with a view to providing
some much needed employment, and
partly in order to ensure that adequate
supplies of electrical energy would be
available to the town of Lurgan during
the forthcoming winter, arrangements
were made for the extension of the hightension line from Lisburn to Lurgan. In
this way, thanks to the hearty co-operation of the parties immediately concerned, it has been possible to avoid the
necessity for an extension of the existing
generating station at Lurgan which,
owing to the rapid development in the
use of electricity in that town, was becoming taxed to its full capacity.
The area of immediate development,
which lies to the south of a line drawn
from Belfast to Dungannon, is supplied at
present by six statutory undertakings
owned by local authorities, namely,
Lurgan, Armagh, Newry, Bangor, Holywood and Rostrevor, and five statutory
undertakings owned by companies,
namely, Lisburn, Dunmurry, Portadown,
Markethill and Warrenpoint. In addition to these there are several small nonstatutory undertakings, of which those at
Dromore, Banbridge, Kilkeel, Newcastle,
Castlewellan and Ballynahinch lie in the
direct route of the main transmission
lines proposed by Mr. Kennedy. It will
be readily understood that under the
present system of distribution it is necessary for all these stations to maintain a
substantial proportion of stand-by plant.
Furthermore, in a number of centres the
system of supply is by direct current and
the generating costs and overhead charges
render it impracticable for such undertaking to supply cheap power. Indeed
it may be said that so long as generation
and distribution are in the hands of a
number of small undertakings it will be
impossible to attain the two main objectives which the Government has in mind,
namely, the fostering of industrial development through the provision of cheap
power, and the improvement of the
amenities of life generally throughout the
Province. The electrical developments of
recent years all go to indicate the urgent
necessity for a merging of interests in
order to eliminate those factors which retard development.
That the South-Eastern area still offers
a fruitful field of development is illustrated by the fact that, if we except the
town of Lurgan, there is no statutory undertaking in the area which during the
year 1929 sold as much as 20 units per
head of the population per annum.
Existing charges, too, are on such a scale
as to preclude any general use of the
present facilities for industrial purposes.
The Government feels that it owes it to
the people living in outlying districts to
remedy this state of affairs and to adopt
the best means of making available for
them abundant supplies of electrical
energy for light and power at such prices
as will encourage consumption on a much
larger scale than exists at present.
The short Bill now before the House
proposes to empower the Ministry of Commerce to construct certain main transmission lines to make available bulk
supplies in readiness for the more detailed
scheme which will be submitted to the
House at a later date. Provision will be
made in the Measure that is to follow for
the constitution of a non-profit earning
authority in the shape of an Electricity
Supply Board which will take over the
lines for the construction of which the
necessary authority is being sought by this
Measure. It is eminently desirable that
the work of construction should be pressed
on this winter, partly, as I have already
said, with a view to providing employment and partly to meet the growing
demand for electricity in this area.
Minister of Commerce has kindly undertaken to deal with any points that may
arise during the debate. I beg to move.
The
Mr. CAMPBELL: This Bill seems to
me to be the application of Socialistic
Electricity Supply Bill -
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
principles on a very large scale.
It is, I
think, what may be called a pilot Bill.
As I understand it, it is the forerunner of
another Bill which is to be introduced
later on and under which the Ministry of
Commerce will undertake the control of
electricity throughout Northern Ireland,
the buying out of the different generating
stations and transmission lines of private
undertakings, and the supply of electricity
to all and sundry in the Province who may
require it. That is an extremely large
order. It represents a vast amount of
enterprise, and it will all be concentrated
in the hands of the Ministry except, I
hink, electricity for power purposes in
Belfast. In Belfast the Corporation supply electricity for power purposes, and as
I understand it is not proposed to intrude
in their area. I would be very glad if
the Minister, whom we are all pleased to
see here to-day in the Senate, would
in his reply say whether or not the powers
given by the Bill to the Ministry of Commerce include the power, say, of making
an arrangement for linking up where
necessary and desirable the Northern
scheme of electricity with the large and
extensive Shannon Electricity Scheme
prevailing in the rest of Ireland.
Such
an arrangement might be to the common
advantage of both areas. I sincerely
hope that the Bill which is coming on
next Session will not exclude the possibility of electricity contact with the rest
of Ireland. Switzerland, which is a small
area, is, I understand, supplying electricity to two or three countries to the common advantage of all. I do hope that we
in this little island of ours will not have a
border line as far as electricity is concerned.
Mr. LESLIE: I welcome this Bill as
the forerunner of a Measure for which
there has been an increasing public desire
for many years past, that is the provision
of electric power which so many people
want for various purposes--the provision
of that power by means of bulk supply
controlled by strong hands, that is controlled by people in command of sufficient
resources not only of money but of
talented assistance. It has been found
again and again that a very small electric
supply company cannot command sufficiently skilled assistance, or a sufficient
amount of capital to carry out their
business to the satisfaction of their clients.
This particular Bill is, as we have just been
told, the forerunner of a major Measure.
I do not detect in it the Socialistic taint
to which the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell) referred. It seems to me
that there is no indication here whatever
of any intention on the part of the
Government to assume the control of
supply in the future. All that there is is
an intention on the part of the Govern
ment, if Parliament consents, to take over
for the moment the elementary work that
has to be done-to do the necessary spade
work and set up the necessary authority,
and then to leave the matter to be carried
out by companies or persons supported by
the necessary capital drawn from wherever
they may see fit.
As regards the Bill itself, I must take
a line which I think is not the line perhaps that I generally take. It certainly
is not the line I will take in a few minutes
on another Measure. I am rather doubtful whether the Government on this
occasion has asked for sufficient power.
The position is that the proposal is to
carry out all the work that has been
adumbrated within twelve months. I am
inclined to doubt whether twelve months
is a sufficiently long period, or whether
it would not be better to have asked for
this power for two years. If it should
be decided that two years would be a
better period than twelve months the Bill
may require some trifling amendment in
other respects, but I do not think any
amendment will be required if the Government are confident that they can do
all that has to be done within the twelve
months and bring in the major Measure
at an early date next year in order that
it may pass, if it is to pass, through
all its stages before December, 1931.
The only questions I shall have to ask
the right hon. Gentleman are whether he
is quite satisfied that there is a power here
for the Ministry to take payment for such
supplies of electricity as they may make--
I think myself. there is implied powerand also whether the position of the town
of Bangor is regarded as sufficiently safeguarded. I do not think there is any
other question I have to ask. I would
merely like to reiterate what I askedas to whether twelve months is sufficient.
and remind the House that I do not think
this Measure is of a class which could
properly be continued under the Expir
ing Laws Continuance Measure, and that
it is a Measure to which Parliament ought
not to give an extended course of life after
it is once passed. If further time is re-
537 Electricity Supply Bill-

4 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading
quired the time for giving that time is
110W. I support the Second Reading of
the Bill.
Mr. MCALLISTER: There is just one
question to which I would like to address
myself, especially after the remarks of
Senator Leslie and the Deputy-Leader of
the House. Both seemed to agree that
the supply of electricity cannot adequately
be carried out by small undertakings.
Senator Long, I think, stated that small
undertakings could not foster industrial
undertakings, and Senator Leslie seemed
to concur to some extent. What I want
to ask the Government is this: Is it a fact
that they have given sanction to local
authorities entering into agreement with
small private companies to supply electricity to expend colossal sums of money
in cables and standards to enable the
supply to be brought twenty and thirty
niles distant to the urban districts which
they represent? May I point out this
aspect of that matter to the Government?
It will be a very serious matter for those
various local authorities to spend such
large sums of money without the greatest
possible security that these local companies or undertakings are in a position
at all times to supply the necessary demand.
I would like to ask the Minister of
Commerce-who as Senator Campbell said
we are glad to see here and from whom
as I know from past experience we will
receive a courteous and as far as he can
the best possible reply-can he justify
the fact that these local authorities are
allowed to pledge the rates of the various
towns, which in many instances are very
badly mishandled, for the time to come,
and does he believe that the small local
companies will be at all times in a
sufficient financial position to supply the
towns they represent. I personally think
that it is a very grave matter to sanction
loans to local authorities on this basis.
entirely agree with the Deputy-Leader of
the House and Senator Leslie in their
belief that the local authorities should
only be allowed to apply for loans of
money in the case of some undertakings
that the public and the Government are
perfectly sure are capable of carrying out.
the demands placed upon them.
I
There is just one other little matter I
would like to refer to. In the erecting
of these standards in the country districts
the Government, to my mind, do not seem
t
to have protected the interests of the
farmers sufficiently. These standards
seem to be erected at the will of these
companies in the very centre of small
fields of one acre. As far as I can see
that would not do so much injury to the
field and would supply their wants equally
well if they were placed in the corner or
the end of a field. The placing of the
standards right in the centre of the field
is putting an impediment on the farmer
in carrying out his work. I would like
to ask the Minister of Commerce if they
have power under the Bills already passed
to control these standards, and if in future
he will see some provision is made to protect the farmers from this unnecessary
grievance.
or
In
Mr. ARMSTRONG: I notice that
Armagh is one of the towns included in
this Measure. I notice also there is
power to "purchase upon such terms as
will be agreed upon by the owners of any
generating station stations."
Armagh a very extensive and very costly
system has been established, and I am
sure, though the owners may not be unreasonable, it may be difficult to arrive at
terms which would adequately compensate
them for their outlay. I do not observe
in the Bill that there is any power as to
arbitration or compulsion. Therefore, I
would like the right hon. Gentleman to
kindly tell us how that is to be dealt with,
in case that terms offered were objected to.
I daresay it will lead to a reduction in the
cost. We have at present to pay 9d a
unit. Of course if terms can be arranged
I am sure it will be beneficial to the community.
The
MINISTER OF
COMMERCE
(Mr. Barbour): I may say that I much
appreciate the criticism which has been
offered to certain points in this Measure,
and which I can see is founded upon a real
desire to obtain enlightenment on certain
points, and also to ensure careful consideration of what this Bill hopes to accomplish. Of course hon. Senators will
understand that this is only a provisional
Measure, and the point to which Senator
Leslie alluded will bring home to this
House the sincerity of the Government in
their desire to proceed with the least possible delay to the promotion of the further
major Measure which it is hoped will be
presented to the Houses of Parliament at
the opening of the Spring Session. It is
in the confidence that we will be able to
get over all the difficulties that may exist
539 Electricity Supply Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Barbour]
that we are preparing the Bill, and also to
meet the views which undoubtedly will be
expressed by the different boards, whether
municipal or companies or private undertakers, when the exact terms of the Bill
are available for them. We are confident
that Parliament will be able to deal with
the matter next Spring, and the twelve
months asked for in this Bill is ample to
provide for as a purely transitional period.
As to whether the Measure is Socialistic
or not, I would classify the supply of
electricity rather as one of those great
public utility undertakings-an undertaking dealing with one of the vital necessities of life, as although electricity is a
comparative newcomer when compared
with such an important matter as the
supply of water or later the supply of gas,
still in the short time that has elapsed
since its development I think it has
assumed in our daily requirements just as
essential and vital a part as the other two
to which I have referred. Therefore, it
is desirable that such an important service
should be provided within the reach, if
possible, of every member of the community, and we think that can best be
done by being a non-profit-earning
authority.
Now, as to the matter of socialistie
principle, I dare say that principle may
be resented as an encroachment upon the
field of private enterprise. The supply of
electricity throughout our area, I say
with
regret, has not up to now attracted private enterprise to anything like the extent it should. I may inform the
Members of this honourable House that of
the supply of electricity sold in Northern
Ireland in the year 1929, 693 million units
were sold by local authority undertakings,
and a balance of approximately 1,300,000
units was supplied by undertakings owned
by companies. In other words, of the
total supply of electricity sold in Northern Ireland only 1.8 per cent. was supplied by private companies. If we exclude Belfast we find that over 4,000,000
units were supplied by local authority
undertakings as against 1,300,000, which,
as I have informed hon. Members, was
sold by companies, that is to say, approximately 75 per cent. of the current sold
in districts outside Belfast were generated
by local authority undertakings.
Senator Leslie has referred to one of the
advantages which we hope to derive from
this Measure, that is, combining the supply of electricity throughout the Province
!
in the hands of comparatively few undertakings. Hon. Members will, of course,
have noticed that there are some parts f
the Province which the Ministry does not
propose to deal with in the meantime.
Practically the whole of the County of
Antrim is already under the charge of
the Antrim Electricity Supply Company,
under powers which were granted to it by
this Parliament. It is not proposed to
encroach on their area at all, nor for the
time being, on the area controlled by the
County and City of Belfast.
I dare say some of the criticism advanced by my old colleague, Senator
McAllister, has maybe been prompted by
his experience in County Antrim, which is
under the County Antrim Electricity
Supply Company. But there was one
matter to which he referred and to which
I think other Senators also referred, and
that was the question of the disturbance
of farmers by reason of the placing of
standards upon their property. Of
course, it is unfortunately necessary to
enter private property in order to find
positions for these standards, but I know
it is the desire as far as possible to locate
them so that they shall do the least possible injury and interfere as little as
possible with the working of these lands.
As far as possible they are placed in the
hedges, but owing to the needs of the service they have to follow a very straight
line, and there are also restrictions in the
length of the spans between the standards.
So far as they can I know those entrusted
with the charge of finding positions for
these standards do their utmost to meet
the views of the farmers. Unfortunately
it is not possible to do so in all cases.
We have consulted the different undertakings and I believe the general method
of arriving at the payments to be made for
these would be by assuming by the Board
of Electricity when the time comes, or by
the Ministry of Commerce as at present,
responsibility for the loan charges which
the different municipalities or urban
boroughs have incurred in providing a
supply of electricity. Of course, drawing
from a big supply, such as the harbour
station of the Belfast city undertaking.
would ensure a very much more reliable
source of supply than any small generating
station would be able to give.
Referring again to Senator Campbell,
he seemed to think that the Ministry
eventually was going to provide this service and run the electricity undertaking
541 Agricultural Produce Bill

4 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading.
in the area under this Bill.
so.
That is not
It is the intention of the major
Measure that as soon as possible the Ministry of Commerce should hand over the
entire conduct of this matter to the Electricity Board which will be set up under
the Bill to be promoted in the Spring.
That would be a public board similar perhaps to the Belfast Water Board, or a
board of that kind.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second time') agreed to.
Agricultural Produce (Meat Regulation)
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: In moving the Second
Reading of this Bill, I need hardly remind
the House that the Northern Ireland export trade in meat and meat products is
of the greatest importance to our agricultural industry. The volume of business
which is conducted almost exclusively with
Great Britain was valued last year at,
roughly, two and a quarter million
pounds. Unfortunately, this trade is at
the moment in the gravest danger.
It is a regrettable fact that consignments of unsound meat from this country
have reached the British market, and
have been condemned there by the Public
Health Authorities. As a result, these
authorities have requested that arrangements should be made whereby meat sent
from Northern Ireland to Great Britain
would be subject to inspection and
certified as sound before export. If we
are not prepared to comply with these
requirements we shall be confronted
immediately with an embargo on the exportation of meat to Great Britain.
One of the requests the Government
received was from the Department of
Health for Scotland, which complained
that the local authority of the City of
Glasgow had discovered that fresh pork
affected with tuberculosis had been
imported from Northern Ireland.
letter states:
The
The Minister is doubtless aware that
supplies of meat imported from the
Argentine and other distant countries
are inspected by a qualified veterinary
surgeon at the time and place of
slaughter, and that only sound meat is
passed for export, and the Department
are concerned that meat imported into
Scotland from Northern Ireland should
carry with it a similar guarantee of
soundness given after skilled veterinary
inspection. The Irish Free State
Government have intimated that they
intend to introduce a Bill during the
next Session of the Oireachtas, which it
is hoped to pass into law in time to
become operative towards the early
Autumn of this year, and which will
provide for the inspection and certification of fresh meat exported from the
Free State.
The Department trust that it may be
found practicable at an early date to
secure that only sound meat is exported
to this country from Northern Ireland.
We must remember, too, that all other
countries sending meat to cross-Channel
markets give the desired guarantees as to
soundness. Consequently it is imperative.
that Northern Ireland meat exported to
Great Britain should have the required
guarantees as to soundness. The Measure
now introduced is designed to achieve that
object. The proposals in the Bill have
received the approval of the interests
mainly concerned, and it has been framed
so as to disturb as little as possible the
system on which the valuable pork trade
of Northern Ireland is at present conducted.
Briefly, the Bill provides as follows.
Firstly, in regard to fresh meat-no fresh
meat, that is, pork, beef or mutton, may
be exported unless the animal has been
inspected both before and after slaughter
and the meat passed as sound.
animal must be killed on suitable premises. Furthermore, all exporters of
fresh meat must be licensed, and an
annual fee of £1 paid for each licence.
The
Secondly, in regard to bacon and hams
-all bacon-curing premises must be
licensed, and all carcases arriving at a
bacon-curing factory must be inspected.
Curers will be required to pay a licence
fee of £10 per annum where the number
of carcases dealt with is 200 a year or over,
and £5 per annum if under 200 a year.
Small fees will be charged in respect of
all carcases examined, so that the Measure
should be substantially self-supporting.
In order to avoid possible misconception
I wish to make it quite clear that the Bill
in no way cuts across the powers already
held by Public Health Authorities, and
in the administration of the Measure it is
proposed that the closest co-operation
should exist between the Ministry and
such authorities. I beg to move.
543 Agricultural Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
Mr. LESLIE: The object of the Bill
as it has just been outlined here by the
hon. Senator, the Parliamentary Secretary, and as it was outlined in another
place by the right hon. Gentleman, the
Minister of Agriculture, is a very admirable object indeed. We have been told
that the value of our export trade in meat
of one kind and another is 24 millions per
annum. That is a sum which it would
not be possible for us here in Northern
Ireland to neglect. It is obvious we must
take all possible and reasonable steps to
save that trade. It appears we have been
attacked from Scotland for something in
the nature of negligence in the past. We
have not been given, so far as I know, any
details showing how that 24 millions is
made up, how much of it, for instance,
is fresh beef and mutton, how much is
fresh pork, and how much is cured pigs'
flesh, that is to say, bacon and hams.
am rather inclined to believe that if the
figures were analysed it would be found
that a very high percentage, between 80
and 90, would consist of cured meat rather
than fresh.
I
It is a pity that more of the figures
and something more of the correspondence
with the authorities in Great Britain have
not been laid on the Table of the House,
and I think it is unfortunate that the opportunity was not taken to issue a White
Paper a week or so before this Bill was
introduced. If it had been so issued in
another place intimation would have been
given in the Press and no doubt we could
have obtained copies in the same way as
some of us were able to obtain another
White Paper which has recently been before us.
We should have had more detail.
The Bill has been subjected to a great deal
of misrepresentation, but I must say that
a great deal of that misrepresentation is
due to the fault of the authors of the Bill.
They have tried to accomplish too much
and to some extent they have gone about
it in the wrong way. However, we shall
see how those things work out when we
come to examine the Measure in more
detail.
One thing that has been stated it has
just been stated by the hon. Senator (Mr.
Long) is that the Bill provides for inspection before the killing of animals intended
to be sold across the water as fresh meat.
I cannot find anything in the Bill prescribing that there shall be inspection before
killing, nor can I find anything in
the Bill definitely prescribing, in language
which a plain man could readily understand, that killing is only to take place
at some registered slaughter house. No
doubt, however, we shall receive further
information on that subject later.
I have said, I think, that the authorities
have attempted to accomplish too much.
We have in the text of the Bill before us
proposals that might very well be broken
up into practically three separate Bills,
one dealing with fresh meat, whether pork
or otherwise, another dealing with the
cured product, and a third Bill dealing,
if necessary, with poultry. We have not
yet heard that there have been any complaints made from across the water as to
the method of sending poultry, but poultry
is included in this Bill. I shall take an
opportunity at a later stage of asking this
House we shall then be in Committee---
to cut the reference to poultry out of the
Bill altogether and to leave that to be
dealt with, if necessary, when the time
arrives, separately, because those things
which are applicable to poultry are not
necessarily applicable to meat in the fresh
sense, and vice versa.
It follows from this association of so
many various subjects that the Billthough the Bill, if I may say so, seems to
me to be very well drafted-is very hard
to follow, and it is not at all clear to
those who are mostly concerned. Many
people will read this Bill. There are Bills
and Bills. Some like, for instance, the
Exchequer and Financial Provisions Bill,
which was dealt with earlier this afternoon, will probably never be read by anybody except the departmental experts, but
this Bill will be read by the farmers and
dealers throughout the country, and I
venture to say that they will find it very
puzzling. Again and again they will have
to say they do not understand this or that
provision. If I may take an example, it
seems to me that the word "fresh" ought
to be inserted in two or three places as
qualifying the word "meat,' not that it
is necessary from a purely drafting point
of view but in order to make the meaning
quite clear, and in the third instance that
particular word seems-if I appreciate the
Bill correctly, and I am not sure that I
do-to be absolutely essential in order to
protect the public from the prohibition of
the crating of hams and bacon. Although
it does not say that a man shall not send
bacon across the water in a crate that
appears to be what the Bill has done.
545 Agricultural Produce Bill-

4 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading
Again, this somewhat curious method of
dealing with the Irish Free State cannot
but cause confusion. We find constant
references throughout the Bill to the sending of meat to Great Britain, the Irish
Free State, and the Isle of Man, and
thea, somewhere about the seventeenth or
eighteenth Clause in the Bill we find that
nothing in this Bill shall apply to the
Irish Free State except, except, except,
and so on. I do not think that is fair to
the ordinary man. We are told in no
paragraph of the Bill as to what is fresh
meat, that is to say I suppose when meat
ceases to be fresh, but I imagine that
fresh meat does not, at all events, include
chilled or frozen meat. I do not believe
there is at the moment any trade in chilled
or frozen meat in Northern Ireland, but
it is possible that it might arise in the
future.
Now, passing to the other part of the
argument with which I opened, namely,
that some of the things it is sought to do
are being done in the wrong way, I think
there is a great deal too much of the
Ministry throughout the Bill altogether,
and a great deal too little is left to the
ordinary economic laws and to ordinary
private enterprise. It cannot, I think,
be denied that if one man sends rotten
meat to another man he is not going to
do it twice. And again, in spite of what
was said with regard to working in the
utmost amity and fellowship with local
authorities, it does seem to me that there
are dangers of overlapping in the Bill.
There is also, I am afraid, a danger with
regard to inspectors. Is this Bill going
to lead to the appointment of more
inspectors paid out of public funds or not?
If it is not, if there are already sufficient
inspectors available to do the work cast
upon them by this Bill it would seem to
follow that there are at the moment, when
the Bill is not law, too many inspectors
for the work they have to do. Either, I
am afraid, we have been wasting public
money in the past or we are about to
waste it in the future.
Something was said, too, about the Bill
being self-supporting. If the Bill is to
be self-supporting it can only mean that
the money to support the Bill is to be provided out of the pocket of the producer or
the handler of the meat, that is to say,
that prices will go down. And again, why
does the Minister of Agriculture think it
necessary in these matters invariably to
provide for licensing somebody to do something which, apart from the statute, he
would have a perfect right to do? Why
cannot the Department be content with
merely registering the premises that have
to be registered? Why go through this
form of licensing? Moreover, in this particular Bill, although the licence for fresh
meat export is limited in cost to £1, in
the case of the bacon curer apparently a
£10 penalty and it is an annual penalty
and not paid once and for all-is to be imposed. It is true that, yielding to strong
representations in another place, a concession was made to the man who did not
Ideal with more than 200 carcases in the
year, and he is now allowed off with £5.
But to impose a heavy licence duty such as
£10, or even £5, by way of control of
trade seems to me to be going dangerously
near the limit of the powers conferred
upon us, not so much by the Government
of Ireland Act itself as by the Miscellaneous Provisions Act of two years ago,
which, as the House will remember,
extended those powers and gave us the
right to legislate in matters necessary
for the maintenance of sound standards
of agricultural produce. It seems to me
that we are using this licence as a source
of revenue, and that is not a matter which
seems to be worthy of our commendation.
Again, throughout the Bill we seem to
be seeking to legislate in advance of the
requirements of to-day. Sometimes we
are in advance, and sometimes it would
seem we omit to take into consideration
the possibility of differences in practice
arising in the future. I have said that
there is somewhat too much of the Ministry about this Bill. If that be so there
is far too little right of appeal against
the decisions of the Ministry. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). When the Bill
first made its appearance in another place
there was still less right of appeal than
there is now. On that matter, again,
representations were made, and again a
concession was granted, that is to say the
concession which the other House demanded and would have insisted upon was
made. I do not think there is yet
sufficient right of appeal in the Bill and
I shall take the opportunity later on of
asking this House in Committee to give
the right of appeal on two further matters to which I need not now refer.
Still further, here we have the Ministry
embarking, as it states, upon a crusade
with the best possible object, an object
in which it desires the support, assistance.
and goodwill of everybody concerned, and
it starts off with the introduction of a
547 Agricultural Produce Bill--
.48
THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
minimum fine. The moment a minimum
fine is put into any Bill it always raises
my suspicions, and I think it raises dislike, and even disgust, in the minds of
many others. Why in the world on an
occasion such as this, when, as I say, good
feeling on all sides is needed for this Bill,
should we go cut of the way to introduce
a source of discord? As a matter of fact
we shall find when we come to examine
the Bill in Committee that the introduction of a minimum fine on this occasion
is peculiarly ridiculous. It is always absurd, but in this case, if we do not make
an alteration, nobody who reads the Bill
with any intelligence can refrain from
laughing at it, and I do not think that,
for the sake of preserving the practice of
the Ministry in this matter, this Bill
should be exposed to the ridicule of all the
Parliaments of the world.
Now there are some particular difficulties in the Bill. There is this question
of the slaughter of pigs. A great deal
has been said about it. It is perfectly
clear from the Bill that a pig which is to
be killed and exported as fresh meat can
be killed at a registered slaughter house,
and we have been told, I understand, that
unless it is killed at a registered slaughter
house it cannot be exported as fresh pig.
That seems to be one of the common expressions, and that is what we have been
told. But it can, of course, be killed,
cured, and turned into bacon without
going to a registered slaughter house, or
so it would appear. But the Bill is really
lamentably deficient in clearness upon that
point, and I hope that we shall get some
more information on the matter later.
Then there is another obvious difficulty.
We all know that in some parts of the
country a farmer has a pig killed and he
does not know whether the ultimate destination of that pig is the fresh market or
the cured market. Very often he does
know. In my own part of the world I
think there are practically no pigs sold
for export as fresh meat, but in many other
parts different practices prevail in different
markets, and it is really very difficult,
if you take the farmers as a whole, for
them to know what is going to happen
ultimately to their pigs. If a farmer has
to send his pig to a registered slaughter
house, and if it has to be inspected before
slaughter if it is for export as fresh pork
is all a matter of great difficulty, and we
ought to have a great deal more definite
!
information on this subject than has yet
been given to us.
Again, there is the case of what I call
the small bacon curer. I have been in
places in years gone by where it was the
habit of the small agricultural labourer to
keep a pig, or two or three pigs, and it
was the custom in those houses when a pig
was killed for that pig to be cured and
sold, very locally indeed, in the form of
ham or bacon. The provision in this Bill
seems to be admirably designed for putting
an end to anything of that sort. So far
as one can see it completely kills the man
who wishes to carry on a small bacon
curing business. I shall deal at a later
stage with the extraordinarily heavy fee
which the bacon curer is required to pay.
It is £10 a year for the man carrying on
business as a bacon curer. I am not going
to join the group of misrepresentors by
suggesting that the expression "carrying
on business" means that a man cannot
cure a pig for his own use, but I should
like some information as to how many
pigs you must cure in the year before you
can be said to carry on business. You
can get off with a £5 licence if you do not
cure more than 200, but we are not told
how many we have to cure before we carry
on business.
There are other very heavy expenses
cast on the farmer in this matter.
If we
refer to Clause 6-a Clause which, so far
as I can see, can only be properly
approached by a proposal for its complete excision in order that something
more explanatory may be inserted-we
are told that a person who holds a
licence under this Bill is to pay
at such times as may be prescribed
such and such fees. How often these fees
are to be paid is left to the imagination;
whether he is to pay the fee as the pig
goes into the slaughterhouse, whether he
is to pay as it leaves the slaughterhouse,
whether, again, he is to pay at the time
of inspection, or when it is in transit, we
are not told. That is a matter that
should be cleared up.
Cover
the
Later on in the Bill there is provision
for the deduction of one shilling,
Clause 10. I think, to
expenses of the buyer and to provide
insurance. What on earth interest
the farmer takes in the expenses of the
buyer I do not know, but the farmer has
to pay that shilling. It is quite easy to
say that a shilling is not much. I received the other day from some unknown
549 Agricultural Produce Bill-

4 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading

far
18:55
correspondent a copy of the "Mid-Ulster
Mail," in which it was pointed out that,
during the month to the end of
September, that shilling would, as regards Cookstown alone, amount to £600.
It went on to say that that was a very
nice endowment for the big pork buyer.
I do not want to go into details any more
than is necessary, but I cannot conceal my
opinion that the effect of this Bill, if it
becomes an Act, will be to force this trade,
which we want to foster, into the hands
of the big combines, to kill the small
trader, and to exclude would-be new
entrants from coming into the business.
That is a possibility which I think we
ought not to permit. Do we want to put
the whole of this pork trade into the
hands of the large combines, or do we
want to encourage the smaller trader?
I shall not oppose this Bill in the way of
moving its postponement for six months,
for the reason to which I referred at the
commencement of my speech, namely, the
very great necessity of doing what we can
to safeguard the trade, but I cannot blind
myself to the fact that I should not be at
all sorry if, after this Bill has passed the
Committee stage in this House, it were
dropped and brought in again next year.
I cannot see myself that any great harm
would be done, for hon. Members will see
on reference to the last page, that it is not
proposed that the Bill should come into
immediate operation. On the contrary,
if my memory serves me right, the provisions of the Bill are to be brought into
operation at such time or times as the
Governor of Northern Ireland may
appoint, and different dates may be
appointed for different provisions of the
Act.
Although I am not moving the rejection.
or postponement of the Bill, the Committee stage will, I think, be lengthy and
perhaps rather contentious. There will be
a good many Amendments on the Paper.
I imagine. Some of them, no doubt, will
be put down merely to draw an explanation, which is quite a useful course to
adopt. Other Amendments will be consequential. But there will be somemore I think than usual-that will require to be pressed to a decision, and in
those I shall ask for the independent
judgment of the Members of the House.
I think we ought to remember in dealing
with questions of this kind, affecting the
trade of the country, that we are not dealing in any way with party questions or
acting on party lines. We are dealing
with a matter which is very important to
the trade of the country, and which is
extremely important to the producer,
normally the farmer, who has been passing through an extremely hard time.
Speaking generally, the farmers know
their business fairly well, but perhaps not
quite so well as the Ministry may wish. I
do not oppose the Second Reading of this
Bill, but I trust it will be materially
modified later on.
Mr. McHUGII: In getting up to make
a few observations on this Bill I must
confess to you that I know very little
about it, because from the start to the
finish of the Measure it is a mass of confusion. This unfortunate Six-County
area has for the last ten years been trying
its utmost not to promote progress, but to
crush out in every possible way the farming community. We have had other
matters introduced here and passed
through this House-legislation dealing
with live animals, which leaves the farmers at the present moment in a nost
hopeless position. To-day we are asked
to pass the Second reading of this particular Measure on what ground? The
only reason put forward by the Deputy-
Leader of the House is a letter written
by the Health Committee of Glasgow.
Anyone who has been to Glasgow must
form the opinion that that committee
would be much better employed in
cleaning up their own city.
The strange thing is that this Bill deals
with the Irish Free State and with the
Isle of Man, from which no complaints
have ever come, and yet here we find
the Government putting every possible
obstacle in the way of the unfortunate
farmer. What this Bill means is this,
that thousands of poor men in Northern
Ireland who keep a pig or two continuously
about the place for the sole purpose of
keeping their little trifle of money together, will not be able to kill their pigs
without first informing the Ministry that
it is their intention to do so. In other
words, their pigs are to be subjected to
inspection. In another place, the Minister
of Agriculture, in reply to the point with
which I am dealing, stated that farmers
could combine for the purpose of getting
inspectors, veterinary surgeons, to inspect
their pigs before they are killed. Otherwise their pigs could not be sold as fresh
pork.
551 Agricultural Produce Bill- -

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. McHugh]
In the name of all that is wonderful,
may I ask if that is not one of the most
absurd propositions ever put forward.
These poor people are to send for a
veterinary surgeon and give him three
guineas to have their pigs inspected. The
next important question is the killing of
the pig, and where it should be killed.
The pigs must be killed at a registered
slaughter house, if we are to follow the
law as it is laid down here. This means
that this Bill will pass into the hands of
policemen who will be continually on the
track of unfortunate farmers. When the
farmer brings his pig to the market he is
liable to a penalty of one shilling, as the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) has
pointed out. That is, at the present
moment, every farmer who brings a pig
into market will be charged one shilling
for carriage and six shillings for inspection. The Government now to complete
the thing are providing that another 2s 6d
will be charged on each pig that goes into
market. And this is all in the interests
of the unfortunate farmers, if we are to
believe one half of what has been said by
the Minister in charge of the Bill.
What is really wrong with the Northern
Government is this: They never give
proper thought and consideration to the
Measures before they submit them to
Parliament. They are always rushing
and then amending Bills. We have
amended over fifty Bills since I came into
this House in order to repair their own
shortcomings. This Measure is now being
rushed through without the consideration
that such an important Bill requires,
affecting as it does one of the most important industries in Northern Ireland--
pig rearing.
The most important question that arises
under this Bill is that of inspection. All
this could be done in a far simpler way
than by calling upon the farmers to contribute their small mite towards expenses.
You have in this city and in other places
in Northern Ireland where the exportation
of pigs takes place a full staff of inspectors
at the ports examining everything that
leaves alive or dead. If those men did
their duty, in the name of all that is
wonderful, how did the meat complained
of ever get to Glasgow? You have a big
staff of inspectors at the ports, and it is
their duty to inspect this food that is passing over to Scotland and elsewhere in
order to see that it is perfectly sound.
Inspectors are appointed to do this duty,
and they are paid a first-class salary,
although I am sure they are not satisfied
with their salary-those men are never
satisfied. Now, you are transferring this
burden of inspection to the unfortunate
farmers.
I most strongly protest against this Bill,
as I find it is clearly going to crush the
small man out of pig rearing altogether.
There is nothing in the Bill to encourage
anybody to rear pigs, but there are many
handicaps. Take the case of the small
grocer who kills a number of pigs for sale
in his own shop. Under this Bill he must
have, first of all, a licence to do so.
Secondly, the pigs must be killed at a
slaughter house, and he must produce a
veterinary surgeon's certificate that they
were perfectly sound and free from disease.
Any person who looks at the existing price
of pork may well ask himself will these
people be able to stand this.
Anybody who has followed the agricultural industry in Northern Ireland
must come to the conclusion that the
farmers are driven almost to the wall.
The Ministry, unfortunately, is giving
the final crush now by heaping more expense upon the industry, and calling upon
it to provide revenue that it should not be
expected to provide. If the Government
have inspectors at the ports, I do not see
why the farmers should be called upon to
pay this shilling and two-and-six per pig.
This Bill should be immediately thrown
out by this House until the proposals contained in it have been more carefully
considered. Otherwise the pork industry
of Northern Ireland will cease to exist so
far as the poor and helpless small farmers
are concerned.
Mr. MCALLISTER: I think the justification for the introduction of this Bill
was the fact that it was to maintain the
pork industry and preserve our markets
in England. But I am credibly informed
by practical farmers and merchants and
curers that the greatest cause of the present state on our pork business in England is because farmers in Northern
Ireland have been encouraged to breed
the wrong class of pig. I think there is
no doubt that the Ministry of Agriculture
must carry upon their shoulders a very
great part of the responsibility for the
precarious position in which this great
industry is at the present time. They
have, if I may say so, been more pigheaded than usual on this question. They
553 Agricultural Produce Bill---

4 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading
have persisted in encouraging the Northern
arner to preserve in breeding a class of
pig which undoubtedly matures early and
from which the farmer receives his money
early, but it is productive of so much fat
that it has practically driven our pork
industry out of the markets of England.
I agree with my hon. Friend, who has
just sat down, and I think there is a consensus of opinion that in these Six
Counties we are being over legislated. I
think farmers have had a tremendously
onerous time in discharging the obligations under this pin-pricking legislation
to which they have been subjected.
again join most sincerely in the protest
made by hon. Senator Leslie against this
principle of the minimum fine. It defeats
the ends of justice and serves no useful
purpose. I am quite convinced that these
ninimum fines are not put in honestly,
but are put in as a means of raising
revenue. You will notice that these Bills
in which the minimum fines occur, such as
the Motor Bill-the first Bill in which the
minimum fine was introduced-and all
the other Bills do obviously bring a huge
revenue into the Government pockets from
the minimum fines. Anyone who is in the
habit of attending in the local petty
sessions court must be struck with the
tremendous tax raised by means of the
minimum fines that have been put upon
the community since the Government
started this practice. I have not the
slightest hesitation in saying it is a dishonest attempt to extort revenue out of
the pockets of the people.
Another thing that I take exception
to is this. I join with Senator Leslie
in saying that there is far too much
power left in the hands of the Ministry
under all these Bills. I think that it is
perfectly indefensible that a man, in the
pork business say, should have to depend
on the Ministry as to whether he is allowed
to take up a continuous business, whether
they will issue a licence to him, or
whether they shall on their own initiative
refuse it. I think it is absolutely indefensible for any Act of Parliament to
authorise that. We are going to be the
most official-ridden country in the world
if we allow this sort of thing to continue.
I also notice that the Ministry have
again secured to themselves power to make
regulations. I do not know whether hon.
Senators have seen some of the regulations
made under these Bills that I have seen.
Take the Motor Bill. The regulations are
far bigger in volume and more ponderous

and more vital in their operation than
the Bill is itself. Surely as a self-respecting legislative body we cannot delegate our
own responsibilities to officials. If we
are going to make certain laws let us make
them in Parliament, and do not let us
delegate them into the hands of officials
who might by some particular whim or
idea transform the whole legislation that
we passed. That is a thing no selfrespecting Member of any legislative assembly can for a moment tolerate. I also
had sent to me the paper which the hon.
Senator referred to. I think he made
rather a mistake in quoting the figures.
It is pointed out that in the market in
Cookstown, in the month of September,
this tax upon the farmers of a shilling per
pig amounted to £24, and for the year it
amounted to £670. I think the hon.
Senator made an error there.
Mr. LESLIE: Probably I said month.
I meant year.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I wish to say a very
few words with regard to this Bill. The
object of it, of course, is all right, to
raise the quality of the pork. But we in
this House, I think, are fairly entitled to
consider how it affects the pockets of the
farmers. I do say that I think we would
have welcomed the presence and assistance
of the Minister of Agriculture with regard to a Measure of this kind. We had
a very interesting and illuminating speech
from the Minister of Commerce on the
subject of electricity.
One or two points in this Bill deserve
attention. It means certainly the introduction of pork curers' licences. It also
means the introduction of fresh meat
licences. And I take it as a natural
sequel it means the introduction of inspectors in an area in which we have a
superabundance of officials. There is a
great difficulty-and perhaps the Deputy-
Leader of the House can say whether I
am right or not, but there is an impression abroad that notwithstanding this Bill
a man can go round the countryside buying pork from farmers and be entirely
outside the four corners of this Bill.
not sure whether that is right or not, but
it is a matter on which I should like a
little enlightenment from the Deputy-
Leader of the House, because, as he has
said, the Bill is a complex one.
I am
Then the impression is also abroad that
the farmer who has dead pigs to sell to
curers in the market is not hit by the Bill
555 Agricultural Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Campbell]
either. I am not sure whether that impression is right or wrong, but perhaps the
Deputy-Leader of the House will clear it
up. Then there is the very remarkable
Clause 10, a very singular Clause in any
Act of Parliament.
It says:
Where the carcase of a pig is bought
by or on behalf of a licensed baconcurer, the buyer may deduct from the
price of the carcase such sum, not exceeding one shilling, as may from time.
to time be prescribed so as to defray
the expenses of the buyer and to insure
the buyer against loss in the event of
the carcase being subsequently certified
by an inspector, acting in the exercise.
of his powers under this Act, to be unfit for human consumption; and if such
deduction is made the seller of the carcase shall not be liable in respect of any
loss arising in the aforesaid event.
I do not know whether the impression
is right or not, but is that a kind of
insurance clause against say a dishonest
farmer sending to the market the carcase
of a pig which he knows to be diseased?
Is it possible that by the mere formal deduction of one shilling from the price of
a diseased carcase the buyer is free from
further trouble, and that the man who
sends a carcase of this kind into the market is also insured from trouble arising
therefrom. If that is the meaning of the
Clause I certainly would strongly object.
It would be putting a premium on a dishonest bargain. Then although the fines
in these cases seem to be very small, to
the farmer of the North of Ireland struggling with very heavy odds, not the least
of them being the climatic odds of this
part of the country, I think the fines are
an important matter. I hope it will be
dealt with in Committee and some effort
made to reduce these minimum fines.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I rise to
support this Bill, and the reason I do so is,
with the object of retaining and increasing
the markets for our surplus products. We
are living at the door of the finest market
in the world. We are in competition
with our colonies, who have a long seaboard and high costs of conveyance, and
who are subjected to far more drastic regulations than any that are contained in
this Bill in order to ensure a pure food
supply. If we do not follow what is done
in other countries our markets will be lost
to us altogether. The criticism of this
Bill has been about as severe as any 1
have heard in this House, but I think
there is a misunderstanding about the
Bill. I do not think there is anything
in the Measure to prevent a farmer killing his pigs just as he has been in the
habit of doing in the past, and selling
them in the markets of the Six Counties.
I can continue to kill pigs as I have
always done and send them to the Belfast market. The Bill only prohibits
the export of impure meat to the three
countries named in the Measure, and
it has nothing to do with meat killed
for home consumption. In all such
Measures as these the criticism of the
Ministry of Agriculture seems to be
very severe in the markets and fairs of
the Province, and it is generally a
question of finding fault with the number of inspectors. This criticism very
often originates with some person who
has applied for a job as an inspector
and did not get it.
Mr. McHUGH:
a job.
I did not apply for
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I know that,
but there are hundreds of these people
who go to fairs and markets who do
apply. Slaughter-houses in the country
are frequently in an unsanitary condition, and when meat begins to go high
it is sent into cold storage and then put
into casks and shipped in a putrid condition, with the result that our entire
Ulster produce is damaged. We heard
the same condemnation of the Egg Bill
when it was going through either this
House or the other House, but before
that Egg Bill was introduced Ulster
eggs were the lowest-priced eggs in the
London market, whereas to-day they
command the highest price. There are
two millions of money involved in the
export meat trade, and on this question
of impure foodstuffs our Ministry of
Agriculture is up against the Ministry
of Agriculture and the Ministry of
Health in England. If impure food is
sent from this Province across the water
those Ministries put their foot down and
say they will not have it, and they are
perfectly justified in doing so. I think
this condemnation of the Ulster Ministry
of Agriculture is rather unfair. If they
had not brought in their regulations in
connection with sheep scab the only
profitable product we have to export
would have been prohibited from landing on the other side of the water. The
same applies to black scab. If the
557 Agricultural Produce Bill—-

4 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading
Ministry had allowed potatoes to be exported from black scab areas the Ministry
in England would have shut the door
against them.
I sometimes wonder where we would have
been had it not been for these regulations. If the markets in England had
been closed against our surplus produce
we would have had to close down altogether. It may be the case that the
number of inspectors in this country is
more than in other countries, in proportion to the population, but that is because there are so many small farmers
in Northern Ireland. In England and
Scotland you have farmers with from
200 to 1,000 acres, and it is to their interest to see that their produce is marketed in good condition because they
have such large quantities to sell. But
a farmer in this country with one or
two tons of potatoes, or two or three
barrels of apples, thinks he is doing an
awfully clever thing if he gets away with.
inferior produce, because he has no more
to dispose of. Consequently there is
more necessity to look after him.
I have no doubt that the protection
the farmer wants will arrive before
very long, and that Great Britain
will follow the flag for its supplies,
but they must be pure and sound.
For these reasons I support the Bill.
Mr. BARCLAY: I support the
Second Reading of this Bill. As the son
of a farmer, and as one who has been
brought up in the country, I know a
little about the pig industry, and
if there is one thing more than
another that I appreciate in this Bill it
is the inspection of dead pigs. I have
known of more than one or two instances
where pigs have been diseased, and as
soon as the farmer realised they were
diseased he sent for the butcher and they
were slaughtered. They were perhaps
taken to a market a number of miles
away and sold, and no doubt the pork
would be converted into bacon and ham
and sold to the public. It might have
found its way into the City of Belfast,
or been shipped across to England or
Scotland. I am very glad that this particular Clause will mean a tightening up
of the supervision over dead pigs, and
I hope that in future diseased pigs will
be kept in the pig house until they die
and are buried. I have also known instances where a COW was in a healthy
condition, but when she came to calve
something went
was going to die.
sent for and she
taken by the butcher to a market town.
What he did with her I do not know,
but I expect the meat was sold for consumption by working-class people. I am
very pleased that such a thing will be
made very
difficult in the future, and
for this reason I have very great pleasure
in supporting the Bill.
wrong and the COW
The local butcher was
was slaughtered and
Mr. LONG: The quantity of pork exported from Northern Ireland ports last
year was 62,061 cwts.; bacon, 199,841
cwts.; hams, 107,480 cwts.; beef and
mutton-these are not shown separately
-3,489 cwts.; and coarse meat, 43,390
cwts. Therefore, meat for export must
be inspected both before and after
slaughter. Some Senators appeared to
be under a misconception as to the extent of the powers under this Bill.
Fresh pork or pork intended for export
as
fresh meat must be inspected, the
British authorites inform us, both before
and during slaughter. The entrails must
be examined by a skilled veterinary
surgeon.
Mr. LESLIE: Where in the Bill does
the hon. Gentleman find the information
to that effect?
Mr. LONG: The hon. Senator will
find that power is contained in the Bill,
but at the moment I cannot put my
finger on the particular part. With regard to the ordinary man's pig, to
which Senators Hill Dickson and
McHugh referred, the ordinary farmer
can still kill his pig at home and sell it for
bacon in the ordinary course. Another
matter that seemed to cause misapprehension was the fee of 1s. This is, as
has been pointed out, an insurance
Clause and Senator Campbell need have
no fear that there is any danger of the
Clause being used by the dishonest seller.
The buyer having to bear the loss will
take care to reject any carcase obviously
diseased. In the main, however, disease
is not obvious. It is only discovered
when the pig is cut up. May I illustrate
this? A farmer takes in ten pigs to
Strabane market, and he pays 1s. per
pig. The buyer accepts delivery, and if
on inspection one of the pigs, worth say
£6, is found to be unsound the buyer
bears the entire loss. The farmer who
sold the pig is at no loss whatever. That
is the meaning of this insurance.

past, but it appears to satisfy the bacon
curers and farmers with whom I have
talked, and I think the House may rest

Senator Leslie raised a point as to dealings with the Free State. We
following the plan set up by the Marketing of Potatoes Act which was passed by
this House two years ago and is working

In the Bill poultry is only dealt with so

are
I think those are about all the points
that I recollect were raised on the debate.
I should like to point out that the inspection by the Ministry of Agriculture
before slaughter will be carried out with
the utmost consideration for the convenience of exporters. No elaborate
precautions will be made regarding
places for slaughter. The only thing
that will be insisted on is absolute cleanliness. The Government are determined
to export only sound meat. They do not
feel, as Senator Leslie has suggested, that
this will be a burden on the farmer. The
Senator also suggested that there would
be a waste in future if there had not
been a waste in the past. The Government feel that even if more inspectors
have to be appointed-and I am not certain of that-it would be in the interests
of the farmers to preserve their market
as Senator Hill Dickson eloquently
pointed out.
Mr. McHUGH: Do I understand the
hon. Gentleman to state that there will
be no inspection of meat for home consumption?
Mr. LONG: At the factories, but not
before killing.

Constabulary Bill.

CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s. 4 of 12
and 13 Geo. 5, c. 8, as to application of

Question put: "That the Clause stand

Mr. LONG: Some questions were
asked by Senator Leslie on the Second
Reading. The hon. and learned Member
referred in the course of the Second
Reading debate to the word "damages"
in this Clause. This word has a considerable tradition behind it; it is taken from
a very old Constabulary Act, namely, the
Act of 1836. The damages covered by
this Clause only refer to such sums as
may be awarded by any Justice to a
member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary
on a summary conviction; and, as any
such payments can only refer to damages
to property, namely, in the case of a
policeman, his uniform and equipment,
the replacement of which is an expense
which falls upon the State, it would
appear to be only proper that the
damages or compensation should be
appropriated in aid of the expenditure
on the Constabulary Force. This Clause.
does not affect the right of members of
the Force to recover personally damages
or compensation awarded by any competent court in a civil action.
Again, on this Clause, the hon. and
learned Member inquired as
to the
reason for the repeal of the words "or
directed by a court to be paid into the
said fund," which are contained in paragraph (b) of sub-section 5 of Section 4
of the Constabulary Act, 1922. The
Minister of Home Affairs is advised that,
as Justices have no power under existing
law to make an award except to the
prosecutor or aggrieved party, there is
no legal significance in the words which
have been omitted from the repealed
paragraph, which was based
the
provisions of the R.I.C. Code and Regulations governing the former Reward
Fund.
on
Constabulary Bill-
Adjournment-
4 NOVEMBER 1930
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the
hon. Senator for looking into the matter.
To take the second matter first, one can
only rejoice that the words referred to
and now proved to be ineffective will
finally be relegated to obscurity. What
I was afraid of from the older Act was
that the reference was to some payment
directed to be made by a superior court.
I could not, I admit, find out how it
worked.
As regards the other matter, the personal compensation to which I directed
my observations, for instance a police
constable who has been assaulted and has
had his head broken as well as his helmet,
if the court fines the assailant so much
and directs so much to be given as compensation by the assailant, that is
physical damage quite irrespective of the
damaged helmet which is the property
of the State. I think we may take it
the position will not be affected and the
constable will be quite safe, just as safe
as he would be if he had recovered his
compensation for a broken head in a
civil court.
Mr. LONG: That is correct.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.

Mr. JOSEPH CUNNINGHAM:
would like to ask the Deputy-Leader of
the House would it not be more convenient to meet to-morrow? There seems
to be a lot of business to be done yet,
and if it is left over to Thursday there
is a risk that it might not be completed.
It would be more convenient to meet tomorrow.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am sure the
House would be obliged to the Deputy-
Leader if he would inform us of the
nature of the business to be taken on
Thursday.
Mr. McHUGH: I take exception to
the Deputy-Leader of the House proposing to adjourn until Thursday. I came
here 100 miles this morning and I must
go back 100 miles to-morrow morning and
then come back another 100 miles the
next morning. According to the papers
before us we have any amount of work
to do to-morrow.
Mr. LONG: I also have to go a
lengthy distance, and it is not to my personal convenience to adjourn until
Thursday. The officials say we have no
work for to-morrow, and so we cannot
meet before Thursday. I regret Senator
McHugh is inconvenienced as I am personally, and also that other Members are

Business of the House

inconvenienced as well, but it is unavoidable under the circumstances. In reply

Mr. CAMPBELL. No.

Mr. CAMPBELL: Could the Deputy-
Leader of the House say when we will

Mr. LONG: 1 should be inclined to
consult the convenience of the House in

assured me there is no desire to rush

consulted. That is also my personal view
of the matter, and I would be glad to
have the views of hon. Members as to the
most convenient date for the Second
Reading. I suggest Wednesday.
Mr. LESLIE: I suggest next week.
We have only just seen the Bill in its
final form.
Mr. McHUGH: I suggest to-morrow
for the Second Reading of this Bill. We
would have ample time to-morrow to discuss it.
Mr. LONG: May I supplement what
I have said by saying that as far as I
see at present the House will be
obliged to sit three days next week,
Tuesday, Wednesday, and Thursday?
can
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty-five minutes after
Five o'clock until Thursday next.
