Appropriation Bill
6 NOVEMBER 1930
THE SENATE.

Thursday, 6th November, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
The Registrar-General's Eighth Annual
Report containing General Abstracts of
Marriages, Births and Deaths registered
in Northern Ireland during the year
1929.
Ministry of Labour:
Contributory Pensions (Joint Committee) Regulations (Northern Ireland),
1930 (No. 2).
Laid before the House (pursuant to
Act) and ordered to lie on the Table.
Questions to Minister.
LORD CARSON'S
STATUE AND
PARLIAMENT HOUSE.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether it is the intention of
the Government to set apart a site within
the precincts of the new Houses of Parliament for a statue of Lord Carson of
Duncairn, subscribed for by his friends,
and, if so, whether the Government will
not be departing from the long established
practice of Governments in Great Britain
regarding the erection within Parliamentary precincts of statues of public men
so long as they are participants in current
controversial politics.
Mr. LONG: The Government, in response to an application received from
the Committee in charge of the arrangements for the provision of a statue
of Lord Carson of Duncairn, granted
permission for the statute to
be
erected on a suitable site in the vicinity
of the new Parliament Buildings at Stormont. This particular application was
acceded to in view of the pre-eminent and
outstanding services rendered to Ulster by
Lord Carson.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Would it not have
been desirable to have asked for the assent
of Parliament in this matter?
Mr. LONG: I can only say that the
Government think that this is eminently
the proper action in the circumstances.
AGRICULTURE IN ELEMENTARY
SCHOOLS.
Mr. McALLISTER asked the Leader
of the House if he is aware that an agricultural school was recently opened in
Cambridgeshire under the Local Education
Authority; whether this school is of elementary standard with a very definite
agricultural bias; whether the objects were
warmly approved of by the President of
the Board of Education, and whether he
will consider the introduction of such
schools in Northern Ireland.
Mr. LONG: My right hon. Friend has
seen a newspaper account of the opening
of a college at Sawston, Cambridgeshire,
the function having been performed last
week by His Royal Highness the Prince
of Wales.
This college appears to be an interesting experiment by the Local Education
Authority for the provision of a rural
educational centre for a group of villages
in the county. It seems that it is to provide adult education for rural students
and that it also will contain a central
school for the older children of elementary
school age.
The Minister is rather of opinion that
the curriculum of the proposed central
school may not be quite on the lines which
he thinks the hon. Member has in mind;
he intends, however, to make inquiries
and to ascertain more exact particulars
which he will communicate to the hon.
Member as soon as he receives them.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: This is a Money Bill of
the usual kind of which it is only necessary for me to move formally the Second
Reading. The details are, I think, fully
given in the text and, as will be seen
from the Schedule, the money is required
for the Unemployment Fund. I beg to
move.
567 Tithe Rentcharge Bill —

THE SENATE
Second Reading,
Mr. LESLIE: I entirely concur with
the remarks of the hon. Member in his
assertion that this is a Money Bill which
it is not within the prerogative of this
House to amend. At the same time, I
think we ought to get, before we finally
part with the Bill, a little more information as regards the large sum of money to
which the Schedule relates. The sum
granted, as appears in the Schedule, is
£261,000. Hon. Members will remember
that the Budget of Northern Ireland for
the current financial year is balanced
with a surplus of £3,000, and that has
been done after making a provisional
allowance for the Imperial contribution.
I think we may fairly ask the Government to let us know at a later date-because it is possible the figures are not
available at the moment-what effect the
passing of this Bill will have on the Budget statement, and perhaps on the Imperial contribution. It is obvious, of
course, that the House could not refuse
the money, but we might be told a little
more about it.
Mr. LONG: As it is unlikely there
will be a Committee stage of this Bill I
shall take the opportunity on the Third
Reading of replying to the point raised
by the hon. and learned Member.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill be now committed."--(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion negatived.
Ordered, That the Bill be read the
Third time on Tuesday next.
Tithe Rentcharge and Variable Rents
Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: The main provisions of
this Measure are rendered necessary by
circumstances which I propose to explain
as briefly as possible.
In the year 1838 Parliament substituted
the system of tithe rentcharges for the
former compositions for tithes which were
largely payments in kind. The principle,
however, of fluctation according to the
seasons was retained; under the 1838 Act
a tithe rentcharge was regulated by Courts
of Quarter Sessions in accordance with
the rise or fall in the price of corn as
published in the Dublin Gazette.
In 1887 the Dublin Gazette altered the
method on which they published the
prices of corn, and until 1898 no exception was taken to the arrangement
whereby these published prices formed the
basis for the calculation both of tithe rentcharges and variable rents. In 1898,
however, it was contested in the Courts,
and the Court of Appeal eventually decided that, as the prices of corn in the
Dublin Gazette were not true average
prices, the Courts of Quarter Sessions had
no power to vary these tithe rentcharges.
To meet this difficulty, an Act was passed
in 1900 setting up a new canon of variation by reference to the average percentage of variation of fair rents in each county
ascertified by the Land Commission. These
calculations were to apply for terms of
fifteen years; the first period of fifteen
years thus established (in 1900) ended in
1915, and the second period of course
terminates in the present year.
The
A difficulty has now arisen, however,
in applying to the tithe rentcharges and
variable rents this principle, namely, that
of taking the average percentage of
variation of fair rents in each county.
The system of fixing fair rents by means
of a judicial tribunal was abolished by
the Northern Ireland Land Act of 1925,
and so some other principle of variation
for tithe rentcharges and variable rents.
must be found to take its place.
alternative method is proposed in Clause
1 of the present Bill. Sub-clause (1) deals
with cases where the rentcharge or
variable rent is payable to the Ministry
of Finance and prescribes a reduction of
eight per cent. as the proper variation,
as it is estimated that that percentage
would have been about the figure arrived
at on the former basis of computation.
Sub-clause (2) of Clause (1) deals with
cases of tithe rentcharges or variable rents
which are payable to persons other than
the Ministry of Finance. In the cases
of these private persons it is hardly appropriate that Parliament should enter
directly into a private affair between one
individual and another, and so the reduction of eight per cent. is only to apply
if both parties are in agreement. If
they do not so agree, then the sub-clause
prescribes that they must have recourse
to such certificate as the Ministry of
Finance as successors to the Land Commission-can issue under the system which
has been in force since 1900.
Clause 2 of the Bill deals with a class
of tithe rent charges which were trans-
569 Tithe Rentcharge Bill-

6 NOVEMBER 1930
Electricity Supply Bill
ferred at the time of the disestablishment
from the Irish Church to the Church Commissioners and subsequently to the Irish
Land Commission. Sub-clause (1) of
this second Clause gives the Ministry of
Finance power to dispose of such of
these tithes as they were hitherto
prevented from
from selling, and merge
them in the Church Temporalities Fund,
which is in the hands of the Exchequer. Sub-clause (2) deals with perpetuity rents and prescribes the basis of
calculation on which they can be sold.
The matters in this Bill are, as hon.
Members will have appreciated, somewhat
complicated and are entirely of a financial nature. The whole effect of the Bill
is of comparatively minor importance and
the purpose of it is to facilitate the Ministry of Finance in dealing with these
expiring classes of charges. I beg to
move.
Mr. LESLIE: There is very little in
the Bill which will, I think, awaken attention. As we have just been told, it is
of a very minor character, but there is
something that is not in the Bill, and as
to that I think that possibly Members of
this House, and certainly the public outside, would like some information. The
Bill deals with pretty much one section of
person, the Section 3 person of the Act
of 1900, but there is also the Section 1
person. That is the person who commuted his tithe rentcharge for a terminable payment. Originally the terminable
payment was to exist for fifty-two years,
but Section 1 of the Act of 1900 reduced
that term to forty-five years.
There are a
comparatively large number of such
annuities, if I may use the expression,
still running. The rate of interest obtainable was one thing, and the rate of
interest for some years past is quite
another. And there are people still paying this 45-year annuity, and they want
to know whether their term ought not now
to be reduced as the natural consequence
of the greater interest producing power
of money which has occurred from 1900
to the present time. That is certainly a
matter which awakens attention when the
Tithe Rentcharges Bill is before Parliament. It is a matter on which I should
like further information, but I shall not
delay the Second Reading by asking for
it at the moment.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill read a Second time accordingly and
committed to a Committee of the Whole
House for Tuesday.

11 NOVEMBER 1930
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 11th November, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Papers Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Home Affairs:
The Northern Ireland Winter Assize
Order, 1930.
Ministry of Labour:

1930.
Laid before the House (pursuant to

Larne Urban District Council Bill.

Resolved, "That this Bill do now pass.'
And a message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the Amendments
made by the Joint Committee without
Amendment.
Questions to Minister.
APPOINTMENT OF MAGISTRATES.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the total
number of persons appointed to the Commission of the Peace since the year 1921
in each county and county borough; and
how many of these persons in each county
and county borough were Catholics.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The total
number of persons appointed to the Commission of the Peace since 1921 in each
county and county borough is as
follows:
Belfast County Borough
Londonderry County Borough
Antrim


Armagh

Down

Fermanagh
Londonderry
Tyrone


As the religion of persons appointed to
the Commission of the Peace does not
constitute one of the qualifications of such
appointment, I regret I have no information in regard to the latter part of the
hon. and learned Member's question.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Can the noble Lord
state why there should be such economy
of information on this point?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is not
a question of economy of information, it
is a question of the difficulty of getting
the information. The Lieutenant or the
Vice-Lieutenant of the County receives
recommendations from various people in
the county and he eventually forwards
his recommendation to the Governor, who
appoints the magistrates. There is no
way in which the information as to the
religion of magistrates can be tabulated.
Possibly the Lord Lieutenant might have
the information at his disposal, but I
cannot say definitely; I can speak only
for myself. It would be impossible for
the Governor who appoints the magistrates to have the information, because
there is no source from which he could
get it unless it is sent in to him with the
recommendation, but in point of fact that
is not done.
Col. SHARMAN CRAWFORD: Is a
person's religion a necessary qualification
for the position of magistrate?
Mr. CAMPBELL: Do such considerations ever enter into the question at all
in Northern Ireland?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
say what the practice is in other counties,
but I can say for my own self, acting as
Vice-Lieutenant of Tyrone, that if I
have recommended to me a person who
in my opinion is suitable to be a magistrate, and the bench to which he is likely
to be appointed requires a magistrate and
is not over-crowded with magistrates, I
certainly give effect to the recommendation, whatever the person's religion may
be. I am speaking only for myself. I
cannot speak for others.
COMPOSITION OF R.U.C.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House whether he can state the numbers of district inspectors, head constables.
sergeants and constables respectively of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary, and how
many of each are Catholics.

Viscount CHARLEMONT: The total
numbers of district inspectors, head constables, sergeants, and constables respectively of the Royal Ulster Constabulary,
and the numbers of Catholics in these
ranks, are as follow:-
County inspectors 9-of whom 3 are
Catholics.
District inspectors 43-of whom 6 are
Catholics.
Head constables 71--of whom 28 are
Catholics.
Sergeants 468-of
Catholics.
whom 144 are
Constables 2,268--of whom 333 are
Catholics.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I am much obliged
for the very full information on this
subject.
R.U.C. AND CRUELTY TO
ANIMALS.
Major DOBBS asked the Leader of the
House what instruction, if any, the Royal
Ulster Constabulary have received with
regard to taking action in cases of cruelty
to horses and other animals brought to
their notice.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think it necessary to read the very lengthy
Section of the Royal Ulster Constabulary
Code containing the exhaustive instructions to the police in regard to the enforcement, etc., of the law relating to
Cruelty to Animals, but it will, perhaps,
suffice if I give the hon. and gallant
Member the assurance that the police have
received such instructions, and are thus |
fully aware of the action to be taken in
cases of cruelty, and of the necessity for
the exercise of vigilance in the enforcement of the law in this respect.
BILL PRESENTED.
Protection of Hares Bill (S).
"to suspend the operation of certain
enactments contained in the Protection
of Animals Act, 1911, as amended by
the Protection of Animals (Miscelleanous
Provisions) Act (Northern Ireland). 1926,
so far as those enactments apply to the
coursing or hunting of hares, was presented by Mr. Leslie, read a First time,
and ordered to be read a Second time on
Friday.

Bill." (Viscount Charlemont).

In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair.
CLAUSES 1 (Restriction on sending
meat to Great Britain, Irish Free State,
and Isle of Man), and 2 (Power of Ministry of Agriculture to grant licences for
sending of meat to Great Britain, Irish
Free State, and Isle of Man), ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Registration of Premises
for slaughtering and crating).
*
*
*
*
(b) The Ministry of Agriculture
may refuse to register any premises unless
satisfied that not less than a prescribed
number of animals will be dealt with
annually on such premises, and may
cancel the registration of any premises
where the number of animals dealt with
in any year has fallen below the prescribed number;
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
3, page 3, to leave out paragraph (b). I
desire to direct the attention of the Committee to paragraph (b) on page 3, dealing with the Ministry's powers to register
premises or to cancel such registration
having regard to the number of animals
dealt with on those premises. One inquires, in the first instance, what this
provision is for, and one feels that if
this power is given to the Ministry it
must be presumed it was meant to be
used. It seems to me that as the number that will be required is not stated
the power may be used to the detriment
of the very small person, the person
whose transactions are not very large.
Therefore such a man is entitled to the
protection of Parliament. There is, as a
matter of fact, some appeal given which
hou. Members will find in Clause 19 of
the Bill, but I am going to suggest that
the appeal in that case is of no practical
assistance, because the point to which the
Court would direct attention would, I
think, simply be, "Has this man fallen
577 Agricultural Produce Bill-
11 NOVEMBER 1930
Committee

That
below the specified number."
number the Ministry will have power to
prescribe.
I do not think that the excision of this
paragraph will do the Bill any harm, because it seems fairly obvious that a man
will not desire to be registered, or desire
that his registration should be continued
in force, unless he will make use of it.
A man should not be deprived of his
registration, and no one should have the
power to deprive him of it, on account
of some circumstances which may be
purely accidental. I beg to move.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT:
The
was
Amendment would mean that the Ministry would be obliged to register premises
where even one animal a year
slaughtered. In other words, to inspect
one pig, a veterinary inspector might
have to be sent down and the cost of
inspection might be equal to the value of
the animal. The Amendment could not,
therefore, be accepted unless the question.
of economy was completely disregarded.
It may, however, be stated that the prescribed number will not be high. In
fact, the policy of the Ministry will be to
do everything possible to encourage the
development of a fresh pork export trade,
particularly to the London market.
Mr. LESLIE: I should like to ask if
the right hon. Gentleman can state now
what the prescribed number will be. He
says it must not fall to one, but will not
be high, and he tells us also that it is
necessary to send down a veterinary inspector, and so on, but that does not
appear in the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It would
depend on the district in which the inspection would take place.
Mr. LESLIE: I am afraid that on
that I am unable to withdraw the Amendment.
Question put: "That paragraph (b) be
left out."
The Committee divided: Content, 4;
Not Content, 15.
CONTENT.
Barclay, Mr. William
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. R.
Charlemont, The Viscount
Clark, Sir G. S.
Crawford, Colonel The Right Honourable
R. G. Sharman
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
""
Leslie, Mr. James G.

NOT-CONTENT.
Cunningham, The Right Honourable
Samuel
Dickson, Mr. James H.
Dixon, Sir T. J.
Dobbs, Major A. F.
Glendinning, Mr. J. C.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
4, line 1, after "premises" ("as crating
premises; and") to insert "or used by a
licensed bacon-curer for purposes in connection with his business as such baconcurer. I spoke on this point on the
Second Reading and I said then that I
was not sure that I appreciated the intention of this provision of the Bill. I
am afraid I have to make the same confession again. I have tried very hard to
see what it is proposed to enact, and the
only conclusion I could come to is embodied in the Amendment on the Paper.
I approached the consideration of this
question by assuming, perhaps wrongly,
that the crating of meat means the
putting of meat in a crate. Then I had
to ask myself what, for this purpose, is
a crate. The ordinary idea of a crate
and one which we all know is a thing made
with six sides, something after the manner of a hurdle. It is used, perhaps, for
Herdman, Sir E. C.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Long, Mr. John A.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Porter, Mr. John Porter
sending a bicycle by train or other larger
package. But there is a special crate
used in the meat export trade, which is
more like a hanging larder, rather like
the structure that goes round on a cart
during pheasant shooting. This crate
goes backwards and forwards to England
and it also goes on lorries round the
country collecting dead meat.
Still being left uncertain as to what
exactly they are getting at, it seems to
me that they have definitely prohibited
here any form of crating for the export
of any kind of meat, either fresh meat or
cured meat, to which the provisions of this
Act apply except on registered premises
referred to in the first Sub-clause--and
these registered premises are to be used,
as the Committee will remember, for the
slaughter of animals by persons holding
a fresh meat licence or else crating in
premises registered for this purpose as
crating premises. If the expression
K
579 Agricultural Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
"fresh meat" had been used here, or if
in line 39 the expression had been "the
provisions of this section" instead of "the
provisions of this Act" there would have
been no difficulty. But as it is I am
driven to the conclusion that the effect
of the Clause as it stands would be to
prevent a licensed bacon-curer sending
his meat across the water in a crate if he
desired to do so. The ordinary method
of sending bacon across the water is in a
bundle or, perhaps, in a case or box. So
far as I know, it is not usual to use a
crate of any sort. But there is nothing
to prevent him using a crate containing
some half-dozen packages if he wants to
do so, and I do not think he ought to be
prevented. Accordingly, I am suggesting
the insertion of the words on the Paper.
I must say I do not suggest them with
any great confidence, for I am still unable
to appreciate exactly what the intention
is.
Viscount
CHARLEMONT: In the
trade "crating" is confined to fresh pork,
which to be exported as such must, under
the terms of the Bill, have been killed
on registered premises under veterinary
supervision. The Amendment proposed
would suggest that pork could be brought
from a country market to curers' premises and, instead of being turned into
bacon or hams, could be shipped as fresh
pork. That would be contrary to one of
the main objects of the Bill which is to
ensure that fresh meat for export shall
be dealt with on proper slaughtering
premises and exported direct from such
premises or from special premises that
may be used for crating.
There is, of course, no intention whatever of preventing a curer packing his
bacon and hams in any way he considers most desirable. If in an exceptional case he should even wish to crate,
the Bill provides for this.
Mr. LESLIE: I think with that explanation I may ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 4 (Restriction on carrying on
business of curing bacon and hams).
(1) A person shall not carry on the
business of curing bacon and hams
unless he holds a licence for that purpose under this section, and any person
who carries on or attempts to carry on
the said business in contravention of
the provisions of this section shall be
guilty of an offence under this Act.
(2) Subject to the provisions of this
Act, the Ministry of Agriculture shall
have power to grant, revoke and
suspend licences authorising the persons named therein to carry on the
business of curing bacon and hams.
(3) The Ministry of Agriculture
shall, on application being made in the
prescribed manner, and on payment of
the fee specified in this section, grant
to any person, upon such conditions as
may be prescribed, a licence for the
purpose aforesaid:
Provided that the said Ministry may
revoke or suspend a licence if satisfied
that the holder thereof has not complied with the prescribed conditions,
and may refuse to grant a licence to
an applicant who was formerly licensed
under this section but whose licence
has been revoked.
(4) The fee for the granting or renewal of a licence under this section
shall be ten pounds: provided that the
said fee shall be five pounds for a licence
limited to the case where the number
of carcases examined by an inspector
under this Act in the period of twelve
months to which the licence relates is
less than two hundred.
(5) The holder of a licence under this
section is in this Act referred to as a
licensed bacon-curer."
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
4, line 33, to leave out from "carcases'
("number of carcases examined") to the
end of line 35 ("than two hundred') and
insert "stated by the applicant for the
licence as expected to be dealt with in the
course of his business as a bacon-curer
during the period of twelve months after
the granting of the licence next ensuing
shall exceed fifty and shall not exceed two
hundred. And provided further that
where the number of carcases as aforesaid is less than fifty the fee for a licence
limited to fifty carcases shall be ten
shillings."
The difficulty in this Clause is again
that of the very small man. I seem to be
holding rather a brief for the small man,
but I cannot help that. It seems to me the
Ministry have consulted only the big
people, but apart from that we have be-
5.1 Agricultural Produce Bill-

11 NOVEMBER 1930
Committee
fore us that the fee for the licence is to
be £10, but if only 200 carcases are dealt
with in the year the fee may come down
to £5. It seems to me that we have not
yet come down far enough. I am still
anxious about the very small farmer in
the country or even the agricultural
labourer who may wish to cure two or
three or more pigs in the course of the
year and sell the cured product locally
to his friends and neighbours.
In default of the information I asked
for as to how many carcases had to be
cured in the year before a curer could be
said to carry on a business-in default of
that information, it seems to me that if a
man cures six pigs and sells them locally
he is caught and is required to take out
a licence at a cost of £5. I think that
is too much. I have suggested that if he
is not going to cure more than 50 piga
he ought to get his licence for 10s. Such
a man will not be a person in a very large
way of business, and I imagine his
premises can be inspected just as easily.
I have to ask the Committee to read
the expression "shall not exceed 200" as
"shall be less than 200" being the form
of phrase used elsewhere.
It
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I understood
that this Bill did not refer to anything
connected with killing for either fresh or
salted pork for consumption at home.
is only for export. There is nothing to
prevent a man killing and selling his pigs
as fresh pork or salted pork, or any other
kind, provided it is not going to be
shipped.
Mr. LESLIE: We are not at the
moment dealing with killing pigs at all;
we are dealing with curing.
Mr. McHUGH: I think this Bill has
created great confusion, because the
question we are dealing with now is the
question of curing. Now the practice, at
least, in my locality and for miles around
is for the grocer to purchase a certain
number of pigs, cure them at his own
place for the purpose of selling them over
the counter to his customers. According
to my reading of this Bill he would be
committing an illegal act. That is not a
proper provision. The licence demanded
here is the sum of £5. The Government
could then claim 10s per pig. That means
we are going to crush out this little incan
dustry which this man carries on. It
will also prevent the small farmer for ever
eating any Irish bacon. The moment that
this Act passes it will be impossible for the
poor man to buy Irish bacon. All these
shillings which the Government
claim are going to come out of the farmers' pockets. I hold that the Clause
introduced by Senator Leslie is one which
the Government should accept if they are
really serious in giving the poor farmers
an opportunity of pushing along in their
own way. This Clause has nothing to do
with the question of importing or exporting pigs. It is simply a question of
curing. I have pleasure in supporting
the Amendment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The small
man can kill as many pigs as he likes and
sell them locally. In order to facilitate
small curers, a very considerable concession was made in another place by which
establishments curing less than 200 carcases a year will be granted licences at
half-price, that is £5. The Bacon Curers'
Association, representing about 60
curers, have unanimously approved these
fees. The licence fees could not be further reduced without cutting across the
principle that the Measure is to be substantially self-supporting. I regret I
cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. McHUGH: In the case of 50 pigs
you are charging £5.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sorry to be so
obstructive, but the argument of the
noble Viscount seems to prove the case
I have made. He says that the small
farmer can sell as many pigs as he likes,
but he does not say that the small curer
can do that. He says he has consulted
the larger people who are anxious to put
the small curers out of their little businesses. I cannot see that the interests of
the small men who want to kill and cure
a small number are protected. I am
afraid I cannot withdraw the Amendment. The little man is entitled to more
consideration than he is getting.
Question put: "That these words be
left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 7;
Not Contents, 13.

The following Amendment stood on the
Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Page 4, after line 35, insert as a new
sub-clause--
"(5) Where a licence limited to a
number of carcases less than two hundred has been granted under this section, and the number of carcases dealt
with under such licence exceeds, or is
expected by the licensee to exceed, during the currency of such licence the
number of carcases limited by such
licence, the licensee shall be entitled to
obtain a licence for such further number of carcases as he may specify on
payment of an additional fee calculated
as may be prescribed."
Mr. LESLIE: Having regard to the
last Division the Amendment on the
Paper is a matter which I think can be
dealt with by the Ministry without
putting words in the Bill. If the noble
Viscount agrees, I need not trouble the
Committee in formally moving. The
point is as to what is to happen to the
man who wants to go beyond that. Perhaps I had better move the Amendment
without making a speech.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is
assumed that the hon. Member has in
mind a sliding scale for a licence fee
where the number of carcases dealt with
is 200 or more per annum.
That proposal would be open to the same objection
as the previous Amendment and would,
in addition, be difficult to work in practice. Even, however, if the principle
were accepted the Amendment on the
Paper would not, apparently, attain the
object in view, because the concluding
words of the Amendment leave it to the
Ministry to calculate as may be prescribed.
The Amendment cannot, therefore, be
accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: That is very much the
answer that I expected. The Amend-

Porter, Mr. John Porter
Herdman, Sir E. C.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Long, Mr. John A.

ment was drafted in the hope that the
earlier Amendment would have gone
through. As it is, the only point between us is as to the man who takes out
a licence for 200 pigs. He finds then he is
going to kill 201. What is going to happen? I presume he will have to get a
licence for 500, on payment of a further
£5. That is the point I want to raise,
and on that the noble Viscount would
probably be satisfied.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will give
the information at a later stage.
Mr. LESLIE: It is not necessary to
put anything in the Bill about that, and
I ask leave to withdraw.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSE 5 (Provisions as to consignment of meat under licence) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Fees for examination of
meat consigned under licence).
A person who holds a licence under
this Act shall, for the examination of
meat thereunder, pay to the Ministry
of Agriculture at such times as may be
prescribed fees at the following
rates:
For each carcase (including offals)
examined:
Beef-One shilling.
Pork-Threepence.
Mutton or lamb-Three halfpence.
Goat-Three halfpence.
Horse One shilling.
Mr. CAMPBELL: It may meet the
convenience of the Committee if I move
these five Amendments on the Order
Paper as one Amendment.
The whole
five being on the same subject matter,
they are simply a variation of the rates
of fees in respect of licences.
Agricultural Produce Bill--
Committee
11 NOVEMBER 1930
The CHAIRMAN: You can only move
one Amendment, but you can speak on
all the Amendments.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
page 5, line 26, leave out "one shilling"
and insert "sixpence." I think it would
be very desirable in connection with this
Bill that we should endeavour as far as
we can to mitigate any antagonism there
is among the poorer classes as to its provisions. It is a Bill that in some degree
will depend for success on the COoperation of the farming community, large
and small. There is undoubtedly-and
there was an expression of this in this
House-a good deal of irritation with regard to certain parts of the Bill. This
Amendment of sixpence instead of one
shilling may seem a small matter, but I
understand that in the case of small farmers in the North of Ireland who at the
present time are hard hit and find it
exceedingly difficult to make ends meet,
and to whom every sixpence in the week
counts, it is of very considerable importance that the fees to be charged in the
examination of meat should be as reasonable as possible. I understand that the
basis on which the shilling has been fixed
is that the system of inspection set up
by the Bill should pay its own way.
Would it not pay its way even with the
slight reduction I suggest? Even if it
Barclay, Mr. Wm.
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. R.
Charlemont, The Viscount
Clark, Sir G. S.
Crawford, Col. the Rt. Hon. R. G.
Sharman
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
I
does not, and if there is some expenditure
incurred by the State, would it not be
better for it to fall on broad public
shoulders than see it borne by individual
farmers? I recommend this matter to
the consideration of the Committee.
think we should give every encouragement
we can to the small, hard-hit farmers of
the North of Ireland, and I do ask that
we should vote on this matter in their
interests. It does not conflict with the
broad interests of the promoters of the
Bill.
While
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
having sympathy with the small farmer,
whose hard times we all deplore, I would
like to say that the inspection fees set out
in the Bill are the same as those already
in force in the Irish Free State and are
the lowest that could be charged without
completely undermining the financial
aspect of the Measure, as already explained. I regret I cannot accept the
Amendment.
The CHAIRMAN: Do you wish to
press the Amendment ?
Mr. CAMPBELL: I do.
Question put: "That this word be left
out."

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
5, after line 30 (end of Clause) to insert:
"Provided that no fee shall be payable
in respect of the examination of any carcase under this Act other than the fee
payable for one examination of such carcase under the provisions of this section."
I should like to believe, and indeed I
do believe, that the Amendment on the
Paper carries out the intention of the
text, but I am afraid it is not left quite
as definite as I think it ought to be for
the protection of these unfortunate people
who have to pay these sums of money,
whether they be shillings or sixpences.
As the text stands it would seem that
one of these carcases is
every time
587 Agricultural Produce Bill--

THE SENATE
Committee
[Mr. Leslie]
examined a fee has got to be paid. The
intention, I believe, is that once a carcase
has been examined and a fee paid in
respect of that carcase that carcase should
be free from further fees, but there may
be more than one examination of the same
carcase and we ought to make it quite
definite that only one fee shall be payable.
Accordingly, I put the Amendment on the
Paper in, I think, the clearest language
I could adopt, but I might have expressed
myself more briefly or neatly. I say:
"Provided that no fee shall be payable in respect of the examination of
any carcase under this Act other than
the fee payable for one examination of
such carcase under the provisions of
this section."
It will be observed that the examination
we are dealing with is an examination
under the Act, that is, any one of the four
or five examinations which may take place,
and for these, however many there may
be, my contention is that only one fee
should be paid, and that is the fee or fees
which the Committee have just decided.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I cannot
accept this Amendment, but I should
like to give the hon. and learned Member a definite assurance that there is no
intention of charging more than once
for the examination of the same carcase.
Mr. LESLIE: With that explanation.
I beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 7 (Permits for consignments
of meat in exceptional cases) ordered to
stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 8 (General provisions as to
licences and permits).
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, on page
6, after line 16 (end of Clause), to insert
as a new Sub-clause :
"(5) Where any person deems himself aggrieved by the revocation or suspension of a licence or permit granted
under this Act, such person may appeal
to the County Court, and the provisions
of section nineteen of this Act shall
apply to such appeal."
The object of this Amendment is selfapparent. It is to give a right of appeal
against a revocation or suspension of a
licence or permit, and it seems to me it
is a necessary and proper extension of
powers of appeal such as are already given.
in other circumstances in Clause 19. It
will be observed that the condition
under which a licence or permit may be
revoked or suspended by the Ministry of
Agriculture is:
I
"by reason of the non-compliance of
the holder of the licence or permit with
any condition applying thereto, or for
any other reason which the said Ministry may deem to be sufficient."
That provision is extremely strong.
think it is too strong a power to place
in the hands of the Ministry unless the
right of an appeal to a court of law is
given. A good many considerations may
enter into this, and the person who has
the licence or the permit ought, I think,
to have an appeal here as under the other
circumstances in Clause 19.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In practice, no licence or permit would be revoked
unless the holder committed a most
serious breach of the Act, such as endeavouring to send out unsound meat and
uncertified meat. The Ministry has
similar powers of revocation or suspension
under the Marketing of Eggs and Marketing of Potatoes Acts. There have
been no revocations or suspensions under
the Marketing of Potatoes Act, and very
few under the Marketing of Eggs Act.
In no single case has there been any
serious complaint that the Ministry has
used its powers unreasonably. Both in
this country and elsewhere it is a well understood principle that the granting, revocation or suspension of licences or permits of this kind are administrative Acts
which can be reviewed in Parliament if
anything unreasonable is done and are
not appropriate matters for reference to
a County Court.
Br. LESLIE: I dislike to be putting
the Committee to the trouble of perpetual Divisions-
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Hear, hear.
Mr. LESLIE- -but at the same time
I am afraid that one cannot accept the
argument that because wrong has been
done twice already, in the Marketing of
Potatoes Act and the Marketing of Eggs
Act, that is an excuse for doing the same
thing the third time.
Agricultural Produce Bill-
Committee.
11 NOVEMBER 1930
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is not
wrong.
Mr. McHUGH: I think it is.
Mr. LESLIE: This is a warning to
Parliament to be extremely chary in
granting powers, and I do not think I
ought to withdraw the Amendment.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted."

Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 9 (Disposal of meat unfit for
human consumption) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 10 (Deduction from price of
carcase to cover expenses and risk of condemnation under the Act).
Where the carcase of a pig is bought
by or on behalf of a licensed baconcurer, the buyer may deduct from the
price of the carcase such sum, not exceeding one shilling, as may from time
to time be prescribed so as to defray
the expenses of the buyer and to insure
the buyer against loss in the event of
the carcase being subsequently certified
by an inspector, acting in the exercise
of his powers under this Act, to be unfit for human consumption; and if such
deduction is made the seller of the carcase shall not be liable in respect of
any loss arising in the aforesaid event.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
7, lines 14 and 15, to leave out "one
shilling" ("not exceeding one shilling')
and insert "sixpence."
I am rather in a difficulty with the first
of the Amendments standing in my name
in respect to this Clause, and indeed with
the others also, because, as will be seen
on looking further down the Paper, the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell)
has an Amendment to leave out the entire Clause. When speaking on the
Second Reading of the Bill I said it
seemed to me that the only way to deal
with this Clause was to leave it out, but,
I will take these three Amendments in
their order as they come, although I am
only moving the first one now, that is to
leave out "one shilling" and insert "sixpence," I think a shilling is too high to
insert here.
Glendinning, Mr. J. C.
Herdman, Sir E. C.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
They
This is an insurance fund. A separate
fund apparently is not going to be set up
anywhere, but it seems to me the buyers
are going to take these shillings and put
them in their pockets, and so establish
an unrecognised insurance fund.
may carry them to a separate account in
the bank and have a separate insurance
fund in the same way as some people conduct their fire insurance. As far as the
seller is concerned this shilling is an insurance, but it is apparently not entirely
insurance because it is partly encroached
upon, as we see in the next line, in order
to recoup the buyer his expenses. I am,
however, attacking that separately. At
all events, the buyer is to be recouped
his expenses and insured against loss. If
all that is to come out of the shilling it
does not seem to me the shilling is too
much.
I cannot but remember that under the
Slaughter of Animals (Compensation)
Act the rate charged for insuring a pig
against foot and mouth disease is only
1d. I think, subject to what others may
have to say, that one shilling is too high.
I confess I am very doubtful whether
an all-round reduction from 1s to 6d is
a good thing in itself since the man who
supplies the good bacon is paying for the
default of the man who supplies the bad
bacon. Meantime, I move to reduce the
shilling to sixpence.
Major DOBBS: For some years the
sum of 4d at least has been deducted from
the price of the carcase of every pig sold
in Northern Ireland-
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Sixpence.
Major DOBBS: Fourpence at the
least. That has been for porterage. T
take it that has to come out of ths
shilling standing in this Clause?
Agricultural Produce Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Viscount CHARLEMONT: That is
true.
Major DOBBS: In Clause 6, which has
come before us to-day, 3d has also to
come out of this shilling for the inspection
of the carcase. That makes, if we take
4d for porterage and 3d for inspection,
7d, or if we take 6d for porterage and 3d
for inspection, 9d. When those figures of
7d or 9d come out of the shilling it leaves
5d in one case and in the other 3d to act
as insurance. This is an insurance to the
curer for possible condemnation of the
whole carcase or part of it. Supposing a
pig's head, which probably cost 7s 6d, is
condemned for tuberculosis in the brain,
the curer has only 3d or 5d to stand
against that loss. If the whole pig is
condemned he has 3d or 5d against the
£9 which he would probably get for the
pig. It does not seem to me that the
farmer is being unfairly dealt with.
an
the
There is an analogous case at the pesent
time in the case of fat cattle. If a farmer
sells a fat bullock or a fat bull there is
deducted from his price the sum of 2s.
If he sells a fat cow 4s is deducted from
his price. The butcher is understood to
put a like sum to the sum that is deducted
from the farmer's price, and this acts as
insurance fund. Sometimes
butchers actually do carry out an insurance with this money, and sometimes they
look upon
it from the point of view that
they are getting 2s, or 4s, as the case may
be, so often during the year that they are
compensated for any possible loss, and that
it is worth their while not to insure. At
any rate, that is the method of compensating them should a beef carcase be condemned. I should also like to point out
that this deduction of 1s was unanimously
agreed to by the Farmers' Union and by
the curers, and I believe it was also agreed
to by the Department's own committee
that had this matter under consideration.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I understand
that after this charge has been in operation for twelve months if the Ministry
find that the money has accumulated the
charge will then be reduced.
Mr. McHUGH: One thing that is quite
clear about this is that there is no Clause
in the whole Bill to prevent the buyer
from charging a larger sum. At the
present time, as the hon. and gallant
Senator (Major Dobbs) has told us, 6d is
charged for the carriage of all carcases of
pork. That will be turned into 1s the
moment this Bill comes into force, but
there is nothing in the Bill to prevent the
buyer from imposing an even heavier
penalty, say 1s 6d, comprised of the 6d
which he charges now and the 1s which
he will under this Bill deduct from the
seller of the pork as insurance. The
unfortunate farmer will be fleeced, because
there is no protection whatever for him
in this Bill. The only thing in the Bill
is that he is called upon to pay out of
his own pocket for alleged improvements
which will take place in the price of pork.
There will be any amount of officials
appointed to look after him, and he will
be called upon to pay for them. If this
6d which they impose now is added to
the 1s for insurance then the unfortunate
farmer is going to pay 1s 6d for each
dead pig of which he disposes in market.
The matter is not quite clear yet, and
there should be some guarantee given that
the farmer will not be fleeced by the
buyer.
Mr. HILL DICKSON:
Anything beyond the amount mentioned in the Bill
would surely be an illegal charge. As a
matter of fact, the 6d that is charged for
porterage at the present time is not a legal
charge at all.
Mr. McHUGH: It is the custom.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: But it is not a
legal charge.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that the
reference to porterage has brought up the
whole question of tolls and marketing,
and takes us far from the question of this
1/-, which is the real question before us
at the moment. The discussion just now
would seem to me to be more appropriate
to the next Amendment on the Paper. I
should think that, unless the noble Viscount approves of the alteration from 1/-
to 6d, it would save time to withdraw the
Amendment and to go on to the other
one. I do not know what the noble
Viscount thinks on the matter.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: When you
are dealing with a great number of
Amendments like this I think it would be
easier really to take each Amendment as
it comes. Then if a person is moving a
consequential Amendment, or, as the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) is
proposing to do in this case, to leave out
Agricultural Produce Bill-
Committee.
11 NOVEMBER 1930
I
the whole Clause, obviously I would be
guided by whether the earlier Amendments have been adopted or rejected.
think it would be less confusing to take
these matters as they arise. That is my
private opinion.
With regard to this particular sum, I
would like to say that at the present
moment if there were no Clause in the Bill
the buyer could certainly increase the deduction, as Senator McHugh has said.
But as the Bill stands it prescribes the
charge as such sum not exceeding 1/- as
from time to time may be prescribed. This
will give the Ministry power to review the
charge periodically. If actual statistics
show that 1/- is too high, having regard
to the number of carcases condemned, the
Ministry can reduce the charge.
In any
case the position will be reviewed at regular intervals so as to ensure that the
farmer is getting value for his money in
this connection. As the hon. Senator
(Mr. Hill Dickson) has said, the present
charge of 6d is a matter of custom and not
of law, but with the increase of that 6d
to a sum not exceeding 1/- the farmer is
really getting very good value for his
money.
Mr. McHUGH: How?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Because he
is insured against possible loss, and I do
not think under these circumstances the
charge is too high. Of course it is true
here, as it is in all matters of insurance,
that the people who do not claim anything
are paying for those who do. That is a
misfortune common to all people who pay
fire insurance premiums. As one who
pays myself I wish it were not so, but it
apparently is the economic law.
sorry I cannot accept the Amendment.
I am
Mr. LESLIE: I wish to withdraw the
Amendment proposing to reduce 1/- to 6d.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
10, page 7, line 16, to leave out "defray
the expenses of the buyer and to" ("so
as to defray the expenses of the buyer and
to insure the buyer.")
I
This Amendment raises the question of
defraying the expenses of the buyer.
want to know what those expenses are
supposed to be and what the seller of the
carcase has to do with them. It is obvious
that any expenses the buyer, or the whole
body of buyers, can establish will have to be
considered by the Ministry when the question arises as to whether they are to prescribe 1/- or a lesser sum, and if so, what
lesser sum, at the end of the year. The
expenses of the buyer would seem to me
to include possibly the expenses of his attendance at the market, probably his
raiway fare, and SO on. The whole
expression seems to me to be extremely
vague, although it may be, as we have
heard, that it was discussed at meetings
of the Farmers' Union and of the pork
curers and various other people, representing presumably themselves, but as to
whose claims to speak for the whole body
of farmers of Northern Ireland we have
no information.
But supposing they did so, I still think
they ought to have inquired what these
expenses are. Do they include porterage
for which, rightly or wrongly, 4d or 6d is
undoubtedly being charged and paid
to-day, and are they supposed to include.
the 3d referred to in Clause 6, which
engaged our attention a few minutes ago?
If that is so, I admit, as my hon. and
gallant Friend (Major Dobbs) says, that
there is not much left out of 1/-, and the
expenses of the buyer have to be defrayed
out of what is left of the shilling.
If
the noble Viscount can tell us that this
porterage, perhaps the market tolls, if
any, and all other charges are to be regarded as expenses of the buyer-though-
I am not sure that I can suggest that the
toll is part of the expenses of the buyer;
I am rather inclined to think it is part of
the expenses of the seller-
It is all
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
included in the shilling.
Mr. LESLIE: Who is going to pay
that porterage?
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Nobody.
Mr. LESLIE: That is the point I am
on. What is included in this shilling?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Porterage
and insurance.
Mr. LESLIE: I think I need not do
more than move the amendment, now that
the position is clear.
I should
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
like to say that, so far as I know, the 1/-
includes everything, that is to say it includes porterage and insurance.

Mr. LESLIE: That clears that up,
the amendment may be withdrawn.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
and
in
Mr. LESLIE : I beg to move,
Clause 10, page 7, line 19, at end ("to be
unfit for human consumption;') to add
"or condemned or ordered to be destroyed
as unfit for human consumption under any
enactment contained in the Public Health
(Ireland) Acts, 1878 to 1918, or in any
local Act, or in this Act."
It
The words on the Paper are designed
to give the man who has paid 1/-, or such
sum as may be prescribed, for insurance
protection against attack by the person
to whom he sold the pig in respect of any
loss occasioned by the subsequent condemnation of the flesh of that pig.
seems to me that it is not quite clear that
the expression "inspector" in line 18 is
sufficiently comprehensive to cover the case
where the condemnation is not made by
an inspector of the Ministry, but is made
under the powers of the Public Health
Acts, or of a local Act, or by another
inspector who may come along even while
the meat is in transit. He is usually a
policeman in that case, who takes it before
the magistrate, and so on. At all events
I refer to anybody other than the
inspector who sees the pork either on the
registered premises or in the curing
factory, as the case may be. It appears
to be clear that if a man pays 1/-, that is
to say, if the seller claims 1/-, if he suffers
the reduction of 1/- from his price in order
that he may get insurance, that man
ought to be fully indemnified against any
action that may be taken. It is possible
sometimes, I suppose, for the purchaser
of a defective pig to identify the man
who sold it to him. At all events the
seller, unless there has been an admitted
fraud, or a fraud which can be proved,
should have for his shilling complete insurance. But whether the intention is
that he should have that complete insurance and that the insurance should cover
every form of inspection is a matter of
doubt. If it does, of course the amendment will not be required, but in the
meantime I beg to move the addition of
the words on the Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Carcases to
which the Clause applies will be inspected.
at bacon factories by inspectors appointed
or recognised by the Ministry, who will
be responsible for passing or condemning
the carcases. The Amendment is, therefore, unnecessary.
Mr. LESLIE: In fact, the noble Viscount states that the possibility which I
have suggested will not happen, and that
the man is fully covered. That is all I
want to ensure.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move that
Clause 10 be left out of the Bill. This
Clause is very broad and general in its
terms. It is not very easy for the nonagriculturist to follow it. What strikes
me is this, that a buyer may deduct from
the price of the carcase of the pig any sum
up to one shilling to defray his expenses
and to insure himself against loss in the
event of the carcase being subsequently
certified unfit for human consumption.
That may be perfectly right. The buyer
case like that may be perfectly
innocent, and it is only right that he
should be protected against loss. But
then the Clause goes on to state that in
the event of the buyer making such deduction and the seller submitting to the
deduction, for the small sum of threepence,
fivepence or sixpence, the seller in his
turn is preserved free from all loss arising
out of the transaction.
in a
If the seller is an innocent man and
has merely made a mistake that could not
be detected or foreseen in any way it
would be right that he should be relieved
from any loss of that kind. But suppose,
and I think the case is not improbable,
and may happen from time to time, the
seller is not quite an innocent man and
sends in a carcase which is diseased, would
he not be entitled to the protection of this
Clause also, it is so wide and general in
its terms? The moment the buyer makes
the deduction and the seller submits to it,
the buyer no longer looks to the seller for
any compensation for loss. He gets recouped from the insurance fund, and is
not concerned to go beyond it. The seller
may perpetrate a fraud upon an innocent
buyer by selling a carcase which is
diseased, and, in such circumstances, I
think the seller should not have the means
of coming in under the shelter of this
Clause. I am perfectly certain that no
one in this House would agree to give the
protection of the law in a case of this kind.
Mr. LESLIE: It seems to me that the
hon. and learned Senator has destroyed
this Clause on the ground to which I
alluded earlier in the discussion. If this
deduction is going into the man's pocket
597 Agricultural Produce Bill-

11 NOVEMBER 1930
Committee
he takes his chance, but if it is going to
establish a fund under the control of
somebody else, then I think the buyer has a
distinct incentive to lend himself to fraud.
I think it would have been very much better
if, as I said in the Second Reading debate,
separate Bills had been introduced to deal
with matters dealt with in this Measure.
I am inclined to think this Bill would be
better if this Clause were not in it.
It is rather regrettable that this is to
some extent an agreed Clause, except that
the point is made that the Clause is only
necessary because some people will sell bad
pigs. But there is no reason why the
people who sell good pigs should pay for
the bad pigs. It is not quite comparable
to the question of fire insurance. If you
suffer from the effects of a fire and claim
on an insurance policy, you have met with
a misfortune. In this case you are simply
paying for somebody else's default.
think I must support the hon. and learned
Counsel in his Motion for the deletion of
the Clause. I confess I am not very
happy about it.
I
Major DOBBS: I cannot see that a
buyer is going to buy a pig if he has any
doubts at all that there may be something
wrong with it, because he is not going to
lose the £9 or so that he has paid for that
pig It may be that he has bought a
whole lot of pigs. He has deducted sixpence or whatever it is for those pigs, and
at the close of the day it may come to £2
or £3. I do not see why he should buy
a pig if by any chance at all it is unsound.
He will pass it by, and let somebody else
buy it.
Mr. DORMAN: This Clause presents
itself to me as being calculated to put a
premium on fraud. It apparently leaves
it open to any designing individual to go
through the country and buy pigs, especially if he has any information that there
may be diseased pigs available. He may
bring those pigs to a market or fair, and
offer them for sale. He is not compelled
to have an examination of any pigs he
brings to the market; he is not able to
give any guarantee that they are free
from disease. It appears to me that the
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. Robert
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Clark, Sir George S.
Crawford, Col. the Right Hon. R. G.
Sharman
Clause ought to be left out of the Bill as
it is calculated to promote and assist
fraudulent individuals in escaping from
the common law consequences of their
violation of the law.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I submit that
this Bill does not do away with the public
health authority. They are there, and if
a man comes in with an unfit animal he
is prosecuted before it ever reaches the
buyer. This Clause applies only when
the pig is bought under circumstances in
which they both think it sound, and after
examination it has been proved to be unsound; but if a fraudulent person such
as has been spoken of comes into the market with bad pigs he can be prosecuted
under the Public Health Acts for submitting it for sale at all.
Of course
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
there are fraudulent sellers of pigs, but I
cannot think there are so many as would
be supposed from some of the criticisms
of this Bill. Buyers are not compelled
to buy pigs, and it may be that a buyer
who knows his business would know something of the man from whom he bought
it, and whether the pig was unsound or
not. He is not bound to buy a pig in a
poke. If a man by mistake once accepted
a pig that was unfit for human consumption, he would not accept one again.
think buyers can be regarded as fit to protect their own interests and not to lose by
a transaction, which is in the ordinary
course of buying and selling. I do not
think the omission of this Clause would be
at all advantageous, nor do I think as it
stands it puts any premium on fraud.
Of course there is not an Act of Parliament in the world which a fraudulent per
son cannot get round if he gets the time
and opportunity to do it, and is not afraid
of being put into prison if he fails.
the same time I do not think the premium
on fraud in this case is so great as to
tempt people to take advantage in any
way.
At
Question put, "That this Clause be left
out.'

599 Agricultural Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Liability of carriers)——
Any carrier who accepts or carries
any meat which is being, or intended
to be, sent out of Northern Ireland to
Great Britain, the Irish Free State or
the Isle of Man, and which is not accompanied by the prescribed certificate or
prescribed indication as to the sender's
identity, or by a permit relating to such
meat, shall be guilty of an offence under
this Act.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
11, page 7, line 23, after the word "who'
("any carrier who accepts') to insert the
word "knowingly."
This is a somewhat small point. The
Clause, as regards the carrier, is undoubtedly likely to be of use, but I think
it ought to provide that the carrier shall
not be liable to the penalty under this
Clause unless he really knows that the
meat which he carries is in contravention
of the provisions of this Bill. It is perfectly easy, I think, for anyone who wants
to contravene the Bill by sending meat
without the prescribed certificate or prescribed indication to conceal his meat in a
package. The introduction of this word
"knowingly" is a very old question. It
raises what my hon. and learned Friend
(Mr. Campbell) will know as mens rea.
Is this carrier to be punished unless he
really knows he is doing wrong? I think
the introduction of this one word would
possibly save injustice, and it would be,
perhaps, some slight indication that the
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dorman, Mr. R.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Clark, Sir G. S.
Crawford, Col. the Right Hon. R. G.
Sharman
CONTENT.
Ministry of Agriculture does not intend
always to maintain a wholly unconciliatory attitude in the discussion of this
Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sure
that the last thing the Minister of Agriculture would intend would be not to be
conciliatory, but at the same time the
Clause as it stands has been agreed to in
consultation with the representatives of
the carrying companies. It will be observed
that Clause 17, Section 5, provides that
no prosecution could be instituted for a
breach of this Clause without the consent
of the Ministry. Such sanction would
clearly not be given except where the
curer acted knowingly. In the circumstances I think the Amendment is
unnecessary.
Mr. LESLIE: By way of protest, such
a protest as one can make against the
dual line of argument, I shall not withdraw the Amendment. The noble Viscount said the Clause had been arrived
at after consulting the larger carriers.
He has left out, as usual, throughout this
Bill, the consideration of the small man.
He has also pointed out that a prosecution
cannot be instituted except with the consent of the Ministry. That is making the
Ministry judge in its own court. Purely
as a matter of protest I am afraid I must
put the Committee to the trouble of
dividing.
Question put, "That this word be inserted."

grading and packing of meat).
The Ministry of Agriculture may
make rules under this Act in regard to:
(a) the conditions to be observed in
connection with the carriage of
meat to which this Act applies;
and

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
12, page 7, line 35, in paragraph (b), to
Agricultural Produce Bill-
Committee
11 NOVEMBER 1930
The
leave out the words "grading and." This
Amendment relates to the really important matter of the grading of meat.
grading of meat, apparently whether for
export or for home consumption, is undoubtedly dealt with in the title of the
Bill, but on the Second Reading of this
Measure nothing was said in regard to
grading. Consequently, I think hon.
Members, including myself, have not the
remotest idea to what extent it is contemplated to carry out grading, or what
object it is expected to effect, although
we may have a very good idea what the
But I think we are entitled to
object is.
considerably more information than we
have at the moment. We want to know,
amongst other things, whether the time
has come for the grading of meat, or
whether a little time of waiting should be
allowed to elapse, as was done in the case
of the Marketing of Eggs and other Acts
of that nature, during which the new
powers were given a sort of trial run before they went too far; what grading is
there going to be; how it is going to be
announced to the people; and what standard of quality is going to be set as regards
meat.
When I speak of the quality of meat I am
not thinking of rotten as opposed to good
meat, but of the various other questions
the housewife asks the local butcher, the
coarseness of the meat, and so on. How
far is it proposed to go in that respect,
and what is to happen to meat which
does not come up to the grading standard?
On those matters we ought to have a
great deal more information, and perhaps
on this point more than on any other one
is driven to the conclusion that it would
have been much better if this Bill had
been referred to a Joint Committee of
both Houses in order that minor details
could have been discussed in a less formal manner. For the moment, however,
it will suit my purpose if I move that
these two words be omitted.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The grading of meat is already in force to a considerable extent in Great Britain and the
practice will be further developed there.
While there is no immediate intention of
putting in force in Northern Ireland a
system of grading meat for export, it is,
nevertheless, considered desirable, in view
of possible developments in this direction
across Channel, that the Ministry would
have powers to make regulations regarding the grading of meat. I have not got
very much information at the present
moment with regard to the actual rule
proposed, but if the hon. and learned
Senator will withdraw his Amendment in
the meantime I might promise to give
the information at a later stage of the
Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I think I should be falling in with the wishes of both the Senate
and the public if I acceded to that request. The Ministry is asking me for a
blank cheque, but as they are not going
to cash it at the moment it does not
matter.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
12, page 7, line 35, at the end of paragraph (b) to add "and (c) the marking
of packages containing such meat." This
is a small matter, scarcely, perhaps,
worth mentioning, but it seems to me that
the words I have on the Paper are necessary in order to make the following
proviso reasonably intelligible. There is
nothing about the marking of packages in
the earlier Clauses of the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Ministry is legally advised that the Clause as
it stands enables the Ministry to make
rules regarding the marking of packages.
of meat to which the Act applies.
these circumstances the addition suggested
is not considered necessary.

Mr. LESLIE: I do not think this is a

CLAUSE 13 (Power to enter premises.
and examine consignments, etc.).
Any authorised officer shall for the
purpose of this Act have power—
(a) to enter, at all reasonable times,
any premises registered under this
Act, or in respect of which an application for registration under
this Act has been made, or premises owned, occupied or used, or
suspected by such officer of being
owned, occupied or used by any
person for the slaughter of animals,
the preparation of meat for sale,
the curing of meat or the crating
of meat;

found on such premises and to

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
13, page 8, line 7, after the word
"animals" (slaughter of animals) to
insert in connection with the sending of
fresh meat from Northern Ireland to
Great Britain, the Irish Free State, or
the Isle of Man." It seems to me that
the words I have on the Paper are required in order to carry out the intention
of the Bill. However, I am not very
clear upon that because of the reference
to "purposes of this Act" at the bottom
of page 7. If I understand it correctly,
the intention is to confine these inquisitorial visits of the various minions of the
Ministry to the class of premises specially
described in the Bill: that is the registered
premises and trading and curing premises. It is not, I presume, intended to
invade the private slaughter-house or the
ordinary precincts of the farm under the
powers given here, but as I am not sure,
and as I am always anxious to protect
people from any form of oppression or
annoyance, I think it would, perhaps,
be well if those words were put in, if
they are what is intended by the Bill.
Accordingly, I beg to move.
In order
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
that the Ministry may see that the provisions of the Act are observed, it is
necessary that authorised officers should
have power to enter any premises used or
suspected of being used for the slaughter
of animals. The limitation proposed in
the Amendment would hamper unnecessarily the Ministry in endeavouring
to discharge the duties imposed upon it.
I think the hon. and learned Senator will
see that slaughter houses might be exporting one month and not the next and
SO on. I regret I cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: That is the whole point.
They must be suspected of slaughtering
with a view to sending fresh meat to
Great Britain. Unless there is a more
reasonable explanation I cannot withdraw.
Question put, "That those words be
there inserted.'

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
8, line 11, to leave out
"without payment." I think this Amendment is much
more serious than the last. The proposal
here is that the inspector shall have
power to take, without payment, samples
of meat. Now, that principle of taking
without payment seems to me to be
entirely new. If hon. Members will
refer to the Marketing of Potatoes Act
about which we heard so much, they will
not find any power to take samples even
of potatoes without payment. If they
will refer to the Food and Drugs Act,
they will not find that inspectors are to
take samples without payment. They
will find that inspectors are to procure
samples, and they know that if an inspector goes into a shop to take a sample
Glendinning, Mr. J. C.
Lavery, Mr. T. R.
Long, Mr. John A.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
of, say, tea for the purpose of analysis
he has to pay for it.
the
I do not say, it will be observed, that
the inspector is to pay for it, but I do say
they ought not to take it without payment. To allow an inspector to go into
a place and take meat without payment
seems to me to have very serious objections indeed. It gives the man
opportunity of being positively dishonest.
As a matter of fact, I might go further
and remind hon. Senators that the Government of Ireland Act says that you
shall not take property without payment.
Nobody would imagine that would be
stretched to say that you are not to take
a potato, but the fact remains that in
dealing with samples of meat the aggrega-

tion of these samples during the year
would amount really to property which is

Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think it is in common sense to expect the
inspector who is taking meat for analysis.
to pay for the small portion he is likely

Question proposed: "That Clause 13

Mr. LESLIE: Before finally parting
with Clause 13 I would like to direct the
attention of the Committee to Sub-Clause
(6) dealing with the case-I hope the unlikely case, and I should like to say almost
impossible case-of the authorised officer
disclosing information obtained by him,
and the fine of £50, if he does so. I
think it is a fairly common Sub-Clause,
but what I want to draw attention to is
in connection with Sub-Clause (5) of
Clause 17, on page 13, that is, that a
prosecution for an offence under this Act
shall not be instituted except with the
consent of the Ministry. That is if it
should happen that an officer of the
Ministry be false to his trust that man
is not to be prosecuted except by the consent of the Ministry who employs him.
I did not put an Amendment on the
Paper, but I hope somebody else will do
so, possibly even the Government. I will
direct further attention to the matter at
a later stage. Meanwhile, it is not
worth while to oppose the Clause.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in page
10, after line 31 ("the said Ministry may
think just') insert as a new sub-clause-
"(6) Where any person deems himself aggrieved by reason of the refusal
of the Ministry of Agriculture to pay
compensation or sufficient compensation
under the provisions of sub-section five
of this section, such person may appeal
to the county court, and the provisions
of section nineteen of this Act shall
apply to such appeal."
This Clause gave a certain amount of
trouble in another place, and as a result
of some of that trouble Sub-Clause 5, or
part of it, was added to the Clause there.
I am afraid that the provisions of Sub-
Clause 5 are insufficient to support their
purpose, which presumably is that compensation be paid to any person unjustly
treated in respect of the detention or destruction of his property, but before anybody can get anything in the way of compensation they have got to satisfy the
Ministry of Agriculture, who like to be
Agricultural Produce Bill-
Committee
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
the judge in their own case. Apparently
the Ministry is to decide the compensation, which is to be of such amount "as
the said Ministry may think just."
I do not think that Clause is of very
much use as it stands. I think there are
words in it, as was pointed out elsewhere,
that destroy its utility. I think also it is
incomplete. If a person is aggrieved by
reason of the refusal of the Ministry to
pay compensation or by reason of the
Ministry not paying sufficient compensation, I think there should be an appeal
open to a court of law. This appeal is
perhaps rather different from the other to
which I have already directed the attention of the Committee. It seems to me
a very clear case, indeed. Are we going
to set the Ministry above the law, and
deprive the subject of recourse to the law
of his country, or are we not?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In dealing
with this Amendment it should be remembered that the powers of seizure given by
the Clause are limited to meat which is
exported or suspected of being exported
illegally. In practice this would mean
that fresh meat was being sent out without
the certificate required by Clause 5 (c);
or, in the case of cured meat, without the
prescribed indication as to the sender.
This is a question of fact, and is in no
Campbell, Mr. T. J.
Dobbs, Major A. F.
Barclay, Mr. William
Charlemont, The Viscount
Crawford, Col. the Right Hon. R. G.
Sharman
Cunningham, Mr. Joseph
way different from the holding up of a
motor-car by a policeman to make
inquiries as to why the owner has not
the necessary licence. The possibility of
a mistake being made and loss thereby
caused in such circumstances is therefore
almost nil. The Ministry has already
made a substantial and, it is believed,
unprecedented, concession by providing
that where an officer does anything unreasonable compensation can be awarded.
In the circumstances the Ministry regrets
that the Amendment cannot be accepted.
Mr. LESLIE: I really cannot in this
twentieth century accept the suggestion
that Government departments can do no
wrong. It has been impressed on us again
and again, sometimes very tragically,
that they can. I do not feel justified in
consenting to submitting any fellow subject to a possible injustice which can only
arise, no doubt, on a rare occasion owing
to some default somewhere. The possibility exists all the same, and this
would seem to me to render it more easy
for the Ministry to make what the noble
Viscount is pleased to describe as a concession. I cannot withdraw this Amendment.
Question put: "That these words be
inserted."

Clause ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSE 15 (Provisions as to dead
rabbits and dead poultry).
Mr. LESLIE: On a point of order, I
submit that this Clause is not within the
title and is outside the scope of the Bill.
I think that is a question for you, my
lord. The title is before you. A definition of meat will be found in the definition Clause, and in another Clause it will
appear that poultry is not meat. The
last reference will be found in lines 10
and 11 on page 14.
McHugh, Mr. J.
Long, Mr. John A.
Pakenham, Col. H. A.
Quigley, Mr. A.
The CHAIRMAN: In my opinion
poultry is included in meat, and it was
also so ruled in another place. I consider,
therefore, that we should proceed.
Mr. LESLIE: In that case I simply
move on ordinary grounds that the Clause
should be left out. I think that a
Clause-a rather lengthy Clause-dealing
with the packing of poultry is entirely
inappropriate to a Bill dealing with the
other matters which have been engaging
our attention this afternoon. I cannot
imagine myself that anybody in the
future looking for information in respect
of poultry will look for it in a Bill deal-
609 Agricultural Produce Bill-
Committee
11 NOVEMBER 1930

ing with meat, the marketing of meat and
so forth. I think it will be a very
serious inconvenience. I do not see that
there has been any public demand for
the regulation as to the packing of
poultry. I am rather of opinion, on the
other hand, that the ordinary person
would prefer in that respect to be left
to manage his own business in his own
way.
We did not hear anything, I think,
at an earlier stage about this packing of
poultry, and I do not myself understand
why the word hare should be introduced
as poultry. There does not seem to be
any great connection between the two.
Surely this is not the hare to which I
drew attention at an earlier stage. I
imagine it is a sort of Belgian hare
kept in a hutch-a tame rabbit. If it is
the ordinary wild hare then have
you
the Ministry overlapping the Game Act.
I must submit that this question of
poultry should be left over until there is
a demand for it. As a matter of fact, I
can only imagine that this is a piece of
legislation in advance, the Ministry being
of opinion, owing to the number of hens
which are kept in Northern Ireland, and
the number of eggs produced, that some
of these eggs will be hatched, and that
the number of cockerels which will necessarily follow is likely to increase, and,
therefore, the poultry trade may get some
sort of move on. I suppose that is why
the proposals are made, but I do not
think there is sufficient reason.
We
should not give the Ministry a blank
cheque or a book of blank cheques in
order that they may when the time
comes make regulations. Therefore, I
beg to move that Clause 15 be omitted
from the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: A good
many complaints have been received by
the Ministry from both carrying companies on this side and in Great Britain,
and from wholesale merchants in many of
the cities of Great Britain. The Amendment suggests that poultry and rabbits,
including hares, be altogether omitted.
from the Bill. It will be observed that

the Clause restricts the Ministry's powers
to the making of rules regarding the
