The Ministry is not prepared to go so
far at the moment, until fuller information regarding the conditions under
which this trade is conducted is obtained.
We are, however, satisfied from experience that the packing, especially of
poultry, calls for improvement in certain
Another important object of
Clause 15 is to obtain information which
will enable us to consider the case for
further legislation, such as has been suggested. It may be observed that any
rules regarding packing will be made in
consultation with representatives of the
trade. I regret, therefore, I cannot
accept the Amendment.
cases.
Mr. DORMAN: The whole object of
the Bill is to raise the standard of the

the Ministry has been wise in including
all kinds of meat.

Hear, hear.

Mr. DORMAN: In the particular lines

think it is advisable that we should by

I support the Clause.

Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 16 (Power of Ministry of

The following Amendment stood in the

Page 11, line 27, to leave out from

Clause ordered to stand part of the

CLAUSE 17 (Offences, prosecutions,
and applications of sums).
(1) Any person guilty of an offence
under this Act for which no special
penalty is provided shall be liable on
conviction-
(a) in the case of a first offence to a
fine of five pounds or any greater
sum not exceeding ten pounds,
(b) in the case of a second offence to a
fine of ten pounds or any greater
sum not exceeding twenty pounds,
(c) in the case of a subsequent offence,
to a fine of twenty pounds or any
greater sum not exceeding fifty
pounds, or, if in the opinion of
the court the offence was committed
by the personal act, default or
negligence of the person accused,
and the court is of opinion that a
fine will not meet the circumstances
of the case, to imprisonment for a
term not exceeding three months.
(2) Any person who obstructs
impedes an authorised officer, or
member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary, in the exercise of any of his
powers under this Act shall be liable
on conviction to a fine not exceeding
twenty pounds.
(3) Any person whoor
(a) in connection with an application
for the issue of a licence or permit
under this Act makes any statement or furnishes any information
which to his knowledge is in any
material respect false or misleading; or
(b) forges or fraudulently alters or
uses, or permits to be fraudulently
altered or used, any licence, permit
or other document issued, or mark
or indication prescribed, under
this Act;
shall be liable on conviction to imprisonment for a term not exceeding
three months or to a fine not exceeding
twenty pounds, or to both such imprisonment and fine.

Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
page 12, lines 8 and 9, to leave out "of
five pounds or any greater sum" ("first
offence to a fine of five").
I commend this Amendment to the
good sense of the Committee. The
Clause under consideration reads:
Any person guilty of an offence
under this Act for which no special
penalty is provided shall be liable on
conviction-
(a) in the case of a first offence to a
fine of five pounds or any greater
sum not exceeding ten pounds,
(b) in the case of a second offence to a
fine of ten pounds or any greater
sum not exceeding twenty pounds,
(c) in the
of a subsequent
offence
case
Reading that Clause, it covers the case
where no penalty is provided in the Bill
in regard to offences under the Act.
have scanned the Bill as carefully as I
could and I have found two prior Clauses
in which a penalty is provided for an
offence under this Bill. Under Clause 9,
Sub-Clause 3, any person who acts in
contravention of that Section is liable in
the case of a first offence to a fine not
exceeding £20, and of a second or subsequent offence to a fine not exceeding
£50. And that Clause deals with a
person who disposes of meat unfit for
human consumption. There is no minimum fine in that case. I cannot conceive of a graver offence under the Act,
and yet the Bill in dealing with that
particular class of offence omits any
minimum fine but leaves it to the discretion of the court in the case of a first
offence to put on a penalty not exceeding
£20, and in the case of a subsequent
offence one not exceeding £50.
The other case in which I find a penalty
imposed by this Bill is Clause 13, Sub-
Clause 6, which states that:
If any authorised officer discloses any
information obtained by him in the
exercise of his powers under this Act,
except to persons acting in the execution of this Act, he shall be liable on
conviction to a fine not exceeding fifty
pounds.
613 Agricultural Produce Bill-

11 NOVEMBER 1930
Committee
Those, I may be wrong, as far as my
searches go, are the only two prior
cases in which penalties are provided
in the Bill. They are not minimum
penalties, though they deal each with
a very grave class of offence.
It
is left to the discretion of the court
to say what the penalty shall be. Under
Clause 17 there may be a very technical
offence, some breach of the regulations or
rules of the merest minor character, and
yet in the case of a farmer struggling
under very heavy odds the court is left
no option but to put on a fine of £5 at
the very least.
I would appeal strongly to the good
sense of the Committee in this matter to
say whether or not there is reason for
the Amendment. I submit to the Committee that we shoud leave the fine to the
court. It may be a matter of no great
public importance, an infringement of
some regulation, and it should be left to
the court in the case of a first offence if
they thought it was a grave matter to
impose a penalty of £10, or if they
thought it was one of a trivial character
a fine of 10d. I ask hon. Members to say
what I suggest is reasonable.
Mr. LESLIE: My name is also on this
Amendment. The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell), who moved it,
referred the Committee to Sub-Clause (4)
of Clause 15, which constitutes it an
offence for a carrier to carry dead rabbits
or dead poultry in a package which is
not marked as provided by the regulations of the Ministery. That man, if he is
convicted, will be fined £5. That is one of
the ridiculous things here, which I pointed
out on the Second Reading. I will not
keep the Committee any longer on the
matter because there is very little one can
say on the subject of minimum fines except that it is very, very unfortunate
that a Ministry which wants its proposals
accepted should go out of its way to introduce a Clause of this kind. The
minimum fine is most unpopular in the
country and amongst the magistrates who
have to act upon it.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Question?
Mr. LESLIE: I think I may speak
from some acquaintance with magistrates.
I attend benches with very considerable
regularity, and very rarely, indeed, do I
find magistrates in favour of a minimum
fine. I support the Amendment.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
May I say on my first appearance since
my return to this country how heartily
I enjoy seeing you, Mr. Chairman, my
Lord, in the position which you now
hold. If you only conduct the affairs
of this House from the Chair as well as
I sincerely hope you will the Senate will
be very well satisfied.
I consider this the most important
thing in the whole Bill. I was going to
go away earlier in the afternoon, but
when I saw this Amendment
on the
Paper I felt bound to stay and express
my opinion. I heartily disagree with
the hon. Senator (Mr. Leslie) about the
opinion of the magistrates. Under other
Acts I have heard many magistrates express their great pleasure and satisfaction
at not being called upon to judge of the
amount of the fines which they should
impose. If you took a poll of the magistrates I think you would find that a great
many of them agree with the principle
of a minimum fine. I consider this one
of the most important Clauses in the Bill.
and I hope and trust it will be carried
because without it, in my opinion, the
Bill would be useless.
Mr. McHUGH: The hon. and gallant
Senator (Colonel Sharman-Crawford),
who has just sat down, has made one of
the most lamentable statements ever heard
in any public assembly.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Mr. McHUGH: It was a complete confession of his inability to discharge his
duty as a magistrate without the Government coming to the rescue and laying
down certain penalties which he must
impose. It is a terrible state of affairs
when the Government of a country must
come to the rescue and uphold and support a man who is so weak-minded as to
be entirely incapable of discharging his
duty without the aid and assistance of an
Act of Parliament. If that be true, and
if the magistrates who are called upon to
discharge these duties are so weak-minded
as the hon. and gallant Gentleman says,
then undoubtedly the Government are
entitled to lay down not only a minimum
fine but a maximum fine. In other
words, they should send a postcard to
every magistrate telling him what fines to
impose on the individuals who come before him.
615 Agricultural Produce Bill-

THE SENATE
Committee.
Colonel SHARMAN - CRAWFORD:
Good.
Mr. McHUGH: I am not a bit surprised that the Government are anxious
to get rid of these magistrates. In fact,
this is the only matter on which they
permit them to adjudicate at all. I take
it that that is the result of the deputation
which approached them some time ago,
threatened to pull down Ulster if they
interfered with them any more, and stated
that this was an honour handed down
from the days of King William to the
present day, and that it was terrible that
the Government should interfere with
them in any way. But, notwithstanding
this, the hon. and gallant Gentleman tells
us that he is so weak that he rejoices
that the Government have come to the
rescue and have put in this minimum fine
to prevent him from doing justice to his
fellow-man.
Colonel SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Nonsense.
We
Mr. McHUGH: If you are going to
have these honorary magistrates in the
Six Counties at all you should give them
at least some little discretionary power.
You have no right as a Government to
say to those people on whom you confer
an honour, "You must do so and so.
will put a muzzle on you, and will not
allow you any discretionary power whatever. You must decide always in our
favour, and in accordance with a certain
minimum which we will lay down, or
otherwise we will consider you entirely
incapable. We conferred the honour on
you, not that you might discharge your
duties as a magistrate, but for some
political assistance which you may have
given us in the past. That is the only
reason we have given you this honour."
I certainly protest in the strongest possible manner against the minimum fine in
this Bill, because it is a gross insult to
any man who holds the Commission of
the Peace.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I happen to
be chairman of our Diseases of Animals
Committee of our Down County Council.
We have done a great deal in trying to
eradicate sheep scab and other diseases of
that sort. Consequently we have to
initiate prosecutions. In some cases in
our county we do get a sort of fair treatment from the Bench, but in the neighare
bouring county in nineteen prosecutions
for offences of this kind, which
damaging our whole trade in lambs across
the water, 6d was the amount of the fine.
In another adjoining county ld was the
amount of the fine, and this went on until
our inspectors refused to prosecute further.
Complaints have been made about the
number of inspectors we have. If this
sort of thing were put down by a minimum fine there would be no need for
half the number of inspectors. The thing
would be wiped out immediately. I quite
admit that there are some benches that
do their duty well-and I think Ballymoney is one of the very best in the whole
County of Antrim-but there are others
that have no regard for anything except
the friendship of their neighbours.
Mr. McHUGH: Where?
Mr. CAMPBELL:
Colonel
Everywhere.
In what county?
SHARMAN CRAWFORD:
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM :
After hearing the short and concise speech
made by my hon. and learned Friend
(Mr. Leslie), I would ask the Government
if they could give the dog his first bite,
and, in the case of a first offence, instead
of fining a man £5 as a minimum, leave
the penalty at a fine not exceeding £10.
That would give the magistrates, or the
court, an opportunity for discretion in
the case of a first offence. I, for one,
would give them no discretion afterwards.
I just make this suggestion. I know the
infinite trouble that has been taken over
this Bill, and some of the reasons for
its introduction, but I would ask the
Government if they could see their way
to give the dog his first bite.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In considering this Amendment it should be
remembered that this Bill has been promoted because of complaints made by
the British Authorities that diseased
meat is arriving in Great Britain from
Northern Ireland. Every other country
sending meat to Great Britain is already
doing what is required. By the Bill we
in Northern Ireland are promising to
fall into line. It would, therefore, be a
very serious matter if after the Act comes
into operation diseased and uncertified
meat was found to be arriving from Northern Ireland. Therefore, the offence of
617 Agricultural Produce Bill -

11 NOVEMBER 1930
Committee.
sending out uninspected meat must be regarded as a very serious one demanding
a severe penalty. Moreover, it should be
remembered that the profit to be derived
from the export of unsound meat is presumably very high, and the temptation to
evade the Act is therefore substantial.
Anything less than a £5 penalty would
not be sufficient. It will be observed
that in the Act the minimum penalty is
not applied indiscriminately. It relates only to major offences as follows:-
1. Export or attempt to export by unlicensed persons.
2. Export of uncertified produce by
licensed traders.
3. Breaking the conditions of export
permits.
4. Carrying on the business of bacon
curing without a licence.
The presumption here would be that the
person concerned was avoiding control so
as to export illicitly by sending out in
the name of a licensed trader.
It will be observed that the ordinary
penalties are prescribed for minor offences,
failure to carry out an inspector's directions in regard to condemned meat,
obstruction, making of false statements,
and so on.
I regret therefore that I cannot accept
the Amendment as proposed, and I cannot, unfortunately, accept the suggestion
of the right hon. Senator who has just
sat down (Mr. Samuel Cunningham), as
this Bill must fall into line with other
Bills prescribing the same penalties.
Question put, "That those words be
left out."
The Committee divided: Contents, 3;
Not Contents, 13.

Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in
Clause 17, page 12, line 24, after the
word "officer" ("authorised officer, or
member of the Royal Ulster Constabulary"), to insert the word "inspector."
This is a very trivial matter. It seems
to me on referring to earlier Sections in
the Bill that possibly an inspector may
be something different from an authorised
officer. If that is so, he ought to be
protected from obstruction just as much
as an authorised officer. It is not a matter of great importance, but I am pointing it out, and beg to move the insertion
of the word "inspector."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Inspectors
under the Act will be authorised officers,
and the Amendment therefore is not
necessary.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in
Clause 17, page 12, line 37, after the
first "or" ("or mark or indication prescribed") to insert the word "label."
This is not very important, but it is
rather more important than the other.
Far be it from me to suggest that a label
cannot under any circumstances be a
document, but a label is not a "document
issued under this Act." I think if the
Committee will refer back to page 8,
Clause 13, line 27, 28 and so on, they
will see that "marked" is distinguished
from "labelled." It is, as the Committee
is aware, a practice which commands
general approval-when the Ministry of
Agriculture is not involved-to specify
quite definitely what your criminal
offence is. I think at the cost of making
even one alteration in this Bill this word
should be inserted.
If
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I do not
think it is necessary to introduce the word
"label," because it is surely sufficiently
covered by "indication prescribed."
it is inserted, you will make it absolutely
necessary that the sender should have a
label, whereas if he has an indication
showing his name and address, and where
the package comes from, I submit that
would be sufficient. I do not know if
the hon. and learned Senator desires to
press his Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: My point is in regard
to the fraudulent alteration or use of a
label.

not quite certain of this matter.

CLAUSE 19 (Provisions as to Appeals).
(1) Any person aggrieved by a
decision of the Ministry of Agriculture-
(a) refusing to register any premises
under this Act; or
(b) cancelling the registration of
any premises under this Act,
may appeal to the County Court having jurisdiction where the premises.
concerned are situate, and the decision
of that Court shall be final and conclusive.
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move, in Clause
19, page 14, line 38, after the word "Act"
at the end of paragraph (b) to insert,
"or (c) refusing to grant or renew any
licence under this Act." This is one of
the appeal Clauses, and once again I rise
to suggest that a man ought to be given
a right of appeal. In this case the appeal
is against a decision of the Ministry refusing to register any premises, or cancelling the registration of any premises
under the Act. As it is, he is entirely in
the hands of the Ministry, and that, as
I have said before, I do not think ought
to be. It may be that a man's trade is
at stake, but it certainly means that
unless there is an appeal you leave the
Ministry to be the judge in its own case.
I do not think there is any use in repeating arguments I have used on other
occasions, but I still maintain that this
is a case in which there ought to be an
appeal given, and if so, this particular
paragraph should be added to the Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I am sorry
I have to resist this Amendment on the
same grounds on which I opposed an
alteration to Clause 8. Any fines or
penalties under this particular Clause are
reviewable by Parliament and I do not
think it forms an appropriate matter for
reference to a Court of Appeal.
Mr. LESLIE: I am not abandoning
the principle by saving the House the
trouble of a Division.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 20 (Interpretation).
(1) In this Act, unless the context
otherwise requires, the following expressions have the meanings hereby
respectively assigned to them, that is
to say: :-
"Animals" means cattle, pigs,
sheep, goats and horses:
"Meat" means the carcase or part
of a carcase or offals of an animal:
"Offals" means all entrails, fat and
parts of an animal, other than a
dressed carcase, which are capable of
use for food for human consumption:
"Rabbits" includes hares:
"Poultry" means domestic fowls,
turkeys, geese and ducks:
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
Clause 20, page 15, leave out lines 17,
18, and 19 ("Rabbits" includes hares:
"Poultry" means domestic fowls, turkeys,
geese, and ducks).
Mr. LESLIE: I will not move this
Amendment. Yes, I will-(laughter)-
for the purpose of getting an explanation
as to what kind of hares and rabbits the
Bill deals with; whether it means the
ordinary wild rabbit, or whether it is
proposed to confine it to the tame hare
and rabbit. As regards poultry, I should
621 Agricultural Produce Bill-

11 NOVEMBER 1930
Appropriation Bill
also like to know why guinea fowl and
pigeons are not included, as generally
they are, as, for instance, in the Poultry
Act of 1911. Not being anxious that
poultry should be brought within the scope
of the Bill at all I am quite pleased to
see even guinea fowl and pigeons escape,
but I think the Committee should be told
why.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Might I ask the
right hon. Gentleman what is a domestic
fowl under this Clause.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If only
hon. and learned Senators would move to
delete certain words and refrain from
asking the Leader of the House conundrums I should be very pleased. I am
quite unable to say anything more than
that in the Bill "poultry" means domestic
fowl, and domestic fowl are such animals
with two legs and wings as would be supposed to be domestic fowls by hon. Members. (Laughter). If any further information on that point is desired I will
obtain it from the Ministry concerned and
give it at a later stage.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not press the
Amendment. We have got all the information we are likely to get.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without Amendment to the House.

House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without Amendment.
Moved, "That this Report be now received." (Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Appropriation (No. 2) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. LONG: On the Second Reading
of this Bill Senator Leslie asked what
effect the passing of this Bill would have
on the Budget, and on the Imperial contribution. I am informed by the Minister of Finance that it would be premature
for him to attempt to indicate at the
present juncture what the effect of the
present Bill on his Budget estimates is
likely to be. Account would have to be
taken of possible savings, and of the fact
that the amount of the Imperial contribution which appeared in his Budget
statement was only a provisional figure.
I beg to move, "That this Bill be now
read the Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: I am obliged to the hon.
Senator for looking into the question. I
think that is probably all the information
that could be furnished at this time. One
does not want to prolong the discussion
by raising the general question of unemployment, which is almost pertinent to this
particular Bill. I think we must be content to accept that information as being
the best available at the moment.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass."
(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any amendment.
