In Committee accordingly.
Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1 (Amendment of s. 3 of 63
and 64 Vict., c. 58, and s. 90 of 3 Edw. 7,
c. 37).
Mr. LONG: In reply to a question put
by the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) on the Second Reading, I should
like to say that this Bill is simply to
enable the variation every fifteen years
contemplated in the Act of 1900 to be
carried out. This variation in regard to
Section 3 persons was to take place every
fifteen years.
The variation for Section 1
persons was a final concession in the 1900
Act, and they received no revision of
terms in 1915, consequently neither are
they going to receive any concession in
1930. Furthermore, it might be pointed.
Electricity Bill.
Adjournment.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
out that by means of simple arithmetic,
taking 45 years, which is the term given
in Section 1 of the 1900 Act in respect
of these annuities which commenced any
time after 1870, he would find that the
number of annuities now outstanding is
negligible. They have almost vanished
from our accounts.
Mr. LESLIE: Again I am obliged to
the hon. Senator. I am sorry to say that
the negligible numbers include several
people I happen to know. But that is a
detail. I quite agree that this particular
Bill is not the appropriate opportunity
for raising the question. The fact remains that the terms of the final settlement in 1900 might perhaps be varied in
1930 owing to the difference in the interestproducing power of money. But that
affords no reason for opposing the Clause
in this particular Bill.
CLAUSE 2 (Sale of tithe rentcharges
and perpetuity rents, 35 and 36 Vict.,
c. 90. 20 Geo. 5, c. 4 (1930)) and 3 (Short
title and interpretation) ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported
without amendment to the House.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment.
Moved: "That this report be now
received."—(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Electricity (Interim Supply) Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read the
Third time."-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved: "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate agree to the said Bill without any
amendment.
Motion for Adjournment.
DUCHESS OF ABERCORN'S
Viscount
SPEECH.
CHARLEMONT:
Before
moving the adjournment of the House,
perhaps it would give satisfaction to hon.
Senators if I outlined the possible business
for this week. I would like to adjourn
until to-morrow for the Second Reading
of the Motor Bill. I like to call things
by their proper names, but that will be
a sufficient description of the Bill. The
Second Reading of this Bill was postponed
from last week to suit the convenience of
hon. Senators, and I regret to say that
owing to that postponement the Committee
stage must follow rather shortly after the
Second Reading. But the text of the Bill
has been circulated some time, and I
think hon. Senators will have it sufficiently
in their heads not to mind the short time
elapsing between the Second Reading and
the Committee stage. The Committee
stage will therefore have to be taken
either on Friday this week or on Monday
of next week-(Hon. Senators: Friday.)
-whichever hon. Senators prefer. Therefore if we meet to-morrow and adjourn
to-morrow until Friday the Committee
stage would be taken then. I beg to
move: "That this House do now adjourn
until to-morrow."
Mr. LESLIE: And we do not sit on
Monday?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: No; it was
Monday or Friday.
Mr. DORMAN: I rise to draw attention to a very serious constitutional question. We have to-day been commemorating the memory of the dead, of the
millions who laid down their lives during
the world war for what they conceived to
be the establishment of justice, and right
and liberty in the country. Since that
Armistice which we were commemorating
to-day was signed dynasties have fallen
and crowns have crumbled into dust, but
His Gracious Majesty King George and
Queen Mary have won the esteem, and I
believe the affection not only of their own
people, but of the statesmen of the world.
(Hon. Senators: Hear, hear). They have
won that esteem and that affection, I believe, in the first place, because as constitutional monarchs they have never
descended into the political arena; in the
second place, because they have never
Adjournment.
Adjournment.
11 NOVEMBER 1930
taken sides with one section of their people
against another; in the third place, because they have impartially and consistently performed the duties of a constitutional monarch; and in the fourth place,
they have never attempted to impose their
will upon any section of their people over
the heads of the people's representatives
in Parliament assembled.
I believe it is the sincere desire of His
Gracious Majesty King George and Queen
Mary that all the viceroys and vicereines
in the British Commonwealth of Nations
should follow their example and their
footsteps in the matters which I have mentioned just now. But what do I find? I
see a meeting held in the Ulster Hall,
reported in the "Northern Whig," of 27th
October, of Boy Scouts over which the
right hon. Gentleman the Premier of Northern Ireland presided and in which the
Vicereine of this particular part of the
Commonwealth of Nations, Her Grace the
Duchess of Abercorn, described the Boy
Scouts as a bulwark against Socialism and
Communism and unnecessary lawlessness.
Socialists have entered into the political
arena. They have sent me to this House
as their representative and they are represented in another place. The Prime
Minister of the Empire, the Minister
whose function it is to advise His Majesty
from day to day and the Governors of some
of the States in the Commonwealth are
Socialists. They are not lawless; they
have never been lawless. They have always advocated obtaining redress of any
wrongs which they conceive to be imposed
on their fellow-citizens in the Empire by
legal, lawful, constitutional
means.
Therefore, I feel that I would be a moral
coward, as a representative of that body,
if I did not embrace this the first opportunity of offering my protests on this particular matter. I have no desire to be
disrespectful to Her Grace the Duchess-
Mr. LESLIE: Can any action of the
Duchess of Abercorn come under the cognisance of this House?
Mr. DORMAN: If the House will bear
with me, it states here that the Duchess
described the Boy Scouts as a bulwark
against Socialism and Communism and
unnecessary lawlessness, thereby coupling
the Party which I represent in this
honourable House with other parties
guilty of lawlessness.
Mr. SPEAKER: I do not think this
is a matter to be raised on the Adjournment. I will try to find a more suitable
time for the hon. Member to raise the
question.
Mr. DORMAN: Thank you, my Lord.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at Five
minutes after Six o'clock.
Oral Answers
Motor Vehicles Bill
THE SENATE
THE SENATE.

Wednesday, 12th November, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Paper Presented to Parliament.
Ministry of Finance:
Appropriation and Consolidated Fund.
Services Accounts, 1929-30. (By Act).
Question to Minister.
MOTOR VEHICLES ACT, 1926-
ALLOWANCES TO RESIDENT
MAGISTRATES.
Mr. CAMPBELL asked the Leader of
the House if he will state the average
annual allowances paid since the passing
of the Motor Vehicles Act, 1926, to resident magistrates stationed outside Belfas
for travelling to petty sessions beyond
their districts, to hear cases under that
Act, and the average annual allowances
paid to them in the same period for
travelling to petty sessions within their
districts to hear all classes of cases.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I regret it
has not been possible in the time available
to obtain the information asked for by the
hon. and learned Member, and I may add
that as resident magistrates are necessarily
frequently sent to courts outside their own
districts to deal with cases other than those
arising out of the Motor Vehicles Act, I
fear it would be impossible in many
instances to distinguish the expenses attributable to cases under that Act.
The compilation of particulars of the
nature sought would involve very careful
examination of the accounts and records
of resident magistrates' duties, and having
regard to the fact, as I have already indicated, that the expenses directly attributable to motor cases alone could not in
many instances be distinguished. I venture
to suggest to the hon. and learned Member that the labour involved in obtaining
the information for which he asks would
be out of proportion to its utility, and I
trust that in these circumstances he will
not press the matter.
Mr. McHUGH: Arising out of the
question, I would like to ask the Leader
of the House is there not a flat rate laid
down for travelling to and from a county,
and is this not applied to every other
officer?
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I should
think so, but I would like to be able to
give the assurance "yes" or "no." If
the hon. Member will wait until our next
sitting I will have the information for him
without a question being put.
Mr. McHUGH: Thank you.
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This Bill
is a further instalment of our legislation
in regard to motor and road traffic. I do
not think it will be necessary for me to go
into the purpose of the Bill at any great
length. I would sooner take each Clause
and explain it as far as I can.
The first Part deals with a number of
miscellaneous matters which it is thought
proper to include. Clause 1 deals with
the validity in Northern Ireland of a
driver's licence granted in Great Britain.
The Clause in our Bill, of course, only
deals with the part that it is necessary for
us to take in the matter of reciprocity of
drivers' licences, but there will be a corresponding provision in the British legislation whereby licences granted here will
be recognised across the Channel, so that
the arrangement is fully reciprocal. I am
sure hon. Members will agree that this is
satisfactory, and I may say that it implements an informal arrangement which has
been in existence for some years.
Clause 2 deals with a matter which has
been considered at the request of the Postmaster-General. The purpose of the
Clause is to impose an obligation on every
bus owner, who runs a regular service, to
carry mails in the same way as railways
are obliged to do at present. Of course,
a bus has not the same capacity for this
purpose as a train, and so suitable provisos are made in sub-clause (1) to limit
the duty of the bus owner in a reasonable
way. The method of payment for this
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
12 NOVEMBER 1930
service is laid down in sub-clause (2); the
tribunal which was set up under the 1929
Act will have power to determine the
proper charges in the absence of agreement between the parties.
Clause 3 of this part of the Bill removes
any doubts which may exist as to the
powers of local authorities to incur expenditure on traffic signals.
Clause 4 is concerned with the practice
of taking a motor car without leave of the
owner where the offence does not come
within the category of stealing. There
has been a regrettable increase in this
class of offence recently, and the Government asks for stronger powers than exist
at present to put it down.
Part II of the Bill, that is from Clause
5 to Clause 16, inclusive, deals with the
question of third party risks arising out
the use of motor cars. When the last
Motor Traffic Bill was going through
Parliament, the Government considered
very carefully whether compulsory third
party insurance should then be applied to
private vehicles. But there was the difficulty that it would be almost impossible
to introduce it in Northern Ireland until
a similar scheme was inaugurated in Great
Britain, because, of course, it would have
entailed a difference in the insurance
arrangements, and the companies would
hardly have been willing to do this for
such a small area as our own. However,
in the meantime Great Britain is introducing this compulsion, and accordingly the
time has arrived when we feel able to
follow suit. The main features of the
scheme are, of course, that all motor
vehicles must be insured against third
party risks, with certain exceptions which
are prescribed in the Bill, such as the
cases of vehicles owned by county or
borough councils, the Belfast Harbour and
Water Commissioners, and other persons
who make a deposit of a sufficient sum
against their possible liabilities.
Clause 11 is of special interest in that
it provides for hospital charges up to a
certain limit. These institutions have
been very badly used in a good many cases
in the past by having treated injured
people without having been able to recover any payment, even though the
injured person may have got substantial
compensation from an insurance company.
The object of the proposed Clause is to
ensure that the hospitals will get a reason.
able amount up to a certain limit for their
services.
Part III of the Bill consists of definitions and interpretations, and in Clause
19 prescribes that it shall come into operation on such appointed days as may be
laid down by the Minister of Home
Affairs; and different parts of the Act
may be introduced on different dates.
The main purpose of the Bill is, of
course, the matter of compulsory third
party insurance. I think that it is now
possible to introduce successfully this very
desirable provision, and I do not believe
that it will operate harshly on any responsible motor owners or put undue power
into the hands of the insurance companies
I beg to move.
Mr. CAMPBELL: This Motor Vehicles
and Road Traffic Bill was the tit-bit in
the window dressing of the Government
for the present Session. The thought
occurs to me that very shortly this House
will be rising; winter has begun; conditions of unemployment are rather appalling, manufactures and industries are
really drifting and floundering, and the
Government in this Bill are simply
fiddling with a very serious question, and
have produced to the House a Bill which
is really but a faint image, a simulacrum
of a Road Traffic Bill. The Bill is a most
disappointing one. By contrast with the
Bill which has recently emerged from the
Parliament at Westminster, which is a
robust Act, this is a mere emaciated
starveling of a Bill.
There are one or two matters in the Bill
which demand attention. In the first
place, at the very beginning of the Bill,
as has happened in other recent Measures,
the Government, I think injudiciously,
are trying to erect a kind of Chinese wall
between this area and the rest of Ireland.
By a very early Clause in the Bill we give
the free run of our roads in Northern
Ireland to motor drivers who are licensed
in Great Britain. That may be perfectly
right, but at the same time we make no
concession whatever to motor drivers who
may be licensed in adjoining counties in
the Free State, or in any part of the Free
State. The law with regard to motor
driving on the public highway there is
not dissimilar to the law in Northern Ireland and in Great Britain, and it is
regrettable that in this matter we have
not, at least, reciprocity, and that we do
not give the same facilities to drivers-
Irish drivers-from the rest of Ireland as
we give to drivers from Scotland and
England.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
With regard to the question of hospital
expenses, it seems to me that the liability
to pay hospital expenses is confined to
cases where legal liability is admitted by
the insurers, where an accident has taken
place; where a policy is in existence, and
where the insurers say "We admit we are
liable to pay under this policy in respect
of this accident." But I humbly submit
to the House that it does not extend to a
case of an ex-gratia payment in respect of
an accident with a motor car on the public
highway. There may be cases-perhaps
more numerous than those in which there
is an admission of liability-where an
accident has happened; where a party has
been hurt, and where the insurers say
"We are under no liability whatever in
this matter. We did nothing wrong. The
blame lies on you, but, in order to avoid
the expense of long litigation, we will
make what is called an ex-gratia payment
outside our policy altogether and without
any admission or acknowledgment of
liability." The Bill does not extend to
that class of case, a fairly numerous class
of case, and I submit that it ought to
extend to cases of that class.
I also submit that the limit of £25,
which is the maximum in the case, is
inadequate. That sum would very soon be
eaten up nowadays in the treatment and
maintenance of an injured party in one
of our first-class up-to-date hospitals. It
would not cover the expenses very far,
and I think it would be a wise step to
increase that amount knowing, as we do,
the enormous burdens now placed upon
hospitals by the increasing number of
accidents which are taking place on our
highways owing to the universal use of
motor cars.
A son,
Then there is one matter further to
which I wish to refer. The Bill does not
extend to the case of the unauthorised
use of a motor car by a member of the
family of the insured person.
for instance, may take out a private
motor car belonging to his father without
his father's knowledge, or authority, or
consent. An accident may happen while
the car is out on that unauthorised
journey, and it may be due to his son's
negligence. The Bill is of no use in a
case of that kind. The father is under
no responsibility for the unauthorised use
of the car by his son, and the son may
not be worth powder and shot.
The result of that would be that the injured
motorist or the injured pedestrian, or the
family of the slain motorist or pedestrian,
might whistle down the wind for compensation.
In addition, I submit it would be a
much simpler principle for the insurance
to follow the car. Instead of being
annexed to a particular party, the owner
or driver of the car, it ought to extend
to the use of the car by any person, especially by a member of the family of
the person insured. It can be readily
appreciated that in a case where the
authority given by the father to the son
is disputed it might be exceedingly hard
to prove that authority.
One other matter which the Bill does
not deal with is to be found in the lack
of system caused by the more or less unbusinesslike way of dealing with these
motor traffic cases. They have to be heard
by resident magistrates. The local justices have no jurisdiction. We very often
read of cases where a great deal of confusion is caused by a man being summoned
to a particular court and no resident
magistrate being available, whereas in an
adjoining area there may be a resident
magistrate waiting to try motor business.
There seems a great deal of haphazard
work and a lack of business capacity in
dealing with the matter. I think the
resident magistrates in their own particular districts ought to be able to deal with
all classes of motor car offences that arise
in their districts without calling in at
unnecessary public expense the services of
resident magistrates from a distance.
I recollect, speaking of my own personal
experience, of a case, not under the Motor
Car Acts, which required the attendance
of two magistrates. The case was fixed
for 11 o'clock in the morning. One of
the resident magistrates was there
punctually at that hour ready to try the
case, but the other resident magistrate at
that precise hour, eleven o'clock, had to
be elsewhere in a court in another county
50 miles away. He did not arrive until
half-past four o'clock, and at half-past
four o'clock the case which should have
begun at eleven o'clock started.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to support this Bill, but in doing so I should
like to get a little further information
on one or two matters which perhaps the
noble Lord, the Leader of the House,
may be able to give. By Clauses 9 and
10 we get the fact that an insured person will have to have a certificate of insurance, and when applying for the
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
12 NOVEMBER 1930
ordinary motor tax in the earlier part of
the year will have to show the certificate
of insurance before getting the ordinary
motor tax paid. I am the proud possessor
of three motor cars-one is a lorry, one is
for my own use, and one is for the use of
my family. They were all bought at
different times, and the insurance falls
due, on some of them in July and on
others at different times. I want to be
assured that this certificate of insurance
which is to be presented on 1st January
will cover all those insurances up to that
time. Or will there have to be some
other arrangement made?
On the question of this certificate of
insurance, the owner or driver of the car
has to present the certificate of insurance
when demanded by the proper authority.
This is rather a cumbrous way of doing it.
We have to carry enough things as it is
in these days of licences, and to have to
carry another beastly bit of paper will
possibly cause a great deal of disruption
in many families. I suggest we might
really do something like this. At present
a motor licence is borne on the car in a
little circular disc, and I suggest it would
be quite possible for this certificate of
insurance also to be carried on the car, a
simple thing showing the insured person's
name, the company with which he is insured, and the class of vehicle that is
insured.
That would be of great assistance to the
man who gets into his car in a hurry in
the morning and leaves his certificate
behind, and it would also be a great help
to a constable or other person entitled to
stop cars and ask for certificates. It would
cost very little more, and would simplify
matters both from the point of view of the
owner and the official. He would simply
have to look at it as he looks at the tax
certificate. It would save a lot of trouble,
and I do recommend it heartily as a way
of making this thing run more smoothly.
I do not think it would be at all difficult
or impossible. It would probably be very
useful indeed.
Those two points are the points I wanted
particularly to stress. I congratulate the
Government on having had the pluck to
put in that Clause about catching joyriders. I think it should be extremely
useful, and I hope it will put down this
offence which is becoming much too common in this country as well as in England.
I rather join with my hon. and learned
Friend, Senator Campbell, in regard to
some sort of reciprocity with the Free
State, but we shall hear about that later.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am in entire
agreement with the principle embodied in
this Bill, but I think the Government are
not to be commended on their choice of
machinery for carrying out matters under
the Measure. It is a very serious thing
for Parliament to delegate to insurance
companies power to say who shall or who
shall not drive a motor car, because that
is what this Bill really does. A person
cannot get a licence and cannot drive
a car unless he has a policy issued
to him by an insurance company.
If the insurance
company refuse,
the Bill stands
at the present
moment, the applicant has no redress. I
submit, and will press the question very
strongly, that as it stands this is a Clause
to which Parliament should not give its
approval. Would it not be an absurd
thing for us to delegate our powers to an
insurance company to
say who in
Northern Ireland should drive a motor
car and who should not? I suggest that
that could be amended by providing some
kind of tribunal. There should be some
way of getting redress by means of a
tribunal for cases of this description.
as
This is a tremendous power to put in
the hands of insurance companies.
Insurance companies now know that
motorists must insure their cars. Insurance companies can and will combine for
a
common purpose to raise premiums,
and I think the House, before they pass
this Bill, should be assured that there
will be some tribunal to examine the
profits these insurance companies make to
show they are not justified in putting any
burden they like on the shoulders of
motorists. I think it is a terrible thing
that these companies should have such
powers. In the first place they can say
who is to drive a motor car, and in the
second place they can say at what price
he is to drive, because there is no control
over them at all. The insurance
company can charge whatever they like,
and under this Bill the public, and the
Government, and everybody else are
helpless.
Surely that is a position that we as a
legislative body could not possibly give
our approval to, especially when there
must be, and can be, some means of controlling these companies by setting up
some tribunal to deal with these two
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. McAllister]
matters, to deal, first of all, with a man
who is prevented from driving a motor
car, and, secondly, to control the
premiums that these people under this
Bill can, if they like, extort from the
pockets of the whole motoring community
of Northern Ireland. It is a very
dangerous position. It would be very
dangerous for this House or for the
Government to delegate that control to
public limited liability companies.
I am also in favour of Clause 4, which
deals with joy-riding; but I doubt very
much if the Clause as it stands at present
will be effective in carrying out the purpose for which it is intended. On the
wording of the Clause I think it will be
very difficult in many instances to prove
joy-riding.
Another thing I wish to draw the attenof the House to is the wholesale power
that this Bill gives to a Government Department of making regulations under
this Bill. I have no hesitation in saying
that when the principal Motoring Bill was
passed neither the House of Commons nor,
this House had the slightest idea that
they were giving into the hands of the
Government offices power to make the
regulations which emanated from them.
It is all very well to say that these regulations will be put on the Table of the
House. I suggest that they should be
published and circulated to Members before they are finally passed.
I
With regard to hospitals, I am in entire
agreement with what has been said.
think these institutions in Northern Ireland which have done so much for the
people of Northern Ireland require consideration in this House. I have always
admired the voluntary services day after
day, sometimes for many hours a day,
that the medical profession have given
to the sick and suffering here and in other
places. For that reason the hospitals are
entitled to be protected. When you find
a body of men like the doctors giving
so much of their time day in and day out,
surely it is not too much to assure them
that the hospitals will not be deprived of
the remuneration to which they are entitled.
This is really included in the sum paid
by the insured, because he must prove the
cost of medical treatment and hospital
fees in order to get a certain sum of
money. It is then not his money. The
hospital is undoubtedly entitled to it. I
give that part of the Bill my wholehearted support. With these observations I shall close, but I would like again
to stress the two points I have made, that
until some scheme is devised the public
will be protected from being deprived of
licences, and also that they will be protected against the wholesale raising of
premiums to a prohibitive amount.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
rise with pleasure to support this Bill,
which I feel is a good one. I am specially
interested in two points that have been
mentioned, one by the hon. and gallant
Member behind me (Colonel Pakenham),
who spoke of certificates and licences, and
the other the making of regulations, a
subject which has been dealt with by the
hon. Member (Mr. McAllister). I do
not see why, if the licence cannot be
issued without the owner being insured,
one need carry a certificate of insurance.
If I apply for a licence I take my certificate of insurance, and I cannot get a
licence without it. If that licence is
affixed to the car, the police and everyone
else know it would not be there unless
there was an insurance effected to merit
it. Therefore, I think that to simplify
matters it would be better to do away
with having to carry the certificate of
insurance at all, and to make the one
document serve both purposes.
There is a tendency, as the hon. Senator
in front (Mr. McAllister) has mentioned,
of
Government Departments making
regulations and putting them on the
Table. I think that before any regulations under this Motor Bill, or indeed
any Bill that comes before this House,
are passed, we should demand that those
regulations be placed before Parliament,
and we should have a say in whether they
are reasonable or not. The tendency is
for the making of regulations to get into
the hands of officials. I think our
present officials are good, but officials die
away, and we do not know what kind
of officials we may have in future. Therefore I think that, as we are responsible to
the country, we should have a say in the
regulations made under these Measures.
I hope that the Government in this Bill
will provide some means by which the
regulations must be submitted to this
House before they become operative.
As far as insurance is concerned, I
have no doubt that at the present time
there is enough competition amongst the
insurance offices to keep the rate reasonable, but if at a future date the insurance offices put up the rate beyond a
"
舅
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
12 NOVEMBER 1930
reasonable price we here, as well as the
people in England, will require a tribunal
to go into the figures as the hon. Senator
(Mr. McAllister) has said, and to put a
stop to their power of bleeding the public
by means of a monopoly which they
possess.
I agree that it is time something was
done to deal with joy-riding. A few
days ago my son was called to a house
to see a sick person. When he came out
his motor was away. It was just possible
that he might have had to go for a doctor.
This happened at night, and if he had
been asked to go for a doctor he would
have been without his car. His car
turned up near at hand the next morning,
and was all right when the police came
upon it. There was no harm done, but,
as I have said, the occurrence might have
been serious if he had had to go for a
doctor. The sooner these petty thefts
are done away with the better. I have
great pleasure in supporting the Bill as
a whole.
Mr. LESLIE: It is really a great relief, after the long and dreary discussion,
according to the newspapers, which we
had yesterday afternoon, to turn to a Bill
which is alike concise and well arranged
and easy to follow. As a matter of fact
I propose to support the Bill on its merits.
The Bill before us follows wherever possible the English Measure which became
law on 1st August last. The English
Measure, as the hon. and learned Senator
in front of me (Mr. Campbell) has already reminded us, is a consolidating
Measure, and I very much wish that we
could have a consolidating Measure ourselves, but I am afraid I must admit that
I do not think the time for that has quite
come yet. I do not think we are quite
ready to consolidate our motor traffic law,
and therefore until that happens we must
continue to proceed-I had better not
say step by step-pace by pace guided to
some extent by trial and error. The Bill
follows the English Act almost word for
word in its corresponding Clauses, and I
think it would be very undesirable to
make any change in the wording, if we
could avoid it, in order that, as has been
pointed out before, we may have the
benefit of the application of any judicial
decisions which may be given in England.
I do not think there will be any great
difficulty either in passing the Bill through
the House or in its administration; but I
think any difficulties that may arise will
on
be difficulties of our own making, and I
am not sure that it will be possible for
us to remove them altogether on this
occasion. In going through the Bill, 1
am going to take the various points in
the order of the Bill itself. First of all,
this question of the reciprocity of
licences, I am very glad, indeed, that
now, some four years after I spoke at
very considerable length on the subject of
the reciprocity of licences when the Motor
Bill which became the Act of 1926 was
before us, the matter has been put on a
completely satisfactory footing.
I hope
the time may come when we shall be able
to offer reciprocity also to the Free State,
but I am not satisfied, I regret to say,
that the time for that is just yet.
The next Clause is the one dealing with
mail services, and that one need not hesitate to support, but I am inclined to be
afraid that for once quite sufficient power
has not been taken in the matter of adjusting charges. In going through the
Clause it seemed to me, although I may
be wrong, that we are tying the hands of
the tribunal just a little too tightly in the
matter of charges to be made for the
mail services. It does not seem to me
that mail services should necessarily be
conducted on the same rate as applies to
parcels. The mail service imposes responsibilities which are not perhaps equally
the same in the case of the ordinary parcel.
As regards the matter on which everybody seems to agree in describing as joyriding, I am quite in support of the
Clause, but I do not approve of confining
the jurisdiction in these cases to the resident magistrates. If hon. Members will
turn to the Clause they will see that there
is a possibility of a defence, that is, a
reasonable belief of a lawful authority.
I do think that, directly that question is
raised, it becomes a question for a jury,
or, if not for a jury, certainly not for decision by one man sitting alone, with an
appeal to another man sitting alone. It
is not a matter that ought to be decided
in that way.
That is one of the difficulties which I think we have made for ourselves. But I do not propose to put any
Amendment on the Paper myself on that
matter on this occasion, because I think
this House comparatively recently agreed
to confine the jurisdiction in motor cases
to resident magistrates. As they have
done so recently, I hesitate to bring the
question up again. I propose to pass on
with an expression of disapproval in that
particular connection.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
To come to Part II, one cannot, I
think, but be struck with the apparent
fact that the framers of the Bill, like the
framers of the Act in England, seem to
attach a great deal more importance to
the possession of the certificate than they
do to the actual insurance of which it is
the evidence. I do not think any very
great difficulty will arise in practice, because I imagine that, as has been suggested by the right hon. Gentleman in
front (Mr. Samuel Cunningham) and by
my hon. and gallant Friend (Colonel
Pakenham), a provision will be made to
issue something which can be carried on
the car. That is all right for the onecar man, and it is all right for my hon.
and gallant Friend (Colonel Pakenham),
who has three cars which are insured at
different times, but I cannot see that it is
so simple for the three-car man with one
policy covering the three cars. That is
a common practice. A short time ago I
had two cars. I have only one car now,
but I may have two cars again. There
will have to be some provision made for
those cases, which will become of great
importance when you are dealing with a
firm with 20 or 30 lorries. I am pretty
certain that an arrangement can be made
without any serious difficulty.
I think one must ask by way of a question the Government to assure us that
they are satisfied as to the meaning of the
expression, "User of a car.”
The point
that occurs to one is absolutely plain, that
that Clause does not catch the mere passenger. There is no statutory definition
that I know of. I have not been able
to find any decision which definitely goes
the full way.
The word is used in the
English Act, but it is used so repeatedly
that it is not liable to be misunderstood. I
believe there is a case on, I think, Section
95 of the Railway Clauses Act of the
forties of last century which may answer
the question, but I think for the sake of
the public the Government ought to give
us the meaning of the expression.
On the general question of this thirdparty insurance, I must say I am not
enthusiastic about it. I must say at
once that, although I myself am not
enthusiastic on the subject, one must
recognise that this insurance has been
provided for across the water, and was
asked for both in this House and in
another place when earlier Motor Bills
were before us. We have been informed
that regrettable accidents have occurred
where there has been no actual redress.
The objection seems to be first, if you
look theoretically at the question, it is
a great misfortune for a man to be killed
by a motor car, but it is really no greater
misfortune for that man to be killed by
a milk cart. Accidents with horse-drawn
vehicles do still occasionally occur.
I am
making the only answer to that. The
horse-drawn vehicle rarely goes at a speed
greater than 12 miles an hour, and if
anybody were to make that objection the
answer is to confine your motor car to
twelve miles an hour, and label it accordingly. I do not think anyone would
approve of that. The effect of this new
Clause will be to put off the road a certain
number of the smaller vehicles owned
by the smaller people. There will be a
resulting loss in road revenue.
On the question of an increase in
insurance rates, I think that in the event
of oppression by the raising of rates or
unjust refusal to accept certain owners,
the motor organisations themselves are
strong enough to form their Own
insurance body. They would have no
difficulty in depositing the necessary
funds, and I think if the worst comes to
the worst, that is a course which could
be taken. It would be a somewhat better
course perhaps than for the Government
itself to take up insurance. Then there
is the risk that this new policy of requiring
a policy will result in a great crop of
litigation. I cannot help thinking that
as a result of this Bill there will be many
cases in which some person will be injured
and will rush into court to get the benefit
of it. We shall hear a great deal of Mr.
Butterfield and his ride through the
streets of Derby and the doctrine of contributory negligence which grew therefrom.
Further, there remains the risk that it
being known that in these cases the
liability is with the insurance companies
damages on an undue scale will be
awarded. If that is so, it is rather in
that direction I think that the danger lies
of an undue rise in insurance premiums.
(Hon. Members: Hear, hear.) If I remember aright there was the same difficulty some ten years ago, may be less,
when most unreasonable damages were
given in one case, and the insurance
companies, I think, limited their risk for
some time, a year or two, to a smaller
sum or required a higher premium. Again
I think the same answer will be given.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
12 NOVEMBER 1930
I rather gather from the hon. Senator
in front of me that we may expect the
Amendment Paper to be illuminated by
some amendments on this matter, just as
I expect the right hon. Gentleman in front
of me will also illuminate the Amendment
Paper in respect of the matters he alluded
to. I should like a little more information as regards this invalid carriage.
Invalid carriages are exempted from the
scope of the Bill, and that includes, I
imagine, those used as an ambulance. If
an ambulance kills a man it is just as
unfortunate for that man as if he had
been killed by a motor car or a milk cart.
In England an invalid carriage is not
exempted, unless it is a carriage which
does not proceed at a greater rate than 20
miles an hour. I should like to be told
why it has not been thought advisable to
insert the same rate here and to limit an
invalid carriage to 20 miles an hour.
I pass from that to another small difficulty that we made ourselves. If the
House will turn to Clause 9 they will see
that provision may be made by regulations
under Section 12 of the Roads Act. That
raises in a very acute form the difficulty
we are in at the moment as regards the
application of the Section of the 1929 Act
confining the jurisdiction in motor cases.
to resident magistrates, the difficulty
being as to whether matters such as the
rere red reflector of a bicycle, which is
prescribed, require the attention of a
resident magistrate or not. The point is,
I believe, regarded as doubtful. Different
opinions prevail. I myself refuse to adjudicate until the court or Parliament
clears up the matter.
I think that was
not the intention of Parliament, but the
matter now arises.
Now we come to the general question
of regulations. I think that regulations
are very much required in the case of this
Bill, and I think it is a Bill in which they
will do no great harm. There are, if I
may say so, three classes of regulations
which are recognised by Parliament.
There is class 1, from the Parliamentary
point of view, that is, the regulation
which is to be laid before Parliament and
which is not to come into operation until
it is approved by both Houses of Parliament. That is the class to which I think
the right hon. Gentleman referred.
There is a second class into which the
regulations under this Bill will come, that
is, regulations which are to be laid on
the Table of the House and may be objected to within the statutory period,
The
otherwise they will become law.
third class, which is perhaps the most
numerous class, is the regulation which is
made and comes into effect at once, and
is simply presented to Parliament. Even
in that case the fact of presentation of
these regulations appears in the Journals
and the Debates, and copies can be obtained in the Library, and Members can
get the information if they choose to read
their papers. But we shall require
regulations here.
With regard to the matter of presentation at police station-and I am able to
pass the expression "police station" even
in Northern Ireland-presentation in
person or otherwise, I think we must remove as unsuitable for Northern Ireland,
the requirement that a man who has the
misfortune to meet with an accident must
report within 24 hours. That may be all
very well in England, but it is rather too
strong for Northern Ireland. That also
raises the question as to whether the man,
as the result of the accident, will be in a
position to report anything in 24 hours
or 240 hours. I think also the regulations should make it a little easier for
the man buying a new car and entering
into insurance.
ex
We now come to the question of hospitals of which so much has been said. I
do not think I have heard anyone, either
in the House or outside, make any complaint whatever against this contribution
to hospitals. I think it is generally regarded as a debt overdue. I have heard
it suggested that the Clause does not
apply to an ex gratia payment. If an
ex gratia payment is made to A there is
nothing to stop making another
gratia payment to B. Again, it has been
said that £25 is too small a maximum.
Perhaps that may be so. But I cannot
help thinking that if we are to raise
the maximum, having regard to the
extra burden thereby placed on the insurer, the rise ought to be on a percentage scale, that is to say, it should have
some relation to the total amount of liability.
A somewhat curious point has been put
to me in respect of these hospital cases.
I am intending to put something on the
Paper about it in order that we may have
the advantage of hearing the Government
view on the subject. I shall put an
Amendment on the Paper, but I wish to
say at once that in the event of the Government not accepting it I shall not divide
the Committee. The point put to me is
L
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
that an insurer-the inquirer was an insurance company-the insurer desires to
get a medical report on the progress of
some in-patient in respect of whom
liability has arisen. The insurer desires
to obtain a medical report from the hospital. The hospitals at the present time
say, "You shall have the report if you pay
two guineas for it." If now compulsory
payment is made that two guineas should
disappear. Now, I am not satisfied that
the power of regulations is quite wide
enough to cover some of the cases I have
mentioned, and if not I shall ask the
House, in Committee, to extend it within
reasonable limits.
There remains only the question of
what we are going to do as regards people
in the service of the Crown. In England
there has been a Clause put into the Act,
the English Act, Section 121, for the
information of anybody who wants to
know. It is quite general. Now, we
cannot legislate in respect of the Army or
the Air Force, and both the Army
and the Air Force have a good
many rather large lorries operating
in Northern Ireland. It might be thought
that these lorries are sometimes a source
of danger. There is also the question of
the Post Office. I think we must ask the
Government to tell us what they propose
to do about that. It may be that there is
some reciprocity arrangement, but I think
we are entitled to urge that if an ordinary
lorry driver has to be insured compulsorily
so also must the Army driver, or we must
be informed that a satisfactory provision
has been made. If I remember right it is
not the practice in His Majesty's Army
to insure anything, that is to say, the
Army Vote carries its own liabilities. Of
course it has Parliament behind it.
There remains the question of a public
highway which is referred to in the definitions. In this Act the expression "public
highway" includes a roadway to which
the provisions of the principal Act apply,
The effect will be to bring the application
of that expression into force as regards the
whole of motor vehicles. That reminds
me of a small question that arose in the
last Act as to whether in the definition
of being under the influence of drink
or drugs the premises of the Belfast
Harbour Commissioners were sufficiently
covered. It seems to raise a bigger question as to these roadways, but the Belfast.
Harbour Commission is a sufficient instance. I think I would ask for further
information on that point; if it is not convenient at the moment it will do perfectly
well at a later time. Although several
of us will probably contribute Amendments I hope the passage of the Bill will
be a smooth one. I support the Bill and
I hope it will give satisfaction to our
people.
Major DOBBS: The local authorities
have been given power to provide and
maintain mechanically-operated signals for
the control of traffic. 1 do hope that the
Government will point out to the local
authorities that there are strangers, and
it is no good having mechanical signals at
certain points, cross-roads and so on, if
you do not make it clear that you have
got that signal. It is quite easy for the
people of Belfast to know about the signals
in Belfast if they are using those roads,
but there may be somebody from the
other end of England suddenly coming to
the cross-roads where these mechanical
signals are in force, and it is very necessary that he should know that there
are mechanical signals being worked there.
On the old Roman Road, Watling
Street, about 150 miles from London-1
am not quite certain-there is one of these
green, yellow and red signals, and about
200 or 250 yards on either side of the
four roads there is a very small miniature
one pointing out the fact that there is
this signal. I think it is very important
because it is quite impossible sometimes to
see these signals. Take a different case.
Take Shaftesbury Square. I think it is
quite impossible for anybody to drive to
Shaftesbury Square and know that there
is this circular traffic.
Then there is another point I would
draw attention to, that is the more effective control of roads under repair so as
to avoid inconvenience to traffic generally. In England I think the usual course
is that the contractor supplies the men
who work the traffic there, and over here
the constabulary do it. I really would
like to ask if there is any reason why the
contractors' men should do it in England
and our constabulary should have to do
it here. With regard to Clause 4, I do
hope that this Clause is sufficiently
strong. I think the question of joyriding, such as the borrowing of a car
without sanction, is on the face of it a
serious thing. You may have the car
destroyed. Last Christmas Eve I saw a
car turned upside down. It was a brand
new car two days before, and a very ex-
Motor Vehicles Bill
Adjournment
12 NOVEMBER 1930
pensive one. When I saw it it was worth,
I suppose, scrap iron, hardly more at any
rate. That car had been taken out.
That is purely material damage, but
there have been some bad cases in England. Cars have been taken out there and
driven, and we had some terrible accidents
resulting in death. That is a very serious
thing. There is another case—where you
have these joy-riders or cars being taken
out without permission. If you read the
accounts of the snatch-and-grab robberies
on jewellers' shops and so on in our large
cities you will find that the perpetrators
had suddenly taken a car from the side of
a street and used it to make away.
hope this Clause is going to be sufficiently
strong to put that down if only in a
partial way.
I
Mr. GLENDINNING: In view of the
public interest taken in this Bill I would
like to ask if the noble Viscount would
give us information as to what time will
elapse between the passing of the Bill and
putting the provisions into operation?
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: So many
questions have been asked on this Bill on
various Clauses, that I think it would be
simpler for me not to give the answers
now, and if the House would agree
would give them on the different Clauses
when the Bill is being considered in Committee. If hon. Members are put to any
inconvenience in regard to putting
Amendments down for the Committee
stage, I may say it will be open to them
to put them down on the Third Reading.
I would only say in general that I am glad
the Bill has received a general measure of
support. With regard to the first question
put by the hon. and learned Senator
(Mr. Campbell), who regretted there
was not reciprocity with the Free State,
I would point out that the Free State has
no reciprocal arrangement with Great
Britain. I do not think, therefore, the
time has come yet-I hope it will comewhen a reciprocal arrangement can be
entered into between ourselves and the
Free State. As to the remainder of the
questions I would like to give replies at a
future date.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
Whole House for Friday.
Tithe Rentcharge and Variable Rents
Bill.
Order for Third Reading read.
Moved, "That this Bill be now read the
Third time" (Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass"-
(Mr. Long.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill without any Amendment.
Adjournment.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until Friday," when I hope to take the
Committee stage of the Bill of which we
have taken a Second Reading to-day.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
twenty minutes after Five
o'clock until Friday next.
Protection of Hares Bill 648
Oral Answers
THE SENATE
THE SENATE

Friday, 14th November, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Question to Minister.
DISHORNING OF CATTLE.
Major DOBBS: I beg to ask the
Leader of the House what steps are being
taken by the Police to prevent the infringement of the law regarding the dishorning of cattle, and how many convictions have been obtained by the Police
in Northern Ireland for non-compliance
with this Act since 1921.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In accordance with the general instructions.
contained in the Police Code, the Royal
Ulster Constabulary are giving the question of cruelty to animals in all its forms
the closest attention, and I am informed
that the utmost vigilance is observed by
them to prevent cruelty in connection with
the matter specifically referred to by the
hon. and gallant Senator in his question.
I am in a position to inform the hon. and
gallant Senator that since 1921, 2,929
persons have been proceeded against
in connection with alleged cruelty to
animals under the various Protection of
Animals Acts, and, of these, 2,212 persons were convicted. I am sorry that
from the figures available it is not possible to differentiate between the various
classes of offences included under this
general heading of conviction for cruelty
to animals, and in order to obtain the
specific information which the hon. and
gallant Senator asked for it would be
necessary to make inquiries from every
police station and clerk of petty sessions
in Northern Ireland. This, of course,
could not be done in the short time there
has been since the question was put down
on the Order Paper. I think, however,
that from the figures which I have given
the House will be satisfied that the Royal
Ulster Constabulary are certainly most
active in endeavouring to prevent cruelty
to animals in any form whatsoever.
Major DOBBS: I beg to thank the
noble Lord for his answer. At the same
time I should like that these figures should
be obtained.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will see
what I can do. It will be obviously impossible to get the figures within the time.
this session is lasting, but I will supply
them to the hon. and gallant Gentleman
Protection of Hares Bill (S.).
Order for second reading read.
Mr. LESLIE: I desire, in the first
place, to express my regret that owing
to the approaching close of the session it
is necessary to ask the House to deal with
this matter at such short notice. The Bill,
however, of which I have the honour to
move the Second Reading is in itself so
short and so obviously required-and I
shall in the course of my speech read to
the House the whole of the sub-section to
which I have referred that I think there
will be no serious difficulty in following
the proposals in the Bill.
Public opinion in the Province is very
much exercised indeed as regards the
general question of coursing or hunting
captive hares. A recent occurrence at
Dungannon attracted widespread attention.
I was not present at that meeting,
and therefore cannot speak from my own
knowledge. Nor was I present at the
proceedings at the petty sessions court
which followed that meeting, and again I
cannot speak of my own knowledge. But
the occurrence did attract widespread
attention and something nearly approaching public indignation. Later than that,
I regret to say, in my own immediate
neighbourhood a meeting-the first under
the auspices of a newly established
association was held, and the date
selected for holding this opening meeting
was the eleventh of this month, and the
time selected was half-past eleven in the
morning. I was not there at that meeting either.
The announcement of the meeting
aroused, I think I may fairly say, strong
indignation in the town of Ballymoney
and the surrounding district. The rector
of the parish delivered on Sunday morning
a most eloquent sermon against the proposal. Two members of the Presbyterian
Church addressed their respective congregations-so I understand-and the parish
priest, using the term as it applies to
another communion, took, I am informed,
certain steps, which are just the steps
which I should have expected that
reverend gentleman to take from my
knowledge of his character. Nevertheless
649 Protection of Hares Bill-

14 NOVEMBER 1930
Second Reading
the meeting was held at Ballymoney, but
it is fair to state that according to information the number of hares killed did not
approach the number killed at Dungannon; also, I understand, the arrangements for the escape of the hares were
at least up to the average requirements.
It remains then to try to deal with the
public indignation I have spoken of. How
should Parliament deal with it? It is I
think obvious that the occasion does not
call for a general tirade against what are
called blood sports. It is not a case for
what might be described as reckless prohibition, because that would do more
harm than good. Unless one is reasonably careful to try to deal with the
problem on proper lines one might find
oneself doing something to interfere with
the normal proceedings of a pack of
harriers. That, I imagine, would find no
support either in this House or outside.
One has to keep clear of those things. It
seems to me that the situation can be
dealt with to some extent, and it is better
that an attempt should be made to deal
with it in a moderate way and, in the
first instance at all events, by a proposal
from a private Member. And the time
to which the session has advanced renders that course perhaps a little easier,
since it is obvious that if the House
honours me by reading the Bill a Second
time this afternoon it will be quite impossible for it to reach the Statute Book
during the current session. That will
give time for further consideration of the
matter by the House with something in
the nature of a concrete proposal before
them. It seemed to me that the production of this Bill might be regarded as a
contribution to the discussion of a point
in which much interest is taken.
Now, I have just said that the case does
not call for anything in the way of reckless prohibition, and it is therefore necessary to inquire in the first instance as to
whether the coursing and hunting of
hares, that is, captive hares the case to
which I am directing my speech-does at
the present time enjoy any special statutory privilege or statutory protection. I
think it cannot be denied that it does,
that is to say, a coursing meeting such as
was held at Dungannon is a lawful meeting. Prima facie it was a lawful meeting,
and it seems to me that the way to deal
with the subject is to remove the special
privilege or protection given to such meetings. If such meetings are to be conducted at all- as to that I say nothing,
for I do not like to attack the sport of
others, merely because it is a sport in
which I do not myself engage-I should
wish to leave that sport to be conducted
in accordance with the general law without favour and without affection.
What is the general law on that subject? I do not want to go further back
than the Act of 1911 which deals with the
protection of animals. As the House will
remember there were earlier statutes beginning, I think, in 1849, and a very
important one passed in 1900 dealing with
wild animals in captivity.
These were
repealed by the Consolidation Act of 1911,
which is the law both in Great Britain
and in Northern Ireland. The earlier
provisions of that Act-the first Section
are, I think, sufficiently well known.
They provide that if any person shall
cruelly ill-treat, torture, infuriate, or
terrify any animal or shall cause or procure that to be done and so on, or cause
any unnecessary suffering, that person
shall be guilty of an offence under the
Act.
The Section says "terrify any animal,"
and it is first of all necessary that we
should satisfy ourselves what is meant by
"animal" in that particular statute.
"Animal" for the purposes of the Act of
1911 is any domestic or captive animal.
We have thus to go a little further and
ascertain what is meant by the expression
"captive animal." "Captive animal”
means any animal, not being a domestic
animal, of whatsoever kind or species which
is in captivity or confinement or which
is subjected to any appliance or contrivance for the purpose of preventing its
escape from captivity or confinement.
Those are the words of the Act of 1911.
These were not found wholly sufficient
for their purpose, and after a good deal
of contest in the courts some amendments
were made, but I do not want to deal
with them particularly at the moment.
I wish to read now to the House the
third Sub-Section of the first Section of
which I have just given a résumé, because
that is the one with which we are particularly concerned. The sub-section
says:
Nothing in this section shall render
illegal any act lawfully done under the
Cruelty of Animals Act, 1876-
I may pause to remind the House that
this Act was not repealed in 1911. It is
an Act dealing solely with the somewhat
Protection of Hares Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Leslie]
unpleasant subject of scientific experiments on animals, a matter which is not
in dispute this afternoon.
Nothing in this Section . . . shall
apply to the commission or omission of
any act in the course of the destruction,
or the preparation for destruction, of
any animal as food for mankind, unless
such destruction or such preparation
was accompanied by the infliction of unnecessary suffering.
That is not in issue. Now we come to
paragraph (b), which states the subsection shall not apply:-
to the coursing or hunting of any
captive animal, unless such animal is
liberated in an injured, mutilated, or
exhausted condition; but a captive
animal shall not, for the purposes of
this section, be deemed to be coursed
or hunted before it is liberated--
and so on. An addition was made to
that sub-section in England in 1921 and
in Northern Ireland in 1926 under our
Protection of Animals (Miscellaneous
Provisions) Act of that year. The addition wasand a captive animal shall not be
deemed to be coursed or hunted within
the meaning of this sub-section if it is
coursed or hunted in an enclosed space
from which it has no reasonable chance
of escape.
even
That then is the law so far as it is
provided by statute. I am afraid, having
regard to the circumstances that have been
brought before us, this sub-section,
with this addition, does not go far enough,
and, therefore, I have tried to deal with
the matter in the Bill before the House.
In that Bill there will be found nothing
in the nature of direct prohibition, and
nothing in the nature of repeal. The proposal is to leave the matter to the ordinary
law under Sub-Section 1, of Section 1,
by removing the special privilege and I
propose leaving it, during the time this
Bill should be open for the consideration
of the public, to those interested and to
the lawyers to fight it out amongst themselves. They may mangle the Bill as they
please. The Bill will not squeal, though
the hares might; nor shall I, but I do
think we ought to be able to arrive at
something alike humane, reasonable, and
workable.
I
The House will note that I provide
that the Bill should not come into immediate operation. It is always right, I
think, in cases of this sort to leave a little
waiting time, and I have not provided
that the Bill should be permanent.
have limited it to one year, and if it
should pass this Session it could be kept
alive if necessary by the Expiring Laws
Continuance Act, or resuscitated by
special legislation. I do not think there
is anything more I need say except to
remind the House again that the effect
of the Bill, as drafted, will be that it
It will
will apply only to captive hares.
not apply to a wild hare, the sort of hare
that asserts its freedom in the wild common of nature. There is nothing here
to stop or put any hindrance in the way
of ordinary legitimate sport. All that is
done is to say that if this special form of
coursing or hunting is to be carried out
it shall be carried out in accordance with
the ordinary law which prohibits the infliction of unnecessary suffering on any
captive animal. I beg to move, "That
the Bill be now read a Second time."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think it
would be wise at this juncture to give
the point of view of the Government.
The Government have had a very short
time to consider in detail the effects of
the proposal contained in this Bill, but
I do not think I need go into these proposals as the hon. and learned Senator
who introduced the Bill has explained
them so very fully. The whole question
is one which requires very careful consideration, and the introduction of the
Bill will afford an opportunity of seeing
to what extent the feeling of the House
is in favour of legislation in this direction.
The Government sees no objection to give
this Bill a Second Reading, but thinks
that a definite expression of opinion as
to its views is hardly possible at the
present moment.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I should like
to back up my hon. and learned Friend
in his endeavour to produce a little cleaner
work in coursing in enclosed spaces. I,
therefore, will back him up very decidedly
in this Bill, because I do think there
certainly is a crying demand on the part
of a large number of people for better
workmanship in the matter of chasing
hares in enclosed spaces. There are, no
doubt, many cases where hares are put
into an enclosed space and coursed too
soon after they are put there. In other
words, are they given a really fair chance
Protection of Hares Bill-
Second Reading
14 NOVEMBER 1930
of escape? In many cases the hares are
so short a time in the enclosure before
the meeting that they are not capable of
doing their best on their four legs. Therefore, I have much pleasure in supporting
the Bill.
Mr. CAMPBELL: If this Bill passes
its Second Reading in this House it will
amount to greyhound legislation. I, myself, did not see the text of the Bill until
I actually arrived in the House to-day.
I do not know if any other hon. Senator
has had time to peruse the text of the
Bill before he arrived in the House. I
am rather surprised at the hon. and
learned Senator starting this hare so late
in the season. I think there used to be
braw coursing around Ballymoney district. No self-respecting man could be
callous in the infliction of unnecessary
suffering to hares or other animals. Hon.
Members will all agree with that, but
humane statutes have been passed in London and by our own Parliament here for
the express purpose of purging this sport
of any leaven of cruelty.
The law as it stands to-day permits
the coursing of hares provided they are
not liberated in an exhausted, mutilated,
or injured condition, and provided also
that if they are coursed in an enclosed
space they shall have a reasonable chance
of escape.
That is the law as it exists
to-day. The statutes in which these laws
are enshrined were passed for the express
purpose of making coursing as humane as
it can be made.
The characteristic of modern coursing is
a trial of swiftness between two greyhounds, and not the capture of the game
pursued. The object is not the capture
of the pursued game. The public interested in coursing have not a grain more
of cruelty in their composition than the
critics of coursing. Generations of gentlemen of character and eminence have
patronised the Waterloo Cup, the blue
ribband of the leash since 1836-almost
one hundred years ago.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: That is open
coursing.
Mr. CAMPBELL: How many
kills are
there in open coursing? How
kills
many
are there of hares in the Waterloo Cup?
The
Can any hon. Senator say that?
You cannot
proportion is fairly high.
have greyhounds stimulated into action
without a certain number of hares falling.
Why is this Bill limited to hares?
Is
it because of an incident at Dungannon?
Is it because of an incident, a very regrettable incident, at Ballymoney? I think it
is to be deplored that upon a very solemn
occasion and at a very solemn hour such
a thing should have happened. Are these
two episodes any justification for a massed
attack on coursing as a sport?
Is it possible this Bill is a surrender to clamour
outside the House? Lord Macaulay said
the Puritans hated bear baiting not because it gave pain to the bear but because
it gave pleasure to the spectators, not for
the sake of the bear, not because of the
cruelty, but on the grounds of the
pleasure it gave the spectators. It was
the sport, not the cruelty, that gave the
offence.
I think that legislation which would be
more to the point would be a Measure to
prevent the presentation of films of
American vice to the young in our towns
and country areas, films that are rotten
to the core.
I know that this winter many
children in Northern Ireland will be
suffering cruelly from want of proper food
and raiment, and my concern is more for
them. Is no other sport cruel?
Angling is cruelty to the fish. I am told
it is even dishonest because you attract
the fish and act fraudulently towards it
by attracting it towards your bait. Fowling is cruel. Nowadays we have got so
refined in our ideas in regard to animals
that I believe Pekingese pups, those puny
pet pups, have far more money spent on
them than human creatures.
I do not know of any genuine public
demand for this Bill. If it becomes law
in this Session I think the prospect of its
success is very remote. I believe it will
probably bring in its train disobedience
and futility. It will be a futile Measure.
It certainly would make illegal in Northern Ireland what is legal throughout
the length and breadth of Great Britain,
because our statutes on this sport are
exactly the same as those in Scotland and
England. We talk about walking step
by step, but in this matter we are going
far ahead.
Mr. McHUGH: I think the hon. and
learned Senator (Mr. Leslie) should have
attended these meetings himself before he
ventured to introduce a Bill of this kind.
Like many others he took his advice apparently from two Churches, from his
own Presbyterian Church, and then from
the parish priest--
655 Protection of Hares Bill-
THE SENATE

Second Reading
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: Not
Presbyterian.
I
Mr. McHUGH: I am sorry.
am not saying the hon. and learned
Senator is very religious. (Laughter).
What I am saying is that, apparently,
he acted on the advice of two clergymen, and the result of that interview which he had with them is what
he describes as this moderate Bill brought
in for our consideration. To my mind
this is a most extraordinary Bill, because
it is a bold attempt to crush out the coursing of hares in future in Northern Ireland. Everybody who knows anything
about coursing, and I myself have
attended I suppose 50 meetings, knows
that the hare must be enclosed before she
is raced, and this Bill introduced by the
hon. and learned Senator is to put a
stop to that altogether. In other words,
he will compel you to go to the mountains
and fields to look for your hare.
Colonel PAKENHAM: Hear, hear.
Mr. McHUGH: The hon. and gallant
Colorel cries "Hear, hear." I could
quite understand the hon. and learned
Senator if he challenged those gentlemen
who hunt, because there is just as much
cruelty in hunting as in coursing. I have
been present at hunting meetings. I am
sure the hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Pakenham) who supported the Bill is one
of the principal leaders of hunting.
saw cruelty at those meetings repeatedly.
I saw three, four and six dogs fighting
and tearing the hare in pieces, and yet the
hon. and gallant Member never said a
In
word about cruelty at those meets.
I
this Bill the small people are aimed at—
the poor people. They have no right to
have any sport in Northern Ireland. Nobody except the hon. and gallant Colonel
is entitled to have any sport. I cannot
accuse the hon. and learned Senator (Mr.
Leslie) because he himself admits that he
is not of a sporting nature. What does a
person who is not of a sporting nature
know about the matter? He admits himself he knows nothing about it, and yet
he comes here with this Amending Bill
and asks us to accept it to put a stop to
certain cruelty which he knows nothing
about.
That is the sum and substance of his
whole argument, which was supported by
the hon. and gallant Member. I will
support the Bill if the hon. and gallant
Colonel rises to the occasion, and says,
"We will put a stop to hunting and
coursing in Ireland." That would be
dealing out the law equally to rich and
poor alike. I will give him my support
if he puts a stop to that sport, otherwise
I have to say that this is a bold attempt
to crush out the only sport the poor people
have. In regard to the Ballymoney incident, let me point out that they import
the hares there. Certainly landlords in
Northern Ireland keep parks in which
they stock a large number of hares for
the purpose of coursing meetings. People
organising these meetings purchase these
hares, and any amount of cruelty will
take place because they are too long confined. The hon. and gallant Colonel was
good enough to say that the hares required a rest. My experience is that the
longer you confine a hare the more feeble
it becomes. If you want a hare to give
a splendid race confine her as short a
time as possible. If possible race her
before twelve hours from the time she is
caught. The longest race I ever saw did
not exceed 200 yards. You very seldom
see a race of 400 yards. There is always
a proper escape except at badly organised
meetings. At proper coursing meetings
there are always proper escapes so as to
prevent cruelty. I cannot at all support
the Bill.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
First of all I should like to say that I am
sorry that this Bill is introduced so late,
but the promoter has given a very good.
reason for that. There are one or two
points the hon. Member (Mr. McHugh)
made that I would like to refer to.
put the promoter of this Bill into the
community that I belong to.
He
Mr. McHUGH: Which I was sorry to
hear.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM:
That is the Presbyterian community. I
want to say that the hon. and learned
Senator does not belong to that body.
Mr. LESLIE: Hear, hear.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: As
to the hon. and gallant Member (Colonel
Pakenham), I was surprised to hear from
him that he was a leading man in the
hunting world. I presume if he did hunt.
657 Protection of Hares Bill
Agricultural Produce Bill- 658
14 NOVEMBER 1930
it would be with the Killultagh Harriers,
but I never saw him with that pack. I
never knew him to be with it. I think I
am right in saying that the hon. and
gallant Colonel does
does not hunt with
harriers at all. I for one would be very

but I think where cruelty is attached to
any sport it is not sportsmanship and
should be done away with. If the hares
in Dungannon were let out in an enclosure, and if the escape was big enough
for the dogs to get out after them, then
they had little chance of escape in a
strange country. That is not sport, and
I would like to see it regulated in a proper
way.
I
As to the Ballymoney incident, I know
nothing about it, but I do hope that if
there is any cruelty something will be
done to do away with it, but not do away
with really good sport. When I came in
here first I was against the Bill, because
I did not know whether it had to do with
the electric hare or the enclosed hare.
know what the Bill is now, and as it will
only run for a year it may be worth while

to put a rat in a barrel with a ferret, but
I would say that the hare should get a
chance.
Mr. LESLIE: I do not observe that
any other hon. Member desires to speak,
and I have little more to say. I must
thank the noble Lord, the Leader of the
House, for the manner in which he conferred the Government's benediction on
the purpose, at all events, of the Measure,
and I should like to thank my hon. and
gallant Friend (Colonel Pakenham), and
my right hon. Friend (Mr. Samuel Cunningham) for their support also. I turn
to those who disagree with me. The hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) said
that statutes have been passed for the controlling of these matters. I have only to
say about that that these statutes have
failed to achieve their purpose. (Hon.
Members: Hear, hear). The hon. and
learned Member also said that this kind
of coursing is necessary for trials of speed
between greyhounds. I am not aware
that the speed of greyhounds is in itself
a matter of very great public importance.
It seems to me that if the speed of greyhounds cannot be tested without infliction
of unnecessary suffering the time may
come when the maintenance of greyhounds
should be reconsidered.
Mr. CAMPBELL: What about staghounds?
Mr. LESLIE: I will deal with that
in a moment. It was stated that the
episodes I related are isolated incidents.
They are not. I had this Bill in my
mind before I heard of the proceedings
at Dungannon. The Bill is not a surrender to popular clamour. I am not in
favour of surrendering to anybody about
anything, but it is an admission that
public opinion is moving in a certain way.
I certainly do not desire to deprive anybody of honest pleasure. The general
trend of the arguments against the Bill
may be summed up in the extraordinary
proposal that two wrongs or any number
of wrongs added together make one right.
That is really the purport of the arguments against the Bill.
to
I may further state that there is no
religion in the matter at all. I do not
think religion should be brought into the
matter particularly. If anyone wants to
pursue that subject they may refer if
they like
Coleridge's "Ancient
Mariner," where they will find quite a
remarkable sermon preached on a text
which is not in Holy Writ, and if they
prefer to take their instruction from
prose they can refer to Jeremy Bentham.
As to the matter of staghounds, I would
venture to remind the House that the
Bill at all events is an endeavour to keep
to the point which is the coursing or
hunting of hares. The other matters I
need not enter into. I venture to hope
that the House will give the Bill a Second
Reading.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Bill accordingly read a Second time.

was
Viscount CHARLEMONT: On the
Committee
Leslie
stage Senator
promised that at the next stage further
