As already explained, there is no immediate intention of making any regulations regarding the grading of meat, but
in view of developments in this direction.
across Channel it might at any time be
necessary that the Ministry should be able
659 Agricultural Produce Bill

THE SENATE
Motor Vehicles Bill-
[Viscount Charlemont]
to make grading regulations, so far at
least as meat for export to Great Britain
is concerned. Grades will naturally depend, to some extent at all events, on the
grades in force in Great Britain at a
particular time. Detailed information
regarding progress in this matter across
Channel is given in a recent report of an
inter-departmental committee on the
grading and marking of beef, and if the
hon. Senator wishes I will send him a
copy. I can, of course, assure the hon.
Senator that any regulations on this subject would only be made in close consultation with the interests concerned.
With regard to Clause 4 of the Bill,
Senator Leslie inquired on the Committee
stage what fee would be charged where
the number of carcases was 200 or slightly
over that figure.
The answer is that the fee will be £10
for 200 carcases or over. As already explained a very considerable concession
was made when it was agreed to give
licences for under 200 pigs at £5, that is,
half price. Anything in the nature of a
sliding scale would raise administrative
difficulties, and would also cut across the
principle that this Measure is to be substantially self-supporting. I regret,
therefore, that it is not possible to make
any further concession on the point raised.
I beg to move, "That this Bill be now
read the Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: I am thankful to the
noble Lord for saying he will supply me
with a copy of the report on the grading
and marking of beef. I shall be happy
to receive it in due course. When the
noble Viscount refers to consultation with
all interests as regards the grading of
meat, I do venture to hope that all
interests will be consulted, not only the
large handlers and middlemen but somebody representing the farmers or the
farmers themselves, who may have some
valuable information to give. As regards
the Bill itself, I regret, and I cannot
refrain from saying so, the uncompromising attitude adopted in this matter, and
I fear for a sympathetic administration
of the Bill when it becomes an Act.
However I do not want to dwell too much
on that now. If I may quote an old
historic saying, "I dislike not the match,
but I hate the manner of wooing." I do
hope the Bill will be administered in a
kindly way, and may produce some of
the benefits which its authors seem to
anticipate.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I rise, not to oppose
the Third Reading of the Bill, but to
express my regret that Amendments proposed in the Committee stage with a view
to bettering the Bill were not accepted by
the Government. I hope the Bill will
work out smoothly, but I feel that some
of the smaller men will be hit by some
of the provisions. However, the uncompromising attitude referred to by the hon.
and learned Senator must be expected
here because outside the ranks of the
minority the smaller farmers have no
direct representation in this House.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the

Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for the House to be put into Committee read.

On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
[Viscount BANGOR in the Chair].
CLAUSE 1-(Provisions as to drivers'
licences granted in Great Britain).
*
(1) If the Minister of Home Affairs
certifies that satisfactory provision is
made by the law of Great Britain for
the granting of licences to drive motor
cars, it shall be lawful for the holder of
such licence to drive and be employed
in driving in Northern Ireland a motor
car of any class or description which he
is authorised by that licence to drive,
and which he is not disqualified for
driving under the Motor Vehicles and
Road Traffic.
*
*
(4) Nothing in this Section shall
authorise any person to drive or be employed in driving a public service
vehicle in Northern Ireland unless he
holds such licence as is required by subsection (1) of Section 12 of the principal
Act.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
14 NOVEMBER 1930
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 1, page 1, line 9, after the word
"Britain" ("the law of Great Britain")
to insert the words "or the Irish Free
State."
For the reasons that I advanced on the
Second Reading of the Bill I beg to submit this Amendment to the sympathetic
consideration of the Committee. The object of the Amendment is to ensure to
motorists belonging to the rest of Ireland,
outside the Six Counties, the same facilities with regard to drivers' licences as are
afforded to drivers holding licences.
granted in Great Britain. I think it
would be a good business proposition, and
would advantage and benefit our Northern
motorists. We may be told that we have
no reciprocal arrangements with the Free
State. Well, let us begin.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government cannot accept this Amendment.
The object of the Clause is to give
statutory effect to an informal arrangement which has been in existence for some
time. There is no corresponding arrangement with the Irish Free State and the
practical difficulties in making any such
arrangement with the Irish Free State
would be very great indeed. The Irish
Free State being a Dominion comes under
the international circulation arrangements, and there is no evidence that the
Free State desires any other arrangement.
The Free State requires a driver holding a North of Ireland licence to take
out a Free State driving licence when
driving in the Free State, and similarly
a person living in the Irish Free State
holding a licence in that country is required, when he drives in either Northern
Ireland or Great Britain to take out a
licence in those countries.
Amendment negatived.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, in Clause 1, page 2, line 26, after
the word "authorise" ("Nothing in this
Section shall authorise'), to insert the
words:
"(a) a person resident in any part of
Ireland to drive or to be employed in
driving a motor car in Northern
Ireland unless he holds such licence
as is required by Section three of the
principal Act; or."
The object of this Amendment is to
prevent the Clause being used for the
purpose of evading the licence duty of
10/- which will come into force in Northern Ireland on the 1st January next.
As the Clause at present stands, especially having regard to the provision which
is being made in the British Regulations
for the issue of licences to persons not
resident in Great Britain, it would be
possible for a person in Northern Ireland
to obtain a British driving licence, which,
under the Clause in its present form,
could be used over here and obviate the
necessity of taking out a 10/- licence here.
The Clause is designed to carry out an
arrangement to facilitate drivers holding
licences in Northern Ireland who are
going temporarily to Great Britain, and
conversely to facilitate drivers holding
British licences who are coming temporarily to Northern Ireland, but it was
never the intention that persons resident
in either country should drive in their
own countries under licences issued by the
other country. In view of the lower
licence fee in Great Britain there would,
of course, be no inducement to people
resident in Great Britain to take out
Northern Ireland licences to enable them
to drive in their own country, but in the
case of Northern Ireland, the higher
licence fee might very possibly induce
people resident in Ireland to try to avoid
the higher fee by taking out British
licences, and, accordingly, this Amendment provides that residents in Ireland
are not to drive in Northern Ireland
unless they hold Northern Ireland
licences.
It will be observed that the Amendment deals with persons not merely resident in Northern Ireland, but in any part
of Ireland, and the reason for this is that
there are many people living near the
Border who hold both Northern Ireland
and Irish Free State licences. It is obviously undesirable to put a resident in
the Irish Free State who holds two
licences in a better position than the
resident in Northern Ireland who also
holds the two licences. If it were not for
this Amendment it would be possible for
the Irish Free State resident to obtain
a British licence to drive which would be
valid in Northern Ireland, and thus create
a differentiation in his favour.
Amendment agreed to.
Question proposed, "That the Clause,
as amended, stand part of the Bill."
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Mr. LESLIE: Before we part with this
Clause there are two points i should like
to refer to. The very opening words of
the first sub-section are: "If the Minister
of Home Affairs certifies that satisfactory
provision is made by the law of Great
Britain for the granting of licences to
drive motor cars," and so on. I presume
I am correct in assuming that when the
Minister has given his certificate he will
publish an announcement of that certificate in the "Belfast Gazette," and therefore there will be no necessity for making
any provision in the Bill for dealing with
a matter that will only happen once.
should like an assurance that that certificate will be published in some way that
will draw the attention of the public to
it, and, of course, as a Member of Parliament, I suppose I must presume that the
best way of bringing matters to people's
attention is to publish an announcement
in the "Belfast Gazette."
I
The second point is we are told twice,
I think, that under certain circumstances
people are to forward their licences to the
Minister of Home Affairs. We are not
told what the Minister of Home Affairs
is going to do with these licences when he
gets them. There is, as the Committee
is aware, similar provision arranged to
meet the converse case in the Statute
passed by the Parliament of the United
Kingdom.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will give
the assurance desired by the hon. and
learned Senator in the first part of his
question.
With regard to the second
part of the question, I shall give him the
information asked for at a later date.
Clause, as amended, ordered to stand
part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 2-(Conveyance of mails by
public service vehicles).
*
*
*
*
*
*
The remuneration for any service performed in pursuance of this section shall
be such amount, not exceeding the
charges payable for the conveyance of
parcels and passengers, respectively, as
shall be agreed upon between the Postmaster-General and the owner, or, in
default of agreement, determined by
the Tribunal established by section
seven of the Motor Vehicles and Road
Traffic Act (Northern Ireland), 1929;
and any matter of difference arising
under the proviso to sub-section (1) of
this section shall also be determined by
the said Tribunal. The said Tribunal
shall, for the purpose of determining
any matter under this sub-section, have
the same powers as they have in the case
of a matter within their jurisdiction
under section seven of the said Act.
Mr.
*
*
*
*
*
*
LESLIE : I beg to move, in
Clause 2, page 3, line 18, to leave out
from the word "amount" ("shall be such
amount') to the word "as" in line 20.
I put this Amendment on the Paper
suggesting the omission of certain words
in order that I might elicit a little explanation. I have a doubt as to whether
it is quite fair that mail bags should be
charged at the same rate as other parcels
of corresponding size and weight. The
view might be taken that they should
be charged at a lower rate.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government cannot accept this Amendment,
as it appears to them that the limitation
is by no means unreasonable.
In the first place it will be noticed that
these mails are only to be carried as an
incidental to the ordinary services, and
that the Postmaster-General is not entitled to compel the operator to make
special runs. In fact it is clear that the
obligation to carry mails is to be subordinated to the main purpose of the vehicles,
namely, the carrying of passengers.
The rates for carrying ordinary parcels.
would, of course, have regard to the fact
that traffic of this nature is of a spasmodic
and casual character, whereas, on the
other hand the carrying of mails, where
such is arranged would be steady business going on day after day. It is therefore probable that it would be unreason.
able to allow for steady traffic of this
kind as high rates as would be allowed for
merely casual traffic. It is difficult to
conceive that any hardship is likely to
result, nor has the Government received
any representations on behalf of the omnibus interests which would lead them to
think that any injustice is likely to arise.
For that reason I cannot accept the
Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I ask leave to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 3 (Expenditure by local
authorities on traffic signals).
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Motor Vehicles Bill-
11 NOVEMBER 1930
Committee.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: In moving
that this Clause stand part of the Bill,
I have to reply to the hon. and gallant
Senator (Major Dobbs) who made the
suggestion that, where traffic signals are
erected by a local authority, notices
should be placed in such a way that a
warning would be given to motorists to
look out for a traffic signal.
This is a suggestion which will receive.
careful consideration, and when the more
extensive erection of traffic signals now
in contemplation is undertaken, it will
be carefully gone into.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
CLAUSE 4 (Taking motor car without
owner's consent or other authority to be
an offence) ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move as a
new Clause, after Clause 4-
(1) It shall not be lawful for more
than one person, in addition to the
driver, to be carried on any two-wheeled
motor cycle, nor shall it be lawful for
any such one person to be so carried
otherwise than sitting on a proper seat
securely fixed to the cycle behind the
driver's seat.
(2) If any person is carried on any
such cycle in contravention of the provisions of this Section, the driver of
the cycle shall be liable in the case of
the first conviction to a fine not exceeding five pounds, and in the case of
a second or subsequent conviction to a
fine not exceeding ten pounds.
The object of this new Clause is not in
any way to affect the ordinary use of a
motor bicycle. It in no way interferes
with the proper and reasonable use of a
motor bicycle. It simply seeks to restrict
the pastime of pillion riding, and the two
Clauses which I venture to submit to the
Committee would assimilate the law in
Northern Ireland to the law which now
exists in Great Britain, because these two
Clauses have been copied almost word for
word from the Act passed in 1930 by the
Parliament of the United Kingdom.
Therefore, if they are rejected, there will
be one law and one practice in regard to
pillion riding throughout the length and
breadth of Great Britain, and a different
law and a different practice throughout
the length and breadth of Northern Ireland. It is perfectly legal under this
Clause, as it is in England, to drive a
motor bicycle with one person sitting on
it, but it is wholly illegal to carry more
than one person on a motor bicycle, and
it is illegal to carry one person otherwise
seated than on a proper safe seat. I
think that is a wise and salutary provision.
All who have ordinary experience of
road traffic will agree that pillion riding
is often indulged in by giddy and thoughtless youths. They go out on these motor
cycles to show off. They go scorching
through our streets and careering through
our country roads, and they are a source
of very great danger to motor drivers and
to themselves. This is especially so when
it comes to going round a bend in a
country road where you have three people
seated on a motor cycle not thinking of a
motor car coming round the bend in the
opposite direction. We do sometimes
find in Northern Ireland very deplorable
results from this practice. I remember
myself one case that occurred on a road
very convenient to Belfast, where three
persons were seated on a motor cycle
which came into collision with a public
service vehicle. As a result, two of them
were killed and a third lost a foot and was
crippled for life. I ask the Committee.
whether we would not be wise to bring
our law into uniformity with the law in
Great Britain for the protection, to a
large extent, of the motoring public, and
to an even greater extent of these young
people themselves. I beg to move.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I rise to support this new Clause. I think that as it is
already incorporated in a Bill in England
the Government should accept it. If the
Government are not prepared to accept
any Amendment on any Bills that are
brought before this House there does not
seem to be much use in our coming here
at all. I think this is an Amendment
that should be accepted, because more
accidents are due to this class of motor
vehicle than to any other. A great many
accidents are due to pillion riding, which
is even more dangerous when there are
more than one or two persons riding on
the motor cycle. I admit that for the
ordinary individual going to his work it
is a convenience for him to be allowed to
take a rider behind him, but when he goes
out at night with two or three persons
behind him he becomes a danger to traffic.
I am more than pleased to see that the
hon. and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell)
supports the minimum fine in this case.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
Mr. DORMAN: I support the Amendment, and I trust the Government will
accept it. I speak as one who has had
half a century's experience of cycling,
having ridden a tandem and also a motor
cycle for some years, and I can assure the
Committee that any person in charge of
a motor cycle with a person pillion riding
behind him has to be accustomed to the
method of balancing the machine of which
he has control. I believe the Government
will be doing the right thing if they take
steps to prevent this practice of more
than one person being carried on the
pillion of a motor cycle. It is impossible
for the person in charge of the
machine to properly control it unless he is accustomed to the method
of balancing of the individual behind him, especially when turning a
corner. I hope the Government will
accept the Amendment in the interests of
the foolish young persons concerned, as
well as in the interests of the general
public.
Mr. GLENDINNING: There has been
so much said in support of this Amendment that I do not wish to add to it.
At the same time I think the first paragraph of the suggested new Clause might
be improved by inserting after the word
"seat" the words "provided by the manufacturers for the purpose." That would
prevent the owner of a motor cycle providing some sort of a temporary seat
himself and claiming that it was a proper
seat under this Clause. I thoroughly
approve of this new Clause, and I think
its adoption would lead to a decrease in
the loss of life through motor cycle accidents. If there had been a provision in
the Bill to prohibit motor cycles and side
cars I should have felt strongly inclined
to support it, because of all the types of
motor vehicles on our roads I think the
motor cycle and side car is the most
dangerous and causes more accidents than
any other vehicle. The system of pillion
riding has led to great loss of life on our
roads, and I have very great pleasure in
supporting the Amendment with the suggestion I have already made, namely, that
after the word "seat" the words "provided by the manufacturers for the purpose" be inserted.
Mr. LESLIE: It is very interesting to
hear a discussion on the proposed new
Clause, but there are one or two matters
to which I think I ought to direct the
attention of the Committee. First of all,
may I say that the title of the Bill before
us and the title of the English Road
Traffic Act, 1930, which has been referred
to, are not quite the same. I do not say
more than that. Secondly, the Clause
which it is proposed to insert in the Bill
is not the same as Section 16 of the English Measure. There is a difference between them. I think it means only two
words, but they are very important words,
and if we did adopt this new Clause we
should not be assimilating our law to that
of Great Britain. I am perhaps a little
surprised that the hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Campbell) should attack
rather heavily on this occasion a sport in
which he does not himself participate. I
thought perhaps he would not have done
that, but on the whole I am not very much
in favour of this new Clause.
I hope the hon. Senator opposite (Mr.
Glendinning) will forgive me if I say that
his suggestion to provide that the only
seat to be recognised should be that provided by the manufacturers would be a
little unfair perhaps to our home industries, and a little restrictive on the users
of motor cycles. The whole subject was,
I think, discussed in 1926, when the first
of our Motor Traffic Bills was before us,
and one of our friends on my left objected
very strongly to the suggested prohibition
of pillion riding. I think it was stated
at that time on behalf of the Ministry
that it was thought that if the public
interest necessitated any action on the
subject the Ministry possessed powers to
deal with it by Regulations. I dissented
from that view, and I think at that time
the only powers quoted were those conferred by the Act of 1896. I would not
care to make the same assertion to-day,
because we have since passed other Acts
dealing with motor traffic. On the whole,
I do not think it would be quite right to
insert a prohibitive Clause of this
character in the Bill before us. I do not
think there has been sufficient public demand for it, and I am not sure that it
would be quite fair to the users of motor
cycles. I am certainly not satisfied that
to insert a Clause even only slightly
different to the English Clause would be
an advisable thing to do.
Mr. CAMPBELL: Dealing with the
point raised by Senator Leslie, the words
in the new Clause I have proposed are
an exact reproduction of the Section in
the English Act with the exception that
I have omitted the words "sitting astride."
In that respect my new Clause allows &

little more latitude than the English Section, but in substance it is the same thing
exactly.
because the father could not be responsible
for the unauthorised act of his own son.
The Bill does not extend to cases
of
that kind which might often
arise, especially in the case of a
man who Owns a motor car and
has a family of young sons or daughters.
The son or daughter would be no mark
for any damages, and the father would be
immune, and now that the very important
principle of compulsory insurance against
third party risks has been introduced, I
think we should embrace cases of the kind
I have foreshadowed.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The objection to the Clause proposed by the hon.
and learned Senator (Mr. Campbell) is
that it would not prevent pillion riding,
but would merely prevent the use of a
motor cycle by three persons instead of
However, in view of the expressions
of opinion in support of the Amendment
I would not like to refuse it absolutely at
the present stage. I think it is possible
that a Clause might be produced before
the next stage of the Bill to prevent more
than one person, in addition to the driver,
riding a motor cycle. Perhaps the hon.
and learned Senator would consider withdrawing his Amendment in the meantime.
Of course, I cannot promise definitely that
the Government would accept such a
Clause, but at any rate we would give it
consideration between now and the Report
stage.
Mr. CAMPBELL: On that assurance
I beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 5 (Users of motor cars to
be insured against third-party risks).
(1) Subject to the provisions of this
Part of this Act, it shall not be lawful
for any person to use, or to cause or
permit any other person to use, a motor
car on a public highway unless there is
in force in relation to the user of the
motor car by that person or that other
person, as the case may be, such a policy
of insurance or such a security in respect of third-party risks as complies.
with the requirements of this Part of
this Act.
""
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move, in
Clause 5, page 5, line 19, after the
word "person," to insert, "or a member of the family of that person.'
The object of the Amendment is to guard
against a hardship that might be caused
in a case where the father of a family is
insured against third party risks.
A
member of his family, without his knowledge, might take out his car and during
the use of the car by that member of the
family an accident might happen. In
that event there would be no libility on
the part of the father and, consequently,
on the part of the insurance company,
Major DOBBS: There is one question
I would like to ask. There is nothing in
this Clause that actually bears upon it,
but at the same time I would like to get
information on the point. It is this. If
the owner of a motor car insurance
against third party risks is involved in an
accident, probably everything goes all
right and the insurance company is going
to pay. Then it is found that the driver
is not in possession of a driving licence,
or perhaps it is out of date. I understand
that in some cases the insurance company
has then repudiated all liability. Under
the system of compulsory third party
insurance such a thing as that would
be terribly hard on the third party who
was injured and was left without any
security for his damages.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Even if
the Government were prepared to accept
the principle which the hon. and learned
Senator has in mind in putting down this
Amendment, I hardly think that the
words which he suggests would appropriately carry out that purpose, but the
principle which he is seeking to establish
is one which the Government could not
possibly accept in this Bill. He is really
seeking to establish an entirely new
principle in the legal system of this
country. It is not the object of this Bill
to increase the liabilities of motorists to
third parties whom they may injure, but
to ensure, so far as possible, that where
the motorist has incurred a liability he
will be able to meet that liability.
Hon. Members must realise that the Bill
simply compels motorists to insure against.
their legal liabilities, but the hon. and
learned Senator now proposes that the
owner of a motor car shall be liable not
only for the acts of himself, his servant,
or agent, but for the acts of some person
who is using his car without any authority
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
from him. This would be a revolutionary change in the law, and the Committee will realise still more the drastic
nature of the hon. and learned Senator's
proposal if I point out that the car owner
would be liable, not merely if the car was
used by a member of his family without
his authority, but in contravention of his
instructions. If a member of an owner's
family uses the car without being properly
covered by insurance, he will be liable to
severe penalty, and if the owner permits
him to use the car without seeing that
there is proper insurance, the owner will
be liable to severe penalties. Beyond this
protection the Government is unable to
go, and it would be entirely impracticable
to accept the hon. and learned Senator's
Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg leave to withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I beg
to move to insert: "Notwithstanding anything in the Act, the authorities are not
to issue a motor licence until they are
satisfied that the applicant is insured in
accordance with this Act."
The CHAIRMAN: May I ask the right
hon. Gentleman what Clause he is speaking on?
Mr. SAMUEL
Clause 5.
CUNNINGHAM:
The CHAIRMAN: I am afraid I have
not got this Amendment.
to
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I am
sorry I had not time to put the Amendment on the Paper. The reason I bring
it forward is that I realise there is a
great deal of doubt as to who would suffer
in certain cases. Third-party insurance,
I take it, is the most important part of
the Bill. Now, if I insure my car I get
a certificate from the insurance company
say I am insured. Without that certificate the licensing authority will not issue
a licence. My insurance may run out on
30th April. The police or the authorities
ask my driver for the licence and for the
certificate of insurance. They may not
ask for the certificate until some days into
May, and an accident may happen to me
on 1st May. Shall I be covered? We are
sitting here and passing a Bill specially
for third-party insurance, and we say the
licence will not be issued without proper
insurance, but that insurance runs off before the licence runs off. It is just possible that I might let my insurance cease
on 30th April and an accident might happen on 1st May. I might be a man of
straw. I would have nothing to pay, and
the innocent third party would suffer.
The Act would be of none effect.
What I think should be done is this.
When I go to get my licence I should hand
in a certificate from the insurance company to say I am covered for the time on
the licence. The insurance company then
issues me a licence, and as long as that
licence runs I have a third-party policy
against it. I might not want to insure
for the full year, but in a case like this
where we are protecting a third party it
is not the insured or the insurance company that should be taken into consideration. It should be the third party, the
public on the street.
I might give some other illustrations.
For instance, it is just possible that I
might procure that insurance from the
insurance company by fraud. I might fill
up my application form fraudulently, but
some innocent person would have to suffer.
I think when the insurance company have
handed their certificate to the licensing
authority that should be sufficient. If
some person is to suffer I think it should
be the insurance company. They could
easily insure within themselves against a
case like that, and I think they ought to
do it and not the innocent public. As I
said before this Bill is entirely brought
for the sake of the public, and I think
that as far as possible they should be
protected.

Government do not see that it is of some

Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: Before this
Clause is disposed of I should like to
answer some questions raised on the previous stages of the Bill. Senator Leslie
inquired as to invalid carriages which are
exempt from the provisions of the Bill.
He appeared to think that an invalid carriage might include such things as ambulances, but an invalid carriage is defined
in Clause 5, Sub-Clause (5) of the Bill to
be-a motor-car the weight of which unladen does not exceed 5 cwt., and which
is specially designed and constructed for
the use of persons suffering from some
physical defect or disability and used
solely by such persons. It is clear from
this definition that the vehicle is one
which is unlikely to do any damage to
third parties on the road.
Several Members expressed some alarm
as to whether insurance companies would
not be in a position to raise rates unduly
as a result of everyone being compelled to
insure, and it was suggested that a
tribunal should be set up to regulate rates.
When it was decided to devise a scheme
requiring compulsory insurance, it was
desired to limit interference with the insurance business to the smallest possible
extent, and it must be realised that this
scheme is essentially an experiment, and
that, therefore, it is desirable to hasten
slowly. The Government do not think
that there is any probability that insurance companies will unduly raise rates.
In the negotiations with the insurance
companies, the companies very reasonably
would not give any undertaking that the
rates would not be increased, but they
did undertake that they would only raise
the rates if experience showed that it was
necessary. Hon. Senators are, doubtless,
aware there is extremely keen competition amongst the insurance companies,
and there are many powerful companies
which are not tariff companies.
Furthermore, the Government of Northern Ireland have had a scheme of compulsory insurance of public service
vehicles in operation for over three years,
and there has been no indication that
insurance companies have taken advantage of that scheme to raise rates uduly.
On the contrary, there has been distinct
evidence of the desire of the companies
to base their rates on claim experience,
and it is within the knowledge of the
Ministry of Home Affairs that, where
the claims experience in respect of an
operator has shown an improvement, the
insurance companies voluntarily reduced
the premium rates.
The Ministry has no reason to suppose
that an equally reasonable attitude will
not be adopted by the companies in respect of the vehicles which are now made
the subject of compulsory insurance. It is
obviously desirable to await developments
in this matter, and to avoid setting up
complicated machinery which, in all probability, will not be required. The Committee may rest assured that this is a
matter that will be carefully watched, but
in the meantime, the Government do not
think that there is any necessity to deal
with it though I am now giving the information as requested.
Mr. LESLIE: It was I who invited the
noble Marquis to look into the question
of invalid carriages. I think I must
admit that the weight of even the kind of
ambulance which I had in mind could
scarcely be got down to five cwt. The
intention of the definition must be something rather more like a bath chair which
an unfortunate sufferer can operate for
himself. But my particular point was that
in the English Act the definition is exactly
the same, but the speed is limited to 20
miles an hour. That is the point to which
I rather wanted attention directed, and
that has not been answered. Perhaps
further inquiry might be made. Probably the noble Viscount will find on
further inquiry that such a vehicle as is
now allowed could not go at twenty miles
an hour even down hill, but still it is
possible, and I would like some further
information if he could get it.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I will get
the information.
Mr. LESLIE: Thank you.
Clause ordered to stand part of the Bill.
CLAUSE 6 (Requirements in respect
of policies).
*

"(4) A policy shall be of no effect
for the purposes of this Part of this
Act unless and until there is delivered
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Amendment
THE SENATE
by the insurer to the person by whom
the policy is effected a certificate (in this
Part of this Act referred to as "a
certificate of insurance ") in the prescribed form and containing such particulars of any conditions subject to
which the policy is issued and of any
other matters as may be prescribed,
and different forms and different particulars may be prescribed in relation
to different cases or circumstances.
"(5) In this Part of this Act the expression "policy of insurance" includes
a covering note."
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in Clause
6, page 8, line 7, at the beginning of Subclause (4) to insert "Save as prescribed
by regulations to be made under this
Act."
The object of this Amendment, with its
corollary in the Amendment to the next
Clause, which hon. Members will see upon
the Paper, is to try to clear up, if I can,
what is to happen to a man in regard to
the necessary intermediate periods. If a
man goes into a motor establishment and
buys a motor car, or if he buys a secondhand car, he will be required not only to
have a policy of insurance but also a
certificate to say that he has got it before
he can drive the car. What a man does,
I imagine, when he buys a car is to ring
up an insurance company, or their agent,
and say, "I have bought such and-such-a
motor car. Hold me covered; and the insurance company promptly telephones
back to say, "We are posting form for
your proposal form to-night." Actually
the filling up of the form of proposal and
getting the certificate all takes time, and
it is not really fair that a man should be
deprived of the use of his motor car while
these formalities are being completed. It
may be that the period occupied while this
is being done will be covered by regulations made by the Ministry under the Bill,
but I am not satisfied that the power does
exist. I am sure, however, that there is
no intention or desire to irritate the unfortunate motorist. I have, therefore,
put down an Amendment in the fewest
possible words I could think of. I hope,
at all events, we will be able to obtain
some explanation of how it is proposed to
deal with the circumstances I have outlined. In the meantime, I beg to move
the Amendment as it stands on the Order
Paper.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This
Amendment appears to be directed to
providing for the case which the hon.
and learned Senator raised on the Second
Reading, namely, the case where a man
buys a new car; and he appeared to think
that there might be some difficulty in the
purchaser obtaining the necessary certificate of insurance before he brought the
car home. He, therefore, proposes to
give the Ministry power to make regulations to provide for such a case, but it
would appear that the words which he
proposes to insert would give
give the
Ministry very extensive powers of altering the provisions of the Bill.
It does not seem to the Government
that any difficulty arises in the case which
he has in mind. Sub-clause (5) of the
Clause lays down that the expression,
"policy of insurance," includes what is
known as a cover note. This is the
document which is issued by the insurance company holding the insured
covered pending the issue of the policy.
and a regulation will be made corresponding to a regulation already made in
England to the effect that every such
cover note shall contain a certificate on
the back thereof to the effect that the
cover note is issued in accordance with the
provisions of Part II of this Bill.
Clearly, no sensible person will want to
take delivery of a car and to drive it before it is covered by insurance, and the
course that has been indicated will provide an easy and convenient method of
dealing with the matter.
Inquiries have been made, and it has
been ascertained that it is possible to
have a car registered, taxed and insured
within two or three hours, and, therefore, it is very unlikely that any inconvenience would arise. In any event
it is undesirable that a car should be
driven on the roads, even for a
short
time, without being adequately covered
by insurance. I think the Amendment
was moved more for the purpose of eliciting that information than with any
desire to alter the Bill. Therefore, 1
am not saying whether I accept it or
not.
Mr. LESLIE: As we now have the
definite assurance that I wanted that the
expression, "covering note" in the Bill
is held by the Government to meet the
point there is no object in pressing the
Amendment, especially as it would
create a difference between ourselves and
Great Britain, and might possibly cause
inconvenience. Therefore, I ask leave
to withdraw the Amendment.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.

Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question proposed, "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I have some
further information to impart. Senator
Pakenham was anxious to know the
position when an owner owned more than
one car covered by one policy of insurance. Provision will be made by
regulations under the Act for the purpose
of obviating any inconvenience in this
connection. Probably it will be necessary
to arrange for the issue of a separate
certificate in respect of each vehicle.
It
will also be necessary to arrange for a
number of certificates, where the policy
covers a certain number of vehicles, without specifying the particular vehicles.
Such a policy is usually issued in the
case of a person owning a large fleet of
vehicles, all of which are not in use at
any particular time.
On Question, Motion agreed to.

Mr. LESLIE: I desire to move an
Amendment in Clause 9 which is not
upon the Paper, but of which I think
you, Mr. Chairman, my Lord, have got
a copy. It will come on page 10, line 13,
at the end of the Clause, and I may say

the new Clause which, will
you
I
originally proposed to move after Clause
17 on the other side of the Paper. There
is some little difficulty-a difficulty in my
own mind at all events-as to the proper
constitution of a court of summary jurisdiction for hearing charges dealt with
under Section 8 of the original Act of
1926, and it seems to me that the same
difficulty may arise under this Bill. On

any avoidable difference in the method of

of an Act of Parliament. I think, therefore, if we insert the words which I have
on the Paper here we shall be bringing
an offence under this Clause exactly into
line with the Section of the principal Act
which deals with the constitution of courts
and matters of that sort. If I remember
rightly it is Section 21.
If the Government are not prepared to
accept this Amendment I hope they will
take the opportunity of saying quite definitely that they are advised that in any
case arising out of-probably I should
hardly say arising out of but concerning
-such things as red rear lights, in order

point has been questioned. I understand

CLAUSE 10 (Requirements as to production of certificate of insurance or of
security.
(2) If in any case where, owing to the
presence of a motor car on a public
highway, an accident occurs involving
personal injury to another person, the
driver of the motor car does not at the
time give his name and address
and the name and address of the
owner of the motor car and produce his
certificate to a police constable or to
some person who, having reasonable
grounds for so doing, has so required,
the driver shall as soon as possible, and
in any case within twenty-four hours
of the occurrence of the accident, report
the accident at a police station or to a
police constable and thereupon give his
name and address and the name and
address of the owner of the motor car
and produce his certificate, and if he
fails to do so he shall be guilty of an
offence under the principal Act.

*
*
*
*
Mr. LESLIE: I beg to move in page
10, line 36, leave out from "possible"
("as soon as possible') to report
("report the accident'') in line 37.
The Clause before us follows as usual
the corresponding Clause in the English
Act. The reference is Section 40 in the
English Act and Clause 10 in the Bill
before us.
It seems to me that the Clause
of an
like some other Clauses in the Bill still
following the Sections of the English Act
assumes that the motorist is necessarily a
criminal of the worst type and is to be
treated rather more severely than the
ordinary ticket-of-leave man. I imagine
that this is only a temporary phase on the
part of the promoters of the English Act
and may pass off. My point is whether it
is really reasonable to require a driver as
soon as possible within twenty-four hours
accident to report to
a police
station. Is that a reasonable requirement
putting aside the question as to whether
the unfortunate man may be so damaged
himself that he cannot report to anyone?
Ought we to insert those words or rely on
the common-sense of the police and magistrate? You must remember that the
magistrate will be a resident magistrate.
I am sorry to say that there are some
resident magistrates operating in Northern Ireland whose views as regards the
criminality of motorists are rather strict.
I should not myself choose a resident
magistrate as my tribunal. Can we rely
on that enactment, as it will be, having
reasonable regard to the liberty of the
subject and his relief from unnecessary
inconvenience? In order to obtain information, and to elicit the views of others, I
beg to move.
It is not
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
unreasonable to insist that the driver of
a car should report any accident as soon as
possible, and in any case within twentyfour hours of the occurrence of the accident. He is not tied to any particular
police station, and it is difficult to
suggest that, unless the driver is incapacitated as a result of the accident, he
would be unable to make a report within
that period. It is inconceivable that the
police would prosecute a man for failing
to report if he were so injured that he
could not do so, and if the police did act
unreasonably in such a case, there is no
minimum penalty, and obviously the
681 Motor Vehicles Bill-

14 NOVEMBER 1930
Committee.
magistrates would be in a position to deal
with such a case in such a way as the
circumstances would warrant. For that
reason I must refuse to accept the Amendment.
Mr. LESLIE: I will not press it. I
do not want to make a change if I can
help it.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Question proposed: "That the Clause
stand part of the Bill."
Colonel PAKENHAM: Might I be
allowed to ask a question? On the
Second Reading of this Measure I requested the Government to consider the
question of the certificate of insurance,
and if they think it possible to devise
some method by which motorists might be
allowed to have an additional shield on
their cars carrying the certificate of insurance, or-and this would be easier still
-surcharge the annual licence with the
letters "T.P.I."-third party insurance.
That would avoid a lot of trouble and give
a great deal of satisfaction to a number of
people, and it would also help a great
deal in the question of traffic regulations and police supervision.
do not say they should not have
certificate. The authorities are not going
to issue the annual licence unless the
motorist has got the insurance certificate.
If some such plan were adopted it would,
as I say, save a great deal of trouble.
think it would redound to everybody's
satisfaction and make the Bill more easily
worked.
I
a
I
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I think
this is an attractive proposal, but it is
feared that it would give rise to very
considerable administrative difficulties.
In the first place it would mean that
most of the existing policies would have
to be altered so as to synchronise with
the dates of the issue of the excise licence,
and this would involve insurance
companies in a very considerable amount
of extra administrative expenses. It is
possible that in many cases the term of
the policy would not be co-extensive with
the currency
of the licence. Many
licences are taken out quarterly or halfyearly, and there would be no guarantee
that the certificate had not become void
for
some reason or other during the
currency of the licence; but one of the
most serious difficulties would be this,
that it would be necessary for the driver
to have evidence of the character of the
insurance which is in force with respect
to the vehicle. For instance, the policy
may be an owner-driver policy, and there
would be nothing to show the policeman
what was the nature of the insurance on
the vehicle. If he has to assume that
because a vehicle is licensed, it is duly
insured, he would have no means of detecting offences where the vehicle is not
being driven in accordance with the limitations of the policy of insurance. The
certificate will contain particulars of the
limitations in use imposed by the policy.
If a vehicle is insured to be driven by
the owner only, that fact will be stated
on the certificate. I am afraid that it is
not possible to work on the suggestion of
the hon. and gallant Senator. As I have
said, it is an attractive idea.
Clause ordered to stand part of the
Bill.
CLAUSE 11 (Liability of insurers,
etc., for expenses of hospital treatment).
(1) Where any payment is made by
an authorised insurer under a policy
issued under this Part of this Act, or
by the owner of a motor car, in relation to the user of which a security
under this Part of this Act is in force,
or who has made a deposit under this
Act, in respect of the death of or
bodily injury to any person arising out
of the use of a motor car on a public
highway, and the person who has so
died or been bodily injured has to the
knowledge of the authorised insurer or
such owner received treatment in a
hospital in respect of the fatal or other
bodily injury so arising, there shall
also be paid by the authorised insurer
or such owner to such hospital the expenses reasonably incurred by the
hospital in affording such treatment to
an amount not exceeding twenty-five
pounds for each person so treated:
Provided that this section shall not
apply in any case where a charge has
been made by the hospital in respect
of such treatment.
*
*
*
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
page 11, line 32, after "Act" ("Under
this Act") inesrt "or any ex gratia payment is made by an authorised insurer or
such other owner.
The object of the Amendment is to
make clear the position with regard to a
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
class of case which does arise from time
to time as a sequel to motor-car accidents
on the public highway. Clause 11 deals
with the liability of insurers for the expenses of hospital treatment, and it provides for cases where payments are made
by authorised insurers under a policy
issued under this part of the Act in respect of the death of or bodily injury to
any person arising out of the use of a
motor-car on the public highway. As I
understand the case it applies to cases
where liability in law is admitted by the
insurer, or where liability in law has been
established against the insurer. I think it
does not extend to the case-the rather
common case-where liability is disputed
by an insurer who says: My injured
driver was not negligent and I shall fight
the claim made in respect of this accident." The fight begins, and sometime
afterwards negotiations arise with a view
to settlement and to avoiding expense,
worry and litigation.

Then when outside the court the insurer
says, "I still maintain I am right and
free from all responsibility for this accident on the part of my driver, but while
denying liability I am prepared purely
ex gratia to make payment of a certain
amount and have the thing disposed of."
The foe is captured by that proposal,
and very properly, because he also is in
peril of the litigation turning against him
and losing his case. He may be captured
and an ex gratia payment pass between
the two. That, to my mind,
be a
may
payment not made under a policy of insurance, but one made outside a policy
of insurance without any admission of
legal liability, and if the payment is
not made under the insurance policy
I fear the insurer may be entitled
to say this payment is not made
under this Clause. The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) remarked on the
Second Reading of the Bill that an insurer when making an ex gratia payment
to an insured person would also be free to
make an ex gratia payment to a hospital,
but of course an insurer will only pay
what he is compelled to pay under this
Clause.
Mr. LESLIE: Is the hon. and learned
counsel thinking more about the method
of recovering payment under a policy than
anything else? It seems to me that a
question might arise as to whether an
ex gratia payment is a payment under a
policy. It really is a voluntary act on
the part of the insurers because they are
afraid of the law or because they think
it is the cheapest way out. I, myself,
think it would be a payment under the
policy, but it is almost certain that the
question will arise in practice, and something may have to be provided later on.
After all this is, to some extent, an experimental Clause in an experimental Bill.
Even in England they have not much
information on this point. I think the
point is worthy of attention.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: The Government's view is that it is reasonable that
if the insurance company make a payment
under their policy in respect of the accident, the hospital should not be left out;
but surely it would be an extraordinary
extension of the principles which now
regulate the law in respect of negligence
if it were said that because "A" makes
an ex gratia payment to "B" although
he has no legal liability whatsoever, "A"
is then to become liable to pay a further
sum to "C." If the payment is in respect
of a legal liability under the policy, then
the hospital has a claim under this Section
but it would certainly be an extraordinary
state of affairs if ex gratia payments were
to create further liabilities on the person
making them. For that reason I cannot
accept the Amendment.
;
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move in
Clause 11, page 11, line 42, after the
word "treated" to insert the words "and
such expenses shall be a debt recoverable
by the hospital in a County Court, and
the decision of that Court shall be final.”
I do not intend to speak at length in
regard to this Amendment, but it seems
to me that the hospital expenses ought to
be paid by the insurer, that the debt is
created between the insurer and the
nospital, and that the hospital should
have the ordinary remedy against the insurer. I propose that the decision of the
County Court should be final, with a view
to keeping the legal expenses within
reasonable bounds.
This
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
Amendment is, in the opinion of the
Government, quite unnecessary, and
certainly might result in considerable
difficulties. The Bill creates a liability to
685 Motor Vehicles Bill- -

Committee.
pay, and therefore a debt due to the

would be necessary to issue a writ in the

to the hospitals, but would take away

and other debts, and that the ordinary
right of appeal of decision in the County
Court should be taken away.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg leave to
withdraw the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

a
Mr. LESLIE: The Amendment on the

Paper would be thought to meet the case.
Such words do not occur in the Act in force
across the water, and it occurred to me

might give us their views.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: If the

Colonel SINCLAIR: I am not quite

It
Colonel SINCLAIR: It occurs to my
mind that when a person is admitted to
hospital as the result of a motor
accident, very often the condition is

On the other hand, if after a reasonable
stay in hospital and after having undergone treatment a report in writing on
the patient is required, then it would
seem to me to have all the importance
and weight of a legal document.
might be that even at that stage the

The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Campbell:-
After Clause 16, insert as new Clause-
"For the purposes of the trial of a
person for an offence under this Act,
or the Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic
Acts, 1926 and 1929, by a court of
summary jurisdiction consisting of a
resident magistrate sitting alone, and
the punishment thereof, the summons
may require such person to appear at
such court, on the date appointed for
the holding of the ordinary petty sessions at the place where such trial
might be held, or on any other weekday
appointed by a resident magistrate,
and in other respects such trial, and
the proceedings antecedent and subsequent thereto, shall be in accordance
with the Summary Jurisdiction (Ireland) Acts."
Mr. CAMPBELL: As hon. Members
are aware, cases under the Motor Vehicles
Act must be heard by resident magistrates.
Summonses, I believe, are issued in a
haphazard fashion. Experience has
shown that there might be only one motor
car case for hearing at one petty sessions
court, and resident magistrates might
have to make a long journey to attend
that court. At another court, on the
same day half a dozen motor cases might
have to be deferred for want of resident
magistrates being in attendance. The
present system which leads to those results
is both troublesome and expensive. The
procedure, I believe, is this: A summons
is issued, the petty sessions clerk requires
a special court, a resident magistrates'
court, and if they find the resident magistrate in the district is engaged on that day,
they look for another resident magistrate
who is idle, and then summon him to
attend. Why should not a resident
magistrate hear all motor car cases arising
in his own district? Why should not
summonses issued in his own district be
sent to him for signature? He could then
arrange a court and hear all the cases.
At present many petty sessions courts
in the same district clash with one another
and also clash with the courts in other
districts, with the result that some
resident magistrates are engaged on some
days with both hands and on other days
they are idle. The dates of petty sessions
courts cannot be changed without the
consent of the local magistrates, and I
believe the local justices are not in a
mood just now to facilitate the resident
magistrates. I submit that if resident
magistrates had power to appoint a day
for hearing motor car cases these difficulties would be largely lessened, and each
resident magistrate could easily dispose
of all the cases in his own district.
change would not in any way curtail the
powers of the local justices, and I do not
think they would object. In fact, I think
it would benefit the local justices by leaving them to hold their own courts. It
would benefit the motor car offenders, who
at the present time have to wait several
hours for the termination of the ordinary
petty sessions court before their cases are
heard. It would also be an advantage to
the professional men.
The
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As I understand it the proposal of the hon. and
learned Senator is designed with a view to
mitigating the inconvenience which at
present arises in a few instances as a
result of having to send resident magistrates to petty sessions for the hearing
of motor cases. I fear his proposal
would fail in its object and would not
be to the public advantage.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Committee.
14 NOVEMBER 1930
As the House is aware, the dates of
petty sessions courts are matters of common knowledge, and, accordingly, the
Press and members of the public who
may be interested are afforded an opportunity of attending the court, and the
legal practitioners who attend at petty
sessions can arrange their business with
a full knowledge beforehand of the dates
of their court business. If special
arrangements for attendance at courts
for motor cases have to be made it seems
inevitable that public inconvenience will
result and possibly increased expense.
There seems, moreover, no reason why
motor cases should be singled out for such
special treatment, and an arrangement
which would provide for such cases being
heard at times when the public would
have no knowledge of the date of hearing
and when the interested parties themselves would only become aware of the
dates on the issue of the summonses,
would prove inconvenient to all concerned and would take away from the
public character of the court proceedings.
I may point out, moreover, that administrative difficulty and confusion would
result, which would outweigh any possible advantages.
It will be realised that there are many
cases other than motor cases requiring
the attendance of resident magistrates.
In some instances two resident magistrates are necessary. Where possible a
resident magistrate of an adjoining district who is not required at petty sessions in the ordinary course and who is
not urgently required on other duty in
his own district is detailed to attend.
For this purpose the days when a resident magistrate has no petty sessions
court in his own district represent what
I may term a working margin, which
enables this to be done. If such days
were now to be taken up at the discretion of the resident magistrates for
hearing motor cases, which are at present
heard on ordinary court days, it will be
obvious that the difficulties generally of
providing resident magistrates at the
various courts would be so increased as
to render administrative action exceedingly difficult. For these reasons I am
afraid I cannot accept the Amendment.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to withdraw
the Amendment.
Amendment, by leave, withdrawn.

authority" for certain purposes) ordered

and

Mr. LESLIE: I understand that the

the question will arise whether it would

Viscount CHARLEMONT: To tell the

Tuesday, 18th November, 1930.
after
The Senate met at a Quarter
Three of the clock, Mr. Speaker in the
Chair.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I did not hear that
tacit acquiescence. (Laughter).
Viscount CHARLEMONT:
too tacit.
It was
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Suspension of Standing Order
Moved: "That Standing Order No.
XXV be suspended for this day's sitting,
for the purpose of taking the Third
Reading of the Motor Vehicles and Road
Traffic Bill."-(Viscount Charlemont).
Mr. CAMPBELL: I protest against
the suspension of Standing Orders.
The fact that this is a motor Bill is no
reason why the time-limit should be
used. There has been no time wasted
on the discussion of this Bill, and the
noble Lord gave no reason why we should
depart from the usual rules on this
particular Bill.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is
merely a question of whether this House
would be justified in meeting to-morrow
to do the concluding stages after the
Report stage. It would be really a
formal matter. There are two Amendments on the Order Paper which I think
will be satisfactorily disposed of, and
that is the only reason I can put forward
for haste. It would really be scarcely
worth while for the whole House to have
to meet to-morrow for business which
could not possibly take more than three
or four minutes. At the same time I
will leave it entirely to the discretion of
the House.
Mr. LESLIE: I think the proposal
to suspend Standing Orders was tacitly
agreed to at the last meeting.
It was
intimated that that would be the proposal of the Government, and no objection was taken at the time. Indeed most
of us came up here this afternoon on the
understanding, implied if not expressed,
that we should not be required to attend
again to-morrow. (Hon. Members:
Hear, hear). I hope, remembering the
tacit acquiescence, the hon. and learned
Member will not think it necessary to
pursue the matter further.
Protection of Hares Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Moved: "That this House do now resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Leslie.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(Viscount BANGOR in the Chair).
CLAUSES 1 and 2 (Citation) ordered
to stand part of the Bill.
Preamble and Title agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill be reported to
the House without amendment.
Ordered: That the House do now
resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported without amendment.
Moved: "That this Report be now
received." (Mr. Leslie.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendments read.
Moved: "That this Report be now received." (Viscount Charlemont.)
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move
after Clause 4 to insert
(1) It shall not be lawful for more
than one person, in addition to
the driver, to be carried on any
two-wheeled motor cycle, nor
shall it be lawful for any such one
person to be so carried otherwise
than sitting on a proper seat
securely fixed to the cycle behind
the driver's seat.
Motor Vehicles Bill-
Report of Amendments
THE SENATE
(2) If any person is carried on
any
such cycle in contravention of the
provisions of this Section, the
driver of the cycle shall be liable
on summary conviction in the case
of the first offence to a fine not
exceeding five pounds, and in the
case of a second or subsequent
offence to a fine not exceeding ten
pounds.
I move this Amendment in the interests
of the travelling public, both motorists.
and pedestrians, and also in the interests
of the young people who, to a very large
extent, use motor cycles as a means of
pillion riding to their own danger. Hon.
Members will observe it does not prevent
or seek to prevent the driving of a
bicycle by one person with another person
on a seat securely fixed to the cycle behind
the driver's seat, but it does prevent more
than two persons riding on a motor cycle.
I need not repeat the reasons I have given
already. I beg to move.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: As the
opinion of the House was very much in
favour of this Amendment at the previous
meeting, I will accept it.
Amendment agreed to.
Mr. CAMPBELL: May I have leave
to make a verbal Amendment which is
consequential? In the third line of the
second paragraph I wish to leave out the
word "the" and insert "a," making it
read "a first offence."
Mr. SPEAKER: I think that is a
printer's mistake which can
can be made
right.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, after Clause 17 to insert as a new
Clause:
18 (Exemption of Crown from Part
II of Act). Part II of this Act shall not
apply to the Crown.
I do not think I need give any great
explanation of this Clause. I think it
stands to reason that we cannot include
the Crown. It would be a matter of the
smaller including the larger.
Mr. DICKSON: Am I to understand
that Mr. Campbell's Amendment does
not apply to Crown officials riding motor
cycles? (Laughter.)
Viscount CHARLEMONT: This is a
very nice point. I think it is really
Crown vehicles that are meant to be excluded from Part II of the Bill. If a
Crown official gets on a private vehicle
and takes more than one pillion rider, or
runs over some one, he would be liable to
be proceeded against and mulcted in
damages just the same as any private
person would be. Theoretically speaking,
I am in the service of the Crown, and if
we pursued the point raised by Senator
Hill Dickson to its utmost length I might
take a thousand people on my pillion, a
thing I would be very sorry to do.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think the Clause
would not apply to Crown officials, because I have never seen a Crown official
riding a motor cycle. I have never even
heard of one riding a motor cycle.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: Civil servants?
Mr.
CAMPBELL: They use other
vehicles of a larger type.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: The
noble Leader of the House says he might
be called a Crown servant. I would like
to ask him for a definition of a Crown
servant.
VISCOUNT CHARLEMONT: I am
afraid I spoke in a rather broad way
before. I do not think I would come
under the Clause of the Bill referred to.
I would take a Crown vehicle to be an
Army, Navy, Air Force, Police, or Post
Office tender. To my certain knowledge
none of the Ministries of the Government
of Northern Ireland have any vehicles
that could be described as Crown vehicles
under this Bill. There are certainly
none in the Ministry of Education.
Mr. LESLIE: Might I suggest that
perhaps the solution of the difficulty,
which seems to perplex some hon. Senators, will be found if they will read the
provisions of sub-clause (1) of Clause 5.
I think that makes the position perfectly
clear.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendments were accordingly
reported, and agreed to by the House.
Viscount CHARLEMONT:: In moving the Third Reading of this Bill, T
should like to give one or two assurances
and
other information
some
on
some
Motor Vehicles Bill
Adjournment-
18 NOVEMBER 1930
of the Clauses. The hon. and learned
Senator (Mr. Leslie) asked what the
Ministry of Home Affairs would do with
the particulars of convictions of British
motorists in Northern Ireland which the
court has to forward to the Ministry of
Home Affairs. The particulars would be
sent to the Ministry of Transport for
transmission to the local authority which
issued the licence, so that they might be
recorded in the Register.
On Clause 5 there was another question
as to the speed of invalid carriages. I
would say that, theoretically, there is no
speed limit for invalid carriages, but at
the same time, as they are nearly always
in the category of bath chairs, it is unlikely that their speed will cause any
danger to the public. I think there was
a third question raised, but I am afraid
I do not remember what it was about.
am
Mr. LESLIE: Rear red lights.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I
obliged to the hon. and learned Member
for reminding me. Offences relating to rear
red lights will be triable by the ordinary
justices. I beg to move, "That this Bill
be now read the Third time."
Mr. LESLIE: I have, not for the first
time, to thank the noble Leader for the
trouble he has taken in looking into and
replying to the various inquisitions put
forward during the passage of the Bill.
If I understood him correctly as regards
the third matter he mentioned, he stated
the Government was definitely of the
opinion that ordinary magistrates might
hear those little trivial cases relating to
red rear lights. If that is so it will remove a possible cause of difficulty arising
from the earlier Statute.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass."
-(Viscount Charlemont.)
On Question, Motion agreed to.
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to acquaint them that the
Senate have agreed to the said Bill with
some Amendments, to which they desire.
their concurrence.
Adjournment.
UNEMPLOYMENT.
Viscount CHARLEMONT: I beg to
move, "That this House do now adjourn
until such time as Mr. Speaker shall
appoint."
Mr. CAMPBELL: Before we separate
to-day, not to re-assemble for a few
months, I would like to express the concern with which some hon. Members of
this House view the privations with which
many of our people in Northern Ireland
will be face to face during the coming
winter months, and the apathy or helplessness of the Government in face of the
situation in which they find themselves
placed. The unemployed in Northern
Ireland on the 10th inst., a little over a
week ago, numbered 72,562, an increase
of about 2,000 since the beginning of
November. One stands aghast at these
figures. Behind them is a terrible background of suffering and want, and the
Government, confronted with those
figures, seem powerless to rise above mere
humdrum routine administration. Between now and next March, when I suppose we reassemble, the Government will
be hibernating in the winter months, and
the only matter I can see which will be
engaging their dreams will be the coursing of hares, March hares.
The fact is the Government is a dead
tired Government. Our necessities are increasing, our expenditure is on a scale not
exceeded by any Government outside
Great Britain, and, pursuing the ordinary
progress of a spendthrift, the Government.
seems to grow more reckless the deeper it
plunges. We have officials out of all proportion to the reasonable needs of our
small area and population. We have
reason to think that this year will be the
worst the linen trade has ever experienced.
Shipbuilding is in a parlous state, and
agriculture is in a parlous state, but
nothing seems to matter. The Government jogs along quite happy looking and
quite self-satisfied. They used to say in
the Army, "Old soldiers never die.
Apparently the hope of politicians here is
that old Governments will never die.
A very distinguished statesman in England has said, referring to administration,
that one of the essential things required
is vitamines and red corpuscles. In our
anaemic administration here those two
things are never seen.
""
Viscount CHARLEMONT: It is the
greatest mistake on the part of the hon.
and learned Member to suppose that when
this House or the other House is not
sitting the Government are hibernating.
On the contrary, when they have not to
meet the criticisms and reply to the talk-
Adjournment-
Unemployment
THE SENATE
[Viscount Charlemont]
ing of various Members of the Opposition
that is the very time when they can get
down to some real work. Of course, the
unemployment figures are extremely bad,
but I would suggest that the Government
are not in the least apathetic about the
matter. They do their level best to keep
the figures within reasonable limits.
Senator Campbell referred to unemployment in the shipbuilding industry, but
how in the world can the Government help
the fact that the steamship companies do
not place their orders in Belfast, or that
they do not place any orders anywhere at
all.
The Government can, by means of
the Loans Guarantee and other Acts,
assist shipbuilders and steamship companies, but their power does not go so
far as to force people to place orders which
they would not otherwise give. The same
thing applies to other branches of trade
and industry. I can only promise that
during their time of "hibernation" the
Government will keep a wakeful eye on
this problem, and will do the utmost in
their power to relieve the situation. At
the same time, it is not to be expected
that the Government of Northern Ireland
can do anything greater than the Government of Great Britain or the Governments
of other countries which are faced with
precisely the same problem.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly, at a
quarter before Four o'clock,
until such date as Mr.
Speaker shall appoint.

Prorogation
25 NOVEMBER 1930
THE SENATE.

Tuesday, 25th November, 1930.
The Senate met at Five Minutes before
Twelve of the Clock noon, Mr. SPEAKER
in the Chair.

Royal Assent.
Mr. LONG: I am authorised by the
Prime Minister, on his behalf, to inform
the Senate that His Grace the Governor
of Northern Ireland, in the name of and
on behalf of His Majesty the King, has
been pleased to give his Assent to the
following Bills:--
Agricultural Produce (Meat Regulation).
Constabulary.
Electricity (Interim Supply).
Motor Vehicles and Road Traffic.
Third Parties (Rights Against Insurers).
Expiring Laws Continuance.
Weights and Measures (Amendment).
Exchequer and Financial Provisions.
Tithe Rentcharge and Variable Rents.
Appropriation (No. 2).
Larne Urban District Council.
McNulty's Divorce.
Prorogation of Parliament.
Moved: "That the Gentleman Usher
of the Black Rod be commanded to inform
the Commons that the Proclamation proroguing the present session of Parliament
is to be read forthwith, and inviting the
Commons to attend in order to hear the
same."--(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The Commons being at the Bar, with
their Speaker, the Proclamation was read
to both Houses of Parliament by the
Speaker of the Senate (in pursuance of
His Grace's command), as followeth :
BY HIS GRACE THE GOVERNOR OF
NORTHERN IRELAND.
A PROCLAMATION
For Proroguing the present Parliament
of Northern Ireland.
WHEREAS it is provided by section
eleven of the Government of Ireland Act,
1920, as modified by and in pursuance of
certain enactments amending the said
Act, that the Governor of Northern
Ireland shall in His Majesty's name
summon, prorogue and dissolve the Parliament of Northern Ireland;
AND WHEREAS it is desirable that this
present Parliament of Northern Ireland
should be prorogued to the third day of
March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Thirty-One.
NOW I, JAMES ALBERT ED-
WARD, DUKE OF ABERCORN,
KNIGHT OF THE MOST NOBLE
ORDER OF THE GARTER, KNIGHT
OF THE MOST ILLUSTRIOUS OR-
DER OF ST. PATRICK, Governor of
Northern Ireland, by this Proclamation
do publish and declare His Majesty's
Pleasure that the said Parliament be, as
from the reading of this Proclamation in
the Parliament of Northern Ireland,
Prorogation
Prorogation
THE SENATE
prorogued to Tuesday, the Third day
of March, One Thousand Nine Hundred
and Thirty-One.
AND I do hereby further declare His
Majesty's Royal Will and Pleasure to be
that the said Parliament shall on the said
Third day of March, One Thousand Nine
Hundred and Thirty-One, assemble and
be holden for the despatch of divers
urgent and important affairs, and the
Senators and Members of the House of
Commons of Northern Ireland are hereby
required to give their attendance accordingly.
Given at the Council Chamber, Belfast,
this 25th day of November, One Thousand
Nine Hundred and Thirty.
Signed by his own hand.
ABERCORN.
GOD SAVE THE KING.
in
End of the Second Session (opened

Tuesday, 11th March, 1930) of the Third
Parliament of Northern Ireland
the Twenty-first year of the Reign of
His Majesty King George the Fifth.
