UNIVERSITY of CALIFORNIA
AT
LOS ANGELES
LIBRARY
UC SOUTHERN REGIONAL LIBRARY FACILITY
A 000 175 243 5

Thursday, 7th May, 1930.
The Senate met at a Quarter after
Three of the Clock, Mr. SPEAKER in
the Chair.
Questions to Minister.
LOUGH NEAGH POLLAN FISHING.
The following question stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Colonel
Pakenham:
:-
To ask the Leader of the House
whether, in view of the known heavy decline in the pollan fishing industry in
Lough Neagh, the Government would
consider the advisability of prohibiting
the catching and sale of pollan for a
period of two years in the interests of the
industry.
""
Colonel PAKENHAM: I would ask
for the indulgence of the House if I
amplify somewhat the question on the
Paper, as it has a rather bald appearance
as it stands. Pollan, whose ichthyological name is coregonus, exists
only in two Loughs and both
are in
Ireland, Lough Neagh and a Lough
in the west. In Professor Seeley's
"Fresh Water Fishes of Europe,'
published in 1886, he records that in 1880
in one haul in Lough Neagh 17,220 of
these fish were caught at one time, and
were sold on the spot for a sum of £23.
Now it is quite common for fishermen
to go out and bring in twelve fishes, and
on many occasions this year and last year
they went out and brought in none at
all. It is not likely that in fifty years
the quality of the water in Lough Neagh
has altered so much that those fish cannot
live in it, and, therefore, the decline
must be put down to other causes.
know, and I have been told by my fishermen friends, that it is due to the use by
certain fishermen of nets of too small
mesh, and especially to the use of drag
nets, which catch immature fish that do
not live to the spawning age. The result
is that the supply of fish has declined to
a tremendous extent. We ought to be
able to arrange to prevent that in some
way. The Free State, when they feared
that partridges were going to be entirely
eradicated, prohibited the shooting or
sale of partridges for three years, with
I
the result, I understand, that they have
largely increased their stock. If the
Free State can do that for a game bird,
which principally exists for sport and
amusement, how much more should we
do in this country for an industry on
which a large number of people depend
for their livelihood.
Mr. LONG: There are about 150 men
dependent on the fishing of pollan, and
loans have been granted to some twelve
persons of sums ranging from £10 to £56.
There are seventy licence holders.
It is common knowledge that the
catches of pollan in Lough Neagh during
recent years have shown a heavy decline.
As the report of an Inquiry conducted in
1924 at the instance of the Imperial
Secretary of State indicated, the causes
of this decline were largely a matter of
opinion. To some extent they were no
doubt natural causes which the total prohibition suggested by the hon. and
gallant Member would not remove.
Whilst the Government would be
naturally reluctant to make any regulations which would inflict hardship upon
the fishermen concerned, the continued
depletion of the pollan stocks appears to
warrant consideration, and to this end
the Minister of Commerce proposes to
submit the hon. and gallant Member's
suggestion for the views of the local
Board of Conservators and the Ministry
of Commerce Fishery Advisory Committee.
Mr. McALLISTER: I am sorry I
did not know until I came into the House
that this question was on the Paper.
I should have thought the principal
people to be consulted in connection with
this matter were the fishermen themselves,
because they are the people mostly concerned. If the catching and sale of
pollan is prohibited for a period of two
years what is to happen to the fishermen
who have spent their whole lifetime
engaged in this occupation, and who
depend upon it for their livelihood. Are
they for a period of two years to be
denied the right to earn their livelihood.
Would it not be much more to the point
if steps were taken to vest in these fishermen the rights that are now vested in a
limited company. I think this is a very
grave question so far as the fishermen are
concerned, and I am sorry I did not have
more notice of it, especially in view of
the reply of the Deputy-Leader of the
House. He gave no indication that the
Oral Answers.
Local Bills.
7 MAY 1931
fishermen of Lough Neagh would be consulted in any action that might be taken
to carry out the suggestion of the hon.
and gallant Senator. The Board of Conservators are to be consulted, but I should
have thought the people most directly and
most vitally concerned, namely, the fishermen, ought to be the first to be consulted.
I am sorry the Deputy-Leader of the
House did not state that these people
would be consulted, because it is a very
serious matter for them. The Government are the trustees of the owners of
this fishing, a limited company, but they
are also the trustees of the poor people
who live on the shores of Lough Neagh
and who earn their living by means of
this industry. I sincerely hope that these
fishermen will have an opportunity of
placing their views before the Government before they come to any conclusion
as to the future of this fishing industry.
are
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
disagree with what the hon. Member
(Mr. McAllister) has said. I rather take
the view that the steps suggested by the
hon. and gallant Member (Colonel Pakenham) will be the salvation of the fishermen of Lough Neagh. It has been stated
that at the present time 150 men
dependent on the fishing of pollan for a
living, but if the present state of affairs
is allowed to continue for another two
years the fishing of pollan may have come
to an end. Pollan is one of the rarest
fish in Europe, and it would be a great
pity if it was allowed to die out. I agree
that something should be done not only
for the good of the fish but for the good
of the fishermen also.
Colonel
PAKENHAM:
Senator
McAllister is wrong when he talks about a
company owning the pollan fishing rights.
They are entirely vested in the fishermen.
The company owns the eel fishing.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I think it would
be a wise thing if in any steps the Government contemplate taking the wishes of
the fishermen concerned were consulted.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
am sure that would be done.
Mr. LONG: In reply to Senator
McAllister, I should like to state that the
fishermen are represented on the Board
of Conservators who are to be consulted.
However, I will convey the suggestion of
the hon. Member to the Ministry of Commerce for their consideration.
Message from the Commons.
The Commons have read a Second Time
Macartney's Divorce Bill; they have
passed the Ministry of Home Affairs
Provisional Order Confirmation (Strangford Waterworks) Bill; they agree to the
Amendments made by the Joint Committee to the Belfast Harbour Bill; and they
have passed the Planning and Housing
Bill, to which they desire the concurrence
of this House.
Macartney's Divorce Bill.
Resolved: "That this Bill be now read
a First Time."
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Order Confirmation (Strangford
Waterworks) Bill.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read a
First Time."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Planning and Housing Bill.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read a
First Time."-(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Ministry of Home Affairs Provisional
Order Confirmation (Banbridge
Union Borrowing) Bill.
Moved: "That this Bill be now read a
Second Time."—(Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Marketing of Eggs Bill.
Order for Second Reading read.
Mr. LONG: In presenting this Bill
for the consideration of hon. Members I
think it will be sufficient if I explain the
circumstances which have rendered it
necessary for the Ministry of Agriculture
to make a further addition to the existing
code of legislation dealing with the
marketing of eggs.
Marketing of Eggs Bill-
Second Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Long]
Since 1926 Northern Ireland eggs have
shared with British eggs the advantage of
being the only eggs marketed in Great
Britain which do not require to have the
name of the country of origin marked on
the shell. Northern Ireland eggs are,
therefore, in the same position as English
new laid eggs, and as a consequence of the
improvement in our egg trade which has
resulted from previous Acts, we have been
able to obtain a better price for our eggs
than those from any other country exporting to the British market. It is true that
a small proportion of the English eggs
coming under a special National Mark
Scheme do command a slightly better price
and there is a possibility that this scheme
may in the future embrace a very large
proportion, if not all, of the home produced eggs marketed in England. Foreign
countries, such as Holland and Denmark,
are also succeeding in their efforts to
improve the quality of their eggs sent to
Great Britain, and it may be that the
consistently high quality which Danish
and Dutch eggs have attained may result
in the preference of the British consumer
for unmarked Northern Ireland eggs being
weakened unless our eggs continue to
maintain the lead in quality.
Unfortunately it became apparent last
summer that a quantity of the eggs exported from Northern Ireland ports did
not come up to the standards prescribed by
the Marketing of Eggs Acts, and investigations conducted by the Ministry show
that this inferior quality on the part of a
proportion of our eggs is due to improper
methods of production on the farm.
Cockerels are too frequently retained with
the laying flock from May onwards, and
this leads to the production of fertile eggs,
which rapidly become second quality.
Then again, producers are prone to wash
eggs which have been soiled, and these
washed or steeped eggs deteriorate rapidly
in quality.
Another practice which is harmful to
our trade is the storage of eggs on the
farm under unsuitable conditions pending collection of these eggs by collectors.
The loss to the trade through these
second-quality eggs is very considerable.
amounting to £30,000 or £40,000 per
annum on a conservative basis.
Consequently, after the fullest consultation with the Ministry's Advisory
Committee on Eggs it was decided to
promote the present Bill, which aims at
increasing the revenue of the Ministry
from licences under the Marketing of
Eggs Bill so as to provide funds for the
conduct of an intensive educational campaign amongst producers. Representatives of the trade are satisfied that this
campaign must be conducted by the
Ministry to prove successful, and the increase in fees, which is proposed so as to
enable the Ministry to carry on this
educational campaign has been wholeheartedly accepted by the egg trade. I
wish to emphasise, therefore, that the
Bill is an agreed Measure.
The proposals with regard to fees are
that two kinds of licences should be
issued: First, a shipper's licence, and
second, a collector's licence. The fee for
a shipper's licence will be £3 per annum,
and for a collector's licence £1 per
annum. In addition, the Bill authorises
the Ministry to levy a charge not exceeding a penny per case of 360 eggs exported. To start with, however, it is
not intended that this charge should exceed d. per case, and any variations
which are subsequently made will be
made on the advice of the Advisory Committee, and after full consultation with
the trade.
It is estimated that in this way a sum
of between £2,500 and £3,000 will be
available for the educational scheme
which it is proposed to introduce.
There is one remaining matter in the
present Bill which deserves mention. It
will be observed that in Clause 6 powers
are being taken to strengthen the law
which applies to the principal Act, that
1s, to the Marketing of Eggs Act of 1924.
The proposal in the Bill as it stands is
that there should be an appeal allowed
from the dismissal of a case under that
Act by a Court of summary jurisdiction,
and that such an appeal should be heard
by the County Court Judge or the
Recorder sitting alone. Some exception
was taken to this way of dealing with
the matter in another place from a legal
point of view-(Mr. Leslie: Hear hear)-
as it was considered that, although
there are precedents for allowing such
appeals, it would be better to adhere to
the old principle that a person should
not be put on trial twice for the same
offence and so the Government would be
prepared to accept an Amendment to
Sub-Clause (1) of this Clause at a later
stage, which would have the effect of ensuring that the original cases will be
tried by a resident Magistrate, and that
301 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

7 MAY 1931
Second Reading
this provision giving the right of appeal
should then disappear. As I have said,
however, it is very necessary that something should be done to strengthen the
law in this matter.
In regard to Sub-Clause (3) of Clause
6, there is also a matter which I may mention specially. It will be observed that the
Bill as it stands provides that a case may
be brought under the principal Act by
or with the consent of the Ministry, or
by a District-Inspector or other officer of
the Royal Ulster Constabulary, or by
an inspector or other officer of the Ministry authorised in that behalf by
direction of the Ministry, and so forth.
Representations were made in another
place that this was going too far and
would allow an inspector of the Ministry
to take it on himself to institute proceedings without the consent of the Ministry, and would also allow such a person
to prosecute in Court. The Government
is quite prepared to meet this objection,
and it is proposed that a suitable
Amendment should be put down to this
effect in the Committee stage. I beg to
move.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I rise to
support this Bill because I think it goes
in the right direction, which is the
education of our farmers in the proper
upbringing and rearing of poultry. But
I do not honestly think I can look forward with very much pleasure to the
gentleman who has got to go about instructing because it is very well known
among ourselves and among our people
that they have the habit of running
cockerels and hens together, and it is
a very hard thing to tell the farmer, and
to tell the farmer's wife, that that is an
unsound proposition. Some go so far
as to say that unless you have cockerels
with them they lay no eggs. I do not
envy the gentleman who has got to go
about talking to them. Another thing
to be considered is how are you going
to start this instruction. Very often in
connection with the Farmers' Union
we have meetings of farmers arranged to
come and listen to someone talking about
milk production. Very often after long
notice meetings have been called, and
the usual thing is that about two people
turn up.
The rest do not come. I do
not envy the gentleman who has got to
go round the farms teaching the farmer
and the farmer's wife. If the Minister
thinks he can do this I say more power
to his elbow. I hope he will succeed, but
I think he will find he will have a hard
row to hoe in teaching people who think
they know a great deal better than the
inspector. As the trade is paying most
of the money, there is no fault to find.
Mr. SPEAKER: I should like to remind hon. Members that if they wish to
speak they should rise at once in their
places.
Mr. LESLIE: I am sure, like the
hon. and gallant Senator on my left, we
are all disposed to welcome anything
that will render greater in the future
than in the past the success which has
attended the efforts of the Ministry in
the direction of improving, the price and
quality of the Northern Ireland egg.
We are all at one on that point. But
we ought to remember sometimes, perhaps, the old saying to the effect that
pride goes before a fall. It is not quite
right for it to be suggested that there is
no possible method of education in these
matters except by means of the Ministry.
There are periodicals devoted to eggs
and poultry published in the adjacent
island, commonly called Great Britain,
and there are opportunities of obtaining
information in what I may call the more
ordinary manner. As far as that goes
there would seem to be no objection to an
educational campaign for which the
money is provided, not out of the public
pocket, but out of the pockets of various
classes of dealers in eggs.
I was sorry to hear the Parliamentary
Secretary speaking of the egg trade as
though these dealers of one kind or
another were the only people engaged in
the egg trade. It seemed to me that the
person possessing the actual producer,
the hen, was a person very much
engaged in the egg trade and a person
whose views should be taken into consideration, and the particular reason
why he should be taken into consideration is that one is a little afraid lest this
acceptance of additional tribute from
the dealers may have the result of
driving all the trade into the hands of
these dealers, and putting these dealers
into the position of being able to rig the
price of eggs as against the producerif I may use that expression as meaning
still the man who possesses the hens.
There is a danger of that arising. We
do know from experience that the existence of the middleman has not been
303 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Second Reading
[Mr. Leslie]
altogether to the advantage of the producer, and it seems to me that has to be
carefully guarded against.
The House will notice the proposal in,
I think, Clause 5 as regards the restriction of what I may call the direct supply
of eggs.
That is only to be undertaken
by the ordinary person under the conditions specified. I should like the
right hon. Gentleman, the Minister, to
tell us definitely what I know is the intention, which is not in the Bill, that is,
that the permit to export quantities exceeding 300 eggs will not cost the
producer any money. All that is intended to happen is that steps should be
taken to see that those eggs go from
proper places. I should like to be assured on that point.
One passes for the moment to Clause
6. I cannot admit that if the proposals
in Clause 6 become law they would in
any way strengthen the law. I should
take the other view. I should say they
would destroy it. I imagine if there is one
thing more settled in principle, it is the
point to which the Parliamentary Secretary himself alluded, that is, the objection
to a man being vexed twice on the same
ground. It must be remembered if you
resort to summary jurisdiction at all you
must at least ensure that the jurisdiction
will be summary. The moment you
allow an appeal to an unsuccessful
prosecutor you strike at the root of that
summary jurisdiction. It does no harm, 1
think, to remember that all summary
jurisdiction is a little bit apart from the
ordinary principles which govern this
matter. The old-fashioned principle
was that a man was entitled to something in the nature of a jury. I need
not quote the original Latin or the date.
Everybody will know what I mean.
That is why in so many cases where
statute matters are tried summarily it
is so
common to require at least two
magistrates' decision. That is of great
importance.
There is something else left out. The
Parliamentary Secretary did not tell us
or give any indication what case he had
for making an alteration in the law or
seeking to make an alteration. I think before the House is asked to make any departure in this matter from ordinary
principles a full case ought to made out.
It may be possible or probable that
there has been a case or there may have
been cases in one or the other of the
country districts where the bench of
magistates has dismissed a prosecution
which the Department considered should
have resulted in a conviction. That
may or may not be. If it be the point
I can only say the court heard the case
and gave their decision. It was their
But we
business and nobody else's.
have not been told that there have been
any number of cases of that class. Therefore, I should be very glad if an Amendment is put down, and I gather it will
be put down by the Government itself
expunging that. I hope something will
be substituted and not have an appeal
from one man sitting alone to another.
There is a point about Clause 6 which
might commend itself to us, but I do not
include in that the proposal that an
officer of the Ministry should be permitted to appear in court and conduct
the case. I have found in more ways
than one in a lengthy experience that if
a case has to be argued before any
judiciary it is very much better done by
a trained lawyer. The trained lawyer
is of definite assistance to the court; the
untrained civilian is frequently from the
point of view of the adjudicators a positive nuisance, and the ordinary person
prosecuting on behalf of some other body
has not, as a rule, got ground into him
those principles which the lawyer, whose
business it is to appear as advocate in
court, has. He will not, as a rule, be
content that the prosecution should leave
the facts before the court and leave the
court to say where these facts lead.
is, as a rule, far to apt to adopt a partisan attitude. However commendable
it may be in civil cases where the duty
of counsel is to get a verdict for his client
if he reasonably can, it should not be introduced in criminal cases. The ordinary person cannot be relied upon to
separate proceedings in civil cases from
those in criminal cases. It is a most
important principle, and I detest any
alteration in it. Too much has been
done in that direction already, but those
things are more or less matters of detail.
We are told that Clause 6 is not intended
to stand, and, therefore, I cannot see
any reason for objecting to the Bill as a
whole.
He
Mr. McALLISTER: I would like to
support the case made by the hon. and
learned Senator in respect of prosecutions
before Courts of Summary Jurisdiction.
It is a well established and well tried prin-
305 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

7 MAY 1931
Second Reading
ciple of law, and once we create a precedent in giving a right of appeal in cases
like this, it will lead to appeals in all
cases, with costly consequences.
With
regard to the other matter of prosecu
tions being conducted by officials of the
Ministry, I also support Senator Leslie's
arguments, but from another aspect.
You may get an inexperienced prosecutor
who does not know the rules of procedure, and he invariably asks questions
that should not be asked, and which do
an injustice to the defendant. Before he
is pulled up the damage is done. I have
seen these untrained and unskilled prosecutors do grievous injustice to defendants, who are entitled to be prosecuted
according to the strict method of the law.
On that principle I support the remarks
of Senator Leslie.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
support the Bill. We have heard a
great deal about the law, but my experience teaches me that we have to take
into consideration the prejudices of the
people. To illustrate my point I should
like to quote an experience of my own.
When I was allowed to be a landlord I
visited one of my tenants' houses to
examine a roof. The house was dirty,
and I looked underneath the bed and saw
a pot of milk or cream apparently put
there to "riz" as they called it. I said,
"Surely it is not clean", and the man
replied, "That may be, but it was good
enough for my father, and it is good
enough for me". There is no use trying
to educate the people until these prejudices disappear, and we must try to
educate the people in that direction. I
think Senator Leslie said the principal
party to be consulted was the hen.
Mr. LESLIE: No, the owner of the
hen.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
would say the principal person to be
consulted was the owner of the cockerel.
Mr. HILL DICKSON: I support the
Bill, and I think the previous speakers
will agree with me that education is
spreading all over the North of Ireland
through the medium of the Ministry's
centres of instruction at Antrim and
Cookstown. The students from these
schools are spreading their knowledge.
throughout the Province with beneficial
results. I should like to ask the Parliamentary Secretary what he was referring
to when he spoke of National Mark eggs.
The MINISTER OF AGRICULTURE
(Sir E. Archdale): I am very grateful to
the Senate for the favourable reception
they have given to this Bill. With
regard to what the hon. and gallant
Senator (Colonel Pakenham) said about
the mistaken notions of farmers' wives
on the subject of allowing cockerels to
run with the hens, I am afraid the ladies
of the Province will be rather antagonistic
to his views. The Ministry are not going
to take any immediate or forcible steps
under this Bill, but they will allow ample
time for those concerned to become
educated to the fact that when eggs are
fertile they will not keep. Senator Leslie
could not permit a Bill to pass without
making considerable remarks upon it.
He is very kind in his attention to all the
Bills we bring in, and sometimes he puts
down Amendments which I think are
already in the Bill. I may point out, in
reply to one of his questions, that permits
to send eggs abroad will be given to all
large poultry farmers, who will not have
to pay for a licence. A man with a great
number of poultry like Senator Colonel
Pakenham will be allowed to send eggs
abroad. He will not have to have a
licence. I am glad Senator Leslie did not
say he wanted the hens to be consulted,
because he certainly seemed to convey
that in his speech.
With regard to the question of prosecutions being conducted by officials of the
Ministry, an Amendment which is to be
moved in Committee will remove that provision from the Bill, and prosecutions may
only be conducted by a person learned in
the law, a police officer, or other such
person. Provision is made for appeals,
because at the present time the Ministry
know of hundreds of cases where prosecutions have been brought, and the magistrate who has most power on the bench
is most likely himself a producer of eggs.
Very often decisions are given which are
certainly not borne out by the facts.
That is the reason why, at the wish of the
legal Members in another place, we
amended Clause 6 of the Bill. Senator
Hill Dickson asked what the National
mark was. In England and Scotland all
eggs that are certified as newly laid, and
which fulfil all the necessary conditions,
are given a National Mark, and National
Mark eggs have increased enormously in
England. That is the principal reason
that we are trying to tighten up the
marketing of eggs in Northern Ireland
as to enable them to compete with
SO
307 Marketing of Eggs Bill-

THE SENATE
Wild Birds Bill-
[Sir Edward Archdale]
National Mark eggs. At the present
time National Mark eggs bring the
highest price in the English markets, and
Northern Ireland eggs come next, and unless we do tighten up our marketing conditions our eggs will drop in the English
market, because there are now so many
National Mark eggs on sale from Scotland and all parts of England.
The Ministry will do all in their power
to lessen the dfficulties of the producers
of eggs, who are chiefly ladies, and they
will not move too rapidly or act in an
arbitrary manner SO as to damage the
good-will of the producers or collectors.
of eggs.
The collectors are in favour of
this Measure, and they would be willing
to pay more than £1 if the Ministry required it, but we are of opinion that a
fee of £1 will raise all the money that
is required, and we do not want to charge
them more than we can possibly help.
On Question, Motion ("That the Bill
be now read a Second Time') agreed to.
Bill committed to a Committee of the
whole House for Tuesday next.
Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Order for the House to be put into
Committee read.
Mr. LONG: It is not intended to proceed with the Committee Stage of this
Bill to-day, as it is considered that the
time that has elapsed since the Second
Reading is too short. On the Motion for
the Adjournment I will endeavour to
ascertain whether Tuesday next would be
convenient for hon. Members to take the
Committee Stage.
Education Bill.
Order for receiving Report of Amendment read.
Moved, "That this Report be now
received." (Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
The said Amendment was accordingly
reported and agreed to by the House.
Wild Birds Protection Bill (S.).
Order for Third Reading read.
Mr. CAMPBELL: I beg to move,
"That this Bill be now read the Third
Time". If the House agrees to the Third
Reading I propose to move two Amendments with a view to meeting certain
criticisms passed in this House with regard to two parts of the Bill. The first
Amendment is to make it clear that the
prohibition directed against the use of
mechanically propelled boats or aircraft,
for the purpose of killing, injuring, or
taking birds is to be strictly confined to
boats or aircraft used in the actual immediate pursuit of birds. It does not
extend and never was intended to extend
beyond that. The second Amendment is
directed to the Section of the Bill dealing
with the powers and duties of the police.
In moving the Third Reading of this
Bill I should like to say that the Bill
compresses into twenty Clauses a code of
protection for birds in Northern Ireland.
Wild birds have now slight protection
under the law, and the great majority of
the public hold that they ought to get
comprehensive protection against the
cruel and the thoughtless. The present
state of the law is a state of anarchy. The
time has come for an endeavour to bring
the law into harmony with public sentiment. The Bill reverses the old principle
of the law that birds, their eggs, and nests
are in general unprotected, with special
exceptions, and substitutes for it the
sounder principle that birds, their eggs,
and nests are in general protected unless
specially excepted. The Bill gives protection all the year round to all wild birds
except 27 species, their eggs, and nests.
People should remember that the birds
which are hurtful to the interests of the
farmer and gardener are very few, that
while some work mischief they also do
good, and that the great majority of birds
in our area are not only innocuous, but
actually beneficent. The Bill enables the
authorities to exclude from protection at
any time any species of birds whose
operations may be found injurious to
agriculture or other reasonable human
interests. I should mention that in
Scotland fifty-four kinds of birds have
absolute protection all the year round
against all classes of the community.
General protection of birds is the law in
Holland, and is working well. It is also
in operation in various places in the
United States of America.
Wild Birds Bill-
Third Reading
7 MAY 1931
I should wish at this stage to tender
very sincere thanks to friends and
opponents in this House for their extreme
patience and courtesy in all stages of this
Bill. I tender special thanks to you, Mr.
Speaker, my Lord. I should also like to
include the very admirable Clerk of the
Senate for his record of the proceedingsa wonderful work of accuracy. It would
be an omission on my part also if I did
not mention the assistance given by a
public servant of conspicuous ability, Mr.
R. P. Pim, who has a special knowledge of
this subject. This Bill has now been before
the House-
Colonel
Mr. Leslie.
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
Mr. CAMPBELL: I forgot my hon.
Friend (Mr. Leslie). It would be very
ungenerous on my part if I did not
acknowledge his great work on this Bill.
I admire his Parliamentary draughtsmanship and his deep knowledge of the subject.
I am very grateful to him.
As I said, the Bill has been before
Parliament and the public for weeks, and
no note of opposition to it has been
sounded in any responsible outside.
quarter. I have proofs of the sympathy
the Bill has evoked among all classes. I
have received invaluable hints from
various persons and organisations concerned with the protection of bird life,
and am grateful for that help.
The issue raised by the Bill is: Do we
really want to protect the birds of this
Province? Are we really determined on
it? Are we prepared
prepared to take the
measures necessary for
that object
according to the most competent authorities in this area-men of the eminence of
Professor Gregg-Wilson? The House is
aware of the representative character of
the Committee which recommended the
Measure, farmers, scientists, labourers,
landowners, and practical ornithologists
who reported unanimously in favour of
the scheme of protection of bird life
which this Bill provides, particularly in
view of the effect of such protection upon
the agriculture of the Province of Ulster.
That report has been laid before Parliament. I have never heard of any serious
challenge to its main recommendations.
I should like to say that a highly respected Member of this House, Mr. Hill
Dickson, rendered meritorious service as
a member of the Committee. The
intention of the Committee was to give a
real and not an illusory grudging degree
of protection to our wild birds.
The House will remember that, speaking in this House on 29th October last
the Deputy Leader of the House said
that the Government were generally
speaking in full sympathy with the
objects aimed at by the Committee and
presented in their report. One chief recommendation of the Committee was that
all birds in Northern Ireland with the
exception of some species should be protected all the year round from all classes
of the public alike. It was not the view
of the Committee that they should give
protection only to rare birds in danger
of extinction. No doubt we should
protect the aristocrats among the birds,
but we should not stop there. We should
protect the common birds also which give
charm to the countryside. There will
be general agreement with the statement
of Senator Leslie that 999 out of every
1,000 owners or occupiers of land would
never think of shooting innocuous or
beneficent birds on their land. The
desire of the farmers is to protect not to
kill those birds. The food of the great
majority of our birds consists of grubs
and insects, and such birds are true
friends of the farmer.
I think the House must have felt the
force of the very impressive speech the
hon. and gallant Senator, Colonel Sinclair, delivered in the House on the
Report stage. There spoke the scientist
and the scholar, dealing with the subject
of the beneficent effect of the continued
existence of bird life. Very often we
forget the benefits and the gratitude we
One realises that if it
owe to birds.
were not for the existence of birds we
would suffer from the ravages of insect
life. It is said, and I believe it is a
scientific fact that seven years or so without bird life on the globe would leave us
without a blade of grass and without
vegetation of any kind.
I should like also to say that in this
Province if it were not for the existence
of broad demesnes bird life in Ulster today would be much poorer than it is. The
birds find sanctuary there. I think it is
due to the existence of these large
demesnes to a large extent that the Province to-day is rich in bird life. This Bill
goes a long way. It cannot, of course, do
complete justice to all birds whose habitat
is in or around our area. We are, for
instance, powerless to protect sea birds
Wild Birds Bill-
Third Reading
THE SENATE
[Mr. Campbell]
from being killed on the high seas round
our coasts by the fuel oil cast from ships
-from the terrible death of starvation
made more terrible as a distinguished
writer says, by a sense of degradation, for
a sea bird's plumage is its pride.
We set little store by some of our birds.
For instance, there is the green-finch. A
passion for the green-finch possesses the
peasantry of Germany who often undergo
privations to bring a green-finch in fine
song. Here we rank the green-finch as a
rather unwelcome, mischievous chap.
Travellers who have been in the backwoods
of Australia say nothing is more depressing there than the absence of bird-song.
The silence is eerie to one coming from
Northern Ireland with its variety of bird
life and bird song.
I should like to tell a little story. Ex-
President Roosevelt during his visit to
England on his way home after his world
tour asked that somebody who knew the
songs of English birds should go with
him for a walk in the country, and as the
songs were heard tell him what the birds
were.
Sir Edward Grey (now Lord
Grey of Falloden), then Foreign Secretary, went with him. Mr. Roosevelt
picked out the song of the blackbird as
the best of the songs they heard. He
recognised the song of the golden-crested
wren, the smallest bird in these isles,
and also the only bird we have in common with the North-American continent.
It is an arresting picture-these two remarkable statesmen lost to the world for
about 20 hours listening to the cries and
notes of the wild birds of the countryside. Mr. Roosevelt came from a country
throughout whose vast area-from the
waters of the Atlantic to the waters of
the Pacific-Federal laws prohibit the
caging of native wild birds.
I will conclude by saying that we
know very little yet about birds, their
mysterious sense of direction, or their
instinct of migration, but I am strongly
urging the House that it is our duty to
safeguard bird life for its advantages to
the community at large. I think the
birds of Ulster are a precious heritage
and possession of the people; a heritage
which we should cherish and a possession
we should preserve. I beg to move that
the Bill be now read the Third time.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
ask for information. Is it too late to
say anything about the schedule of the
Bill?
Mr. SPEAKER: That should have
come on at the Committee Stage.
Mr. LESLIE: The hon. and learned
Senator in the course of his address
rendered thanks to a great many people
including myself, and I think the House
should really thank the hon. and learned
Senator for the pleasure he has given us
by the introduction of this Bill, a Bill
dealing with a subject with which everybody is more or less in sympathy.
There are one or two things which the
hon. and learned Senator said with which
I am afraid I cannot agree. He said the
law was in a state of anarchy. I do not
agree that that is so. I do agree that it
is very much misunderstood.
there is a general idea perhaps that birds
not in the schedule of the original Act of
1880 could be destroyed at any time by
anybody. That is not so.
When he
I think
spoke of the dangers which sea-birds experienced from oil fuel I am not quite
sure whether he remembered that there
is an Act on the Statute Book passed a
few years ago which does give some protection in that matter. I am not sure,
speaking from memory, whether any
special mention was made of Northern
Ireland, nor do I think it was necessary,
being a sea matter. There is no protection, so far as I know against destruction
or injury of birds flying against either
telegraph wire of to-day or the electric
power cable of to-morrow.
However, we all wish this Bill well.
It will be leaving us now to go to another
place, and it will have to face there the
blast of public opinion, be it favourable
or unfavourable, I do not know. It will
now be considered in the somewhat wider
atmosphere, whether the Bill does carry
out its purpose of simplifying the law, and
I am afraid the criticism may be urged
that by putting birds in categories, the
first of which consists entirely of exceptions-almost entirely of exceptions as i
appears on the paper-simplification has
not been arrived at. It is also to be
ascertained whether public opinion will
stand the complete suppression of bird
nesting, and the complete abolition of the
open season for all birds other than semigame birds placed in the third category
in the schedule.
I think criticism will be again directed
to the lack of definition of game. I am
afraid that the problem of the waterhen-I take it as an example-will be
brought up, as I brought it up here. It
Wild Birds Bill-
7 MAY 1931
Third Reading
will be remembered that under the Bill
a man cannot shoot a waterhen at any time
during the year, lest if a peregrine falcon
comes along he might shoot it by mistake
because he thought it was a waterhen.
All these matters will be considered in
another place, and it only remains to
express good wishes for the Bill in whatever form it may ultimately assume.
Mr. SPEAKER: The hon. and gallant
Member (Colonel Sharman-Crawford) is
entitled to make any remarks he desires
on any part of the Bill. I was previously
under the impression that he wished to
move an Amendment to the Schedule.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
desired to make a few remarks about the
rook in Category 1 of the Schedule. I
have a rookery on my property, and from
my experience of farming I look upon
the rook as one of my best friends. He
may do some damage, but I am sure the
good he does on a farm in destroying
pests, especially the wireworm, is much
greater than any harm he does. I have
a rookery at Crawfordsburn, and it would
be a sad day for me if that rookery came
to an end.
Colonel PAKENHAM: I would like
to ask the hon. and gallant Senator if
he has ever seen rooks on the top of corn
stooks and seen the damage they do.
They come in such vast numbers that no
corn stooks can exist with them.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: I
disagree entirely with the hon. and
gallant Senator.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: I
always understood that the rook never
alighted on the ground where it had its
rookery. It went to other people's land.
If that is the case I can quite understand
the hon. and gallant Senator (Colonel
Sharman-Crawford) wishing to keep his
rookery, if the rooks feed on their neighbours.
Colonel
SHARMAN-CRAWFORD:
It is not true.
Mr. SAMUEL CUNNINGHAM: In
olden times in Scotland pigeon-houses
were kept, and the pigeons fed on
neighbours' premises. Other people
were not allowed to kill them, so the
owners were able to get pigeon pie from
pigeons fed not by themselves but by
their neighbours.
Mr. BARCLAY: I do not agree that
rookeries should be protected. My experience some years ago in the country
was that these rookeries were a nuisance
to the farming community. I have seen
great plots of land where turnips have
been sown, and after they were thinned
and were beginning to grow nicely the
rooks pulled them out one by one and left
them lying on the ground. The farmer
lost practically his whole crop. I have
also seen them destroying crops of
potatoes. When the new potatoes were
beginning to form at the roots the rooks
pulled the tops out of the ground and
ate the young potatoes. I cannot see
why we should protect a bird like that.
Colonel SHARMAN-CRAWFORD: It
is a mistake to say that. I have had
crops of potatoes and oats, and whatever
loss I may incur from rooks I receive
greater benefits from them in other ways.
Mr. McCORKELL: There seems to be
considerable interest in this question of
the rook, and there also seems to be considerable confusion. We are getting
rather into a mist as to what the rook
does and does not do. It has been stated
that the rook is being protected under
this Bill. The rook is one bird that is
not being protected; in fact, he has lost
the protection he once had. Many of us
have a rookery, and we can look after it
quite well ourselves. If the rooks get too
plentiful we can thin them out, and if they
become too scarce we can prevent people
encroaching near the rookery. I have
known rooks to kill ducks, although that
is not their usual practice, and we can
get rid of them if they do it. I wish the
Bill well because Senator Campbell has
taken a great deal of trouble with it,
although he has given us some trouble
about it.
How-
Personally I am afraid the Bill will not
have the success which it should merit. We
all want birds protected, but I in common
with other Members think there is a good
deal wrong with the present Bill.
ever, it seeks to protect our bird life, and
on that ground I support it. .I could if I
liked go on criticising it for hours. For
instance, I might ask why the starling,
a most useful, insectivorous bird, is to
be allowed to be murdered. However,
on the whole I will support the Bill, and
I think that if we stopped following the
mist and got on with the business we
would do better.
On Question, Motion, ("That the Bill
be now read the Third time") agreed to.
CLAUSE 1 (Illegal methods of exposing to injury, taking, or killing birds).
315 Marketing of Fruit Bill-
Committee

*

(d) to pursue in any mechanically
propelled boat or in any aircraft any
bird for the purpose of killing, injuring or taking such bird.
Amendment made:
Page 1, line 18, leave out "to pursue
in any mechanically propelled boat or in
"to use any
any aircraft" and insert
mechanically propelled boat or any aircraft in actual and immediate pursuit of"
-(Mr. Campbell).
CLAUSE 15 (Powers and duties of
officers and constables of the Royal Ulster
Constabulary).
*
*
*
*
*
*
(6) An officer or constable shall, if
he has reasonable cause to suspect that
any offence under this Act has been,
is being, or is about to be, committed
on any land, have power to enter on
the land without the consent of the
owner or occupier and inspect the land,
and any person who by himself, or by
any person in his employment or acting
with his knowledge or consent, refuses,
fails, or unreasonably delays, to admit
such officer or constable, or hinders him
in such inspection, shall be guilty of
an offence under this Act.
*
*
*
*
Amendment made: :-
*
*
Page 10, leave out from "land" in line
24 to the end of sub-section (6)—(Mr.
Campbell).
Moved, "That this Bill do now pass.
-(Mr. Campbell).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
""
And a Message was sent to the House
of Commons to carry down the said Bill
and desire their concurrence.
Marketing of Fruit Bill.
Order for the House to be again put into
Committee read.
Moved, "That this House do now again
resolve itself into Committee on the said
Bill." (Mr. Long).
On Question, Motion agreed to.
In Committee accordingly.
(Viscount BANGOR in the Chair).
CLAUSE 9 (Savings).
The provisions of this Part of this
Act shall not apply to-
(a) fruit as respects which the
Ministry of Agriculture or an
*
authorised officer is satisfied that it
was grown outside Northern Ireland,
and which is not unpacked in Northern Ireland before being sent out of
Northern Ireland, but so that the
container bears no mark or other indication calculated to lead to the
belief that the fruit is Northern Ireland produce;
*
*
*
*
*
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :
Clause 9, page 7, line 1, leave out from
"and" to the end of line 6, and insert
"is not being, or intended to be, sent out
of Northern Ireland under the authority
of a licence or permit under this Part of
this Act."
Mr. LESLIE: This is the first of two
Amendments which have proved to be
necessary. It may be remembered that
on the Committee Stage of the Bill I
pointed out that there seemed to me to
be a certain discordance between two
passages in the Bill, and it being obvious
that some alteration was necessary, I was
not quite clear as to whether it would be
more convenient to deal with the matter
by means of an Amendment moved after
the Third Reading, or whether it might
not be more simple to re-commit the Bill
in order that the alteration might be
made in whatever place seemed to be the
most suitable. It now appears that it is
better to alter the wording of Clause 9
by omitting the words relating to fruit
grown outside Northern Ireland, and
substituting the words on the Paper.
Accordingly, I beg to move.
Mr. LONG: In accepting this Amendment I wish to thank the hon. and
learned Senator for the assistance he has
given in this matter, and the interest he
has shown.
Amendment agreed to.
CLAUSE 17 (Application of Act).
The provisions in this Act shall apply
to fruit grown or produced in Northern Ireland, and not to any other fruit;
and if, in a prosecution for an offence
under this Act in relation to any fruit,
the question arises whether such fruit
was grown or produced in Northern Ireland, the burden of proving that such
fruit was grown or produced elsewhere
shall rest upon the person charged with
the offence.
The following Amendment stood on the
Order Paper in the name of Mr. Leslie :-
317 Marketing of Fruit Bill
Adjournment
7 MAY 1931

Clause 17, page 12, line 27, leave out
from "shall" to end of line 34 and
insert "not, save as therein expressly provided, apply to fruit grown outside Northern Ireland; and where, in any prosecution for an offence under this Act, it
is in issue whether any fruit was, or was
not, grown in Northern Ireland, it shall
be sufficient as respects that issue for the
defendant to satisfy the court that he had
reasonable grounds for believing that such
fruit was not grown in Northern Ireland.'
Mr. LESLIE: This is the second half
of the necessary transaction. We get rid
of the words "shall apply to fruit grown
or produced in Northern Ireland," and we
re-adjust the latter part of the Clause.
As regards the first part of the Amendment I need say nothing, but as regards
the second part a little should be said.
There is always a reluctance to interfere
in any way with the old rule about a
man being regarded as innocent until he
is proved guilty, but a more modern
influence has brought itself to bear on that
matter, and I am not sure that as yet it
has met with quite the attention it deserves.
What I mean is that in 1923 we adopted
here a Criminal Evidence Act based on
the English Act. The result of that is
that any man charged with an offence
can, if he chooses, go into the
witness box and tell his story
oath. If he does not go into the witness
box the court, as a rule, has very little
difficulty in deciding as to his guilt or
innocence. If he does go into the witness
box, let us say, in connection with a
matter relating to apples, which he says
were grown outside Northern Ireland
and which the prosecution suggest were
not, the very first question he will be
asked on cross-examination is: "Where
did you get these apples?" The fact that
that possibility exists makes it more easy
than ever it was before to avoid using a
form of words which might be vindictive.
on
I suggest a new form of words which 1
think meets the requirements. Accordingly I submit to the House that this
second Amendment will remove the
ambiguity to which I drew attention,
and will perhaps improve the Bill a little
bit. Therefore I move to leave out everything in Clause 17, from the word
"shall," and to insert in place of the
words taken out "not save as therein expressly provided, apply to fruit grown outside Northern Ierland; and where, in any
prosecution for an offence under this Act,
it is in issue whether any fruit was, or
was not, grown in Northern Ireland, it
shall be sufficient for the defendant to
satisfy the court that he had reasonable
grounds for believing that such fruit was
not grown in Northern Ireland."
Mr. LONG: The Government are prepared to accept the Amendment.
On Question ("That the words proposed to be left out stand part of the
Question.") Motion negatived.
On Question, ("That these words be
there inserted.")
Mr. LESLIE: You are now putting the
Question that these words should be inserted. I am afraid I did not notice that
on the Paper. They are not quite
correctly printed. The intention was
that they should read: "it shall be sufficient as respects that issue."
Amendment, as amended, agreed to.
Ordered: That the Bill, as re-committed, be reported with Amendments to
the House.
Ordered: That the House do now resume.
House resumed accordingly.
Bill reported with Amendments to the
House.
Adjournment.
Mr. LONG: I beg to move "That this
House do now adjourn." I suggest that
we should adjourn until Tuesday, if that
would suit Members of the House, to take
the Committee Stage of the Electricity
(Supply) Bill. They would also have the
Committee Stage of the Marketing of
Eggs Bill, and the Third Reading of the
Fruit Bill. I should like to know whether
Tuesday would suit Members of the
House to deal with the Committee Stage
of the Electricity (Supply) Bill.
Mr. LESLIE: I wonder if we can get
any further information as to what else
will happen next week. Suppose we say
Tuesday, will it be necessary to sit on
Wednesday or Thursday? If the work
is getting short, so to speak, possibly an
extra day might be given to the Electricity Bill. I do not think it matters very
much. I have not myself studied the
Electricity Bill.
On Question, Motion agreed to.
Adjourned accordingly at
Twenty Minutes before
Five o'clock until Tuesday
next.
